topics.title: Static
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET411 - ELECTRONIC INSTRUMENTATION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Static

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In the context of an **Electronic Measurement System**, the term **"Static Characteristics"** refers to the set of performance attributes of an instrument that describe its behaviour when the measurand (the quantity being measured) is **constant** or **varying very slowly with time** — i.e., under **steady-state (DC) conditions** where the input is not a function of time or its time-variation is so slow that dynamic effects (inertia, thermal lag, etc.) can be neglected.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Verbatim Style):**
> *"Static characteristics of a measurement system are those characteristics that describe the performance of the instrument when the input is either held constant or is varying slowly enough that time-dependent (dynamic) effects become negligible. They define the steady-state relationship between the true value of the measurand and the output of the instrument."*

Mathematically, a static measurement system is modelled as a **memory-less, time-invariant function**:

$$
y = f(x)
$$

where $x$ is the static input (measurand) and $y$ is the output (indication). Because there is no explicit time dependence, the system can be fully characterized by a single-input, single-output (SISO) calibration curve plotted on a static $x$–$y$ graph.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The Bathroom Weighing Scale 🧍‍♂️⚖️**
>
> Imagine you step onto a digital bathroom scale and simply **stand still** on it. The number you see is the scale's *output*, and your actual body weight is the *input*. Now, you are not jumping, not moving, not vibrating — everything is **calm and steady**. The qualities the scale exhibits *right now, in this still moment* — how close the reading is to your true weight (**accuracy**), whether it gives the same reading every time you step off and on (**repeatability**), whether the smallest weight change it can detect is 100 g or 1 kg (**resolution**), and whether a 1 kg standard weight always shows exactly 1 kg more than a 2 kg standard weight (**linearity**) — are all **static characteristics**.
>
> If you were to **jump** on the scale, the needle would swing wildly — that would then be governed by the **dynamic characteristics** (a different Module). For now, "static" = **steady, calm, unchanging in time**.

### 1.3 The Two Broad Categories

Static characteristics are conventionally divided into two logical groups:

| **Group** | **What it Describes** | **Examples** |
|---|---|---|
| **Ideal (Desired) Characteristics** | Properties an instrument *should* have to be considered "good" | Accuracy, Precision, Sensitivity, Resolution, Linearity |
| **Undesirable (Error-Inducing) Characteristics** | Imperfections and parasitic effects | Static Error, Hysteresis, Dead Zone, Drift, Noise (static component) |

### 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics (Bolded)

- The standard reference temperature for instrument specifications is **T = 25 °C (298.15 K)** unless otherwise stated.
- Sensitivity is expressed in units of **output / input** (e.g., mV/°C, V/bar, mm/µm).
- Resolution is expressed in units of the **input measurand** (e.g., 0.1 °C, 0.01 mm).
- Accuracy is expressed as a **percentage of Full-Scale (FS)** or **percentage of Reading (% RDG)**.
- The instrument is assumed to operate in the **International Standard Atmosphere (ISA)**: 1 atm = **101.325 kPa**, 25 °C, 50% RH.

> [!TIP]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** For KTU 2024, Module 1 of PEEET411 typically covers the fundamental **static and dynamic characteristics** of measurement systems. Static characteristics form the foundation — every later instrument (transducer, sensor, ADC) you study in this course is judged using *these very same metrics*.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Generic Static Calibration Model

A measurement instrument under static conditions can be represented as a **two-port functional block**:

$$
\boxed{\;x_{\text{in}}(t) \;\xrightarrow{\;\text{Measurement System}\;}\; y_{\text{out}}(t)\;}
$$

Under static conditions, the time-dependence drops out, leaving:

$$
y = f(x)
$$

Expanding $f(x)$ as a **Taylor series** around an operating point $x_0$:

$$
y = f(x_0) + \left.\frac{df}{dx}\right|_{x_0} (x - x_0) + \left.\frac{1}{2!}\frac{d^2f}{dx^2}\right|_{x_0} (x - x_0)^2 + \dots
$$

The first term is a constant offset (zero-error), the second term defines the **sensitivity (gain)**, and higher-order terms give rise to **non-linearity**.

### 2.2 Detailed Description of Each Static Characteristic

#### 2.2.1 Accuracy
- **Definition:** The closeness of an indicated value $y_{\text{indicated}}$ to the *true* value $x_{\text{true}}$ of the measurand.
- **Quantitatively:** $\text{Accuracy} = 1 - \left| \dfrac{y_{\text{indicated}} - x_{\text{true}}}{x_{\text{true}}} \right|$
- Usually expressed as **% of Full Scale (FS)** or **% of Reading**.

#### 2.2.2 Precision
- **Definition:** The ability of the instrument to **reproduce** a given reading when the same input is applied repeatedly under identical conditions.
- High precision does **not** imply high accuracy (a mis-calibrated but repeatable instrument is precise but inaccurate).

#### 2.2.3 Sensitivity
- **Definition:** The ratio of the change in output to the corresponding change in input.
- Mathematically: $S = \dfrac{dy}{dx}$ (for small-signal/static regime)
- A *highly sensitive* instrument produces a large output change for a tiny input change.

#### 2.2.4 Resolution (Discrimination)
- The **smallest measurable change** in the input that produces a detectable change in the output.
- Limited by the least-count of the readout (e.g., the LSB of a digital display, or the smallest graduation on a Vernier scale).

#### 2.2.5 Linearity
- A system is **linear** if the static calibration curve $y(x)$ is a *straight line* throughout the range.
- Linearity is quantified by the **maximum deviation** of the actual curve from the *best-fit straight line* (or the *zero-based line* passing through origin).
- $\text{Non-linearity (\%) } = \dfrac{\Delta y_{\max}}{y_{\text{FS}}} \times 100$

#### 2.2.6 Hysteresis
- The phenomenon where the output for a given input **depends on the direction** (increasing or decreasing) from which the input is approached.
- Caused by internal friction, magnetic hysteresis, backlash, or elastic after-effects.
- Quantified as: $\varepsilon_h = \dfrac{\vert y_{x \uparrow} - y_{x \downarrow} \vert_{\max}}{y_{\text{FS}}} \times 100\,\%$

#### 2.2.7 Dead Zone / Threshold
- The **smallest range of input** below which the output does not respond at all.
- For inputs $\vert x \vert < x_{\text{threshold}}$, $\;y = 0$ (or no detectable change).

#### 2.2.8 Static Error
- The difference between the indicated value and the true value at a specific input:
- $e_s = y_{\text{indicated}} - x_{\text{true}}$
- Can be expressed absolutely (in units of measurand) or relatively (as a fraction/% of FS).

#### 2.2.9 Drift
- A slow, unwanted change of the output with **time**, even when the input is held constant.
- Sub-types: **Zero drift** (offset shifts) and **Sensitivity drift** (gain shifts).
- Common causes: aging of components, temperature variations, supply-voltage fluctuations.

#### 2.2.10 Repeatability & Reproducibility
- **Repeatability:** Variation in output when the *same* input is applied repeatedly under *identical* conditions (same observer, same instrument, same environment, short time interval).
- **Reproducibility:** Variation when conditions are *changed* (different observer, lab, ambient temperature, time-of-day, etc.).
- Reproducibility error is always ≥ Repeatability error.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The table below is the **one-page survival sheet** for KTU board-exam problems on Static Characteristics. Memorize the column headers and units; the numerical values fall out from problem data.

| **#** | **Characteristic** | **Defining Formula** | **Typical Unit** | **Key Limiting Factor** |
|:---:|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Sensitivity ($S$) | $S = \dfrac{\Delta y}{\Delta x}$ | output / input | Internal gain, transducer constant |
| 2 | Static Error ($e_s$) | $e_s = y_{\text{ind}} - x_{\text{true}}$ | input units | Calibration, offset |
| 3 | % Error | $\% e = \dfrac{e_s}{x_{\text{true}}} \times 100$ | % | — |
| 4 | % Error (of FS) | $\% e_{FS} = \dfrac{e_s}{y_{FS}} \times 100$ | % FS | — |
| 5 | Resolution | Smallest $\Delta x$ producing a detectable $\Delta y$ | input units | LSB, least count |
| 6 | Linearity (Non-linearity) | $\% NL = \dfrac{\Delta y_{\max}}{y_{FS}} \times 100$ | % FS | Sensor geometry, electronics |
| 7 | Hysteresis | $\% H = \dfrac{\vert y_{\uparrow} - y_{\downarrow} \vert_{\max}}{y_{FS}} \times 100$ | % FS | Friction, backlash, magnetic domains |
| 8 | Dead Zone | Range of $x$ for which $y$ does not change | input units | Threshold of detection |
| 9 | Drift (Zero) | $\dfrac{\Delta y_0}{\Delta t}$ at constant $x=0$ | output / time | Aging, temperature |
| 10 | Drift (Sensitivity) | $\dfrac{\Delta S}{\Delta t}$ | (output/input)/time | Component aging |
| 11 | Repeatability | $\sigma$ of repeated readings (same conditions) | input units | Random noise, friction |
| 12 | Reproducibility | $\sigma$ of repeated readings (changed conditions) | input units | Calibration, environment |

> [!WARNING]
> **CRITICAL TABLE SYNTAX RULE (Followed Above):** Note that all absolute-value bars are written as `\vert` inside the math — the raw `|` character would have broken the markdown table. Always use this convention in your own KTU answer sheets when typesetting.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Static characteristics directly govern the **selection, calibration, and quality-assurance** of:

- **Industrial Process Control:** A thermocouple in a furnace must have known sensitivity (mV/°C) and linearity, otherwise the closed-loop PID controller will oscillate or produce steady-state offset.
- **Medical Instrumentation:** A sphygmomanometer (BP apparatus) must have ≤ ±3 mmHg static error to be clinically safe.
- **Aerospace & Avionics:** Pitot-static tubes in aircraft rely on accurate static pressure readings (hence the name "static port" — a beautiful pun in your syllabus!).
- **Automotive Sensors:** Engine Control Units (ECUs) trust MAP, MAF, and O₂ sensors that have been characterized for sensitivity, linearity, and drift over the vehicle's 10-year lifetime.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Relationship Between Sensitivity, Range, and Full-Scale Output

**Statement to prove:** For a linear static instrument, the sensitivity is the slope of the calibration line, and is given by $S = \dfrac{y_{FS}}{x_{FS}}$, where $y_{FS}$ is the full-scale output and $x_{FS}$ is the full-scale input.

**Derivation (Exhaustive Step-by-Step):**

**Step 1:** Under static, linear conditions, the calibration equation is a straight line:

$$
y = mx + c
$$

where $m$ is the slope (gain) and $c$ is the intercept (offset).

**Step 2:** By definition, the **sensitivity** of a static instrument is the slope of the output-versus-input curve:

$$
S \equiv m = \frac{dy}{dx} \bigg\vert_{\text{linear regime}}
$$

**Step 3:** For two known operating points on the line, namely the **zero point** $(x=0, y=c)$ and the **full-scale point** $(x = x_{FS}, y = y_{FS})$:

$$
\text{Slope} = \frac{y_{FS} - c}{x_{FS} - 0}
$$

**Step 4:** If the instrument is **zero-adjusted** (so that $c = 0$ when $x=0$), this simplifies to:

$$
\boxed{\,S = \frac{y_{FS}}{x_{FS}}\,}
$$

**Step 5 (Real-number illustration):** Suppose a pressure transducer has range $x_{FS} = 100$ bar and produces $y_{FS} = 5$ V at full-scale. Then:

$$
S = \frac{5 \text{ V}}{100 \text{ bar}} = 0.05 \text{ V/bar}
$$

This is a derived physical constant for that specific instrument.

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Non-Linearity (Independent, Best-Fit, and Zero-Based Methods)

A real calibration curve $y_{\text{actual}}(x)$ deviates from an ideal straight line. The deviation is measured as **non-linearity**.

**Step 1:** The output deviation at any input $x_i$ is:

$$
\Delta y_i = y_{\text{actual}}(x_i) - y_{\text{ideal}}(x_i)
$$

**Step 2:** Three common choices of $y_{\text{ideal}}(x)$ lead to three definitions of non-linearity:

**(a) Independent Linearity (Best-Fit Straight Line):**
The ideal line is the one that **minimizes the sum of squared deviations** $\sum_i (\Delta y_i)^2$ (a least-squares fit).

$$
\boxed{\,\%NL_{\text{indep}} = \frac{\vert \Delta y \vert_{\max}}{y_{FS}} \times 100\,}
$$

**(b) Zero-Based Linearity (Through Origin):**
The ideal line is forced to pass through $(0,0)$ and the full-scale point $(x_{FS}, y_{FS})$. Slope is $S = y_{FS}/x_{FS}$.

$$
\boxed{\,\%NL_{\text{zero}} = \frac{\vert y_{\text{actual}}(x_i) - S\,x_i \vert_{\max}}{y_{FS}} \times 100\,}
$$

**(c) Terminal-Based Linearity (End-Point Line):**
The ideal line connects the actual measured points at $x = x_{\min}$ and $x = x_{\max}$.

**Step 3:** Most KTU textbook problems use the **zero-based** or **end-point** form. Be sure to read the question carefully to identify which.

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Combined Hysteresis + Linearity Error (Worst-Case Static Error)

**Step 1:** The total worst-case static error at any input $x_i$ combines:
- (i) the non-linearity deviation $\Delta y_{NL,i}$,
- (ii) the hysteresis deviation $\Delta y_{H,i}$,
- (iii) the zero-offset $c$.

$$
e_{\text{total}, i} = c + \Delta y_{NL,i} + \Delta y_{H,i}
$$

**Step 2:** In the most pessimistic (additive) bound:

$$
\boxed{\,\%e_{\text{worst}} = \frac{\vert c \vert + \vert \Delta y_{NL} \vert_{\max} + \vert \Delta y_{H} \vert_{\max}}{y_{FS}} \times 100\,}
$$

**Step 3 (KTU Numerical Example Format):**
A transducer is rated 0–200 °C with output 0–10 V. Specifications list: zero offset $c = 0.05$ V, max non-linearity $= 0.04$ V, max hysteresis $= 0.06$ V. Compute the worst-case static error as % FS.

$$
\% e_{\text{worst}} = \frac{0.05 + 0.04 + 0.06}{10} \times 100 = 1.5\,\%\text{ FS}
$$

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — "Which Characteristic When?"

The following matrix helps you **identify the right characteristic** for any given KTU problem statement.

| **Symptom in the Question / Real System** | **Likely Characteristic to Discuss** |
|---|---|
| Reading changes by 0.5 V when input changes by 1 °C | **Sensitivity** |
| Two consecutive readings of the same value differ by ±2 units | **Repeatability / Precision** |
| Reading drifts slowly over hours/days at constant input | **Drift (Zero or Sensitivity)** |
| Going up and then coming down the scale gives two different readings for the same input | **Hysteresis** |
| Instrument reads 0 for inputs from 0 to 0.5 °C, then starts responding | **Dead Zone / Threshold** |
| Calibration curve is a curve, not a straight line | **Non-linearity** |
| Reading differs from the standard (true) reference value | **Static Error / Accuracy** |
| Smallest increment the display can show is 0.1 °C | **Resolution** |
| Readings differ when measured in two different labs | **Reproducibility** |

### 3.5 Worked-Out Numerical Derivation — Sensitivity of a Multi-Range Instrument

**Problem:** A voltmeter has ranges 0–1 V, 0–10 V, and 0–100 V. The full-scale deflection current is $I_{FSD} = 50\;\mu\text{A}$ through a series multiplier resistor $R_s$. Derive the sensitivity (in $\Omega$/V) for each range.

**Step 1:** Sensitivity of an analog voltmeter is defined as $S_v = \dfrac{R_s}{I_{FSD}}$ in $\Omega$/V (a "volt-sensitivity" rating).

**Step 2:** For range $V_{FS}$, the multiplier resistor is:

$$
R_s = \frac{V_{FS}}{I_{FSD}} - R_m
$$

where $R_m$ is the meter movement resistance. Ignoring $R_m$ (since $R_s \gg R_m$):

$$
R_s \approx \frac{V_{FS}}{I_{FSD}}
$$

**Step 3:** Plug in for each range:

- **0–1 V range:** $R_s = \dfrac{1\text{ V}}{50 \times 10^{-6}\text{ A}} = 20{,}000\;\Omega = 20\text{ k}\Omega$
- **0–10 V range:** $R_s = \dfrac{10}{50 \times 10^{-6}} = 200{,}000\;\Omega = 200\text{ k}\Omega$
- **0–100 V range:** $R_s = \dfrac{100}{50 \times 10^{-6}} = 2{,}000{,}000\;\Omega = 2\text{ M}\Omega$

**Step 4:** The volt-sensitivity (k$\Omega$/V) is:

$$
S_v = \frac{R_s}{V_{FS}} = \frac{1}{I_{FSD}} = \frac{1}{50\;\mu\text{A}} = 20{,}000\;\Omega/\text{V}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Key insight:** For an analog voltmeter, the *per-volt sensitivity* is **a constant** equal to $1/I_{FSD}$, independent of the selected range. The total multiplier resistance $R_s$ scales linearly with the range. This is a standard KTU board-question concept.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Generic Measurement-System Block Diagram (Functional Architecture)

The following Mermaid diagram depicts the **functional flow** of a complete static measurement chain, showing how each static characteristic arises at a particular stage.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Measurand x_true] --> B[Primary Sensor Element]
    B --> C[Variable Conversion Element]
    C --> D[Variable Manipulation Element]
    D --> E[Data Transmission Element]
    E --> F[Data Presentation Element]
    F --> G[Output y_indicated]

    A2[Static Errors injected here] -.-> B
    C2[Hysteresis and Linearity errors] -.-> C
    D2[Drift over time] -.-> D
    E2[Noise Pickup] -.-> E
    F2[Resolution limited by display] -.-> F
    G2[Accuracy comparison] -.-> G

    style A fill:#e3f2fd,stroke:#0d47a1
    style G fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20
    style A2 fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17
    style C2 fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17
    style D2 fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17
    style E2 fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17
    style F2 fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17
    style G2 fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17
```

**How to read this diagram:**
- The **blue-shaded leftmost block** is the *true* physical quantity in the world.
- The **green-shaded rightmost block** is the *indicated* value presented to the user.
- Every yellow-shaded "error injection" point in the middle represents a **static characteristic** that you must quantify.
- A perfect instrument would mean $y_{\text{indicated}} = x_{\text{true}}$ — i.e., the rightmost output equals the leftmost input.

### 4.2 Hysteresis Loop — Schematic Representation

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph HYS[Hysteresis Loop in Calibration Curve]
        direction TB
        P1[Start x = 0] --> P2[Increase x to x_FS - upper curve y_up]
        P2 --> P3[Reverse x back to 0 - lower curve y_down]
        P3 --> P4[Decrease x below 0 - extends to -x_FS]
        P4 --> P5[Reverse again to x_FS - close the loop]
    end

    P2 -.->|gap = hysteresis at any x| P3

    style P1 fill:#bbdefb,stroke:#0d47a1
    style P2 fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20
    style P3 fill:#ffcdd2,stroke:#b71c1c
    style P4 fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20
    style P5 fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The **green path** is the *increasing-input* branch.
- The **red path** is the *decreasing-input* branch.
- The vertical gap between green and red paths at any $x$ is the **hysteresis error** at that input.
- The maximum such gap (over the entire range) is the **$\Delta y_H$** used in the worst-case error formula.

### 4.3 Static Characteristic — Classification Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    SC[Static Characteristics of Instrument] --> IDEAL[Ideal Desired Properties]
    SC --> UNDESIR[Undesirable Error Properties]

    IDEAL --> ACC[Accuracy]
    IDEAL --> PRE[Precision / Repeatability]
    IDEAL --> SEN[Sensitivity]
    IDEAL --> RES[Resolution]
    IDEAL --> LIN[Linearity]

    UNDESIR --> SE[Static Error]
    UNDESIR --> HYS[Hysteresis]
    UNDESIR --> DZ[Dead Zone]
    UNDESIR --> DR[Drift]
    UNDESIR --> NL[Non-linearity]
    UNDESIR --> REP[Reproducibility Error]

    style SC fill:#1976d2,stroke:#0d47a1,color:#ffffff
    style IDEAL fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20
    style UNDESIR fill:#ffcdd2,stroke:#b71c1c
    style ACC fill:#e8f5e9,stroke:#1b5e20
    style PRE fill:#e8f5e9,stroke:#1b5e20
    style SEN fill:#e8f5e9,stroke:#1b5e20
    style RES fill:#e8f5e9,stroke:#1b5e20
    style LIN fill:#e8f5e9,stroke:#1b5e20
    style SE fill:#ffebee,stroke:#b71c1c
    style HYS fill:#ffebee,stroke:#b71c1c
    style DZ fill:#ffebee,stroke:#b71c1c
    style DR fill:#ffebee,stroke:#b71c1c
    style NL fill:#ffebee,stroke:#b71c1c
    style REP fill:#ffebee,stroke:#b71c1c
```

### 4.4 Linear vs. Non-Linear Calibration Curves (Conceptual Plot)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph IDEAL_LINE[Ideal Linear Response]
        A1[0,0] --> A2[x_FS, y_FS]
    end

    subgraph ACTUAL_CURVE[Actual Response]
        B1[0,0] --> B2[Slight upward bow]
        B2 --> B3[Passes through x_FS, y_FS]
    end

    ACTUAL_CURVE -.->|max deviation Delta y_NL at midpoint| IDEAL_LINE

    style A1 fill:#bbdefb
    style A2 fill:#bbdefb
    style B1 fill:#ffe0b2
    style B2 fill:#ffe0b2
    style B3 fill:#ffe0b2
    style IDEAL_LINE fill:#e3f2fd,stroke:#0d47a1
    style ACTUAL_CURVE fill:#fff3e0,stroke:#e65100
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation:** Non-linearity is **always** quoted as a *deviation from a straight line*, not from a curve. A common student mistake is to say "the curve is non-linear" without specifying *with respect to which ideal line* — independent, zero-based, or terminal. The KTU examiner deducts marks for this omission.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (2-Mark / 3-Mark Format)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question]**
*Define the following terms with one example each:*
*(i) Static Sensitivity*
*(ii) Resolution of a measuring instrument*

**Model Answer (3 marks — KTU Key Pattern):**

*(i) **Static Sensitivity:** It is defined as the ratio of the change in output (response) of the instrument to the corresponding change in the input (measurand), measured under steady-state (static) conditions.
Mathematically:* $S = \dfrac{\Delta y}{\Delta x}$ *or* $\dfrac{dy}{dx}$.
*Example: A thermocouple with a sensitivity of 40 µV/°C produces an EMF of 40 µV for every 1 °C rise in temperature.*

*(ii) **Resolution:** It is the smallest change in the input (measurand) that produces a detectable change in the output (indication) of the instrument. It is also called *discrimination* and is governed by the least count of the display.
*Example: A digital thermometer that displays temperature to one decimal place has a resolution of 0.1 °C.*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Question]**
*Differentiate between **Accuracy** and **Precision** in a measurement system. Give an example of an instrument that is precise but not accurate.**

**Model Answer (3 marks — KTU Key Pattern):**

| **Basis** | **Accuracy** | **Precision** |
|---|---|---|
| Definition | Closeness of indicated value to the *true* value | Closeness of two or more indications to *each other* under identical conditions |
| Affected by | Calibration error, systematic error | Random errors, repeatability |
| Quantitative | % of True Value or % of FS | Standard deviation of repeated readings |
| Example | A thermometer reads 99.8 °C when true is 100 °C → accurate | A mis-calibrated thermometer reads 101.2 °C every time → precise but not accurate |

**Example of precise but not accurate:** A digital stop-watch that has been set 0.5 s fast will display the same wrong time consistently — it is **highly precise** (reproducible) but **not accurate** (systematic offset of 0.5 s).

---

### 5.2 Part B — ESE Module Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice Pattern)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Pattern (As per Academic Regulations):** Each Part-B question in the End Semester Examination (ESE) carries **14 marks** and typically has sub-parts **(a) for 7 marks** and **(b) for 7 marks**. You are offered an **internal choice** — you must answer EITHER the full OR pair of sub-questions. Below, two alternative questions (A and B) are given.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**Q.A. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 / Adapted KTU Pattern]**

**(a)** Define the term *static characteristics* of a measurement system. Explain any **five** ideal static characteristics in detail, with suitable examples and units. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A pressure transducer is used to measure pressures in the range **0 – 10 bar**. Its output voltage ranges from **0 V to 5 V**. The following calibration data are obtained:

| Applied Pressure (bar) | Output (V) — Increasing | Output (V) — Decreasing |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| 0 | 0.05 | 0.10 |
| 2 | 1.10 | 1.18 |
| 4 | 2.15 | 2.22 |
| 6 | 3.18 | 3.25 |
| 8 | 4.20 | 4.28 |
| 10 | 5.05 | 5.05 |

Calculate the **(i)** static sensitivity, **(ii)** zero-based non-linearity as % FS, **(iii)** maximum hysteresis as % FS, and **(iv)** worst-case static error as % FS. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution to Q.A(b):**

**(i) Static Sensitivity:**
Using the end-point (zero-based) approximation:

$$
S = \frac{y_{FS} - y_0}{x_{FS} - x_0} = \frac{5.05 - 0.05}{10 - 0} = \frac{5.00}{10} = 0.50 \;\text{V/bar}
$$

**(ii) Zero-Based Non-Linearity:**

First compute ideal output at each pressure (zero-based line $y_{ideal} = 0.05 + 0.50x$):

| $x$ (bar) | $y_{ideal}$ (V) | $y_{actual,\uparrow}$ (V) | $\Delta y = y_{act} - y_{ideal}$ |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| 0 | 0.05 | 0.05 | 0.00 |
| 2 | 1.05 | 1.10 | +0.05 |
| 4 | 2.05 | 2.15 | +0.10 |
| 6 | 3.05 | 3.18 | +0.13 |
| 8 | 4.05 | 4.20 | +0.15 |
| 10 | 5.05 | 5.05 | 0.00 |

$\vert \Delta y \vert_{\max} = 0.15\text{ V}$

$$
\%NL_{\text{zero}} = \frac{0.15}{5.00} \times 100 = 3.0\,\%\,\text{FS}
$$

**(iii) Maximum Hysteresis:**

| $x$ (bar) | $y_{\uparrow}$ (V) | $y_{\downarrow}$ (V) | $\vert y_{\uparrow} - y_{\downarrow}\vert$ |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| 0 | 0.05 | 0.10 | 0.05 |
| 2 | 1.10 | 1.18 | 0.08 |
| 4 | 2.15 | 2.22 | 0.07 |
| 6 | 3.18 | 3.25 | 0.07 |
| 8 | 4.20 | 4.28 | 0.08 |
| 10 | 5.05 | 5.05 | 0.00 |

$\vert y_{\uparrow} - y_{\downarrow}\vert_{\max} = 0.08\text{ V}$

$$
\%H = \frac{0.08}{5.00} \times 100 = 1.6\,\%\,\text{FS}
$$

**(iv) Worst-Case Static Error (additive bound):**

$$
\%e_{\text{worst}} = \%\text{offset}_{zero} + \%NL + \%H = \frac{0.05}{5.00}\times 100 + 3.0 + 1.6
$$

$$
= 1.0 + 3.0 + 1.6 = 5.6\,\%\,\text{FS}
$$

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**Q.B. [KTU University Exam – June 2023 / Adapted KTU Pattern]**

**(a)** Define and distinguish between **drift**, **hysteresis**, and **dead zone** in a measurement instrument. Explain how each of these affects the static accuracy of the system. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A digital voltmeter has **3½ digit** display and four ranges: **200 mV, 2 V, 20 V, 200 V**. Compute the resolution (in mV) for each range, and hence explain why the smallest range is preferred for high-accuracy low-voltage measurements. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution to Q.B(b):**

A 3½ digit display can show a maximum reading of **1999** (since the half-digit can only display 0 or 1).

The resolution (least count) for a range is:

$$
\text{Resolution} = \frac{\text{Full-Scale Range}}{1999}
$$

- **200 mV range:** $\text{Resolution} = \dfrac{200 \text{ mV}}{1999} \approx 0.1 \text{ mV} = 100\;\mu\text{V}$
- **2 V range:** $\text{Resolution} = \dfrac{2\text{ V}}{1999} \approx 1 \text{ mV} = 1000\;\mu\text{V}$
- **20 V range:** $\text{Resolution} = \dfrac{20 \text{ V}}{1999} \approx 10 \text{ mV}$
- **200 V range:** $\text{Resolution} = \dfrac{200 \text{ V}}{1999} \approx 100 \text{ mV} = 0.1 \text{ V}$

**Conclusion:** The 200 mV range offers the **finest resolution (100 µV)**, which is why it is preferred for low-voltage measurements (e.g., thermocouple outputs, sensor signals). Choosing a higher range (e.g., 20 V) for a 50 mV signal would yield a display reading of "00.0" with no useful digits — loss of *information* due to coarse resolution.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to use $y_{FS}$ (not $x_{FS}$) in the denominator** of the non-linearity/hysteresis formula. Marks deducted: **1–2 out of 7**.
> 2. **Confusing % FS with % of Reading.** When the problem says "as % of full scale," use $y_{FS}$; when it says "as % of indicated value," use the *true* value of the input.
> 3. **Not specifying which linearity method** is used (zero-based, end-point, or independent). Examiners expect you to state it explicitly.
> 4. **Assuming zero offset when not given** — always check whether the calibration data at $x = 0$ gives $y = 0$ or some non-zero offset.
> 5. **Mixing up repeatability and reproducibility** — *repeatability* = same observer/instrument/time; *reproducibility* = changed conditions.

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Last-Night Revision Sheet):**

- **Static characteristics describe steady-state behaviour** — input constant or slowly varying.
- The generic static model is $y = f(x)$ — *memoryless* and *time-invariant*.
- **Accuracy** = closeness to *true* value. **Precision** = closeness of repeated readings to *each other*.
- **Sensitivity** $S = \dfrac{\Delta y}{\Delta x}$ — slope of the calibration curve; unit = output/input.
- **Resolution** = smallest detectable input change = least count of the display.
- **Linearity** = straight-line calibration. **Non-linearity** % = $\dfrac{\vert \Delta y \vert_{\max}}{y_{FS}} \times 100$.
- **Hysteresis** = output depends on direction of input travel. Worst-case at the mid-range typically.
- **Dead Zone** = small input range where output is *zero* (below threshold).
- **Drift** = slow change in output with time at constant input. Two types: *zero drift* and *sensitivity drift*.
- **Static error** $e_s = y_{\text{ind}} - x_{\text{true}}$. Expressed as **% FS** or **% of Reading**.
- **Repeatability** < **Reproducibility** (the latter includes the former, plus more variation).
- For a linear instrument: $S = \dfrac{y_{FS}}{x_{FS}}$.
- For a 3½-digit DMM with $N = 1999$ max: $\text{Resolution} = \dfrac{\text{Range}}{1999}$.
- **Worst-case static error** is the *algebraic sum* of the magnitudes of all individual static errors.
- Always use the **%FS** denominator (i.e., $y_{FS}$) for non-linearity and hysteresis — not the input range.
- The Mermaid diagrams above are your **mental visual aid** for drawing block diagrams in the exam.
- For a multi-range analog voltmeter, the *per-volt sensitivity* ($1/I_{FSD}$) is **constant**; only the multiplier resistor changes with range.

> **End of Module 1 — Static Characteristics Study Notes (PEEET411, KTU 2024 Scheme)**

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Dynamic

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## Dynamic Response of Measurement Systems

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU Definition (Dynamic Characteristics):**
> The *dynamic characteristics* of a measurement system describe its behaviour when the input quantity applied to the system is varying rapidly with time. Unlike *static characteristics* (which deal with steady-state, time-invariant inputs), dynamic characteristics quantify **how fast, how accurately, and how stably** the instrument output follows a time-varying input.

In a practical engineering environment, measurands (temperature, pressure, displacement, voltage, current, strain, etc.) are **never truly constant**. They fluctuate due to process transients, noise, vibration, and load variations. An ideal instrument should reproduce these variations faithfully at its output, but real instruments possess **inertia, damping, and energy-storage elements** (masses, springs, capacitors, inductors, thermal masses), which cause the output to lag, overshoot, or distort the input.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "Dynamic" Matters in Electronic Instrumentation:**
> When measuring a fast-changing process variable (e.g., shaft vibration of a turbine, ECG signal, or transient over-voltage on a power line), the instrument's dynamic limitations — not its static accuracy — usually become the dominant source of measurement error. Hence, KTU examiners test this topic heavily as it combines theory with practical industrial relevance.

---

### Conceptual Analogy — The Spring–Mass–Damper Picture

Imagine you are driving a car with an **analog speedometer needle** and you suddenly press the accelerator. The needle does not jump instantly to the new speed; it rises, may slightly overshoot the true value, oscillate a couple of times, and finally settle. Why? Because the needle assembly has a **mass**, a **spring** pulling it back, and **air friction damping** its motion.

- The **mass** stores kinetic energy → causes the system to "overshoot" (inertia).
- The **spring** stores potential energy → pulls the needle back to the correct reading (restoring force).
- The **damper** dissipates energy → smooths the oscillations (friction).

This exact triplet — *mass, spring, damper* — mathematically models **first-order** and **second-order** instrument behaviour. The dynamic response is essentially the **time-domain and frequency-domain translation** of this physical model.

### Standard Dynamic Test Signals

To characterise an instrument dynamically, the input is forced to follow a *standard test signal* and the output is observed. The four classical test signals used in KTU-level instrumentation are:

| Test Signal | Mathematical Form $\mathbf{x(t)}$ | Physical Meaning |
|-------------|-------------------------------------|------------------|
| **Step input** | $x(t) = A \cdot u(t)$ | Sudden switch-on (e.g., a relay closing) |
| **Ramp input** | $x(t) = A \cdot t$ | Linearly increasing load |
| **Parabolic input** | $x(t) = \dfrac{A \cdot t^{2}}{2}$ | Constant acceleration |
| **Sinusoidal input** | $x(t) = A \sin(\omega t)$ | Steady AC vibration |

> [!TIP]
> The **step input** is the most common test signal in KTU examinations because it reveals the instrument's **rise time, settling time, overshoot, and steady-state error** in a single test.

### Dynamic Error

> [!DEFINITION]
> **Dynamic Error** is defined as the difference between the true value of the measurand (which is changing with time) and the value indicated by the instrument, **excluding any static calibration error**. Mathematically,
> $$\boxed{\,e_{d}(t) \;=\; x(t) \;-\; y(t)\,}$$
> where $x(t)$ is the time-varying true input and $y(t)$ is the indicated (output) value.

### Transfer Function — The Heart of Dynamic Analysis

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Transfer Function** $G(s)$ is defined as the **Laplace transform of the output divided by the Laplace transform of the input**, assuming **all initial conditions are zero**.
> $$\boxed{\,G(s) \;=\; \frac{Y(s)}{X(s)} \;=\; \frac{\mathcal{L}\{y(t)\}}{\mathcal{L}\{x(t)\}}\,}$$
> where $s = \sigma + j\omega$ is the **complex frequency variable**. The transfer function completely characterises the linear, time-invariant (LTI) dynamic behaviour of the instrument.

---

### Key Physical Quantities in Dynamic Analysis

- **Time constant** ($\tau$): measured in **seconds (s)**
- **Natural (undamped) frequency** ($\omega_{n}$): measured in **rad/s**
- **Damping ratio** ($\zeta$, zeta): **dimensionless** ($0 \le \zeta < \infty$)
- **Damped natural frequency** ($\omega_{d}$): **rad/s**
- **Bandwidth** ($BW$): **Hz** or **rad/s**
- **Settling time** ($t_{s}$): **seconds (s)**
- **Rise time** ($t_{r}$): **seconds (s)**
- **Peak overshoot** ($M_{p}$): **dimensionless** or **%**

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Instruments by Order

The dynamic order of an instrument is determined by the **highest order of the differential equation** that relates its output to its input. KTU syllabus emphasises the following three categories:

### (a) Zero-Order Instrument
The output is **directly proportional** to the input **at every instant of time**, with no energy storage.

$$y(t) = K \cdot x(t)$$

$$G(s) = K$$

- **Static sensitivity** = $K$
- **Dynamic error** = $\mathbf{0}$ for any input
- Examples: ideal potentiometer, ideal linear amplifier, ideal resistive shunt.
- No physical instrument is truly zero-order; only an **ideal** mathematical model.

### (b) First-Order Instrument
Contains **one energy-storage element**. The governing differential equation is:

$$a_{1} \frac{dy(t)}{dt} + y(t) = K \cdot x(t)$$

Dividing by $a_{1}$ and defining the **time constant** $\tau = a_{1}$:

$$\boxed{\,\tau \frac{dy(t)}{dt} + y(t) = K \cdot x(t)\,}$$

Transfer function:
$$G(s) = \frac{K}{\tau s + 1}$$

Examples: liquid-in-glass thermometer, simple RC low-pass filter, thermal system with lumped capacitance.

### (c) Second-Order Instrument
Contains **two independent energy-storage elements**. The governing ODE is:

$$a_{2} \frac{d^{2}y}{dt^{2}} + a_{1} \frac{dy}{dt} + y(t) = K \cdot x(t)$$

Dividing by $a_{2}$ and normalising:

$$\boxed{\,\frac{1}{\omega_{n}^{2}} \frac{d^{2}y}{dt^{2}} + \frac{2\zeta}{\omega_{n}} \frac{dy}{dt} + y(t) = K \cdot x(t)\,}$$

Transfer function:
$$G(s) = \frac{K}{\frac{s^{2}}{\omega_{n}^{2}} + \frac{2\zeta s}{\omega_{n}} + 1}$$

Examples: galvanometer with moving coil, accelerometer, LVDT with seismic mass, load cell, Bourdon tube pressure gauge.

---

## 2.2 Step Response of a First-Order Instrument

For a step input $x(t) = A \cdot u(t)$, the output is:

$$y(t) = K A \bigl(1 - e^{-t/\tau}\bigr)$$

| Property | Value |
|----------|-------|
| Initial value $y(0)$ | $0$ |
| Final (steady-state) value $y(\infty)$ | $KA$ |
| Value at $t = \tau$ | $0.632 \, KA$ |
| Value at $t = 2\tau$ | $0.865 \, KA$ |
| Value at $t = 4\tau$ | $0.982 \, KA$ |
| Value at $t = 5\tau$ | $0.993 \, KA$ (practical settling) |
| Time to reach 98% of final value | $\approx 4\tau$ |

> [!NOTE]
> A first-order system **never overshoots** the final value. It approaches the steady state **asymptotically** and is therefore called a *non-oscillatory* or *overdamped-relative* response.

---

## 2.3 Step Response of a Second-Order Instrument

The step response depends entirely on the damping ratio $\zeta$:

| Case | Condition | Nature of Response |
|------|-----------|---------------------|
| Undamped | $\zeta = 0$ | Sustained oscillations at $\omega_{n}$ |
| Under-damped | $0 < \zeta < 1$ | Damped oscillations, overshoot present |
| Critically damped | $\zeta = 1$ | Fastest non-oscillatory response |
| Over-damped | $\zeta > 1$ | Slow, sluggish, no overshoot |

The under-damped step response is:

$$y(t) = K A \left[ 1 - \frac{e^{-\zeta \omega_{n} t}}{\sqrt{1-\zeta^{2}}} \sin\!\left( \omega_{d} t + \phi \right) \right]$$

where the **damped natural frequency** and **phase angle** are:

$$\omega_{d} = \omega_{n} \sqrt{1-\zeta^{2}} \quad , \quad \phi = \cos^{-1}(\zeta)$$

### Key Time-Domain Specifications (Second-Order, Under-damped)

$$\boxed{\,\text{Peak Overshoot } M_{p} = e^{-\zeta\pi / \sqrt{1-\zeta^{2}}}\,}$$

$$\boxed{\,\text{Rise Time } t_{r} \approx \frac{\pi - \phi}{\omega_{d}} \approx \frac{1.8}{\omega_{n}}\,}$$

$$\boxed{\,\text{Settling Time } t_{s} \approx \frac{4}{\zeta \omega_{n}} \quad (\pm 2\% \text{ criterion})\,}$$

$$\boxed{\,\text{Peak Time } t_{p} = \frac{\pi}{\omega_{d}} = \frac{\pi}{\omega_{n}\sqrt{1-\zeta^{2}}}\,}$$

---

## 2.4 Frequency Response

Replacing $s = j\omega$ in the transfer function yields the **sinusoidal steady-state transfer function** $G(j\omega)$, whose magnitude and phase are functions of $\omega$.

### First-Order Instrument Frequency Response

$$\vert G(j\omega) \vert = \frac{K}{\sqrt{1 + (\omega \tau)^{2}}} \quad , \quad \angle G(j\omega) = -\tan^{-1}(\omega \tau)$$

At $\omega = 1/\tau$, the magnitude drops to $K/\sqrt{2}$ — this defines the **bandwidth** for a first-order system.

### Second-Order Instrument Frequency Response

$$\vert G(j\omega) \vert = \frac{K}{\sqrt{\left( 1 - \dfrac{\omega^{2}}{\omega_{n}^{2}} \right)^{2} + \left( \dfrac{2\zeta \omega}{\omega_{n}} \right)^{2}}}$$

- **Resonant peak frequency** (only when $\zeta < 1/\sqrt{2}$):
$$\omega_{r} = \omega_{n} \sqrt{1 - 2\zeta^{2}}$$
- **Maximum magnitude** (resonant peak):
$$M_{r} = \frac{K}{2\zeta \sqrt{1-\zeta^{2}}}$$
- **Bandwidth** (frequency where magnitude drops to $K/\sqrt{2}$): solved from $\vert G(j\omega) \vert^{2} = K^{2}/2$.

---

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet — Single-Page Revision Table

| # | Concept | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|---------|---------|---------------|
| 1 | Zero-order transfer function | $G(s) = K$ | $K$ = static sensitivity |
| 2 | First-order transfer function | $G(s) = \dfrac{K}{\tau s + 1}$ | $\tau$ in **s** |
| 3 | Second-order transfer function | $G(s) = \dfrac{K}{\dfrac{s^{2}}{\omega_{n}^{2}} + \dfrac{2\zeta s}{\omega_{n}} + 1}$ | $\omega_{n}$ in **rad/s**, $\zeta$ dimensionless |
| 4 | First-order step response | $y(t) = KA\bigl(1 - e^{-t/\tau}\bigr)$ | Asymptotic |
| 5 | First-order rise time (0–63.2%) | $t = \tau$ | **s** |
| 6 | First-order settling time (2%) | $t_{s} \approx 4\tau$ | **s** |
| 7 | Second-order peak overshoot | $M_{p} = \exp\!\bigl(-\pi\zeta / \sqrt{1-\zeta^{2}}\bigr)$ | Fraction, multiply by 100 for % |
| 8 | Second-order rise time | $t_{r} \approx 1.8 / \omega_{n}$ | **s** |
| 9 | Second-order settling time (2%) | $t_{s} \approx 4 / (\zeta \omega_{n})$ | **s** |
| 10 | Second-order peak time | $t_{p} = \pi / \bigl(\omega_{n}\sqrt{1-\zeta^{2}}\bigr)$ | **s** |
| 11 | Damped natural frequency | $\omega_{d} = \omega_{n} \sqrt{1 - \zeta^{2}}$ | **rad/s** |
| 12 | Resonant peak frequency | $\omega_{r} = \omega_{n} \sqrt{1 - 2\zeta^{2}}$ | **rad/s** (valid if $\zeta < 1/\sqrt{2}$) |
| 13 | Magnitude of resonant peak | $M_{r} = K \big/ \bigl(2\zeta\sqrt{1-\zeta^{2}}\bigr)$ | dimensionless |
| 14 | Dynamic error | $e_{d}(t) = x(t) - y(t)$ | — |
| 15 | Phase angle (under-damped) | $\phi = \cos^{-1}(\zeta)$ | **radians** |

> [!TIP]
> **Examination Tip (KTU 2024):** When asked *"What is the time constant of the thermometer if it reaches 63.2% of final reading in 8 s?"*, the answer is $\tau = 8$ s, **not** 8/0.632. The 63.2% rule is a *signature* of first-order systems.

### Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Process control loop tuning:** Choosing $\zeta \approx 0.7$ gives the best compromise between speed and overshoot for industrial PID loops.
- **Seismograph design:** $\zeta \approx 1$ (critical damping) prevents spurious oscillations during earthquake recording.
- **Oscilloscope probe compensation:** The probe's adjustable capacitor tunes its first-order time constant to match the oscilloscope input, achieving a flat (ideal) frequency response.
- **Crash-test accelerometers:** Require very high $\omega_{n}$ to faithfully track millisecond-scale transients.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Step Response of a First-Order Instrument

**Given:** First-order governing equation and a step input.

**Step 1 — Write the governing differential equation.**

The general first-order instrument obeys:
$$\tau \frac{dy(t)}{dt} + y(t) = K \cdot x(t)$$

**Step 2 — Apply the Laplace transform (zero initial condition $y(0)=0$).**

Using $\mathcal{L}\{dy/dt\} = sY(s) - y(0) = sY(s)$:

$$\tau \, s \, Y(s) + Y(s) = K \, X(s)$$

**Step 3 — Factor out $Y(s)$ to obtain the transfer function.**

$$Y(s) \, (\tau s + 1) = K \, X(s)$$

$$G(s) = \frac{Y(s)}{X(s)} = \frac{K}{\tau s + 1}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute the step input $x(t) = A \cdot u(t)$ whose Laplace transform is $X(s) = A/s$.**

$$Y(s) = \frac{K}{\tau s + 1} \cdot \frac{A}{s} = \frac{KA}{s(\tau s + 1)}$$

**Step 5 — Perform partial-fraction expansion.**

$$\frac{KA}{s(\tau s + 1)} = \frac{KA}{s} - \frac{KA \cdot \tau}{\tau s + 1} = \frac{KA}{s} - \frac{KA}{\tau s + 1}$$

(We split $\dfrac{1}{s(\tau s + 1)} = \dfrac{A_{1}}{s} + \dfrac{A_{2}}{\tau s + 1}$ and solve: $A_{1} = 1$, $A_{2} = -\tau$.)

Rewriting the second term to match the standard Laplace form:
$$\frac{KA}{\tau s + 1} = \frac{KA/\tau}{s + 1/\tau}$$

**Step 6 — Take the inverse Laplace transform.**

$$y(t) = KA \cdot \mathcal{L}^{-1}\!\left\{\frac{1}{s}\right\} - \frac{KA}{\tau} \cdot \mathcal{L}^{-1}\!\left\{\frac{1}{s + 1/\tau}\right\}$$

$$\boxed{\,y(t) = KA \Bigl( 1 - e^{-t/\tau} \Bigr)\,}$$

**Step 7 — Verify limiting cases.**

- At $t = 0$: $y(0) = KA(1 - e^{0}) = 0$ ✓ (output starts at zero)
- At $t \to \infty$: $y(\infty) = KA(1 - e^{-\infty}) = KA$ ✓ (output reaches static sensitivity $K$ times step magnitude)

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Second-Order Step Response (Under-damped)

**Given:** Standard second-order ODE with $0 < \zeta < 1$ and a step input $x(t) = A u(t)$.

**Step 1 — Governing ODE and transfer function.**

$$\frac{1}{\omega_{n}^{2}} \frac{d^{2}y}{dt^{2}} + \frac{2\zeta}{\omega_{n}} \frac{dy}{dt} + y = K A$$

$$Y(s) = \frac{KA}{s \left( \dfrac{s^{2}}{\omega_{n}^{2}} + \dfrac{2\zeta s}{\omega_{n}} + 1 \right)}$$

**Step 2 — Factor the denominator polynomial.** The characteristic equation:

$$s^{2} + 2\zeta\omega_{n} s + \omega_{n}^{2} = 0$$

By the quadratic formula:

$$s_{1,2} = -\zeta \omega_{n} \pm j \omega_{n} \sqrt{1 - \zeta^{2}} = -\zeta\omega_{n} \pm j\omega_{d}$$

where $\omega_{d} = \omega_{n}\sqrt{1-\zeta^{2}}$.

**Step 3 — Partial-fraction expansion** (three simple poles: $s = 0$, $s = s_{1}$, $s = s_{2}$).

$$Y(s) = \frac{KA}{s(s - s_{1})(s - s_{2})}$$

The standard inverse-Laplace result is:

$$y(t) = KA \left[ 1 - \frac{e^{-\zeta \omega_{n} t}}{\sqrt{1-\zeta^{2}}} \sin\!\left( \omega_{d} t + \phi \right) \right]$$

where the phase angle satisfies $\cos\phi = \zeta$, $\sin\phi = \sqrt{1-\zeta^{2}}$, so:

$$\phi = \tan^{-1}\!\left( \frac{\sqrt{1-\zeta^{2}}}{\zeta} \right) = \cos^{-1}(\zeta)$$

**Step 4 — Derive peak overshoot $M_{p}$.**

Peak occurs when $\dfrac{dy}{dt} = 0$. Differentiating and setting the sine term to zero (non-trivial root) gives:

$$\omega_{d} t_{p} = \pi \quad \Rightarrow \quad t_{p} = \frac{\pi}{\omega_{d}} = \frac{\pi}{\omega_{n}\sqrt{1-\zeta^{2}}}$$

Substituting $t = t_{p}$ into the response:

$$y(t_{p}) = KA \left[ 1 - \frac{e^{-\zeta\omega_{n} t_{p}}}{\sqrt{1-\zeta^{2}}} \sin(\pi + \phi) \right]$$

Since $\sin(\pi + \phi) = -\sin\phi = -\sqrt{1-\zeta^{2}}$:

$$y(t_{p}) = KA \left[ 1 + e^{-\zeta\omega_{n} t_{p}} \right]$$

Therefore the peak overshoot (fraction above steady state) is:

$$M_{p} = \frac{y(t_{p}) - KA}{KA} = e^{-\zeta\omega_{n} t_{p}} = e^{-\zeta\pi / \sqrt{1-\zeta^{2}}}$$

$$\boxed{\,M_{p} = \exp\!\left( -\frac{\pi \zeta}{\sqrt{1-\zeta^{2}}} \right)\,}$$

**Step 5 — Useful numerical shortcuts.**

- For $\zeta = 0.5$: $M_{p} = e^{-\pi(0.5)/\sqrt{0.75}} = e^{-1.8138} \approx 0.163 = 16.3\%$
- For $\zeta = 0.707$ (the famous "optimal" value): $M_{p} \approx 4.3\%$
- For $\zeta = 1.0$: $M_{p} = 0$ (no overshoot, critically damped)

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: First-Order Sinusoidal Response & Bandwidth

**Given:** $G(s) = \dfrac{K}{\tau s + 1}$ and $x(t) = A \sin(\omega t)$.

**Step 1 — Substitute $s = j\omega$ for steady-state sinusoid.**

$$G(j\omega) = \frac{K}{1 + j\omega\tau}$$

**Step 2 — Compute magnitude and phase.**

$$\vert G(j\omega) \vert = \frac{K}{\sqrt{1 + (\omega\tau)^{2}}} \quad , \quad \angle G(j\omega) = -\tan^{-1}(\omega\tau)$$

**Step 3 — The output is a sinusoid of the *same frequency* but attenuated and phase-lagged.**

$$y(t) = \frac{KA}{\sqrt{1 + (\omega\tau)^{2}}} \sin\!\left( \omega t - \tan^{-1}(\omega\tau) \right)$$

**Step 4 — Bandwidth definition** ($|G| = K/\sqrt{2}$, i.e. $-3$ dB point):

$$\frac{K}{\sqrt{1 + (\omega_{BW}\tau)^{2}}} = \frac{K}{\sqrt{2}} \;\Rightarrow\; (\omega_{BW}\tau)^{2} = 1 \;\Rightarrow\; \omega_{BW} = \frac{1}{\tau}$$

$$\boxed{\,f_{BW} = \frac{1}{2\pi\tau}\,}$$

The instrument can faithfully track signals only up to frequency $1/\tau$ rad/s.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — First-Order vs Second-Order

| Feature | First-Order | Second-Order |
|---|---|---|
| Number of energy-storage elements | **1** | **2** |
| Governing ODE | $\tau \dot{y} + y = Kx$ | $\dfrac{1}{\omega_{n}^{2}} \ddot{y} + \dfrac{2\zeta}{\omega_{n}} \dot{y} + y = Kx$ |
| Transfer function | $K / (\tau s + 1)$ | $K / (s^{2}/\omega_{n}^{2} + 2\zeta s/\omega_{n} + 1)$ |
| Step response | Exponential, **no overshoot** | Oscillatory if $\zeta < 1$ |
| Settling time (2%) | $4\tau$ | $4 / (\zeta\omega_{n})$ |
| Output shape | Always monotonic | Sinusoidal envelope |
| Frequency response | Single break at $1/\tau$ | Resonance peak if $\zeta < 1/\sqrt{2}$ |
| Phase at high $\omega$ | $-90^{\circ}$ | $-180^{\circ}$ |
| Pole locations | One real pole at $s = -1/\tau$ | Two complex poles at $s = -\zeta\omega_{n} \pm j\omega_{d}$ |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of an Instrument under Dynamic Input

The following Mermaid block-diagram depicts how a time-varying measurand $x(t)$ is processed by the instrument, leading to the indicated output $y(t)$, and how the dynamic error $e_d(t)$ is generated.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    srcA[Time-Varying Measurand x of t] --> ftrA[Sensor Element Energy Storage]
    ftrA --> ftrB[Signal Conditioner Amplifier Filter]
    ftrB --> ftrC[Output Stage Display Recorder]
    ftrC --> outA[Indicated Output y of t]
    srcA -.-> errComp[Reference Comparator]
    outA -.-> errComp
    errComp --> errA[Dynamic Error e sub d of t]
    style srcA fill:#E8F4FF,stroke:#2B6CB0,color:#000
    style ftrA fill:#FFF4E0,stroke:#B7791F,color:#000
    style ftrB fill:#FFF4E0,stroke:#B7791F,color:#000
    style ftrC fill:#FFF4E0,stroke:#B7791F,color:#000
    style outA fill:#E6FFEA,stroke:#2F855A,color:#000
    style errComp fill:#FFE5E5,stroke:#C53030,color:#000
    style errA fill:#FFE5E5,stroke:#C53030,color:#000
```

### Interpretation of the Block Diagram

- The **Time-Varying Measurand** $x(t)$ enters the sensor.
- The **Sensor** introduces energy storage (e.g., thermal mass, seismic mass, capacitance) — this is the *physical origin* of dynamic lag.
- **Signal conditioner** (amplifier, filter) further modifies the dynamic response.
- The **output stage** drives a display or recorder.
- The **comparator** continuously subtracts $y(t)$ from $x(t)$ to give the dynamic error $e_d(t)$.

---

## 4.2 Decision Flow for Identifying the Order of an Instrument

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    qStart[Instrument under test] --> qAsk{How many independent energy storage elements}
    qAsk -- Zero --> qZero[Zero Order Equation y equals K times x No dynamics]
    qAsk -- One --> qFirst[First Order Equation tau d y plus y equals K x]
    qAsk -- Two --> qSecond[Second Order Equation d2 y plus 2 zeta d y plus y equals K x]
    qZero --> outFinal[Choose G of s equals K]
    qFirst --> outSpec1[Choose G of s equals K over tau s plus 1]
    qSecond --> outSpec2[Choose G of s equals K over s squared over omega sub n squared plus 2 zeta s over omega sub n plus 1]
    style qStart fill:#E8F4FF,stroke:#2B6CB0,color:#000
    style qAsk fill:#FFF4E0,stroke:#B7791F,color:#000
    style qZero fill:#E6FFEA,stroke:#2F855A,color:#000
    style qFirst fill:#E6FFEA,stroke:#2F855A,color:#000
    style qSecond fill:#E6FFEA,stroke:#2F855A,color:#000
    style outFinal fill:#FFE5E5,stroke:#C53030,color:#000
    style outSpec1 fill:#FFE5E5,stroke:#C53030,color:#000
    style outSpec2 fill:#FFE5E5,stroke:#C53030,color:#000
```

---

## 4.3 Step-Response Waveform — Damping Comparison

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    pA[Step Input A u of t] --> sA[Response 1 zeta equals 0.0 Undamped Sustained Oscillations]
    pA --> sB[Response 2 zeta equals 0.3 Underdamped Damped Oscillations Overshoot Present]
    pA --> sC[Response 3 zeta equals 1.0 Critically Damped Fastest Non Oscillatory]
    pA --> sD[Response 4 zeta equals 2.0 Overdamped Slow Sluggish]
    sA --> noteA[Peak Overshoot Mp equals 1.0 equals 100 percent]
    sB --> noteB[Peak Overshoot Mp approx 37 percent]
    sC --> noteC[Peak Overshoot Mp equals 0 percent Optimal speed no overshoot]
    sD --> noteD[Peak Overshoot Mp equals 0 percent Slowest rise]
    style pA fill:#E8F4FF,stroke:#2B6CB0,color:#000
    style sA fill:#FFE5E5,stroke:#C53030,color:#000
    style sB fill:#FFF4E0,stroke:#B7791F,color:#000
    style sC fill:#E6FFEA,stroke:#2F855A,color:#000
    style sD fill:#F0E6FF,stroke:#6B46C1,color:#000
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Interpretation of the Waveform Diagram:**
> Reading the four branches left-to-right, you can see that as $\zeta$ increases from 0 to 2, the response transitions from **sustained oscillation** to **damped oscillation** to **critically-damped smoothness** to **sluggish delay**. The KTU examiner often asks to identify the *optimum* damping — and the answer is **$\zeta = 1$ for fastest non-oscillatory response**, or **$\zeta \approx 0.7$ for the best compromise between speed and overshoot** in control systems.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Define the term "dynamic error" of a measurement system. How does it differ from static error?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Dynamic error is defined as the **difference between the true instantaneous value of the time-varying input $x(t)$ and the value indicated by the instrument $y(t)$** at the same instant of time, *excluding* any static calibration error:

$$e_{d}(t) = x(t) - y(t)$$

It is called "dynamic" because it arises **only when the input varies with time**. In contrast, **static error** is the error observed when the input is *constant* (or changes so slowly that the instrument reaches equilibrium). Static error is a *steady-state* concept; dynamic error is a *transient* concept, depending on the rate of change of input, the instrument's time constant / natural frequency, and damping ratio.

**(1 mark) Definition + (1 mark) formula + (1 mark) clear difference from static error.**

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**For a first-order instrument, derive the expression for the time constant and explain its physical significance.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The governing equation of a first-order instrument is:

$$\tau \frac{dy(t)}{dt} + y(t) = K \cdot x(t)$$

where $\tau$ is the **time constant** (units: seconds). For a step input of magnitude $A$:

$$y(t) = KA \bigl(1 - e^{-t/\tau}\bigr)$$

At $t = \tau$, the output reaches $y(\tau) = 0.632 \, KA$, i.e. **63.2 %** of its final value.

**Physical significance:** The time constant is a measure of the **speed of response** of the instrument.
- **Small $\tau$** → fast response → instrument can track rapid changes.
- **Large $\tau$** → slow response → instrument lags significantly behind the input.

It also defines the **bandwidth**: $f_{BW} = 1 / (2\pi\tau)$.

**(1 mark) formula + (1 mark) 63.2 % interpretation + (1 mark) bandwidth / physical meaning.**

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (Module Internal Choice, 14 Marks)

---

### Question A — 14 Marks  **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**(a)** With a neat labelled block diagram, explain the **generalised measurement system** and identify where the *dynamic lag* originates. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For a second-order underdamped measurement system, derive expressions for **peak overshoot, peak time, and settling time** in terms of $\omega_{n}$ and $\zeta$. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Generalised Measurement System & Origin of Dynamic Lag (7 marks):**

A measurement system consists of three primary stages:

1. **Sensor / Primary sensing element** — converts the measurand (temperature, pressure, displacement, etc.) into a more usable form (electrical, mechanical).
2. **Variable-conversion element / Signal conditioner** — amplifies, filters, and linearises the signal.
3. **Data-transmission element / Output stage** — displays, records, or transmits the reading.

**Origin of dynamic lag:** The dynamic lag originates **in the primary sensing element** because it inevitably contains *energy-storage elements* (thermal mass $C$, mass $m$ on a spring $k$, capacitor $C$ in a circuit). These storage elements resist instantaneous change. The secondary stage also contributes lag if it contains $RC$ or $RL$ networks.

```
   Measurand x(t)  →  [Sensor 1 energy storage]  →  [Signal Conditioner]  →  [Display]
                          │
                          └── dynamic lag arises here
```

If the sensor is **thermal** (e.g., thermometer bulb), its heat capacity $C$ and convective resistance $R$ form a first-order system with time constant $\tau = RC$.

If the sensor is **mechanical** (e.g., seismic-mass accelerometer), the mass $m$, spring $k$, and damper $c$ form a second-order system with $\omega_{n} = \sqrt{k/m}$ and $\zeta = c / (2\sqrt{km})$.

**(1 mark) diagram + 1 mark stage identification + 2 marks thermal example + 2 marks mechanical example + 1 mark conclusion.**

**(b) Derivation of Second-Order Time-Domain Specifications (7 marks):**

Refer to **Derivation 2 in Section 3.2** of these notes for the complete algebra. Summary:

- **Peak time:** $t_{p} = \dfrac{\pi}{\omega_{n}\sqrt{1-\zeta^{2}}}$ ... **(2 marks)**
- **Peak overshoot:** $M_{p} = e^{-\pi\zeta / \sqrt{1-\zeta^{2}}}$ ... **(3 marks)**
- **Settling time (2% criterion):** $t_{s} = \dfrac{4}{\zeta\omega_{n}}$ ... **(2 marks)**

Each formula must be derived (not merely stated) starting from the under-damped step response, as shown in the full derivation.

---

### Question B — 14 Marks  **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

**(a)** A thermometer is modelled as a first-order system with time constant $\tau = 12$ s and static sensitivity $K = 0.8$ mV/$^{\circ}$C. It is suddenly plunged from $0^{\circ}$C into a bath at $100^{\circ}$C. **(i)** Write the equation of the output voltage. **(ii)** Find the voltage at $t = 6$ s, $24$ s, and $60$ s. **(iii)** What is the steady-state error? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With a suitable block diagram, derive the **frequency-response expressions** (magnitude and phase) for a first-order instrument. Define **bandwidth** and explain its engineering significance. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Thermometer Problem (7 marks):**

**(i) Output equation** — The thermometer's transfer function is $G(s) = \dfrac{K}{\tau s + 1} = \dfrac{0.8}{12s + 1}$.

Input is a step of magnitude $A = 100$ °C, so $X(s) = 100/s$.

$$Y(s) = \frac{0.8}{12s+1} \cdot \frac{100}{s} = \frac{80}{s(12s+1)}$$

Performing partial fractions (as shown in Section 3.1):

$$Y(s) = \frac{80}{s} - \frac{80 \cdot 12}{12s+1} \cdot \frac{1}{12} \cdot 12 = \frac{80}{s} - \frac{80}{12}\cdot\frac{12}{12s+1}$$

Inverse Laplace:

$$y(t) = 80 \bigl(1 - e^{-t/12}\bigr) \;\text{mV} \quad \text{for } t \ge 0$$

**(2 marks for the equation)**

**(ii) Voltage at specific instants:**

- $t = 6$ s: $y(6) = 80(1 - e^{-0.5}) = 80(1 - 0.6065) = 80(0.3935) = 31.48$ **mV** ... **(1 mark)**
- $t = 24$ s (= $2\tau$): $y(24) = 80(1 - e^{-2}) = 80(1 - 0.1353) = 80(0.8647) = 69.18$ **mV** ... **(1 mark)**
- $t = 60$ s (= $5\tau$): $y(60) = 80(1 - e^{-5}) = 80(1 - 0.00674) = 80(0.99326) = 79.46$ **mV** ... **(1 mark)**

**(iii) Steady-state error** — The true temperature is 100 °C, corresponding to $100 \times 0.8 = 80$ mV ideal. Final reading is 80 mV, hence:

$$e_{ss} = 80 - 80 = 0 \text{ mV}$$

A first-order system has **zero steady-state error for a step input** because its pole is at $s = -1/\tau$ (no pole at origin). **(2 marks)**

---

**(b) Frequency Response of First-Order Instrument (7 marks):**

Replace $s = j\omega$ in the transfer function:

$$G(j\omega) = \frac{K}{1 + j\omega\tau}$$

Magnitude and phase:

$$\vert G(j\omega) \vert = \frac{K}{\sqrt{1 + (\omega\tau)^{2}}} \quad , \quad \angle G(j\omega) = -\tan^{-1}(\omega\tau)$$

**Block diagram:**

```
        x(t)  →  [ G(s) = K / (τs+1) ]  →  y(t)
                       │
                       └── s = jω  →  |G(jω)| ∠φ(ω)
```

At $\omega = 0$: $\vert G \vert = K$, phase $= 0^{\circ}$.
At $\omega = 1/\tau$ (the corner / break frequency): $\vert G \vert = K/\sqrt{2}$ ($-3$ dB), phase $= -45^{\circ}$.
At $\omega \to \infty$: $\vert G \vert \to 0$, phase $\to -90^{\circ}$.

**Bandwidth definition:** The bandwidth $f_{BW}$ is the frequency at which the magnitude of the frequency-response function drops to $1/\sqrt{2}$ of its DC value (i.e. **$-3$ dB point**). For a first-order system:

$$f_{BW} = \frac{1}{2\pi\tau}$$

**Engineering significance:** It defines the *highest frequency* of input that the instrument can measure faithfully (within 3 dB). For accurate measurement, the input signal's significant frequency components must lie well *below* $f_{BW}$. Example: a thermocouple with $\tau = 5$ s has $f_{BW} \approx 0.032$ Hz — it cannot follow rapid thermal fluctuations.

**(1 mark) substitute $s=j\omega$ + 2 marks magnitude and phase + 1 mark corner frequency + 1 mark bandwidth formula + 2 marks significance.**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
>
> 1. **Confusing static and dynamic error.** Students often write "dynamic error is the difference between indicated value and true value at steady state." This is *static* error. Dynamic error explicitly requires a **time-varying** input.
> 2. **Using $M_{p}$ in percent without converting.** If a question states $M_{p} = 16.3\%$, the value plugged into the formula $e^{-\pi\zeta/\sqrt{1-\zeta^{2}}}$ is **0.163**, not 16.3.
> 3. **Forgetting the under-damped constraint.** The formulas for $t_{p}$, $t_{r}$, $M_{p}$ apply **only** when $0 \le \zeta < 1$. If the question gives $\zeta \ge 1$, you must use the *critically/over-damped* response — no overshoot exists.
> 4. **Missing units.** KTU examiners deduct marks if $\tau$ is given as "8" instead of "8 s" or if $\omega_{n}$ is in Hz instead of rad/s. Always carry units.
> 5. **Bandwidth convention.** First-order $f_{BW} = 1/(2\pi\tau)$ is the **$-3$ dB frequency**. Second-order bandwidth requires solving a quadratic and is *not* simply $\omega_{n}/(2\pi)$.

---

## TOPIC RECAP — Rapid Revision Checklist

- [x] **Static characteristics** deal with constant inputs; **dynamic characteristics** deal with time-varying inputs.
- [x] The four **standard test signals** are step, ramp, parabolic, and sinusoidal.
- [x] **Zero-order** instrument: $G(s) = K$. No dynamics, no error.
- [x] **First-order** instrument: $G(s) = K / (\tau s + 1)$. Step response: $y(t) = KA(1 - e^{-t/\tau})$.
- [x] **Second-order** instrument: $G(s) = K / (s^{2}/\omega_{n}^{2} + 2\zeta s/\omega_{n} + 1)$. Behaviour depends entirely on $\zeta$.
- [x] **Time constant** $\tau$ = time to reach 63.2% of final value (first-order).
- [x] **Damping ratio** $\zeta$: 0 (undamped) → 1 (critical) → ∞ (overdamped).
- [x] **Peak overshoot** $M_{p} = \exp(-\pi\zeta/\sqrt{1-\zeta^{2}})$ — only for under-damped second-order.
- [x] **Settling time** $t_{s} \approx 4/(\zeta\omega_{n})$ for 2% band.
- [x] **Bandwidth** of first-order system: $f_{BW} = 1/(2\pi\tau)$.
- [x] **Resonant peak** appears in second-order only when $\zeta < 1/\sqrt{2}$.
- [x] **Dynamic error** $e_{d}(t) = x(t) - y(t)$ is the *transient* discrepancy.
- [x] Always quote $\omega_{n}$ in **rad/s** and $f_{BW}$ in **Hz**; never mix them in calculations.
- [x] Practical settling: $t \approx 4\tau$ for first-order; $t \approx 4/(\zeta\omega_{n})$ for second-order.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Field
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEEET411 - ELECTRONIC INSTRUMENTATION > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Field ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Definition of "Field" in Electronic Instrumentation In the context of **Electronic Instrumentation**, the term **"Field"** refers to the broad **domain or branch of physical quantities** that an instrument is designed to sense, transduce, and quantify. Every measurable physical phenomenon in nature exists as a *field* — a region in space where a physical quantity has a defined value at every point. Common fields encountered in instrumentation include **magnetic field (B)**, **electric field (E)**, **temperature field (T)**, **pressure field (P)**, **strain field (ε)**, **flow field (Q)**, **light/illuminance field (Eᵥ)**, and **chemical/concentration field (C)**. > [!NOTE] > **Syllabus Definition (KTU 2024 Scheme – PEEET411 Module 1):** > A *field* in instrumentation is the physical domain containing a measurable quantity whose variation (in space or time) carries information. The instrument converts this field variation into a standardized electrical signal (voltage/current) suitable for display, recording, or control. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Instrumentation as the "Senses" of a Machine Imagine a human body without its senses — blind, deaf, numb, and unaware of the surroundings. Such a body cannot react, learn, or survive. **Electronic Instrumentation is exactly the "nervous system" of any modern engineering plant or device.** | Human Sense | Instrumentation Equivalent | Field Measured | |---|---|---| | Touch (skin) | Strain gauge, Load cell | Mechanical Field (Force, Strain) | | Temperature perception | Thermocouple, RTD, Thermistor | Thermal Field | | Vision (eyes) | LDR, Photodiode, Camera | Optical/Radiant Field | | Hearing (ears) | Microphone, Hydrophone | Acoustic Field | | Smell/Taste | pH sensor, Gas sensor | Chemical Field | | Balance (inner ear) | Accelerometer, Gyroscope | Inertial Field | Just as the human nervous system converts stimuli from these fields into nerve impulses (electrical signals), an electronic instrument converts field variations into standardized electrical outputs (typically **1–5 V** or **4–20 mA** in industry). > [!IMPORTANT] > **Core Insight:** All instrumentation — irrespective of the *field* it measures — ultimately reduces to the measurement of **ONE fundamental quantity: ELECTRICAL SIGNAL (Voltage or Current)**. This is the unifying principle of Electronic Instrumentation. ### 1.3 Standard Physical Constants in Instrumentation Fields The following constants are routinely used while quantifying fields: | Constant | Symbol | Value | Unit | Field of Use | |---|---|---|---|---| | Boltzmann Constant | $k$ | $1.380649 \times 10^{-23}$ | J/K | Thermal Noise | | Planck's Constant | $h$ | $6.62607015 \times 10^{-34}$ | J·s | Optical Field | | Speed of Light | $c$ | $2.99792458 \times 10^{8}$ | m/s | Optical Field | | Vacuum Permittivity | $\varepsilon_{0}$ | $8.8541878128 \times 10^{-12}$ | F/m | Electric Field | | Vacuum Permeability | $\mu_{0}$ | $4\pi \times 10^{-7}$ | H/m | Magnetic Field | | Stefan-Boltzmann | $\sigma$ | $5.670374419 \times 10^{-8}$ | W/(m²·K⁴) | Radiation Field | | Universal Gas | $R$ | $8.314462618$ | J/(mol·K) | Pressure/Thermal | | Standard Gravity | $g$ | $9.80665$ | m/s² | Mechanical Field | ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Classification of Instrumentation Fields Engineering fields of measurement are broadly classified into **six fundamental domains**, each governed by its own physical laws and sensor technologies: #### A. **Mechanical Field** Covers measurement of force, pressure, displacement, velocity, acceleration, torque, and flow. The governing laws are Newton's laws of motion and fluid mechanics (Bernoulli, Continuity). - *Primary Sensors:* Strain gauge, LVDT, Capacitive pickup, Piezoelectric crystal, Turbine flow meter, Orifice plate. #### B. **Thermal Field** Covers temperature, heat flux, and thermal conductivity. Governed by Zeroth, First, and Second Laws of Thermodynamics. - *Primary Sensors:* Thermocouple (Seebeck effect), RTD (resistance vs temperature), Thermistor (NTC/PTC), Bimetallic strip, Pyrometer (radiation). #### C. **Electrical & Magnetic Field** Covers voltage, current, resistance, capacitance, inductance, magnetic flux density, and electric field strength. Governed by Maxwell's equations and Ohm's law. - *Primary Sensors:* Shunt resistor, Hall effect sensor, Current transformer, Rogowski coil, Search coil, Fluxgate magnetometer. #### D. **Optical / Radiant Field** Covers illuminance, luminance, color, image, and electromagnetic radiation. Governed by quantum theory and wave optics. - *Primary Sensors:* Photodiode, Phototransistor, LDR (CdS cell), Pyrometer, CCD/CMOS array, Spectrometer. #### E. **Chemical / Analytical Field** Covers pH, humidity, gas concentration, dissolved oxygen, and chemical composition. Governed by electrochemistry, spectroscopy, and mass-action laws. - *Primary Sensors:* Glass electrode (pH), Electrochemical gas sensor, IR absorption sensor, Mass spectrometer. #### F. **Biological / Biomedical Field** Covers ECG, EEG, EMG, blood pressure, blood oxygen (SpO₂), and body temperature. Governed by biophysics and physiology. - *Primary Sensors:* Ag/AgCl electrodes, Pulse oximeter, Piezoelectric ultrasound transducer, Optical plethysmograph. ### 2.2 The Generalized Measurement Chain Every field-measuring instrument — irrespective of which physical quantity it targets — follows the **same universal signal chain**: $$ \text{Physical Field} \xrightarrow{\text{Sensor}} \text{Analog Electrical Signal} \xrightarrow{\text{Signal Conditioner}} \text{Standardized Output} \xrightarrow{\text{Display / Control}} $$ The **three universal stages** are: 1. **Sensing Element (Primary Transducer):** Detects the field variation and converts it to a raw electrical signal. *Example:* Thermocouple junction converts thermal field into millivolts. 2. **Variable Conversion Element (Secondary Transducer):** Refines the raw signal (e.g., converts resistance change to voltage via a Wheatstone bridge). 3. **Signal Conditioner + Output Stage:** Amplifies, filters, linearizes, and standardizes the signal for display (CRO, DMM) or transmission (4–20 mA loop). ### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Formula | Description | Units | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $y = a + bx$ | Ideal linear input-output (static calibration) relation | $y$ in output units, $x$ in input units | | 2 | $\text{Sensitivity } (S) = \dfrac{dy}{dx} \approx \dfrac{\Delta y}{\Delta x}$ | Slope of calibration curve | output unit / input unit | | 3 | $\text{Resolution} = \dfrac{\text{FS range}}{2^{n}}$ | For an $n$-bit ADC based instrument | input unit | | 4 | $\text{Span} = x_{max} - x_{min}$ | Full-scale input range | input unit | | 5 | $\text{Threshold} = \min(\Delta x) : \Delta y \neq 0$ | Smallest detectable input change | input unit | | 6 | $\text{Hysteresis } H = \dfrac{\Delta H_{max}}{FS} \times 100\%$ | Dead-band between upscale/downscale readings | % | | 7 | $\text{Non-linearity} = \dfrac{\Delta y_{max,dev}}{FS} \times 100\%$ | Worst-case deviation from best-fit straight line | % | | 8 | $\text{Drift} = \dfrac{y(t) - y_{0}}{y_{0} \times \Delta t}$ | Output variation with time at constant input | unit/time | | 9 | $\text{Signal-to-Noise Ratio} = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{V_{signal}}{V_{noise}}\right)$ | Quality of measurement | dB | | 10 | $P = V \cdot I$ | Power in electrical field measurement | Watt | | 11 | $E = \dfrac{V}{d}$ | Electric field strength (parallel plate) | V/m | | 12 | $B = \dfrac{\mu_{0} N I}{2r}$ | Magnetic field at center of coil | Tesla | | 13 | $V_{oc} = S \cdot \Delta T$ | Seebeck EMF (thermal field) | V | | 14 | $R_{T} = R_{0}(1 + \alpha \Delta T)$ | RTD resistance variation | $\Omega$ | | 15 | $V_{out} = V_{ex} \cdot \dfrac{\Delta R / R}{4 + 2(\Delta R / R)}$ | Wheatstone bridge output (approx) | V | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Board Tip:** Always quote the **units** along with numerical sensitivity values. Marks are deducted when sensitivity is written without dimensions. (e.g., write *"Sensitivity = 0.5 mV/°C"* — not just *"Sensitivity = 0.5"*.) ### 2.4 Real-World Utility of Field-Based Instrumentation - **Process Industries (Oil & Gas, Chemical):** Pressure, flow, level, temperature — *the big four fields* measured continuously in every refinery using **4–20 mA HART protocol** instruments. - **Aerospace:** Strain, acceleration, vibration, magnetic heading (gyrocompass). - **Medical Diagnostics:** ECG (electric field of heart), EEG (brain), SpO₂ (optical field). - **Automotive:** Crankshaft position (magnetic field via Hall sensor), MAP pressure, oxygen concentration (Lambda sensor). - **Smartphones:** Accelerometer (mechanical field), Magnetometer (magnetic field), Ambient light sensor (optical field), Proximity sensor (electric field). - **Renewable Energy:** Wind anemometry, solar irradiance pyranometer. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation of the Generalized Measurement System Output Consider a generic instrument subjected to an input field $x(t)$ (e.g., temperature, pressure, force). The instrument's output $y(t)$ is related to $x(t)$ through a cascade of three transfer stages, each with its own static gain $K_i$ and dynamic time constant $\tau_i$. **Step 1 — Establish the input–output equation for stage 1 (Sensor):** The sensor converts the physical field into a low-level electrical signal. For a first-order sensor (most common case): $$ \tau_{1}\dfrac{dy_{1}}{dt} + y_{1} = K_{1}\, x(t) $$ **Step 2 — Apply the static (steady-state) condition** $\dfrac{dy_{1}}{dt}=0$: $$ y_{1,ss} = K_{1}\, x $$ This is the **sensitivity relationship** in the static regime. **Step 3 — Cascade with stage 2 (Signal Conditioner):** $$ \tau_{2}\dfrac{dy_{2}}{dt} + y_{2} = K_{2}\, y_{1} $$ **Step 4 — Substitute** $y_{1} = K_{1} x$ (assuming stage 1 is much faster, $\tau_{1}\to 0$): $$ \tau_{2}\dfrac{dy_{2}}{dt} + y_{2} = K_{1} K_{2}\, x $$ **Step 5 — Cascade with stage 3 (Output/Display stage) with gain $K_{3}$:** $$ \tau_{3}\dfrac{dy_{3}}{dt} + y_{3} = K_{1} K_{2} K_{3}\, x $$ **Step 6 — Final overall transfer function** (Laplace domain): $$ \dfrac{Y_{3}(s)}{X(s)} = \dfrac{K_{1} K_{2} K_{3}}{(\tau_{1}s+1)(\tau_{2}s+1)(\tau_{3}s+1)} $$ **Step 7 — Steady-state overall sensitivity** (set $s=0$): $$ K_{overall} = K_{1}\,K_{2}\,K_{3} $$ The **overall sensitivity** is the **product** of individual stage sensitivities. ### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Style) **Problem:** A thermocouple (sensitivity $K_{1}=40\ \mu V/°C$) is connected to an instrumentation amplifier (gain $K_{2}=250$) followed by a 12-bit ADC with a reference voltage of $V_{ref}=5\ V$ and range $0$–$500\ °C$. Find: (a) Overall sensitivity in V/°C. (b) ADC resolution in °C. (c) Output voltage when temperature $=350\ °C$. **Solution:** **(a) Overall sensitivity:** $$ K_{overall} = K_{1} \cdot K_{2} = (40 \times 10^{-6}\,\text{V/°C}) \cdot 250 = 0.01\ \text{V/°C} = 10\ \text{mV/°C} $$ **(b) ADC Resolution:** Number of quantization levels: $$ L = 2^{n} = 2^{12} = 4096 $$ Voltage per LSB: $$ \Delta V = \dfrac{V_{ref}}{L} = \dfrac{5\ \text{V}}{4096} = 1.2207\ \text{mV} $$ Resolution in °C: $$ \Delta T = \dfrac{\Delta V}{K_{overall}} = \dfrac{1.2207\ \text{mV}}{10\ \text{mV/°C}} \approx 0.122\ °C $$ **(c) Output voltage at $T = 350\ °C$:** $$ V_{out} = K_{overall} \cdot T = (0.01\ \text{V/°C}) \cdot (350\ °C) = 3.5\ \text{V} $$ > [!NOTE] > **Verification:** The output 3.5 V lies within the 0–5 V ADC range, confirming the instrument is correctly designed. ### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Field-wise Comparison for Board Exams | Field | Typical Sensor | Output Quantity | Typical Range | Governing Law | |---|---|---|---|---| | Temperature | K-type Thermocouple | EMF | $-200$ to $1370\ °C$ | Seebeck Effect | | Temperature | Pt100 RTD | Resistance | $-200$ to $850\ °C$ | $R = R_{0}(1+\alpha T)$ | | Pressure | Strain-gauge load cell | mV/V | $0$–$1000\ bar$ | Piezo-resistive | | Displacement | LVDT | AC Voltage | $\pm 1\ mm$ to $\pm 500\ mm$ | Faraday's Law | | Magnetic Field | Hall Sensor | Voltage | $\pm 100\ mT$ | Lorentz Force | | Flow | Orifice + DP cell | mA (4–20) | $0$–$10^{6}\ l/h$ | Bernoulli's Equation | | pH | Glass Electrode | mV | $0$–$14\ pH$ | Nernst Equation | | Light | Photodiode | Current | $10^{-9}$ to $10^{-3}\ A$ | Photoconductivity | | Acceleration | MEMS Accelerometer | V/g | $\pm 2g$ to $\pm 200g$ | Newton's 2nd Law | ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Generalized Block Diagram — Instrumentation across any Field ```mermaid flowchart LR A["PHYSICAL FIELD
Temperature Pressure Strain
Flow Magnetic Light"] A --> B["PRIMARY SENSOR
Thermocouple Strain Gauge
LVDT Hall Photodiode"] B --> C["VARIABLE CONVERSION
Wheatstone Bridge
Charge Amplifier"] C --> D["SIGNAL CONDITIONER
Amplifier Filter Linearizer
Cold Junction Comp"] D --> E["ADC / STANDARD OUTPUT
4 to 20 mA 0 to 10 V
Digital HART Modbus"] E --> F["DISPLAY AND CONTROL
CRO DMM Recorder
PLC DCS SCADA"] classDef fieldBox fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#FF8C00,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef sensorBox fill:#B0E0E6,stroke:#4682B4,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef condBox fill:#98FB98,stroke:#228B22,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef outBox fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#C71585,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 class A fieldBox class B,C sensorBox class D condBox class E,F outBox ``` ### 4.2 Classification Tree of Instrumentation Fields ```mermaid flowchart TD ROOT["FIELDS OF
ELECTRONIC INSTRUMENTATION"] ROOT --> MECH["MECHANICAL FIELD
Force Strain Pressure
Displacement Flow Torque"] ROOT --> THERMO["THERMAL FIELD
Temperature Heat Flux
Thermal Conductivity"] ROOT --> ELEC["ELECTRICAL FIELD
Voltage Current Power
Resistance Capacitance"] ROOT --> MAG["MAGNETIC FIELD
Flux Density H Field
Permeability Reluctance"] ROOT --> OPT["OPTICAL FIELD
Illuminance Luminance
Color Wavelength IR UV"] ROOT --> CHEM["CHEMICAL FIELD
pH Humidity Gas
Concentration Moisture"] ROOT --> BIO["BIOMEDICAL FIELD
ECG EEG EMG
Blood Pressure SpO2"] MECH --> M1["Strain Gauge LVDT
Load Cell Turbine Meter"] THERMO --> T1["Thermocouple RTD
Thermistor Pyrometer"] ELEC --> E1["Shunt CT PT
Hall Sensor Rogowski Coil"] MAG --> MG1["Hall Probe Fluxgate
Search Coil SQUID"] OPT --> O1["Photodiode LDR CCD
Pyranometer Spectrometer"] CHEM --> C1["Glass Electrode IR Gas
Sensor Mass Spectrometer"] BIO --> B1["AgAgCl Electrode
Pulse Oximeter Ultrasound"] classDef root fill:#FFD700,stroke:#B8860B,stroke-width:3px,color:#000 classDef lvl1 fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#000080,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef lvl2 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,stroke-width:1px,color:#000 class ROOT root class MECH,THERMO,ELEC,MAG,OPT,CHEM,BIO lvl1 class M1,T1,E1,MG1,O1,C1,B1 lvl2 ``` ### 4.3 Closed-Loop Field Measurement (with Feedback) ```mermaid flowchart LR IN["Input Field x"] --> SUM(("+")) SUM --> FWD["Forward Path
Sensor Amplifier"] FWD --> OUT["Output y"] OUT --> FBK["Feedback Element
beta"] FBK --> SUM SUM -.-> ERR["Error e = x minus beta y"] classDef err fill:#FF6347,stroke:#8B0000,stroke-width:2px,color:#FFF class ERR err ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **Closed-loop systems have:** > $$ \text{Closed Loop Gain} = \dfrac{G}{1 + G\beta} $$ > where $G$ is forward path gain and $\beta$ is feedback factor. If $G\beta \gg 1$, gain $\approx \dfrac{1}{\beta}$ — this is the principle behind all **high-accuracy industrial transmitters**. ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each) --- **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** Define a *field* in the context of Electronic Instrumentation.
