# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Static Characteristics — Structured Analytical Breakdown

### 2.1.1 Accuracy
- **Definition:** Closeness of the instrument's output (reading) to the **true value** of the measured quantity.
- **Mathematical form:**

$$\text{Accuracy} = 1 - \left\vert \frac{\text{Measured Value} - \text{True Value}}{\text{True Value}} \right\vert$$

- **Expressed in % of true value** (often called "accuracy as a percentage of full-scale reading").
- **Real-world utility:** Calibration certificates of industrial instruments quote accuracy in %FS (Full Scale).

### 2.1.2 Precision
- **Definition:** The degree of **reproducibility / repeatability of measurements** — that is, how closely multiple readings of the *same* quantity agree with each other, irrespective of how close they are to the true value.
- **Conceptual clarity:** A dartboard analogy — all darts clustered tightly (high precision) but far from bullseye (low accuracy) is *precise but not accurate*.
- **No single formula** — precision is usually expressed as the **standard deviation (σ)** of a set of repeated readings.

> [!NOTE]
> **Common KTU Pitfall:** Students often interchange *precision* with *accuracy*. **Accuracy is about "true value"; Precision is about "closeness among themselves".**

### 2.1.3 Sensitivity (Static Sensitivity, K)
- **Definition:** The **ratio of the change in output to the change in input** that causes it.

$$K = \frac{\Delta q_{\text{out}}}{\Delta q_{\text{in}}} = \frac{dq_{\text{out}}}{dq_{\text{in}}}$$

- **Units:** Output unit per input unit (e.g., mV/°C for a thermocouple).
- **High sensitivity ≠ High accuracy.** A highly sensitive voltmeter may still be inaccurate.

### 2.1.4 Resolution (Discrimination)
- The **smallest change in input** that can be detected and faithfully indicated by the instrument.
- Example: A digital multimeter displaying 3½ digits has a resolution of 0.1 mV on a 200 mV range.

### 2.1.5 Linearity
- The degree to which the **output-input calibration curve is a straight line**.
- A perfectly linear instrument has a constant sensitivity K over its full range.
- **Non-linearity (%FS)** is often quoted as:

$$\% \text{Linearity} = \frac{\Delta y_{\max}}{y_{\text{FS}}} \times 100$$

### 2.1.6 Hysteresis
- The **difference in output** for the *same* input, depending on whether the input is **increasing** or **decreasing**.
- Caused by friction, backlash in gears, magnetic hysteresis in cores, or elastic after-effects.

$$\varepsilon_H = \left\vert y_{\text{increasing}} - y_{\text{decreasing}} \right\vert \bigg\vert_{\text{at same input}}$$

### 2.1.7 Dead Zone (Dead Band)
- The **largest range of input values** for which the output does **not change at all**.
- It is caused by friction, insensitivity of the detector, or threshold effects.
- Dead band is *larger* than resolution — no output change occurs *at all* over this range.

### 2.1.8 Repeatability
- The ability of an instrument to give the **same output** when the **same input is applied repeatedly under identical conditions** (same observer, same instrument, same environment, short time interval).
- Expressed as **% of full scale** for a specified number of trials.

### 2.1.9 Reproducibility
- The ability of an instrument to give the **same output** for the same input under **changed conditions** (different observer, different location, different time — long interval).
- Reproducibility is **always worse than or equal to repeatability**.

### 2.1.10 Drift
- The **unwanted change in output** over a period of time, *with the input held constant*.
- **Zero Drift:** Output changes when the input is *zero* (e.g., due to aging, temperature).
- **Sensitivity Drift:** The slope (sensitivity) of the calibration curve changes with time.

### 2.1.11 Range and Span
- **Range:** The minimum to maximum value of the input the instrument is designed to measure.
- **Span:** Range = Maximum − Minimum input. (A thermometer with range −20 °C to +100 °C has a span of 120 °C.)

### 2.1.12 Threshold
- The **smallest input change** needed to produce a detectable output change — it is the **boundary of the dead zone**.

### 2.1.13 Static Error
- The **difference between the measured value and the true value** under static conditions:

$$e_s = A_m - A_t$$

where $A_m$ = measured value, $A_t$ = true value. Often quoted as %FS.

---

## 2.2 Dynamic Characteristics — Structured Analytical Breakdown

When the input varies with time, the instrument output may **lag, overshoot, oscillate, or settle** before reaching the true final value. These are quantified by dynamic characteristics, derived by applying **standard test signals** to the system:

| Test Signal | Use |
|---|---|
| Step input (unit step) | Rise time, time constant, settling time, overshoot, steady-state error |
| Ramp input | Tracking error, velocity lag |
| Sinusoidal input (frequency sweep) | Frequency response, bandwidth, natural frequency |

### 2.2.1 Speed of Response
- The rapidity with which an instrument responds to a **change in the measured quantity**.
- Quantified via: **rise time (tr)**, **time constant (τ)**, and **settling time (ts)**.

### 2.2.2 Fidelity
- The ability of the instrument to **faithfully reproduce the input signal** in the output — i.e., the output's shape should be a true replica of the input's shape (no distortion).

### 2.2.3 Lag
Two principal types:
- **Retardation Lag (First-order lag):** Output reaches its final value **asymptotically** without oscillation. Characterized by a **time constant τ**.
- **Time Delay Lag (Dead Time / Transport Lag):** Output is a **delayed replica** of the input by a fixed time $T_d$ (no shape change, just shifted). Caused by propagation, fluid travel time, etc.

### 2.2.4 Dynamic Error
- The **difference between the true instantaneous value** of the measured quantity and the value indicated by the instrument, when the input is **changing with time**.

$$e_d(t) = A_t(t) - A_m(t)$$

### 2.2.5 Time Constant (τ)
- For a **first-order system** with step input, τ is the time required for the output to reach **63.2 %** of its final value, or equivalently, the time at which the rate of change of output equals the initial rate of rise.

$$q_o(t) = q_{ss} \left(1 - e^{-t/\tau}\right)$$

### 2.2.6 Rise Time (tr)
- The time taken by the output to go from **10 % to 90 %** of its final (steady-state) value — standard engineering definition (0–100 % rise time is also used).

### 2.2.7 Settling Time (ts)
- The time required for the output to settle and remain within a tolerance band (commonly ±2 % or ±5 %) of the final value.

### 2.2.8 Overshoot
- The **maximum amount by which the output exceeds the final (steady-state) value** in a transient response, expressed as a % of the final value.

$$\% \text{Overshoot} = \frac{q_{\text{peak}} - q_{ss}}{q_{ss}} \times 100$$

### 2.2.9 Damping Factor / Damping Ratio (ζ)
- For a **second-order system**, ζ describes how oscillations decay.
- ζ = 0 → Undamped (sustained oscillations)
- 0 < ζ < 1 → **Underdamped** (oscillates, overshoots)
- ζ = 1 → **Critically damped** (fastest non-oscillatory response)
- ζ > 1 → **Overdamped** (sluggish, no overshoot)

### 2.2.10 Natural Frequency (ωn)
- The **undamped natural frequency** of the second-order system — the oscillation frequency if there were no damping.

### 2.2.11 Bandwidth (BW)
- The range of input signal frequencies over which the instrument's output follows the input within a specified accuracy (typically −3 dB point in the frequency response).

### 2.2.12 Dead Time (Pure Delay)
- A **fixed time delay** between input change and the start of the output response. Caused by transport lag (fluid flow, mechanical link, communication delay).

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Characteristic | Formula / Expression | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Accuracy | $1 - \left\vert \dfrac{A_m - A_t}{A_t} \right\vert$ | Dimensionless, % |
| 2 | Static Sensitivity | $K = \dfrac{\Delta q_o}{\Delta q_i}$ | Output/Input |
| 3 | Static Error | $e_s = A_m - A_t$ | Same as input unit |
| 4 | Hysteresis Error | $\varepsilon_H = \left\vert y_{\uparrow} - y_{\downarrow} \right\vert$ | Output unit |
| 5 | % Non-linearity | $\dfrac{\Delta y_{\max}}{y_{FS}} \times 100$ | % |
| 6 | First-order step response | $q_o(t) = K A \left(1 - e^{-t/\tau}\right)$ | — |
| 7 | Time at 63.2 % | $q_o(\tau) = 0.632 \, q_{ss}$ | s |
| 8 | Time at 95 % | $t_{95\%} = 3\tau$ | s |
| 9 | Time at 98 % | $t_{98\%} \approx 4\tau$ | s |
| 10 | Settling time (2 % band) | $t_s \approx 4\tau$ | s |
| 11 | % Overshoot (2nd order) | $\%M_p = e^{-\zeta \pi / \sqrt{1-\zeta^2}} \times 100$ | % |
| 12 | Damped natural frequency | $\omega_d = \omega_n \sqrt{1-\zeta^2}$ | rad/s |
| 13 | Bandwidth (−3 dB) for 1st order | $f_{-3dB} = \dfrac{1}{2\pi\tau}$ | Hz |
| 14 | Steady-state error (1st order, step) | $e_{ss} = 0$ | — |
| 15 | Pure time delay | $q_o(t) = q_i(t - T_d)$ | — |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** For first-order systems, **steady-state error to a step input is ZERO**, but for a ramp input it is **non-zero** (equal to $A \cdot T$, where A is ramp slope). This is a high-yield 7-mark question pattern.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Selection of a pressure transducer** for a rocket engine test stand: dynamic characteristics (natural frequency, bandwidth) matter more than static accuracy, because the pressure varies at kHz rates.
- **Industrial weighing scale:** static characteristics (linearity, hysteresis, repeatability) dominate.
- **Seismograph / accelerometer:** bandwidth and natural frequency are critical.
- **Patient ECG monitor:** fidelity, bandwidth (0.05–150 Hz), and low noise drift are essential.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: First-Order System Step Response

A first-order measurement system is described by a single energy-storage element. Its governing differential equation is:

$$T \dfrac{dq_o(t)}{dt} + q_o(t) = K \, q_i(t)$$

where:
- $T$ = time constant of the system (s),
- $K$ = static sensitivity (output per input),
- $q_i(t)$ = input,
- $q_o(t)$ = output.

### Step 1 — Apply a unit step input
Let $q_i(t) = A \cdot u(t)$ where $u(t)$ is the unit step function (and A is the magnitude).

$$T \dfrac{dq_o}{dt} + q_o = K A$$

### Step 2 — Take the Laplace transform (assuming zero initial conditions)
$$T s \, Q_o(s) + Q_o(s) = \dfrac{K A}{s}$$

### Step 3 — Solve for the output
$$Q_o(s) = \dfrac{K A}{s(Ts + 1)}$$

### Step 4 — Partial fraction expansion
$$\dfrac{1}{s(Ts+1)} = \dfrac{1}{s} - \dfrac{T}{Ts+1} = \dfrac{1}{s} - \dfrac{1}{s + 1/T}$$

Therefore:
$$Q_o(s) = K A \left( \dfrac{1}{s} - \dfrac{1}{s + 1/T} \right)$$

### Step 5 — Take the inverse Laplace transform
$$q_o(t) = K A \left( 1 - e^{-t/T} \right)$$

This is the **canonical first-order step response** of a measurement system. The key inferences:

- At $t = 0$ : $q_o(0) = 0$ (no instantaneous jump).
- At $t = T$ : $q_o(T) = KA(1 - e^{-1}) = 0.632 \, KA$ → 63.2 % of final value.
- At $t \to \infty$ : $q_o(\infty) = KA$ → final steady-state value.
- The response is **monotonically increasing** with no overshoot.
- The slope at $t=0$ is:

$$\left. \dfrac{dq_o}{dt} \right\vert_{t=0} = \dfrac{KA}{T}$$

i.e., if the initial slope were maintained, the output would reach the final value in time $T$ — which is the geometric meaning of the time constant.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Second-Order System Step Response

A second-order measurement system (e.g., a seismic mass + spring + damper) is described by:

$$T_m^2 \dfrac{d^2 q_o}{dt^2} + 2 \zeta T_m \dfrac{dq_o}{dt} + q_o = K \, q_i(t)$$

where:
- $T_m = 1/\omega_n$ is the natural period,
- $\omega_n$ is the natural (undamped) frequency,
- $\zeta$ is the damping ratio,
- $K$ is the static sensitivity.

### Step 1 — Apply unit step input of magnitude A
$$T_m^2 \dfrac{d^2 q_o}{dt^2} + 2 \zeta T_m \dfrac{dq_o}{dt} + q_o = K A$$

### Step 2 — Define the standard form
Letting $\omega_n = 1/T_m$:

$$\dfrac{1}{\omega_n^2} \dfrac{d^2 q_o}{dt^2} + \dfrac{2 \zeta}{\omega_n} \dfrac{dq_o}{dt} + q_o = K A$$

### Step 3 — Underdamped case ($0 < \zeta < 1$) — Laplace transform
$$\left( \dfrac{s^2}{\omega_n^2} + \dfrac{2 \zeta s}{\omega_n} + 1 \right) Q_o(s) = \dfrac{K A}{s}$$

$$Q_o(s) = \dfrac{K A \omega_n^2}{s \left( s^2 + 2 \zeta \omega_n s + \omega_n^2 \right)}$$

### Step 4 — Roots of the characteristic equation
$$s_{1,2} = -\zeta \omega_n \pm j \omega_d$$

where $\omega_d = \omega_n \sqrt{1 - \zeta^2}$ is the **damped natural frequency**.

### Step 5 — Partial fractions
After applying standard Laplace tables:

$$Q_o(s) = \dfrac{K A}{s} - \dfrac{K A (s + \zeta \omega_n)}{(s + \zeta \omega_n)^2 + \omega_d^2} - \dfrac{K A \zeta \omega_n}{(s + \zeta \omega_n)^2 + \omega_d^2}$$

### Step 6 — Inverse Laplace transform
$$q_o(t) = K A \left[ 1 - \dfrac{e^{-\zeta \omega_n t}}{\sqrt{1 - \zeta^2}} \sin\left( \omega_d t + \phi \right) \right]$$

where $\phi = \cos^{-1}(\zeta)$ is the phase angle.

### Step 7 — Peak overshoot
Setting $\dfrac{dq_o}{dt} = 0$ at the first peak $t_p$:

$$t_p = \dfrac{\pi}{\omega_d} = \dfrac{\pi}{\omega_n \sqrt{1 - \zeta^2}}$$

$$M_p = \dfrac{q_o(t_p) - q_{ss}}{q_{ss}} = e^{-\zeta \pi / \sqrt{1 - \zeta^2}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 14-Mark Question Pattern:** "For a second-order instrument with $\zeta = 0.6$ and $\omega_n = 10$ rad/s, find the percentage overshoot, peak time, and settling time (2 % band)." — A near-exact 2022 KTU question style.

---

## 3.3 Worked Example — Numerical KTU-Style Problem

**Q:** A first-order temperature measurement system has a time constant of 5 s and a static sensitivity of 0.1 mV/°C. The input temperature is suddenly changed from 0 °C to 100 °C at $t = 0$. Find (a) the output at $t = 5$ s, (b) the time required to reach 95 % of the final value, and (c) the steady-state error.

### Part (a) — Output at t = 5 s
$$q_o(t) = K A \left(1 - e^{-t/T}\right)$$

$$q_o(5) = (0.1)(100) \left(1 - e^{-5/5}\right)$$

$$q_o(5) = 10 \left(1 - e^{-1}\right) = 10 (1 - 0.3679)$$

$$q_o(5) = 10 \times 0.6321 = 6.321 \; \text{mV}$$

### Part (b) — Time to reach 95 % of final value
$$0.95 = 1 - e^{-t/T}$$

$$e^{-t/T} = 0.05$$

$$t = -T \ln(0.05) = -5 \times (-2.9957)$$

$$t = 14.98 \; \text{s} \approx 3T \text{ (matches the rule)}$$

### Part (c) — Steady-state error (to step input)
For a first-order system under a step input, the steady-state error is **zero** (the output reaches $KA$ exactly as $t \to \infty$).

$$e_{ss} = \lim_{t \to \infty} \left( KA - q_o(t) \right) = KA - KA(1 - 0) = 0$$

**Model Answer:** 6.321 mV, 14.98 s, 0 V.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — First vs. Second Order

| Property | First-Order System | Second-Order System |
|---|---|---|
| Governing Equation | $T \dot{q_o} + q_o = K q_i$ | $T_m^2 \ddot{q_o} + 2 \zeta T_m \dot{q_o} + q_o = K q_i$ |
| Step Response | Monotonic exponential rise | May oscillate, overshoot, decay |
| Overshoot | None (always) | Present if $\zeta < 1$ |
| Key Parameter | Time constant τ | Damping ratio ζ & natural frequency ωn |
| Steady-state error to step | 0 | 0 |
| Steady-state error to ramp | Non-zero (= $A \cdot T$ for 1st order) | Non-zero, depends on $\zeta, \omega_n$ |
| Bandwidth | $BW = 1/(2\pi T)$ | Depends on ζ and ωn (−3 dB frequency) |
| Settling time | ≈ 4τ (2 % band) | $\approx \dfrac{4}{\zeta \omega_n}$ (2 % band) |

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — General Measurement System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Physical Quantity Input] --> P2[Primary Sensing Element]
    P2 --> P3[Variable Conversion Element]
    P3 --> P4[Variable Manipulation Element]
    P4 --> P5[Data Transmission Element]
    P5 --> P6[Data Presentation Output]
    P1 -. acts on .- P2
    P6 --> P7[Observer or Data Storage]
```

> **Figure 4.1** — Generic functional block architecture of an electronic measurement system, showing the signal flow from physical input to the final displayed output.

---

## 4.2 Functional Flow — Static vs. Dynamic Characteristics Mapping

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph SC[STATIC CHARACTERISTICS]
        S1[Accuracy]
        S2[Precision]
        S3[Sensitivity]
        S4[Resolution]
        S5[Linearity]
        S6[Hysteresis]
        S7[Dead Zone]
        S8[Repeatability and Reproducibility]
        S9[Drift]
        S10[Span and Range]
    end
    subgraph DC[DYNAMIC CHARACTERISTICS]
        D1[Speed of Response]
        D2[Fidelity]
        D3[Lag: Retardation and Time Delay]
        D4[Dynamic Error]
        D5[Time Constant]
        D6[Rise Time]
        D7[Settling Time]
        D8[Overshoot]
        D9[Damping Ratio]
        D10[Natural Frequency and Bandwidth]
    end
    IN[Measurement System] --> SC
    IN --> DC
    SC --> OUT1[Evaluated at constant or slowly varying input]
    DC --> OUT2[Evaluated under time-varying test input step, ramp, sinusoidal]
```

> **Figure 4.2** — Static and dynamic characteristics as parallel analytical layers applied to the same measurement system. Static characteristics are evaluated under steady-state input; dynamic characteristics are evaluated under time-varying input.

---

## 4.3 Step Response Schematic — First-Order vs. Second-Order

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph F1[First Order System]
        F1A[Step Input Applied] --> F1B[Output Rises Exponentially]
        F1B --> F1C[Reaches 63.2 percent at t = tau]
        F1C --> F1D[Asymptotic to Steady State]
        F1D --> F1E[No Overshoot]
    end
    subgraph S2[Second Order System]
        S2A[Step Input Applied] --> S2B{Underdamped zeta less than 1}
        S2B -->|Yes| S2C[Output Oscillates and Overshoots]
        S2C --> S2D[Settles after Damped Oscillations]
        S2B -->|No| S2E[Critically Damped or Overdamped]
        S2E --> S2F[Monotonic Rise, Slower than First Order]
    end
```

```mermaid
flowchart TB

    B --> C[Record Magnitude Ratio M of omega and Phase Lag phi of omega]
    C --> D[Plot Bode Diagram]
    D --> E[Read Off Natural Frequency omega_n]
    D --> F[Read Off Bandwidth BW at minus 3 dB]

```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define the following terms with examples:**
**(a) Static sensitivity**  
**(b) Hysteresis error**  
**(c) Dead zone**

### Model Answer
**(a) Static Sensitivity:** The ratio of the change in output to the corresponding change in input under steady-state conditions.

$$K = \dfrac{\Delta q_{\text{out}}}{\Delta q_{\text{in}}}$$

*Example:* A thermocouple producing 40 µV per °C change in temperature has a static sensitivity of 40 µV/°C.

**(b) Hysteresis Error:** The difference in the output reading of an instrument when the same input is approached from opposite directions (increasing vs. decreasing).

*Example:* A pressure gauge may read 100.2 kPa when the pressure is rising through 100 kPa, but 99.7 kPa when it is falling through 100 kPa — giving a hysteresis error of 0.5 kPa.

**(c) Dead Zone:** The largest range of input values for which the output does not change at all. It is caused by friction, backlash, or threshold insensitivity.

*Example:* A Bourdon tube pressure gauge may not move its needle at all for input changes below 0.5 kPa — that 0.5 kPa window is its dead zone.

---

### Question 2 — `[KTU University Exam - June 2023]`
**Differentiate between accuracy and precision. Why is a precise instrument not necessarily accurate?**

### Model Answer
| Basis | Accuracy | Precision |
|---|---|---|
| Definition | Closeness to the **true value** | Closeness of **repeated readings** to each other |
| Measures | Systematic error | Random error / scatter |
| Affected by | Calibration error | Random noise, observer variability |
| Quantified by | % error relative to true value | Standard deviation (σ) of readings |
| Analogy | Dart hits near bullseye | Darts cluster tightly (anywhere) |

A precise instrument may consistently give the *same* reading for the same input — but if it is miscalibrated, that reading is *systematically wrong*, making it precise but not accurate. Example: A miscalibrated digital weighing scale always reading 102 g instead of 100 g has perfect precision (σ = 0) but a 2 % accuracy error.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

**(a)** Explain the following static characteristics of a measurement system with suitable diagrams and formulas:  
    (i) Accuracy    (ii) Sensitivity    (iii) Linearity    (iv) Hysteresis    (v) Drift **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A first-order measurement system has a time constant of 8 seconds and a static sensitivity of 0.05 V/°C. The input is changed suddenly from 20 °C to 200 °C.  
    (i) Derive the expression for the output as a function of time.  
    (ii) Find the output at $t = 4$ s, $t = 16$ s, and $t \to \infty$.  
    (iii) Calculate the time required for the output to reach 98 % of its final value. **(7 Marks)**

### Model Answer — Part (a)
**Accuracy:** Accuracy is the degree of closeness of an instrument's output to the true value of the measured quantity. It is quantified as:

$$\% \text{Accuracy} = \left(1 - \dfrac{\vert A_m - A_t \vert}{A_t}\right) \times 100$$

A high-accuracy instrument has minimal systematic error.

**Sensitivity:** Static sensitivity is the slope of the calibration curve — the ratio of incremental change in output to the corresponding change in input:

$$K = \dfrac{\Delta q_o}{\Delta q_i}$$

A high-sensitivity instrument produces a large output for a small input change. The units of K are output-units/input-unit.

**Linearity:** A measurement system is linear if its output-input calibration curve is a perfectly straight line over its working range, i.e., K is constant. Real systems deviate; the deviation is called non-linearity and is specified as % of full scale:

$$\% NL = \dfrac{\Delta y_{\max}}{y_{FS}} \times 100$$

**Hysteresis:** Hysteresis is the phenomenon in which the output of the instrument for a given input depends on whether the input is increasing or decreasing. The maximum separation between the up-curve and down-curve, expressed in %FS, is the hysteresis error. Sources include friction, backlash, and magnetic/elastic after-effects.

**Drift:** Drift is the slow, unwanted change in output of an instrument over time, with the input held constant.  
- **Zero drift** = output changes at zero input (e.g., due to temperature variation).  
- **Sensitivity drift** = the slope of the calibration curve changes with time (e.g., due to aging of components).

### Model Answer — Part (b)
**(i) Derivation:**
For a first-order system:

$$T \dfrac{dq_o}{dt} + q_o = K q_i$$

With $q_i(t) = 200 \cdot u(t)$, $K = 0.05$ V/°C, $T = 8$ s:

Step input change: $\Delta q_i = 200 - 20 = 180$ °C.

Steady-state output: $q_{ss} = K \cdot \Delta q_i = 0.05 \times 180 = 9$ V (superimposed on the 20 °C reading baseline of 1 V → total 10 V at $t \to \infty$).

The dynamic response (increment about 1 V) is:

$$q_o^{\text{inc}}(t) = 9 \left(1 - e^{-t/8}\right) \; \text{V}$$

$$q_o(t) = 1 + 9 \left(1 - e^{-t/8}\right) = 10 - 9 e^{-t/8} \; \text{V}$$

**(ii) Output values:**
- $t = 4$ s: $q_o(4) = 10 - 9 e^{-0.5} = 10 - 9 (0.6065) = 10 - 5.459 = 4.541$ V
- $t = 16$ s: $q_o(16) = 10 - 9 e^{-2} = 10 - 9 (0.1353) = 10 - 1.218 = 8.782$ V
- $t \to \infty$: $q_o(\infty) = 10$ V

**(iii) Time to reach 98 % of final value:**
98 % of $(10 - 1) = 9$ V incremental change = 8.82 V. Therefore total output = 1 + 8.82 = 9.82 V.

$$9.82 = 10 - 9 e^{-t/8}$$

$$9 e^{-t/8} = 0.18 \implies e^{-t/8} = 0.02$$

$$t = -8 \ln(0.02) = 8 \times 3.912 = 31.3 \; \text{s} \approx 4T$$

---

### Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice) — `[KTU University Exam - June 2022]`

**(a)** With neat diagrams, explain the following dynamic characteristics of a measurement system:  
    (i) Speed of response    (ii) Time constant    (iii) Rise time    (iv) Settling time    (v) Overshoot **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A second-order measurement system has natural frequency $\omega_n = 20$ rad/s and damping ratio $\zeta = 0.4$. Find:  
    (i) Damped natural frequency $\omega_d$  
    (ii) % Overshoot  
    (iii) Time to first peak $t_p$  
    (iv) Approximate 2 % settling time $t_s$ **(7 Marks)**

### Model Answer — Part (a)
**Speed of Response:** The rapidity with which an instrument's output responds to a change in the measured quantity. Quantified by rise time, time constant (for first order), or natural frequency and damping ratio (for second order).

**Time Constant (τ):** For a first-order instrument, the time required for the output to reach 63.2 % of its final (steady-state) value after a step input. Alternatively, if the initial slope were maintained, the output would reach the final value in time τ.

$$q_o(t) = K A (1 - e^{-t/\tau})$$

**Rise Time (tr):** The time for the output to go from 10 % to 90 % of its final value (standard definition for first-order systems). For first-order systems, $t_r \approx 2.2 \tau$.

**Settling Time (ts):** The time required for the output to settle and remain within a specified tolerance band (commonly ±2 % or ±5 %) of the final value. For first-order: $t_s \approx 4\tau$ (2 % band). For second-order: $t_s \approx 4/(\zeta \omega_n)$.

**Overshoot (Mp):** The amount by which the output peak exceeds the final steady-state value, expressed as a percentage. For underdamped second-order systems:

$$\% M_p = e^{-\zeta \pi / \sqrt{1 - \zeta^2}} \times 100$$

### Model Answer — Part (b)
Given: $\omega_n = 20$ rad/s, $\zeta = 0.4$.

**(i) Damped natural frequency:**
$$\omega_d = \omega_n \sqrt{1 - \zeta^2} = 20 \sqrt{1 - 0.16} = 20 \sqrt{0.84} = 20 \times 0.9165 = 18.33 \; \text{rad/s}$$

**(ii) % Overshoot:**
$$M_p = e^{-\zeta \pi / \sqrt{1 - \zeta^2}} = e^{-0.4 \pi / 0.9165} = e^{-1.371} = 0.2540$$

$$\% M_p = 25.4 \;\%$$

**(iii) Time to first peak:**
$$t_p = \dfrac{\pi}{\omega_d} = \dfrac{\pi}{18.33} = 0.1714 \; \text{s}$$

**(iv) 2 % Settling time (using approximation):**
$$t_s \approx \dfrac{4}{\zeta \omega_n} = \dfrac{4}{0.4 \times 20} = \dfrac{4}{8} = 0.5 \; \text{s}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**  
> **Common mistakes in this question:**  
> 1. **Confusing ωn and ωd** — many students incorrectly use ωn as the oscillation frequency. Always remember ωd is the *actual* frequency seen in the response.  
> 2. **Overshoot formula sign error** — the exponent is **negative**; missing the minus sign gives a number > 1, which is dimensionally impossible.  
> 3. **Settling-time formula** — for the 2 % band, use $4/(\zeta \omega_n)$; for 5 % band, use $3/(\zeta \omega_n)$. Mixing them costs 2 marks.  
> 4. **Forgetting to convert** — KTU expects rad/s, but some students write the answer in Hz (lose 1 mark).  
> 5. **First-order vs second-order confusion** — a second-order system with $\zeta = 1$ is *critically damped* and behaves like a first-order, but it is *not* first-order — it still has a second-order differential equation.  

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist (read 5 minutes before exam)**

- **Static characteristics** are evaluated under *constant or slowly varying* input; **dynamic characteristics** under *time-varying* input.
- **Accuracy** is closeness to true value; **precision** is closeness among repeated readings. They are *not* synonyms.
- **Sensitivity K** is the slope of the calibration curve; it has units of output/input.
- **Linearity** means constant K over the full range.
- **Hysteresis** is the difference between the up-curve and down-curve at the *same* input.
- **Dead zone** is the input range over which there is *no* output change.
- **Repeatability** is under identical conditions; **reproducibility** is under *changed* conditions. Reproducibility ≤ Repeatability.
- **Drift** is unwanted output change with time at *constant* input. Zero-drift and sensitivity-drift are the two types.
- **Span = Max input − Min input**; range is from min to max.
- **First-order system** → governed by $\tau$; no overshoot; monotonic exponential step response.
- **Time constant τ** is the time to reach 63.2 % of the final value.
- **$t_{95\%} \approx 3\tau$**, **$t_{98\%} \approx 4\tau$** — these are *memorize-by-heart* values for KTU.
- **Second-order system** → governed by $\omega_n$ and $\zeta$.
- **ζ = 0** (undamped) → sustained oscillation; **ζ = 1** (critically damped) → fastest non-oscillatory; **0 < ζ < 1** (underdamped) → overshoots and oscillates.
- **% Overshoot** = $e^{-\zeta \pi / \sqrt{1-\zeta^2}} \times 100$.
- **Settling time (2 % band)** = $4/(\zeta \omega_n)$ for second-order; $4\tau$ for first-order.
- **Steady-state error of first-order to a step input is zero** — but to a ramp input, it is non-zero.
- **Dynamic error** = true instantaneous input − indicated value; it is a function of *time*, not a single number.
- **Rise time** is conventionally measured from 10 % to 90 % of final value.
- **Fidelity** is the faithful reproduction of input shape in the output.
- **Lag has two forms**: retardation lag (asymptotic rise, characterized by τ) and time-delay lag (pure time shift, characterized by $T_d$).
- **Bandwidth** of a first-order system = $1/(2\pi\tau)$ Hz (−3 dB cutoff).
- **Mnemonic for second-order behavior:** "ζ = 0.7" gives 4.6 % overshoot — a useful benchmark number for KTU problems.
- **Standard test inputs for dynamic analysis:** step (most common in KTU), ramp, parabolic, sinusoidal.
- **Resolution** is the smallest detectable input change; it is a property of the *display* (e.g., last digit on a DMM).

> **End of Module 1 Topic: Static and Dynamic Characteristics — PEEET411 Electronic Instrumentation**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Zeroth and First Order Instruments and Time Responses
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET411 - ELECTRONIC INSTRUMENTATION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Zeroth and First Order Instruments and Time Responses

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Zeroth Order Instrument

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Zeroth Order Instrument:** A measurement system whose output is *instantaneously* and *exactly* proportional to its input, with **no energy storage elements** and **no time-dependent behavior**. The system is governed by an algebraic (non-differential) equation.

**Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology):**
A zeroth order instrument is one in which the static sensitivity $K$ relates the output $\theta_o(t)$ to the input $\theta_i(t)$ at every instant of time, independent of frequency or rate of change of input.

$$\theta_o(t) = K \cdot \theta_i(t)$$

where the **static sensitivity** $K$ is a pure constant (dimensionless or with appropriate units).

**Physical Constants & Standard Metrics:**
- **Static Sensitivity:** $K = \dfrac{\theta_o}{\theta_i}$ — a **constant** for all input amplitudes and frequencies.
- **Time Response:** Perfectly flat; no rise time, no settling time, no time constant.
- **Bandwidth:** **Infinite** (in theory) — the instrument responds equally well to DC, AC, and ultra-high-frequency signals.

**Conceptual Analogy (Plain English Intuition):**
> Imagine a perfectly rigid, massless spring scale with no friction. The instant you place a 1 kg apple on it, the needle jumps to exactly "1 kg" with **zero delay** and stays there. If you instantly replace it with a 5 kg bag, the needle jumps from 1 to 5 in **zero time** — no wobble, no settling, no lag. That is a zeroth order instrument: *what you put in is what you read out, instantly, every single moment, with absolute fidelity.*

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Zeroth Order Examples:**
> - A **linear potentiometer** (ideal, massless slider) measuring linear displacement.
> - An **ideal resistive voltage divider** measuring DC voltage.
> - A **theoretical ideal thermocouple** in a perfectly instantaneous heat-flow scenario.
> - A **purely resistive strain gauge** bridge (ignoring lead inductance).

---

## 1.2 First Order Instrument

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **First Order Instrument:** A measurement system containing **one independent energy storage element**, whose dynamic behavior is described by a **first-order linear ordinary differential equation**. The output does *not* respond instantaneously to input changes but rather rises/falls exponentially toward a final value.

**Formal Definition:**
A first order instrument relates the time-varying output $\theta_o(t)$ to the time-varying input $\theta_i(t)$ via the canonical first order differential equation:

$$\tau \dfrac{d\theta_o(t)}{dt} + \theta_o(t) = K \cdot \theta_i(t)$$

where:
- $\tau$ = **Time constant** (seconds) — the measure of the system's *inertia* or *sluggishness*.
- $K$ = **Static (DC) sensitivity** — same role as in zeroth order.
- $\dfrac{d\theta_o}{dt}$ = rate of change of output with respect to time.

**Conceptual Analogy (Plain English Intuition):**
> Think of a thermometer plunged into a hot cup of tea. It does **not** instantly show the tea's temperature — it climbs slowly, faster at first, then slower, gradually approaching the true temperature. That slow climb is governed by one energy storage element: the *thermal mass* of the mercury (or thermistor) storing heat. The thermometer "remembers" its old temperature and takes time to "forget" it and adopt the new one. That temperature probe is a textbook **first order instrument**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World First Order Examples:**
> - **Mercury-in-glass thermometer** (thermal mass + heat conduction).
> - **Single-pole RC low-pass filter** used as a signal conditioner.
> - **Liquid-in-glass manometer** measuring pressure.
> - **Thermistor** temperature sensor (thermal mass dominates).
> - **RL circuit** sensing current (single inductor storage).
> - **Dashpot-loaded spring** force gauge.

---

## 1.3 Order of an Instrument — The Governing Idea

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU High-Yield Concept:** The **order** of an instrument equals the number of **independent energy storage elements** in the system, which equals the order of the differential equation relating output to input.
>
> | Storage Element | Physical Realization | Energy Stored |
> |---|---|---|
> | Capacitor $C$ | Electrical | Electric field: $\frac{1}{2}CV^2$ |
> | Inductor $L$ | Electrical | Magnetic field: $\frac{1}{2}LI^2$ |
> | Mass $m$ | Mechanical | Kinetic: $\frac{1}{2}mv^2$ |
> | Spring $k$ | Mechanical | Potential: $\frac{1}{2}kx^2$ |
> | Fluid capacitance | Hydraulic | $\frac{1}{2}CV^2$ |
> | Thermal capacitance | Thermal | $mc\Delta T$ |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Zeroth Order Instrument — Deep Analysis

**Operational Logic Steps:**

- **Step 1 — Identify storage elements:** A zeroth order system has *zero* independent energy storage elements. No mass, no spring, no capacitor, no inductor, no thermal mass.
- **Step 2 — Write governing equation:** Pure algebraic relationship $\theta_o = K\theta_i$, *not* a differential equation.
- **Step 3 — Apply transfer function (Laplace domain):** With zero initial conditions,
$$\dfrac{\theta_o(s)}{\theta_i(s)} = K$$
- **Step 4 — Analyze frequency response:** Substitute $s = j\omega$,
$$\dfrac{\theta_o(j\omega)}{\theta_i(j\omega)} = K$$
The magnitude is **constant** at **$K$** for *all* frequencies $\omega \in [0, \infty)$, and the phase shift is **exactly zero** for all frequencies.
- **Step 5 — Interpret:** The instrument has **infinite bandwidth** and **zero phase distortion** — physically unrealizable but an important *benchmark* for real instruments.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters in engineering:** Zeroth order behavior is the **gold standard**. Engineers design amplifiers, sensors, and signal conditioners so that their *operating frequency range* lies well within the band where the instrument behaves effectively as zeroth order. For example, an audio amplifier is designed to be "DC-coupled" and flat from 20 Hz to 20 kHz, behaving essentially as a zeroth order system within that band.

---

## 2.2 First Order Instrument — Deep Analysis

**Operational Logic Steps:**

- **Step 1 — Identify the single storage element:** Examples: a capacitor (electrical), a fluid mass (hydraulic), a thermal mass (thermal), a moving mass with viscous damping but no spring (mechanical).
- **Step 2 — Write the governing first-order ODE:** Using force-balance, KVL, energy-balance, or thermal-balance depending on the physical domain.
- **Step 3 — Laplace transform with zero initial conditions:**
$$(\tau s + 1) \theta_o(s) = K \theta_i(s)$$
- **Step 4 — Obtain the transfer function:**
$$G(s) = \dfrac{\theta_o(s)}{\theta_i(s)} = \dfrac{K}{\tau s + 1}$$
This is the **standard form** of a first order transfer function — recognize it instantly in the exam.
- **Step 5 — Identify pole location:** Single real pole at $s = -\dfrac{1}{\tau}$ in the left-half s-plane, confirming **BIBO stability**.
- **Step 6 — Frequency response:** Substitute $s = j\omega$,
$$G(j\omega) = \dfrac{K}{1 + j\omega\tau}$$
- **Step 7 — Magnitude and phase:**
$$|G(j\omega)| = \dfrac{K}{\sqrt{1 + (\omega\tau)^2}}$$
$$\angle G(j\omega) = -\arctan(\omega\tau)$$

**Corner Frequency / Cutoff Frequency:**
At $\omega = 1/\tau$, the magnitude drops to $K/\sqrt{2} \approx 0.707K$ (i.e., **$-3$ dB** point) and the phase lag is **$-45°$**.

**Real-World Utility:**
- **Signal filtering:** RC low-pass filters in audio equipment, anti-aliasing filters in data acquisition.
- **Sensor dynamics:** Thermocouples, thermistors, pressure transducers all exhibit first order lag.
- **Control systems:** Many plant models (e.g., a heated tank, a motor velocity loop) are first order.
- **Biomedical instrumentation:** ECG amplifier input stages, EMG signal conditioning.

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master the following table. Every formula here is a *guaranteed* high-yield item for KTU Module 1 questions.**

| # | Quantity / Concept | Formula | Notes / Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Zeroth order output | $\theta_o = K\theta_i$ | Algebraic; no time-dependence |
| 2 | Zeroth order transfer function | $G(s) = K$ | Constant for all $s$ |
| 3 | Zeroth order bandwidth | $BW = \infty$ | Ideal benchmark |
| 4 | First order governing ODE | $\tau \dot{\theta}_o + \theta_o = K\theta_i$ | $\dot{\theta}_o \equiv d\theta_o/dt$ |
| 5 | First order transfer function | $G(s) = \dfrac{K}{\tau s + 1$ | Standard form |
| 6 | Time constant (RC circuit) | $\tau = RC$ | Seconds |
| 7 | Time constant (thermal) | $\tau = RC_{th} = \dfrac{mc}{hA}$ | Mass × sp.heat ÷ (ht. coeff × area) |
| 8 | Time constant (liquid manometer) | $\tau = \dfrac{\mu V}{gAh}$ | See derivation |
| 9 | Time constant (mechanical) | $\tau = \dfrac{c}{k}$ (dashpot/spring) | Damping ÷ stiffness |
| 10 | Pole of first order system | $s = -1/\tau$ | Left half plane → stable |
| 11 | Step response | $\theta_o(t) = K\theta_i\bigl(1 - e^{-t/\tau}\bigr)$ | For step input $A$ at $t=0$ |
| 12 | Step response initial slope | $\left.\dfrac{d\theta_o}{dt}\right\rvert_{t=0} = \dfrac{K\theta_i}{\tau}$ | Tangent at origin |
| 13 | Value at $t = \tau$ | $\theta_o(\tau) = 0.632 \cdot K\theta_i$ | **63.2%** of final value |
| 14 | Value at $t = 2\tau$ | $\theta_o(2\tau) = 0.865 \cdot K\theta_i$ | 86.5% |
| 15 | Value at $t = 3\tau$ | $\theta_o(3\tau) = 0.950 \cdot K\theta_i$ | 95% |
| 16 | Value at $t = 4\tau$ | $\theta_o(4\tau) = 0.982 \cdot K\theta_i$ | 98.2% (≈ 2% settling) |
| 17 | Value at $t = 5\tau$ | $\theta_o(5\tau) = 0.993 \cdot K\theta_i$ | 99.3% (≈ 1% settling) |
| 18 | Settling time (2% criterion) | $t_s \approx 4\tau$ | Practical "settled" time |
| 19 | Settling time (1% criterion) | $t_s \approx 4.6\tau$ | Tighter criterion |
| 20 | Settling time (0.5% criterion) | $t_s \approx 5.3\tau$ | High-precision instruments |
| 21 | Rise time (10% → 90%) | $t_r \approx 2.2\tau$ | First order rise time |
| 22 | Ramp response | $\theta_o(t) = K\bigl(At - A\tau + A\tau e^{-t/\tau}\bigr)$ | For ramp $At$ |
| 23 | Ramp error / velocity lag | $e(t) = K A \tau \bigl(1 - e^{-t/\tau}\bigr) \to K A \tau$ | Steady-state error |
| 24 | Impulse response | $\theta_o(t) = \dfrac{KA}{\tau} e^{-t/\tau}$ | For impulse $A\delta(t)$ |
| 25 | Magnitude response | $\vert G(j\omega) \vert = \dfrac{K}{\sqrt{1 + (\omega\tau)^2}}$ | Bode magnitude |
| 26 | Phase response | $\angle G(j\omega) = -\arctan(\omega\tau)$ | Bode phase |
| 27 | Cutoff / corner frequency | $\omega_c = 1/\tau$, $f_c = 1/(2\pi\tau)$ | $-3$ dB frequency |
| 28 | Static sensitivity | $K = \theta_{o,ss}/\theta_{i,ss}$ | Ratio of final values |
| 29 | DC gain | $G(0) = K$ | $s=0$ evaluation |
| 30 | High-frequency gain | $G(j\omega)\vert_{\omega \to \infty} \to 0$ | Asymptotic roll-off $-20$ dB/decade |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake in KTU Valuation:** Students often confuse $\tau = RC$ with $1/(RC)$. The **time constant** is the product $RC$ (or equivalent in mechanical/thermal domains), not the inverse. The **cutoff frequency** is $1/\tau$, the inverse of the time constant.

---

## 2.4 Why Zeroth vs. First Order Matters — Engineering Perspective

A practical sensor or instrument almost always has at least one dominant energy storage element. The first order model is the **simplest non-trivial** dynamic model and is the workhorse of:
- **Calibration and specification:** Datasheets specify the "thermal time constant" of a thermocouple, the "settling time" of a pressure transducer, the "rise time" of a photodiode amplifier.
- **Compensation design:** Knowing $\tau$ allows analog or digital compensation (e.g., lead networks) to make the instrument behave as if it were zeroth order over the band of interest.
- **Sampling rate selection:** Per Nyquist, sampling rate must be at least $2 \times$ signal bandwidth; the first order cutoff $f_c$ sets that bandwidth.
- **Stability analysis in feedback loops:** Many feedback control systems have first order plant dynamics.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of First Order Differential Equation (Generic)

Consider a measurement system with a single energy storage element. Let:
- $x_i(t)$ = input (measurand)
- $x_o(t)$ = output (reading)
- $K$ = static sensitivity
- $\tau$ = time constant (to be derived from physical constants)

A first order linear system obeys the energy balance / force balance / KVL:

$$\tau \dfrac{dx_o(t)}{dt} + x_o(t) = K \cdot x_i(t)$$

**Step-by-step logic:**

- The output cannot change instantaneously because the storage element resists it.
- The rate of change of output is proportional to the *difference* between $K x_i$ (the "desired" output) and the current output $x_o$.
- This is mathematically the same as a discharging capacitor, a cooling body, or a damping dashpot — they all share the same exponential character.

---

## 3.2 Derivation of Step Response (Most Important Derivation for KTU)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This is the single most-asked derivation in Module 1 of PEEET411. Memorize and understand it cold.**

**Problem Setup:** A first order instrument with transfer function $G(s) = \dfrac{K}{\tau s + 1}$ is subjected to a step input of magnitude $A$ applied at $t=0$, i.e., $x_i(t) = A \cdot u(t)$ where $u(t)$ is the unit step.

**Step 1 — Take the Laplace transform of the input:**

$$x_i(s) = \mathcal{L}\{A \cdot u(t)\} = \dfrac{A}{s}$$

**Step 2 — Multiply by the transfer function to get output in s-domain:**

$$x_o(s) = G(s) \cdot x_i(s) = \dfrac{K}{\tau s + 1} \cdot \dfrac{A}{s} = \dfrac{KA}{s(\tau s + 1)}$$

**Step 3 — Perform partial fraction expansion:**

We want to write
$$\dfrac{KA}{s(\tau s + 1)} = \dfrac{C_1}{s} + \dfrac{C_2}{\tau s + 1}$$

Multiply both sides by $s(\tau s + 1)$:

$$KA = C_1(\tau s + 1) + C_2 s$$

**Step 4 — Solve for the constants by cover-up method:**

**For $C_1$** — set $s = 0$:

$$C_1 = \left. KA \right\rvert_{s=0} = KA$$

**For $C_2$** — set $\tau s + 1 = 0 \Rightarrow s = -1/\tau$:

$$C_2 = \left. \dfrac{KA}{s} \right\rvert_{s = -1/\tau} = \dfrac{KA}{-1/\tau} = -KA\tau$$

**Step 5 — Substitute back into the partial fraction form:**

$$x_o(s) = \dfrac{KA}{s} - \dfrac{KA\tau}{\tau s + 1}$$

Rewrite the second term so the denominator is in standard $s$-form (divide numerator and denominator by $\tau$):

$$x_o(s) = \dfrac{KA}{s} - \dfrac{KA}{s + 1/\tau}$$

**Step 6 — Take the inverse Laplace transform term by term:**

Using the standard pairs $\mathcal{L}^{-1}\{1/s\} = u(t)$ and $\mathcal{L}^{-1}\{1/(s+a)\} = e^{-at}u(t)$:

$$x_o(t) = KA \cdot u(t) - KA \cdot e^{-t/\tau} \cdot u(t)$$

**Step 7 — Final step response expression (for $t \geq 0$):**

$$\boxed{x_o(t) = KA \left(1 - e^{-t/\tau}\right)}$$

**Interpretation:**
- At $t = 0$: $x_o(0) = KA(1 - 1) = 0$ → output starts from zero.
- At $t \to \infty$: $x_o(\infty) = KA(1 - 0) = KA$ → output reaches the steady-state value $KA$.
- The exponential approaches the final value **asymptotically**; it *never* mathematically reaches it, but in practice, by $t = 4\tau$ to $5\tau$ it is "close enough" (98.2% to 99.3%).

**Verification of Initial Slope:**

$$\dfrac{dx_o}{dt} = KA \cdot \dfrac{1}{\tau} e^{-t/\tau}$$

At $t = 0$:

$$\left. \dfrac{dx_o}{dt} \right\rvert_{t=0} = \dfrac{KA}{\tau}$$

This means if the response continued *linearly* at its initial rate, it would reach the final value $KA$ in exactly time $\tau$ — another intuitive definition of the time constant.

---

## 3.3 Derivation of Ramp Response

**Problem Setup:** Input is a ramp $x_i(t) = A t \cdot u(t)$ where $A$ is the rate (units of measurand per second). The Laplace transform:

$$x_i(s) = \dfrac{A}{s^2}$$

**Step 1 — Multiply by transfer function:**

$$x_o(s) = \dfrac{K}{\tau s + 1} \cdot \dfrac{A}{s^2} = \dfrac{KA}{s^2(\tau s + 1)}$$

**Step 2 — Partial fraction expansion:**

$$\dfrac{KA}{s^2(\tau s + 1)} = \dfrac{C_1}{s} + \dfrac{C_2}{s^2} + \dfrac{C_3}{\tau s + 1}$$

Multiply through by $s^2(\tau s + 1)$:

$$KA = C_1 s(\tau s + 1) + C_2(\tau s + 1) + C_3 s^2$$

**Step 3 — Solve by inspection and substitution:**

**For $C_2$** — set $s = 0$:

$$C_2 = \dfrac{KA}{1} = KA$$

**For $C_3$** — set $\tau s + 1 = 0 \Rightarrow s = -1/\tau$:

$$C_3 = \left. \dfrac{KA}{s^2} \right\rvert_{s=-1/\tau} = \dfrac{KA}{1/\tau^2} = KA\tau^2$$

**For $C_1$** — compare coefficients of $s^2$ (the $s^2$ coefficient on LHS is 0):

$$0 = C_1 \tau + C_3 \Rightarrow C_1 = -\dfrac{C_3}{\tau} = -KA\tau$$

**Step 4 — Substitute back:**

$$x_o(s) = -\dfrac{KA\tau}{s} + \dfrac{KA}{s^2} + \dfrac{KA\tau^2}{\tau s + 1}$$

Rewrite the third term by dividing numerator and denominator by $\tau$:

$$x_o(s) = -\dfrac{KA\tau}{s} + \dfrac{KA}{s^2} + \dfrac{KA\tau}{s + 1/\tau}$$

**Step 5 — Inverse Laplace transform:**

$$x_o(t) = -KA\tau + KAt + KA\tau e^{-t/\tau} \quad (t \geq 0)$$

**Step 6 — Final ramp response:**

$$\boxed{x_o(t) = KAt - KA\tau + KA\tau e^{-t/\tau}}$$

**Steady-state analysis (as $t \to \infty$):**

$$x_{o,ss}(t) = KAt - KA\tau$$

The output lags the ideal ramp $KAt$ by a constant offset of $KA\tau$ — this is the **velocity error** or **ramp error**:

$$\boxed{e_{ss} = KA\tau}$$

**Why the error exists:** A first order system cannot track a *constantly increasing* input exactly because it has inertia; it always lags by an amount proportional to its time constant.

---

## 3.4 Derivation of Impulse Response

**Problem Setup:** Input is an impulse $x_i(t) = A \delta(t)$. Laplace transform:

$$x_i(s) = A$$

**Step 1 — Output in s-domain:**

$$x_o(s) = \dfrac{K}{\tau s + 1} \cdot A = \dfrac{KA}{\tau s + 1}$$

**Step 2 — Express in standard form:**

$$x_o(s) = \dfrac{KA/\tau}{s + 1/\tau}$$

**Step 3 — Inverse Laplace transform:**

$$\boxed{x_o(t) = \dfrac{KA}{\tau} e^{-t/\tau} \quad (t \geq 0)}$$

**Interpretation:** The output is an exponentially decaying curve, starting at $KA/\tau$ at $t=0^+$ and decaying to zero as $t \to \infty$. The **area under the impulse response** equals $KA$, which equals the *step response's final value* — this is a basic property of linear systems and is often tested.

---

## 3.5 Derivations of Time Constants in Various Physical Domains

### (a) RC Electrical Circuit (Low-Pass Filter)

**Setup:** Series combination of $R$ and $C$, with output taken across $C$. Input voltage $v_i(t)$, output voltage $v_o(t)$.

**Step 1 — Apply KVL around the loop:**

$$v_i(t) = v_R(t) + v_o(t)$$

**Step 2 — Express $v_R$ in terms of current:**

$$v_R(t) = i(t) \cdot R = R \cdot i(t)$$

**Step 3 — Express current as derivative of capacitor charge/voltage:**

$$i(t) = C \dfrac{dv_o(t)}{dt}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute back into KVL:**

$$v_i(t) = RC \dfrac{dv_o(t)}{dt} + v_o(t)$$

**Step 5 — Identify canonical first order form:**

$$\tau \dfrac{dv_o}{dt} + v_o = K v_i, \quad \text{with} \quad \tau = RC, \quad K = 1$$

---

### (b) Thermal System (Thermometer / Thermocouple in Liquid Bath)

**Setup:** A thermal mass with heat capacity $C_{th}$ (J/°C) at temperature $T_o$, immersed in a bath at temperature $T_i$, with heat transfer coefficient $h$ (W/m²·°C) and surface area $A$ (m²).

**Step 1 — Energy balance:** Net rate of heat flow into the mass equals rate of heat storage:

$$hA(T_i - T_o) = C_{th} \dfrac{dT_o}{dt}$$

**Step 2 — Rearrange into standard form:**

$$C_{th} \dfrac{dT_o}{dt} + hA T_o = hA T_i$$

**Step 3 — Identify constants:**

$$\tau = \dfrac{C_{th}}{hA}, \quad K = 1$$

**Step 4 — Verify units:** $[C_{th}] = $ J/°C, $[hA] = $ W/°C = J/(s·°C), so $\tau = $ (J/°C) / (J/(s·°C)) = **seconds**. ✓

---

### (c) Liquid-in-Glass Manometer

**Setup:** A U-tube manometer of uniform cross-section $A$ filled with liquid of density $\rho$, viscosity $\mu$, with one leg connected to a pressure source $P_i$ and the other leg open to atmosphere. The height difference $h$ between the two legs is the output.

**Step 1 — Force balance (pressure force vs. viscous drag):**

$$A(P_i - P_{atm}) = \mu \dfrac{A}{\ell} \dfrac{dh}{dt} \cdot h$$ (simplified form)

For small laminar flow, the viscous resistance is $R = 8\mu \ell / (\pi r^4)$ for a tube of radius $r$ and length $\ell$.

**Step 2 — The governing equation reduces to:**

$$R \cdot A \rho g \cdot \dfrac{dh}{dt} + h = \dfrac{(P_i - P_{atm})}{(\rho g)}$$

**Step 3 — Time constant:**

$$\tau = \dfrac{8\mu \ell A \rho g}{\pi r^4 \rho g} = \dfrac{8\mu \ell A}{\pi r^4}$$

> [!NOTE]
> The exact form varies by derivation; for KTU exams, the manometer time constant is typically given as $\tau = \dfrac{8\mu L}{\pi r^4 \rho g}$ where $L$ is the length of the fluid column in each leg, $r$ the tube radius, $\mu$ the dynamic viscosity, and $\rho$ the fluid density.

---

## 3.6 Analytical Summary Table

| Test Input | Input $x_i(t)$ | Output $x_o(t)$ for $t \geq 0$ | Steady-State Behavior |
|---|---|---|---|
| Step $A$ | $A \cdot u(t)$ | $KA(1 - e^{-t/\tau})$ | Reaches $KA$ asymptotically |
| Ramp $At$ | $A t \cdot u(t)$ | $KAt - KA\tau + KA\tau e^{-t/\tau}$ | Lags ideal by $KA\tau$ |
| Impulse $A\delta(t)$ | $A\delta(t)$ | $\dfrac{KA}{\tau} e^{-t/\tau}$ | Decays to zero |
| Sinusoid $A\sin\omega t$ | $A\sin\omega t$ | $\dfrac{KA}{\sqrt{1+(\omega\tau)^2}}\sin(\omega t - \phi)$ | Steady-state sinusoid with phase lag $\phi = \arctan(\omega\tau)$ |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

**Define zeroth order instrument. Give two examples and state its transfer function.**

**Model Answer:**

A zeroth order instrument is a measurement system in which the output is **instantaneously and exactly proportional** to the input, with **no energy storage elements** and hence no time-dependent dynamic behavior.

**Equation:**
$$\theta_o(t) = K \cdot \theta_i(t)$$

**Transfer function:**
$$G(s) = \dfrac{\theta_o(s)}{\theta_i(s)} = K$$

where $K$ is the static sensitivity, a constant.

**Examples (any two):**
1. An ideal linear **potentiometer** measuring linear displacement (no moving mass, no friction, no inductance).
2. An ideal **resistive voltage divider** measuring DC voltage.
3. An ideal **strain gauge** in a purely static (no inertia) loading condition.

**Mark Distribution (as per KTU valuation key):**
- Definition with equation: 1.5 marks
- Two examples: 1 mark
- Transfer function: 0.5 mark

---

### Question 2 **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**Define time constant of a first order instrument. What percentage of the final value is reached at $t = \tau$, $t = 3\tau$, and $t = 5\tau$?**

**Model Answer:**

The **time constant $\tau$** of a first order instrument is defined as the time required for the step response to reach **63.2%** of its final steady-state value, *if* the response continued at its initial rate of change. Equivalently, $\tau$ is the constant in the governing differential equation $\tau \dfrac{d\theta_o}{dt} + \theta_o = K\theta_i$.

For a step input of magnitude $A$, the response is
$$\theta_o(t) = KA\bigl(1 - e^{-t/\tau}\bigr)$$

**Values at specific times:**

| Time $t$ | $\theta_o(t)$ | % of Final Value $KA$ |
|---|---|---|
| $\tau$ | $KA(1 - e^{-1}) = 0.632\,KA$ | **63.2%** |
| $3\tau$ | $KA(1 - e^{-3}) = 0.950\,KA$ | **95.0%** |
| $5\tau$ | $KA(1 - e^{-5}) = 0.993\,KA$ | **99.3%** |

**Mark Distribution:**
- Definition of $\tau$: 1 mark
- Step response equation: 1 mark
- Three percentage values: 1 mark (0.5+0.25+0.25 or similar)

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A (Choice 1) **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Question]**

**(a) Derive the step response of a first order instrument. Hence explain the term "time constant" and "settling time" with reference to the response curve.** **(7 marks)**

**(b) A thermocouple used to measure the temperature of a furnace has a thermal time constant of 8 seconds and a static sensitivity of 0.08 mV/°C. The furnace temperature is suddenly changed from 200 °C to 500 °C. Find: (i) the initial rate of change of thermocouple output voltage, (ii) the voltage at $t = 4$ s, (iii) the time required for the output to reach 99% of its final value, and (iv) the steady-state output voltage.** **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for (a)

**Derivation of step response:**

A first order instrument obeys:
$$\tau \dfrac{d\theta_o(t)}{dt} + \theta_o(t) = K\theta_i(t)$$

For a step input of magnitude $A$ at $t=0$, $\theta_i(t) = A u(t)$.

Taking Laplace transform with zero initial conditions:
$$(\tau s + 1)\theta_o(s) = K \cdot \dfrac{A}{s}$$

$$\theta_o(s) = \dfrac{KA}{s(\tau s + 1)}$$

Partial fractions:
$$\theta_o(s) = \dfrac{KA}{s} - \dfrac{KA}{s + 1/\tau}$$

Inverse Laplace:
$$\theta_o(t) = KA\bigl(1 - e^{-t/\tau}\bigr), \quad t \geq 0$$

**Time constant $\tau$:** It is the time taken for the step response to reach $(1 - e^{-1}) = 0.632$ or **63.2%** of its final value $KA$. Geometrically, it is the time intercept of the tangent drawn to the response curve at $t = 0$, with the final-value line $y = KA$.

**Settling time $t_s$:** It is the time taken for the response to enter and remain within a specified percentage (typically 2% or 5%) of the final value. For first order systems, $t_s \approx 4\tau$ (2% criterion) or $t_s \approx 3\tau$ (5% criterion).

---

#### Model Solution for (b)

**Given data:**
- $\tau = 8$ s
- $K = 0.08$ mV/°C
- $\theta_i$ step from 200 °C to 500 °C, so step magnitude $A = 500 - 200 = 300$ °C

**Final value of output:**
$$\theta_o(\infty) = KA = 0.08 \times 300 = 24 \text{ mV}$$

**Step response equation:**
$$\theta_o(t) = 24\bigl(1 - e^{-t/8}\bigr) \text{ mV}$$

**(i) Initial rate of change of output voltage:**

$$\dfrac{d\theta_o}{dt} = 24 \cdot \dfrac{1}{8} e^{-t/8} = 3 e^{-t/8} \text{ mV/s}$$

At $t = 0$:
$$\left.\dfrac{d\theta_o}{dt}\right\rvert_{t=0} = 3 \text{ mV/s}$$

**Verification via shortcut formula:** $\dfrac{KA}{\tau} = \dfrac{24}{8} = 3$ mV/s ✓

**(ii) Voltage at $t = 4$ s:**

$$\theta_o(4) = 24\bigl(1 - e^{-4/8}\bigr) = 24\bigl(1 - e^{-0.5}\bigr)$$

$$= 24(1 - 0.6065) = 24 \times 0.3935 = 9.444 \text{ mV}$$

**(iii) Time to reach 99% of final value:**

Set $\theta_o(t) = 0.99 \times 24 = 23.76$ mV:

$$0.99 = 1 - e^{-t/8}$$

$$e^{-t/8} = 0.01$$

$$-\dfrac{t}{8} = \ln(0.01) = -4.6052$$

$$t = 8 \times 4.6052 = 36.84 \text{ s}$$

**(iv) Steady-state output voltage:**

$$\theta_{o,ss} = KA = 24 \text{ mV}$$

**Mark Allocation (7 marks for part b):**
- Identification of step magnitude and final value: 1 mark
- (i) Initial slope calculation: 2 marks
- (ii) Voltage at $t=4$ s: 1.5 marks
- (iii) Time to 99% (requires $\ln$ step): 2 marks
- (iv) Steady-state value: 0.5 mark

---

### Question B (Choice 2 — Alternative) **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question]**

**(a) With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the general first order instrument. Derive the expression for its step response and sketch the response curve, clearly marking the time constant and the steady-state value.** **(7 marks)**

**(b) The temperature of a liquid bath is measured using a first order mercury thermometer having a time constant of 12 s. The bath temperature changes linearly at a rate of 2 °C/min. Determine: (i) the steady-state error (velocity lag) of the thermometer, (ii) the time required for the thermometer to come within 1 °C of the true bath temperature, and (iii) the maximum possible error if the ramp continues for a very long time.** **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for (a)

**Block diagram and concept:** (Discuss standard form as in Section 4.2 above.)

A first order instrument has one energy storage element. The block diagram consists of the input feeding into a comparator (summing junction) that compares the input with the unity feedback from the output. The error is multiplied by static sensitivity $K$ and passed through the storage element with transfer function $\dfrac{1}{\tau s + 1}$.

**Step response derivation:** (Same derivation as in Question A, part a.)

$$\theta_o(t) = KA\bigl(1 - e^{-t/\tau}\bigr)$$

**Sketch description:** Curve starts at origin, rises with initial slope $KA/\tau$, asymptotically approaches the horizontal line $\theta_o = KA$. Mark the point $(\tau, 0.632\,KA)$ and the line $\theta = KA$ as the asymptote.

---

#### Model Solution for (b)

**Given data:**
- $\tau = 12$ s
- Ramp rate $A = 2$ °C/min $= \dfrac{2}{60}$ °C/s $= \dfrac{1}{30}$ °C/s $\approx 0.0333$ °C/s
- Assume $K = 1$ °C/°C (thermometer reads directly in temperature)

**Ramp response equation:**

$$\theta_o(t) = KAt - KA\tau + KA\tau e^{-t/\tau}$$

$$\theta_o(t) = A(t - \tau) + A\tau e^{-t/\tau}$$

**Steady-state expression (as $t \to \infty$, exponential dies out):**

$$\theta_{o,ss}(t) = A t - A\tau$$

**(i) Steady-state error (velocity lag):**

$$e_{ss} = \theta_i(t) - \theta_{o,ss}(t) = At - (At - A\tau) = A\tau$$

$$e_{ss} = \dfrac{1}{30} \times 12 = \dfrac{12}{30} = 0.4 \text{ °C}$$

**(ii) Time to come within 1 °C of true temperature:**

The true bath temperature at time $t$ is $T_{bath}(t) = T_0 + At$, where $T_0$ is the initial temperature. The thermometer reading lags by the error $e(t)$:

$$e(t) = A\tau\bigl(1 - e^{-t/\tau}\bigr)$$

Set $e(t) = 1$ °C:
$$1 = 0.4\bigl(1 - e^{-t/12}\bigr)$$

$$\dfrac{1}{0.4} = 1 - e^{-t/12}$$

$$e^{-t/12} = 1 - 2.5 = -1.5$$

**Problem:** $e^{-t/12}$ is always positive, so we cannot reach 1 °C of error with the steady-state value being 0.4 °C. This means the error can **never exceed 0.4 °C** — the maximum error is bounded.

**Correction:** If the question asks for "within 1 °C", and the steady-state error is only 0.4 °C, then the thermometer *always* reads within 1 °C once steady state is reached. The time to *reach* the steady-state error bound is when $e(t)$ first equals a value approaching 0.4 °C, say 0.39 °C (within 1% of steady state):

$$0.39 = 0.4(1 - e^{-t/12})$$

$$e^{-t/12} = 0.025$$

$$t = -12 \ln(0.025) = 12 \times 3.689 = 44.27 \text{ s}$$

*(Note: If the exam intends a "1 °C error" with different parameters such that the steady-state error exceeds 1 °C, the same formula applies with $e(t) = 1$ in the left side.)*

**(iii) Maximum possible error:**

$$\lim_{t \to \infty} e(t) = A\tau = 0.4 \text{ °C}$$

**Mark Allocation (7 marks for part b):**
- Ramp rate conversion and ramp response equation: 1.5 marks
- (i) Steady-state error: 2 marks
- (ii) Time calculation (with correct interpretation of bounded error): 2.5 marks
- (iii) Maximum error: 1 mark

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cause Mark Deductions in KTU 2024 Evaluations:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the step magnitude in ramp problems:** When a bath "changes from 200 °C to 500 °C", the step is **300 °C**, not 500 °C. Reading the "from" value as zero loses 1–2 marks.
>
> 2. **Unit mismatch in ramp rate conversion:** $A$ in the ramp response equation $x_i = At$ must be in measurand **per second**, not per minute. Convert $2$ °C/min $\to 1/30$ °C/s *before* plugging in.
>
> 3. **Confusing $\tau$ with $1/\tau$:** Writing $RC$ as $1/(RC)$ is a fatal error in any time-constant or frequency-response problem. The time constant is the **product** of $R$ and $C$, and the cutoff frequency is the **inverse** of $\tau$.
>
> 4. **Skipping the partial fraction step:** Examiners award 2–3 marks specifically for showing the partial fraction expansion. Writing only the final answer without derivation loses those marks.
>
> 5. **Wrong settling-time criterion:** $4\tau$ is for the **2%** criterion. If the question specifies 1% or 5%, use $4.6\tau$ or $3\tau$ respectively. KTU answer keys are strict on this.
>
> 6. **Forgetting the $\delta(t)$ area in impulse problems:** The impulse has *zero duration* but finite area. Treating the impulse as a "very fast step" is a common conceptual error.
>
> 7. **Sign error in ramp lag:** The ramp error is **positive** (output lags input), and the lag is $KA\tau$, **not** $-KA\tau$.
>
> 8. **Failing to convert logarithms correctly:** $e^{-t/\tau} = 0.01$ gives $t/\tau = \ln(100) = 4.605$, not $\log_{10}$. Many students use $\log_{10}$ by mistake.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **Print this checklist and revise 24 hours before the KTU exam.**

- [x] **Zeroth order = algebraic equation, no energy storage, transfer function $G(s) = K$, infinite bandwidth, zero phase shift.**
- [x] **First order = one energy storage element, ODE $\tau \dot{\theta}_o + \theta_o = K\theta_i$, transfer function $G(s) = K/(\tau s + 1)$.**
- [x] **Pole of first order system is at $s = -1/\tau$ — always in the left-half plane → stable.**
- [x] **Step response: $\theta_o(t) = KA(1 - e^{-t/\tau})$; reaches 63.2% at $t=\tau$, 95% at $t=3\tau$, 98.2% at $t=4\tau$, 99.3% at $t=5\tau$.**
- [x] **Initial slope of step response = $KA/\tau$ (this defines $\tau$ geometrically).**
- [x] **Ramp response: $\theta_o(t) = KAt - KA\tau + KA\tau e^{-t/\tau}$; steady-state ramp error = $KA\tau$ (velocity lag).**
- [x] **Impulse response: $\theta_o(t) = (KA/\tau) e^{-t/\tau}$; area under curve = $KA$.**
- [x] **Settling time (2% criterion) = $4\tau$; (1% criterion) = $4.6\tau$.**
- [x] **Rise time (10% → 90%) = $2.2\tau$.**
- [x] **Cutoff frequency $\omega_c = 1/\tau$ → $-3$ dB point, phase = $-45°$.**
- [x] **High-frequency roll-off = $-20$ dB/decade; phase approaches $-90°$.**
- [x] **Static sensitivity $K$ = ratio of final output to input amplitude for step input.**
- [x] **Time constant formulas:** Electrical: $\tau = RC$; Thermal: $\tau = C_{th}/(hA)$; Mechanical (dashpot-spring): $\tau = c/k$.
- [x] **Examples of zeroth order:** ideal potentiometer, resistive voltage divider, ideal strain gauge.
- [x] **Examples of first order:** mercury thermometer, RC filter, liquid manometer, thermistor, RL circuit.
- [x] **Why first order matters:** datasheets quote $\tau$ and settling times; Nyquist uses cutoff frequency; compensators use $\tau$ to flatten response.
- [x] **Master the partial-fraction method** — it is the gateway derivation for step, ramp, and impulse responses.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Resistive Transducers for Liquid Level and Humidity
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET411 - ELECTRONIC INSTRUMENTATION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Resistive Transducers for Liquid Level and Humidity

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Resistive Transducers — Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Resistive Transducer (KTU Definition):**
> A *resistive transducer* is a sensing device that converts a non-electrical physical quantity (such as displacement, liquid level, humidity, force, or pressure) into a proportional change in electrical **resistance**, which can be measured using a Wheatstone bridge, ohmmeter, or appropriate signal-conditioning circuit.

The general governing relation is:

$$R = \rho \cdot \frac{L}{A}$$

where **ρ** = resistivity of the sensing element (Ω·m), **L** = effective length of the conducting path (m), and **A** = cross-sectional area of the conducting path (m²). Any measurand that alters **ρ**, **L**, or **A** produces a usable output.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Variable Water Tap"

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Intuition Check:**
> Imagine a water tap that you rotate to control flow. The more you rotate, the more water passes through. A resistive transducer works on the same logic — the physical quantity (level/humidity) *physically rotates* or *slides* an internal contact, changing the length of resistance wire in the circuit. Measure the resistance, and you indirectly know the physical quantity.

A water-tank float is a perfect real-world example: as water rises, a float slides a wiper up a long resistor strip. The resistance seen by the measuring instrument is directly proportional to the water level.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

> [!NOTE]
> - **Resistivity (ρ)** of pure water ≈ **18.2 MΩ·cm** at 25 °C (very low conductivity).
> - **Conductivity (σ) = 1/ρ** for a conductive liquid (e.g., sea water ≈ **4 S/m**, tap water ≈ **0.05 S/m**).
> - **Relative Humidity (RH)** is a *dimensionless* ratio expressed in **% RH**.
> - Standard reference: **0 % RH (perfectly dry)** to **100 % RH (saturation)**.

## 1.4 Why Resistive Transducers for Level & Humidity?

Both **liquid level** and **humidity** are measurands that can be *naturally transduced* into resistance change because:

- **Liquid Level** → the height of a conductive liquid alters the *effective immersed length* of electrodes (resistance inversely proportional to immersed length).
- **Humidity** → water vapour absorbed by a *hygroscopic* material alters its **resistivity (ρ)** by several orders of magnitude.

This makes resistive sensing both **simple** and **low-cost** compared to ultrasonic, optical, or capacitive alternatives.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Resistive Transducers for Liquid Level — Three Engineering Variants

### 2.1.1 Float-Operated Resistance Transducer (Potentiometric Level Sensor)

**Construction:**

- A vertical cylindrical/rectangular resistance element of total length **L** and total resistance **R₀** is fixed inside the tank.
- A **float** (hollow metal/plastic sphere) rides on the liquid surface.
- The float is mechanically coupled to a **sliding wiper (brush contact)** through a pivoted lever.
- A constant excitation voltage **V_ex** is applied across the full resistance strip.

**Working Principle — Step-by-Step Logic:**

1. As liquid level **h** rises, the float rises proportionally.
2. The lever mechanism translates the float's vertical motion into horizontal motion of the wiper.
3. The wiper taps a point along the resistance strip at height **h**.
4. The effective resistance measured between the wiper and the bottom terminal is:

$$R_x = \frac{R_0}{L} \cdot h$$

5. The output voltage (in a potentiometric divider) is:

$$V_{out} = V_{ex} \cdot \frac{h}{L}$$

6. Hence the **transducer behaves as a linear voltage divider** whose output is directly proportional to liquid level.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Output Linearity:** Potentiometric float sensors are inherently **linear** provided the resistance strip has uniform cross-section and resistivity. Non-linearity arises only from contact resistance variations and end-effects.

---

### 2.1.2 Resistance Tape (Submersible Probe) Level Sensor

**Construction:**

- A long, thin **resistance tape** (e.g., nickel-chrome wire) is suspended vertically inside the tank.
- The tape is enclosed in a perforated protective sheath that allows liquid contact but protects the wire mechanically.
- A heavy **anchor weight** keeps the tape taut.
- The tape acts as one arm of a **Wheatstone bridge**.

**Working Principle:**

1. The portion of tape *above* the liquid surface is in dry air — its resistance is high and constant.
2. The portion of tape *below* the liquid surface is in thermal contact with the (cooler) liquid. The cooling effect **reduces** the tape's resistivity.
3. Alternatively, in some designs, the immersed portion forms a low-resistance path because the conductive liquid itself shorts parallel resistance.
4. The net resistance change **ΔR** is proportional to the immersed length, hence the level.

$$R_{net} = R_{dry} + R_{wet}(h)$$

$$R_{wet}(h) = R_0 \cdot \frac{h}{L}$$

where **h** is the immersed length.

---

### 2.1.3 Conductivity (Electrode) Type Level Sensor

**Construction:**

- Two or more **stainless steel electrodes** of different lengths are inserted vertically into the tank.
- The conductive liquid (water, acid, etc.) acts as the **electrical conductor** between electrodes.
- An **AC excitation** is used (DC causes electrolysis and electrode damage).

**Working Principle:**

1. When liquid touches the tip of an electrode, the electrical circuit between that electrode and the common (ground) electrode is completed through the conductive liquid.
2. The resistance of the liquid column between electrodes is:

$$R_{liquid} = \rho \cdot \frac{d}{A_{eff}}$$

where **d** = inter-electrode distance, **A_eff** = effective cross-sectional area of the liquid path.

3. For a **continuous level measurement**, two long parallel electrodes of length **L** are used. The resistance between them decreases as more electrode length is immersed:

$$\frac{1}{R} = \sigma \cdot \frac{A_{eff}}{L} \cdot h$$

Equivalently:

$$R(h) = \frac{L}{\sigma \cdot A_{eff} \cdot h}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Conductivity sensors only work for CONDUCTIVE liquids.** They cannot sense the level of oil, petrol, or deionized water. Always check the conductivity of the process fluid before selection.

---

## 2.2 Resistive Transducers for Humidity (Resistive Hygrometers)

### 2.2.1 Working Principle

> [!NOTE]
> A **resistive humidity sensor** uses a *hygroscopic* material whose electrical resistivity changes predictably with the amount of water vapour absorbed from the surrounding air.

When water molecules adsorb onto the sensing film:

- **Ionic conduction** is enhanced (H⁺ and OH⁻ ions from dissociated water act as charge carriers).
- The resistance of the film drops by **3 to 5 orders of magnitude** between dry and saturated conditions.

### 2.2.2 Common Hygroscopic Sensing Materials

| Material | Mechanism | Typical Resistance Range (0-100% RH) |
|----------|-----------|--------------------------------------|
| **Lithium Chloride (LiCl)** | Salt absorbs water, ionic conduction | 1 MΩ → 1 kΩ |
| **Carbon-impregnated polymer** | Polymer swells, carbon particles separate | 10 MΩ → 1 kΩ |
| **Aluminium Oxide (Al₂O₃)** | Porous oxide adsorbs water, ionic conduction | 1 GΩ → 1 MΩ |
| **Doped semiconductor (e.g., SnO₂)** | Surface chemisorption of water | 100 MΩ → 10 kΩ |

### 2.2.3 Mathematical Model

The resistance-humidity characteristic is **highly non-linear** and best described by an exponential model:

$$R(RH) = R_0 \cdot e^{\gamma \cdot (1 - RH/100)}$$

where:
- **R₀** = resistance at 100 % RH (saturated),
- **γ** = material-dependent sensitivity constant (typically 3–6),
- **RH** = relative humidity in percent.

A linearised approximation near mid-range is:

$$R \approx R_{50} \cdot \left[ 1 - \alpha \cdot (RH - 50) \right]$$

where **R₅₀** is resistance at 50 % RH and **α** is the linearised sensitivity coefficient (≈ 0.6 % per % RH for LiCl).

### 2.2.4 Construction

- Two **interdigitated electrodes** (comb pattern) are deposited on a ceramic substrate using thick-film or thin-film techniques.
- The hygroscopic film is coated over the electrode pattern.
- A **polymer filter membrane** protects the sensor from dust and liquid water.
- AC excitation (typically 1 kHz to 10 kHz) prevents polarization of the hygroscopic film.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Equations for KTU 2024 ESE — Module 1**

| # | Equation | Quantity | Units | Notes |
|---|----------|----------|-------|-------|
| 1 | $R = \rho \cdot L / A$ | Basic resistance | Ω | Foundation equation |
| 2 | $R_x = (R_0 / L) \cdot h$ | Float-type level | Ω | h = level, L = strip length |
| 3 | $V_{out} = V_{ex} \cdot (h/L)$ | Potentiometric output | V | Linear with level |
| 4 | $R_{liq} = \rho \cdot d / A_{eff}$ | Conductivity probe | Ω | ρ of liquid, d = gap |
| 5 | $R(h) = L / (\sigma \cdot A_{eff} \cdot h)$ | Immersed electrode | Ω | σ = 1/ρ of liquid |
| 6 | $R(RH) = R_0 \cdot e^{\gamma \cdot (1 - RH/100)}$ | Humidity sensor | Ω | Exponential model |
| 7 | $RH = (p_{water}/p_{saturation}) \cdot 100$ | Definition of RH | % | p = partial pressure (Pa) |
| 8 | $G = \sigma \cdot A / L$ | Conductance form | S | Reciprocal of (4) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Application Real-World Footnote:** Float-type resistive sensors dominate domestic water-tank level indicators (₹200–₹500 market segment in India). Conductivity probes are used in industrial boiler drum level alarms. Resistive humidity sensors (LiCl type) are found in HVAC controllers, weather stations, and pharmaceutical cleanrooms.

---

## 2.4 Comparative Engineering Selection Table

| Property | Float Resistive | Conductivity Probe | Resistive Humidity |
|----------|----------------|--------------------|--------------------|
| **Output** | Resistance / Voltage | Resistance (binary or continuous) | Resistance |
| **Range** | Full tank height | Discrete points / continuous | 0 – 100 % RH |
| **Linearity** | Excellent (linear) | Step / non-linear | Exponential (needs linearisation) |
| **Power consumption** | Nil (passive) | Requires AC excitation | Requires AC excitation |
| **Sensitivity to contamination** | Moderate (wiper wear) | High (electrode fouling) | Moderate (filter clogging) |
| **Cost (relative)** | Low | Very low | Low to moderate |
