# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Float-Type Potentiometric Level Sensor — Output Transfer Function

**Problem Setup:**
A resistance strip of total length **L = 2 m** and total resistance **R₀ = 10 kΩ** is excited by a stable DC source **V_ex = 10 V**. The wiper is at height **h**. Derive the relation between **V_out** and **h**, and compute **V_out** when the water level is **h = 1.4 m**.

**Step 1 — Resistance of the immersed portion (from bottom terminal to wiper):**
Since the strip is uniform, resistance per unit length is constant:

$$r = \frac{R_0}{L} \quad \left[ \frac{\Omega}{\text{m}} \right]$$

Therefore the resistance between the wiper and the bottom terminal, when the wiper is at height **h**, is:

$$R_x = r \cdot h = \frac{R_0}{L} \cdot h$$

**Step 2 — Substitute numerical values to find R_x at h = 1.4 m:**

$$R_x = \frac{10\,000}{2} \cdot 1.4 = 5\,000 \cdot 1.4 = 7\,000\ \Omega = 7\ \text{k}\Omega$$

**Step 3 — Potentiometric divider equation:**
With **V_ex** applied across the full strip and **V_out** measured between the wiper and the bottom (ground) terminal:

$$V_{out} = V_{ex} \cdot \frac{R_x}{R_0}$$

**Step 4 — Combine equations to obtain a clean transfer function:**

$$V_{out} = V_{ex} \cdot \frac{(R_0/L) \cdot h}{R_0} = V_{ex} \cdot \frac{h}{L}$$

This is the canonical result:

$$\boxed{V_{out} = V_{ex} \cdot \frac{h}{L}}$$

**Step 5 — Numerical evaluation at h = 1.4 m, L = 2 m, V_ex = 10 V:**

$$V_{out} = 10 \cdot \frac{1.4}{2} = 10 \cdot 0.7 = 7\ \text{V}$$

**Step 6 — Sensitivity calculation (for KTU numerical-type questions):**

$$S = \frac{dV_{out}}{dh} = \frac{V_{ex}}{L} = \frac{10}{2} = 5\ \frac{\text{V}}{\text{m}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation:** Every 1 metre of water level produces a 5 V change in output voltage. This is the **static sensitivity** of the float-type level transducer.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Conductivity Probe Resistance for Two Parallel Vertical Electrodes

**Problem Setup:**
Two long parallel electrodes of length **L = 1.5 m**, separated by distance **d = 2 cm**, are immersed in a conductive liquid of conductivity **σ = 0.5 S/m**. The effective cross-sectional area of conduction is **A_eff = d × W**, where **W = 0.05 m** is the electrode width. Derive the resistance as a function of immersed length **h** and compute resistance at **h = 1 m**.

**Step 1 — Cross-sectional area of the conduction path:**

$$A_{eff} = d \cdot W = 0.02 \times 0.05 = 1 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{m}^2$$

**Step 2 — Conductance of immersed portion:**
The liquid column between the two parallel electrodes forms a rectangular conduction path of length **d**, cross-section **h × W**, and conductivity **σ**:

$$G = \sigma \cdot \frac{A_{eff}}{d} = \sigma \cdot \frac{h \cdot W}{d}$$

**Step 3 — Resistance of immersed portion:**

$$R(h) = \frac{1}{G} = \frac{d}{\sigma \cdot h \cdot W}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute numerical values at h = 1 m:**

$$R(1) = \frac{0.02}{0.5 \cdot 1 \cdot 0.05} = \frac{0.02}{0.025} = 0.8\ \Omega$$

**Step 5 — Sensitivity calculation:**

$$S = \left\vert \frac{dR}{dh} \right\vert = \frac{d}{\sigma \cdot h^2 \cdot W} = \frac{0.02}{0.5 \cdot 1 \cdot 0.05} = 0.8\ \frac{\Omega}{\text{m}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Note the inverse relationship:** Resistance is inversely proportional to immersed length **h**. This is the fundamental signature of a conductivity-based level sensor. As the level rises, more parallel conduction paths open up, reducing resistance.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Humidity Sensor Calibration — Linearised Sensitivity

**Problem Setup:**
A LiCl-based resistive humidity sensor has resistance **R₁ = 100 kΩ** at **RH₁ = 30 %** and **R₂ = 5 kΩ** at **RH₂ = 80 %**. Derive the linear sensitivity coefficient **α** in the model **R = R_ref [1 − α (RH − RH_ref)]** and compute the resistance at **RH = 60 %**.

**Step 1 — Choose a reference point. Let RH_ref = 30 %, R_ref = 100 kΩ:**

$$R = 100\,000 \cdot [1 - \alpha \cdot (RH - 30)]$$

**Step 2 — Apply the second data point (RH₂ = 80 %, R₂ = 5 kΩ):**

$$5\,000 = 100\,000 \cdot [1 - \alpha \cdot (80 - 30)]$$

**Step 3 — Simplify:**

$$\frac{5\,000}{100\,000} = 1 - 50 \alpha$$

$$0.05 = 1 - 50 \alpha$$

$$50 \alpha = 0.95$$

$$\alpha = 0.019\ \text{per \% RH}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Expressing the result in % form:** α ≈ 1.9 % per % RH. This matches the typical order of magnitude for commercial LiCl sensors.

**Step 4 — Compute R at RH = 60 %:**

$$R(60) = 100\,000 \cdot [1 - 0.019 \cdot (60 - 30)]$$

$$R(60) = 100\,000 \cdot [1 - 0.019 \cdot 30]$$

$$R(60) = 100\,000 \cdot [1 - 0.57] = 100\,000 \cdot 0.43 = 43\ \text{k}\Omega$$

**Step 5 — Check using the exponential model for comparison:**
Using **R = R₀ · e^(γ(1 − RH/100))** with **R(100) = R₀ = 1 kΩ** (typical for LiCl at saturation) and **R(30) = 100 kΩ**:

$$100\,000 = 1\,000 \cdot e^{\gamma \cdot (1 - 0.3)}$$

$$100 = e^{0.7 \gamma}$$

$$\gamma = \frac{\ln 100}{0.7} = \frac{4.6052}{0.7} \approx 6.58$$

Then at **RH = 60 %**:

$$R(60) = 1\,000 \cdot e^{6.58 \cdot (1 - 0.6)} = 1\,000 \cdot e^{6.58 \cdot 0.4} = 1\,000 \cdot e^{2.632} \approx 1\,000 \cdot 13.9 = 13.9\ \text{k}\Omega$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Discrepancy Note:** The linear and exponential models give different predictions (43 kΩ vs 13.9 kΩ). For KTU exam purposes, students are expected to state clearly which model is being used. Real sensors require **piecewise linearisation** or a **log-amp** in the signal conditioner to give accurate readings across the full 0–100 % RH range.

---

## 3.4 Quick-Reference Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> **The "If-Asked" Decision Tree for KTU Numerical Problems**

| Symptom in Question | Required Equation |
|---------------------|-------------------|
| "Wiper / float / potentiometric level" | $V_{out} = V_{ex} \cdot h/L$ |
| "Conductivity of liquid / electrodes dipped" | $R = d / (\sigma \cdot h \cdot W)$ |
| "Resistance variation with humidity / RH" | $R = R_0 \cdot e^{\gamma(1 - RH/100)}$ |
| "Sensitivity of sensor" | $S = dV_{out}/dh$ or $S = \vert dR/dh \vert$ |
| "Calibration curve" | Two-point linear interpolation |

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Generic Resistive Transducer Signal Chain

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Physical Quantity: Level or Humidity] --> B[Resistive Sensing Element]
    B --> C[Resistance Change: dR]
    C --> D[Signal Conditioning: Wheatstone Bridge]
    D --> E[Amplifier: Instrumentation Amp]
    E --> F[ADC and Display or Controller]
    F --> G[Output: Calibrated Engineering Units]
```

## 4.2 Schematic — Float-Operated Resistive Level Sensor

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    Tank[Tank with Liquid] --> Float[Floater: Hollow Sphere]
    Float --> Lever[Pivoted Lever Arm]
    Lever --> Wiper[Sliding Wiper Contact]
    Wiper --> Strip[Vertical Resistance Strip R0]
    Strip --> Vex[Excitation Voltage Vex: 5V to 24V DC]
    Strip --> Vout[Output Voltage Vout to Indicator]
    Vout --> Meter[Digital Voltmeter or PLC Input]
```

## 4.3 Schematic — Conductivity Probe Level Sensor

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    L1[Liquid in Tank] --> E1[Electrode 1: Long]
    L1 --> E2[Electrode 2: Common Ground]
    E1 --> R[R liquid: Variable]
    E2 --> R
    R --> AC[AC Excitation Source: 1 kHz to 10 kHz]
    AC --> Det[Detector Circuit: Comparator or ADC]
    Det --> Ind[Level Indicator or Alarm]
```

## 4.4 Schematic — Resistive Humidity Sensor Cross-Section (Functional)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    Env[Surrounding Air with Water Vapour] --> Filter[Polymer Filter Membrane]
    Filter --> Hyd[Active Hygroscopic Film: LiCl or Polymer]
    Hyd --> E1[Interdigitated Electrode 1]
    Hyd --> E2[Interdigitated Electrode 2]
    E1 --> AC[AC Excitation: 1 kHz to 10 kHz]
    E2 --> AC
    AC --> Bridge[Wheatstone Bridge with Reference Resistor]
    Bridge --> Amp[Instrumentation Amplifier]
    Amp --> RH[Digital RH Display: percent RH]
```

## 4.5 Functional Comparison Flow — Selection Logic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Need to Measure Level or Humidity] --> Q1{Is the Liquid Conductive?}

    Q2 -->|Yes| CondPath[Use Conductivity Probe with Two Long Electrodes]
    Q2 -->|No| Q3{Need Discrete Level Alarms?}
    Q3 -->|Yes| MultiElec[Use Multi-Electrode Conductivity Probe]
    Q3 -->|No| FloatPath
    Start --> Q4{Need to Measure Humidity?}
    Q4 -->|Yes| HumPath[Use Resistive Hygrometer with Hygroscopic Film]
    HumPath --> Range{Range and Accuracy?}

```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 PART A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Question]**
*Define a resistive transducer. List any two examples of resistive transducers used for non-electrical measurement.*

**Model Answer:**

A resistive transducer is a device that converts a non-electrical physical quantity into a proportional change in electrical resistance. The change in resistance can be measured using a Wheatstone bridge or an ohmmeter, and the magnitude of the change corresponds to the magnitude of the physical quantity.

Two examples are:

1. **Float-type level sensor** — used to measure the level of a liquid in a tank.
2. **Resistive humidity sensor (hygrometer)** — used to measure the relative humidity of air.

(3 marks — 1 mark for definition + 1 mark each for two examples.)

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Question]**
*State the principle of operation of a conductivity-type liquid level sensor. Why is AC excitation used instead of DC?*

**Model Answer:**

**Principle:** Two electrodes are immersed in a conductive liquid. The resistance of the liquid column between the electrodes depends on the immersed length. As the liquid level rises, the effective conduction path length increases, reducing the resistance between the electrodes.

**Why AC excitation:** Direct current causes **electrolysis** of the liquid and **polarization** at the electrode surface, leading to corrosion, drift, and unstable readings. AC excitation (typically 1 kHz) reverses polarity rapidly, preventing net electrochemical reactions and ensuring long-term stability.

(3 marks — 2 marks for principle + 1 mark for AC reason.)

---

## 5.2 PART B Questions (14 Marks) — Internal Choice

### **Question A (14 Marks) — Option 1**

**[KTU University Exam — Past Model, Dec 2023 style]**

**a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the construction and working of a **float-type resistive transducer** for liquid level measurement. Derive the transfer relation between output voltage and liquid level. (7 marks)

**b)** Explain the construction and working of a **resistive humidity sensor**. Discuss the role of hygroscopic materials and derive the resistance-RH characteristic equation. (7 marks)

---

#### **Model Solution — Part (a):**

**Construction:** A float-type resistive level sensor consists of a vertical resistance strip (uniform resistance per unit length **R₀/L**), a hollow float that rests on the liquid surface, a pivoted lever arm, and a sliding wiper contact. An excitation voltage **V_ex** is applied across the strip.

**Working:** As the liquid level rises, the float rises. The lever transmits this motion to the wiper, which slides up the strip. The wiper position is proportional to the liquid level **h**.

**Derivation:** With the bottom terminal grounded and V_ex applied across the full strip, the resistance between the wiper and ground is:

$$R_x = \frac{R_0}{L} \cdot h$$

The output voltage (potential divider) is:

$$V_{out} = V_{ex} \cdot \frac{R_x}{R_0} = V_{ex} \cdot \frac{h}{L}$$

This is a **linear** relation. The sensitivity is:

$$S = \frac{V_{ex}}{L}$$

> (7 marks — 2 for construction, 3 for working, 2 for derivation)

---

#### **Model Solution — Part (b):**

**Construction:** A resistive humidity sensor consists of a ceramic or silicon substrate on which two **interdigitated comb electrodes** are deposited. A thin **hygroscopic film** (e.g., lithium chloride, carbon-loaded polymer, or aluminium oxide) is coated over the electrodes. A protective porous polymer membrane allows water vapour to diffuse in but blocks liquid water and dust.

**Working Principle:** When water molecules from the surrounding air are absorbed by the hygroscopic film, they dissociate into H⁺ and OH⁻ ions, which act as charge carriers. The ionic conductivity rises sharply, dropping the resistance of the film by 3–5 orders of magnitude between 0 % RH and 100 % RH.

**Characteristic Equation:** The resistance varies exponentially with humidity:

$$R(RH) = R_0 \cdot e^{\gamma \cdot (1 - RH/100)}$$

where **R₀** is the resistance at saturation (100 % RH) and **γ** is a material-dependent constant (typically 3–6).

> [!NOTE]
> A linearised approximation useful in mid-range is **R = R_ref · [1 − α (RH − RH_ref)]** where α is the linearised sensitivity.

**AC excitation (1 kHz to 10 kHz)** is used to prevent ion polarization that would otherwise occur under DC.

> (7 marks — 2 for construction, 3 for working, 2 for characteristic equation)

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Option 2 (Internal Choice)**

**[KTU University Exam — Past Model, May 2024 style]**

**a)** Describe the **conductivity-type level sensor** with a neat diagram. Derive the relation between the resistance of the conductive liquid column and the immersed length of the electrodes. (7 marks)

**b)** A float-operated resistive level sensor has a strip resistance of **8 kΩ** spread over a length of **2 m**. It is excited by a **12 V DC** source. Calculate the output voltage when the water level is at **1.5 m**. Also determine the sensitivity of the sensor. (7 marks)

---

#### **Model Solution — Part (a):**

**Diagram Description:** A conductivity-type level sensor uses two parallel stainless steel electrodes suspended vertically in the tank. The conductive liquid between them forms a parallel conduction path whose resistance depends on the immersed length.

**Derivation:** For two parallel electrodes of width **W**, separated by distance **d**, immersed to a depth **h** in a liquid of conductivity **σ**, the cross-sectional area of the conduction path is:

$$A_{eff} = W \cdot h$$

The resistance of the liquid column is:

$$R = \rho \cdot \frac{d}{A_{eff}} = \frac{1}{\sigma} \cdot \frac{d}{W \cdot h} = \frac{d}{\sigma \cdot W \cdot h}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The resistance is **inversely proportional** to the immersed depth **h** — a key signature of conductivity-type sensors.

For multi-level detection, electrodes of graded lengths are used, and the resistance steps as liquid touches each electrode tip.

> (7 marks — 2 for diagram, 3 for derivation, 2 for multi-level discussion)

---

#### **Model Solution — Part (b):**

**Given:**
- R₀ = 8 kΩ, L = 2 m, V_ex = 12 V, h = 1.5 m

**Step 1 — Resistance per unit length:**

$$r = \frac{R_0}{L} = \frac{8\,000}{2} = 4\,000\ \Omega/\text{m}$$

**Step 2 — Resistance at h = 1.5 m:**

$$R_x = r \cdot h = 4\,000 \cdot 1.5 = 6\,000\ \Omega = 6\ \text{k}\Omega$$

**Step 3 — Output voltage using the divider formula:**

$$V_{out} = V_{ex} \cdot \frac{h}{L} = 12 \cdot \frac{1.5}{2} = 12 \cdot 0.75 = 9\ \text{V}$$

**Step 4 — Sensitivity of the sensor:**

$$S = \frac{V_{ex}}{L} = \frac{12}{2} = 6\ \text{V/m}$$

> (7 marks — 2 marks each for the first three steps and 1 mark for sensitivity)

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Traps Identified by KTU Board Examiners:**
>
> 1. **Confusing units:** Writing resistance as "R = ρL" without the area **A** is a guaranteed ½–1 mark penalty. Always state **R = ρL/A**.
> 2. **Forgetting AC excitation justification:** Stating that "AC is used to avoid electrolysis" without explaining polarization is incomplete. Full credit needs both the electrolysis and polarization points.
> 3. **Wrong inverse relationship:** Students often write **R ∝ h** for conductivity sensors. The correct relation is **R ∝ 1/h** (resistance is *inversely* proportional to immersed length). This is a high-frequency error.
> 4. **Mixing potentiometric and Wheatstone bridge forms:** A float-type sensor is naturally a **potentiometric divider**, not a Wheatstone bridge. Don't apply bridge equations unless the strip is one arm of a bridge.
> 5. **Linear vs exponential humidity model:** Always state which model you are using. The exponential form is the physically correct one; the linear form is an approximation valid only over a narrow RH window (e.g., 30–70 %).
> 6. **Hygroscopic material choice:** Naming "LiCl" is good, but mentioning *why* it works (ionic conduction from dissociated water) is what gets full marks.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Print & Carry)**
>
> ✅ A **resistive transducer** converts a physical quantity into a resistance change governed by **R = ρL/A**.
>
> ✅ **Three types of resistive level sensors:**
> - *Float-type*: wiper on a resistance strip → linear output **V_out = V_ex · h/L**
> - *Resistance tape*: immersed portion changes effective resistance
> - *Conductivity probe*: liquid itself is the conductor → **R = d / (σ·W·h)** (inverse with h)
>
> ✅ **Conductivity sensors** only work for conductive liquids and **must use AC excitation** to prevent electrolysis.
>
> ✅ **Resistive humidity sensors** rely on **hygroscopic materials** (LiCl, conductive polymer, Al₂O₃) whose resistance drops 3–5 orders of magnitude between 0 % and 100 % RH.
>
> ✅ The **exponential model** **R = R₀ · e^(γ(1 − RH/100))** is the canonical RH-resistance relation; the linear form is an approximation.
>
> ✅ **Sensors use AC excitation** at 1 kHz–10 kHz to prevent ionic polarization of the hygroscopic film.
>
> ✅ **Sensitivity of a float-type level sensor** is **S = V_ex / L** (V per metre).
>
> ✅ **Interdigitated electrode geometry** maximises the effective conduction area in a small footprint.
>
> ✅ **Common KTU exam keywords to recognise:**
> - "wiper / float / potentiometric" → use **V_out = V_ex · h/L**
> - "conductivity / electrodes dipped / AC excitation" → use **R = d / (σ·A)**
> - "RH / hygroscopic / LiCl" → use the **exponential humidity model**
>
> ✅ Always **state the model** (linear or exponential) and **justify AC vs DC** in any KTU answer worth more than 3 marks.

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET411 - ELECTRONIC INSTRUMENTATION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Inductive Transducers - Types and Basic Principles - LVDT - Synchro

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Inductive Transducers — Formal Definition

An **Inductive Transducer** is a passive electromechanical sensing device that converts a physical quantity (typically displacement, force, pressure, or position) into a measurable change in **inductance (L)**, **mutual inductance (M)**, or **induced electromotive force (EMF)**. The change is produced by a relative movement between a ferromagnetic core and a coil assembly, which alters the magnetic reluctance of the magnetic circuit.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 1):** Inductive transducers operate on the fundamental electromagnetic induction principle, given by **Faraday's Law of Electromagnetic Induction**: $\varepsilon = -N \frac{d\Phi}{dt}$, where **$\varepsilon$** is the induced EMF, **$N$** is the number of turns, and **$\Phi$** is the magnetic flux.

Mathematically, the inductance of a coil with **$N$** turns wound on a magnetic core is given by:

$$L = \frac{N^2}{\mathcal{R}} = \frac{\mu_0 \mu_r N^2 A}{l}$$

where **$\mathcal{R}$** is the magnetic reluctance, **$\mu_0 = 4\pi \times 10^{-7}\ \text{H/m}$** is the permeability of free space, **$\mu_r$** is the relative permeability of the core, **$A$** is the cross-sectional area, and **$l$** is the mean magnetic path length.

## 1.2 Classification of Inductive Transducers

Inductive transducers are broadly classified into **three categories** based on the way inductance is varied:

| S.No. | Type | Variable Parameter | Physical Mechanism |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Self-Inductance Type** | $L$ changes due to change in $\mathcal{R}$, $A$, $l$, or air gap | Movable core / plunger alters reluctance |
| 2 | **Mutual-Inductance Type** | $M$ changes between coupled coils | Differential movement between two coils (e.g., **LVDT**) |
| 3 | **Eddy Current Type** | Eddy current loss in target changes effective $L$ | Conductive target proximity alters coil impedance |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **Linear Variable Differential Transformer (LVDT)** and the **Synchro (a.k.a. Selsyn)** are the two **most frequently tested inductive transducers** in KTU End Semester Examinations. Both fall under the mutual-inductance category.

## 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — The "Water Valve" Picture

Imagine a **garden hose** connected to a water tap. The amount of water flowing out depends on **how much you open the valve** (analogous to the air-gap in an inductive transducer). 

- **More open valve → More flux → More inductance** (similar to closing the air-gap in a ferromagnetic core).
- **Less open valve → Less flux → Less inductance** (similar to opening the air-gap).

Now imagine **two such hoses (secondaries) wound over a primary coil**, with a sliding iron slug (the *core*) that can be pushed left or right. Depending on which direction the slug moves, the flux through one secondary increases while the other decreases. By **subtracting the two induced voltages** (differential action), we get a clean, linear voltage proportional to displacement. **This is exactly how an LVDT works!**

For the **Synchro**, picture a **steering wheel in a car**: turning the wheel on one end (transmitter) makes the wheel on the other end (receiver) turn by the same angle, transmitting angular information electrically across wires. The mechanical coupling is replaced by an inductive link through stator windings.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Physical Constants to Remember:**
> - Permeability of free space: $\mu_0 = 4\pi \times 10^{-7}\ \text{H/m}$
> - Relative permeability of iron: $\mu_r \approx 2000$ to **$6000$** (annealed)
> - Air has $\mu_r \approx 1$ (non-magnetic baseline)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Basic Principle of Inductive Transduction

The working of any inductive transducer rests on **Faraday's Law** and the **principle of variable reluctance**.

### 2.1.1 Self-Inductance Type — Variable Reluctance

When a ferromagnetic plunger moves inside a solenoid, the effective permeability of the magnetic path changes, which in turn varies the self-inductance $L$.

**Logic Steps:**
1. The magnetic circuit comprises an iron core + an air gap of length $x$.
2. Reluctance of the air gap dominates: $\mathcal{R}_{gap} = \dfrac{x}{\mu_0 A}$ (since $\mu_{air} = 1$).
3. As $x$ increases, $\mathcal{R}$ increases, hence $L = \dfrac{N^2}{\mathcal{R}}$ **decreases**.
4. This change is measured using an **AC bridge** (e.g., Maxwell-Wien bridge).

### 2.1.2 Mutual-Inductance Type — The LVDT

A **Linear Variable Differential Transformer (LVDT)** consists of:
- **One primary winding (P)** energized by an **AC excitation** (typically **1 V to 24 V RMS at 50 Hz to 20 kHz**).
- **Two identical secondary windings (S1 and S2)** wound on either side of the primary, connected in **series-opposition** (i.e., their EMFs subtract).
- A **soft-iron movable core** that slides coaxially inside the former.

**Operational Logic:**
1. AC excitation creates an alternating flux in the primary.
2. The flux links with S1 and S2 through the movable core.
3. When the core is at the **null position (central)**: $e_1 = e_2$ ⇒ $e_{out} = e_1 - e_2 = 0$.
4. When the core moves toward **S1**: $e_1 > e_2$ ⇒ $e_{out}$ is **positive** (say, in-phase with primary).
5. When the core moves toward **S2**: $e_2 > e_1$ ⇒ $e_{out}$ is **negative** (180° out-of-phase with primary).
6. The **magnitude** of $e_{out}$ is proportional to displacement $|x|$, and the **phase** indicates direction.

### 2.1.3 The Synchro — Angular Position Transducer

A **Synchro** is essentially a rotary transformer that transmits angular position electrically between a **transmitter** and a **receiver** (or between two receivers in a torque transmission system).

**Constructional Details:**
- **Stator**: Three windings **S1, S2, S3** placed 120° apart in space, connected in a **Y (star) configuration**.
- **Rotor**: A single winding, energized by an **AC reference voltage** (typically **$V_r \sin(\omega t)$** at **50 Hz, 110 V or 400 Hz, 26 V** in aerospace systems).

**Operational Logic:**
1. When the rotor of the transmitter is at angle $\theta$ from a reference, the mutual inductances between rotor and the three stator windings become functions of $\theta$.
2. The induced stator voltages in the transmitter are:
   - $V_{s1} = K \sin(\theta) \sin(\omega t)$
   - $V_{s2} = K \sin(\theta + 120°) \sin(\omega t)$
   - $V_{s3} = K \sin(\theta + 240°) \sin(\omega t)$
3. These three voltages are transmitted through a **3-wire link** to the receiver's stator.
4. The receiver's rotor aligns itself to **minimize the net magnetic torque**, producing a **mechanical rotation equal to $\theta$**.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| S.No. | Quantity | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Self-Inductance | $L = \dfrac{\mu_0 \mu_r N^2 A}{l}$ | Henry (H) |
| 2 | Mutual Inductance | $M = k\sqrt{L_1 L_2}$ where $k$ is coupling coefficient | Henry (H); $0 \le k \le 1$ |
| 3 | Induced EMF | $e = -N \dfrac{d\Phi}{dt} = -L \dfrac{di}{dt}$ | Volt (V) |
| 4 | LVDT Output Voltage | $e_{out} = e_1 - e_2 = K_{LVDT} \cdot x$ | V; linear in displacement $x$ |
| 5 | LVDT Sensitivity | $S = \dfrac{\Delta e_{out}}{\Delta x}$ | V/mm (typically **0.1 to 1 V/mm**) |
| 6 | Null Voltage (Residual) | $e_{null} \ne 0$ due to harmonics; minimized by graded windings | V; should be **< 1% of full-scale** |
| 7 | Synchro Stator Voltage | $V_{s_i} = V_{max} \cdot \cos(\theta - (i-1)\cdot 120°)$ | V; $\theta$ is rotor angle |
| 8 | Synchro Torque | $T \propto V_{rotor}^2 \cdot \sin(\theta_{Tx} - \theta_{Rx})$ | N·m; for torque transmission mode |
| 9 | Phase Sensitivity of LVDT | Direction of $x$ inferred from **phase** of $e_{out}$ w.r.t. primary | Phase reversal at null |
| 10 | Frequency Range | LVDT excitation: **50 Hz to 20 kHz**; Synchro: **50/60/400 Hz** | Hz |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Tip:** The phrase **"series-opposition"** for LVDT secondaries is worth **at least 1 mark** in short-answer questions. Always specify that S1 and S2 are wound in **opposite directions** so that their EMFs subtract.

## 2.3 Engineering Utility

- **LVDT**: Used as the primary feedback element in **servo-hydraulic actuators, aircraft control surfaces, CNC machine tool positioning, bore gauges, and rocket engine thrust vectoring**. Its infinite resolution, frictionless operation, and ruggedness make it ideal for **hostile environments**.
- **Synchro**: Forms the backbone of **remote position indication** in radar antennas, gun turrets, and aircraft autopilots. The **Synchro pair** is functionally equivalent to a **"mechanical ethernet"** for angular data.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the LVDT Output Voltage Equation

Let the primary winding be energized by an **AC voltage** $V_p = V_p \sin(\omega t)$. The alternating primary current establishes a flux $\Phi_p$ in the core.

**Step 1 — Express the flux linking each secondary**

Let the core be displaced by a distance $x$ from the null (center) position. Define:
- Flux linking secondary S1: $\Phi_1 = \Phi_0 (1 + k x)$
- Flux linking secondary S2: $\Phi_2 = \Phi_0 (1 - k x)$

where $\Phi_0$ is the flux at null and $k$ is a **geometric coupling constant** (per mm).

**Step 2 — Apply Faraday's Law to each secondary**

By Faraday's Law of Electromagnetic Induction, the EMF induced in a coil is the negative time-derivative of the flux linkage ($N\Phi$).

$$\begin{aligned}
e_1 &= -N_s \frac{d\Phi_1}{dt} = -N_s \frac{d}{dt}\left[ \Phi_0(1 + kx) \right] \\[4pt]
&= -N_s (1 + kx) \frac{d\Phi_0}{dt} \quad \text{(since } x \text{ is static for a static displacement)}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Express $\frac{d\Phi_0}{dt}$ in terms of the primary excitation**

If the primary flux varies sinusoidally as $\Phi_0 = \Phi_{max} \sin(\omega t)$, then:

$$\begin{aligned}
\frac{d\Phi_0}{dt} = \omega \Phi_{max} \cos(\omega t)
\end{aligned}$$

Substituting back:

$$\begin{aligned}
e_1 &= -N_s \omega \Phi_{max} (1 + kx) \cos(\omega t) \\[4pt]
&= E_{max} (1 + kx) \cos(\omega t) \quad \text{where } E_{max} = N_s \omega \Phi_{max}
\end{aligned}$$

Similarly, for S2:

$$\begin{aligned}
e_2 &= E_{max} (1 - kx) \cos(\omega t)
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Compute the differential (series-opposition) output**

Since S1 and S2 are connected in **series-opposition**, the net output is $e_{out} = e_1 - e_2$:

$$\begin{aligned}
e_{out} &= E_{max} (1 + kx) \cos(\omega t) - E_{max} (1 - kx) \cos(\omega t) \\[4pt]
&= E_{max} \cos(\omega t) \left[ (1 + kx) - (1 - kx) \right] \\[4pt]
&= E_{max} \cos(\omega t) \cdot (2kx) \\[4pt]
e_{out} &= (2k E_{max}) \cdot x \cdot \cos(\omega t)
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5 — Define the LVDT constant and write the final form**

Let $K_{LVDT} = 2k E_{max}$. The output is an **amplitude-modulated** signal:

$$\boxed{\,e_{out}(t) = K_{LVDT} \cdot x \cdot \cos(\omega t)\,}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Examination Insight:** A static displacement $x$ produces an **AC** output of amplitude proportional to $x$. The displacement information resides in the **envelope (amplitude)** and the direction in the **phase polarity**. The frequency $\omega$ of the output equals the **excitation frequency** of the primary.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — LVDT Operating Conditions

| Core Position | $e_1$ | $e_2$ | $e_{out} = e_1 - e_2$ | Phase (w.r.t. primary) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Null (center) | $E_0$ | $E_0$ | $0$ | Indeterminate (null) |
| Displaced toward S1 (positive $x$) | $E_0 + \Delta E$ | $E_0 - \Delta E$ | $+2\Delta E$ | **In-phase** with primary |
| Displaced toward S2 (negative $x$) | $E_0 - \Delta E$ | $E_0 + \Delta E$ | $-2\Delta E$ | **180° out-of-phase** with primary |

## 3.3 Derivation of Synchro Stator Voltages (Transmitter Mode)

Let the rotor of a synchro transmitter carry a current $i_r = I_m \sin(\omega t)$, producing a sinusoidally time-varying rotor MMF.

**Step 1 — Define the angular geometry**

The three stator windings S1, S2, S3 are displaced by **120°** in space. Let the rotor's magnetic axis make an angle $\theta$ with respect to the S1 axis.

**Step 2 — Mutual Inductance between rotor and each stator**

The mutual inductance $M_i$ between the rotor and stator $i$ depends on the **cosine of the angle** between their magnetic axes:

$$M_1 = M_{max} \cos(\theta)$$
$$M_2 = M_{max} \cos(\theta - 120°)$$
$$M_3 = M_{max} \cos(\theta - 240°)$$

**Step 3 — Compute the induced stator EMFs**

$$\begin{aligned}
e_{s1} &= M_1 \frac{di_r}{dt} = M_{max} \cos(\theta) \cdot \frac{d}{dt}\left[ I_m \sin(\omega t) \right] \\[4pt]
&= M_{max} I_m \omega \cos(\theta) \cos(\omega t) = K \cos(\theta) \cos(\omega t)
\end{aligned}$$

Similarly:

$$e_{s2} = K \cos(\theta - 120°) \cos(\omega t)$$
$$e_{s3} = K \cos(\theta - 240°) \cos(\omega t)$$

where $K = M_{max} I_m \omega$ is the synchro constant.

**Step 4 — Receiver side alignment (Torque mode)**

The receiver rotor experiences a net torque proportional to the sine of the **angular mismatch** $\delta = \theta_{Tx} - \theta_{Rx}$:

$$\boxed{\,T_{shaft} = K_T \sin(\theta_{Tx} - \theta_{Rx})\,}$$

> [!NOTE]
> At equilibrium, $\theta_{Tx} = \theta_{Rx}$ and torque vanishes. This is the **principle of torque transmission** in differential synchro systems.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 LVDT — Functional Architecture Flow (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[AC Excitation Source] --> B[Primary Winding P]
    B --> C[Soft Iron Movable Core]
    C --> D[Secondary Winding S1]
    C --> E[Secondary Winding S2]
    D --> F[Differential Subtractor Series Opposition]
    E --> F
    F --> G[Phase Sensitive Demodulator]
    G --> H[DC Output Proportional to Displacement x]
    H --> I[Display or Controller]

    style A fill:#f9e79f,stroke:#7d6608
    style B fill:#aed6f1,stroke:#1b4f72
    style C fill:#f5b7b1,stroke:#78281a
    style D fill:#a9dfbf,stroke:#196f3d
    style E fill:#a9dfbf,stroke:#196f3d
    style F fill:#d7bde2,stroke:#512e5f
    style G fill:#f8c471,stroke:#7e5109
    style H fill:#abebc6,stroke:#0e6251
    style I fill:#85c1e9,stroke:#154360
```

**Explanation of the Flow:**
1. The **AC Excitation Source** (e.g., 5 V RMS at 5 kHz) feeds the **Primary Winding P**.
2. The **Soft Iron Movable Core** couples the alternating flux to both secondaries.
3. **S1 and S2** produce EMFs that are fed in **series-opposition** to the **Differential Subtractor**.
4. A **Phase-Sensitive Demodulator** recovers the displacement information (amplitude and direction) into a **DC signal**.
5. The final output drives a **Display or Process Controller**.

## 4.2 Synchro Transmitter–Receiver Pair Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph TX [SYNCHRO TRANSMITTER]
        A1[AC Reference Voltage V_r sin wt] --> A2[Rotor Winding at angle theta_TX]
        A2 --> A3[Stator Windings S1, S2, S3 displaced 120 deg apart]
    end

    A3 -->|3-wire link| B1[Stator Windings of Receiver]
    B1 --> B2[Receiver Rotor at angle theta_RX]
    B2 --> B3[Alignment Torque proportional to sin theta_TX minus theta_RX]
    B3 --> B4[Mechanical Shaft rotates to match theta_TX]

    style A1 fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#7d6608
    style A2 fill:#f8c471,stroke:#7e5109
    style A3 fill:#aed6f1,stroke:#1b4f72
    style B1 fill:#aed6f1,stroke:#1b4f72
    style B2 fill:#f5b7b1,stroke:#78281a
    style B3 fill:#d7bde2,stroke:#512e5f
    style B4 fill:#82e0aa,stroke:#196f3d
```

**Explanation of the Flow:**
- The **left subgraph (TX)** is the transmitter: a reference AC excites the rotor; the stator produces three position-dependent voltages.
- A **3-wire interconnect** carries the stator voltages across to the receiver.
- The **receiver** (right side) experiences a torque that drives its rotor to align with the transmitted angle, producing a mechanical shaft rotation equal to $\theta_{TX}$.

## 4.3 Comparison Block — LVDT vs Synchro

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph LVDT_BLOCK [LVDT]
        L1[Linear Displacement x] --> L2[Inductive Coupling via Sliding Core] --> L3[AC Output voltage proportional to x]
    end

    subgraph SYNCHRO_BLOCK [SYNCHRO]
        S1[Angular Displacement theta] --> S2[Inductive Coupling via Rotating Rotor] --> S3[Mechanical Shaft Rotation equals theta]
    end

    style L1 fill:#fdebd0,stroke:#7d6608
    style L2 fill:#aed6f1,stroke:#1b4f72
    style L3 fill:#abebc6,stroke:#0e6251
    style S1 fill:#fdebd0,stroke:#7d6608
    style S2 fill:#aed6f1,stroke:#1b4f72
    style S3 fill:#abebc6,stroke:#0e6251
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]**
**Q: With a neat sketch, explain the construction and working of an LVDT. State any two advantages.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The **LVDT (Linear Variable Differential Transformer)** is a passive inductive transducer used to measure linear displacement. It consists of a single primary winding $P$ and two identical secondary windings $S_1$ and $S_2$ wound symmetrically on a cylindrical former. A soft-iron core slides inside the former. The primary is energized by an AC source (typically 1–24 V RMS, 50 Hz–20 kHz).

**Working:** When the core is at the **null (central) position**, the EMFs induced in $S_1$ and $S_2$ are equal and opposite, giving **zero output**. When the core moves toward $S_1$, the induced EMF in $S_1$ increases while that in $S_2$ decreases, producing a **net output voltage** whose **magnitude** is proportional to displacement and whose **phase** indicates the direction.

**Advantages:**
1. **Infinite resolution** — the output is continuous, with no quantization.
2. **Frictionless operation** — no mechanical contact between core and windings, ensuring long life and high reliability.

*(1 Mark — Construction, 1 Mark — Working, 1 Mark — Advantages)*

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Supplementary Exam]**
**Q: What is a Synchro? Differentiate between torque transmission and voltage transmission modes.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

A **Synchro** is a rotary inductive transducer that transmits angular position electrically between a transmitter and a receiver through a 3-wire link. It is essentially a rotary transformer with a single rotor winding and three stator windings displaced by 120°.

**Difference:**

| Feature | Torque Transmission | Voltage Transmission |
|---|---|---|
| **Output** | Mechanical shaft rotation | Electrical voltage proportional to angular error |
| **Use** | Remote angular indication directly | Feedback in servo control systems |
| **Signal** | Strong torque produced | Weak voltage; needs amplification |
| **Accuracy** | Limited by mechanical loading | Higher; no shaft load |
| **Application** | Gyro indicators, gun turret pointers | Radar antenna control, machine tool servo |

*(1 Mark — Definition, 2 Marks — Difference Table)*

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, End Semester]**

### Question A (14 Marks)

**Q: (a)** Derive the output voltage equation of an LVDT. Show that the output is linear for small displacements and explain how the direction of displacement is detected. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** An LVDT has a sensitivity of **0.5 V/mm** and a total measurement range of **±20 mm**. If the excitation is **5 V RMS at 5 kHz**, determine: **(i)** the maximum differential output voltage, **(ii)** the output voltage for a core displacement of **8 mm toward S1**, and **(iii)** the phase of the output with respect to the primary. **(7 Marks)**

---

### Model Solution for Question A

#### Part (a) — Derivation (7 Marks)

Refer to **Section 3.1** of these notes for the complete step-by-step derivation. The final result is:

$$e_{out}(t) = (2k E_{max}) \cdot x \cdot \cos(\omega t) = K_{LVDT} \cdot x \cdot \cos(\omega t)$$

**Key explanatory points to include in the answer:**

1. State Faraday's Law and define the primary excitation. **(1 Mark)**
2. Express the flux linking each secondary as $\Phi_1 = \Phi_0(1 + kx)$ and $\Phi_2 = \Phi_0(1 - kx)$ for displacement $x$. **(1 Mark)**
3. Compute $e_1$ and $e_2$ using Faraday's Law. **(2 Marks)**
4. Compute the differential output $e_{out} = e_1 - e_2$ and simplify. **(2 Marks)**
5. **Direction Detection**: The phase of the output reverses (0° → 180°) as the core crosses the null position. A **phase-sensitive demodulator** (e.g., a synchronous demodulator using the same excitation as reference) extracts the sign of $x$. **(1 Mark)**

#### Part (b) — Numerical (7 Marks)

**Given:**
- Sensitivity: $S = 0.5\ \text{V/mm}$
- Measurement range: $\pm 20\ \text{mm}$
- Excitation: $V_p = 5\ \text{V RMS}$, $f = 5\ \text{kHz}$

**(i) Maximum Differential Output Voltage** **(2 Marks)**

Maximum displacement: $|x_{max}| = 20\ \text{mm}$

$$V_{out,max} = S \times |x_{max}| = 0.5\ \text{V/mm} \times 20\ \text{mm} = 10\ \text{V RMS}$$

**(ii) Output Voltage for 8 mm Displacement toward S1** **(3 Marks)**

$$V_{out} = S \times x = 0.5\ \text{V/mm} \times 8\ \text{mm} = 4\ \text{V RMS}$$

**(iii) Phase of Output w.r.t. Primary** **(2 Marks)**

Since the displacement is **toward S1** (positive direction by convention), the output is **in-phase (0°)** with the primary excitation voltage.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:**
> 1. **Do not forget the units** — V/mm sensitivity is critical; the answer **10 V** is dimensionless without the unit.
> 2. Many students write **"in-phase"** for both positive and negative displacement — this loses **1 mark**. Always state the convention explicitly.
> 3. The **null output is not exactly zero** in a real LVDT due to harmonic distortions and winding asymmetries. The residual voltage is called the **"null voltage"** or **"residual voltage"** and is typically **< 1% of full-scale**.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — *Alternative Choice*

**Q: (a)** Explain the construction and working of a Synchro transmitter-receiver pair. Derive expressions for the three stator voltages as a function of rotor angle. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A synchro transmitter has a rotor excited by a **$V_r = 50\ \text{V}$ RMS, $f = 400\ \text{Hz}$** reference. The rotor is at an angle of **$\theta = 30°$** from the S1 axis. Find the **RMS voltages** induced in the three stator windings. Take the synchro constant as **$K = 40\ \text{V/RMS}$**. **(7 Marks)**

---

### Model Solution for Question B

#### Part (a) — Theory (7 Marks)

Refer to **Section 2.1.3** and **Section 3.3** of these notes for the derivation. The key results are:

$$\begin{aligned}
V_{s1} &= K \cos(\theta) \\[4pt]
V_{s2} &= K \cos(\theta - 120°) \\[4pt]
V_{s3} &= K \cos(\theta - 240°)
\end{aligned}$$

**Points to include in answer:**
1. Constructional diagram of the synchro pair (rotor + 3 stator windings at 120°). **(2 Marks)**
2. Working principle — rotor excited, stator EMFs induced as a function of $\theta$, transmitted over 3-wire link. **(2 Marks)**
3. Derivation of stator voltage expressions using mutual inductance and Faraday's Law. **(3 Marks)**

#### Part (b) — Numerical (7 Marks)

**Given:**
- $K = 40\ \text{V}$ (RMS constant, since stator voltages are RMS values scaled by $K$)
- $\theta = 30°$

**Step 1 — Compute the three stator RMS voltages:**

$$V_{s1} = K \cos(30°) = 40 \times 0.8660 = 34.64\ \text{V RMS}$$

$$V_{s2} = K \cos(30° - 120°) = 40 \cos(-90°) = 40 \times 0 = 0\ \text{V RMS}$$

$$V_{s3} = K \cos(30° - 240°) = 40 \cos(-210°) = 40 \cos(150°) = 40 \times (-0.8660) = -34.64\ \text{V RMS}$$

**Step 2 — Interpret the result:**

$$\boxed{\,V_{s1} = +34.64\ \text{V RMS}, \quad V_{s2} = 0\ \text{V RMS}, \quad V_{s3} = -34.64\ \text{V RMS}\,}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall for Synchro Problems:**
> 1. **Negative voltages ARE valid** — the cosine function can yield negative values, indicating reversed phase of that stator's voltage w.r.t. the reference. Do not write "no voltage" when the answer is negative.
> 2. The **3 stator voltages sum to zero** at all rotor positions (Kirchhoff's Voltage Law on the Y-connected stator). This is a **useful sanity check**: $34.64 + 0 + (-34.64) = 0$. ✓
> 3. The **frequency** of the stator voltages is the **same as the rotor reference** (400 Hz here). The rotor angle only modulates the **amplitude and phase** — NOT the frequency.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Quick Revision Checklist for KTU Module 1 — Inductive Transducers**

- **LVDT = Linear Variable Differential Transformer** — measures **linear** displacement, not rotary.
- LVDT secondaries are always in **series-opposition** (differential connection).
- **Output amplitude ∝ displacement**; **output phase indicates direction**.
- **Excitation frequency** for LVDT typically lies in the range **50 Hz to 20 kHz** (higher frequencies reduce size but increase eddy-current losses).
- **Advantages of LVDT**: infinite resolution, robust, frictionless, low hysteresis, high sensitivity.
- **Limitations of LVDT**: requires AC excitation, dynamic response limited by excitation frequency, sensitive to stray magnetic fields.
- **Synchro = rotary transformer** for **angular** position transmission; 3 stator windings 120° apart + 1 rotor winding.
- **Two operating modes**: (i) **Torque transmission** — direct mechanical reproduction of angle; (ii) **Voltage (control) transmission** — error voltage used in servo loops.
- **Synchro constant** $K$ relates rotor angle to stator voltage amplitude.
- **Differential Synchro**: combines two transmitters and one receiver, used for **addition/subtraction of angles** in analog computation.
- **Phase-sensitive demodulation** is **mandatory** for recovering DC output from LVDT's AC envelope.
- **Null position** in LVDT: theoretically zero output, but practically has a **residual voltage** of 0.5%–2% of full scale due to winding asymmetries and harmonics.
- **Faraday's Law** is the unified theoretical foundation: $e = -N \dfrac{d\Phi}{dt}$.
- The **mutual inductance** $M$ between two coils depends on the **cosine of the angle** between their magnetic axes — a key concept for synchro derivations.
- **KTU 2024 weightage tip**: Module 1 typically contributes **15–20%** of the total marks in the End Semester Examination. LVDT alone can fetch **7 to 14 marks** in Part B.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Capacitive Transducers - Types and Basic Principles - Thickness Measurement
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET411 - ELECTRONIC INSTRUMENTATION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Capacitive Transducers - Types and Basic Principles - Thickness Measurement

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is a Capacitive Transducer?

A **Capacitive Transducer** is a passive electronic sensor that converts a physical measurand (such as displacement, pressure, thickness, humidity, or liquid level) into a measurable change in **electrical capacitance**. It operates on the fundamental principle that capacitance depends on three geometric/dielectric parameters: the **overlapping plate area (A)**, the **separation between plates (d)**, and the **permittivity of the dielectric medium (ε)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU Definition:** *A capacitive transducer is a device in which the variation of any one of the parameters of a capacitor (area, distance between plates, or dielectric constant) due to a measurand produces a proportional change in capacitance, which can be conditioned and measured by an appropriate AC bridge, oscillator, or charge amplifier circuit.*

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy: The "Air-Gap Squeeze"

Imagine two square metal plates held parallel to each other with a thin air gap. Now think of squeezing them slightly closer using your fingers — the smaller the gap, the more "electrical charge holding capacity" the system gains. This "charge-holding capacity" is exactly what **capacitance** represents.

- **Squeeze harder (d decreases) → Capacitance rises sharply**
- **Slide one plate sideways (A decreases) → Capacitance drops linearly**
- **Insert a piece of plastic (ε increases) → Capacitance rises proportionally**

In a **thickness measurement application**, the unknown thickness of a sheet (say, a metal foil or a paper sheet) is precisely the **gap 'd'** between two capacitor plates. By measuring C, we can compute d.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** In KTU Module 1, capacitive transducers are categorized by the *parameter that varies*. For thickness measurement specifically, the **Variable Distance (Gap) type** is the most relevant configuration, although the **Variable Dielectric type** also plays a role when measuring the thickness of non-conducting sheets.

## 1.3 Classification of Capacitive Transducers (At a Glance)

| Variation Type | Parameter Changing | Common Application |
|---|---|---|
| **Variable Distance (Gap)** Type | Plate separation `d` | **Thickness, Displacement, Pressure** |
| **Variable Area** Type | Effective overlap `A` | Linear/Rotary displacement |
| **Variable Dielectric** Type | Permittivity `εᵣ` | Liquid level, Humidity, Thickness of insulators |

## 1.4 Why Use Capacitive Transducers for Thickness?

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Metric:** Capacitive thickness gauges can resolve displacements on the order of **10⁻¹² m (picometer range)** when paired with high-precision capacitance bridges, making them ideal for non-contact measurement of thin films, paper, plastic sheets, and metal foils in industrial process control.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Fundamental Operating Principle

A parallel-plate capacitor stores electrical energy in the form of an electric field between two conductive electrodes separated by a dielectric. The capacitance is governed by the universal formula:

$$C = \frac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}{d}$$

Where:
- $C$ = Capacitance in **Farads (F)**
- $\varepsilon_0$ = Permittivity of free space = **8.854 × 10⁻¹² F/m**
- $\varepsilon_r$ = Relative permittivity (dielectric constant) of the medium
- $A$ = Effective overlapping area of plates in **m²**
- $d$ = Distance between the plates in **m**

Any measurand that changes $A$, $d$, or $\varepsilon_r$ produces a proportional change in $C$, which is then detected by the signal conditioning circuitry.

## 2.2 Three Types — Detailed Working

### 2.2.1 Type 1: Variable Distance (Gap) Type
- One plate is fixed; the other is connected to a movable diaphragm or sensing probe.
- Movement of the sensing element changes the air gap $d$.
- **Sensitivity** is the highest among all three types because $C \propto 1/d$ (non-linear, hyperbolic).
- Used directly in **thickness measurement** of conductive/semi-conductive sheets.

### 2.2.2 Type 2: Variable Area Type
- Plate separation $d$ is held fixed, but the overlapping area $A$ changes due to linear/angular motion.
- Output is **linear** with displacement: $C \propto A$.
- Used for measuring large displacements (mm to cm range).

### 2.2.3 Type 3: Variable Dielectric Type
- A slab of the material under test (or a liquid) slides/inserts between the plates.
- The effective permittivity changes between $\varepsilon_0$ (air) and $\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r$ (material).
- Used for **non-contact thickness measurement of insulating sheets** and for liquid level sensing.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $C = \frac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}{d}$ | Basic parallel-plate capacitance | Foundation formula |
| 2 | $\Delta C = \frac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}{d^2} \cdot \Delta d$ | Change in C due to gap change | Thickness/displacement |
| 3 | $S = \frac{dC}{dd} = -\frac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}{d^2}$ | Sensitivity (negative, hyperbolic) | Transducer design |
| 4 | $\frac{\Delta C}{C} = -\frac{\Delta d}{d}$ | Normalized change (small-signal approx.) | Linear analysis |
| 5 | $C_{total} = C_1 + C_2$ | Capacitors in parallel (multiple dielectrics) | Variable dielectric type |
| 6 | $d = \frac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}{C}$ | Solving for thickness (inversion) | **Thickness readout** |
| 7 | $f = \frac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{LC}}$ | LC oscillator frequency | Signal conditioning |
| 8 | $V_{out} = V_{in} \cdot \frac{C_1 - C_2}{C_1 + C_2}$ | Differential capacitive bridge output | High-precision readout |
| 9 | $\varepsilon_{eff} = \frac{d_1}{\frac{d_1}{\varepsilon_1} + \frac{d_2}{\varepsilon_2}}$ | Series dielectric model | Multi-layer thickness |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exam Tip:** The minus sign in the sensitivity equation $S = -{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}/{d^2}$ is critical. It indicates an **inverse relationship**: as $d$ increases, $C$ decreases (and vice versa). Many students forget the sign and lose marks.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Capacitive thickness gauges are deployed in:
- **Paper and pulp industries** for online sheet thickness monitoring.
- **Metallurgical rolling mills** to control foil thickness (aluminum, copper) in real time.
- **Semiconductor fabrication** to measure wafer and photoresist layer thickness.
- **Plastic film extrusion lines** for quality control of polymer sheets.
- **Non-destructive testing (NDT)** of coatings and insulation layers.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Capacitive?** Compared to inductive, resistive, or optical methods, capacitive sensing offers **non-contact operation**, **extremely high resolution (sub-micron)**, **low temperature drift**, and **excellent long-term stability**, making it the workhorse technology for sub-millimeter thickness gauging.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Sensitivity of Variable-Gap Capacitive Transducer

**Step 1 — Start with the fundamental parallel-plate equation.**

$$C = \frac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}{d}$$

**Step 2 — Differentiate both sides with respect to the gap distance d, treating A and ε as constants.**

$$\frac{dC}{dd} = \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A \cdot \frac{d}{dd}\left(\frac{1}{d}\right)$$

**Step 3 — Apply the standard calculus rule that the derivative of 1/d is −1/d².**

$$\frac{dC}{dd} = \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A \cdot \left(-\frac{1}{d^2}\right)$$

**Step 4 — Final sensitivity expression (with the negative sign preserved).**

$$S = \frac{dC}{dd} = -\frac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}{d^2}$$

**Interpretation:** The sensitivity magnitude $\vert S \vert = \frac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}{d^2}$ is **inversely proportional to d²**. This means at very small gaps, the transducer becomes extremely sensitive — a 1 µm change in thickness can produce a large capacitance change.

## 3.2 Derivation: Normalized Change in Capacitance for Small Variations

**Step 1 — When the gap changes from d to d + Δd, the new capacitance is:**

$$C + \Delta C = \frac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}{d + \Delta d}$$

**Step 2 — Take the ratio of new to original capacitance.**

$$\frac{C + \Delta C}{C} = \frac{d}{d + \Delta d}$$

**Step 3 — For small $\Delta d \ll d$, use the binomial approximation $\frac{1}{1+x} \approx 1 - x$ where $x = \Delta d / d$.**

$$\frac{C + \Delta C}{C} = \frac{1}{1 + \frac{\Delta d}{d}} \approx 1 - \frac{\Delta d}{d}$$

**Step 4 — Subtract 1 from both sides to obtain the fractional change.**

$$\frac{\Delta C}{C} \approx -\frac{\Delta d}{d}$$

This linear approximation justifies using capacitive transducers in differential mode for accurate thickness readouts.

## 3.3 Derivation: Thickness of a Non-Conductive Sheet Using Variable Dielectric Method

**Step 1 — Consider two fixed plates separated by total distance D. A dielectric sheet of thickness t and relative permittivity εᵣ is inserted, with air gap (D − t) on either side.**

**Step 2 — The configuration behaves as three capacitors in series: air, dielectric, air. The total capacitance is:**

$$\frac{1}{C} = \frac{D - t}{\varepsilon_0 A} + \frac{t}{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A} + \frac{D - t}{\varepsilon_0 A}$$

**Step 3 — Combine the two identical air-gap terms.**

$$\frac{1}{C} = \frac{2(D - t)}{\varepsilon_0 A} + \frac{t}{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}$$

**Step 4 — Factor out $1/(\varepsilon_0 A)$.**

$$\frac{1}{C} = \frac{1}{\varepsilon_0 A} \left[ 2(D - t) + \frac{t}{\varepsilon_r} \right]$$

**Step 5 — Solve for t algebraically (this is the standard KTU exam-style inversion).**

$$t = \frac{2D \varepsilon_r - \varepsilon_r \varepsilon_0 A / C}{2\varepsilon_r - 1}$$

Or in its cleaner form:

$$t = \frac{2D \varepsilon_r \cdot (C \varepsilon_0 A) - \varepsilon_r}{C \varepsilon_0 A (2\varepsilon_r - 1)}$$

**Step 6 — Simplify (validating dimensions). The result gives the unknown thickness t as a function of measured C, known D, A, and known εᵣ of the sheet material.**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this derivation matters in KTU exams:** It combines the series-capacitor concept with the variable-dielectric principle and tests the student's ability to handle algebraic inversions. Expect 7–10 mark questions on this pattern.

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix (Variable-Gap vs Variable-Dielectric for Thickness)

| Parameter | Variable-Gap (Conductive Sheet) | Variable-Dielectric (Insulating Sheet) |
|---|---|---|
| **Best for** | Conductive / metallic sheets | Insulating / dielectric sheets |
| **Sensitivity** | Very high (1/d² dependence) | Moderate (linear in t) |
| **Linearity** | Non-linear (hyperbolic) | Approximately linear |
| **Range** | µm to mm | µm to a few mm |
| **Equation used** | $d = \varepsilon_0 A / C$ | $t = f(C, D, \varepsilon_r)$ (series model) |
| **KTU exam weightage** | High | High |
| **Major pitfall** | Forgetting negative sign in S | Algebra errors in series inversion |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Capacitive Thickness Measurement — Variable Gap Type

This Mermaid block diagram shows a typical two-plate capacitive sensor arrangement used for measuring the thickness of a metal sheet passing through a rolling mill or production line.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA[Fixed Capacitive Plate TOP] --> nodeB[Conductive Sheet under test]
    nodeB --> nodeC[Fixed Capacitive Plate BOTTOM]
    nodeA --> nodeD[AC Excitation Source]
    nodeC --> nodeD
    nodeD --> nodeE[Capacitance Bridge or Oscillator]
    nodeE --> nodeF[Signal Conditioner Amplifier]
    nodeF --> nodeG[ADC and Microcontroller]
    nodeG --> nodeH[Digital Thickness Display mm]
    nodeG --> nodeI[Feedback to Actuator for closed loop control]
    style nodeA fill:#1f3a5f,color:#ffffff
    style nodeB fill:#c0392b,color:#ffffff
    style nodeC fill:#1f3a5f,color:#ffffff
    style nodeD fill:#27ae60,color:#ffffff
    style nodeE fill:#f39c12,color:#000000
    style nodeF fill:#f39c12,color:#000000
    style nodeG fill:#8e44ad,color:#ffffff
    style nodeH fill:#34495e,color:#ffffff
    style nodeI fill:#34495e,color:#ffffff
```

**Interpretation of the flow:**
1. The unknown sheet is positioned between two fixed capacitive plates.
2. The sheet thickness determines the gap d, hence the capacitance C.
3. An AC source drives the bridge, producing an imbalance voltage proportional to the thickness.
4. After amplification, the signal is digitized and displayed.
5. In a closed-loop system, the output also drives a roller actuator to maintain target thickness.

## 4.2 Capacitive Thickness Measurement — Variable Dielectric Type

This Mermaid block diagram shows a configuration where the unknown sheet itself acts as the dielectric between the plates.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA[Top Plate Fixed] --> nodeB[Air Gap D minus t]
    nodeB --> nodeC[Dielectric Sheet Thickness t]
    nodeC --> nodeD[Air Gap D minus t]
    nodeD --> nodeE[Bottom Plate Fixed]
    nodeA --> nodeF[Equivalent Series Capacitor Network]
    nodeE --> nodeF
    nodeF --> nodeG[Capacitance Measurement Circuit]
    nodeG --> nodeH[Thickness Calculation using t equation]
    nodeH --> nodeI[Display and Data Logging]
    style nodeA fill:#1f3a5f,color:#ffffff
    nodeB:::airgap
    style nodeC fill:#c0392b,color:#ffffff
    style nodeD fill:#1f3a5f,color:#ffffff
    style nodeE fill:#1f3a5f,color:#ffffff
    style nodeF fill:#f39c12,color:#000000
    style nodeG fill:#8e44ad,color:#ffffff
    style nodeH fill:#27ae60,color:#ffffff
    style nodeI fill:#34495e,color:#ffffff
    classDef airgap fill:#ecf0f1,color:#000000,stroke:#7f8c8d
```

**Interpretation:** The two fixed plates sandwich the dielectric sheet. The combined air–dielectric–air series capacitance is measured and used to compute t using the derived formula.

## 4.3 Functional Block Diagram of a Complete Capacitive Thickness Gauge

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    node1[Sensor Head two plates] --> node2[Oscillator circuit sine wave]
    node2 --> node3[Capacitance to Voltage Converter C to V]
    node3 --> node4[Linearization circuit inverse hyperbolic]
    node4 --> node5[Amplifier and Filter stage]
    node5 --> node6[Display Driver and HMI]
    node5 --> node7[Process Controller PLC or DCS]
    style node1 fill:#1f3a5f,color:#ffffff
    style node2 fill:#27ae60,color:#ffffff
    style node3 fill:#f39c12,color:#000000
    style node4 fill:#f39c12,color:#000000
    style node5 fill:#8e44ad,color:#ffffff
    style node6 fill:#34495e,color:#ffffff
    style node7 fill:#34495e,color:#ffffff
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Mermaid Block Diagrams?** Capacitive transducer setups are typically two-plate mechanical assemblies. Rather than attempting a physically inaccurate schematic with primitive Mermaid shapes, these flow-based functional block diagrams clearly communicate the **signal path, conditioning chain, and feedback loop**, which is exactly what the KTU 2024 OBE-based exam pattern rewards.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Define a capacitive transducer. List its three main types based on the parameter that varies.**

**Model Answer:**

A capacitive transducer is a sensor that converts a physical quantity into a measurable change in capacitance. It works on the principle that the capacitance of a parallel-plate capacitor depends on the plate area (A), the distance between the plates (d), and the dielectric permittivity (ε).

The three types are:
1. **Variable distance (gap) type** — where d varies.
2. **Variable area type** — where A varies.
3. **Variable dielectric type** — where εᵣ varies.

*(Full 3 marks: definition + 3 types clearly listed.)*

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
**State the expression for the sensitivity of a variable-gap capacitive transducer and comment on its non-linearity.**

**Model Answer:**

$$S = \frac{dC}{dd} = -\frac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}{d^2}$$

The sensitivity is **inversely proportional to d²**, which means it is **non-linear** (hyperbolic). As the gap d decreases, the sensitivity magnitude increases rapidly. This non-linearity is the principal drawback of the variable-gap type and necessitates linearization circuitry in high-precision applications.

*(Full 3 marks: formula + 2 lines of explanation.)*

---

## 📘 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> Choose **ONE** of the following two full questions. Each contains two sub-parts of 7 marks each.

---

### **🔹 Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022 Model Paper]*

**(a)** Derive the expression for the fractional change in capacitance of a parallel-plate capacitive transducer when the gap changes by a small amount Δd. Hence, obtain its sensitivity.

**(b)** A parallel-plate capacitive transducer has plate area $A = 400 \text{ mm}^2$ and air gap $d = 0.5 \text{ mm}$. Calculate:
   1. The nominal capacitance.
   2. The change in capacitance if the gap reduces by 0.05 mm.
   3. The sensitivity at the nominal operating point.

---

#### ✅ Model Solution to Question A

**Part (a) — 7 Marks**

**Step 1.** Capacitance at gap d:

$$C = \frac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}{d}$$

**Step 2.** Capacitance at gap $d + \Delta d$:

$$C + \Delta C = \frac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}{d + \Delta d}$$

**Step 3.** Taking the ratio:

$$\frac{C + \Delta C}{C} = \frac{d}{d + \Delta d} = \frac{1}{1 + \frac{\Delta d}{d}}$$

**Step 4.** Using the binomial expansion $\frac{1}{1+x} \approx 1 - x$ for $\vert x \vert \ll 1$:

$$1 + \frac{\Delta C}{C} \approx 1 - \frac{\Delta d}{d}$$

**Step 5.** Therefore, the fractional change is:

$$\boxed{\frac{\Delta C}{C} \approx -\frac{\Delta d}{d}}$$

**Step 6.** Differentiating the original equation with respect to d:

$$S = \frac{dC}{dd} = -\frac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}{d^2}$$

**Part (b) — 7 Marks**

**Given:** $A = 400 \text{ mm}^2 = 400 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^2 = 4 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2$, $d = 0.5 \text{ mm} = 5 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}$, $\varepsilon_r = 1$ (air), $\Delta d = -0.05 \text{ mm}$ (reduction).

**1. Nominal Capacitance:**

$$C = \frac{8.854 \times 10^{-12} \times 1 \times 4 \times 10^{-4}}{5 \times 10^{-4}}$$

$$C = \frac{3.5416 \times 10^{-15}}{5 \times 10^{-4}} = 7.083 \times 10^{-12} \text{ F} \approx 7.08 \text{ pF}$$

**2. Change in Capacitance:**

$$\Delta C = -\frac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}{d^2} \cdot \Delta d$$

$$\Delta C = -\frac{8.854 \times 10^{-12} \times 1 \times 4 \times 10^{-4}}{(5 \times 10^{-4})^2} \times (-5 \times 10^{-5})$$

$$\Delta C = -\frac{3.5416 \times 10^{-15}}{2.5 \times 10^{-7}} \times (-5 \times 10^{-5})$$

$$\Delta C = -1.4166 \times 10^{-8} \times (-5 \times 10^{-5}) = 7.083 \times 10^{-13} \text{ F} \approx 0.708 \text{ pF}$$

**3. Sensitivity:**

$$S = -\frac{8.854 \times 10^{-12} \times 1 \times 4 \times 10^{-4}}{(5 \times 10^{-4})^2}$$

$$S = -\frac{3.5416 \times 10^{-15}}{2.5 \times 10^{-7}} = -1.4166 \times 10^{-8} \text{ F/m} \approx -14.17 \text{ pF/mm}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout (Q-A):** Many students lose 2–3 marks by:
> - Forgetting to convert mm² and mm to SI units (m² and m) before substitution.
> - Dropping the **negative sign** in the sensitivity equation.
> - Using the wrong binomial approximation direction; remember it's $\frac{1}{1+x} \approx 1 - x$, not $1 + x$.

---

### **🔹 Question B (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – June 2023 Model Paper]*

**(a)** Explain with a neat diagram how a capacitive transducer can be used for measuring the thickness of a **non-conductive (dielectric) sheet** using the variable-dielectric principle. Derive the governing equation relating thickness t to measured capacitance C.

**(b)** A capacitive thickness sensor has two parallel plates of area $A = 600 \text{ mm}^2$ separated by a fixed distance $D = 2 \text{ mm}$. A dielectric sheet of relative permittivity $\varepsilon_r = 4$ is inserted between the plates. The measured capacitance is $C = 4.5 \text{ pF}$. Determine the thickness t of the sheet.

---

#### ✅ Model Solution to Question B

**Part (a) — 7 Marks**

**Diagram:** *(Refer to Section 4.2 — Variable Dielectric Type Mermaid Block Diagram)*

**Working Principle:** The dielectric sheet of unknown thickness t and relative permittivity εᵣ is placed between two parallel plates separated by a fixed distance D. The remaining gap on either side is filled with air, giving the system the structure of three series capacitors: air, dielectric, air.

**Derivation:**

**Step 1.** Three capacitors in series, each with area A:

$$C_{air} = \frac{\varepsilon_0 A}{(D - t)/2}, \quad C_{sheet} = \frac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}{t}$$

**Step 2.** Reciprocal sum for series combination:

$$\frac{1}{C} = \frac{2(D - t)}{\varepsilon_0 A} + \frac{t}{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}$$

**Step 3.** Factor out $1/(\varepsilon_0 A)$:

$$\frac{1}{C} = \frac{1}{\varepsilon_0 A}\left[2(D - t) + \frac{t}{\varepsilon_r}\right]$$

**Step 4.** Solve for t. Rearranging:

$$\frac{\varepsilon_0 A}{C} = 2D - 2t + \frac{t}{\varepsilon_r}$$

$$\frac{\varepsilon_0 A}{C} = 2D - t\left(2 - \frac{1}{\varepsilon_r}\right)$$

$$\boxed{t = \frac{2D - \frac{\varepsilon_0 A}{C}}{2 - \frac{1}{\varepsilon_r}} = \frac{2\varepsilon_r\left(D - \frac{\varepsilon_0 A}{2C}\right)}{2\varepsilon_r - 1}}$$

**Part (b) — 7 Marks**

**Given:** $A = 600 \text{ mm}^2 = 6 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2$, $D = 2 \text{ mm} = 2 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}$, $\varepsilon_r = 4$, $C = 4.5 \text{ pF} = 4.5 \times 10^{-12} \text{ F}$.

**Step 1.** Compute $\frac{\varepsilon_0 A}{C}$:

$$\frac{\varepsilon_0 A}{C} = \frac{8.854 \times 10^{-12} \times 6 \times 10^{-4}}{4.5 \times 10^{-12}}$$

$$= \frac{5.3124 \times 10^{-15}}{4.5 \times 10^{-12}} = 1.1805 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}$$

**Step 2.** Compute the denominator $\left(2 - \frac{1}{\varepsilon_r}\right)$:

$$2 - \frac{1}{4} = 2 - 0.25 = 1.75$$

**Step 3.** Compute the numerator $2D - \frac{\varepsilon_0 A}{C}$:

$$2D = 2 \times 2 \times 10^{-3} = 4 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}$$

$$2D - \frac{\varepsilon_0 A}{C} = 4 \times 10^{-3} - 1.1805 \times 10^{-3} = 2.8195 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}$$

**Step 4.** Final thickness:

$$t = \frac{2.8195 \times 10^{-3}}{1.75} = 1.611 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m} \approx 1.61 \text{ mm}$$

**Verification:** Since $t < D$ (1.61 mm < 2 mm), the result is physically valid. ✅

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout (Q-B):** Common mistakes:
> - Using $C_1 + C_2$ (parallel) instead of $\frac{1}{C_1} + \frac{1}{C_2}$ (series) for the three-layer sandwich.
> - Forgetting to halve the air gap — there are **two** air regions (top and bottom), each of thickness $\frac{D-t}{2}$.
> - Algebraic slip during the inversion step; always isolate $t$ *last* and substitute values *first* if intermediate numbers are unwieldy.

---

## 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ✅ Capacitive transducers convert **any change in A, d, or εᵣ** into a change in capacitance C.
- ✅ The fundamental formula $C = \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A / d$ is the **single most important equation** for this module.
- ✅ For **thickness measurement of conductive sheets**, use the **Variable-Gap Type**; sensitivity $S = -\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A / d^2$ is **non-linear and very high at small gaps**.
- ✅ For **thickness measurement of insulating sheets**, use the **Variable-Dielectric Type**; the governing equation is $t = \frac{2\varepsilon_r(D - \varepsilon_0 A / 2C)}{2\varepsilon_r - 1}$.
- ✅ The **negative sign** in sensitivity indicates an **inverse relationship** — never drop it.
- ✅ Capacitance is measured in **Farads (F)**; typical transducer values lie in the **pF range** — remember $1 \text{ pF} = 10^{-12} \text{ F}$.
- ✅ Permittivity of free space $\varepsilon_0 = 8.854 \times 10^{-12} \text{ F/m}$ — memorize this constant.
- ✅ For small signals, $\Delta C / C \approx -\Delta d / d$ is the **linearized small-signal approximation**.
- ✅ Signal conditioning: capacitive transducers always need an **AC excitation source** and a **bridge or oscillator circuit** to convert C into a measurable voltage or frequency.
- ✅ **Applications:** Paper thickness, metal foil rolling, plastic film extrusion, semiconductor wafer inspection, NDT of coatings.
- ✅ **Common KTU exam traps:** unit conversions (mm² → m², mm → m), sign errors, series-vs-parallel capacitor confusion, missing the air gap on **both sides** of the dielectric sheet.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Piezoelectric Transducers
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET411 - ELECTRONIC INSTRUMENTATION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Piezoelectric Transducers

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of Piezoelectric Transducer

A **Piezoelectric Transducer** is a passive, active-type electromechanical sensor that converts mechanical energy (force, pressure, acceleration, or vibration) into a measurable electrical signal (charge or voltage) — and vice versa — by exploiting the **piezoelectric effect** exhibited by certain non-centrosymmetric crystalline materials.

The term *piezo* originates from the Greek word **"piezein,"** meaning *to press or squeeze*. The phenomenon was first discovered by **Jacques and Pierre Curie in 1880**.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 1):**
> A piezoelectric transducer is classified as an **active transducer** because it does not require an external excitation power supply to generate an output signal. It is its own miniature power source, producing an EMF (electromotive force) directly in response to applied mechanical stress.

### The Two Complementary Effects

**1. Direct (Generator) Piezoelectric Effect:**
When a mechanical force (stress) is applied across certain faces of a piezoelectric crystal, equal and opposite electrical charges appear on its opposite faces. The magnitude of charge is proportional to the applied force.

$$Q = d \cdot F$$

where:
- $Q$ = generated electric charge (Coulombs)
- $d$ = piezoelectric charge constant (Coulombs/Newton)
- $F$ = applied mechanical force (Newtons)

**2. Converse (Motor) Piezoelectric Effect:**
When an electric field is applied across a piezoelectric crystal, it undergoes a measurable mechanical deformation (strain). This is the working principle of piezoelectric actuators and ultrasonic transducers.

$$x = d \cdot E \cdot t$$

where:
- $x$ = mechanical displacement (meters)
- $E$ = applied electric field (V/m)
- $t$ = thickness of the crystal (meters)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The same piezoelectric constant $d$ governs **both** effects — a fundamental symmetry described by the Curie symmetry principle. This makes piezoelectric devices inherently **reversible transducers**.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Crystal Microphone" Intuition

Imagine a **gas lighter** used in kitchens. When you press the button, a small spring-loaded hammer strikes a piezoelectric crystal inside. The mechanical impact generates thousands of volts across the crystal faces, producing a spark that ignites the gas. The crystal *itself* converted your finger's mechanical push into electricity.

Similarly, a **piezoelectric microphone** works in reverse on a microscopic scale: sound waves (vibrations in air pressure) push against a thin diaphragm bonded to a piezoelectric crystal. The crystal flexes, charges appear on its faces, and these charges are amplified into an audio signal.

> **Think of it this way:** A piezoelectric crystal is like a *two-way energy translator*. Just as a translator converts English to French and back, the crystal converts mechanical energy to electrical energy and back, using the same underlying atomic mechanism — the displacement of ions inside its lattice.

---

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Material Constants (Bold for KTU emphasis):**

| Parameter | Symbol | Typical Value for Quartz | Typical Value for PZT-5A |
|-----------|--------|--------------------------|--------------------------|
| Piezoelectric charge constant | $d$ | **2.3 × 10⁻¹² C/N** | **400 × 10⁻¹² C/N** |
| Piezoelectric voltage constant | $g$ | **0.058 V·m/N** | **0.041 V·m/N** |
| Relative permittivity | $\varepsilon_r$ | **4.5** | **2000** |
| Curie temperature | $T_c$ | **573 °C** | **365 °C** |
| Density | $\rho$ | **2650 kg/m³** | **7750 kg/m³** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Dielectric Constant Significance:** The free-space permittivity is $\varepsilon_0 = 8.854 \times 10^{-12}$ F/m. The absolute permittivity of a piezoelectric material is $\varepsilon = \varepsilon_r \cdot \varepsilon_0$. This determines the **capacitance** of the crystal — a critical factor in output voltage calculation.

---

## 1.4 Why Piezoelectric Transducers Matter in Instrumentation

In modern electronic instrumentation, piezoelectric transducers are the **backbone of dynamic measurement systems** because they:

- Have an **extremely wide frequency range** (0.1 Hz to over 100 kHz)
- Possess **excellent linearity** under proper loading
- Are **rigid and compact**, with high natural frequencies
- Generate **self-powered outputs** (no external excitation needed)
- Provide **high sensitivity** to dynamic forces and pressures

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Common Application Domains (Examinable):**
> 1. **Microphones and hydrophones** (acoustic sensing)
> 2. **Accelerometers and vibration sensors** (industrial monitoring)
> 3. **Pressure transducers** (engine cylinders, hydraulic systems)
> 4. **Ultrasonic transducers** (medical imaging, NDT)
> 5. **Force sensors and load cells** (precision manufacturing)
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Crystallographic Basis of Piezoelectricity

Piezoelectricity arises from the **asymmetric charge distribution** within the unit cell of certain crystals. For a material to be piezoelectric, its crystal structure must **lack a center of symmetry**. When mechanical stress deforms the unit cell, the centers of positive and negative charges displace differently, producing a net dipole moment and hence surface charges.

### 2.1.1 Classification of Piezoelectric Materials

**A. Natural Crystals:**
- **Quartz (SiO₂)** — high stability, low $d$, used in precision frequency standards
- **Rochelle Salt (NaKC₄H₄O₆·4H₂O)** — very high $d$ but temperature-sensitive
- **Tourmaline** — naturally polarized

**B. Synthetic Piezoelectric Ceramics (Ferroelectrics):**
- **Lead Zirconate Titanate (PZT)** — most widely used; high $d$ and $g$
- **Barium Titanate (BaTiO₃)** — earlier generation ceramic
- **Lithium Niobate (LiNbO₃)** — used in surface acoustic wave (SAW) devices

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examination Point:** PZT and BaTiO₃ are **ferroelectric** materials — they possess spontaneous polarization that can be reoriented by a strong DC field (poling). Above their **Curie temperature** $T_c$, they lose piezoelectricity and become paraelectric. Always operate below $T_c$.

---

## 2.2 Modes of Operation

A piezoelectric crystal can be operated in several mechanical modes, depending on the direction of applied force relative to the poling axis (z-axis convention):

| Mode | Force Direction | Poling Direction | Output Direction |
|------|-----------------|------------------|------------------|
| **Longitudinal (d₃₃)** | Parallel to poling (z) | z | Electrodes perpendicular to z |
| **Transverse (d₃₁)** | Perpendicular to poling (x) | z | Electrodes perpendicular to z |
| **Shear (d₁₅)** | Tangential | z | Electrodes on perpendicular faces |

The **transverse (d₃₁) mode** is widely used because it provides a **force-amplification effect**: a small force over a large area produces a charge equivalent to a large force over a small area.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why d₃₁ mode is preferred in accelerometers:** The transverse coefficient $d_{31}$ allows mechanical advantage. If the electrode length is $L$ and thickness is $t$, the voltage generated is multiplied by the ratio $L/t$, which can be made very large.

---

## 2.3 Equivalent Circuit of a Piezoelectric Transducer

A piezoelectric crystal behaves electrically as a **capacitor** (because of its parallel-plate electrode geometry filled with a dielectric) and mechanically as a **spring-mass-damper system**.

### 2.3.1 Electrical Equivalent Circuit

The transducer can be represented by either a **voltage source model** or a **charge source model**:

**Voltage Source Model** (suitable for low-frequency, high-impedance voltage amplifiers):
- Voltage source $V = Q/C_a$ in series with capacitor $C_a$ and resistor $R_a$ (insulation/leakage resistance)

**Charge Source Model** (suitable for high-frequency, charge amplifier interfacing):
- Charge source $Q = d \cdot F$ in parallel with capacitor $C_a$ and resistor $R_a$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Key Insight:** The cable capacitance $C_c$ between transducer and amplifier appears **in parallel with $C_a$** in the voltage-source model. This means cable length directly affects output voltage — a major disadvantage. The **charge amplifier** overcomes this by making output independent of cable capacitance.

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Formulas for KTU Board Examination (Module 1)**

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | $Q = d \cdot F$ | Charge generated by applied force | C, C/N, N |
| 2 | $C_a = \dfrac{\varepsilon_r \varepsilon_0 A}{t}$ | Capacitance of crystal (A = electrode area) | F |
| 3 | $V = \dfrac{Q}{C_a} = \dfrac{d \cdot F \cdot t}{\varepsilon_r \varepsilon_0 A}$ | Open-circuit output voltage | V |
| 4 | $g = \dfrac{d}{\varepsilon_r \varepsilon_0}$ | Piezoelectric voltage constant (relates $d$ and $g$) | V·m/N |
| 5 | $V = g \cdot F \cdot \dfrac{t}{A}$ | Voltage in terms of $g$ | V |
| 6 | $S_v = g \cdot t$ | **Voltage sensitivity** (V/N) | V/N |
| 7 | $S_q = d$ | **Charge sensitivity** (C/N) | C/N |
