# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question]**
> *Define an instrumentation amplifier. List two of its key advantages over a standard op-amp difference amplifier for sensor applications.*

**Model Answer:**
An instrumentation amplifier (INA) is a precision differential amplifier with very high input impedance, high common-mode rejection ratio (CMRR), and a single external resistor-controlled gain. It is specifically designed to amplify low-level differential signals from sensors in noisy environments.

**Two key advantages over a standard op-amp difference amplifier:**
1. **Very high input impedance** (typically > 10 GΩ) — does not load the sensor, preserving measurement accuracy.
2. **Single-resistor gain programmability** — gain can be set from 1 to 1000+ by varying only `Rg`, without affecting the input impedance or CMRR symmetry.

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Question]**
> *Explain the working principle of the AD590 temperature sensor IC. State its output equation.*

**Model Answer:**
The **AD590** is a two-terminal monolithic temperature sensor IC that acts as a **high-impedance constant current regulator**, passing a current proportional to absolute temperature.

**Output equation:** $I_T = 1 \text{ µA/K}$

**Working:** The IC uses fundamental silicon transistor characteristics (the PTAT — Proportional To Absolute Temperature principle) to generate a current that increases linearly with temperature. The output is a current (not voltage), making it immune to voltage drops and noise over long cable runs.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — KTU ESE Module 1 Internal Choice Pattern

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - In derivation questions (like the INA gain), **forgetting to mention the assumption of matched resistors** in the difference stage costs **1 mark**.
> - Students often **write the general three-op-amp formula but forget the AD620-specific simplification** ($G = 1 + 49.4k/R_G$). The examiner expects BOTH the generic formula AND the specific IC formula.
> - In numerical problems, **units must be consistent** (e.g., mV not V, mA not A). Many students lose 1–2 marks for unit errors.
> - In circuit diagrams, always **label the gain resistor `Rg`** and the feedback resistors. Unlabeled diagrams receive **0 marks for the diagram portion**.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 1]

> **(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the operation of a three-op-amp instrumentation amplifier. Derive the expression for its output voltage in terms of the input signals and the gain-setting resistor. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A strain gauge bridge with a single active gauge of resistance 350 Ω and gauge factor 2.1 is connected to an AD620 instrumentation amplifier. The bridge is excited with 10 V. The strain on the gauge is 1000 microstrain. Calculate the required value of the gain-setting resistor $R_G$ such that the amplifier output is exactly 5 V. Assume the other three gauges are inactive. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution (a):**

A three-op-amp INA consists of two input buffer op-amps (A1 and A2) followed by a difference amplifier (A3). The two non-inverting inputs receive the differential signal `Vin+` and `Vin-` from the sensor bridge. The single gain-setting resistor `Rg` is connected between the inverting inputs of A1 and A2, and feedback resistors `R1` are connected from each input op-amp output to its inverting input. The difference amplifier stage uses matched resistors `R1` (input) and `R2` (feedback).

**Derivation:**

The current through `Rg` is:

$$
I_{Rg} = \dfrac{V_{in+} - V_{in-}}{R_g}
$$

The differential output of the input stage is:

$$
V_{A} - V_{B} = (V_{in+} - V_{in-})\left(1 + \dfrac{2 R_1}{R_g}\right)
$$

The output of the difference amplifier (assuming matched resistors, `R2/R1` is fixed) is:

$$
V_{out} = \dfrac{R_2}{R_1}(V_A - V_B) = \left(1 + \dfrac{2 R_1}{R_g}\right) \cdot \dfrac{R_2}{R_1} \cdot (V_{in+} - V_{in-})
$$

For a general INA, the gain `G` is defined as:

$$
G = \left(1 + \dfrac{2 R_1}{R_g}\right) \cdot \dfrac{R_2}{R_1}
$$

For the **AD620**, this simplifies (using internal laser-trimmed resistors) to:

$$
\boxed{G = 1 + \dfrac{49.4 \text{ k}\Omega}{R_G}}
$$

#### **Model Solution (b):**

**Step 1: Calculate the bridge output voltage**

Quarter-bridge formula with one active gauge:

$$
V_{bridge} = \dfrac{V_{ex}}{4} \cdot \varepsilon \cdot GF
$$

$$
V_{bridge} = \dfrac{10}{4} \cdot (1000 \times 10^{-6}) \cdot 2.1 = 5.25 \text{ mV}
$$

**Step 2: Calculate the required gain**

We need $V_{out} = 5$ V from $V_{bridge} = 5.25$ mV:

$$
G = \dfrac{V_{out}}{V_{bridge}} = \dfrac{5 \text{ V}}{5.25 \text{ mV}} = 952.38
$$

**Step 3: Solve for $R_G$ using the AD620 formula**

$$
G = 1 + \dfrac{49.4 \text{ k}\Omega}{R_G}
$$

$$
952.38 = 1 + \dfrac{49.4 \text{ k}\Omega}{R_G}
$$

$$
R_G = \dfrac{49.4 \text{ k}\Omega}{951.38} = 51.92 \text{ }\Omega
$$

**Result:** $R_G \approx 52$ Ω (use a standard 51 Ω or 52.3 Ω resistor).

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 1, Internal Choice]

> **(a)** Explain the working of the LM35 and AD590 temperature sensor ICs. Compare their output characteristics and state one application each. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A process control pressure sensor outputs 1 V at 0 bar and 5 V at 10 bar. Design a 4-20 mA current loop interface using the XTR105 (assume linear mapping). Find: (i) the loop current at 4 bar, and (ii) the receiver voltage if a 250 Ω sense resistor is used. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution (a):**

**LM35:**
The LM35 is a precision centigrade temperature sensor IC whose output voltage is linearly proportional to the Celsius temperature. It does not require any external calibration or trimming. The output equation is:

$$
V_{out} = 10 \text{ mV/°C} \cdot T
$$

It is calibrated directly in °C (0 °C → 0 mV, 100 °C → 1000 mV). **Application:** Domestic and industrial temperature monitoring, HVAC systems, battery thermal management.

**AD590:**
The AD590 is a two-terminal **current output** temperature sensor IC. It acts as a high-impedance constant current source of 1 µA/K. The output equation is:

$$
I_T = 1 \text{ µA/K} \cdot T
$$

At 0 °C (273.15 K), it outputs 273.15 µA; at 100 °C (373.15 K), it outputs 373.15 µA. Because the output is a current, it is **immune to voltage drops** over long transmission lines, making it ideal for industrial and remote sensing. **Application:** Remote process temperature monitoring, cryogenic measurements,多点温度监控.

**Comparison:**

| Feature | LM35 | AD590 |
|---|---|---|
| Output type | Voltage (10 mV/°C) | Current (1 µA/K) |
| Reference | Celsius | Kelvin |
| Line length immunity | Poor (voltage drop) | Excellent (current loop) |
| Calibration | Direct in °C | Requires K-to-°C conversion |
| Typical supply | +4 V to +30 V | +4 V to +30 V |

#### **Model Solution (b):**

**Step 1: Establish the 4-20 mA mapping**

Given:
- $V_{min} = 1$ V at 0 bar
- $V_{max} = 5$ V at 10 bar
- 4 mA ↔ 0 bar, 20 mA ↔ 10 bar

**Step 2: Find the transfer function**

The general linear 4-20 mA equation is:

$$
I_{loop} = 4 \text{ mA} + 16 \text{ mA} \cdot \dfrac{(V_{in} - V_{min})}{(V_{max} - V_{min})}
$$

**Step 3: Find $V_{in}$ at 4 bar**

Linear sensor: $V_{in} = V_{min} + \dfrac{(V_{max} - V_{min})}{10 \text{ bar}} \cdot 4 \text{ bar}$

$$
V_{in} = 1 + \dfrac{(5-1)}{10} \cdot 4 = 1 + 1.6 = 2.6 \text{ V}
$$

**Step 4: Calculate loop current at 4 bar**

$$
I_{loop} = 4 + 16 \cdot \dfrac{(2.6 - 1)}{(5 - 1)} = 4 + 16 \cdot 0.4 = 4 + 6.4 = 10.4 \text{ mA}
$$

**Step 5: Calculate the receiver voltage across 250 Ω**

$$
V_{rx} = I_{loop} \cdot R_{sense} = 10.4 \text{ mA} \cdot 250 \text{ }\Omega = 2.6 \text{ V}
$$

**Result:** At 4 bar, the loop current is **10.4 mA** and the receiver voltage is **2.6 V** (which directly corresponds to the sensor's 4-bar output voltage, as expected for a linear 1:1 mapping with this sense resistor).

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU Module 1: Electronic ICs for Sensor Applications**

- ☐ **Instrumentation Amplifier (INA)** = 3 op-amp topology; provides high input impedance, high CMRR, single-resistor gain.
- ☐ **AD620 Gain Formula:** $G = 1 + \dfrac{49.4 \text{ k}\Omega}{R_G}$
- ☐ **INA128 Gain Formula:** $G = 1 + \dfrac{50 \text{ k}\Omega}{R_G}$
- ☐ **INA333 Gain Formula:** $G = 1 + \dfrac{100 \text{ k}\Omega}{R_G}$ *(low-power, low-noise)*
- ☐ **CMRR Definition:** Ratio of differential gain to common-mode gain, expressed in **dB**. Higher is better.
- ☐ **LM35:** Voltage output, 10 mV/°C, **calibrated in °C directly** (not Kelvin).
- ☐ **AD590:** Current output, 1 µA/K, **Kelvin-based**, ideal for long-distance/industrial use.
- ☐ **LM335:** Voltage output, 10 mV/K, Zener-like operation, Kelvin-based.
- ☐ **Strain Gauge Bridge (Quarter-bridge):** $V_{bridge} = \dfrac{V_{ex}}{4} \cdot \varepsilon \cdot GF$
- ☐ **4-20 mA Current Loop Standard:** 4 mA = zero, 20 mA = full-scale, 0 mA = fault (broken wire).
- ☐ **Smart Sensor** = Sensor + Signal Conditioning + ADC + MCU/Digital Interface (e.g., BMP280).
- ☐ **V-to-I conversion** is essential for noise-immune long-distance sensor signal transmission.
- ☐ **Piezoelectric sensors** need **charge amplifiers** (high input impedance, capacitive input).
- ☐ **LVDT/RVDT sensors** need dedicated ICs (AD598, AD698) for **synchronous demodulation**.
- ☐ **For KTU numericals, always** show: Formula → Substitution → Final answer with correct **units**.

> **Final KTU Pro Tip:** When the question says *"with the help of a suitable IC"*, the expected answer for sensor amplification is almost always an **Instrumentation Amplifier (AD620/INA128)**. For temperature specifically, mention **LM35** (voltage output) or **AD590** (current output). For industrial 4-20 mA transmission, mention **XTR105/XTR101**. Memorize one example from each family.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Micro Electromechanical System (MEMS) - Advantages and Applications
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEEET411 - ELECTRONIC INSTRUMENTATION > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Micro Electromechanical System (MEMS) - Advantages and Applications # SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal KTU 2024 Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Micro Electromechanical Systems (MEMS)** are integrated micro-scale devices (or systems) that combine **electrical** and **mechanical** components fabricated using **semiconductor-compatible batch-fabrication techniques** (such as lithography, etching, and thin-film deposition). They typically range in size from **1 µm to 1000 µm** and integrate sensors, actuators, signal-conditioning circuits, and often an embedded microprocessor on a single silicon (or similar) substrate. In the KTU 2024 syllabus framework (PEEET411 — Electronic Instrumentation), MEMS is positioned as the **frontier where microelectronics meets mechanical engineering**, enabling transducers that sense physical, chemical, or biological phenomena and convert them into electrical signals (or vice-versa). ### Key Terminology (Mandatory for KTU 2-Mark & 3-Mark Definitions) | Term | Precise Meaning | |---|---| | **MEMS** | Micro-Electro-Mechanical Systems — devices with moving parts at the micrometer scale | | **MOEMS** | Micro-Optical-Electro-Mechanical Systems — MEMS with optical elements (mirrors, gratings) | | **BioMEMS** | MEMS designed for biological/medical applications (lab-on-chip) | | **Microsystem** | The generic term that may include MEMS, microfluidics, and microelectronics | | **Substrate** | The base silicon / glass / polymer wafer on which MEMS structures are built | | **Transducer** | The core MEMS element that converts one energy form to another | ## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The Swiss Army Knife on a Chip" > [!NOTE] > **Imagine a Swiss Army knife the size of a grain of sand.** > On a single tiny chip you have: a spring (mechanical), a capacitor (electrical), a mirror (optical), a micro-fluid channel (fluidic), and a tiny antenna (RF). All are carved out of silicon using the same lithography tools used to make computer chips. That is a **MEMS device**. A more engineering-friendly analogy: think of MEMS as the **"Lego block of the physical world"** — just as Legos let you snap mechanical parts together at centimeter scale, MEMS lets you build mechanical/electrical structures at micrometer scale using silicon as the raw material. ## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics The following values are heavily referenced in KTU 2024 derivations and must be memorized: - **Density of silicon (Si):** $\rho_{Si} = 2.33 \times 10^{3} \text{ kg/m}^3$ - **Young's Modulus of silicon:** $E_{Si} = 1.69 \times 10^{11} \text{ Pa}$ (≈ 169 GPa) - **Poisson's ratio of silicon:** $\nu_{Si} = 0.28$ - **Vacuum permittivity:** $\varepsilon_0 = 8.854 \times 10^{-12} \text{ F/m}$ - **Characteristic length scale of MEMS:** **1 µm — 1 mm** - **Surface-area-to-volume ratio:** **> 100 mm⁻¹** (orders of magnitude greater than macro-scale devices) - **Operating voltage of typical MEMS actuator:** **1 V — 100 V** > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why are these numbers special?** Because at the MEMS scale, **surface forces (electrostatic, friction, van der Waals)** begin to dominate over **volume forces (gravity, inertia)**. This counterintuitive behavior is the *defining feature* of MEMS and the heart of every KTU exam question on scaling. # SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Operational Concept — How MEMS "Think" at the Micro Scale A MEMS device is not a "miniature machine tool". It is governed by a **completely different physics** because forces scale differently when you shrink something by a factor `s`: - **Length** $\rightarrow$ scales as $s^{1}$ - **Area** $\rightarrow$ scales as $s^{2}$ - **Volume** $\rightarrow$ scales as $s^{3}$ - **Inertial / gravitational force** $\rightarrow$ scales as $s^{3}$ - **Surface / electrostatic / viscous force** $\rightarrow$ scales as $s^{2}$ - **Stiffness of a beam** $k$ $\rightarrow$ scales as $s^{1}$ - **Mass of a beam** $\rightarrow$ scales as $s^{3}$ - **Natural (resonant) frequency** $f_n \propto \sqrt{k/m}$ $\rightarrow$ scales as $s^{-1}$ (frequency *increases* as device shrinks) This means: as $s \to$ small, the ratio $\dfrac{F_{surface}}{F_{volume}} \propto \dfrac{s^{2}}{s^{3}} = \dfrac{1}{s}$ **grows without bound**. Hence at the microscale, *electrostatic and surface-tension forces win the battle against gravity and inertia*. This is why MEMS accelerometers, microphones, and RF switches are practically realizable. ### Step-by-Step Logic of a Generic MEMS Transducer 1. **Physical stimulus** (acceleration, pressure, temperature, chemical concentration) acts on the **sensing element** (proof-mass, diaphragm, cantilever). 2. The stimulus causes a **mechanical deformation** (deflection, stress, displacement). 3. The deformation is **transduced** into an electrical signal by a **sensing mechanism** — typically one of: - Piezoresistive (stress $\rightarrow$ resistance change) - Capacitive (displacement $\rightarrow$ capacitance change) - Piezoelectric (stress $\rightarrow$ charge) - Optical / Resonant / Thermal 4. The electrical signal is **conditioned** (amplified, filtered, digitized) by on-chip CMOS circuitry. 5. The output is delivered as an **analog voltage, digital word, or frequency** to the external world. ## 2.2 The Five Major Categories of MEMS Devices | # | Category | Example Device | Sensing Principle | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | **Inertial Sensors** | Accelerometer, Gyroscope | Capacitive / Piezoresistive | | 2 | **Pressure Sensors** | Barometric, Blood-pressure | Piezoresistive / Capacitive | | 3 | **RF MEMS** | Switches, Varactors, Filters | Electrostatic actuation | | 4 | **Optical MEMS (MOEMS)** | DMD (Digital Micromirror Device) | Electrostatic + Mirror | | 5 | **Bio/Fluidic MEMS** | Lab-on-chip, DNA arrays | Electrochemical / Optical | ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **The following table is the ONLY set of formulas you need to memorize for the MEMS sub-topic in KTU 2024 PEEET411 Module 1.** All KTU 14-mark derivations in this module are subsets of these equations. | # | Equation (LaTeX) | Variable Definitions | Engineering Use | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $F_{elec} = \tfrac{1}{2}\,\dfrac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}{d^{2}}\, V^{2}$ | $F_{elec}$ = electrostatic force; $\varepsilon_0 = 8.854 \times 10^{-12}$ F/m; $\varepsilon_r$ = relative permittivity; $A$ = plate area; $d$ = gap; $V$ = applied voltage | Force produced by a MEMS capacitive actuator | | 2 | $C = \dfrac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}{d}$ | $C$ = capacitance (Farads) | Capacitive displacement sensor | | 3 | $\dfrac{\Delta C}{C} = \dfrac{\Delta d}{d}$ (for gap-modulated capacitor) | $\Delta C$ = capacitance change; $\Delta d$ = displacement | Sensitivity of gap-mode capacitive sensor | | 4 | $\dfrac{\Delta C}{C} = \dfrac{\Delta A}{A}$ (for area-modulated capacitor) | $\Delta A$ = overlap-area change | Sensitivity of area-mode capacitive sensor | | 5 | $\sigma = E\,\varepsilon$ | $\sigma$ = stress; $E$ = Young's modulus; $\varepsilon$ = strain | Hooke's law for MEMS beam/diaphragm | | 6 | $f_n = \dfrac{1}{2\pi}\,\sqrt{\dfrac{k}{m}}$ | $f_n$ = natural frequency; $k$ = stiffness; $m$ = mass | Resonant frequency of proof-mass system | | 7 | $R_n = \dfrac{\rho\, L}{A}$ | $R_n$ = resistance; $\rho$ = resistivity; $L$ = length; $A$ = area | Piezoresistor (used in strain-sensing) | | 8 | $P = \dfrac{F}{A}$ | $P$ = pressure; $F$ = force; $A$ = area | Pressure sensor input relation | | 9 | $\tau = \dfrac{\eta\,\partial u}{\partial y}$ | $\tau$ = viscous shear stress; $\eta$ = dynamic viscosity | Damping in MEMS (squeeze-film damping) | | 10 | $Re = \dfrac{\rho\, v\, L}{\eta}$ | $Re$ = Reynolds number; $\rho$ = fluid density; $v$ = velocity; $L$ = characteristic length | Determines laminar flow regime in microfluidics | | 11 | $\gamma = \dfrac{1}{L}$ (scaling factor) | $\gamma$ = ratio of surface/volume forces | Central scaling-law metric for MEMS | | 12 | $k \propto \dfrac{E\, w\, t^{3}}{L^{3}}$ | $k$ = stiffness of cantilever; $w$ = width; $t$ = thickness; $L$ = length | Stiffness scaling of MEMS beam | > [!NOTE] > **CRITICAL TYPOGRAPHY RULE (KTU Exam):** Inside all mark-down tables above, the absolute-value bars `$\vert x \vert$` and `$\mid x \mid$` notation was deliberately used instead of `|x|` to avoid breaking the table-rendering engine. ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Automotive**: MEMS accelerometers trigger airbag deployment within 15 ms (trigger threshold ≈ 4g). - **Consumer Electronics**: Smartphones contain ≥ 7 MEMS chips (accelerometer, gyroscope, magnetometer, barometer, microphone, RF switch, fingerprint sensor). - **Medical**: MEMS pressure sensors are used in catheters for blood-pressure monitoring. - **Industrial**: MEMS gyroscopes stabilize drones and industrial robots. - **Aerospace**: MEMS accelerometers and gyroscopes are part of **Inertial Measurement Units (IMUs)** in spacecraft attitude-control systems. - **IoT**: MEMS are the *physical-interface enabler* of every IoT sensor node (the "senses" of the IoT). # SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix > [!IMPORTANT] > **Pedagogical Note (KTU 2024 OBE):** Section 3 is the highest-weightage section in the KTU 14-mark answer script. The Board Examiner expects (i) a clear logical statement, (ii) every algebraic transition shown explicitly, (iii) correct unit analysis, and (iv) a concluding physical interpretation. All four pillars are demonstrated below. --- ## 3.1 Derivation #1 — Scaling of Surface vs. Volume Forces in MEMS **Problem Statement:** Show analytically why surface forces dominate over volume forces as a MEMS device shrinks. Let the linear scaling factor be $\gamma = \dfrac{L_{micro}}{L_{macro}}$. ### Step 1 — Define the linear scaling factor Let the original macro-device have characteristic length $L_0$. After miniaturization, the new length is $L = \gamma L_0$ where $0 < \gamma < 1$. ### Step 2 — Scale a typical volume (gravity / inertial) force A gravitational force is $F_{vol} = m g = \rho \, V \, g$, where $V \propto L^{3}$. After scaling: $$ F_{vol}^{micro} = \rho \, (\gamma L_0)^{3} \, g = \gamma^{3} \, F_{vol}^{macro} $$ **Interpretation:** Volume force shrinks by a factor of $\gamma^{3}$. For $\gamma = 0.001$ (1 mm scaled to 1 µm), volume force drops by $10^{-9}$ (one billionth). ### Step 3 — Scale a typical surface (electrostatic) force An electrostatic force is $F_{surf} = \tfrac{1}{2}\,\varepsilon\,A\,E^{2}$. The area scales as $L^{2}$, while the field (voltage/gap) can be kept constant, so: $$ F_{surf}^{micro} = \gamma^{2} \, F_{surf}^{macro} $$ **Interpretation:** Surface force shrinks only by $\gamma^{2}$. For $\gamma = 0.001$, surface force drops by $10^{-6}$ (a millionth). ### Step 4 — Form the dominance ratio $$ \dfrac{F_{surf}^{micro} / F_{vol}^{micro}}{F_{surf}^{macro} / F_{vol}^{macro}} = \dfrac{\gamma^{2}}{\gamma^{3}} = \dfrac{1}{\gamma} $$ Therefore: $$ \boxed{\;\dfrac{(F_{surf}/F_{vol})_{micro}}{(F_{surf}/F_{vol})_{macro}} = \dfrac{1}{\gamma}\;} $$ ### Step 5 — Numerical substitution Take $\gamma = 10^{-3}$. Then: $$ \dfrac{1}{\gamma} = \dfrac{1}{10^{-3}} = 10^{3} $$ **Result:** At the MEMS scale, the *ratio* of surface force to volume force is **1000 times greater** than at macro scale. This proves quantitatively that surface effects dominate — the foundation of all MEMS design intuition. --- ## 3.2 Derivation #2 — Sensitivity of a Capacitive MEMS Accelerometer **Problem Statement:** Derive the sensitivity $\dfrac{\Delta C}{C}$ of a parallel-plate capacitive MEMS accelerometer when the proof-mass displaces by $\Delta x$ under acceleration $a$. ### Step 1 — Nominal (undisturbed) capacitance $$ C_0 = \dfrac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}{d_0} $$ where $d_0$ is the nominal gap between the two parallel plates. ### Step 2 — Capacitance after displacement $\Delta x$ If the proof-mass moves **toward** the fixed plate (gap-modulated, single-sided), the new gap becomes $d = d_0 - \Delta x$. Therefore: $$ C = \dfrac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}{d_0 - \Delta x} $$ ### Step 3 — Compute the change in capacitance $$ \Delta C = C - C_0 = \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A \left[ \dfrac{1}{d_0 - \Delta x} - \dfrac{1}{d_0} \right] $$ ### Step 4 — Common denominator $$ \Delta C = \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A \cdot \dfrac{d_0 - (d_0 - \Delta x)}{d_0(d_0 - \Delta x)} = \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A \cdot \dfrac{\Delta x}{d_0(d_0 - \Delta x)} $$ ### Step 5 — Form the fractional change $$ \dfrac{\Delta C}{C_0} = \dfrac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A \cdot \dfrac{\Delta x}{d_0(d_0 - \Delta x)}}{\dfrac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}{d_0}} = \dfrac{\Delta x}{d_0 - \Delta x} $$ ### Step 6 — Small-displacement linearization For MEMS accelerometers, $\Delta x \ll d_0$. Apply the binomial approximation $(1 - x)^{-1} \approx 1 + x$ for small $x$: $$ \dfrac{\Delta C}{C_0} \approx \dfrac{\Delta x}{d_0} \cdot \dfrac{1}{1 - \dfrac{\Delta x}{d_0}} \approx \dfrac{\Delta x}{d_0} $$ Hence: $$ \boxed{\;\dfrac{\Delta C}{C_0} \approx \dfrac{\Delta x}{d_0}\;} $$ ### Step 7 — Connect $\Delta x$ to applied acceleration $a$ For a proof-mass $m$ suspended on a spring of stiffness $k$, quasi-static equilibrium under acceleration $a$ gives $k \Delta x = m a$, so: $$ \Delta x = \dfrac{m a}{k} $$ ### Step 8 — Substitute to obtain acceleration sensitivity $$ \boxed{\;\dfrac{\Delta C}{C_0} = \dfrac{m\, a}{k\, d_0}\;} $$ ### Step 9 — Units check $[\Delta C/C]$ is dimensionless. The right-hand side: numerator has units of kg·m/s² = N; denominator has units of (N/m)·m = N. Ratio is dimensionless. ✔ **Physical Interpretation:** To *maximize sensitivity*, the designer should (i) **increase mass $m$**, (ii) **decrease stiffness $k$**, (iii) **reduce gap $d_0$**, and (iv) **increase nominal capacitance $C_0$**. This is exactly the design trade-off that defines the MEMS accelerometer industry. --- ## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Advantages vs. Limitations of MEMS | # | Parameter | Macro-scale Transducer | MEMS Transducer | Engineering Consequence | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | Size | 1 – 10 cm | 10 µm – 1 mm | Enables wearable / implantable | | 2 | Mass | 10 – 100 g | 1 – 100 µg | Suits UAVs, smart-dust | | 3 | Power consumption | 100 mW – 1 W | 1 µW – 10 mW | Suits battery-powered IoT | | 4 | Cost per unit | $5 – $500 | $0.10 – $5 | Mass-market deployment | | 5 | Frequency response | < 1 kHz | up to 1 MHz | Faster sensors, wider bandwidth | | 6 | Fabrication | Manual assembly | Batch (wafer-level) lithography | > 10⁶ units/wafer | | 7 | Reliability | Limited by wear | No contact (often capacitive) | > 10⁹ cycles MTBF | | 8 | Signal-to-noise | High SNR (large signal) | Lower SNR (small signal) | Requires on-chip amplification | | 9 | Packaging | Standard | Hermetic, wafer-level | Costly & technically demanding | | 10 | Stiction / adhesion | Negligible | Significant (micro-scale) | A primary MEMS failure mode | --- ## 3.4 Derivation #3 — Natural Frequency of a MEMS Cantilever Beam (for Resonant Sensors) **Problem Statement:** A MEMS cantilever of length $L$, width $w$, thickness $t$ acts as a resonant mass-spring system. Derive the resonant frequency $f_n$. ### Step 1 — Effective stiffness of a cantilever For a cantilever fixed at one end, the tip stiffness in the vertical direction is: $$ k = \dfrac{E\, w\, t^{3}}{4\, L^{3}} $$ ### Step 2 — Effective mass of the cantilever (first-mode approximation) For a uniform cantilever, the effective mass participating in the fundamental mode is: $$ m_{eff} = 0.24 \,\rho_{Si}\, L\, w\, t $$ ### Step 3 — Substitute into the natural-frequency formula $$ f_n = \dfrac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\dfrac{k}{m_{eff}}} $$ $$ f_n = \dfrac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\dfrac{\dfrac{E\, w\, t^{3}}{4\, L^{3}}}{0.24\,\rho_{Si}\, L\, w\, t}} $$ ### Step 4 — Simplify by canceling $w$ and one $t$ $$ f_n = \dfrac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\dfrac{E\, t^{2}}{4 \cdot 0.24 \,\rho_{Si}\, L^{4}}} $$ $$ f_n = \dfrac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\dfrac{E\, t^{2}}{0.96 \,\rho_{Si}\, L^{4}}} $$ ### Step 5 — Final boxed result $$ \boxed{\;f_n = \dfrac{1}{2\pi}\,\dfrac{t}{L^{2}}\,\sqrt{\dfrac{E}{0.96 \,\rho_{Si}}}\;} $$ ### Step 6 — Numerical evaluation (typical MEMS dimensions) Take $E = 1.69 \times 10^{11}$ Pa, $\rho_{Si} = 2330$ kg/m³, $t = 5$ µm $= 5 \times 10^{-6}$ m, $L = 200$ µm $= 2 \times 10^{-4}$ m. $$ \dfrac{E}{0.96\,\rho_{Si}} = \dfrac{1.69 \times 10^{11}}{0.96 \times 2330} = \dfrac{1.69 \times 10^{11}}{2236.8} = 7.557 \times 10^{7} \text{ m}^{2}/\text{s}^{2} $$ $$ \sqrt{7.557 \times 10^{7}} = 8.694 \times 10^{3} \text{ m/s} $$ $$ \dfrac{t}{L^{2}} = \dfrac{5 \times 10^{-6}}{(2 \times 10^{-4})^{2}} = \dfrac{5 \times 10^{-6}}{4 \times 10^{-8}} = 125 \text{ m}^{-1} $$ $$ f_n = \dfrac{1}{2\pi} \times 8.694 \times 10^{3} \times 125 = \dfrac{1}{2\pi} \times 1.087 \times 10^{6} $$ $$ \boxed{\;f_n \approx 1.73 \times 10^{5} \text{ Hz} = 173 \text{ kHz}\;} $$ **Physical Interpretation:** A typical MEMS cantilever resonates in the **100 kHz range**, which is the operating frequency band of MEMS gyroscopes, mass-sensors, and resonant chemical sensors. This high $f_n$ is why MEMS devices are immune to most ambient mechanical noise (which lies below 1 kHz). # SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Diagram 1 — Generic MEMS Transducer Architecture (Block-Level Functional Flow) This diagram maps the **complete signal path** of a MEMS transducer from physical stimulus to electrical output, replacing a complex cross-sectional drawing with a clear functional block architecture (Board-examiner friendly). ```mermaid flowchart LR inputA[Physical Stimulus
Acceleration Pressure
Temperature Chemical] --> blockB[MEMS Sensing Element
Proof Mass Diaphragm
Cantilever Bridge] blockB --> blockC[Transduction Mechanism
Capacitive Piezoresistive
Piezoelectric Optical] blockC --> blockD[Analog Front End
Charge Amplifier
Instrumentation Amp] blockD --> blockE[Signal Conditioner
Filter Offset Trim
Temperature Comp] blockE --> blockF[ADC
Sigma Delta SAR] blockF --> blockG[Digital Processor
Calibration DSP] blockG --> outputH[Electrical Output
Analog V Digital Bits
PWM Frequency] ``` **Reading the diagram (KTU Examiner's tip):** Trace each block from left to right. The **Physical Stimulus → Sensing Element → Transduction Mechanism** triplet forms the *MEMS core*. The remaining three blocks are *CMOS support circuitry* integrated on the same die or in a stacked package. --- ## 4.2 Diagram 2 — Advantages Taxonomy of MEMS This tree-style diagram organises the **advantages of MEMS** into the four canonical pillars tested in KTU ESE: *Performance*, *Economic*, *Integration*, *Application*. ```mermaid flowchart TD rootA[MEMS Advantages] --> nodeB[Performance Advantages] rootA --> nodeC[Economic Advantages] rootA --> nodeD[Integration Advantages] rootA --> nodeE[Application Advantages] nodeB --> nodeF[Small Size and Mass] nodeB --> nodeG[Low Power Consumption] nodeB --> nodeH[High Frequency Response] nodeB --> nodeI[High Sensitivity and Resolution] nodeC --> nodeJ[Batch Fabrication Low Cost] nodeC --> nodeK[Mass Production Compatibility] nodeC --> nodeL[Reduced Assembly Steps] nodeD --> nodeM[Monolithic Integration with CMOS] nodeD --> nodeN[On Chip Signal Conditioning] nodeD --> nodeO[Multi Sensor Fusion on Chip] nodeE --> nodeP[Wide Domain Coverage] nodeE --> nodeQ[Biomedical Compatibility] nodeE --> nodeR[IoT and Wearable Enablement] ``` **Reading the diagram:** When KTU asks "List any six advantages of MEMS" (3-mark question), pick **two from each of the four branches** to demonstrate structured knowledge. Examiners reward organized taxonomy answers. --- ## 4.3 Diagram 3 — Application Domain Map This radial diagram shows the **six application domains** of MEMS as they appear in KTU Module 1 syllabus, with one representative device per domain. ```mermaid flowchart LR hubM[MEMS Core Technology] --> appA[Automotive] hubM --> appB[Consumer Electronics] hubM --> appC[Healthcare and Biomedical] hubM --> appD[Industrial and Aerospace] hubM --> appE[Telecommunications RF] hubM --> appF[Environmental Monitoring] appA --> devA1[Airbag Accelerometer
TPMS Pressure Sensor] appB --> devB1[Smartphone IMU
MEMS Microphone
Fingerprint Sensor] appC --> devC1[Blood Pressure Catheter
Drug Delivery Micro Pump
Lab on Chip] appD --> devD1[MEMS Gyro for Drones
Inertial Navigation
Vibration Monitor] appE --> devE1[RF MEMS Switch
Tunable Capacitor
Filter Bank] appF --> devF1[Gas Sensor
Particulate Matter Sensor
Humidity Sensor] ``` **Reading the diagram:** When KTU asks "Explain the applications of MEMS in automotive industry" (typical 7-mark sub-question), pick any **two devices from `appA`** and explain the working principle, the MEMS transducer used, and the system-level benefit. A flowchart answer scores more than a bullet-list answer. # SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Define Micro Electro Mechanical Systems (MEMS). List any four advantages of MEMS.** **Model Answer (Board-marked key):** > **Definition:** MEMS are integrated micro-scale devices that combine electrical and mechanical components, fabricated using semiconductor-compatible batch-fabrication processes. They typically have characteristic dimensions in the range **1 µm to 1 mm**. **Four Advantages:** 1. **Miniaturization** — Enables devices with feature sizes of a few microns, allowing integration into wearable and implantable systems. 2. **Low Power Consumption** — Typical power draw is **1 µW – 10 mW**, suiting battery-operated IoT nodes. 3. **Batch Fabrication & Low Cost** — Hundreds of devices are produced simultaneously on a single silicon wafer, reducing per-unit cost to as low as **$0.10**. 4. **High Sensitivity and Frequency Response** — Surface-area dominance yields high signal-to-noise ratios, with operating bandwidths up to **1 MHz**. > [!WARNING] > **Valuation Warning — Part A:** Do *not* write vague advantages like "they are small". Examiners expect *quantified* advantages ("small — sub-millimetre"), and at least one **numerical metric** in your answer. Generic answers lose 1–2 marks even if the content is correct. --- ### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **Differentiate between MEMS and conventional macro-scale transducers in terms of (i) size, (ii) fabrication, (iii) dominant forces.** **Model Answer:** | # | Parameter | Conventional Transducer | MEMS Transducer | |---|---|---|---| | (i) | **Size** | 1 cm – 10 cm | 1 µm – 1 mm | | (ii) | **Fabrication** | Manual assembly of discrete parts | Semiconductor batch fabrication (lithography + etching) | | (iii) | **Dominant Forces** | Volume forces (inertia, gravity) | Surface forces (electrostatic, van der Waals, viscous) | > [!NOTE] > **Examiner's tip:** Use a *table* format for "differentiate" questions. Tables score higher than paragraph-form answers because the comparison is immediate and complete. --- ## 5.2 PART B — Full-Length 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice Pattern) > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU ESE Pattern (2024 Scheme):** Each module has an internal choice. Two 14-mark questions are presented; the student answers ONE. Each 14-mark question is split into **(a) 7 marks** and **(b) 7 marks**. Below, Question A and Question B are completely independent, mirroring the actual KTU question paper layout. --- ### ⭐ QUESTION A — 14 Marks `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **(a) [7 Marks]** With a neat functional block diagram, explain the working principle of a MEMS capacitive accelerometer. Derive the relation between fractional change in capacitance and the applied acceleration. **Model Answer:** **Block diagram (drawn in script):** Refer to *Section 4 Diagram 1* with the relevant blocks: *Acceleration → Proof Mass → Capacitive Gaps → Differential Capacitance → Charge Amplifier → Output Voltage*. **Working Principle:** 1. The accelerometer consists of a **silicon proof-mass** suspended by four compliant springs over a silicon substrate. 2. Two sets of **interdigitated / parallel-plate capacitors** ($C_1$ and $C_2$) are formed between the proof-mass fingers and the fixed substrate fingers. 3. Under acceleration $a$, the proof-mass deflects by $\Delta x$ in the sensing direction. One capacitor's gap *decreases* ($C_1 \uparrow$) while the other *increases* ($C_2 \downarrow$), forming a **differential capacitive half-bridge**. 4. The change in capacitance is read out by a **charge amplifier** or **switched-capacitor circuit**. 5. The differential output voltage is: $V_{out} = \dfrac{V_{ref}\,(C_1 - C_2)}{C_1 + C_2}$ **Derivation:** Refer to Section 3.2 Steps 1–9. The final boxed result is: $$ \boxed{\;\dfrac{\Delta C}{C_0} = \dfrac{m\, a}{k\, d_0}\;} $$ This shows that sensitivity grows linearly with mass $m$, and inversely with stiffness $k$ and gap $d_0$. --- **(b) [7 Marks]** List any **six advantages** and **six applications** of MEMS. **Model Answer:** **Six Advantages (categorised):** 1. **Small size and mass** — sub-millimetre scale. 2. **Low power consumption** — µW to mW range. 3. **Batch-fabrication** — hundreds of devices per wafer → low cost. 4. **High sensitivity** — surface-area dominance amplifies transducer response. 5. **High frequency response** — natural frequencies in the kHz–MHz range. 6. **Compatibility with CMOS** — single-chip integration of sensor + signal conditioning. **Six Applications:** 1. **Automotive** — airbag accelerometers, tyre-pressure monitoring. 2. **Consumer electronics** — smartphone IMUs, MEMS microphones. 3. **Biomedical** — blood-pressure sensors in catheters, drug-delivery micropumps. 4. **Aerospace / Drones** — MEMS gyroscopes for attitude control. 5. **Telecommunications** — RF MEMS switches in mobile handsets. 6. **Environmental** — gas sensors, particulate-matter detectors. > [!WARNING] > **Examiner's Valuation Warning — Part B (b):** Students commonly write "they are used in medical field" or "they are used in mobiles" — both too vague to score. You MUST specify the *exact device name* (e.g., "MEMS gyroscope in iPhone") and the *engineering function* (e.g., "screen rotation, image stabilization"). Each application must carry a *device + function* pair, otherwise 0.5 marks are docked per application. --- ### ⭐ QUESTION B — 14 Marks `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the principle of operation of a MEMS piezoresistive pressure sensor with a neat diagram. Show that fractional change in resistance is proportional to applied pressure. **Model Answer:** **Working Principle:** 1. A thin **silicon diaphragm** is etched from a silicon wafer using anisotropic etching (KOH or DRIE). 2. Four **piezoresistors** are implanted on the edges of the diaphragm, aligned along the <110> crystal direction (maximum piezoresistive coefficient). 3. They are connected in a **Wheatstone bridge** configuration. 4. When external pressure $P$ is applied, the diaphragm deflects. The two piezoresistors at the diaphragm edges experience **tensile stress** (resistance increases), while the two at perpendicular edges experience **compressive stress** (resistance decreases). 5. The bridge becomes unbalanced and produces a **differential output voltage** proportional to pressure. **Diagram (drawn in script):** A square diaphragm with four piezoresistors ($R_1, R_2, R_3, R_4$) at its four edges, connected in a Wheatstone bridge, with the supply $V_{ex}$ across one diagonal and $V_{out}$ measured across the other. **Derivation (Fractional change in resistance vs pressure):** The piezoresistive relation in silicon is: $$ \dfrac{\Delta R}{R} = \pi_l \, \sigma_l + \pi_t \, \sigma_t $$ where $\pi_l$ and $\pi_t$ are the *longitudinal* and *transverse* piezoresistive coefficients, and $\sigma_l, \sigma_t$ are the longitudinal and transverse stresses. For a square diaphragm of side $a$ and thickness $t$ under pressure $P$, the maximum stress at the diaphragm edge is: $$ \sigma_{max} = \dfrac{0.308\, P\, a^{2}}{t^{2}} $$ For p-type silicon along <110>, $\pi_l \approx 71.8 \times 10^{-11}$ Pa$^{-1}$ and $\pi_t \approx -66.3 \times 10^{-11}$ Pa$^{-1}$. Since two resistors see $\sigma_l$ and two see $\sigma_t$, the net bridge unbalance is: $$ \dfrac{\Delta R}{R} \approx \pi_l \, \sigma_{max} - \pi_t \, \sigma_{max} = (\pi_l - \pi_t)\,\sigma_{max} $$ Substituting $\sigma_{max}$: $$ \dfrac{\Delta R}{R} = (\pi_l - \pi_t) \cdot \dfrac{0.308\, P\, a^{2}}{t^{2}} $$ Since $(\pi_l - \pi_t)$, $a$, and $t$ are constants of the device: $$ \boxed{\;\dfrac{\Delta R}{R} = k_{sensor}\, P\;} $$ where $k_{sensor}$ is the **pressure-sensitivity constant** of the sensor. This proves that the fractional resistance change is **directly proportional** to the applied pressure. --- **(b) [7 Marks]** With neat sketches, explain the working of (i) MEMS microphone and (ii) Digital Micromirror Device (DMD). **Model Answer:** **(i) MEMS Microphone:** 1. It consists of a **flexible diaphragm** (typically polysilicon) and a **rigid back-plate** separated by a small air gap (~ 1–4 µm). 2. Sound waves cause the diaphragm to vibrate, modulating the **air-gap capacitance** between diaphragm and back-plate. 3. The varying capacitance is read out by a **charge-pump / ASIC** that converts it into an audio-frequency voltage. 4. A small hole in the back-plate allows air to escape, preventing pressure build-up that would otherwise damp the diaphragm. 5. **Application:** Smartphones, hearing aids, voice assistants. **Working equation:** Following Section 3.2, $\dfrac{\Delta C}{C_0} = \dfrac{\Delta x}{d_0}$, where $\Delta x$ is the sound-pressure-induced displacement of the diaphragm. **(ii) Digital Micromirror Device (DMD):** 1. A DMD chip (used in DLP projectors) contains an array of **microscopic mirrors** (~ 13.7 µm × 13.7 µm each), one per pixel. 2. Each mirror is mounted on a **torsional hinge** above a CMOS address circuit. 3. Electrostatic electrodes beneath the mirror apply a torque that **tilts the mirror by ±12°** about its hinge. 4. At +12°, the mirror reflects light **into** the projection lens (pixel ON); at −12°, light is directed to a **light absorber** (pixel OFF). 5. By rapidly switching each mirror (binary pulse-width modulation), the projector produces **256 grey levels** per color channel. **Application:** DLP projectors, cinema, structured-light 3D scanners. > [!WARNING] > **Examiner's Valuation Warning — DMD Question:** Students often confuse MOEMS (Micro-Optical-EMS) with MEMS. DMD is technically a **MOEMS** device because of its optical mirror — examiners will accept both classifications but reward the *more precise* term. Always mention that DMD is an example of **MOEMS**, a subclass of MEMS. Failure to do so = loss of 0.5 marks. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Final Rapid-Revision Checklist — Print this section before your KTU exam.** ### 🔑 Mandatory Definitions - **MEMS:** Micro-Electro-Mechanical Systems; range **1 µm – 1 mm**. - **MOEMS:** MEMS with optical components (e.g., DMD). - **BioMEMS:** MEMS for biological / medical applications. ### 🔑 Five Canonical MEMS Advantages 1. Miniaturization (size ↓, mass ↓) 2. Low power consumption (µW range) 3. Batch fabrication → low cost 4. High sensitivity (surface-force dominance) 5. CMOS compatibility → on-chip signal conditioning ### 🔑 Six Application Domains (Recall Order) 1. Automotive (airbag, TPMS) 2. Consumer (smartphone, mic) 3. Biomedical (BP catheter, drug pump) 4. Aerospace / Drones (gyro, IMU) 5. RF / Telecom (switch, varactor) 6. Environmental (gas, humidity sensor) ### 🔑 Three Equations You Must Derive Without Notes 1. Surface/volume force scaling: $\dfrac{F_{surf}/F_{vol}}{F_{surf}/F_{vol}}_{macro} = \dfrac{1}{\gamma}$ 2. Capacitive accelerometer sensitivity: $\dfrac{\Delta C}{C_0} = \dfrac{m\, a}{k\, d_0}$ 3. Piezoresistive pressure relation: $\dfrac{\Delta R}{R} = (\pi_l - \pi_t)\,\dfrac{0.308\,P\,a^{2}}{t^{2}}$ ### 🔑 Key Numerical Values to Memorize - $E_{Si} = 1.69 \times 10^{11}$ Pa - $\rho_{Si} = 2330$ kg/m³ - $\varepsilon_0 = 8.854 \times 10^{-12}$ F/m - Typical MEMS gap $d_0 \approx 1$–$4$ µm - Typical MEMS thickness $t \approx 5$–$25$ µm - MEMS natural frequency ≈ **100 kHz** for a 200 µm cantilever ### 🔑 Common Valuation Pitfalls to Avoid - ❌ Writing "MEMS are small" without specifying size range. - ❌ Confusing *piezoresistive* with *piezoelectric* sensors. - ❌ Drawing the *cross-section* (Board-exam time-killer) instead of the *block diagram*. - ❌ Forgetting to mention CMOS integration when asked about MEMS advantages. - ❌ Using the vertical pipe `|` inside mark-down tables in the answer booklet (rendering glitches). ### 🔑 One-Sentence Exam-Ready Summary > *"MEMS are batch-fabricated silicon devices that integrate mechanical sensing elements and electrical readout circuits on a single chip; their defining feature is the dominance of surface forces over volume forces, which enables low-power, low-cost, high-sensitivity transducers used across automotive, biomedical, consumer, aerospace, RF, and environmental applications."* --- > **END OF NOTES — PEEET411 MODULE 1 — MEMS Advantages and Applications** > *Prepared in alignment with KTU 2024 Scheme, OBE-NEP 2020 framework, and Revised Bloom's Taxonomy (Levels: Remember, Understand, Apply, Analyze).*

topics.title: MEMS Micro Sensors and Actuators
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET411 - ELECTRONIC INSTRUMENTATION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** MEMS Micro Sensors and Actuators

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is MEMS?

**MEMS (Micro-Electro-Mechanical Systems)** are miniaturized integrated systems that combine mechanical elements, sensors, actuators, and electronics on a common silicon substrate through microfabrication technology. These devices typically have features in the range of **1 µm to 1000 µm** and operate on physical, chemical, optical, or biological principles.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Definition (2024 Scheme):** MEMS refers to the integration of mechanical elements, sensors, actuators, and electronics on a common silicon substrate through microfabrication technology. The functional elements of MEMS are realized by integrating moving mechanical structures with microelectronic circuits.

> [!NOTE]
> **MEMS vs. Microsystem:** While MEMS focuses on micro-scale electromechanical devices, the broader term **Microsystem Technology (MST)** also includes micro-optical, micro-fluidic, and micro-chemical systems. MEMS is a sub-discipline of MST.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The Smartwatch on a Chip**
> Imagine a tiny "smartwatch" shrunk down to the size of a grain of sand, but without a screen. It has microscopic beams that can bend, gears that can rotate, membranes that can sense pressure, and tiny circuits that convert physical movement into electrical signals — all fabricated together on the same silicon chip. That, in essence, is MEMS: a complete mechanical + electronic system shrunk to the microscale.

## 1.3 Historical Context

The roots of MEMS can be traced back to **1954** with the invention of the **piezoresistive effect in silicon by C.S. Smith**. The modern era of MEMS began in the **1980s** with the development of surface micromachining at **Stanford University** and bulk micromachining at the **University of Wisconsin**.

## 1.4 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

The following physical constants are frequently used in MEMS design and analysis:

| Constant / Metric | Symbol | Value / Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Boltzmann's constant | $k_B$ | $1.38 \times 10^{-23} \, \text{J/K}$ |
| Young's modulus of silicon | $E_{Si}$ | $\mathbf{190 \, \text{GPa}}$ |
| Poisson's ratio of silicon (100) | $\nu_{Si}$ | $0.28$ |
| Density of silicon | $\rho_{Si}$ | $2330 \, \text{kg/m}^3$ |
| Piezoelectric coefficient of quartz | $d_{11}$ | $2.3 \, \text{pC/N}$ |
| Surface tension of water (25°C) | $\gamma$ | $0.072 \, \text{N/m}$ |
| Standard atmospheric pressure | $P_{atm}$ | $101.325 \, \text{kPa}$ |
| Characteristic length scale | $L_0$ | $\mathbf{1 \, \mu m}$ to $1000 \, \mu m$ |

## 1.5 Why MEMS? — The Scaling Advantage

When dimensions shrink to the microscale, several dominant forces change drastically. This is captured by the **scaling laws** that govern microsystems.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Force Dominance in MEMS:** At the microscale, **surface forces (electrostatic, surface tension, friction) dominate**, while **volume forces (inertia, gravity, magnetic) become negligible**. This is the fundamental reason MEMS devices use electrostatic actuation more often than electromagnetic actuation.

The force scaling follows the relation:

$$F \propto L^n \quad \text{where } n \text{ is the power of length}$$

For surface forces (electrostatic, surface tension), $n = 2$, while for volume forces (inertia, gravity), $n = 3$ or higher. Hence, as $L$ decreases, surface forces become dominant.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 MEMS Materials

MEMS devices are constructed from a wide variety of materials, each selected for specific mechanical, electrical, or chemical properties.

### 2.1.1 Substrate Materials

- **Single-crystal Silicon (Si):** The most widely used substrate material. Exhibits excellent mechanical properties and is compatible with IC fabrication.
- **Polysilicon:** Used for structural layers in surface micromachining.
- **Silicon-on-Insulator (SOI):** Provides excellent electrical isolation and is used for high-performance MEMS.

### 2.1.2 Structural Materials

- **Silicon dioxide (SiO₂):** Used as a sacrificial layer and as a structural material.
- **Silicon nitride (Si₃N₄):** High tensile strength, used for membranes and pressure sensors.
- **Polymers (SU-8, PDMS, PMMA):** Used for microfluidics and flexible MEMS.

### 2.1.3 Functional Materials

- **Piezoelectric materials (PZT, ZnO, AlN):** Used for sensing and actuation.
- **Shape Memory Alloys (Nitinol):** Used for large-displacement actuators.
- **Magnetic materials (Ni, Co, permalloy):** Used for magnetic actuation.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Silicon?** Silicon is favored because:
> 1. It has near-perfect mechanical properties (no mechanical hysteresis).
> 2. It is compatible with CMOS processes.
> 3. It exhibits piezoresistivity (change in resistance under stress).
> 4. High Young's modulus (190 GPa) ensures rigidity.

## 2.2 MEMS Fabrication Processes

MEMS fabrication borrows heavily from semiconductor (IC) fabrication but extends it with steps for creating three-dimensional mechanical structures.

### 2.2.1 Photolithography

Photolithography is the process of transferring geometric patterns from a mask onto a substrate using light.

**Steps:**
1. **Substrate preparation** — cleaning and dehydration bake.
2. **Photoresist coating** — spin-coating a light-sensitive polymer.
3. **Soft bake** — drives off solvents.
4. **Exposure** — UV light through the mask exposes selective regions.
5. **Development** — exposed (positive resist) or unexposed (negative resist) regions are dissolved.
6. **Hard bake** — strengthens the remaining photoresist.

### 2.2.2 Etching

Etching is the selective removal of material.

- **Wet etching:** Uses liquid chemicals (e.g., KOH, TMAH, HF).
  - **Isotropic:** Etches equally in all directions (e.g., HF for SiO₂).
  - **Anisotropic:** Etches preferentially along crystal planes (e.g., KOH for Si (100)).
- **Dry etching:** Uses plasma or reactive ions (e.g., RIE — Reactive Ion Etching, DRIE — Deep RIE / Bosch process).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KOH Anisotropic Etching of Silicon:** KOH etches Si (100) planes approximately **400 times faster** than Si (111) planes. This selectivity is exploited to create V-shaped grooves, pyramidal pits, and well-defined mechanical structures.

### 2.2.3 Deposition

- **Physical Vapor Deposition (PVD):** Sputtering, evaporation.
- **Chemical Vapor Deposition (CVD):** LPCVD, PECVD.
- **Electroplating:** For thick metal structures.

### 2.2.4 Bonding

- **Anodic bonding:** Glass-Si bonding using electrostatic force.
- **Fusion bonding:** Direct Si-Si bonding at high temperature.
- **Eutectic bonding:** Using intermediate metal layers (e.g., Au-Si).

### 2.2.5 Sacrificial Layer Process

A sacrificial layer is deposited, patterned, and then a structural layer is deposited over it. The sacrificial layer is later etched away, releasing the free-standing structure. **SiO₂** is the most common sacrificial layer, etched using **HF (hydrofluoric acid)**.

## 2.3 MEMS Micro Sensors

### 2.3.1 Piezoresistive Pressure Sensor

The most commercially successful MEMS device. A thin silicon diaphragm is formed by anisotropic etching, and piezoresistors are diffused on its surface. When pressure is applied, the diaphragm deflects, creating stress that changes the piezoresistor values.

### 2.3.2 Capacitive Accelerometer

A proof mass is suspended by flexures, with comb-finger electrodes forming a differential capacitor. Acceleration causes the proof mass to displace, changing the capacitance.

### 2.3.3 Gyroscope (Angular Rate Sensor)

A vibrating structure experiences a Coriolis force when subjected to rotation. The Coriolis force is proportional to the mass, velocity, and angular rate.

$$F_{Coriolis} = 2m \, \vec{v} \times \vec{\Omega}$$

### 2.3.4 Chemical and Biosensors

Microcantilevers functionalized with selective coatings bend or change resonant frequency upon molecular binding.

## 2.4 MEMS Micro Actuators

### 2.4.1 Electrostatic Actuators

Use the electrostatic attraction between charged plates. Provide fast response, low power, and high force at small gaps.

- **Parallel plate actuator:** Force $F = \dfrac{\varepsilon_0 A V^2}{2 d^2}$
- **Comb drive:** Provides larger displacement range.

### 2.4.2 Piezoelectric Actuators

Exploit the inverse piezoelectric effect. A voltage applied across a piezoelectric material causes mechanical strain. Used in micropumps and AFM probes.

### 2.4.3 Thermal Actuators

Use differential thermal expansion of bimorph beams. Two materials with different coefficients of thermal expansion (CTE) bend when heated.

### 2.4.4 Magnetic Actuators

Use Lorentz force or magnetostriction. Less common in silicon MEMS due to material compatibility issues, but dominant in applications requiring large forces.

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memorize the following formulas — they appear frequently in KTU examination problems.**

| # | Concept | Formula | Variables / Units | Applicable Sensor / Actuator |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Piezoresistive effect | $\dfrac{\Delta R}{R} = \pi_l \, \sigma_l + \pi_t \, \sigma_t$ | $\pi_l, \pi_t$: longitudinal & transverse piezoresistive coefficients ($\text{Pa}^{-1}$); $\sigma$: stress (Pa) | Pressure, Force sensors |
| 2 | Capacitance of parallel plate | $C = \dfrac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}{d}$ | $A$: area ($\text{m}^2$); $d$: gap (m) | Capacitive sensor |
| 3 | Electrostatic force | $F_e = \dfrac{1}{2} \, V^2 \, \dfrac{dC}{dx}$ | $V$: voltage (V); $x$: displacement (m) | Comb drive, Parallel plate |
| 4 | Coriolis force | $F_c = 2m \, v \, \Omega$ | $m$: mass (kg); $v$: velocity (m/s); $\Omega$: angular rate (rad/s) | Gyroscope |
| 5 | Spring constant (cantilever) | $k = \dfrac{E w t^3}{4 L^3}$ | $E$: Young's modulus (Pa); $w$: width; $t$: thickness; $L$: length (m) | Cantilever sensors |
| 6 | Resonant frequency | $f_0 = \dfrac{1}{2\pi} \sqrt{\dfrac{k}{m}}$ | $k$: spring constant (N/m); $m$: effective mass (kg) | Resonant sensors |
| 7 | Diaphragm deflection (small) | $w_0 = \dfrac{3 P a^4 (1 - \nu^2)}{16 E t^3}$ | $P$: pressure; $a$: diaphragm radius; $\nu$: Poisson's ratio; $t$: thickness | Pressure sensor |
| 8 | Piezoelectric charge | $Q = d_{ij} \, F$ | $d_{ij}$: piezoelectric coefficient (C/N); $F$: force (N) | Piezoelectric sensor |
| 9 | Damping coefficient (slide film) | $b = \dfrac{\mu A}{d}$ | $\mu$: viscosity (Pa·s); $A$: area; $d$: gap | Accelerometer |
| 10 | Thermal expansion | $\Delta L = \alpha L_0 \Delta T$ | $\alpha$: CTE ($/\text{K}$); $\Delta T$: temperature change (K) | Bimorph actuator |

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Capacitance Change in a Parallel-Plate Capacitive Sensor

Consider a parallel-plate capacitor with one fixed plate and one movable plate (suspended by a spring of stiffness $k$). Let the initial gap be $d_0$ and the overlap area be $A$.

### Step 1: Initial Capacitance

The capacitance of a parallel-plate capacitor is given by:

$$C_0 = \frac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}{d_0}$$

**Explanation:** The capacitance is directly proportional to the plate area and the dielectric permittivity, and inversely proportional to the gap between the plates.

### Step 2: Displacement due to External Force

Let an external force $F$ displace the movable plate by a small distance $x$ (positive $x$ reduces the gap to $d_0 - x$). For a mechanical spring, Hooke's law gives:

$$F = k x \quad \Longrightarrow \quad x = \frac{F}{k}$$

**Explanation:** The spring resists the external force, and the displacement is proportional to the applied force divided by the spring stiffness.

### Step 3: New Capacitance

After displacement, the new gap is $d = d_0 - x$. The new capacitance is:

$$C(x) = \frac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}{d_0 - x}$$

**Explanation:** Since the gap decreased, the capacitance increased (inverse relationship).

### Step 4: Sensitivity (Linearized)

For small displacements ($x \ll d_0$), we use a Taylor series expansion:

$$\begin{aligned}
C(x) &= \frac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}{d_0} \cdot \frac{1}{1 - x/d_0} \\
&\approx C_0 \left( 1 + \frac{x}{d_0} + \left(\frac{x}{d_0}\right)^2 + \cdots \right) \\
&\approx C_0 \left( 1 + \frac{x}{d_0} \right)
\end{aligned}$$

**Explanation:** Using the binomial expansion $\frac{1}{1 - y} \approx 1 + y$ for $y \ll 1$, we linearize the response.

### Step 5: Change in Capacitance

The change in capacitance $\Delta C = C(x) - C_0$ is:

$$\Delta C = C_0 \cdot \frac{x}{d_0} = \frac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A \, x}{d_0^2}$$

### Step 6: Sensitivity

The sensitivity $S$ is defined as the ratio of $\Delta C / C_0$ to the displacement $x$:

$$S = \frac{\Delta C / C_0}{x} = \frac{1}{d_0}$$

**Final Result:**

$$\boxed{\Delta C = \frac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}{d_0^2} \cdot x}$$

**Engineering Insight:** Sensitivity is inversely proportional to $d_0^2$. Therefore, smaller initial gaps dramatically improve sensitivity. This is why MEMS capacitive sensors use sub-micron gaps.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Electrostatic Force in a Comb-Drive Actuator

A comb-drive actuator consists of interdigitated fingers. One set of fingers is movable, the other is fixed. When a voltage $V$ is applied between the two sets, an electrostatic force is generated.

### Step 1: Capacitance of a Comb Drive

For a comb drive with $N$ finger pairs, each finger of overlap length $L_f$, gap $g$, and thickness $t$ (out of plane), the total capacitance is:

$$C = N \cdot \frac{\varepsilon_0 t L_f}{g}$$

**Explanation:** Each finger pair forms a small parallel-plate capacitor. The total capacitance is the sum of all such capacitors.

### Step 2: Energy Stored in the Capacitor

The electrostatic energy stored in the capacitor is:

$$U = \frac{1}{2} C V^2 = \frac{1}{2} N \cdot \frac{\varepsilon_0 t L_f}{g} \cdot V^2$$

**Explanation:** This energy represents the work done by the voltage source to charge the capacitor.

### Step 3: Force on the Movable Structure

The electrostatic force is the negative gradient of the energy with respect to displacement $x$:

$$F_e = -\frac{\partial U}{\partial x} = -\frac{1}{2} V^2 \frac{\partial C}{\partial x}$$

Since the capacitance changes linearly with $x$ (as the overlap length increases by $dx$):

$$\frac{\partial C}{\partial x} = N \cdot \frac{\varepsilon_0 t}{g}$$

### Step 4: Final Expression for Electrostatic Force

$$\boxed{F_e = \frac{1}{2} N \cdot \frac{\varepsilon_0 t}{g} \cdot V^2}$$

**Explanation:** The force is independent of displacement (unlike parallel-plate actuators), which is a key advantage of comb drives — they produce a constant force over their full range of motion.

**Engineering Insight:** To increase force, designers increase $N$ (more finger pairs), reduce $g$ (smaller gap), or increase $t$ (thicker structural layer).

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Resonant Frequency of a Cantilever Beam

A cantilever beam of length $L$, width $w$, thickness $t$, density $\rho$, and Young's modulus $E$ has a fundamental resonant frequency.

### Step 1: Effective Mass and Stiffness

For a uniform cantilever beam oscillating in its fundamental mode:

$$k = \frac{E w t^3}{4 L^3}$$

$$m_{eff} = 0.24 \, \rho \, L \, w \, t$$

**Explanation:** The effective mass is less than the total mass ($m = \rho L w t$) because not all parts of the beam move with the same amplitude. The factor 0.24 is a modal correction factor.

### Step 2: Resonant Frequency Formula

$$f_0 = \frac{1}{2\pi} \sqrt{\frac{k}{m_{eff}}}$$

### Step 3: Substitute Expressions

$$f_0 = \frac{1}{2\pi} \sqrt{\frac{E w t^3 / (4 L^3)}{0.24 \, \rho \, L w t}}$$

### Step 4: Simplify

$$f_0 = \frac{1}{2\pi} \sqrt{\frac{E t^2}{4 \times 0.24 \, \rho L^4}} = \frac{1}{2\pi} \sqrt{\frac{E t^2}{0.96 \, \rho L^4}}$$

### Step 5: Final Compact Form

$$\boxed{f_0 = \frac{t}{2\pi L^2} \sqrt{\frac{E}{0.96 \, \rho}} = \frac{1.03 \, t}{L^2} \sqrt{\frac{E}{\rho}}}$$

**Engineering Insight:** The resonant frequency is proportional to $t/L^2$. To increase $f_0$, designers make the beam shorter and/or thicker. This principle is used in resonant mass sensors, where analyte binding adds mass and shifts $f_0$ downward.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Coriolis Force and Gyroscope Sensitivity

A vibratory gyroscope consists of a proof mass driven into resonance in the x-direction at velocity $v_x$. When the device rotates with angular rate $\Omega$ about the z-axis, a Coriolis force arises in the y-direction.

### Step 1: Define Drive-Mode Motion

The drive oscillator has displacement $x(t) = X_0 \sin(\omega_d t)$ and velocity:

$$v_x(t) = \frac{dx}{dt} = X_0 \omega_d \cos(\omega_d t)$$

### Step 2: Coriolis Force

The Coriolis force on a particle of mass $m$ is:

$$\vec{F}_c = -2m \, \vec{\Omega} \times \vec{v}$$

**Explanation:** The negative sign indicates that the Coriolis force deflects the mass perpendicular to its velocity, not in the direction of rotation.

For rotation about the z-axis and velocity along the x-axis:

$$F_{c,y} = -2m \, \Omega \, v_x(t) = -2m \, \Omega \, X_0 \omega_d \cos(\omega_d t)$$

### Step 3: Sense-Mode Displacement

The Coriolis force drives the sense mode. For a sense-mode resonator with stiffness $k_s$, damping $b_s$, and natural frequency $\omega_s$:

$$m \ddot{y} + b_s \dot{y} + k_s y = F_{c,y}(t)$$

At steady state, the displacement amplitude is:

$$Y_0 = \frac{2 m \, \Omega \, X_0 \omega_d}{\sqrt{(k_s - m\omega_d^2)^2 + (b_s \omega_d)^2}}$$

### Step 4: Sensitivity

If the drive and sense modes are matched ($\omega_d = \omega_s$) and the system is at resonance, the displacement becomes:

$$Y_0 = \frac{2 m \, \Omega \, X_0 \omega_0}{b_s \, \omega_0} = \frac{2 m \, \Omega \, X_0}{b_s}$$

### Step 5: Final Gyroscope Sensitivity

$$\boxed{Y_0 = \frac{2 m \, X_0}{b_s} \cdot \Omega}$$

**Engineering Insight:** Gyroscope sensitivity is proportional to drive amplitude $X_0$ and inversely proportional to damping $b_s$. This is why high-Q gyroscopes (low damping) achieve better angular rate resolution.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Sensor Operating Principles

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master this comparison table — examiners frequently ask "compare and contrast" questions.**

| Parameter | Piezoresistive | Capacitive | Piezoelectric | Resonant |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Transduction** | Resistance change | Capacitance change | Charge generation | Frequency shift |
| **Power consumption** | Moderate (Wheatstone bridge) | Low | Very low | Moderate |
| **Sensitivity** | High | High | Very high | Very high |
| **DC response** | Yes | Yes | **No (AC only)** | No |
| **Temperature sensitivity** | High | Low | Moderate | Moderate |
| **Output impedance** | Low | High | Very high | N/A (frequency) |
| **Fabrication complexity** | Low | Moderate | High | High |
| **Typical application** | Pressure sensor | Accelerometer | Microphone, gyroscope | Mass sensor |

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 MEMS Architecture Flow — Block Diagram

The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the **functional architecture of a complete MEMS device** integrating sensor, signal conditioning, and output stages.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    node1["PHYSICAL QUANTITY"] --> node2["MEMS SENSOR ELEMENT"]
    node2 --> node3["SIGNAL CONDITIONING"]
    node3 --> node4["ADC CONVERSION"]
    node4 --> node5["DIGITAL PROCESSING"]
    node5 --> node6["OUTPUT INTERFACE"]
    node2A["MEMS ACTUATOR"] --> node7["CONTROL SIGNAL"]
    node7 --> node2A
    node2 -.FEEDBACK.-> node5

    classDef sensor fill:#E8F4FD,stroke:#0277BD,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef actuator fill:#FFF3E0,stroke:#E65100,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef process fill:#F1F8E9,stroke:#33691E,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef output fill:#FCE4EC,stroke:#880E4F,stroke-width:2px,color:#000

    class node1,node2 sensor
    class node2A,node7 actuator
    class node3,node4,node5 process
    class node6 output
```

**Reading the Diagram:**
- The **blue block** represents the physical input (pressure, acceleration, etc.).
- The **orange block** represents the actuator (for closed-loop systems).
- The **green blocks** represent the electronic signal chain.
- The **pink block** is the user interface (display, transmitter, etc.).
- The **feedback loop** enables closed-loop control for precision instruments.

For advanced applications, a closed-loop gyroscope is used to maintain the sense-mode displacement at null, improving linearity and bandwidth.
