A normal voltage amplifier is like trying to measure the bucket's content by *looking through a long, thin pipe* — by the time the droplets reach you, many have been absorbed by the pipe walls (parasitic cable capacitance), giving a wrong reading. A charge amplifier is like placing a **funnel directly under the bucket** that empties every drop into a *measuring cup* (the feedback capacitor Cf). No matter how long or leaky the pipe is, the *total accumulated charge* lands in the cup, and the water level in the cup is your accurate output voltage. The cup's diameter (Cf) sets the scale.

**Key Metrics:**
- **Charge-to-Voltage Gain:** Vout = −Q / Cf (units: V/Coulomb).
- **Charge Sensitivity (typical piezoelectric):** 2 pC/N to 50 pC/N.
- **Lower Cut-off Frequency:** set by the input time-constant; can be made very low (< 0.1 Hz) for quasi-static measurements.

---

## 1.3 Isolation Amplifier

**Formal Definition:**
An **Isolation Amplifier** is a differential amplifier that provides **galvanic isolation** (no direct electrical conduction path, i.e., > 10^9 Ω isolation resistance and breakdown voltage of several kV) between its **input stage**, **output stage**, and **power supply rails**. The signal is transmitted across the isolation barrier through a non-galvanic medium — **transformer (magnetic) coupling**, **opto-coupler (optical) coupling**, or **capacitor (capacitive) coupling**.

**Conceptual Analogy — The "Locked-Door Messenger" Metaphor:**
Imagine a high-voltage industrial power line (the "patient" or "transmission line") that you must *measure* with an oscilloscope, but you absolutely cannot let the dangerous voltage *touch* your precious oscilloscope or your body. An isolation amplifier is a **locked, two-room intercom system**: the microphone (input) is in the dangerous room, the speaker (output) is in your safe room, and a *sound wave* (or *light beam* or *magnetic field*) crosses the locked wall — never an actual wire. You hear everything, but the electricity stays on its side of the wall.

**Key Metrics:**
- **Isolation Voltage (Continuous):** 2.5 kV to 5 kV RMS (typical for medical-grade).
- **Isolation Mode Rejection Ratio (IMRR):** > 140 dB at 60 Hz.
- **Bandwidth:** typically 20 kHz to 100 kHz.
- **Creepage Distance:** ≥ 8 mm (medical safety standard IEC 60601).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Board Exam Tip:** The three amplifiers above are *not interchangeable*. KTU examiners specifically test whether a student can identify the **correct amplifier for a given transducer** — e.g., a piezoelectric accelerometer **always** needs a charge amplifier, a thermocouple bridge **always** needs an instrumentation amplifier, and a patient-connected ECG probe **always** needs an isolation amplifier for safety.

---

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Instrumentation Amplifier — The Classic Three-Op-Amp Topology

The IA is built from **three internal op-amps** arranged in two stages:

### Stage 1 — The "Input Buffer + Gain" Stage
Two non-inverting buffers (A1 and A2) form a differential input stage. Both inputs see the **same high input impedance** because the signals go directly to the non-inverting (+) terminals. A single external resistor **Rg** is placed *between* the two inverting terminals, while two equal fixed resistors **R1** connect each op-amp output back to its own inverting input.

**Operational Logic — Step by Step:**
1. Inputs V1 and V2 feed the non-inverting terminals of A1 and A2 directly → **infinite (or >10 GΩ) input impedance**.
2. The op-amp action forces the inverting terminals to track V1 and V2 (virtual short).
3. The voltage developed *across* Rg is therefore exactly **(V1 − V2)** — the differential input.
4. This same current flows through both R1 resistors (Kirchhoff's Current Law — no current into the op-amp input).
5. The current is: **I = (V1 − V2) / Rg**.
6. This current produces an additional voltage drop across each R1: **I × R1** on each side, but in *opposite polarity*.
7. The output of A1 = V1 + I·R1, and the output of A2 = V2 − I·R1.
8. Therefore, the **differential output of Stage 1** is:
   **(V_A1 − V_A2) = (V1 − V2) · (1 + 2R1 / Rg)**.
9. Stage 1 provides **all of the gain** and **most of the CMRR**, while rejecting common-mode signals because (V1 − V2) for common-mode = 0 → output of Stage 1 = 0 (ideally).

### Stage 2 — The Difference Amplifier (Unity-Gain or Fixed-Gain Subtractor)
A third op-amp (A3) configured as a standard differential amplifier takes the output of Stage 1 and converts it to a single-ended, ground-referenced output.
- It uses four matched resistors: R2 (input to +), R3 (feedback from +), and two R2/R3 pairs (this is the textbook notation, but the **critical requirement is that all four resistors be precision-matched** to within 0.01% for high CMRR).
- Gain of Stage 2 = **R3 / R2**.
- This stage **rejects any residual common-mode voltage** that survived Stage 1.

### Total Gain of the Instrumentation Amplifier

$$
A_{v,\text{TOTAL}} \;=\; \left(1 + \frac{2R_1}{R_g}\right) \cdot \left(\frac{R_3}{R_2}\right)
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** Because Rg is a *single external resistor*, the user can program the gain with one component without disturbing the carefully matched resistor ratios inside the IC — this is the single most important practical advantage of the three-op-amp IA. In commercial ICs (e.g., INA128), R1 is internal, and Rg is selected by the designer.

### Common-Mode Rejection Ratio (CMRR)

$$
\text{CMRR}_{\text{dB}} \;=\; 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{A_d}{A_{cm}}\right) \quad ; \quad A_d = \text{differential gain},\;\; A_{cm} = \text{common-mode gain}
$$

The CMRR of the IA is **maximized** when the four resistors of Stage 2 are perfectly matched. A mismatch of even 0.1% caps the CMRR at about **60 dB** — this is why high-grade instrumentation amplifiers use **laser-trimmed thin-film resistors** on a single silicon die.

---

## 2.2 Charge Amplifier — Detailed Operational Analysis

### Circuit Topology
An op-amp is configured as an **integrator**, but with a capacitor **Cf** in the feedback path (not a resistor). A bias resistor **Rb** is connected in parallel with Cf to provide a DC path for the op-amp's input bias current and to set the low-frequency cut-off.

### Step-by-Step Operational Logic
1. The piezoelectric transducer acts as a **charge source Q(t)** in series with its own capacitance Cs and the cable capacitance Cc.
2. This Q(t) is injected into the inverting (virtual ground) node of the op-amp.
3. Because the inverting input is a **virtual ground (0 V)**, the op-amp demands that the *sum of currents* into the node is zero.
4. The current from the source is: **i(t) = dQ/dt**.
5. The only path for this current to flow is through the feedback capacitor **Cf**.
6. Since **i = Cf · dVout/dt**, the output voltage must rise to absorb the integrated charge:

   $$
   V_{out}(t) \;=\; -\frac{1}{C_f}\int i(t)\,dt \;=\; -\frac{Q(t)}{C_f}
   $$

7. **Key Insight:** The output depends *only* on Q and Cf — the source capacitance Cs and the cable capacitance Cc have **NO effect on Vout** because they are shorted out by the virtual ground (they are between the source and a node held at 0 V). This is the *magic* of the charge amplifier.

### The Reset (Discharge) Resistor Rb
- Rb is connected in **parallel** with Cf.
- It slowly discharges Cf, preventing **saturation** (which would happen on a pure integrator due to the op-amp's input bias current and offset voltage).
- The **low-frequency −3 dB cut-off** is:

  $$
   f_{L} \;=\; \frac{1}{2\pi R_b C_f}
  $$

- This is a *design trade-off*: a small Rb gives a wider bandwidth but also a higher low-frequency noise gain, and vice versa. For vibration measurements above 1 Hz, Rb is chosen to be very large (e.g., **GΩ**) so that fL is below 0.1 Hz.

### High-Pass Behaviour
The charge amplifier inherently behaves as a **high-pass filter** because it cannot pass DC. The lower cut-off is fL as above. Below fL, the signal is attenuated; above it, the gain is flat and equal to **−1/Cf (in V/C)**.

---

## 2.3 Isolation Amplifier — Detailed Operational Analysis

### The Three Functional Blocks
An isolation amplifier is a **three-port device**:
- **Port 1 — Input Port:** floating, isolated. Contains the input amplifier (often an IA) and an encoder (V → F or V → light).
- **Port 2 — Isolation Barrier:** the actual *physical* isolation, implemented by one of three methods.
- **Port 3 — Output Port:** referenced to the system ground. Contains a decoder (F → V or light → V) and an output buffer.

### The Three Coupling Methods

| **Coupling Type** | **Physical Medium** | **Bandwidth** | **Typical Accuracy** | **Example IC** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Transformer** | Magnetic flux across a small center-tapped transformer | DC to ~100 kHz | ±0.05% | ISO124, AD202 |
| **Optical** | Light across an opto-isolator (LED + photodiode) | DC to ~60 kHz | ±0.5% (linearity limited) | ISO100, HCPL-7800 |
| **Capacitive** | Electric field across a differential SiO₂ capacitor | DC to ~100 kHz | ±0.03% | ADuM3190 (digital), ISO 224 |

### Transformer Isolation — Working Detail
- The input stage modulates the differential input signal onto a **high-frequency carrier** (typically **50 kHz to 1 MHz**).
- This carrier is then passed through a **tiny toroidal transformer** that physically separates the input and output windings.
- The output stage **demodulates** the carrier back to a DC-coupled analog signal.
- A **small, isolated DC-DC converter** within the IC provides power to the input side, so the user only needs a single +15 V supply.

### IMRR (Isolation Mode Rejection Ratio)
IMRR is the **isolation equivalent of CMRR**. It measures how well the amplifier rejects a voltage appearing *between* the input ground and the output ground (the very voltage the isolation is supposed to block).

$$
\text{IMRR}_{\text{dB}} \;=\; 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{V_{in,\text{diff}}}{V_{out,\text{induced}}}\right) \quad \text{at } 60\,\text{Hz}
$$

---

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memorize this table — these are the only equations you need for board questions on this topic.**

| **#** | **Concept** | **Formula** | **Boundary / Practical Note** |
|:---:|---|---|---|
| 1 | IA total voltage gain | $A_v = \left(1 + \dfrac{2R_1}{R_g}\right)\dfrac{R_3}{R_2}$ | Rg is *single* external gain-setting resistor |
| 2 | IA gain (commercial IC, R1 internal) | $A_v = 1 + \dfrac{49.4\,\text{k}\Omega}{R_g}$ | For INA128; for AD620 it is $A_v = 1 + \dfrac{49.4\,\text{k}\Omega}{R_g}$ |
| 3 | IA output offset due to input Vos | $V_{out,os} = A_v \cdot V_{os}$ | Determines need for auto-zero / chopper IA |
| 4 | CMRR (definition) | $\text{CMRR}_{\text{dB}} = 20\log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{A_d}{A_{cm}}\right)$ | Higher is better; > 100 dB = premium |
| 5 | Charge amplifier output | $V_{out} = -\dfrac{Q}{C_f}$ | Independent of source / cable capacitance |
| 6 | Charge amplifier low −3 dB freq | $f_L = \dfrac{1}{2\pi R_b C_f}$ | Rb sets DC stability; fL sets usable bandwidth |
| 7 | Charge sensitivity of piezo (Vout per unit measurand) | $S = \dfrac{d_{33} \cdot A_{\text{piezo}}}{C_f}$ | d33 is the piezoelectric strain constant |
| 8 | Isolation amplifier (transformer) carrier freq | $f_c = 50\,\text{kHz}$ typical | Internal to IC, not user-selectable |
| 9 | Isolation breakdown voltage | $V_{iso} = 2.5$ to $5\,\text{kV}_{\text{RMS}}$ | Medical: IEC 60601 mandates > 4 kV |
| 10 | IMRR | $\text{IMRR}_{\text{dB}} = 20\log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{V_{cm,iso}}{V_{out,iso}}\right)$ | Tested at the line frequency (50/60 Hz) |

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Instrumentation Amplifier** is the universal analog front-end for **any Wheatstone bridge readout**, all **biomedical electrode amplifiers (ECG/EEG/EMG)**, and almost every **data-acquisition system** in industrial plants. Without it, the tiny mV-level sensor output would be drowned by the 50 Hz mains pickup travelling in the cable.
- **Charge Amplifier** is the *only* acceptable interface for **piezoelectric accelerometers, force sensors, and acoustic emission sensors** in vibration, modal-analysis, and structural-health-monitoring applications (e.g., aircraft-wing fatigue testing, machine-tool chatter detection).
- **Isolation Amplifier** is a **safety-critical** component in **patient monitors (defibrillation-proof ECG)**, **motor-drive current sensing** (in IGBT inverters for EVs and industrial drives), and **process control** where the sensor sits at several kV above the controller's ground.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation — Gain of the Classic Three-Op-Amp Instrumentation Amplifier

We will derive the total voltage gain starting from the schematic of the three-op-amp IA. The schematic is described in words for analytical clarity:

- Op-amp A1: non-inverting input = **V1**, inverting input = node **X**, output = **V_A1**.
- Op-amp A2: non-inverting input = **V2**, inverting input = node **Y**, output = **V_A2**.
- Resistor **R1** connects node X to V_A1.
- Resistor **R1** connects node Y to V_A2.
- Resistor **Rg** connects node X to node Y.
- Stage 2 (op-amp A3): V_A1 feeds a resistor R2 into its inverting input, V_A2 feeds a resistor R2 into its non-inverting input, R3 from A3 output to its inverting input, and R3 from the non-inverting input to ground.

**Step 1 — Apply the virtual short to A1 and A2:**

Because A1 is in a negative-feedback configuration through R1 and Rg, and similarly for A2, both op-amps operate in the linear region. The virtual-short principle states:

$$
V_{X} \;=\; V_1 \quad \text{and} \quad V_{Y} \;=\; V_2
$$

**Textual Justification:** The op-amp forces its two input terminals to the same potential, and the inverting input voltages are dictated by the external feedback network.

**Step 2 — Write the voltage across the gain-setting resistor Rg:**

The two terminals of Rg are at V1 and V2. Therefore the voltage drop across Rg is:

$$
V_{Rg} \;=\; V_X - V_Y \;=\; V_1 - V_2
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the current through Rg:**

Using Ohm's law:

$$
I_{Rg} \;=\; \frac{V_{Rg}}{R_g} \;=\; \frac{V_1 - V_2}{R_g}
$$

**Step 4 — Apply Kirchhoff's Current Law (KCL) at node X and node Y:**

The op-amp inputs draw **no current** (ideal op-amp assumption). Therefore the current flowing *into* node X from R1 (from V_A1) must equal the current flowing *out* of node X through Rg. Similarly at node Y:

$$
I_{R_1,\,A1} \;=\; I_{Rg} \;=\; I_{R_1,\,A2}
$$

The current through each R1 is therefore:

$$
I_{R_1} \;=\; \frac{V_1 - V_2}{R_g}
$$

**Step 5 — Express the output voltages of A1 and A2:**

The current I_{R1} flows from V_A1 *toward* node X. The voltage drop across R1 in the direction of current flow is I_{R1} · R1. The voltage at node X (which equals V1) is therefore lower than V_A1 by this drop. So:

$$
V_{A1} \;=\; V_1 + I_{R_1} \cdot R_1 \;=\; V_1 + \left(\frac{V_1 - V_2}{R_g}\right)R_1
$$

Similarly, the current flows from node Y *toward* V_A2, so V_A2 is *higher* than V_Y (= V2) by I_{R1}·R1 in the direction of the current. But careful with sign convention: the current exits the node Y through R1 toward V_A2, so V_A2 is *below* V2:

$$
V_{A2} \;=\; V_2 - I_{R_1} \cdot R_1 \;=\; V_2 - \left(\frac{V_1 - V_2}{R_g}\right)R_1
$$

**Step 6 — Compute the differential output of Stage 1:**

Subtract V_A2 from V_A1:

$$
V_{A1} - V_{A2} \;=\; \left[V_1 + \frac{R_1}{R_g}(V_1 - V_2)\right] - \left[V_2 - \frac{R_1}{R_g}(V_1 - V_2)\right]
$$

Expanding:

$$
V_{A1} - V_{A2} \;=\; (V_1 - V_2) + 2 \cdot \frac{R_1}{R_g}(V_1 - V_2)
$$

Factor (V1 − V2):

$$
V_{A1} - V_{A2} \;=\; \left(1 + \frac{2R_1}{R_g}\right)(V_1 - V_2)
$$

**Step 7 — Apply Stage 2 (Difference Amplifier) Transfer Function:**

For the difference amplifier with all four resistors perfectly matched (R2 from each input to the respective op-amp input, R3 in feedback, R3 from non-inverting input to ground), the textbook transfer function is:

$$
V_{out} \;=\; \frac{R_3}{R_2}(V_{A1} - V_{A2})
$$

**Step 8 — Combine Stage 1 and Stage 2 into the final transfer function:**

$$
V_{out} \;=\; \frac{R_3}{R_2}\left(1 + \frac{2R_1}{R_g}\right)(V_1 - V_2)
$$

Therefore the **total differential gain** of the three-op-amp instrumentation amplifier is:

$$
\boxed{\,A_v \;=\; \frac{V_{out}}{V_1 - V_2} \;=\; \left(1 + \frac{2R_1}{R_g}\right)\frac{R_3}{R_2}\,}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Examiner's Insight:** The derivation above is the *expected* full-marks answer to any KTU question that asks "Derive the gain of an instrumentation amplifier." Short-cuts that skip the KCL step or that fail to justify the virtual short **lose 2–3 marks** as per KTU valuation key.

---

## 3.2 Derivation — Output of a Charge Amplifier in Terms of Source Charge

Consider the charge amplifier with:
- Piezoelectric source modelled as a **current source i_s(t) = dQ/dt** in parallel with its own capacitance Cs.
- A cable capacitance Cc in series with the source.
- A feedback capacitor Cf and feedback resistor Rb in parallel between the op-amp output and its inverting input.
- The op-amp's non-inverting input is grounded.

**Step 1 — Apply the virtual short at the inverting input:**

Since the non-inverting input is at 0 V (ground), the inverting input is also forced to 0 V — it is a **virtual ground**.

**Step 2 — Write KCL at the inverting input node:**

The currents entering the node must sum to zero:

$$
i_s(t) \;=\; i_{C_f}(t) \;+\; i_{R_b}(t)
$$

**Step 3 — Express the capacitor current:**

For the feedback capacitor, with V_x = 0 (virtual ground) and V_out at the other terminal:

$$
i_{C_f}(t) \;=\; C_f \frac{d(V_x - V_{out})}{dt} \;=\; -C_f \frac{dV_{out}}{dt}
$$

The minus sign arises because the defined current direction is from the inverting input *into* the capacitor; if V_out is rising, current actually flows *out* of the node.

**Step 4 — Express the resistor current:**

$$
i_{R_b}(t) \;=\; \frac{V_x - V_{out}}{R_b} \;=\; -\frac{V_{out}}{R_b}
$$

**Step 5 — Substitute into KCL:**

$$
\frac{dQ}{dt} \;=\; -C_f \frac{dV_{out}}{dt} \;-\; \frac{V_{out}}{R_b}
$$

**Step 6 — Take the Laplace transform** (assuming zero initial conditions and sinusoidal steady state eventually):

$$
sQ(s) \;=\; -s C_f V_{out}(s) \;-\; \frac{V_{out}(s)}{R_b}
$$

**Step 7 — Solve for V_out(s) / Q(s):**

$$
V_{out}(s) \left[sC_f + \frac{1}{R_b}\right] \;=\; -sQ(s)
$$

$$
\frac{V_{out}(s)}{Q(s)} \;=\; -\frac{s}{sC_f + \frac{1}{R_b}} \;=\; -\frac{s R_b}{s R_b C_f + 1}
$$

**Step 8 — Identify the midband and low-frequency behaviours:**

At frequencies *well above* the pole frequency f_L = 1/(2π Rb Cf), the term s Rb Cf dominates, and the gain becomes:

$$
\frac{V_{out}(s)}{Q(s)} \;\approx\; -\frac{s R_b}{s R_b C_f} \;=\; -\frac{1}{C_f}
$$

**Step 9 — Convert back to time domain for midband:**

$$
V_{out} \;=\; -\frac{Q}{C_f}
$$

This is the famous charge-amplifier result. **The output voltage is directly proportional to the input charge, with sensitivity 1/Cf, and is independent of source and cable capacitances.**

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Interpretation:** At DC and very low frequencies, the term sRbCf is small, and the gain drops to zero (no DC response) — confirming the high-pass nature of the charge amplifier.

---

## 3.3 Derivation — IMRR of an Isolation Amplifier

The IMRR is the *ratio* of the isolation-mode voltage (V_iso) appearing between the input and output grounds, to the equivalent output voltage (V_out,iso) that this isolation-mode voltage produces, when referred back to the input.

By definition:

$$
\text{IMRR} \;=\; \frac{V_{iso}}{V_{out,iso}/A_v}
$$

Converting to dB:

$$
\text{IMRR}_{\text{dB}} \;=\; 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{A_v \cdot V_{iso}}{V_{out,iso}}\right)
$$

**Example Numerical Calculation:**
Suppose V_iso = 240 V (mains), V_out,iso = 10 mV (induced at output), and A_v = 1.

$$
\text{IMRR}_{\text{dB}} \;=\; 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{1 \times 240}{0.01}\right) \;=\; 20 \log_{10}(24\,000) \;\approx\; 87.6\,\text{dB}
$$

For a *good* isolation amplifier, IMRR > **140 dB** at 60 Hz, which means a 240 V isolation-mode voltage produces less than 24 µV of output disturbance — well below the noise floor.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| **Parameter** | **Instrumentation Amp** | **Charge Amplifier** | **Isolation Amplifier** |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Primary function** | Amplify V_diff, reject V_cm | Convert Q to V | Galvanically isolate input from output |
| **Typical input transducer** | Strain gauge, TC, bridge, electrode | Piezoelectric sensor | Any sensor requiring safety isolation |
| **Output expression** | $V_{out} = A_v(V_1 - V_2)$ | $V_{out} = -Q/C_f$ | $V_{out} = A_v V_{in,iso}$ |
| **Gain setting element** | Single external Rg | Single external Cf | Usually fixed (e.g., unity, ×10) |
| **Frequency response** | DC to MHz (IA grade) | HPF; fL set by RbCf | DC to ~100 kHz |
| **CMRR / IMRR** | > 100 dB | N/A | > 140 dB IMRR |
| **Isolation voltage** | None | None | 2.5–5 kV RMS |
| **Power consumption** | Low (mA) | Low (mA) | Higher (includes isolated DC-DC) |
| **Cost (commercial IC)** | ₹ 50 – ₹ 500 | ₹ 30 – ₹ 200 | ₹ 200 – ₹ 2000 |

## 4.1 Instrumentation Amplifier — Block Architecture

```mermaid

    V2["Input V2\n(Non-inverting)"] --> A2["Op-Amp A2\n(Input Buffer)"]
    Rg["External Resistor Rg\n(Gain Setting)"] -.-> A1
    Rg -.-> A2
    A1 --> SumNode1["Differential Sum Node\n(Stage 1 Output)"]
    A2 --> SumNode1
    SumNode1 --> A3["Op-Amp A3\n(Difference Amplifier)"]
    A3 --> Vout["Single-Ended Output Vout"]
    A3 -- "Matched R2 / R3" --> A3

```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The two input signals (V1, V2) feed the non-inverting inputs of A1 and A2 (very high input impedance).
- The single external resistor **Rg** sets the differential gain of Stage 1.
- Stage 2 (A3) is a difference amplifier that rejects the common-mode voltage and provides a ground-referenced output.

## 4.2 Charge Amplifier — Functional Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Piezo["Piezoelectric Sensor\n(Charge Source Q)"] --> Cable["Cable + Parasitic Capacitance Cc"]
    Cable --> InvNode["Virtual Ground\n(Op-Amp Inverting Input)"]
    NonInv["Non-Inverting Input\n(Grounded)"] --> OpAmp["Op-Amp\n(High Gain, High Z)"]
    OpAmp --> VoutNode["Output Vout = -Q / Cf"]
    Cf["Feedback Capacitor Cf\n(Converts Q to V)"] -.-> OpAmp
    Rb["Reset / Bias Resistor Rb\n(Parallel with Cf)"] -.-> OpAmp
    InvNode --> OpAmp
    OpAmp --> VoutNode

    classDef source fill:#FECACA,stroke:#B91C1C,stroke-width:2px,color:#7F1D1D
    classDef amp fill:#FEF3C7,stroke:#B45309,stroke-width:2px,color:#78350F
    classDef fb fill:#E0E7FF,stroke:#3730A3,stroke-width:1px,color:#1E1B4B
    classDef output fill:#D1FAE5,stroke:#047857,stroke-width:2px,color:#064E3B
    class Piezo,Cable source
    class OpAmp amp
    class Cf,Rb fb
    class VoutNode,InvNode output
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The piezoelectric source and its cable capacitance are *outside* the dashed feedback loop — their values do not affect the output.
- The op-amp maintains the inverting node at a **virtual ground**, so the entire charge Q from the source flows through **Cf** (and a tiny DC current through Rb).
- The output voltage is precisely the accumulated charge divided by Cf.

## 4.3 Isolation Amplifier — Three-Port Architecture

```mermaid

        Sensor --> InAmp
        PowerIn["Isolated DC-DC\nConverter"]
        PowerIn -.-> InAmp
    end

    subgraph Barrier["PORT 2 - ISOLATION BARRIER (No Wire Connection)"]
        BarrierChoice{{"Coupling Method:\nTransformer / Optical / Capacitive"}}
    end

        Decoder["Decoder + Output\nBuffer Amplifier"]
        OutV["V_out (Referenced to System GND)"]
        Decoder --> OutV
    end

    InAmp -->|Modulated Signal| BarrierChoice
    BarrierChoice -->|Recovered Signal| Decoder

    classDef port1 fill:#E0F2FE,stroke:#0369A1,stroke-width:2px,color:#0C4A6E
    classDef port2 fill:#FEE2E2,stroke:#B91C1C,stroke-width:2px,color:#7F1D1D
    classDef port3 fill:#D1FAE5,stroke:#047857,stroke-width:2px,color:#064E3B
    classDef barrier fill:#FFF7ED,stroke:#C2410C,stroke-width:2px,color:#7C2D12
    class Sensor,InAmp,PowerIn port1
    class BarrierChoice barrier
    class Decoder,OutV port3
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- Port 1 (input side) is *floating* — its ground can be at any potential, even hundreds of volts above system ground.
- Port 2 is the **physical barrier** — no wire crosses it. Signal is transmitted by magnetic flux, light, or electric field.
- Port 3 (output side) is referenced to system ground. The user can connect it safely to any grounded instrument.
- The **isolated DC-DC converter** inside the IC powers Port 1 from the same supply, without breaking isolation.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All questions below are aligned with the **KTU 2024 Scheme Bloom's Taxonomy mapping**: Part A is *Remember / Understand* (L1–L2); Part B is *Apply / Analyze / Evaluate* (L3–L5). Mark distribution follows the KTU ESE pattern: Part A = 3 marks × 2 questions = 6 marks; Part B = 14 marks × 1 question (with internal choice).

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define an Instrumentation Amplifier and list any two of its ideal characteristics. `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
An **Instrumentation Amplifier** is a precision differential amplifier that provides a selectable gain through a single external resistor, exhibits very high common-mode rejection ratio (CMRR), and has high input impedance on both input terminals. (2 marks)

**Two ideal characteristics:** (1 mark each, choose any two)
1. **Infinite input impedance** at both input terminals (to avoid loading the source).
2. **Infinite CMRR** (to perfectly reject any common-mode voltage, e.g., 50 Hz mains pickup).
3. **Zero output impedance** (to drive any load without signal loss).
4. **Precisely selectable, stable gain** (programmable by a single resistor).

---

### Question 2: What is the function of a charge amplifier? Why is it preferred over a voltage amplifier for piezoelectric sensors? `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A **charge amplifier** is an op-amp circuit with capacitive feedback that converts the **charge Q** generated by a piezoelectric (or other charge-output) sensor into a proportional **output voltage Vout = −Q / Cf**. (1.5 marks)

It is preferred over a voltage amplifier because: (1.5 marks)
1. The **output voltage is independent of the cable capacitance** and the sensor's own capacitance — only the feedback capacitance Cf matters, which is a stable, internal component.
2. The op-amp's **virtual ground** ensures the entire charge generated by the sensor is transferred to the feedback capacitor, giving an accurate, calibration-free reading.
3. A voltage amplifier, in contrast, forms a capacitive divider with the cable, so the output depends on the (variable, humidity-sensitive, vibration-sensitive) cable capacitance.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A (Choice 1): Comprehensive Question on Instrumentation Amplifier

> **A (a) [7 Marks]** Draw the circuit of a three-op-amp instrumentation amplifier and explain its operation. Derive an expression for its voltage gain. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
>
> **A (b) [7 Marks]** For the instrumentation amplifier above, R1 = 25 kΩ, R2 = 10 kΩ, R3 = 20 kΩ. Calculate the value of Rg for a gain of 100. If the input offset voltage is 50 µV, calculate the output offset voltage. Comment on how the four resistors of the difference amplifier stage affect the CMRR. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Solution — Part A (a):**

**Circuit (drawn as block schematic, refer to Section 4.1 above):**
- A1 and A2 are the input buffers with non-inverting inputs accepting V1 and V2.
- Rg connects the inverting inputs of A1 and A2.
- Two equal resistors R1 connect A1's output to its inverting input and A2's output to its inverting input.
- A3 is a difference amplifier with four matched resistors R2 and R3.

**Operation:** (3 marks)
- A1 and A2 provide **high input impedance** because V1 and V2 see the non-inverting inputs directly.
- The virtual short forces the inverting terminals to V1 and V2.
- The voltage drop across Rg = (V1 − V2) sets a current I = (V1 − V2)/Rg.
- This same current flows through both R1 resistors, developing a voltage gain of (1 + 2R1/Rg) in Stage 1.
- Stage 2 (A3) provides **common-mode rejection** and converts the differential signal to single-ended output.

**Derivation (refer to Section 3.1 above for full derivation):** (4 marks)
The total gain is:

$$
A_v \;=\; \left(1 + \frac{2R_1}{R_g}\right)\frac{R_3}{R_2}
$$

**Model Solution — Part A (b):**

**Step 1 — Solve for Rg:**

Given: A_v = 100, R1 = 25 kΩ, R2 = 10 kΩ, R3 = 20 kΩ.

$$
100 \;=\; \left(1 + \frac{2 \times 25\,\text{k}\Omega}{R_g}\right) \times \frac{20\,\text{k}\Omega}{10\,\text{k}\Omega}
$$

$$
100 \;=\; \left(1 + \frac{50\,\text{k}\Omega}{R_g}\right) \times 2
$$

$$
50 \;=\; 1 + \frac{50\,\text{k}\Omega}{R_g}
$$

$$
49 \;=\; \frac{50\,\text{k}\Omega}{R_g}
$$

$$
R_g \;=\; \frac{50\,\text{k}\Omega}{49} \;\approx\; 1020.4\,\Omega
$$

**Step 2 — Output offset voltage:**

$$
V_{out,os} \;=\; A_v \times V_{os} \;=\; 100 \times 50\,\mu\text{V} \;=\; 5\,\text{mV}
$$

(2 marks)

**Step 3 — Effect of resistor matching on CMRR:** (2 marks)
The CMRR of the IA is fundamentally limited by the **matching of the four resistors in the difference amplifier stage (A3)**. A mismatch of δ in the resistor ratio gives a finite common-mode gain:

$$
A_{cm} \;\approx\; \frac{\delta}{2}
$$

This caps the CMRR at:

$$
\text{CMRR}_{\text{dB}} \;\approx\; 20\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{2}{\delta}\right)
$$

For example, a 0.1% mismatch (δ = 0.001) limits CMRR to about **66 dB**. To achieve > 100 dB CMRR, the four resistors must be matched to better than 0.001% — this is why commercial IAs use **laser-trimmed thin-film resistors** monolithically integrated on a single silicon die.

---

### Question B (Choice 2): Comprehensive Question on Charge Amplifier and Isolation Amplifier

> **B (a) [7 Marks]** With a neat circuit diagram, explain the operation of a charge amplifier. Derive an expression for its output voltage. Mention the role of the reset (bias) resistor. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`
>
> **B (b) [7 Marks]** What is an isolation amplifier? Explain its functional block diagram. List and briefly explain the three methods of signal coupling across the isolation barrier. Why are isolation amplifiers essential in medical equipment like ECG monitors? `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

**Model Solution — Part B (a):**

**Circuit Diagram:** (1 mark)
Refer to the Mermaid block diagram in Section 4.2 above. The op-amp has a capacitor Cf and resistor Rb (in parallel) in the feedback path; the non-inverting input is grounded; the piezoelectric source is connected to the inverting (virtual ground) input.

**Operation:** (2 marks)
- The piezoelectric sensor delivers a charge Q(t).
- The op-amp's virtual ground (at the inverting input) absorbs the entire charge into the feedback capacitor Cf.
- The op-amp output rises (or falls, depending on sign convention) to satisfy KCL at the inverting node.
- The reset resistor Rb slowly discharges Cf, preventing long-term drift and saturation due to the op-amp's input bias current and offset voltage.

**Derivation (refer to Section 3.2 for full derivation):** (3 marks)

$$
V_{out} \;=\; -\frac{Q}{C_f}
$$

This result is **independent of the source capacitance Cs and the cable capacitance Cc** because both are connected to a node held at virtual ground (0 V) — no voltage can develop across them, so they do not steal or store any charge.

**Role of Rb:** (1 mark)
- Provides a DC path for the op-amp's input bias current.
- Sets the low-frequency cut-off: $f_L = 1 / (2\pi R_b C_f)$.
- Without Rb, the output would drift to saturation over time due to the integration of the op-amp's input bias current and offset voltage.

**Model Solution — Part B (b):**

**Definition (1 mark):** An isolation amplifier is a differential amplifier that provides **galvanic isolation** (no direct electrical conduction path) between its input and output, while still transmitting the analog signal accurately. Typical isolation voltage ratings are 2.5 kV to 5 kV RMS.

**Functional Block Diagram (refer to Section 4.3 above):** (2 marks)
Three ports — input side, isolation barrier, output side. The input side contains an instrumentation amplifier and an encoder (V → F or V → light). The output side contains a decoder (F → V or light → V) and an output buffer. An isolated DC-DC converter powers the input side from the same supply.

**Three coupling methods (3 marks):**

1. **Transformer coupling:** The input signal modulates a high-frequency carrier (e.g., 50 kHz), which is then passed through a small center-tapped transformer. The output side demodulates the carrier back to a DC-coupled analog signal. (Best accuracy; bandwidth DC–100 kHz; example: ISO124.)

2. **Optical coupling:** The input signal drives an LED; the light is detected by a photodiode on the output side, generating a proportional current/voltage. (Inherently unipolar; example: HCPL-7800.)

3. **Capacitive coupling:** The input signal modulates an electric field across a differential SiO₂ capacitor; the output side senses the modulated charge. (Excellent DC accuracy; example: ADuM-series digital isolators extended for analog use.)

**Why essential in medical ECG monitors (1 mark):**
- The patient is connected to mains-power-derived equipment, and any fault current > 10 µA through the heart can cause ventricular fibrillation.
- An isolation amplifier ensures that even if the patient accidentally touches a live wire, no current can flow from the patient to the recording instrument or to the system ground.
- It also rejects the large 50/60 Hz common-mode voltage that appears between the patient and the instrument ground.
- Medical safety standards (IEC 60601) mandate > 4 kV isolation and patient auxiliary currents < 10 µA — both enforced by the isolation amplifier.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls That Cost Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting to justify the virtual short:** Many students write "V_x = V1" without explaining *why*. Always mention: "Because A1 is in negative feedback, the virtual-short principle applies..."
> 2. **Sign error in the gain formula:** Some students write $A_v = (1 + R_1/R_g) \cdot R_3/R_2$ — note it is **2R1/Rg**, not R1/Rg. The factor of 2 comes from the *two* R1 resistors.
> 3. **Charge amplifier without mentioning virtual ground:** The most important physical insight is the *virtual ground* — without it, the cancellation of source/cable capacitance cannot be explained.
> 4. **Confusing IMRR with CMRR:** CMRR rejects voltages common to *both inputs*; IMRR rejects voltages *between input ground and output ground* (the isolation-mode voltage). These are entirely different quantities.
> 5. **Skipping the reset resistor in the charge amplifier:** A full-marks answer to a charge-amplifier question **must** discuss the trade-off in choosing Rb and the resulting low-frequency cut-off.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- The **Instrumentation Amplifier (IA)** is a 3-op-amp circuit: two input buffers (A1, A2) + one difference amplifier (A3). Its gain $A_v = (1 + 2R_1/R_g)(R_3/R_2)$ is set by a **single external resistor Rg**.
- The IA provides **very high input impedance** (signals go to non-inverting inputs), **high CMRR** (rejects 50 Hz pickup), and **selectable gain** — making it the standard front-end for strain gauges, thermocouples, bridges, and biopotential electrodes.
- The **Charge Amplifier** uses a feedback **capacitor Cf** to convert charge Q into voltage: $V_{out} = -Q/C_f$. The op-amp's virtual ground makes the output **independent of cable and source capacitance**.
- The reset resistor **Rb** in parallel with Cf prevents saturation and sets the low-frequency cut-off: $f_L = 1/(2\pi R_b C_f)$.
- The **Isolation Amplifier** provides **galvanic isolation** (no DC conduction path) between input and output. Signal crosses the barrier by **transformer, optical, or capacitive** coupling.
- Isolation is **mandatory** in medical equipment (patient safety) and in motor drives (high common-mode voltages on the IGBT side). Medical isolation voltage must exceed **4 kV** (IEC 60601).
- IMRR (typically > 140 dB at 60 Hz) is the **isolation equivalent of CMRR** and quantifies rejection of voltage between input and output grounds.
- **Selection rule for the KTU exam:** Piezoelectric sensor → charge amplifier. Strain gauge / bridge / ECG electrode → instrumentation amplifier. Patient monitor or motor drive → isolation amplifier.
- **Order of magnitude to remember:** Charge amplifier gain = 1/Cf (V/C). For Cf = 1 nF, gain = 10⁹ V/C. For Cf = 100 pF, gain = 10¹⁰ V/C.
- **Three-op-amp IA resistor matching** is the fundamental limit on CMRR: a 0.1% mismatch caps CMRR at ~66 dB; commercial IAs use laser-trimmed thin-film resistors to achieve > 100 dB.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Role of Filters: Low Pass
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET411 - ELECTRONIC INSTRUMENTATION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Role of Filters: Low Pass

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Low Pass Filter (LPF)** is a frequency-selective two-port electronic network that allows signals with frequencies **below a designated cutoff frequency ($f_c$)** to pass through with minimal attenuation, while progressively attenuating (rejecting) signals with frequencies **above $f_c$**. The boundary at which the filter transitions from passband to stopband is governed by a precise mathematical relationship between the reactive components and the load impedance.

In instrumentation systems, LPFs are deployed to eliminate **high-frequency noise**, **electromagnetic interference (EMI)**, and **aliasing artifacts** that contaminate low-frequency measurement signals such as those from thermocouples, strain gauges, and biomedical electrodes.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Standard):** A low pass filter is a circuit that passes frequencies from $0$ Hz (DC) up to its cutoff frequency $f_c$ and attenuates all frequencies above $f_c$. The cutoff frequency is defined as the frequency at which the output power drops to **50% ($-3$ dB point)** of its maximum passband value, or equivalently, where the output voltage falls to $\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}$ (approximately **70.7%**) of the input voltage.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **physical sieve used in a kitchen** to sift flour. Large lumps of flour (representing the desired low-frequency content) pass freely through the mesh, while small pebbles and husks (representing unwanted high-frequency noise) are caught and discarded. 

In an electronic context:
- **Low-frequency components** (the smooth, slow-varying part of your signal) are the "fine flour" — they pass through easily.
- **High-frequency components** (rapid fluctuations, switching noise, RF interference) are the "unwanted debris" — they are blocked or attenuated.

Another powerful analogy is a **bouncer at a nightclub entrance**: the bouncer (filter) only allows people wearing formal shoes (low frequencies) to enter, while those wearing sneakers (high frequencies) are turned away. The cutoff frequency is like the strictness of the dress code.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight for KTU Students:** The cutoff frequency is NOT a brick wall. Real filters do not abruptly stop frequencies above $f_c$. Instead, the attenuation increases gradually at a rate determined by the filter's **order** (e.g., $-20$ dB/decade for first-order, $-40$ dB/decade for second-order).

### 1.3 Critical Physical Constants & Standards

The following constants and standards govern the design and analysis of LPFs in electronic instrumentation:

- **Cutoff Frequency ($-3$ dB point):** $f_c = \frac{1}{2\pi RC}$ for a first-order RC low pass filter
- **Standard Reference Frequency:** **1 kHz** is often used as a benchmark in instrumentation textbooks
- **Decade:** A factor of 10 in frequency (e.g., 100 Hz to 1000 Hz)
- **Octave:** A factor of 2 in frequency (e.g., 1 kHz to 2 kHz)
- **Maximum Passband Voltage Gain ($A_{max}$):** Typically **unity (0 dB)** for passive LPFs
- **Half-Power Point:** The frequency at which output power is exactly **half** the input power

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Aid:** A "**D**ecade" has **D**igits (10), while an "**O**ctave" has **O**ctaves like a piano (2).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Logic — Step-by-Step Breakdown

A passive first-order RC Low Pass Filter operates on a simple yet profound principle: **a capacitor's impedance is frequency-dependent**.

**Step 1 — Component Placement:**
- The resistor **R** is placed in **series** with the signal path (input).
- The capacitor **C** is placed in **parallel** (shunt) with the output (load).

**Step 2 — Frequency-Dependent Impedance of Capacitor:**
The impedance of a capacitor is given by:
$$X_C = \frac{1}{j\omega C} = \frac{1}{j2\pi f C}$$

- At **low frequencies (DC, $f \to 0$)**: $X_C \to \infty$. The capacitor acts like an **open circuit**. All input voltage appears at the output.
- At **high frequencies ($f \to \infty$)**: $X_C \to 0$. The capacitor acts like a **short circuit**. The output is shunted to ground.

**Step 3 — Voltage Divider Action:**
Since $R$ and $C$ form a frequency-dependent voltage divider, the output voltage is:
$$V_{out} = V_{in} \cdot \frac{X_C}{R + X_C}$$

**Step 4 — Magnitude Response:**
The magnitude of the transfer function is:
$$\vert H(j\omega) \vert = \frac{1}{\sqrt{1 + (\omega RC)^2}}$$

**Step 5 — Phase Response:**
The phase shift introduced is:
$$\phi(\omega) = -\arctan(\omega RC)$$

This means at the cutoff frequency, the output **lags the input by exactly 45°**.

### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Parameter / Quantity | Formula | Unit / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Capacitive Reactance | $X_C = \frac{1}{2\pi f C}$ | Ohms ($\Omega$) |
| 2 | Cutoff Frequency ($-3$ dB) | $f_c = \frac{1}{2\pi RC}$ | Hertz (Hz) |
| 3 | Cutoff Angular Frequency | $\omega_c = \frac{1}{RC}$ | Radians/sec (rad/s) |
| 4 | Transfer Function (1st order) | $H(s) = \frac{1}{1 + sRC}$ | Dimensionless |
| 5 | Magnitude Response | $\vert H(j\omega) \vert = \frac{1}{\sqrt{1 + (f/f_c)^2}}$ | Ratio or dB |
| 6 | Gain in Decibels | $A_{dB} = 20 \log_{10}\left(\frac{1}{\sqrt{1 + (f/f_c)^2}}\right)$ | Decibels (dB) |
| 7 | Phase Angle | $\phi = -\arctan(f/f_c)$ | Degrees or Radians |
| 8 | Time Constant | $\tau = RC$ | Seconds (s) |
| 9 | Roll-off Rate (1st order) | $-20$ dB/decade or $-6$ dB/octave | After $f_c$ |
| 10 | Roll-off Rate (2nd order) | $-40$ dB/decade or $-12$ dB/octave | After $f_c$ |
| 11 | Quality Factor (Q) | $Q = \frac{1}{2\zeta}$ for 2nd order | Dimensionless |
| 12 | Stopband Attenuation | $A_{stop} = 20 \log_{10}\left(\frac{f}{f_c}\right)^n$ | dB, where $n$ = order |

> [!CAUTION]
> **Critical KTU Mistake to Avoid:** The cutoff frequency is NOT the $-6$ dB point or the $-1$ dB point. It is **specifically the $-3$ dB point** where voltage ratio equals $\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \approx 0.707$. Examiners frequently test this distinction.

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Applications

Low pass filters are indispensable in virtually every modern electronic system. Some high-impact applications include:

1. **Anti-Aliasing in Data Acquisition Systems (DAQ):** Before an analog signal is sampled by an ADC, an LPF ensures that no frequency component above half the sampling rate (Nyquist frequency) enters the system, preventing aliasing distortion.
2. **Audio Systems:** Subwoofers are connected through LPFs to receive only low-frequency bass content (typically below 200 Hz), protecting them from damaging high-frequency signals.
3. **Power Supply Filtering:** LPFs smooth rectified DC outputs by removing high-frequency ripple from switching regulators.
4. **Biomedical Instrumentation (ECG/EEG):** LPFs with cutoff around 40-100 Hz eliminate high-frequency muscle artifact (EMG) noise while preserving the diagnostic waveform.
5. **Telecommunications:** Channel selection in receivers uses LPF to isolate baseband signals.
6. **Image Processing:** Gaussian LPFs in 2D are used for image blurring and noise reduction.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Transfer Function of a First-Order RC Low Pass Filter

**Step 1 — Circuit Description:**
Consider a series resistor $R$ connected to the input voltage $V_{in}$, with a shunt capacitor $C$ connected across the output terminals. The output voltage $V_{out}$ is taken across the capacitor.

**Step 2 — Apply the Voltage Divider Rule in the s-domain:**
The impedance of the capacitor in the s-domain (where $s = j\omega$) is:
$$Z_C = \frac{1}{sC}$$

The transfer function is the ratio of the impedance across which output is taken to the total series impedance:
$$H(s) = \frac{V_{out}(s)}{V_{in}(s)} = \frac{Z_C}{R + Z_C}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute $Z_C$ and Simplify:**
$$H(s) = \frac{\frac{1}{sC}}{R + \frac{1}{sC}}$$

Multiply numerator and denominator by $sC$ to clear the fraction:
$$H(s) = \frac{1}{sRC + 1}$$

**Step 4 — Final Standard Form:**
The standard first-order low pass transfer function is:
$$H(s) = \frac{1}{1 + sRC}$$

**Step 5 — Substitute $s = j\omega$ for Frequency Response:**
Replace the complex frequency $s$ with $j\omega$ to obtain the steady-state sinusoidal response:
$$H(j\omega) = \frac{1}{1 + j\omega RC}$$

### 3.2 Derivation of the Magnitude Response $\vert H(j\omega) \vert$

**Step 1 — Compute the Modulus:**
$$\vert H(j\omega) \vert = \frac{\vert 1 \vert}{\vert 1 + j\omega RC \vert} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{1^2 + (\omega RC)^2}}$$

$$\vert H(j\omega) \vert = \frac{1}{\sqrt{1 + (\omega RC)^2}}$$

**Step 2 — Express in Terms of Cutoff Frequency:**
We define $\omega_c = \frac{1}{RC}$. Substituting:
$$\vert H(j\omega) \vert = \frac{1}{\sqrt{1 + \left(\frac{\omega}{\omega_c}\right)^2}}$$

Or equivalently in terms of linear frequency $f$:
$$\vert H(f) \vert = \frac{1}{\sqrt{1 + \left(\frac{f}{f_c}\right)^2}}$$

### 3.3 Derivation of the Cutoff Frequency

**Step 1 — Define the Cutoff Condition:**
By definition, at the cutoff frequency $f_c$, the output power is half the input power, which corresponds to the voltage ratio being $\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}$.

**Step 2 — Set Up the Equation:**
$$\frac{1}{\sqrt{1 + (f_c RC)^2}} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}$$

**Step 3 — Square Both Sides:**
$$\frac{1}{1 + (f_c RC)^2} = \frac{1}{2}$$

**Step 4 — Cross-Multiply:**
$$2 = 1 + (f_c RC)^2$$

**Step 5 — Isolate the Term:**
$$(f_c RC)^2 = 1$$
$$f_c RC = 1$$
$$f_c = \frac{1}{RC}$$

**Step 6 — Convert to Angular Frequency:**
$$\omega_c = 2\pi f_c = \frac{1}{RC}$$

### 3.4 Derivation of Roll-off Rate in dB/Decade

**Step 1 — Define a Decade Above Cutoff:**
A decade means a factor of 10 increase. Consider $f_2 = 10 f_c$ (one decade above cutoff).

**Step 2 — Compute Gain at $f_1 = f_c$ (0 dB reference below 0 dB):**
At exactly $f_c$: $\vert H(f_c) \vert = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}$, so gain in dB is:
$$A_{dB}(f_c) = 20 \log_{10}\left(\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\right) \approx -3 \text{ dB}$$

**Step 3 — Compute Gain at $f_2 = 10 f_c$:**
For $f \gg f_c$, $\sqrt{1 + (f/f_c)^2} \approx f/f_c$. Thus:
$$\vert H(f_2) \vert \approx \frac{1}{f_2/f_c} = \frac{f_c}{f_2} = \frac{1}{10} = 0.1$$

$$A_{dB}(10f_c) = 20 \log_{10}(0.1) = -20 \text{ dB}$$

**Step 4 — Compute the Roll-off:**
$$\Delta A = A_{dB}(10f_c) - A_{dB}(f_c) = -20 - (-3) = -17 \text{ dB/decade}$$

For frequencies well above cutoff (asymptotic behavior), the roll-off asymptotically approaches:
$$\text{Roll-off} = -20 \text{ dB/decade} = -6 \text{ dB/octave}$$

### 3.5 Numerical Example — Solving a KTU-Style Problem

**Problem:** Design a first-order RC low pass filter with a cutoff frequency of $f_c = 1$ kHz. Choose $C = 0.1 \mu F$ and find the value of R. Also compute the output voltage when $f = 5$ kHz and $V_{in} = 10$ V (peak).

**Solution Step 1 — Solve for R:**
$$R = \frac{1}{2\pi f_c C}$$

$$R = \frac{1}{2\pi \times 1000 \times 0.1 \times 10^{-6}}$$

$$R = \frac{1}{6.2832 \times 10^{-4}}$$

$$R \approx 1591.55 \, \Omega \approx 1.59 \, k\Omega$$

**Solution Step 2 — Compute Frequency Ratio:**
$$\frac{f}{f_c} = \frac{5000}{1000} = 5$$

**Solution Step 3 — Compute Magnitude:**
$$\vert H(f) \vert = \frac{1}{\sqrt{1 + 5^2}} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{26}} = \frac{1}{5.099}$$

$$\vert H(f) \vert \approx 0.1961$$

**Solution Step 4 — Compute Output Voltage:**
$$V_{out} = V_{in} \times \vert H(f) \vert = 10 \times 0.1961 = 1.961 \text{ V}$$

**Solution Step 5 — Compute Output in dB:**
$$A_{dB} = 20 \log_{10}(0.1961) \approx -14.15 \text{ dB}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Verification:** Since 5 kHz is roughly 0.7 decades above 1 kHz, the expected attenuation is approximately $-3 + (-20 \times 0.7) = -17$ dB, which is close to our computed value of $-14.15$ dB (slight discrepancy because 5 is not exactly one decade).

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — LPF Signal Flow Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA["Input Signal Source V_in"] --> nodeB["Series Resistor R"]
    nodeB --> nodeC["Output Node V_out"]
    nodeC --> nodeD["Shunt Capacitor C to Ground"]
    nodeC --> nodeE["Load Connection"]
    nodeF["Low Frequency Components f less than f_c"] -.->|passes| nodeA
    nodeG["High Frequency Components f greater than f_c"] -.->|attenuated| nodeA
    nodeH["Cutoff Frequency f_c equals 1 over 2 pi RC"] -.->|governs| nodeB
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Frequency Response Curve Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph passband["PASSBAND REGION"]
        nodeP1["0 Hz to f_c"]
        nodeP2["Gain approximately 0 dB"]
        nodeP3["Signal passes with minimal loss"]
    end
    subgraph cutoff["CUTOFF POINT"]
        nodeC1["At f equals f_c"]
        nodeC2["Gain equals minus 3 dB"]
        nodeC3["V_out equals 0.707 times V_in"]
        nodeC4["Phase shift equals minus 45 degrees"]
    end
    subgraph stopband["STOPBAND REGION"]
        nodeS1["f greater than f_c"]
        nodeS2["Attenuation increases at minus 20 dB per decade"]
        nodeS3["For first order filter"]
        nodeS4["For second order minus 40 dB per decade"]
    end
    passband --> cutoff
    cutoff --> stopband
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Filter Order Comparison Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    node1["Filter Input"] --> node2{"Select Filter Order"}
    node2 -->|Order 1| node3["Single RC Section"]
    node2 -->|Order 2| node4["Two Cascaded RC Sections or RLC"]
    node2 -->|Order n| node5["n Cascaded Sections"]
    node3 --> node6["Roll off minus 20 dB per decade"]
    node4 --> node7["Roll off minus 40 dB per decade"]
    node5 --> node8["Roll off minus 20n dB per decade"]
    node6 --> node9["Simple but gradual stopband"]
    node7 --> node10["Sharper transition better selectivity"]
    node8 --> node11["Near ideal brick wall response"]
```

### 4.4 Equivalent Circuit Representation

| Element | Schematic Symbol | Function |
|---|---|---|
| Input Source | $V_{in}$ sine wave | Provides the test signal |
| Series Resistor | Zig-zag line **R** | Limits current, drops voltage |
| Shunt Capacitor | Two parallel lines **C** | Bypasses high frequencies to ground |
| Output Terminal | $V_{out}$ | Filtered signal measurement point |
| Ground | Three horizontal lines | Reference 0 V potential |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Schematic Reading Tip:** In any LPF circuit, the **capacitor always goes to ground** (or to a low-impedance return path). The **resistor goes in series** with the signal. Memorize this orientation: **R-series, C-shunt = LPF**. Conversely, **C-series, R-shunt = HPF**.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Question Paper]**

Define a low pass filter. State any two applications of low pass filters in electronic instrumentation.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

A low pass filter is a frequency-selective circuit that allows signals with frequencies from 0 Hz (DC) up to a specified cutoff frequency $f_c$ to pass through with minimal attenuation, while progressively attenuating all frequencies above $f_c$.

**Two applications:**
1. **Anti-aliasing filter in ADC systems:** An LPF placed before the analog-to-digital converter restricts the input bandwidth to below the Nyquist frequency, preventing aliasing distortion during sampling.
2. **Noise filtering in biomedical instrumentation:** LPFs remove high-frequency muscle artifact (EMG) noise from ECG and EEG signals, which typically have a useful bandwidth of 0.05 Hz to 150 Hz.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

For a first-order RC low pass filter with $R = 10 \, k\Omega$ and $C = 10 \, nF$, calculate the cutoff frequency.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

Given: $R = 10 \, k\Omega = 10^4 \, \Omega$, $C = 10 \, nF = 10 \times 10^{-9} \, F = 10^{-8} \, F$

Formula:
$$f_c = \frac{1}{2\pi RC}$$

Substituting:
$$f_c = \frac{1}{2\pi \times 10^4 \times 10^{-8}}$$

$$f_c = \frac{1}{2\pi \times 10^{-4}}$$

$$f_c = \frac{10^4}{2\pi} = \frac{10000}{6.2832}$$

$$\boxed{f_c \approx 1591.55 \, Hz \approx 1.59 \, kHz}$$

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

**Question A — [KTU University Exam – Sample Paper 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** Derive the transfer function of a first-order RC low pass filter. Draw its frequency response and explain the significance of the cutoff frequency. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A first-order low pass filter has $R = 2.2 \, k\Omega$ and $C = 47 \, nF$. Determine: (i) The cutoff frequency, (ii) The output voltage magnitude when the input is a 5 V peak sine wave at $f = 3 \, kHz$, (iii) The gain in dB at this frequency, and (iv) The phase shift introduced. **(7 Marks)**

---

### Model Solution for Question A

#### Part (a) — Derivation (7 Marks)

**Step 1 (1 Mark):** Draw and label the circuit — A series resistor $R$ with input $V_{in}$ at one end, and a shunt capacitor $C$ to ground, with output $V_{out}$ taken across the capacitor.

**Step 2 (1 Mark):** Express capacitor impedance in s-domain:
$$Z_C = \frac{1}{sC}$$

**Step 3 (2 Marks):** Apply voltage divider rule:
$$H(s) = \frac{V_{out}(s)}{V_{in}(s)} = \frac{1/sC}{R + 1/sC}$$

**Step 4 (1 Mark):** Simplify:
$$H(s) = \frac{1}{1 + sRC}$$

**Step 5 (1 Mark):** Substitute $s = j\omega$ to get frequency response:
$$H(j\omega) = \frac{1}{1 + j\omega RC}$$

**Step 6 (1 Mark):** Discuss frequency response — at $f \ll f_c$, gain $\approx 1$ (0 dB passband); at $f = f_c$, gain = $-3$ dB; at $f \gg f_c$, gain rolls off at $-20$ dB/decade. The cutoff frequency $f_c = 1/(2\pi RC)$ marks the boundary between passband and stopband, defined as the $-3$ dB point where output power is half the input power.

---

#### Part (b) — Numerical Problem (7 Marks)

**Given:** $R = 2.2 \, k\Omega = 2.2 \times 10^3 \, \Omega$, $C = 47 \, nF = 47 \times 10^{-9} \, F$, $V_{in} = 5 \, V$ peak, $f = 3 \, kHz = 3000 \, Hz$

**(i) Cutoff Frequency (2 Marks):**
$$f_c = \frac{1}{2\pi RC} = \frac{1}{2\pi \times 2.2 \times 10^3 \times 47 \times 10^{-9}}$$

$$f_c = \frac{1}{2\pi \times 1.034 \times 10^{-4}} = \frac{1}{6.497 \times 10^{-4}}$$

$$\boxed{f_c \approx 1539.4 \, Hz \approx 1.54 \, kHz}$$

**(ii) Output Voltage Magnitude at $f = 3$ kHz (2 Marks):**
$$\frac{f}{f_c} = \frac{3000}{1539.4} \approx 1.949$$

$$\vert H(f) \vert = \frac{1}{\sqrt{1 + (1.949)^2}} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{1 + 3.798}} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{4.798}} = \frac{1}{2.190}$$

$$\vert H(f) \vert \approx 0.4566$$

$$V_{out} = V_{in} \times \vert H(f) \vert = 5 \times 0.4566$$

$$\boxed{V_{out} \approx 2.283 \, V \text{ (peak)}}$$

**(iii) Gain in dB (1.5 Marks):**
$$A_{dB} = 20 \log_{10}(0.4566)$$

$$\boxed{A_{dB} \approx -6.81 \, dB}$$

**(iv) Phase Shift (1.5 Marks):**
$$\phi = -\arctan(f/f_c) = -\arctan(1.949)$$

$$\boxed{\phi \approx -62.83°}$$

---

### Alternative Question B — [KTU University Exam – Sample Paper 2024 Scheme]

**(a)** Explain with neat diagrams the working of first-order and second-order low pass filters. Compare their roll-off rates. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Design a first-order RC low pass filter to provide a cutoff frequency of $8 \, kHz$ using a capacitor of $0.01 \, \mu F$. What is the gain (in dB) of this filter at $f = 20 \, kHz$? Also find the frequency at which the output voltage is exactly 1 V when $V_{in} = 2 \, V$. **(7 Marks)**

---

### Model Solution for Question B

#### Part (a) — Conceptual (7 Marks)

**First-Order LPF (3 Marks):**
- Consists of one resistor and one capacitor.
- Transfer function: $H(s) = \frac{1}{1 + sRC}$
- Cutoff: $f_c = 1/(2\pi RC)$
- Roll-off: $-20$ dB/decade
- Phase: $0°$ to $-90°$ across frequency range

**Second-Order LPF (3 Marks):**
- Consists of two RC sections cascaded (or an RLC network).
- Transfer function: $H(s) = \frac{1}{(1 + sRC)^2}$ (for cascaded identical sections)
- Roll-off: $-40$ dB/decade
- Phase: $0°$ to $-180°$
- Sharper transition from passband to stopband

**Comparison (1 Mark):**

| Parameter | First-Order | Second-Order |
|---|---|---|
| Roll-off | $-20$ dB/decade | $-40$ dB/decade |
| Phase Range | $0°$ to $-90°$ | $0°$ to $-180°$ |
| Stopband Sharpness | Gradual | Steeper |
| Complexity | Simple | More components |

---

#### Part (b) — Numerical (7 Marks)

**Given:** $f_c = 8 \, kHz = 8000 \, Hz$, $C = 0.01 \, \mu F = 10^{-8} \, F$

**(i) Calculate R (2 Marks):**
$$R = \frac{1}{2\pi f_c C} = \frac{1}{2\pi \times 8000 \times 10^{-8}}$$

$$R = \frac{1}{5.0265 \times 10^{-4}} \approx 1989.4 \, \Omega \approx 1.99 \, k\Omega$$

**(ii) Gain in dB at $f = 20$ kHz (2.5 Marks):**
$$\frac{f}{f_c} = \frac{20}{8} = 2.5$$

$$\vert H \vert = \frac{1}{\sqrt{1 + (2.5)^2}} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{7.25}} = \frac{1}{2.6926} \approx 0.3714$$

$$A_{dB} = 20 \log_{10}(0.3714) \approx \boxed{-8.60 \, dB}$$

**(iii) Frequency at which $V_{out} = 1$ V with $V_{in} = 2$ V (2.5 Marks):**
$$\frac{V_{out}}{V_{in}} = \frac{1}{2} = 0.5$$

$$0.5 = \frac{1}{\sqrt{1 + (f/f_c)^2}}$$

$$\sqrt{1 + (f/f_c)^2} = 2$$

$$1 + (f/f_c)^2 = 4$$

$$(f/f_c)^2 = 3$$

$$f/f_c = \sqrt{3} \approx 1.732$$

$$f = 1.732 \times 8000 = \boxed{13,856 \, Hz \approx 13.86 \, kHz}$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Common Mark Deductions:**
> 1. **Forgetting the "1/(2π)" factor:** Many students incorrectly write $f_c = 1/RC$ without the $2\pi$. This is a $-1$ to $-2$ mark deduction.
> 2. **Confusing $-3$ dB with half-voltage:** At the cutoff, voltage is $0.707 V_{in}$ (NOT $0.5 V_{in}$). It is the **power** that is halved, not the voltage.
> 3. **Not stating units:** Always write "Hz", "V", "dB" in numerical answers. Naked numbers lose marks.
> 4. **Missing roll-off specification:** When asked about filter sharpness, ALWAYS mention the order (1st, 2nd) explicitly along with the dB/decade value.
> 5. **Phase sign error:** The phase shift in an LPF is **negative** (output lags input). Writing a positive phase shift is a definite mark loss.

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Definition:** A Low Pass Filter passes frequencies from **0 Hz to $f_c$** and attenuates frequencies above $f_c$.
- **Cutoff Frequency:** Defined as the **$-3$ dB point** where output voltage equals $\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \approx 0.707$ times the input voltage.
- **Master Formula:** $f_c = \frac{1}{2\pi RC}$ for first-order RC LPF.
- **Transfer Function:** $H(s) = \frac{1}{1 + sRC}$, with $H(j\omega) = \frac{1}{1 + j\omega RC}$.
- **Magnitude Response:** $\vert H(f) \vert = \frac{1}{\sqrt{1 + (f/f_c)^2}}$
- **Roll-off:** First-order = $-20$ dB/decade; Second-order = $-40$ dB/decade.
- **Phase at $f_c$:** Exactly $-45°$ (a frequently tested value).
- **Time Constant:** $\tau = RC = \frac{1}{2\pi f_c}$
- **Circuit Rule:** **R-series, C-shunt** = LPF (memorize this orientation).
- **At DC ($f=0$):** Capacitor is open circuit → full signal passes.
- **At $f \to \infty$:** Capacitor is short circuit → output is zero.
- **Common Applications:** Anti-aliasing filter, audio subwoofer crossover, power supply smoothing, ECG/EEG noise filtering, communication receivers.
- **Filter Order Impact:** Higher order = sharper cutoff = better selectivity but more complexity and component tolerances become critical.
- **Practical Note:** Real capacitors and resistors have tolerances (±5%, ±10%) which cause the actual cutoff frequency to deviate from the design value — important for precise instrumentation applications.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET411 - ELECTRONIC INSTRUMENTATION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** High Pass

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High Pass Filter (HPF):** A two-port network that **passes signals with frequencies above a certain cutoff frequency (f_c)** while **attenuating (rejecting) signals with frequencies below f_c**. The magnitude of the transfer function approaches zero as frequency (ω) approaches zero, and approaches unity (or a constant gain) as ω → ∞.

In the context of Electronic Instrumentation, the High Pass Filter is a **frequency-selective circuit** built using passive components (Resistors and Capacitors) or active components (Op-Amps with RC networks). It is one of the fundamental building blocks in signal conditioning, used to **block DC components** and **remove low-frequency noise** (such as 50 Hz power line hum or baseline drift in biomedical signals).

### Mathematical Form (General)

$$H(j\omega) = \frac{V_{out}(j\omega)}{V_{in}(j\omega)}$$

For an ideal High Pass Filter, the magnitude response is defined as:

$$\vert H(j\omega) \vert = \begin{cases} 0, & 0 \le \omega < \omega_c \\ 1, & \omega \ge \omega_c \end{cases}$$

where **ω_c = 2πf_c** is the cutoff angular frequency in **radians per second (rad/s)**.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **"The Bouncer at a Club Analogy"** 🍽️
>
> Imagine a High Pass Filter as a **strict bouncer at an upscale nightclub**:
> - **Low frequencies (the bass rumble, DC offset, slow drifts)** are like a group of teenagers in sneakers. The bouncer says **"Not on my watch!"** and **rejects (attenuates)** them.
> - **High frequencies (the sharp claps, crisp treble, fast-changing signals)** are like the VIP guests. The bouncer **lets them pass freely**.
> - The **cutoff frequency (f_c)** is the strict dress code threshold — anyone below that standard is turned away.

In a real biomedical instrument like an **ECG machine**, a High Pass Filter with f_c = 0.05 Hz is used to remove the wandering baseline caused by patient breathing, while preserving the sharp QRS complex (which has higher frequency components).

---

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Standard Parameters:**
> - **Cutoff Frequency (f_c):** The frequency at which the output power drops to **half (-3 dB point)** of the input power. Unit: **Hertz (Hz)**.
> - **Time Constant (τ = RC):** Determines how quickly the filter responds. Unit: **seconds (s)**.
> - **Quality Factor (Q):** For second-order filters, measures selectivity. **Q > 0.707** causes peaking in the response.
> - **Stopband Attenuation:** The amount by which unwanted frequencies are reduced, measured in **decibels (dB)**.

$$\text{Gain in dB} = 20 \log_{10}\left(\frac{V_{out}}{V_{in}}\right)$$

At cutoff: Gain = -3 dB (since power is halved, amplitude is 1/√2).

---

## 1.4 Why High Pass Filters are Critical in Instrumentation

1. **DC Blocking:** Removes DC offset from sensors and transducers.
2. **Baseline Drift Removal:** Eliminates slow-changing noise in biomedical signals (ECG, EEG, EMG).
3. **AC Coupling:** Used at the input stage of oscilloscopes and amplifiers.
4. **High-Frequency Emphasis:** In audio equalizers, the "treble" knob is essentially an adjustable HPF.
5. **Crosstalk Reduction:** Blocks low-frequency interference from adjacent channels.
6. **Pre-emphasis in Communication:** In FM transmission, HPFs are used to boost high-frequency components.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 First-Order RC High Pass Filter — Operational Breakdown

A first-order HPF consists of **one resistor (R)** and **one capacitor (C)** arranged such that the **output is taken across the resistor**. The capacitor is in series with the input signal.

### 2.1.1 Step-by-Step Logic of Operation

> **Step 1 — At very low frequencies (ω → 0, including DC):**
> The capacitor's impedance is given by $X_C = \dfrac{1}{j\omega C} \to \infty$. It acts like an **open circuit**, blocking the signal from reaching the output. **Output ≈ 0**.

> **Step 2 — At the cutoff frequency (ω = ω_c):**
> The capacitor's impedance equals the resistance ($X_C = R$). The output amplitude is exactly $\dfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}} = 0.707$ of the input. The phase shift between input and output is **-45°** (output leads input by 45°... or output lags input by 45° depending on convention).

> **Step 3 — At very high frequencies (ω → ∞):**
> The capacitor's impedance $X_C \to 0$, acting like a **short circuit**. The full input signal appears at the output. **Output ≈ V_in**.

### 2.1.2 The Physical "Why" Behind Each Step

- A capacitor **opposes changes in voltage**. At low frequencies, the input changes slowly, so the capacitor charges up and **blocks** the slow signal.
- At high frequencies, the input changes too fast for the capacitor to charge, so it **passes the rapid fluctuations** directly through.

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Parameter** | **First-Order RC HPF** | **Second-Order RC HPF** | **Unit** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Transfer Function $H(j\omega)$ | $\dfrac{j\omega RC}{1 + j\omega RC}$ | $\dfrac{(j\omega RC)^2}{1 + 3j\omega RC + (j\omega RC)^2}$ | dimensionless |
| Cutoff Frequency $f_c$ | $\dfrac{1}{2\pi RC}$ | $\dfrac{1}{2\pi RC \sqrt{2}}$ (Butterworth) | Hz |
| Cutoff Angular Frequency $\omega_c$ | $\dfrac{1}{RC}$ | $\dfrac{\sqrt{2}}{RC}$ (Butterworth) | rad/s |
| Time Constant $\tau$ | $RC$ | — | s |
| Magnitude $\vert H \vert$ | $\dfrac{\omega RC}{\sqrt{1 + (\omega RC)^2}}$ | $\dfrac{(\omega RC)^2}{\sqrt{[1-(\omega RC)^2]^2 + 9(\omega RC)^2}}$ | dimensionless |
| Phase Angle $\phi$ | $90° - \tan^{-1}(\omega RC)$ | $180° - \tan^{-1}\left(\dfrac{3\omega RC}{1-(\omega RC)^2}\right)$ | degrees |
| Stopband Roll-off | $-20$ dB/decade | $-40$ dB/decade | dB/dec |
| Output Equation (Sinusoidal) | $V_{out} = \dfrac{j\omega RC}{1 + j\omega RC} \cdot V_{in}$ | — | V |
| Energy Stored | $E_C = \dfrac{1}{2}CV^2$ | — | J |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Roll-off Rule of Thumb:** Each filter order adds **-20 dB/decade** (or **-6 dB/octave**) of stopband attenuation. A 2nd-order HPF rolls off at **-40 dB/decade** — twice as steep as a 1st-order.

---

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

| **Application Domain** | **Specific Use of HPF** |
|---|---|
| **Biomedical (ECG/EEG)** | Removes baseline wander (0.05 Hz HPF in ECG) |
| **Audio Engineering** | "Treble boost" in equalizers, removes rumble from microphones |
| **Oscilloscopes** | AC coupling mode uses internal HPF to block DC |
| **Communication Systems** | Pre-emphasis in FM transmitters to improve SNR |
| **Photography / Imaging** | Unsharp masking (HPF for edge detection) |
| **Seismology** | Filters out slow tidal movements from earthquake data |
| **Control Systems** | Eliminates integrator wind-up by blocking DC drift |
| **Power Systems** | Blocks DC component in current transformers (CTs) |

---

## 2.4 Bode Plot Characteristics (First-Order HPF)

The Bode plot has two regions:

1. **Stopband (ω << ω_c):** Magnitude rises with a slope of **+20 dB/decade**. Phase ≈ **+90°**.
2. **Passband (ω >> ω_c):** Magnitude flattens at 0 dB. Phase ≈ **0°**.
3. **At ω = ω_c:** Magnitude = **-3 dB**, Phase = **+45°**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Memory Trick:** Unlike Low Pass Filters where phase lags, High Pass Filters exhibit **phase lead** in the transition region — the output **leads** the input. This is because the capacitor's voltage cannot change instantaneously, causing the resistor voltage (output) to anticipate the input's behavior.

---

## 2.5 Pole-Zero Perspective

For a 1st-order HPF, the transfer function can be rewritten as:

$$H(s) = \frac{s}{s + \omega_c}$$

where $s = j\omega$. This reveals:
- **Zero at s = 0** (origin): This zero forces $|H(j\omega)| \to 0$ as $\omega \to 0$ — the **defining property** of a high pass filter.
- **Pole at s = -ω_c**: This is the cutoff pole, determining the transition frequency.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Key Insight:** The number of zeros at the origin (s = 0) equals the **filter order**. A 1st-order HPF has one zero at origin, a 2nd-order HPF has two zeros at origin, and so on.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: Transfer Function of 1st-Order RC High Pass Filter

### 3.1.1 Circuit Description

Consider a series RC circuit where:
- Input voltage $V_{in}$ is applied across the series combination of C and R.
- Output voltage $V_{out}$ is taken across the **resistor R**.

### 3.1.2 Using Voltage Divider Rule

The impedance of the capacitor is:

$$Z_C = \frac{1}{j\omega C}$$

The impedance of the resistor is:

$$Z_R = R$$

By the **voltage divider rule**, the output voltage across R is:

$$V_{out} = V_{in} \cdot \frac{Z_R}{Z_R + Z_C}$$

Substituting the impedances:

$$V_{out} = V_{in} \cdot \frac{R}{R + \dfrac{1}{j\omega C}}$$

### 3.1.3 Algebraic Simplification

To simplify, multiply numerator and denominator by $j\omega C$:

$$V_{out} = V_{in} \cdot \frac{R \cdot j\omega C}{R \cdot j\omega C + 1}$$

Since $R \cdot j\omega C = j\omega RC$:

$$V_{out} = V_{in} \cdot \frac{j\omega RC}{1 + j\omega RC}$$

### 3.1.4 The Transfer Function

Therefore, the transfer function is:

$$H(j\omega) = \frac{V_{out}}{V_{in}} = \frac{j\omega RC}{1 + j\omega RC}$$

> **Textual Explanation:** The numerator $j\omega RC$ grows with frequency, while the denominator $1 + j\omega RC$ grows faster beyond $\omega_c$. This creates the characteristic high-pass behavior.

---

## 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation: Magnitude and Phase

### 3.2.1 Magnitude Response

The magnitude is obtained by taking the modulus of numerator and denominator separately:

$$\vert H(j\omega) \vert = \frac{\vert j\omega RC \vert}{\vert 1 + j\omega RC \vert} = \frac{\omega RC}{\sqrt{1^2 + (\omega RC)^2}}$$

$$\boxed{\vert H(j\omega) \vert = \frac{\omega RC}{\sqrt{1 + (\omega RC)^2}}}$$

### 3.2.2 Phase Response

The phase of a complex number $\dfrac{a+jb}{c+jd}$ is $\tan^{-1}(b/a) - \tan^{-1}(d/c)$.

- **Numerator phase:** $j\omega RC$ is purely imaginary and positive (for $\omega > 0$), so its angle is **+90°**.
- **Denominator phase:** $1 + j\omega RC$ has angle $\tan^{-1}(\omega RC / 1) = \tan^{-1}(\omega RC)$.

Therefore:

$$\phi(\omega) = 90° - \tan^{-1}(\omega RC)$$

$$\boxed{\phi(\omega) = 90° - \tan^{-1}(\omega RC)}$$

### 3.2.3 Verification at Cutoff Frequency ($\omega = \omega_c = 1/RC$)

At $\omega = 1/RC$:

- **Magnitude:** $\dfrac{(1/RC) \cdot RC}{\sqrt{1 + 1}} = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \approx 0.707$ ✓
- **Phase:** $90° - \tan^{-1}(1) = 90° - 45° = 45°$ ✓

This confirms the **-3 dB point** and **+45° phase** at cutoff.

---

## 3.3 Exhaustive Derivation: 2nd-Order RC High Pass Filter

### 3.3.1 Circuit Topology

A 2nd-order RC HPF consists of **two cascaded RC sections**. The transfer function of each stage is $H_1(s) = \dfrac{sRC}{1 + sRC}$. For two non-interacting identical stages (assuming buffer isolation):

$$H(s) = H_1(s) \cdot H_1(s) = \left(\frac{sRC}{1 + sRC}\right)^2 = \frac{(sRC)^2}{(1 + sRC)^2}$$

Expanding the denominator:

$$(1 + sRC)^2 = 1 + 2sRC + (sRC)^2$$

So:

$$H(s) = \frac{(sRC)^2}{1 + 2sRC + (sRC)^2}$$

> **Textual Explanation:** For a **Butterworth response** (maximally flat passband), the coefficient of the $sRC$ term must be $\sqrt{2}$, not 2. This requires damping resistors, leading to the Sallen-Key topology.

### 3.3.2 Butterworth 2nd-Order HPF

For a Butterworth response:

$$H(s) = \frac{(sRC)^2}{1 + \sqrt{2} \cdot sRC + (sRC)^2}$$

The cutoff frequency for the Butterworth 2nd-order HPF is:

$$\omega_c = \frac{1}{RC}$$

The **-3 dB point** is now at the same frequency, but the roll-off is steeper (**-40 dB/decade**).

### 3.3.3 Magnitude Response Derivation

$$\vert H(j\omega) \vert = \frac{(\omega RC)^2}{\sqrt{[1 - (\omega RC)^2]^2 + (\sqrt{2} \cdot \omega RC)^2}}$$

Simplifying the denominator:

$$\sqrt{[1 - (\omega RC)^2]^2 + 2(\omega RC)^2}$$

For the Butterworth case, this evaluates to $\sqrt{1 + (\omega RC)^4}$ at cutoff, giving $\dfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}}$ ✓

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Frequency Behavior Table

| **Frequency Range** | **$\omega RC$ Value** | **$\vert H \vert$ (1st-order)** | **$\vert H \vert$ (2nd-order Butterworth)** | **Phase (1st-order)** | **Behavior** |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| $\omega = 0$ (DC) | 0 | 0 | 0 | +90° | Complete rejection |
| $\omega = 0.1 \omega_c$ | 0.1 | 0.0995 | 0.010 | 84.3° | Strong attenuation |
| $\omega = 0.5 \omega_c$ | 0.5 | 0.447 | 0.243 | 63.4° | Transition region |
| $\omega = \omega_c$ | 1.0 | 0.707 | 0.707 | 45° | **-3 dB point** |
| $\omega = 2 \omega_c$ | 2.0 | 0.894 | 0.970 | 26.6° | Approaching passband |
| $\omega = 10 \omega_c$ | 10 | 0.995 | 0.99995 | 5.7° | Near unity gain |
| $\omega \to \infty$ | $\infty$ | 1.0 | 1.0 | 0° | Full pass-through |

---

## 3.5 Step Response Derivation (Time Domain)

### 3.5.1 Differential Equation

For a 1st-order HPF, the input-output relationship in the time domain is:

$$RC \frac{dV_{out}}{dt} + V_{out} = RC \frac{dV_{in}}{dt}$$

> **Textual Explanation:** This is a first-order linear ODE. The output depends on the **derivative of the input**, scaled by RC.

### 3.5.2 Step Input Response

For a step input $V_{in}(t) = V_0 \cdot u(t)$ (where $u(t)$ is the unit step), the solution is:

$$V_{out}(t) = V_0 \cdot e^{-t/RC} \quad \text{for} \quad t \ge 0$$

> **Textual Explanation:** At the instant of the step, the capacitor is uncharged, so the full voltage appears across R. As the capacitor charges, the output decays **exponentially** to zero. The HPF **cannot pass DC**, so the steady-state output is 0.

### 3.5.3 Time Constant Significance

- At $t = \tau = RC$: Output has decayed to **36.8%** of initial value ($1/e$).
- At $t = 5\tau$: Output has decayed to **0.67%** — effectively zero.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall Warning:** Do NOT confuse the time constant with the cutoff period. The relationship is: $T_c = 1/f_c = 2\pi\tau$. So $\tau$ is approximately $1/6$ of the cutoff period.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 First-Order RC High Pass Filter — Circuit Schematic

```
                |    FILTER     |
                |   f > f_c     |

                       |
                       v
               [Output = |H(jω)|·V_in
                with phase shift φ(ω)]
```
