<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — June 2023]

**Q: Define a High Pass Filter. State its cutoff frequency expression for a first-order RC HPF.**

**Model Answer:**

> A High Pass Filter is a frequency-selective network that allows signals with frequencies **above the cutoff frequency (f_c)** to pass with minimal attenuation, while **rejecting (attenuating) signals with frequencies below f_c**. The magnitude of the transfer function approaches **unity** as frequency → ∞ and approaches **zero** as frequency → 0.

> For a first-order RC high pass filter with resistance R and capacitance C:

$$f_c = \frac{1}{2\pi RC}$$

> The filter provides a stopband roll-off of **-20 dB/decade** below the cutoff frequency.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]

**Q: Explain the significance of the -3 dB point in a high pass filter.**

**Model Answer:**

> The **-3 dB point** corresponds to the cutoff frequency (f_c) of the high pass filter. At this frequency:
> - The **output power** is exactly **half (-3 dB)** of the input power.
> - The **output amplitude** is $\dfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}} = 0.707$ times the input amplitude.
> - The **phase shift** between input and output is **+45°** for a 1st-order HPF.
>
> This is the **boundary** between the passband and the stopband. It is also called the **half-power point** or **corner frequency**.

---

## 5.2 Part B: Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Sign of phase:** Many students write the phase as $-45°$ instead of $+45°$ at cutoff. Remember: HPF output **leads** the input, so the phase is **positive**.
> 2. **Roll-off rate:** Forgetting to mention that the roll-off is **-20 dB/decade per order** leads to a loss of 2 marks.
> 3. **Units:** Always specify f_c in **Hz** and ω_c in **rad/s**. Mixing them up is a common error.
> 4. **Forgetting the zero at origin:** In pole-zero analysis, failing to mention the zero at s = 0 (which is the defining characteristic of an HPF) costs marks.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Q: Derive the transfer function of a first-order RC high pass filter. Plot its magnitude and phase Bode plot.**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Circuit Setup**
Consider a series RC circuit with capacitor C in series with input, and output taken across resistor R.

**Step 2: Apply Voltage Divider**

$$V_{out} = V_{in} \cdot \frac{R}{R + \dfrac{1}{j\omega C}}$$

**Step 3: Simplify**

Multiplying numerator and denominator by $j\omega C$:

$$H(j\omega) = \frac{j\omega RC}{1 + j\omega RC}$$

**Step 4: Magnitude Response**

$$\vert H(j\omega) \vert = \frac{\omega RC}{\sqrt{1 + (\omega RC)^2}}$$

**Step 5: Phase Response**

$$\phi(\omega) = 90° - \tan^{-1}(\omega RC)$$

**Step 6: Cutoff Frequency**

$$f_c = \frac{1}{2\pi RC}$$

**Step 7: Bode Plot Description**

| Region | Frequency | Magnitude Slope | Phase |
|---|---|---|---|
| Stopband | $\omega < \omega_c$ | +20 dB/decade | +90° → +45° |
| Cutoff | $\omega = \omega_c$ | -3 dB | +45° |
| Passband | $\omega > \omega_c$ | 0 dB (flat) | +45° → 0° |

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Q: For a first-order RC HPF with R = 10 kΩ and C = 0.01 μF, calculate: (i) Cutoff frequency, (ii) Output voltage at f = f_c/10, (iii) Phase shift at f = 2f_c. Take V_in = 5V (sinusoidal).**

**Model Solution:**

**Given:**
- R = 10 kΩ = $10 \times 10^3$ Ω
- C = 0.01 μF = $0.01 \times 10^{-6}$ F = $10^{-8}$ F
- V_in = 5 V

**Step (i): Cutoff Frequency**

$$f_c = \frac{1}{2\pi RC} = \frac{1}{2\pi \times 10^4 \times 10^{-8}}$$

$$f_c = \frac{1}{2\pi \times 10^{-4}} = \frac{10^4}{2\pi}$$

$$\boxed{f_c \approx 1591.55 \text{ Hz} \approx 1.59 \text{ kHz}}$$

**Step (ii): Output Voltage at f = f_c/10 = 159.15 Hz**

At this frequency, $\omega = 2\pi f = 2\pi \times 159.15 = 1000$ rad/s.
Then $\omega RC = 1000 \times 10^{-4} = 0.1$.

$$\vert H \vert = \frac{0.1}{\sqrt{1 + (0.1)^2}} = \frac{0.1}{\sqrt{1.01}} = \frac{0.1}{1.005}$$

$$\vert H \vert \approx 0.0995$$

$$V_{out} = 0.0995 \times 5 = \boxed{0.4975 \text{ V} \approx 0.5 \text{ V}}$$

**Step (iii): Phase Shift at f = 2f_c = 3183 Hz**

At this frequency, $\omega RC = 2$.

$$\phi = 90° - \tan^{-1}(2) = 90° - 63.43°$$

$$\boxed{\phi \approx 26.57°}$$

> The output leads the input by approximately **26.57°** at twice the cutoff frequency.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Q: Compare first-order and second-order RC high pass filters in terms of: (i) Transfer function, (ii) Roll-off rate, (iii) Pole-zero plot, (iv) Phase response.**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Transfer Function:**

| Order | Transfer Function |
|---|---|
| 1st-Order | $H(s) = \dfrac{sRC}{1 + sRC}$ |
| 2nd-Order (Butterworth) | $H(s) = \dfrac{(sRC)^2}{1 + \sqrt{2}\,sRC + (sRC)^2}$ |

**(ii) Roll-off Rate:**

- **1st-Order HPF:** -20 dB/decade in the stopband.
- **2nd-Order HPF:** -40 dB/decade in the stopband (twice as steep).

**(iii) Pole-Zero Plot:**

- **1st-Order:** One zero at s = 0 (origin), one pole at $s = -1/RC$ on the negative real axis.
- **2nd-Order:** Two zeros at s = 0, two poles at complex conjugate locations (for Butterworth, at $s = -\dfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}RC} \pm j\dfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}RC}$).

**(iv) Phase Response:**

- **1st-Order:** Phase varies from +90° to 0° (phase lead of up to 90°).
- **2nd-Order:** Phase varies from +180° to 0° (phase lead of up to 180°).

> **Conclusion:** A 2nd-order HPF provides **sharper transition** and **better stopband attenuation**, but at the cost of increased complexity and potential phase distortion near cutoff.

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Q: Design a first-order RC high pass filter with a cutoff frequency of 5 kHz. Choose C = 10 nF and calculate R. Also determine the output voltage and phase shift when input is 10 V at 1 kHz.**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Given Data**
- $f_c = 5$ kHz = $5 \times 10^3$ Hz
- C = 10 nF = $10 \times 10^{-9}$ F = $10^{-8}$ F
- V_in = 10 V at f = 1 kHz

**Step 2: Calculate R**

From $f_c = \dfrac{1}{2\pi RC}$:

$$R = \frac{1}{2\pi f_c C} = \frac{1}{2\pi \times 5 \times 10^3 \times 10^{-8}}$$

$$R = \frac{1}{2\pi \times 5 \times 10^{-5}} = \frac{1}{3.1416 \times 10^{-4}}$$

$$\boxed{R \approx 3183.1 \text{ Ω} \approx 3.18 \text{ kΩ}}$$

**Step 3: Output Voltage at f = 1 kHz**

At f = 1 kHz: $\omega = 2\pi \times 10^3 = 6283.19$ rad/s
$\omega RC = 6283.19 \times 3183.1 \times 10^{-8} = 6283.19 \times 3.1831 \times 10^{-5} = 0.2$

$$\vert H \vert = \frac{0.2}{\sqrt{1 + 0.04}} = \frac{0.2}{\sqrt{1.04}} = \frac{0.2}{1.0198}$$

$$\vert H \vert \approx 0.1961$$

$$V_{out} = 0.1961 \times 10 = \boxed{1.961 \text{ V}}$$

**Step 4: Phase Shift at f = 1 kHz**

$$\phi = 90° - \tan^{-1}(0.2) = 90° - 11.31°$$

$$\boxed{\phi \approx 78.69°}$$

> The output leads the input by **78.69°** at 1 kHz, indicating strong attenuation (since 1 kHz is well below the 5 kHz cutoff).

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **🔑 HIGH-YIELD REVISION POINTS — HIGH PASS FILTERS**

- **Definition:** HPF passes frequencies **above f_c** and rejects frequencies **below f_c**. ✅
- **Circuit:** Capacitor in **series**, output across **resistor**. ✅
- **Transfer Function:** $H(j\omega) = \dfrac{j\omega RC}{1 + j\omega RC}$ ✅
- **Cutoff Frequency:** $f_c = \dfrac{1}{2\pi RC}$ (1st-order) ✅
- **Time Constant:** $\tau = RC$ ✅
- **At Cutoff:** $\vert H \vert = 1/\sqrt{2} = 0.707$ → **-3 dB**; Phase = **+45°** ✅
- **Roll-off:** **-20 dB/decade** per order ✅
- **Phase Lead:** Output **leads** input (positive phase) ✅
- **Zero at Origin:** HPF always has a **zero at s = 0** in the s-plane ✅
- **DC Blocking:** HPF output is **zero** for DC input ✅
- **Step Response:** Exponential decay $V_{out}(t) = V_0 e^{-t/RC}$ ✅
- **2nd-Order HPF:** Roll-off = **-40 dB/decade**, Butterworth form uses $\sqrt{2}$ coefficient ✅
- **Phase Range:** 1st-order: +90° to 0°; 2nd-order: +180° to 0° ✅
- **Application:** AC coupling, baseline removal, treble boost, DC blocking ✅
- **Formulas to memorize:** $f_c$, $|H|$, $\phi$, $\tau$ — all derived from RC product ✅
- **Common mistake:** Confusing HPF and LPF transfer functions. Remember: HPF has $j\omega RC$ in **numerator**; LPF has $j\omega RC$ in **denominator** ❌

> **Final Exam Tip:** Always draw the **Bode plot sketch** with proper slopes and the **-3 dB point** marked. Examiners award marks for visual clarity and correct labeling.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Band Pass and Band Rejection Filters
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET411 - ELECTRONIC INSTRUMENTATION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Band Pass and Band Rejection Filters

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Band Pass Filter (BPF)

A **Band Pass Filter (BPF)** is a frequency-selective electronic circuit that allows signals within a specific range (band) of frequencies to pass through with minimal attenuation, while significantly attenuating (rejecting) all frequencies outside this band.

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus):** A Band Pass Filter is a second-order (or higher) selective circuit that transmits signals with frequencies lying between two cutoff frequencies — a **lower cutoff frequency ($f_L$)** and an **upper cutoff frequency ($f_H$)** — and rejects (attenuates) all frequencies below $f_L$ and above $f_H$.

The pass band is defined as:
$$f_L \leq f \leq f_H$$

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Analogy — The "Selective Hearing" Filter:** Imagine you are at a noisy railway station. Your brain acts like a **Band Pass Filter** when you are listening to a friend on a phone call. Your mind **passes** the voice frequency band (roughly 300 Hz to 3.4 kHz for a telephone), **rejects** low-frequency rumble of trains (below 300 Hz), and **rejects** high-frequency whistle/noise (above 3.4 kHz). Only the desired "band" of human voice reaches your conscious hearing.

Another analogy: A **graphic equalizer slider** on a music system that boosts only the mid-range frequencies (say, 1 kHz to 5 kHz) and cuts everything else is acting as a Band Pass Filter.

## 1.2 Band Rejection (Band Stop) Filter (BRF / BSF)

A **Band Rejection Filter (BRF)** — also called a **Band Stop Filter (BSF)** or **Notch Filter** — performs the *opposite* function: it **rejects (attenuates)** signals within a specific frequency band and **passes** all frequencies outside that band.

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition:** A Band Rejection Filter is a frequency-selective circuit that **attenuates signals within a stop band** (between $f_L$ and $f_H$) and **transmits signals at frequencies below $f_L$ and above $f_H$** with minimal loss.

The stop band is defined as:
$$f_L \leq f \leq f_H$$

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Analogy — The "Noise Cancelling" Filter:** Consider active noise-cancelling headphones. They must **remove the 50 Hz mains hum** (and its harmonics) from the audio signal but **pass** the music (which has frequencies from ~20 Hz to 20 kHz). The headphones essentially act as a **Narrow Band Rejection (Notch) Filter** centered at 50 Hz, cutting only that annoying hum band while keeping the rest of the audio intact.

A **wide band rejection filter** is like a "do-not-disturb" zone for a wide spectrum — e.g., a TV antenna filter that rejects the entire UHF band (470–890 MHz) but allows VHF and satellite bands to pass.

## 1.3 Classification Overview

| Filter Type | Abbreviation | Function |
|---|---|---|
| Band Pass Filter | BPF | Passes a band; rejects below & above |
| Band Stop / Rejection Filter | BSF / BRF | Rejects a band; passes below & above |
| Notch Filter | NF | Narrow Band Rejection (high Q) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** The KTU 2024 Scheme emphasizes the **first-order** and **second-order realizations** of BPF and BRF using RC/RL networks, op-amps (active filters), and the **design equations** for $f_L$, $f_H$, bandwidth $BW$, and quality factor $Q$.

## 1.4 Frequency Response — The "Visual Signature"

A quick way to identify each filter from its **Bode magnitude plot**:

- **BPF** → Looks like a **"hill"** or **"bell-shaped curve"** (peaks at center, falls on both sides).
- **BRF/BSF** → Looks like a **"valley"** or **"inverted bell"** (flat on both sides, dips to a minimum at center).

These response shapes are fundamental to communication systems, instrumentation amplifiers, and signal conditioning circuits.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Band Pass Filter (BPF) — Operational Theory

### 2.1.1 Realization Strategy (Cascade Method)

A first-order BPF can be realized by **cascading a first-order High Pass Filter (HPF)** with a **first-order Low Pass Filter (LPF)**, provided that:

$$f_L \text{ (HPF cutoff)} < f_H \text{ (LPF cutoff)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Cascade?** Because the HPF blocks low-frequency noise and DC offsets, and the LPF blocks high-frequency noise and interference. Only the frequencies that "survive" both stages — i.e., those lying between $f_L$ and $f_H$ — appear at the output. This is the **fundamental principle** behind all BPF realizations.

The overall transfer function is the product of the individual transfer functions:
$$H_{BPF}(s) = H_{HPF}(s) \cdot H_{LPF}(s)$$

### 2.1.2 Key Performance Parameters

1. **Lower Cutoff Frequency ($f_L$)** — Determined by the HPF section.
2. **Upper Cutoff Frequency ($f_H$)** — Determined by the LPF section.
3. **Bandwidth (BW)** — The width of the pass band.
4. **Center / Resonant Frequency ($f_0$ or $f_r$)** — The geometric mean of $f_L$ and $f_H$.
5. **Quality Factor ($Q$)** — The "selectivity" or "sharpness" of the filter.

### 2.1.3 The "Why" Behind the Center Frequency

The center frequency is **not** the arithmetic mean; it is the **geometric mean** because filter responses are logarithmic in nature:

$$f_0 = \sqrt{f_L \cdot f_H}$$

This is the frequency at which the **output amplitude is maximum** (for a BPF) or **minimum** (for a BSF).

## 2.2 Band Rejection Filter (BRF / BSF) — Operational Theory

### 2.2.1 Realization Strategy

A **wide-band BRF** is realized by **summing (parallel combining) the outputs of a first-order LPF and a first-order HPF**, where their cutoff frequencies are arranged so that:

$$f_L \text{ (HPF)} < f_H \text{ (LPF)}$$

The transfer function:
$$H_{BSF}(s) = H_{LPF}(s) + H_{HPF}(s)$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Summation?** The LPF passes low frequencies, the HPF passes high frequencies. When we add the two outputs, **all frequencies** appear at the sum output **except** the band between $f_L$ and $f_H$ — the "dead zone" where neither filter passes signal. This creates the rejection band.

### 2.2.2 Narrow Band Rejection (Notch) Filter

A **narrow-band BRF** (high-Q notch filter) is typically built using a **second-order active filter topology** such as:

- **Twin-T Notch Filter** (passive RC network + op-amp for active isolation).
- **Wien Bridge Notch Filter** (uses positive and negative feedback paths).

The notch frequency is given by:
$$f_n = \frac{1}{2\pi RC}$$

with a quality factor:
$$Q = \frac{1}{2(2 - A_v)}$$

where $A_v$ is the op-amp gain. The filter becomes unstable / oscillatory when $A_v = 2$.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following table is a MUST-MEMORIZE for KTU ESE. Every question on BPF/BRF in Module 2 will use one or more of these formulas.**

| # | Parameter | Formula | Unit | Applicable To |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Lower Cutoff Frequency | $f_L = \dfrac{1}{2\pi R_1 C_1}$ | **Hz** | BPF / BRF (HPF stage) |
| 2 | Upper Cutoff Frequency | $f_H = \dfrac{1}{2\pi R_2 C_2}$ | **Hz** | BPF / BRF (LPF stage) |
| 3 | Bandwidth | $BW = f_H - f_L$ | **Hz** | BPF / BRF |
| 4 | Center / Resonant Frequency | $f_0 = \sqrt{f_L \cdot f_H}$ | **Hz** | BPF / BRF |
| 5 | Quality Factor | $Q = \dfrac{f_0}{BW} = \dfrac{\sqrt{f_L \cdot f_H}}{f_H - f_L}$ | dimensionless | BPF / BRF |
| 6 | Pass-band Gain (Cascaded) | $A_v = A_{V1} \cdot A_{V2}$ | dimensionless | BPF |
| 7 | Notch Frequency | $f_n = \dfrac{1}{2\pi RC}$ | **Hz** | Notch Filter |
| 8 | Notch Q-factor | $Q = \dfrac{1}{2(2 - A_v)}$ | dimensionless | Twin-T Notch |
| 9 | Angular Center Frequency | $\omega_0 = 2\pi f_0$ | **rad/s** | All |
| 10 | First-order RC time constant | $\tau = RC$ | **seconds** | All RC filters |

> [!WARNING]
> **Vertical Pipe Restriction:** In your KTU answer sheets (and in tables above), use `Q = f0 / BW` notation. **Do NOT** write `Q = f0 | BW` with vertical pipe symbol — it breaks the KTU valuation parser and may lose presentation marks.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application | Filter Used | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| **Radio receivers (AM/FM)** | Tuned BPF | Select desired station, reject others |
| **Audio equalizers** | Multi-band BPF | Boost/cut specific frequency bands |
| **EEG/ECG instrumentation** | BPF (0.5–40 Hz) | Remove muscle/mains noise |
| **DSL modems** | BRF | Reject specific interferer frequencies |
| **50/60 Hz hum removal** | Notch (BRF) | Eliminate mains interference in biomedical signals |
| **Spectrum analyzers** | BPF (variable) | Sweep through frequency bands |
| **Communication transceivers** | BPF at IF stage | Channel selection at intermediate frequency |

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Transfer Function of a Cascaded First-Order BPF

### Setup

Consider a first-order **RC High Pass Filter** (HPF) with $R_1$, $C_1$ cascaded with a first-order **RC Low Pass Filter** (LPF) with $R_2$, $C_2$. Both stages use a non-inverting unity-gain buffer (voltage follower) for isolation.

### Step 1: Transfer Function of the HPF Stage

The standard transfer function of a first-order RC HPF is:

$$
H_{HPF}(s) = \frac{V_{o1}(s)}{V_i(s)} = \frac{R_1 C_1 \, s}{1 + R_1 C_1 \, s}
$$

**Conversion Logic:** This comes from the voltage divider formed by $C_1$ (impedance $1/sC_1$) and $R_1$ in series. The output is taken across $R_1$. As $s \to \infty$ (high frequency), $|H_{HPF}| \to 1$ (passes); as $s \to 0$ (DC), $|H_{HPF}| \to 0$ (blocks).

### Step 2: Transfer Function of the LPF Stage

The standard transfer function of a first-order RC LPF is:

$$
H_{LPF}(s) = \frac{V_o(s)}{V_{o1}(s)} = \frac{1}{1 + R_2 C_2 \, s}
$$

**Conversion Logic:** Output is taken across $C_2$. As $s \to 0$ (DC), $|H_{LPF}| \to 1$ (passes); as $s \to \infty$, $|H_{LPF}| \to 0$ (blocks).

### Step 3: Overall BPF Transfer Function

Since the stages are cascaded, the overall transfer function is the product:

$$
H_{BPF}(s) = H_{HPF}(s) \cdot H_{LPF}(s) = \left( \frac{R_1 C_1 \, s}{1 + R_1 C_1 \, s} \right) \cdot \left( \frac{1}{1 + R_2 C_2 \, s} \right)
$$

$$
H_{BPF}(s) = \frac{R_1 C_1 \, s}{(1 + R_1 C_1 \, s)(1 + R_2 C_2 \, s)}
$$

### Step 4: Solve for Lower Cutoff Frequency $f_L$

At $f = f_L$, the HPF output is at $-3$ dB (i.e., $|H_{HPF}| = 1/\sqrt{2}$). This occurs when:

$$
R_1 C_1 \, \omega_L = 1 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \omega_L = \frac{1}{R_1 C_1}
$$

Converting to Hz ($f = \omega/2\pi$):

$$
\boxed{f_L = \frac{1}{2\pi R_1 C_1}}
$$

### Step 5: Solve for Upper Cutoff Frequency $f_H$

At $f = f_H$, the LPF output is at $-3$ dB. This occurs when:

$$
R_2 C_2 \, \omega_H = 1 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \omega_H = \frac{1}{R_2 C_2}
$$

$$
\boxed{f_H = \frac{1}{2\pi R_2 C_2}}
$$

### Step 6: Derive the Bandwidth

$$
\boxed{BW = f_H - f_L = \frac{1}{2\pi R_2 C_2} - \frac{1}{2\pi R_1 C_1}}
$$

### Step 7: Derive the Center / Resonant Frequency

The center frequency is the geometric mean of the two cutoffs. This is obtained by finding the frequency at which the overall magnitude response is maximum.

For the cascaded response:
$$|H_{BPF}(j\omega)| = \frac{R_1 C_1 \, \omega}{\sqrt{1 + (R_1 C_1 \, \omega)^2} \cdot \sqrt{1 + (R_2 C_2 \, \omega)^2}}$$

Setting $\dfrac{d}{d\omega} \vert H_{BPF}(j\omega) \vert = 0$ and solving yields the geometric mean. For the common design case $R_1 C_1 = R_2 C_2 = RC$:

$$
\boxed{f_0 = \frac{1}{2\pi RC}}
$$

In the general (asymmetric) case:

$$
\boxed{f_0 = \sqrt{f_L \cdot f_H} = \frac{1}{2\pi \sqrt{R_1 R_2 C_1 C_2}}}
$$

### Step 8: Derive the Quality Factor $Q$

$$
\boxed{Q = \frac{f_0}{BW} = \frac{\sqrt{f_L \cdot f_H}}{f_H - f_L}}
$$

**Conversion Logic:** Q is a measure of filter "selectivity" or "sharpness." A **high Q** means a **narrow**, sharp pass band (highly selective). A **low Q** means a **wide**, gentle pass band.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Transfer Function of a Wide-Band Band Stop Filter

### Setup

A wide-band BSF is formed by **summing** the outputs of an LPF and an HPF in parallel using a summing amplifier (or simply node-addition with unity-gain buffers).

### Step 1: Individual Transfer Functions

$$
H_{LPF}(s) = \frac{1}{1 + R_2 C_2 \, s}, \qquad H_{HPF}(s) = \frac{R_1 C_1 \, s}{1 + R_1 C_1 \, s}
$$

### Step 2: Summation Transfer Function

$$
H_{BSF}(s) = H_{LPF}(s) + H_{HPF}(s)
$$

$$
H_{BSF}(s) = \frac{1}{1 + R_2 C_2 \, s} + \frac{R_1 C_1 \, s}{1 + R_1 C_1 \, s}
$$

### Step 3: Common Denominator Form

Putting over a common denominator:

$$
H_{BSF}(s) = \frac{(1 + R_1 C_1 s) + R_1 C_1 s \, (1 + R_2 C_2 s)}{(1 + R_1 C_1 s)(1 + R_2 C_2 s)}
$$

Expanding the numerator:

$$
H_{BSF}(s) = \frac{1 + R_1 C_1 s + R_1 C_1 s + R_1 R_2 C_1 C_2 s^2}{(1 + R_1 C_1 s)(1 + R_2 C_2 s)}
$$

$$
H_{BSF}(s) = \frac{1 + 2 R_1 C_1 s + R_1 R_2 C_1 C_2 s^2}{(1 + R_1 C_1 s)(1 + R_2 C_2 s)}
$$

### Step 4: Limit Behavior (Sanity Check)

- As $s \to 0$ (low frequency): LPF passes, HPF blocks → $|H_{BSF}| \to 1$ ✓
- As $s \to \infty$ (high frequency): HPF passes, LPF blocks → $|H_{BSF}| \to 1$ ✓
- In the middle band: Both filters partially attenuate → $|H_{BSF}|$ dips below 1 → **Stop band created** ✓

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Notch Frequency of the Twin-T Network

### Setup

A Twin-T notch filter consists of two T-networks in parallel:
- One T-network: two series resistors $R$, one shunt capacitor $2C$.
- Other T-network: two series capacitors $C$, one shunt resistor $R/2$.

### Step 1: Symmetry Argument

By the symmetry of the network, at the **notch frequency**, the two T-sections produce outputs that are **equal in magnitude but opposite in phase** (180° apart), causing complete cancellation.

### Step 2: Notch Frequency Derivation

Setting the imaginary part of the admittance sum to zero:

$$
\omega_n^2 = \frac{1}{R^2 C^2}
$$

$$
\boxed{f_n = \frac{1}{2\pi RC}}
$$

**Conversion Logic:** This is identical in form to the RC LPF/HPF cutoff. The notch is positioned where the two paths cancel.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Worked Example (KTU Style)

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** A BPF is designed with $R_1 = 10 \text{ k}\Omega$, $C_1 = 0.01 \text{ \mu F}$ (HPF stage) and $R_2 = 1 \text{ k}\Omega$, $C_2 = 0.01 \text{ \mu F}$ (LPF stage). Find $f_L$, $f_H$, $f_0$, $BW$, and $Q$.

### Solution

**Step 1:** Compute $f_L$:
$$
f_L = \frac{1}{2\pi R_1 C_1} = \frac{1}{2\pi \times 10 \times 10^3 \times 0.01 \times 10^{-6}}
$$
$$
f_L = \frac{1}{2\pi \times 10^{-4}} = \frac{10^4}{2\pi} = 1591.55 \text{ Hz} \approx 1.59 \text{ kHz}
$$

**Step 2:** Compute $f_H$:
$$
f_H = \frac{1}{2\pi R_2 C_2} = \frac{1}{2\pi \times 10^3 \times 10^{-8}}
$$
$$
f_H = \frac{1}{2\pi \times 10^{-5}} = \frac{10^5}{2\pi} = 15915.5 \text{ Hz} \approx 15.92 \text{ kHz}
$$

**Step 3:** Compute $f_0$:
$$
f_0 = \sqrt{f_L \cdot f_H} = \sqrt{1591.55 \times 15915.5} = \sqrt{2.533 \times 10^7}
$$
$$
f_0 \approx 5032.7 \text{ Hz} \approx 5.03 \text{ kHz}
$$

**Step 4:** Compute $BW$:
$$
BW = f_H - f_L = 15915.5 - 1591.55 = 14323.95 \text{ Hz} \approx 14.32 \text{ kHz}
$$

**Step 5:** Compute $Q$:
$$
Q = \frac{f_0}{BW} = \frac{5032.7}{14323.95} \approx 0.351
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation:** A Q of 0.35 indicates a **wide-band filter** (not highly selective). For high selectivity (narrow-band), one would need $f_L$ and $f_H$ to be very close, giving a small BW and a large Q.

---

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — BPF Realization (Cascade Method)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Input Signal Vi] --> B[Stage 1: First Order HPF R1C1]
    B --> C[Buffer / Voltage Follower OpAmp]
    C --> D[Stage 2: First Order LPF R2C2]
    D --> E[Output Vo = Band Passed Signal]
    F[fL set by R1C1] -.-> B
    G[fH set by R2C2] -.-> D
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Read the diagram left-to-right:** The input signal first encounters the HPF, which removes low-frequency content. The buffered signal then enters the LPF, which removes high-frequency content. The final output contains only the **band** between $f_L$ and $f_H$.

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Wide-Band BRF Realization (Parallel / Summation Method)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Input Signal Vi] --> B[Input Splitter / Buffer]
    B --> C1[Upper Path: LPF R2C2]
    B --> C2[Lower Path: HPF R1C1]
    C1 --> D[Summing Amplifier or Node]
    C2 --> D
    D --> E[Output Vo = Band Rejected Signal]
    F[Passes f below fL] -.-> C1
    G[Passes f above fH] -.-> C2
    H[Rejects f between fL and fH] -.-> D
```

> [!TIP]
> **Why Parallel?** Notice that in the BRF case, the two filter paths operate in **parallel** (not in series) because we need to **preserve** the low and high frequencies, and only the middle band should be cancelled. The LPF and HPF outputs are **added** algebraically at the summing node.

## 4.3 Frequency Response Characteristics (Conceptual Mermaid Plot)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph BPF_Response["BAND PASS FILTER RESPONSE"]
        direction LR
        P1[Low Freq Rejected] --> P2[Gain Rises at fL]
        P2 --> P3[Peak Gain at f0]
        P3 --> P4[Gain Falls at fH]
        P4 --> P5[High Freq Rejected]
    end
    subgraph BRF_Response["BAND REJECTION FILTER RESPONSE"]
        direction LR
        Q1[Low Freq Passed] --> Q2[Gain Steady till fL]
        Q2 --> Q3[Notch Dip at f0]
        Q3 --> Q4[Gain Rises at fH]
        Q4 --> Q5[High Freq Passed]
    end
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Visual Distinction:** In a **BPF**, the curve is a **bell shape** (peak at $f_0$). In a **BRF**, the curve is a **valley shape** (dip at $f_0$). The X-axis represents frequency (log scale), and the Y-axis represents gain in dB.

## 4.4 Detailed Functional Architecture — Narrow-Band Notch Filter (Twin-T)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Input Signal Vin] --> B[Twin-T Passive Network]
    B --> B1[Path 1: Resistor-Resistor-Capacitor T]
    B --> B2[Path 2: Capacitor-Capacitor-Resistor T]
    B1 --> C[OpAmp Non-Inverting Input]
    B2 --> D[OpAmp Inverting Input via Feedback]
    C --> E[Output Node Vout]
    D --> E
    E --> F[Notched Output: 50 Hz Removed]
    G[fn = 1 over 2piRC] -.-> B
    H[Q = 1 over 2 x 2 minus Av] -.-> E
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Functional Note:** The Twin-T topology achieves a **deep, narrow null** at $f_n$ by exploiting the phase opposition of the two T-paths. The op-amp provides gain $A_v$ that controls the **depth and Q** of the notch without loading the passive network.

## 4.5 Comparative Summary Table (as a Mermaid Conceptual Map)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Filters Module 2] --> B[BPF]
    A --> C[BRF]
    B --> B1[Realization: Cascade HPF + LPF]
    B --> B2[Parameters: fL, fH, f0, BW, Q]
    B --> B3[Application: Radio tuning]
    C --> C1[Realization: Parallel HPF || LPF]
    C --> C2[Parameters: fL, fH, f0, BW, Q]
    C --> C3[Application: 50 Hz hum removal]
    C --> C4[Sub-type: Notch filter high Q]
```
