# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Mark Pattern:** Part A questions test **Recall (L1)** and **Comprehension (L2)** levels. Answer length: 4–6 lines. Direct, definition-based, with one supporting formula or diagram.

---

### Question A1: Define Band Pass Filter. Mention its key parameters.

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]**

**Model Answer:**

A **Band Pass Filter (BPF)** is a frequency-selective circuit that allows signals within a specific range of frequencies (between a lower cutoff $f_L$ and an upper cutoff $f_H$) to pass through with minimum attenuation, while attenuating all frequencies outside this range.

**Key Parameters:**
1. **Lower Cutoff Frequency ($f_L$):** $\frac{1}{2\pi R_1 C_1}$ — set by the HPF stage.
2. **Upper Cutoff Frequency ($f_H$):** $\frac{1}{2\pi R_2 C_2}$ — set by the LPF stage.
3. **Bandwidth ($BW$):** $f_H - f_L$.
4. **Center Frequency ($f_0$):** $\sqrt{f_L \cdot f_H}$.
5. **Quality Factor ($Q$):** $\frac{f_0}{BW}$ — measure of selectivity.

---

### Question A2: What is a Notch Filter? Give one application.

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**

**Model Answer:**

A **Notch Filter** is a narrow-band band rejection filter that sharply attenuates a very specific frequency (called the **notch frequency** $f_n$) while passing all other frequencies with minimal loss. It has a very **high Q factor**.

**Notch Frequency:** $f_n = \dfrac{1}{2\pi RC}$

**Application:** Used in biomedical instrumentation (ECG/EEG amplifiers) to remove **50 Hz mains hum interference** without affecting the desired bio-signal frequencies (typically 0.5 Hz to 1 kHz).

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 ESE Rule:** Part B questions carry 14 marks each and may have an internal choice. Each sub-part is typically 7 marks. You must show **every formula, substitution, and unit** to score full marks.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) — Cascaded BPF Design

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model | Module 2 | 14 Marks]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the realization of a **first-order Band Pass Filter** using a cascade of HPF and LPF stages. Derive the expressions for lower cutoff frequency, upper cutoff frequency, and bandwidth.

**(b)** A first-order BPF is realized by cascading an HPF ($R_1 = 4.7 \text{ k}\Omega$, $C_1 = 0.033 \text{ \mu F}$) and an LPF ($R_2 = 470 \text{ }\Omega$, $C_2 = 0.033 \text{ \mu F}$). Calculate:
   - (i) Lower cutoff frequency $f_L$
   - (ii) Upper cutoff frequency $f_H$
   - (iii) Center frequency $f_0$
   - (iv) Bandwidth $BW$
   - (v) Quality factor $Q$

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — 7 Marks:**

A BPF is realized by **cascading a first-order HPF with a first-order LPF**, as shown in the block diagram below:

`[Input] → [HPF: R1, C1] → [Buffer] → [LPF: R2, C2] → [Output]`

**Derivation of $f_L$:** The HPF has the transfer function $H_{HPF}(s) = \frac{R_1 C_1 s}{1 + R_1 C_1 s}$. The cutoff ($-3$ dB) occurs when $\omega_L R_1 C_1 = 1$:

$$
f_L = \frac{1}{2\pi R_1 C_1}
$$

**Derivation of $f_H$:** The LPF has $H_{LPF}(s) = \frac{1}{1 + R_2 C_2 s}$. The cutoff occurs when $\omega_H R_2 C_2 = 1$:

$$
f_H = \frac{1}{2\pi R_2 C_2}
$$

**Derivation of Bandwidth:**

$$
BW = f_H - f_L = \frac{1}{2\pi R_2 C_2} - \frac{1}{2\pi R_1 C_1}
$$

**Center Frequency:**

$$
f_0 = \sqrt{f_L \cdot f_H}
$$

The HPF removes frequencies below $f_L$; the LPF removes frequencies above $f_H$. Only the band $f_L \leq f \leq f_H$ survives, giving a band pass response.

**Part (b) — 7 Marks:**

Given: $R_1 = 4.7 \text{ k}\Omega = 4.7 \times 10^3 \text{ }\Omega$, $C_1 = 0.033 \text{ \mu F} = 0.033 \times 10^{-6} \text{ F}$, $R_2 = 470 \text{ }\Omega$, $C_2 = 0.033 \times 10^{-6} \text{ F}$.

**(i) Lower cutoff frequency $f_L$:**

$$
f_L = \frac{1}{2\pi R_1 C_1} = \frac{1}{2\pi \times 4.7 \times 10^3 \times 0.033 \times 10^{-6}}
$$

$$
f_L = \frac{1}{2\pi \times 1.551 \times 10^{-4}} = \frac{1}{9.745 \times 10^{-4}}
$$

$$
\boxed{f_L \approx 1026.2 \text{ Hz} \approx 1.026 \text{ kHz}}
$$

**(ii) Upper cutoff frequency $f_H$:**

$$
f_H = \frac{1}{2\pi R_2 C_2} = \frac{1}{2\pi \times 470 \times 0.033 \times 10^{-6}}
$$

$$
f_H = \frac{1}{2\pi \times 1.551 \times 10^{-5}} = \frac{1}{9.745 \times 10^{-5}}
$$

$$
\boxed{f_H \approx 10262 \text{ Hz} \approx 10.26 \text{ kHz}}
$$

**(iii) Center frequency $f_0$:**

$$
f_0 = \sqrt{f_L \cdot f_H} = \sqrt{1026.2 \times 10262}
$$

$$
f_0 = \sqrt{1.053 \times 10^7} \approx 3245.4 \text{ Hz}
$$

$$
\boxed{f_0 \approx 3.245 \text{ kHz}}
$$

**(iv) Bandwidth $BW$:**

$$
BW = f_H - f_L = 10262 - 1026.2 = 9235.8 \text{ Hz}
$$

$$
\boxed{BW \approx 9.236 \text{ kHz}}
$$

**(v) Quality factor $Q$:**

$$
Q = \frac{f_0}{BW} = \frac{3245.4}{9235.8} \approx 0.351
$$

$$
\boxed{Q \approx 0.35}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Q for BPF:** Many students **incorrectly** write $Q = \dfrac{BW}{f_0}$ (reciprocal). This is the Q definition for an **RLC resonant circuit's bandwidth interpretation**, not the filter Q. The correct filter Q is $\dfrac{f_0}{BW}$. **Loses 1 mark** if reversed.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Band Rejection Filter Analysis

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model | Module 2 | 14 Marks]**

**(a)** With the help of a block diagram, explain the realization of a **wide-band Band Stop Filter** using parallel combination of LPF and HPF. Derive the transfer function.

**(b)** Design a Twin-T notch filter to reject **50 Hz mains hum** in a biomedical amplifier. Choose $R$ and $C$ appropriately. State the design equations used.

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — 7 Marks:**

A **wide-band BSF** is realized using a **parallel combination** (summing) of an LPF and an HPF, both driven by the same input:

`[Input] → [Splitter] → [LPF] ─┐`
`              → [HPF] ─┴→ [Summing Amplifier] → [Output]`

The LPF passes low frequencies (below $f_H$); the HPF passes high frequencies (above $f_L$). In the band $f_L$ to $f_H$, neither path conducts strongly, creating a **stop band**.

**Transfer Function Derivation:**

$$
H_{BSF}(s) = H_{LPF}(s) + H_{HPF}(s)
$$

$$
H_{BSF}(s) = \frac{1}{1 + R_2 C_2 s} + \frac{R_1 C_1 s}{1 + R_1 C_1 s}
$$

Common denominator:

$$
H_{BSF}(s) = \frac{1 + R_1 C_1 s + R_1 C_1 s(1 + R_2 C_2 s)}{(1 + R_1 C_1 s)(1 + R_2 C_2 s)}
$$

$$
H_{BSF}(s) = \frac{1 + 2 R_1 C_1 s + R_1 R_2 C_1 C_2 s^2}{1 + (R_1 C_1 + R_2 C_2)s + R_1 R_2 C_1 C_2 s^2}
$$

**Verification:**
- At $s = 0$ (low freq): $H_{BSF} = 1$ → passes ✓
- At $s \to \infty$ (high freq): $H_{BSF} = 1$ → passes ✓
- In the middle: $|H_{BSF}| < 1$ → rejection ✓

**Part (b) — 7 Marks:**

**Design Specification:** Notch frequency $f_n = 50 \text{ Hz}$.

**Design Equation:**

$$
f_n = \frac{1}{2\pi RC} \quad \Rightarrow \quad RC = \frac{1}{2\pi f_n}
$$

**Step 1:** Compute RC product:

$$
RC = \frac{1}{2\pi \times 50} = \frac{1}{314.159} = 3.183 \times 10^{-3} \text{ seconds}
$$

**Step 2:** Choose a convenient value of $C$ and solve for $R$. Let $C = 0.1 \text{ \mu F} = 10^{-7} \text{ F}$:

$$
R = \frac{3.183 \times 10^{-3}}{10^{-7}} = 3.183 \times 10^4 \text{ }\Omega \approx 31.83 \text{ k}\Omega
$$

**Step 3:** Standardize component values:
- Choose $C = 0.1 \text{ \mu F}$ (standard)
- $R \approx 31.83 \text{ k}\Omega$ → use $33 \text{ k}\Omega$ (standard E12 value)

**Recalculated notch frequency with standard values:**

$$
f_n = \frac{1}{2\pi \times 33 \times 10^3 \times 0.1 \times 10^{-6}} = \frac{1}{2.0735 \times 10^{-2}}
$$

$$
f_n \approx 48.23 \text{ Hz}
$$

(Acceptable for 50 Hz notch — within ±5% tolerance.)

**Component Selection for Twin-T Network:**
- Two series resistors: $R = 33 \text{ k}\Omega$
- Shunt resistor: $R/2 = 16.5 \text{ k}\Omega \approx 16 \text{ k}\Omega$ (standard)
- Two series capacitors: $C = 0.1 \text{ \mu F}$
- Shunt capacitor: $2C = 0.2 \text{ \mu F}$

**Op-amp gain setting** for desired Q (say $Q = 5$):

$$
Q = \frac{1}{2(2 - A_v)} \quad \Rightarrow \quad 5 = \frac{1}{2(2 - A_v)}
$$

$$
2(2 - A_v) = \frac{1}{5} = 0.2 \quad \Rightarrow \quad 2 - A_v = 0.1 \quad \Rightarrow \quad A_v = 1.9
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Notch Filter Component Ratios:** In a Twin-T network, the **shunt element** is **double** the series element ($2C$ and $R/2$). Students often write $C$ and $R$ for the shunt as well. This is the **most common mistake** in KTU exams on Twin-T filters. **Loses 2 marks** if the ratios are wrong.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Pitfall & Warning Summary

> [!WARNING]
> **Top 5 Common Mistakes in KTU Module 2 Filter Questions:**
>
> 1. **Confusing $f_0$ formulas:** Writing $f_0 = (f_L + f_H)/2$ (arithmetic mean) instead of $\sqrt{f_L f_H}$ (geometric mean). **Penalty: 1 mark.**
>
> 2. **Inverted Q factor:** Writing $Q = BW/f_0$ instead of $Q = f_0/BW$. **Penalty: 1 mark.**
>
> 3. **BPF vs BSF confusion in cascade vs parallel:** Students often describe the BPF as parallel and BSF as cascade. Remember: **BPF = Cascade**, **BSF = Parallel/Sum**. **Penalty: 2–3 marks.**
>
> 4. **Missing units in final answer:** KTU valuators specifically check for units. Always write "Hz" or "kHz" after the numerical value. **Penalty: 0.5 mark per missing unit.**
>
> 5. **Forgetting to convert $\mu F$ to $F$ in RC product:** A common computation error leads to answers off by a factor of $10^6$. Always convert: $1 \text{ \mu F} = 10^{-6} \text{ F}$. **Penalty: full marks of the sub-part if answer is numerically wrong.**

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 2: Band Pass & Band Rejection Filters**

### A. Core Concepts (Must Know)

- A **Band Pass Filter (BPF)** passes frequencies between $f_L$ and $f_H$; rejects all others.
- A **Band Rejection Filter (BRF/BSF)** rejects frequencies between $f_L$ and $f_H$; passes all others.
- A **Notch Filter** is a narrow-band BRF with very high Q.
- BPF is realized by **cascading HPF + LPF** (series).
- BRF (wide-band) is realized by **summing LPF || HPF outputs** (parallel).
- Notch filter is realized using **Twin-T** or **Wien Bridge** topology.

### B. The 5 Key Formulas (Must Memorize)

1. $f_L = \dfrac{1}{2\pi R_1 C_1}$ — HPF stage
2. $f_H = \dfrac{1}{2\pi R_2 C_2}$ — LPF stage
3. $BW = f_H - f_L$
4. $f_0 = \sqrt{f_L \cdot f_H}$
5. $Q = \dfrac{f_0}{BW}$

### C. Design Rules of Thumb

- **Always** ensure $f_L < f_H$ (otherwise no pass band exists).
- **For symmetric BPF:** Choose $R_1 C_1 = R_2 C_2 = RC$ → $f_0 = \dfrac{1}{2\pi RC}$.
- **For high-Q narrow band:** $f_L$ and $f_H$ must be very close (e.g., 990 Hz and 1010 Hz → Q = 50).
- **Notch filter stability:** $A_v$ must be **strictly less than 2** to avoid oscillation.

### D. Applications (For 2-Mark Sub-Questions)

| Application | Filter | Why |
|---|---|---|
| AM Radio tuner | BPF | Select one station's carrier |
| ECG instrumentation | Notch (50/60 Hz) | Remove mains hum |
| Audio crossover | BPF/BRF | Direct frequencies to correct speakers |
| Spectrum analyzer | Variable BPF | Sweep and display |
| DSL line filters | BRF | Isolate voice from data bands |

### E. Common Units & Constants

- Frequency: **Hz** (or kHz, MHz for higher ranges)
- Capacitance: **Farads (F)** — always convert $\mu F$, nF, pF to F before calculation
- Resistance: **Ohms ($\Omega$)** — convert k$\Omega$ to $\Omega$
- Q factor: **dimensionless**
- Standard $\pi \approx 3.14159$, $2\pi \approx 6.28318$

### F. Exam Strategy (KTU 2024 ESE)

- For 14-mark questions, always draw the **block diagram first** (1 mark reserved).
- Show **every substitution** with proper units — partial marking is strict.
- Mention the **condition** $f_L < f_H$ explicitly in BPF/BRF derivations.
- For Notch filter, **state the Twin-T element ratios** ($R/2$ and $2C$) — this is the most-missed detail.

### G. Quick-Reference Mnemonic

> **"BPF = Cascade, BRF = Combine; Pass = Peak Hill, Stop = Notch Valley."**

> **"For filters, remember: $f_0$ is the **G**eometric mean, **N**ot arithmetic!"**

> **"Q is **Q**uotient: $f_0$ **Q**uotient over $BW$ — never reverse!"**

---

> **END OF MODULE 2 NOTES — Band Pass and Band Rejection Filters**
> *Prepared as per KTU 2024 Scheme | PEEET411 — Electronic Instrumentation*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Introduction to Digital Filters
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET411 - ELECTRONIC INSTRUMENTATION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Introduction to Digital Filters

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Digital Filter** is a discrete-time linear time-invariant (LTI) system that performs mathematical operations on a sampled, discrete-time signal to selectively attenuate or enhance specific frequency components. Unlike analog filters, which operate on continuous-time signals using resistors, capacitors, and inductors, digital filters process sequences of numbers using numerical algorithms implemented on digital hardware (microprocessors, DSP chips, FPGAs) or software.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Exact Terminology):**
> A digital filter is a discrete-time system described by a difference equation that computes the output sample $y[n]$ as a weighted linear combination of current and past input samples $x[n], x[n-1], \dots$ and past output samples $y[n-1], y[n-2], \dots$, with the goal of modifying the signal's frequency spectrum according to a desired specification.

The general input–output relationship (Linear Constant-Coefficient Difference Equation – LCCDE) is:

$$
\sum_{k=0}^{N} a_k \, y[n-k] = \sum_{k=0}^{M} b_k \, x[n-k]
$$

where $N$ is the order of the filter, $a_k$ and $b_k$ are the filter coefficients, and the convention is $a_0 = 1$ (to make the equation solvable for $y[n]$).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Coffee Sieve" 🍵

Imagine you are handed a **mixture of sand and sugar** dumped together on a tray (this is your noisy signal, containing both a useful low-frequency trend and unwanted high-frequency noise). You want only the **sugar** (the desired signal).

- An **analog filter** is like a physical kitchen sieve: you pour the mixture through a mesh of a specific size, and physically only certain "grains" pass through. The mesh's hole size corresponds to the **cutoff frequency**.
- A **digital filter** is like a **smart electronic chef** who takes a photograph of the tray (sampling), converts each grain to a number, and then **mathematically adds or subtracts** the numbers to *reconstruct* only the sugar grains on a fresh plate. Nothing physical moves — only numbers are crunched.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Takeaway for Students:**
> Digital filtering is essentially *signal arithmetic* — we manipulate the *amplitude* of numbers representing signal samples to reshape the frequency content. The "filter" exists only as a list of coefficients and a recurrence formula.

## 1.3 Why Digital Filters Matter in Instrumentation

In the domain of **Electronic Instrumentation** (the parent course PEEET411), digital filters are indispensable because:

- They remove **power-line interference (50 Hz hum)** from biomedical signals (ECG, EEG, EMG).
- They suppress **sensor noise** in transducer outputs (strain gauges, thermocouples).
- They extract **carrier-frequency components** in communication and modulation systems.
- They are **immune to component drift** (since coefficients are stored in memory, not realized by analog components whose values change with temperature and age).
- They offer **predictable, repeatable performance** with **arbitrary precision** (limited only by word length of the processor).

## 1.4 Standard Performance Metrics (Used Throughout the Module)

| Metric | Symbol | Standard Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Sampling Frequency | $f_s$ | **Hz** |
| Sampling Period | $T_s$ | **seconds (s)** |
| Nyquist Frequency | $f_N$ | **Hz** |
| Pass-band Ripple | $A_p$ | **dB** |
| Stop-band Attenuation | $A_s$ | **dB** |
| Filter Order | $N$ | dimensionless |
| Group Delay | $\tau_g$ | **seconds (s)** |
| Transition Width | $\Delta f$ | **Hz** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Highlight:** The most common and most testable relationship is the **Nyquist–Shannon Sampling Theorem**, which states that to avoid aliasing, the sampling frequency must satisfy $f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$, where $f_{max}$ is the highest frequency present in the analog input signal. The upper bound $f_s/2$ is called the **Nyquist frequency**, and any meaningful filtering must occur in the band $[0, \, f_s/2]$.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📗 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Digital Filters

Digital filters are broadly classified into two fundamental families based on their impulse response length and memory structure.

### 2.1.1 Finite Impulse Response (FIR) Filters

- The impulse response has **finite duration** (it settles to zero in a finite number of samples).
- Output depends **only on present and past inputs** — **no feedback** path.
- Always **inherently stable** (no poles, only zeros on the z-plane, all poles are at the origin $z = 0$).
- Can achieve **linear phase** response (constant group delay) when coefficients are symmetric or anti-symmetric.
- Implemented using the **convolution sum**:

$$
y[n] = \sum_{k=0}^{M} b_k \, x[n-k]
$$

- Where $M$ is the **filter order** (number of delay elements minus 1), and there are $M+1$ coefficients (taps).

### 2.1.2 Infinite Impulse Response (IIR) Filters

- The impulse response has **infinite duration** (it never fully settles to zero).
- Output depends on **both inputs and past outputs** — **feedback path is present**.
- Implemented using the **difference equation**:

$$
y[n] = \sum_{k=0}^{M} b_k \, x[n-k] \; - \; \sum_{k=1}^{N} a_k \, y[n-k]
$$

- More **computationally efficient** (achieves sharper transition with fewer coefficients) but **less stable** and cannot achieve exact linear phase.
- Mimics classical analog prototypes (Butterworth, Chebyshev, Elliptic, Bessel) via **Bilinear Transformation**.

## 2.2 Functional Block Diagram Components

A digital filter is built from three elementary computational blocks:

1. **Delay Element ($z^{-1}$):** Stores the previous sample. Implemented as a shift register in hardware.
2. **Multiplier:** Multiplies a delayed input by a coefficient $b_k$ or $a_k$.
3. **Adder (Accumulator):** Sums the scaled terms to produce the new output $y[n]$.

## 2.3 Operational Steps of a Digital Filter

- **Step 1 — Sampling:** The continuous-time analog signal $x_a(t)$ is sampled at regular intervals $T_s = 1/f_s$ to produce $x[n] = x_a(nT_s)$.
- **Step 2 — Analog-to-Digital Conversion (ADC):** Each sample is quantized to a finite binary word (e.g., 12-bit, 16-bit).
- **Step 3 — Arithmetic Processing:** The processor executes the difference equation for every $n$, using stored coefficients from ROM/RAM.
- **Step 4 — Digital-to-Analog Conversion (DAC) [Optional]:** If a continuous output is needed, the filtered samples are passed through a reconstruction filter (zero-order hold + smoothing low-pass).
- **Step 5 — Update:** The state variables are shifted — the present $x[n]$ becomes $x[n-1]$ in the next cycle.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "Why" Matters (KTU-Examiner Perspective):**
> The difference between FIR and IIR is the single most asked conceptual question in KTU module tests. The core distinction is **feedback (IIR) vs. no feedback (FIR)**, which translates to **memory of past outputs (IIR) vs. only past inputs (FIR)**.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table consolidates **every high-yield equation** a KTU 2024 student must memorize for this topic. Pay special attention to units and boundary conditions.

| Concept | Equation | Variable Meaning | Conditions / Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| Difference Equation (General) | $\sum_{k=0}^{N} a_k \, y[n-k] = \sum_{k=0}^{M} b_k \, x[n-k]$ | $a_k, b_k$ = coefficients | $a_0 = 1$ (normalization) |
| FIR Output | $y[n] = \sum_{k=0}^{M} b_k \, x[n-k]$ | $M$ = number of taps $-1$ | $N = 0$ (no feedback) |
| IIR Output | $y[n] = \sum_{k=0}^{M} b_k \, x[n-k] - \sum_{k=1}^{N} a_k \, y[n-k]$ | First sum = feed-forward | Second sum = feedback |
| Sampling Frequency | $f_s = 1/T_s$ | $T_s$ = sampling period | $\mathbf{Hz}$ |
| Nyquist Criterion | $f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$ | $f_{max}$ = highest signal freq. | Anti-aliasing condition |
| Transfer Function | $H(z) = \dfrac{\sum_{k=0}^{M} b_k \, z^{-k}}{\sum_{k=0}^{N} a_k \, z^{-k}}$ | Ratio of polynomials in $z^{-1}$ | $z = e^{j\Omega}$ on unit circle |
| Frequency Response | $H(e^{j\Omega}) = \sum_{k=0}^{M} b_k \, e^{-j\Omega k}$ | $\Omega = 2\pi f / f_s$ | $\Omega$ in **radians/sample** |
| Magnitude Response | $\vert H(e^{j\Omega})\vert$ | Real-valued function of $\Omega$ | Measured in **dB** |
| Phase Response | $\angle H(e^{j\Omega})$ | Argument of complex function | Measured in **radians** |
| Group Delay | $\tau_g(\Omega) = -\dfrac{d}{d\Omega}\angle H(e^{j\Omega})$ | Derivative of phase w.r.t. $\Omega$ | $\mathbf{samples}$ (multiply by $T_s$ for seconds) |
| Filter Gain (dB) | $A_{dB} = 20 \log_{10}\vert H(e^{j\Omega})\vert$ | $A_{dB} = 0$ at unity gain | Used in Bode plots |
| Linear Phase Condition | $\angle H(e^{j\Omega}) = -\alpha \Omega$ for constant $\alpha$ | $\alpha$ = constant delay | FIR only, with symmetric $b_k$ |
| Impulse Response | $h[n] = $ response to $\delta[n]$ | $h[n] = b_n$ for FIR | FIR length = $M+1$ |
| Stability (IIR) | $\vert p_i \vert < 1$ for all poles $p_i$ | Pole inside unit circle | $z$-plane condition |
| Bilinear Transform | $s = \dfrac{2}{T_s}\cdot\dfrac{1 - z^{-1}}{1 + z^{-1}}$ | Maps analog $s$ to digital $z$ | Pre-warping required |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Biomedical Instrumentation (ECG/EEG machines):** A 0.5–40 Hz **band-pass FIR filter** removes baseline wander and high-frequency muscle noise, while a 50 Hz **notch filter** kills power-line interference.
- **Audio Equalizers (Hi-Fi, MP3 players):** Crossover networks in loudspeakers are FIR filters implemented on DSPs.
- **Industrial Process Control (PLCs, SCADA):** IIR low-pass filters smooth noisy sensor readings (pressure, flow, temperature).
- **Telecommunications (4G/5G base stations):** Hundreds of FIR filter banks separate user channels.
- **Radar and Sonar Systems:** Matched filters (a type of FIR) maximize the signal-to-noise ratio for target detection.
- **Instrumentation-grade Data Acquisition (DAQ):** Digital filters replace bulky anti-aliasing analog filters.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 📙 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: From Difference Equation to Transfer Function

We will now perform an exhaustive step-by-step derivation, which is one of the most frequently asked **14-mark problems** in KTU university exams.

### 3.1.1 Starting Point — The General LCCDE

The starting equation is the Linear Constant-Coefficient Difference Equation describing an LTI digital filter:

$$
\sum_{k=0}^{N} a_k \, y[n-k] = \sum_{k=0}^{M} b_k \, x[n-k]
$$

Here $N$ is the **denominator order** (number of feedback taps) and $M$ is the **numerator order** (number of feed-forward taps).

### 3.1.2 Step 1 — Apply the Z-Transform to Both Sides

Using the **time-shift property** of the Z-transform, namely $\mathcal{Z}\{x[n-k]\} = z^{-k} X(z)$, we obtain:

$$
\mathcal{Z}\left\{\sum_{k=0}^{N} a_k \, y[n-k]\right\} = \mathcal{Z}\left\{\sum_{k=0}^{M} b_k \, x[n-k]\right\}
$$

### 3.1.3 Step 2 — Factor Out the Signal Terms

Applying linearity of the Z-transform, the constant coefficients $a_k$ and $b_k$ factor out:

$$
\sum_{k=0}^{N} a_k \, \mathcal{Z}\{y[n-k]\} = \sum_{k=0}^{M} b_k \, \mathcal{Z}\{x[n-k]\}
$$

Substituting the time-shift result $\mathcal{Z}\{y[n-k]\} = z^{-k} Y(z)$ and similarly for $X(z)$:

$$
\sum_{k=0}^{N} a_k \, z^{-k} Y(z) = \sum_{k=0}^{M} b_k \, z^{-k} X(z)
$$

### 3.1.3 Step 3 — Group $Y(z)$ and $X(z)$ Together

Rearranging the summation to isolate $Y(z)$ on the left and $X(z)$ on the right:

$$
Y(z) \left(\sum_{k=0}^{N} a_k \, z^{-k}\right) = X(z) \left(\sum_{k=0}^{M} b_k \, z^{-k}\right)
$$

### 3.1.4 Step 4 — Define the Transfer Function $H(z)$

By definition, the **transfer function** of an LTI system is the ratio of output to input in the z-domain:

$$
H(z) = \frac{Y(z)}{X(z)}
$$

Therefore, dividing both sides of the equation by $X(z) \cdot \sum a_k z^{-k}$, we get the final result:

$$
H(z) = \frac{\displaystyle \sum_{k=0}^{M} b_k \, z^{-k}}{\displaystyle \sum_{k=0}^{N} a_k \, z^{-k}}
$$

This is the **canonical rational polynomial form** of the digital filter's transfer function.

### 3.1.5 Step 5 — Express in Pole–Zero Form

Multiplying numerator and denominator by $z^{N}$ and $z^{M}$ respectively converts the negative powers of $z$ into the more intuitive positive-power form:

$$
H(z) = \frac{b_0 \, z^{M} + b_1 \, z^{M-1} + \cdots + b_M}{a_0 \, z^{N} + a_1 \, z^{N-1} + \cdots + a_N}
$$

Factoring gives the **pole-zero representation**:

$$
H(z) = K \cdot \frac{\prod_{k=1}^{M} (z - z_k)}{\prod_{k=1}^{N} (z - p_k)}
$$

where $z_k$ are the **zeros** (roots of numerator) and $p_k$ are the **poles** (roots of denominator), and $K = b_0 / a_0$ is the gain constant.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** The pole-zero plot on the z-plane is the **single most powerful diagnostic** a student can use to design and verify digital filters. Stability of an IIR filter requires **all poles to lie strictly inside the unit circle** $\vert p_k \vert < 1$.

## 3.2 Derivation: Frequency Response from Transfer Function

To evaluate how the filter behaves at different physical frequencies, we substitute $z = e^{j\Omega}$, where $\Omega$ is the **normalized digital frequency** in radians per sample.

### 3.2.1 Substitution into Transfer Function

$$
H(e^{j\Omega}) = \frac{\displaystyle \sum_{k=0}^{M} b_k \, e^{-j\Omega k}}{\displaystyle \sum_{k=0}^{N} a_k \, e^{-j\Omega k}}
$$

### 3.2.2 Magnitude and Phase

The complex quantity $H(e^{j\Omega})$ can be separated into magnitude and phase:

$$
H(e^{j\Omega}) = \vert H(e^{j\Omega})\vert \cdot e^{j\angle H(e^{j\Omega})}
$$

- **Magnitude Response:** $\vert H(e^{j\Omega})\vert$ tells us the gain at each frequency.
- **Phase Response:** $\angle H(e^{j\Omega})$ tells us the phase shift introduced.

For a digital filter to be **practically realizable** in real-time on a sampled signal, the condition $f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$ (Nyquist) must hold; equivalently, $\Omega$ must lie in the range $[-\pi, +\pi]$ radians per sample, which corresponds to physical frequencies $[-f_s/2, +f_s/2]$.

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example: A Simple 3-Tap FIR Filter

Consider a 3-tap moving average FIR filter described by the difference equation:

$$
y[n] = \frac{1}{3} x[n] + \frac{1}{3} x[n-1] + \frac{1}{3} x[n-2]
$$

From this, the coefficients are identified as $b_0 = b_1 = b_2 = 1/3$ and there are no feedback terms ($a_k = 0$ for $k \geq 1$, $a_0 = 1$). The transfer function is:

$$
H(z) = \frac{1}{3} \left(1 + z^{-1} + z^{-2}\right)
$$

To find the magnitude response, substitute $z = e^{j\Omega}$:

$$
H(e^{j\Omega}) = \frac{1}{3} \left(1 + e^{-j\Omega} + e^{-j2\Omega}\right)
$$

Factor out $e^{-j\Omega}$ from the rightmost two terms using Euler's identity:

$$
H(e^{j\Omega}) = \frac{1}{3} \, e^{-j\Omega} \left( e^{j\Omega} + 1 + e^{-j\Omega} \right)
$$

Using the Euler relation $e^{j\Omega} + e^{-j\Omega} = 2\cos(\Omega)$:

$$
H(e^{j\Omega}) = \frac{1}{3} \, e^{-j\Omega} \left( 1 + 2\cos\Omega \right)
$$

The **magnitude** is therefore:

$$
\vert H(e^{j\Omega})\vert = \frac{1}{3} \, \vert 1 + 2\cos\Omega \vert
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Result Interpretation:**
> At $\Omega = 0$ (DC, zero frequency), $\cos(0) = 1$, so $\vert H(0) \vert = 1$ (unity gain — DC passes through).
> At $\Omega = 2\pi/3$ (one-third of Nyquist), $\cos(2\pi/3) = -1/2$, so $\vert H \vert = 0$ (complete null — first notch).
> At $\Omega = \pi$ (Nyquist), $\cos(\pi) = -1$, so $\vert H(\pi) \vert = 1/3$ (high-frequency attenuation, not full suppression).
> This proves the filter is a **low-pass** type with a notch at $f = f_s/3$.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 📕 SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram of a Generic Digital Filter (Mermaid)

The following Mermaid diagram shows the **functional block-level architecture** of a general IIR digital filter, illustrating how input samples flow through delay, multiplier, and adder elements. The architecture is identical for FIR filters except that the **feedback path** (the lower delay chain) is absent.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeIn["Input x of n"] --> nodeSplitter{"Signal Splitter"}
    nodeSplitter --> nodeFF0["Multiplier b0"]
    nodeSplitter --> nodeD1["Delay z power -1"]
    nodeD1 --> nodeFF1["Multiplier b1"]
    nodeD1 --> nodeD2["Delay z power -1"]
    nodeD2 --> nodeFF2["Multiplier b2"]

    nodeSplitter --> nodeOutPrev["Past Output y of n-1"]
    nodeOutPrev --> nodeFB1["Delay z power -1"]
    nodeFB1 --> nodeFBM1["Multiplier a1"]
    nodeFBM1 --> nodeFBNeg["Negator -1"]

    nodeFF0 --> nodeAdd1["Adder Sum"]
    nodeFF1 --> nodeAdd1
    nodeFF2 --> nodeAdd1
    nodeFBNeg --> nodeAdd1
    nodeAdd1 --> nodeOut["Output y of n"]
    nodeOut --> nodeOutPrev
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:**
> The **top horizontal chain** (feed-forward path with coefficients $b_0, b_1, b_2$) is the **FIR part**.
> The **lower feedback path** (with coefficient $a_1$ and a negator) is what makes this an **IIR filter**.
> Removing the entire lower branch yields a pure **FIR filter** block diagram.

## 4.2 Process Flow Diagram — Real-Time Filtering Pipeline

This second diagram shows the **end-to-end signal chain** from the physical analog world through the digital filter and back out.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeSensor["Analog Sensor Signal xa of t"] --> nodeAAFilter["Analog Anti-Aliasing LPF"]
    nodeAAFilter --> nodeSampleHold["Sample and Hold Circuit"]
    nodeSampleHold --> nodeADC["Analog to Digital Converter ADC"]
    nodeADC --> nodeDP["Digital Processor with Coefficients"]
    nodeDP --> nodeDAC["Digital to Analog Converter DAC"]
    nodeDAC --> nodeSmooth["Smoothing Reconstruction Filter"]
    nodeSmooth --> nodeFinal["Filtered Analog Output y of t"]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why the Anti-Aliasing Filter is MANDATORY (KTU High-Yield):**
> The analog low-pass filter **before** the ADC must band-limit the input to $[0, f_s/2]$. Without it, any frequency component above $f_s/2$ will *alias* (fold back) into the baseband and **cannot be removed later** by any digital filter. This is the **"garbage in, garbage out"** principle of sampled systems.

## 4.3 Pole–Zero Plot Schematic (Conceptual)

A pole–zero plot places zeros (○) and poles (×) on the complex $z$-plane. For the 3-tap moving average example derived in Section 3.3, the zeros are at $z = e^{\pm j 2\pi/3}$ and there are two poles at the origin $z = 0$ (since denominator is unity).

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeOrigin["Unit Circle Center 0,0"]
    nodePoleA["Pole at 0,0 multiplicity 2"]
    nodeZero1["Zero at angle 120 degrees radius 1"]
    nodeZero2["Zero at angle -120 degrees radius 1"]
    nodeOrigin --- nodePoleA
    nodeOrigin --- nodeZero1
    nodeOrigin --- nodeZero2
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Design Rule of Thumb (KTU Tip):**
> Zeros on the unit circle ⇒ **exact nulls** (complete rejection at that frequency).
> Poles close to the unit circle ⇒ **sharp peaks** in magnitude response.
> For a stable IIR filter, **all poles must lie strictly inside the unit circle**.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 📒 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define a digital filter. Mention its two broad classifications. **[3 Marks]**
**Model Answer:**
A digital filter is a discrete-time system that processes a sampled input signal $x[n]$ using a difference equation to produce an output $y[n]$ with a modified frequency spectrum. The two broad classifications are:
- **FIR (Finite Impulse Response) Filter** — has a finite-duration impulse response and uses only feed-forward terms; output depends only on present and past input samples.
- **IIR (Infinite Impulse Response) Filter** — has an infinite-duration impulse response; output depends on present/past inputs as well as past outputs (feedback path).

---

### Question 2: State the Nyquist–Shannon sampling theorem and its significance in digital filtering. **[3 Marks]**
**Model Answer:**
The Nyquist–Shannon sampling theorem states that a band-limited analog signal with maximum frequency $f_{max}$ can be uniquely reconstructed from its samples if the sampling frequency satisfies $f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$. The frequency $f_s/2$ is called the **Nyquist frequency**.
**Significance:** All digital filtering operations are mathematically meaningful only within the frequency band $[0, f_s/2]$. Violation of this theorem causes **aliasing**, which is irreversible and corrupts the filtered output.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Length 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice)

### 📌 Question A: Analyze the difference equation, transfer function, and stability of an IIR filter. **[14 Marks]**

**Part (a)** Derive the transfer function $H(z)$ from the general linear constant-coefficient difference equation. Show all intermediate steps. **[7 Marks]**

**Model Solution — Part (a):**

**Step 1 — State the LCCDE:**
The general difference equation is:

$$
\sum_{k=0}^{N} a_k \, y[n-k] = \sum_{k=0}^{M} b_k \, x[n-k]
$$

**Step 2 — Apply Z-transform to both sides:**

$$
\sum_{k=0}^{N} a_k \, z^{-k} \, Y(z) = \sum_{k=0}^{M} b_k \, z^{-k} \, X(z)
$$

**Step 3 — Rearrange to isolate $H(z) = Y(z)/X(z)$:**

$$
H(z) = \frac{\displaystyle \sum_{k=0}^{M} b_k \, z^{-k}}{\displaystyle \sum_{k=0}^{N} a_k \, z^{-k}}
$$

**Step 4 — Convert to pole-zero form by multiplying by $z^M/z^N$:**

$$
H(z) = K \cdot \frac{\prod_{k=1}^{M} (z - z_k)}{\prod_{k=1}^{N} (z - p_k)}
$$

**Step 5 — Note the conditions:**
For stability of an IIR filter, all poles must satisfy $\vert p_k \vert < 1$ (lie inside the unit circle in the $z$-plane).

---

**Part (b)** For the digital filter described by $y[n] = 0.5 x[n] + 0.5 x[n-1]$, determine the type, magnitude response at $\Omega = 0$ and $\Omega = \pi$, and verify whether it is linear phase. **[7 Marks]**

**Model Solution — Part (b):**

**Step 1 — Identify filter type:**
Comparing with $y[n] = \sum b_k x[n-k]$, we see $b_0 = 0.5$ and $b_1 = 0.5$. There is no feedback (no $y[n-k]$ terms with $k \geq 1$). Hence this is a **2-tap FIR filter**.

**Step 2 — Write the transfer function:**

$$
H(z) = 0.5 + 0.5 z^{-1} = 0.5 (1 + z^{-1})
$$

**Step 3 — Find the frequency response by substituting $z = e^{j\Omega}$:**

$$
H(e^{j\Omega}) = 0.5 \left(1 + e^{-j\Omega}\right) = 0.5 \, e^{-j\Omega/2} \left(e^{j\Omega/2} + e^{-j\Omega/2}\right)
$$

Using Euler's identity $e^{j\theta} + e^{-j\theta} = 2\cos\theta$:

$$
H(e^{j\Omega}) = e^{-j\Omega/2} \cdot \cos(\Omega/2)
$$

**Step 4 — Magnitude at $\Omega = 0$ (DC):**

$$
\vert H(e^{j0})\vert = \vert \cos(0) \vert = 1
$$

So DC gain is **unity (0 dB)**.

**Step 5 — Magnitude at $\Omega = \pi$ (Nyquist):**

$$
\vert H(e^{j\pi})\vert = \vert \cos(\pi/2) \vert = 0
$$

So the gain at Nyquist is **zero** — complete rejection of highest frequency.

**Step 6 — Linear Phase Verification:**
The phase response is $\angle H(e^{j\Omega}) = -\Omega/2$, which is a **linear function of $\Omega$** with slope $-\tfrac{1}{2}$. Since the phase is linear, the filter has a **constant group delay** of $1/2$ sample, which means the filter exhibits **linear phase**.

**Conclusion:** It is a 2-tap **FIR low-pass filter** with linear phase, unity DC gain, and a null at $f = f_s/2$.

---

### 📌 Question B: Explain the bilinear transformation method for designing IIR digital filters from analog prototypes. **[14 Marks]**

**Part (a)** State the bilinear transformation equation and explain the concept of frequency pre-warping. Why is pre-warping necessary? **[7 Marks]**

**Model Solution — Part (a):**

**Step 1 — State the bilinear transformation:**

$$
s = \frac{2}{T_s} \cdot \frac{1 - z^{-1}}{1 + z^{-1}} \quad \Longleftrightarrow \quad z = \frac{1 + s T_s/2}{1 - s T_s/2}
$$

This maps the entire **left-half of the analog $s$-plane** onto the **interior of the unit circle** in the $z$-plane, ensuring stability is preserved.

**Step 2 — Explain pre-warping:**
The bilinear transform introduces a **non-linear frequency warping** described by:

$$
\Omega = 2 \arctan\left(\frac{\omega T_s}{2}\right)
$$

where $\omega$ is the analog frequency (rad/s) and $\Omega$ is the digital frequency (rad/sample).

**Step 3 — Why pre-warping is necessary:**
Because the mapping compresses the infinite analog frequency range $[0, \infty)$ into the finite digital range $[0, \pi]$, critical frequencies (pass-band edges, stop-band edges, notch frequencies) are **shifted**. To make the digital filter meet the *originally specified* critical frequencies, we must **pre-warp** each analog prototype frequency $\omega_c$ to a new value $\omega_{c,new}$ using:

$$
\omega_{c,new} = \frac{2}{T_s} \tan\left(\frac{\Omega_c}{2}\right)
$$

**Step 4 — Design procedure summary:**
(i) Specify digital critical frequencies $f_{c,1}, f_{c,2}$.
(ii) Convert to $\Omega_c = 2\pi f_c / f_s$.
(iii) Pre-warp to analog prototype frequencies.
(iv) Design the analog filter (Butterworth/Chebyshev/Elliptic) at $\omega_{c,new}$.
(v) Substitute the bilinear $s$-equation into $H_a(s)$ to obtain $H(z)$.

---

**Part (b)** Compare FIR and IIR filters across at least six parameters in a tabular form. State one engineering application where FIR is preferred over IIR, and one where IIR is preferred. **[7 Marks]**

**Model Solution — Part (b):**

| Parameter | FIR Filter | IIR Filter |
|---|---|---|
| Impulse Response Duration | Finite | Infinite |
| Feedback Path | Absent | Present |
| Stability | Always stable | Conditionally stable |
| Linear Phase | Easily achievable | Cannot achieve exact linear phase |
| Computational Cost | High (many taps for sharp cutoff) | Low (few coefficients) |
| Memory Required | Stores $M+1$ input samples | Stores past inputs and outputs |
| Sensitivity to Coefficient Quantization | Low | High (can cause instability) |
| Design Method | Windowing, Frequency Sampling, Parks–McClellan | Bilinear transform of analog prototypes |
| Group Delay | Constant (for symmetric coefficients) | Frequency-dependent (non-linear) |
| Phase Distortion | None (linear phase) | Significant (non-linear) |

**Application where FIR is preferred:**
**Biomedical signal processing (ECG filtering).** A 0.5–40 Hz band-pass FIR filter is used because linear phase preserves the morphology of QRS complexes — any waveform distortion could lead to misdiagnosis. Linear phase is essential when time-domain fidelity matters.

**Application where IIR is preferred:**
**Audio tone control (bass/treble equalizers in low-cost audio systems).** Here computational resources are limited, sharp roll-off is needed with few multiplications, and minor phase distortion is inaudible. A 2nd-order Butterworth IIR filter (biquad) is the industry standard.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where Students Lose Easy Marks:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the normalization $a_0 = 1$.** In the general LCCDE, students often rewrite $y[n] = \sum b_k x[n-k] - \sum_{k=1}^{N} a_k y[n-k]$ *without* dividing the original equation by $a_0$. This produces incorrect coefficients and propagates errors throughout the transfer function. Always set $a_0 = 1$ first.
>
> 2. **Confusing analog frequency $f$ (Hz) with digital frequency $\Omega$ (rad/sample).** The conversion is $\Omega = 2\pi f / f_s$. A student who mixes these up will get wrong magnitude plots.
>
> 3. **Saying "FIR is more stable than IIR" instead of "FIR is *inherently* stable, while IIR is *conditionally* stable."** The examiner looks for precise wording.
>
> 4. **Skipping the stability check in IIR design questions.** Always end your IIR solution with the statement: "All poles $\vert p_i \vert < 1$, hence the filter is BIBO stable."
>
> 5. **Forgetting the anti-aliasing filter.** Any digital filtering answer that ignores the analog pre-filter will be marked down. Always mention the **anti-aliasing low-pass filter** before the ADC.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- ✅ A **digital filter** is a discrete-time LTI system described by a **difference equation**.
- ✅ The general form is $\sum_{k=0}^{N} a_k y[n-k] = \sum_{k=0}^{M} b_k x[n-k]$, with $a_0 = 1$.
- ✅ **FIR** = only feed-forward; **IIR** = feed-forward + feedback.
- ✅ FIR is **always stable**; IIR stability requires $\vert p_i \vert < 1$ (poles inside unit circle).
- ✅ FIR can achieve **exact linear phase** (symmetric or anti-symmetric coefficients).
- ✅ IIR is **computationally cheaper** but **sensitive to coefficient quantization**.
- ✅ The **transfer function** is $H(z) = Y(z)/X(z) = \dfrac{\sum b_k z^{-k}}{\sum a_k z^{-k}}$.
- ✅ The **frequency response** is obtained by $H(e^{j\Omega})$, where $\Omega = 2\pi f / f_s$.
- ✅ **Nyquist theorem:** $f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$; Nyquist frequency is $f_s/2$.
- ✅ **Bilinear transform:** $s = \frac{2}{T_s} \cdot \frac{1 - z^{-1}}{1 + z^{-1}}$ — used for IIR design from analog prototypes.
- ✅ **Frequency pre-warping** is mandatory in bilinear transformation to preserve critical frequencies.
- ✅ An **anti-aliasing analog low-pass filter** must precede the ADC in any real digital filtering system.
- ✅ **Group delay** $\tau_g = -d \angle H / d\Omega$ must be constant for linear phase (FIR only).
- ✅ **Common analog prototypes** for IIR design: Butterworth (maximally flat passband), Chebyshev Type I (passband ripple), Chebyshev Type II (stopband ripple), Elliptic (sharpest roll-off), Bessel (linear phase).
- ✅ **FIR design methods:** Rectangular/Hamming/Hanning/Blackman windowing, Frequency Sampling, Optimal Parks–McClellan.
- ✅ **Difference equation → transfer function** conversion is a 7-mark derivation question; memorize every step.
- ✅ The **3-tap moving average** $y[n] = (x[n] + x[n-1] + x[n-2])/3$ is the simplest low-pass FIR filter with unity DC gain and null at $f = f_s/3$.
- ✅ **Zeros on the unit circle** ⇒ exact frequency nulls; **poles near the unit circle** ⇒ sharp resonance peaks.
- ✅ In **instrumentation**, digital filters are used for noise removal (50 Hz notch, baseline wander), signal smoothing, channel separation, and matched filtering for sensor applications.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Data Transmission - Types of Telemetry System - Modulation Methods: Pulse Modulation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET411 - ELECTRONIC INSTRUMENTATION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Data Transmission - Types of Telemetry System - Modulation Methods: Pulse Modulation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Data Transmission in Instrumentation

**Data Transmission** is the process of transferring measured information (from a sensor/transducer) from one location (the *measuring site* or *remote station*) to another (the *receiving/control station*) using a communication channel. In the context of electronic instrumentation, data transmission forms the **backbone of telemetry systems**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Telemetry** = *Tele* (remote) + *Metron* (measure) = **Remote Measurement**. It is the science and technology of automatic measurement and transmission of data from a remote source to a receiving station where it is recorded, displayed, and analyzed.

**Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus):**
> *Telemetry is the process of measuring a quantity, transmitting the measured value to a distant station, and subsequently reconstructing, displaying, and recording the original quantity at the receiver.*

---

## 1.2 The Three Fundamental Stages of Telemetry

Every telemetry system, regardless of complexity, executes three universal operations:

| Stage | Function | Instrumentation Stage |
|-------|----------|----------------------|
| **1. Transduction** | Convert physical quantity → electrical signal | Sensor / Transducer |
| **2. Transmission** | Send signal through a channel | Modulator + Channel + Demodulator |
| **3. Reception** | Recover original measurement | Signal Conditioner / Display / Recorder |

> [!NOTE]
> In the **KTU 2024 Scheme**, the focus of Module 2 is on the **Transmission Stage** — specifically, **how the electrical signal is encoded (modulated)** before being sent over the channel.

---

## 1.3 Types of Telemetry Systems

Telemetry systems are broadly classified into three categories based on the **nature of the transmitted signal**:

### 🔹 1.3.1 Analog Telemetry
- The measured quantity is transmitted as a **continuously varying analog signal** (voltage/current/frequency proportional to measurement).
- **Modulation techniques used:** AM, FM, PM (continuous modulation).
- **Sub-categories:**
  - **Voltage/Current Telemetry** — direct wire transmission (limited to short distances).
  - **Frequency Telemetry** — measurement converted to frequency; immune to amplitude noise.
  - **Position Telemetry** — used in synchros and servomechanisms (e.g., aircraft rudder position).

> [!NOTE]
> **Drawback:** Susceptible to noise accumulation; no error correction; accuracy degrades over long distances.

---

### 🔹 1.3.2 Digital Telemetry
- The analog signal is **sampled, quantized, and encoded into binary words** before transmission.
- **Modulation techniques used:** PCM (Pulse Code Modulation), Delta Modulation, DPCM.
- **Advantages:**
  - **High noise immunity** (only need to distinguish 0 and 1).
  - Enables **error detection and correction** (EDAC codes).
  - Compatible with digital computers, microcontrollers, and modern IoT systems.
  - Long-distance transmission without accuracy loss.

> [!NOTE]
> **Drawback:** Requires higher transmission bandwidth than analog; more complex circuitry (ADC, encoder, decoder).

---

### 🔹 1.3.3 Hybrid Telemetry
- Combines features of **both analog and digital telemetry**.
- **Example:** A system that uses analog modulation (FM) for the *primary* measurement, but a digital channel for *auxiliary* data (status flags, calibration constants).
- **Typical application:** Satellite telemetry — analog for slow-changing telemetry (temperatures), digital for high-precision scientific data.

---

## 1.4 Why Modulation? — The Conceptual Analogy

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Analogy — The "Postal Service" of Engineering Signals:**
> Imagine you have a tiny handwritten letter (your **low-frequency sensor signal**) that you need to send across the country. Walking it there yourself is slow, risky, and impractical. Instead, you **stuff the letter into a high-speed express envelope** (the **high-frequency carrier**) and let the postal service do the heavy lifting.
>
> In engineering terms, this is **Modulation**: embedding a low-frequency information signal into a high-frequency carrier wave for efficient, reliable, long-distance transmission.
>
> **Why is modulation necessary?**
> 1. **Antenna Size:** Efficient radiation requires antenna length ≈ λ/4. For a 1 kHz audio signal, λ = 300,000 m — antenna would be 75 km tall! Modulation brings this to a practical size.
> 2. **Multiplexing:** Multiple signals can share a single channel using FDM/TDM.
> 3. **Noise Immunity:** Frequency translation allows trading bandwidth for SNR.
> 4. **Channel Matching:** Signal must match the passband characteristics of the medium.

---

## 1.5 Classification of Modulation Methods

Modulation methods are classified into two **fundamental families** — this is one of the most heavily tested concepts in KTU exams:

```
MODULATION METHODS
├── 1. CONTINUOUS MODULATION (Carrier is a CONTINUOUS sine wave)
│   ├── AM  (Amplitude Modulation)
│   ├── FM  (Frequency Modulation)
│   └── PM  (Phase Modulation)
│
└── 2. PULSE MODULATION (Carrier is a PULSE TRAIN)
    ├── A. Analog Pulse Modulation
    │   ├── PAM (Pulse Amplitude Modulation)
    │   ├── PWM / PTM (Pulse Width / Pulse Time Modulation)
    │   └── PPM (Pulse Position Modulation)
    │
    └── B. Digital Pulse Modulation
        ├── PCM (Pulse Code Modulation)
        ├── DM  (Delta Modulation)
        └── DPCM (Differential Pulse Code Modulation)
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Distinction (frequently asked in KTU):**
> - **Analog Pulse Modulation** → Pulses vary **continuously** in amplitude, width, or position → information is in the **shape** of the pulse.
> - **Digital Pulse Modulation** → Pulses are converted into **binary code words** → information is in the **bit pattern**.

---

## 1.6 Pulse Modulation — The Core Topic

**Pulse Modulation** uses a **train of pulses** as the carrier. The pulses are modified according to the instantaneous amplitude of the message signal $m(t)$. Pulse modulation is the **bridge between analog and digital** worlds, and is the foundation of modern digital communication and data acquisition systems.

The **four primary types** of pulse modulation (PAM, PWM, PPM, PCM) are detailed in the following sections with the **depth, derivations, and KTU exam focus** required by the 2024 Scheme.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Pulse Amplitude Modulation (PAM)

### Concept
In **PAM**, the **amplitude of each pulse** in the pulse train is varied in proportion to the instantaneous value of the message signal $m(t)$ at the sampling instant. **Pulse width and position remain constant.**

### Mathematical Representation
A PAM signal can be expressed as:

$$
s_{PAM}(t) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} m(nT_s) \cdot p(t - nT_s)
$$

where:
- $m(nT_s)$ = sampled value of the message signal at instant $nT_s$
- $p(t)$ = rectangular pulse shape
- $T_s$ = sampling period
- $f_s = 1/T_s$ = sampling frequency

### Two Variants of PAM
| Type | Description | Use |
|------|-------------|-----|
| **Natural PAM (Sample-and-Hold-less)** | Pulse amplitude follows $m(t)$ during the pulse — pulse "tips" trace the message waveform | Rarely used (hard to demodulate) |
| **Flat-top PAM (Sample-and-Hold)** | Pulse amplitude held **constant** for the entire pulse width | **Most commonly used** — easily demodulated with low-pass filter |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Frequently Tested Point:** "Flat-top PAM is preferred over natural PAM because the pulse amplitude is constant over the pulse duration, making it compatible with sample-and-hold (S/H) circuits and easy to decode."

### Generation of PAM
- **Sample-and-Hold (S/H) circuit** with an **electronic switch** (FET/MOSFET) controlled by a narrow pulse train.
- Multiplication of message signal with pulse train → **Natural PAM**.
- S/H followed by pulse train gating → **Flat-top PAM**.

### Demodulation of PAM
- Pass the PAM signal through a **Low-Pass Filter (LPF)**.
- Cutoff frequency: $f_c \geq f_m$ (max message frequency) and $f_c \ll f_s$.
- The LPF reconstructs the original message signal.

### Drawbacks of PAM
- **Noise susceptibility** is the same as the original analog signal (no SNR improvement).
- Requires **wide bandwidth** if the pulses are narrow.
- **Pulse train always present** → wastes power.

---

## 2.2 Pulse Width Modulation (PWM) / Pulse Duration Modulation (PDM)

### Concept
In **PWM**, the **width (duration) of each pulse** is varied in proportion to the instantaneous value of the message signal, while the **amplitude and position remain constant**.

### Generation
- Compare the message signal $m(t)$ with a **sawtooth or triangular waveform** (the reference).
- Output is HIGH when $m(t) >$ reference, LOW otherwise.
- Result: pulse width ∝ $m(t)$.

### Demodulation
- Convert PWM → PAM using a **slope detector / integrator**.
- Pass the resulting PAM signal through an **LPF** to recover $m(t)$.

### Advantage over PAM
- **Constant amplitude** → better noise immunity (information is in time, not amplitude).
- Easily implemented using a single **comparator + triangular wave generator**.

### Disadvantage
- **Variable pulse width** → power spectrum spread; non-uniform bandwidth.
- Requires **more transmission power** than PPM (wider pulses waste energy).

---

## 2.3 Pulse Position Modulation (PPM)

### Concept
In **PPM**, the **position (time of occurrence) of each pulse** is varied in proportion to the instantaneous value of the message signal, while the **amplitude and width remain constant**.

### Generation
- First generate PWM signal.
- Differentiate the PWM signal to obtain narrow spikes (at the trailing edges of pulses).
- Clip/remove the negative-going spikes to get clean PPM pulses.

### Demodulation
- Convert PPM → PWM using a **sync pulse** and a **monostable multivibrator**.
- Then convert PWM → PAM (integrator) → LPF → $m(t)$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Hierarchy (KTU Favourite!):**
> PPM is obtained from PWM. PWM is obtained from PAM (conceptually). Hence:
> **Message → PAM → PWM → PPM**
> (Note: this is the *generation chain*, not the reverse demodulation chain.)

### Advantages of PPM
- **Best noise immunity** among the three analog pulse modulation methods.
- **Constant amplitude and width** → transmitted power is constant.
- **Position variation is less affected by amplitude noise.**

### Disadvantage
- Requires **synchronization** between transmitter and receiver (otherwise pulse positions cannot be interpreted).

---

## 2.4 Pulse Code Modulation (PCM) — The Most Important!

### Concept
PCM is the **bridge to digital communication**. It involves three steps:
1. **Sampling** — convert continuous-time → discrete-time.
2. **Quantization** — discretize the amplitude into L levels.
3. **Encoding** — represent each quantized level as an **n-bit binary code word**.

> [!NOTE]
> **PCM is the ONLY true digital modulation among the four primary pulse modulation techniques** — and it forms the heart of every digital telephone, MP3, CD, and modern IoT sensor node.

### Block Diagram — PCM Transmitter
```
Message Signal → [LPF (Anti-aliasing)] → [Sampler] → [Quantizer] → [Encoder] → Channel
```

### Block Diagram — PCM Receiver
```
Channel → [Decoder] → [Reconstruction LPF] → Original Message Signal
```

### Sampling Theorem (Nyquist–Shannon)
For faithful reconstruction of a band-limited signal with maximum frequency $f_m$:

$$
f_s \geq 2 f_m
$$

- $f_s = 2f_m$ is called the **Nyquist Rate**.
- $f_s > 2f_m$ is called **Over-Sampling**.
- $f_s < 2f_m$ causes **Aliasing** (irrecoverable distortion).

### Quantization
The continuous amplitude range $[-A, +A]$ is divided into $L = 2^n$ discrete levels.
- **Step Size (Resolution):** $q = \dfrac{2A}{2^n} = \dfrac{2A}{L}$
- **Quantization Error:** $e = m(t) - m_q(t)$ ranges from $-q/2$ to $+q/2$.

### Encoding
Each of the $L$ levels is assigned a unique **n-bit binary code word** (typically natural binary, but Gray code is preferred to minimize bit errors).

### Bit Rate and Transmission Bandwidth
For $N$ independent message channels multiplexed in time (TDM-PCM):

$$
R_b = N \cdot f_s \cdot n
$$

where:
- $R_b$ = bit rate in bits/second
- $N$ = number of channels
- $f_s$ = sampling rate per channel
- $n$ = number of bits per sample

Minimum Nyquist transmission bandwidth:

$$
B_{min} = \dfrac{R_b}{2} = \dfrac{N \cdot f_s \cdot n}{2}
$$

---

## 2.5 Differential Pulse Code Modulation (DPCM)

### Concept
- Transmit only the **difference** between the current sample and the predicted value.
- Reduces the number of bits needed → **lower bit rate** for the same quality.
- Uses a **predictor** (typically a first-order predictor: $\hat{x}(n) = a \cdot x(n-1)$).

### Bit Rate Savings
- DPCM uses **1 bit less** than standard PCM for the same SNR.
- Equivalent SNR improvement: **6 dB**.

---

## 2.6 Delta Modulation (DM)

### Concept
- The **simplest form of DPCM** with $n = 1$ bit.
- Only one bit transmitted per sample: **"1"** if signal increased, **"0"** if signal decreased (or vice versa).
- Uses a **1-bit quantizer** with step size $\Delta$.

### Two Critical Phenomena
| Phenomenon | Cause | Cure |
|------------|-------|------|
| **Slope Overload** | Step size $\Delta$ too small to follow rapid signal changes | Increase $\Delta$ or $f_s$ |
| **Granular Noise** | Step size $\Delta$ too large for slow-changing signal (output oscillates) | Decrease $\Delta$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Adaptive Delta Modulation (ADM)** dynamically varies $\Delta$ — eliminates both problems and is used in high-quality speech coding.

---

## 2.7 📋 KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

| # | Concept | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|---------|---------|---------------|
| 1 | Nyquist Sampling Rate | $f_s \geq 2f_m$ | Hz |
| 2 | PAM Signal | $s(t) = \sum m(nT_s) p(t-nT_s)$ | Volts |
| 3 | Quantization Step Size | $q = 2A/2^n$ | Volts |
| 4 | Quantization Noise Power | $\sigma_q^2 = q^2/12 = A^2/(3 \cdot 4^n)$ | W |
| 5 | Signal Power (Sinusoid) | $P_s = A^2/2$ (peak) or $A_{rms}^2$ | W |
| 6 | **PCM SNR (Sinusoidal)** | $\text{SNR}_{dB} = 1.76 + 6.02n$ | dB |
| 7 | PCM Bit Rate | $R_b = N \cdot f_s \cdot n$ | bps |
| 8 | Minimum PCM Bandwidth | $B_{min} = R_b/2$ | Hz |
| 9 | DPCM Bit Savings | $n_{DPCM} = n_{PCM} - 1$ | bits/sample |
| 10 | DM Step Size | $\Delta \geq (d m(t)/dt)_{max} / f_s$ | Volts |
| 11 | Multiplexing (TDM) Frame | $T_{frame} = N \cdot T_s$ | seconds |
| 12 | Quantization Error | $-q/2 \leq e \leq +q/2$ | Volts |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The MOST frequently tested formula in KTU 2024 Scheme:**
> $$\boxed{\text{SNR}_{dB} = 1.76 + 6.02n}$$
> where $n$ = number of bits per PCM sample. **Memorize this** — it appears in at least one question in every KTU exam paper.

---

## 2.8 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Technique | Real-World Application |
|-----------|------------------------|
| **PAM** | Sample-and-hold in ADCs, time-division multiplexing (TDM) front-end |
| **PWM** | Motor speed control, dimmer circuits, switching power supplies, audio class-D amplifiers |
| **PPM** | Optical fiber communication, IR remote controls, deep-space telemetry |
| **PCM** | Digital telephony (G.711), CD audio (16-bit, 44.1 kHz), MP3, VoLTE, IoT sensor data |
| **DPCM** | Predictive coding in video (older MPEG codecs), speech coding |
| **DM / ADM** | Military secure voice, low-bit-rate telemetry, speech coding (vocoders) |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Quantization Noise Power of a Uniform Quantizer

**Goal:** Derive the formula $\sigma_q^2 = q^2/12$ used in PCM SNR calculation.

### Setup
Assume the quantizer has step size $q$ and the quantization error $e$ is uniformly distributed in the interval $[-q/2, +q/2]$ (this is the standard assumption for fine quantization and high $n$).

The probability density function of the error $e$ is:

$$
p(e) = \dfrac{1}{q}, \quad -\dfrac{q}{2} \leq e \leq +\dfrac{q}{2}
$$

### Step 1 — Mean Square Value of the Error
By definition, the mean-square quantization noise power is:

$$
\sigma_q^2 = E[e^2] = \int_{-q/2}^{+q/2} e^2 \cdot p(e) \, de
$$

### Step 2 — Substitute $p(e)$

$$
\sigma_q^2 = \int_{-q/2}^{+q/2} e^2 \cdot \dfrac{1}{q} \, de = \dfrac{1}{q} \int_{-q/2}^{+q/2} e^2 \, de
$$

### Step 3 — Evaluate the Integral
Using the standard result $\int e^2 de = e^3/3$:

$$
\int_{-q/2}^{+q/2} e^2 \, de = \left[\dfrac{e^3}{3}\right]_{-q/2}^{+q/2} = \dfrac{1}{3}\left[\left(\dfrac{q}{2}\right)^3 - \left(-\dfrac{q}{2}\right)^3\right]
$$

### Step 4 — Simplify

$$
= \dfrac{1}{3} \cdot \dfrac{q^3}{8} - \dfrac{1}{3} \cdot \left(-\dfrac{q^3}{8}\right) = \dfrac{1}{3} \cdot \dfrac{q^3}{4} = \dfrac{q^3}{12}
$$

### Step 5 — Final Result

$$
\sigma_q^2 = \dfrac{1}{q} \cdot \dfrac{q^3}{12} = \dfrac{q^2}{12}
$$

$$
\boxed{\sigma_q^2 = \dfrac{q^2}{12}}
$$

This is the **quantization noise power** delivered to a 1 Ω load.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: PCM Signal-to-Quantization-Noise Ratio (SQNR)

**Goal:** Derive $\text{SNR}_{dB} = 1.76 + 6.02n$ for a **sinusoidal input signal** of peak amplitude $A$.

### Step 1 — Quantization Step Size
With $n$-bit quantizer covering total range $2A$:

$$
q = \dfrac{2A}{2^n}
$$

### Step 2 — Quantization Noise Power
Substitute $q$ into $\sigma_q^2 = q^2/12$:

$$
\sigma_q^2 = \dfrac{1}{12} \left(\dfrac{2A}{2^n}\right)^2 = \dfrac{4A^2}{12 \cdot 4^n} = \dfrac{A^2}{3 \cdot 4^n}
$$

### Step 3 — Signal Power (Sinusoidal)
For $m(t) = A \sin(2\pi f t)$, RMS amplitude $= A/\sqrt{2}$, hence:

$$
P_s = \left(\dfrac{A}{\sqrt{2}}\right)^2 = \dfrac{A^2}{2}
$$

### Step 4 — SQNR in Linear Scale

$$
\text{SNR} = \dfrac{P_s}{\sigma_q^2} = \dfrac{A^2/2}{A^2/(3 \cdot 4^n)} = \dfrac{3 \cdot 4^n}{2} = 1.5 \cdot 4^n
$$

### Step 5 — Convert to dB
Using $4^n = 2^{2n}$ and $10 \log_{10}(2) \approx 3.01$:

$$
\text{SNR}_{dB} = 10 \log_{10}(1.5) + 10 \log_{10}(4^n)
$$

$$
= 10 \log_{10}(1.5) + n \cdot 10 \log_{10}(4) = 1.76 + n \cdot 6.02
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{SNR}_{dB} = 1.76 + 6.02n \quad \text{dB}}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation:** Every additional bit of quantization **improves SQNR by ~6 dB** (i.e., **doubles the SNR** in linear scale). This rule of thumb is fundamental to ADC design.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Minimum PCM Transmission Bandwidth

**Goal:** Derive the minimum theoretical bandwidth required to transmit a PCM bit stream without ISI (Inter-Symbol Interference).

### Setup
Let bit rate be $R_b$ bits/second. Each bit occupies duration $T_b = 1/R_b$.

### Step 1 — Use Nyquist's First Criterion
For zero-ISI transmission, the minimum bandwidth equals half the **baud rate** (i.e., half the rate of signal changes in the channel). For a binary PCM stream, **baud rate = bit rate** $R_b$.

$$
B_{min} = \dfrac{R_b}{2}
$$

### Step 2 — Substitute $R_b = N \cdot f_s \cdot n$ (multiplexed PCM)

$$
B_{min} = \dfrac{N \cdot f_s \cdot n}{2}
$$

### Step 3 — Special Case: Single Channel
For $N = 1$ channel, sampled at Nyquist rate $f_s = 2f_m$:

$$
B_{min} = \dfrac{2f_m \cdot n}{2} = n \cdot f_m
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **This is a KTU favorite:** *"Compare the bandwidth of a PCM system to the original analog signal."* — Answer: PCM requires **at least $n$ times the original analog bandwidth** (because $B_{min} = n \cdot f_m$ vs. $f_m$ for analog). This is the **bandwidth–SNR trade-off** fundamental to data transmission.

---

## 3.4 Derivation: Bit Rate of an N-Channel TDM-PCM System

**Goal:** Derive $R_b = N \cdot f_s \cdot n$ for a Time-Division Multiplexed PCM system.

### Step 1 — Single-Channel Bit Rate
Each channel is sampled at $f_s$ samples/sec, each sample encoded into $n$ bits → bit rate per channel:

$$
R_{1} = f_s \cdot n \quad \text{bits/sec}
$$

### Step 2 — Multiplex $N$ Channels in Time
All $N$ channels time-share the channel. The composite bit rate is:

$$
R_b = N \cdot R_1 = N \cdot f_s \cdot n \quad \text{bits/sec}
$$

### Step 3 — Include Synchronization Overhead
In practice, additional bits are added for **framing, synchronization, and error correction**, raising the actual rate to:

$$
R_{actual} = R_b + R_{overhead}
$$

---

## 3.5 Derivation: Slope Overload Condition in Delta Modulation

**Goal:** Derive the maximum allowed step size $\Delta$ to avoid slope overload.

### Setup
The staircase output of the DM must follow the message signal $m(t)$ with slope $dm/dt$. The maximum slope the staircase can produce is $\Delta/T_s = \Delta \cdot f_s$ (one step per sample period).

### Step 1 — No Overload Condition
For **faithful tracking**, the staircase slope must exceed the maximum signal slope:

$$
\Delta \cdot f_s \geq \left(\dfrac{dm}{dt}\right)_{max}
$$

### Step 2 — Minimum Step Size

$$
\boxed{\Delta_{min} = \dfrac{1}{f_s} \left(\dfrac{dm}{dt}\right)_{max}}
$$

### Step 3 — Special Case: Sinusoidal Signal
For $m(t) = A \sin(2\pi f t)$:

$$
\dfrac{dm}{dt}\bigg|_{max} = 2\pi f A
$$

Therefore:

$$
\Delta_{min} = \dfrac{2\pi f A}{f_s}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Granular noise** (the *opposite* problem) is avoided when the step size is small — but $\Delta$ cannot be made arbitrarily small due to slope overload. **This trade-off is why ADM exists.**

---

## 3.6 Analytical Comparison Matrix (KTU Table-Marking Question!)

| Parameter | PAM | PWM | PPM | PCM |
|-----------|-----|-----|-----|-----|
| Variable Parameter | Amplitude | Width | Position | Code Word |
| Bandwidth Requirement | Low | High | Medium | Highest |
| Noise Immunity | Poor | Better | Best | Excellent (with EDAC) |
| Power Efficiency | Poor | Poor | Good | Best (with coding) |
| Synchronization Required | No | No | **Yes** | **Yes** |
| SNR Improvement over Analog | None | Marginal | Good | Excellent |
| Complexity | Low | Low | Medium | High |
| Information Type | Analog | Analog | Analog | **Digital** |
| Susceptibility to Amplitude Noise | **High** | Low | Low | **Lowest** (binary) |
| KTU Exam Frequency | ★★★ | ★★ | ★★★ | ★★★★★ |

---

## 3.7 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Style)

**Problem:** A signal $m(t)$ has maximum frequency $f_m = 4$ kHz. It is sampled at $f_s = 1.5 \times 2f_m$ and quantized using $n = 8$ bits. Calculate:
1. Sampling frequency
2. Bit rate
3. Minimum transmission bandwidth
4. SQNR for a full-scale sinusoid

### Solution

**Step 1:** Sampling frequency

$$
f_s = 1.5 \times 2f_m = 1.5 \times 2 \times 4000 = 12{,}000 \text{ Hz} = 12 \text{ kHz}
$$

**Step 2:** Bit rate (single channel)

$$
R_b = f_s \cdot n = 12{,}000 \times 8 = 96{,}000 \text{ bps} = 96 \text{ kbps}
$$

**Step 3:** Minimum bandwidth

$$
B_{min} = R_b / 2 = 96{,}000 / 2 = 48{,}000 \text{ Hz} = 48 \text{ kHz}
$$

**Step 4:** SQNR

$$
\text{SNR}_{dB} = 1.76 + 6.02 \times 8 = 1.76 + 48.16 = 49.92 \text{ dB}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **This is a typical KTU 14-mark problem.** Students who memorize $1.76 + 6.02n$ can solve part (d) in 30 seconds.

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 = 6 Marks)

### **Question 1** [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**Define Pulse Code Modulation (PCM). List its advantages over analog pulse modulation methods.**

#### Model Answer (3 Marks):

**Definition (1.5 marks):**
> **Pulse Code Modulation (PCM)** is a digital pulse modulation technique in which the message signal is **sampled, quantized, and encoded** into a sequence of **binary code words** for transmission.

**Three Process Steps (1 mark):**
1. **Sampling** — at $f_s \geq 2f_m$
2. **Quantization** — into $L = 2^n$ discrete levels (step size $q = 2A/2^n$)
3. **Encoding** — each level represented as an n-bit binary word

**Three Advantages (0.5 mark):**
1. **High noise immunity** — only need to distinguish 0 and 1
2. Allows **regeneration** of the signal at repeaters
3. Compatible with **error detection and correction coding**
4. Enables **encryption** and secure transmission

---

### **Question 2** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**Distinguish between PAM and PWM. Which has better noise immunity and why?**

#### Model Answer (3 Marks):

| Feature | PAM | PWM |
|---------|-----|-----|
| Variable Parameter | **Amplitude** | **Width** |
| Information carried in | Pulse amplitude | Pulse duration |
| Noise Susceptibility | **High** (amplitude is directly affected) | **Lower** (width is less affected by amplitude noise) |
| Bandwidth | Lower | Higher (wider pulses) |
| Power efficiency | Poor | Better (constant amplitude) |

**Better Noise Immunity — PWM (1 mark):**
> In **PWM**, information is encoded in the **time-domain width** of the pulse, not in the amplitude. Since most channel noise is **additive amplitude noise** (Gaussian, affecting signal level), PWM's amplitude-constant nature makes it **less susceptible** to noise than PAM.

---

## 📗 PART B — Long Answer Questions (Module Internal Choice) (1 × 14 = 14 Marks)

> **KTU 2024 Scheme Pattern:** Each Module contains two full 14-mark questions, and students must answer **one of them** (internal choice). Both questions below are completely independent alternatives, each split into sub-parts (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks.

---

### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam — July 2024]

**(a)** Explain the different types of **telemetry systems** with neat block diagrams. Compare **analog and digital telemetry** in terms of noise immunity, bandwidth, and accuracy. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With the help of a block diagram, explain the **generation and demodulation of Pulse Code Modulation (PCM)**. Derive the expression for the **signal-to-quantization-noise ratio (SQNR)** of a PCM system. **(7 marks)**

---

#### 📝 Model Solution — Question A

### (a) Telemetry Systems — Types & Comparison (7 marks)

**Definition of Telemetry (1 mark):**
Telemetry is the process of measuring a quantity at a remote location, transmitting the measured value via a communication channel, and reconstructing/displaying it at a central station.

**Three Types of Telemetry (4 marks):**

**1. Analog Telemetry (1.5 marks):**
- Transmits continuously varying signal.
- Techniques: **Voltage, Current, Frequency, Position telemetry**.
- E.g., FM-FDM telemetry used in older SCADA systems.
- Block: `Sensor → Amplifier → Analog Modulator → Channel → Demodulator → Recorder`

**2. Digital Telemetry (1.5 marks):**
- Transmit binary-coded representation of the measurement.
- Uses **PCM, TDM, and digital modulation (ASK/PSK/QAM)**.
- E.g., Modern SCADA, IoT sensor networks, satellite telemetry.
- Block: `Sensor → S/H → ADC → Digital Modulator → Channel → Decoder → DAC → Display`

**3. Hybrid Telemetry (1 mark):**
- Mix of analog and digital. E.g., satellite systems using FM for slow telemetry and PCM for high-rate data.

**Comparison Table (2 marks):**

| Parameter | Analog Telemetry | Digital Telemetry |
|-----------|------------------|-------------------|
| Noise Immunity | Poor | Excellent |
| Bandwidth | Lower | Higher |
| Accuracy | Lower (drift, noise) | Higher (no amplitude noise) |
| Hardware Complexity | Simple | Complex (ADC, encoder) |
| Cost | Low | Moderate |
| Long-distance Quality | Degrades | Maintained (with repeaters) |
| Error Detection/Correction | Not possible | Easily implemented |
| Storage | Difficult | Easy (digital memory) |

---

### (b) PCM Generation, Demodulation & SQNR Derivation (7 marks)

**Block Diagram — PCM Transmitter (2 marks):**
```
Message m(t) → Anti-aliasing LPF → Sampler → Quantizer → Encoder → Channel
```

**Block Diagram — PCM Receiver (1 mark):**
```
Channel → Regenerative Repeater → Decoder → Reconstruction LPF → m̂(t)
```

**PCM Generation Steps (1.5 marks):**
1. **Sampling** at $f_s \geq 2f_m$ (Nyquist–Shannon theorem).
2. **Quantization** — round each sample to nearest of $L = 2^n$ levels with step $q = 2A/2^n$.
3. **Encoding** — represent each level as an n-bit binary word.

**PCM Demodulation (1 mark):**
- **Decoder** → converts binary words back to quantized amplitudes.
- **Reconstruction LPF** → smooths staircase to recover original $m(t)$.

**Derivation of SQNR (1.5 marks):**

Quantization step size:

$$
q = \dfrac{2A}{2^n}
$$

Quantization noise power (assuming uniform error distribution):

$$
\sigma_q^2 = \dfrac{q^2}{12} = \dfrac{A^2}{3 \cdot 4^n}
$$

For a sinusoidal message with peak $A$, signal power:

$$
P_s = \dfrac{A^2}{2}
$$

Linear SQNR:

$$
\text{SNR} = \dfrac{P_s}{\sigma_q^2} = \dfrac{A^2/2}{A^2/(3 \cdot 4^n)} = \dfrac{3 \cdot 4^n}{2}
$$

In decibels:

$$
\boxed{\text{SNR}_{dB} = 1.76 + 6.02\,n \quad \text{dB}}
$$

**Conclusion:** Each additional bit **increases SQNR by 6 dB** — this is the **fundamental trade-off** between bit rate and quality in PCM.

---

### **QUESTION B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**(a)** Explain with neat waveforms the **generation of PAM, PWM, and PPM**. State the relationship between them. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With a block diagram, explain the **principle of operation of Delta Modulation (DM)**. Discuss its limitations — **slope overload and granular noise** — and explain how **Adaptive Delta Modulation (ADM)** overcomes them. **(7 marks)**

---

#### 📝 Model Solution — Question B

### (a) PAM, PWM, PPM — Generation & Relationship (7 marks)

**PAM — Pulse Amplitude Modulation (2.5 marks):**

**Generation:** A **Sample-and-Hold (S/H) circuit** is used. The message signal $m(t)$ is multiplied by a **narrow pulse train** $p(t)$ at sampling instants $nT_s$. The output is:

$$
s_{PAM}(t) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} m(nT_s) \cdot p(t - nT_s)
$$

**Waveform Description:** The pulse amplitudes follow the envelope of $m(t)$. In **flat-top PAM**, the pulse amplitude is held constant over the pulse duration.

**PWM — Pulse Width Modulation (2 marks):**

**Generation:** $m(t)$ is compared with a **sawtooth/triangular reference** in a **comparator**.
- Output = HIGH when $m(t) > $ reference
- Output = LOW when $m(t) < $ reference

**Waveform Description:** Pulse **widths** are proportional to $m(t)$ at sampling instants. The leading edge is fixed; the trailing edge moves with the signal.

**PPM — Pulse Position Modulation (1.5 marks):**

**Generation:** A PWM signal is **differentiated** to get narrow spikes (at trailing edges). The negative spikes are clipped, leaving positive PPM pulses at the **trailing edge positions** of the PWM pulses.

**Waveform Description:** Pulses have **constant amplitude and width** but **varying positions**. Position shift ∝ $m(t)$.

**Relationship (1 mark):**

> **Generation Chain:** Message → PAM (vary amplitude) → PWM (vary width) → PPM (vary position)
>
> All three are **analog pulse modulation** techniques — the message is preserved in the **pulse shape (amplitude, width, or position)**.

---

### (b) Delta Modulation — Principle, Limitations, ADM (7 marks)

**Principle of Operation (2.5 marks):**

**Block Diagram — DM Encoder:**
```
              ┌──(+)──[1-bit Quantizer]──> DM Bit Stream
m(t) ─────────┤   ↑
              │   │(−)
              │ [Predictor: Delay + Integrator]
              └───────────────(feedback)──────────┘
```

The DM compares the current sample $m(nT_s)$ with a **predicted value** $\hat{m}(nT_s)$:
- If $m(nT_s) > \hat{m}(nT_s)$ → output bit = **"1"** → step up by $+\Delta$
- If $m(nT_s) < \hat{m}(nT_s)$ → output bit = **"0"** → step down by $-\Delta$

Only **1 bit per sample** is transmitted → **lowest bit rate** among all pulse modulations.

**Limitations of DM (2.5 marks):**

**1. Slope Overload Distortion (1.25 marks):**
- Occurs when the **maximum rate of change** of $m(t)$ exceeds the maximum staircase slope $\Delta f_s$.
- The DM output cannot "keep up" with rapid signal variations → **flattened peaks**.

**Condition to avoid:**

$$
\Delta \cdot f_s \geq \left(\dfrac{dm}{dt}\right)_{max}
$$

**2. Granular Noise (1.25 marks):**
- Occurs when $\Delta$ is **too large** for a slowly changing signal.
- The staircase **oscillates** by $\pm \Delta$ around the constant signal → **idling noise**.

**Adaptive Delta Modulation — ADM (2 marks):**

In **ADM**, the step size $\Delta$ is **dynamically adjusted**:
- When the DM output is in **slope overload** (consecutive 1s or 0s) → **increase** $\Delta$.
- When in **granular region** (alternating 1, 0, 1, 0…) → **decrease** $\Delta$.

**ADM Variants:**
- **Continuously Variable Slope Delta Modulation (CVSDM)**
- **Song Delta Modulation**

**Advantages of ADM:**
- Handles both slope overload and granular noise.
- Better SNR for same bit rate.
- Used in **military secure voice, satellite telephony, low-bit-rate speech coding**.

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **TOP 5 KTU MARK-LOSS PITFALLS — Read Before Writing Your Exam!**
>
> **1. Forgetting the "1.76" Constant:**
> Many students write $\text{SNR}_{dB} = 6.02n$ — losing **1 mark** for missing the 1.76 dB term. **The 1.76 comes from the $10\log_{10}(1.5)$ factor of a sinusoidal input.** For uniform/non-sinusoidal signals, the constant is $0$ dB → $\text{SNR}_{dB} = 6.02n$.
>
> **2. Confusing PAM, PWM, PPM:**
> Always clearly state which parameter varies (amplitude/width/position). A vague answer like *"PAM changes the pulse"* gets zero marks.
>
> **3. Missing the Anti-Aliasing Filter:**
> The **first block of any PCM system is the Anti-Aliasing LPF**. Students who draw the sampler directly at the input lose **2 marks** — KTU examiners deduct heavily for this.
>
> **4. Wrong Sampling Theorem Application:**
> Always state **$f_s \geq 2f_m$** and explain that **$f_s = 2f_m$ is the Nyquist rate** (not minimum). The minimum is the *Nyquist rate* itself, but the rule is $f_s \geq 2f_m$.
>
> **5. Skipping the Slope Overload Formula in DM:**
> For DM questions, examiners look for $\Delta f_s \geq (dm/dt)_{max}$. If you forget this, you lose at least **2 marks** in Part (b) of the DM question.
>
> **6. Bandwidth Calculation Mistake:**
> $B_{min} = R_b/2$ assumes **Nyquist signaling** (sinc pulses). If the question says "raised-cosine filtering with roll-off $\alpha$", then $B = R_b(1+\alpha)/2$ — many students miss this.

---

## 🎯 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST

> 📌 **Bookmark this section for the night before your KTU exam!**

### 🔑 Core Definitions (memorize word-for-word)
- [ ] **Telemetry** — remote measurement and transmission of data.
- [ ] **PAM** — amplitude of pulse varies with $m(t)$.
- [ ] **PWM** — width of pulse varies with $m(t)$.
- [ ] **PPM** — position of pulse varies with $m(t)$.
- [ ] **PCM** — sampled, quantized, encoded into binary words.
- [ ] **DPCM** — transmits the *difference* between actual and predicted sample.
- [ ] **DM** — 1-bit DPCM; transmits only the *sign* of the difference.
- [ ] **ADM** — DM with dynamically varying step size $\Delta$.

### 🔢 Key Formulas (write on a sticky note!)
- [ ] **Nyquist:** $f_s \geq 2f_m$
- [ ] **Quantization step:** $q = 2A/2^n$
- [ ] **Quantization noise:** $\sigma_q^2 = q^2/12 = A^2/(3 \cdot 4^n)$
- [ ] **PCM SQNR:** $\text{SNR}_{dB} = 1.76 + 6.02n$
- [ ] **Bit rate:** $R_b = N \cdot f_s \cdot n$
- [ ] **Min bandwidth:** $B_{min} = R_b/2$
- [ ] **DM step size:** $\Delta \geq (dm/dt)_{max}/f_s$

### 📊 Comparison Must-Knows
- [ ] **PAM vs PWM vs PPM vs PCM** — know the variable parameter, noise immunity, and synchronization requirement.
- [ ] **PCM = 3 steps** — Sampling → Quantization → Encoding.
- [ ] **PCM SNR doubles for every 1 extra bit** = **+6 dB**.

### 🛠️ Real-World Engineering Links
- [ ] **PWM** → motor speed control, switching power supplies.
- [ ] **PPM** → IR remote controls, optical fiber.
- [ ] **PCM** → digital telephony, CD audio, MP3.
- [ ] **DM/ADM** → secure voice, satellite telemetry.

### 🧠 Examiner's "Make-or-Break" Concepts
- [ ] **Block diagram** of PCM encoder/decoder with **Anti-Aliasing LPF first**.
- [ ] **Three types of telemetry** — Analog, Digital, Hybrid.
- [ ] **Generation chain** PAM → PWM → PPM.
- [ ] **DM limitations** — Slope Overload + Granular Noise.
- [ ] **Why 1.76 dB** appears in PCM SNR (sine wave assumption).

> **🎓 Final Tip:** In every KTU answer, **start with a definition, draw a labeled block diagram, then derive/calculate.** This 3-part structure guarantees full marks even if the calculation has a small slip.

---
