## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

**Pulse Amplitude Modulation (PAM)** is a pulse modulation technique in which the **amplitude of a periodic train of pulses** (the carrier) is varied in proportion to the **instantaneous amplitude** of the continuous-time message (baseband) signal, while the **pulse width and pulse position are held constant**.

Mathematically, the modulated signal is expressed as:

$$s(t) = m(t) \cdot p(t)$$

where $m(t)$ is the message signal and $p(t)$ is the rectangular pulse train.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 2):** PAM is treated as the **foundational pulse modulation scheme** from which all other pulse modulation techniques — PWM (Pulse Width Modulation), PPM (Pulse Position Modulation) and PCM (Pulse Code Modulation) — are conceptually derived. Every KTU question on pulse modulation begins from the PAM base.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition

Imagine a **postage-stamp printing press**: the rotating drum (carrier) leaves a series of regular ink impressions on the paper. If you now raise or lower the **ink level of each impression** in step with the height of a sound wave, each stamp captures a *snapshot* of the sound wave at that instant. That, in essence, is PAM — a sequence of pulses whose **heights are stamped by the message waveform**.

- The **drum** → pulse carrier (regular spacing).
- The **ink level** → pulse amplitude.
- The **original sound wave** → message signal $m(t)$.

### 1.3 Standard Reference Metrics

| Metric | Symbol | Standard Value / Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Sampling Frequency | $f_s$ | $\geq 2 f_m$ (Hz) |
| Sampling Period | $T_s$ | $1 / f_s$ (seconds) |
| Pulse Width | $\tau$ | $\tau \ll T_s$ (seconds) |
| Duty Cycle | $D$ | $\tau / T_s$ (dimensionless) |
| Maximum Message Frequency | $f_m$ | Hz |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "$\tau \ll T_s$"?** Narrow pulses mean that the signal occupies only a small fraction of each sampling period. This preserves the **spectral gap** between replicas of $M(f)$, allowing a low-pass filter at the receiver to reconstruct $m(t)$ cleanly. This concept is heavily tested in KTU derivations.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Two Principal Variants of PAM

#### (a) Natural-Sampled PAM (Natural PAM)
The **pulse tops follow the shape of $m(t)$** during the pulse duration. Achieved by passing $m(t)$ through a **gates / chopper / sampling switch** that is closed only during the pulse width $\tau$.

#### (b) Flat-Top PAM (Sample-and-Hold PAM)
The **pulse tops are flat**, with amplitude equal to the value of $m(t)$ at the **sampling instant**. Implemented using a **sample-and-hold (S/H) circuit** or a **zero-order hold**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why two variants?** Natural PAM preserves $m(t)$'s shape, but is **hard to regenerate** because noise distorts the pulse tops. Flat-top PAM is **easy to generate, regenerate, and decode** (especially for TDM), but introduces an aperture-effect distortion $\text{sinc}(f\tau)$ that must be compensated.

### 2.2 Operational Logic — Generation (Bullet Steps)

1. The message signal $m(t)$ is fed to one input of an **analog multiplier / chopper**.
2. A **pulse train generator** produces $p(t)$, a periodic sequence of rectangular pulses of width $\tau$ and period $T_s$.
3. The **multiplier output** is $s(t) = m(t) \cdot p(t)$.
4. For **flat-top PAM**, the multiplier is followed by a **sample-and-hold amplifier** that locks the amplitude to $m(nT_s)$ until the next sample.
5. The output spectrum $S(f)$ contains replicas of $M(f)$ centred at $k f_s$.

### 2.3 Operational Logic — Demoduction (Reception)

1. The received PAM waveform $s(t)$ is applied to a **low-pass filter (LPF)**.
2. The LPF has cutoff frequency $f_c$ such that $f_m < f_c < (f_s - f_m)$.
3. The LPF passes the **baseband replica** centred at $k = 0$ and rejects all higher spectral replicas.
4. For flat-top PAM, an **equalization filter** of gain $1 / \text{sinc}(f\tau)$ compensates for the aperture effect.

### 2.4 The "Why" Behind Each Step

- **Multiplication in time** ↔ **Convolution in frequency** — this is the spectral duplication principle.
- **LPF cutoff placement** — avoids **aliasing** while preserving the original message spectrum.
- **Equalization** — restores the high-frequency content attenuated by the flat-top shape.

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield Cheat Sheet)

| Quantity | Expression | Notes / Units |
|---|---|---|
| Pulse Train (Fourier Series) | $p(t) = \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} C_k \, e^{j 2 \pi k f_s t}$ | $f_s = 1/T_s$ |
| Fourier Coefficient | $C_k = (A \tau / T_s) \, \text{sinc}(k f_s \tau)$ | $A$ = pulse amplitude |
| Natural PAM Time-Domain | $s(t) = m(t) \cdot p(t)$ | Continuous time |
| Flat-Top PAM Time-Domain | $s(t) = \sum_{n} m(nT_s) \, h(t - nT_s)$ | $h(t)$ = rect pulse |
| Natural PAM Spectrum | $S(f) = (A\tau/T_s) \sum_{k} M(f - k f_s)$ | Ideal scaling |
| Flat-Top PAM Spectrum | $S(f) = (A\tau/T_s) \, \text{sinc}(f\tau) \, e^{-j\pi f \tau} \sum_{k} M(f - k f_s)$ | Aperture effect present |
| Sinc Function | $\text{sinc}(x) = \sin(\pi x) / (\pi x)$ | Note: KTU uses normalised sinc |
| Nyquist Rate | $f_s \geq 2 f_m$ | Hz |
| Duty Cycle | $D = \tau / T_s$ | Dimensionless, $0 < D < 1$ |
| Reconstruction Filter Cutoff | $f_m < f_c < f_s - f_m$ | Hz |
| Aperture Effect Equalizer | $H_{eq}(f) = 1 / [\text{sinc}(f\tau) \, e^{-j\pi f\tau}]$ | Compensates flat-top droop |
| Transmission Bandwidth | $BW \approx f_s$ (theoretical), $\approx 1/\tau$ (practical) | Hz |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical KTU Pitfall:** Many textbooks define $\text{sinc}(x) = \sin(x)/x$ (unnormalised). KTU 2024 papers follow the **normalized** $\text{sinc}(x) = \sin(\pi x)/(\pi x)$. Always check which convention your derivation uses. This is a frequent 1-mark loss in valuation.

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Building block** for Time Division Multiplexing (TDM) — multiple PAM signals time-share one channel.
- **Front-end** for PCM — natural/flat-top PAM is the **first stage** before quantization and encoding.
- **Industrial telemetry** — slowly varying physical quantities (temperature, pressure) are transmitted as PAM over long wires.
- **Biological signal acquisition** — ECG, EEG instruments use sample-and-hold PAM for digital recording.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Fourier Series of the Pulse Train $p(t)$

**Statement:** Express a periodic rectangular pulse train of amplitude $A$, width $\tau$ and period $T_s$ as a Fourier series.

**Step 1 — Define one pulse.** A single rectangular pulse of width $\tau$ centred at the origin:

$$h(t) = \begin{cases} A, & \vert t \vert \leq \tau/2 \\ 0, & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

**Step 2 — Periodise.** The pulse train is:

$$p(t) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} h(t - nT_s)$$

**Step 3 — Compute Fourier coefficient.**

$$C_k = \frac{1}{T_s} \int_{-T_s/2}^{T_s/2} p(t) \, e^{-j 2\pi k f_s t} \, dt$$

**Step 4 — Because only the pulse at $n = 0$ lies within one period:**

$$C_k = \frac{1}{T_s} \int_{-\tau/2}^{\tau/2} A \, e^{-j 2\pi k f_s t} \, dt$$

**Step 5 — Perform integration.**

$$C_k = \frac{A}{T_s} \left[ \frac{e^{-j 2\pi k f_s t}}{-j 2\pi k f_s} \right]_{-\tau/2}^{\tau/2}$$

$$C_k = \frac{A}{T_s} \cdot \frac{e^{-j \pi k f_s \tau} - e^{j \pi k f_s \tau}}{-j 2\pi k f_s}$$

**Step 6 — Use Euler's identity** $\sin(x) = (e^{jx} - e^{-jx})/(2j)$:

$$C_k = \frac{A}{T_s} \cdot \frac{2 \sin(\pi k f_s \tau)}{2\pi k f_s}$$

$$\boxed{C_k = \frac{A\tau}{T_s} \, \text{sinc}(k f_s \tau)}$$

where $\text{sinc}(x) = \sin(\pi x)/(\pi x)$.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Spectrum of Natural-Sampled PAM

**Statement:** Obtain the spectrum $S(f)$ of $s(t) = m(t) \cdot p(t)$.

**Step 1 — Fourier series expansion of $p(t)$:**

$$p(t) = \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} C_k \, e^{j 2\pi k f_s t}$$

**Step 2 — Multiply by $m(t)$:**

$$s(t) = m(t) \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} C_k \, e^{j 2\pi k f_s t}$$

**Step 3 — Apply frequency-shift property** $\mathcal{F}\{m(t) e^{j 2\pi k f_s t}\} = M(f - k f_s)$:

$$S(f) = \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} C_k \, M(f - k f_s)$$

**Step 4 — Substitute $C_k$ from Derivation 1:**

$$\boxed{S(f) = \frac{A\tau}{T_s} \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} \text{sinc}(k f_s \tau) \, M(f - k f_s)}$$

**Step 5 — Physical interpretation:**

- The baseband ($k = 0$) replica $M(f)$ is scaled by $A\tau/T_s$ (the average DC value of $p(t)$).
- Higher-order replicas ($k \neq 0$) are **attenuated** by $\text{sinc}(k f_s \tau)$.
- For $\tau \ll T_s$, $\text{sinc}(k f_s \tau) \to 1$ and **all replicas carry equal weight**.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Spectrum of Flat-Top PAM

**Statement:** Derive the spectrum of flat-top PAM generated via zero-order hold.

**Step 1 — Time-domain expression.** Each sample $m(nT_s)$ is held for $\tau$ seconds:

$$s(t) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} m(nT_s) \, h(t - nT_s)$$

where $h(t) = \text{rect}(t/\tau)$ is the rectangular hold pulse.

**Step 2 — Equivalent representation.** Let $m_s(t) = \sum_n m(nT_s) \, \delta(t - nT_s)$ be the **impulse-sampled** message. Then:

$$s(t) = m_s(t) * h(t)$$

**Step 3 — Spectrum.** Convolution in time $\Leftrightarrow$ multiplication in frequency:

$$S(f) = M_s(f) \cdot H(f)$$

**Step 4 — Spectrum of impulse-sampled signal** (sampling theorem):

$$M_s(f) = f_s \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} M(f - k f_s)$$

**Step 5 — Spectrum of rectangular hold pulse:**

$$H(f) = \tau \, \text{sinc}(f\tau) \, e^{-j\pi f \tau}$$

**Step 6 — Combine (Steps 3, 4, 5):**

$$S(f) = f_s \, \tau \, \text{sinc}(f\tau) \, e^{-j\pi f \tau} \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} M(f - k f_s)$$

$$\boxed{S(f) = \frac{\tau}{T_s} \, \text{sinc}(f\tau) \, e^{-j\pi f \tau} \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} M(f - k f_s)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Difference Between Natural and Flat-Top:**
> - Natural PAM: $\text{sinc}$ attenuation depends on **spectral index** $k$ only (carrier-related).
> - Flat-Top PAM: $\text{sinc}$ attenuation depends on **frequency** $f$ continuously (aperture effect).
> - The exponential $e^{-j\pi f \tau}$ introduces a **linear phase delay** of $\tau/2$.

---

### 3.4 Reconstruction (Demodulation) Logic

After transmission through an ideal channel:

**Step 1 — Apply LPF** with cutoff $f_c$ where $f_m < f_c < f_s - f_m$.

**Step 2 — For natural PAM, output is:**

$$m_{rec}(t) = \frac{A\tau}{T_s} \, m(t)$$

A simple gain stage recovers $m(t)$.

**Step 3 — For flat-top PAM, output is distorted:**

$$M_{rec}(f) = \frac{\tau}{T_s} \, \text{sinc}(f\tau) \, e^{-j\pi f \tau} \, M(f)$$

**Step 4 — Cascade an equalizer** $H_{eq}(f) = \dfrac{1}{\text{sinc}(f\tau)} \, e^{j\pi f \tau}$ to compensate:

$$M_{eq}(f) = \frac{\tau}{T_s} \, M(f)$$

Hence $m_{eq}(t) = (\tau/T_s) \, m(t)$ — exact recovery.

---

### 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

**Problem:** A 4 kHz sinusoid $m(t) = 5 \cos(2\pi \cdot 4000 \, t)$ is sampled by natural PAM using pulses of amplitude $A = 10$ V, width $\tau = 25 \, \mu$s, and sampling frequency $f_s = 20$ kHz. Find the spectrum amplitude of the baseband replica.

**Solution:**

$$\text{Scale factor} = \frac{A\tau}{T_s} = \frac{10 \cdot 25 \times 10^{-6}}{50 \times 10^{-6}} = 5$$

Since $M(f)$ for a cosine has two impulses of strength $2.5$ each at $\pm 4$ kHz:

$$S(f) = 5 \cdot \sum_k \text{sinc}(k f_s \tau) \, M(f - k f_s)$$

Baseband replica ($k = 0$) has total amplitude scaling $\times 5$.

For $k = 1$: $f_s \tau = 20{,}000 \times 25 \times 10^{-6} = 0.5 \Rightarrow \text{sinc}(0.5) = 2/\pi \approx 0.637$. So the first replica is attenuated to $63.7\%$ of its true amplitude.

> [!NOTE]
> This attenuation of side-band replicas is precisely why PAM (without further coding) is **bandwidth-inefficient**. KTU PCM questions reference this fact to justify the move to PCM.

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### Q1. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Define Pulse Amplitude Modulation. Distinguish between Natural PAM and Flat-Top PAM.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

PAM is a pulse modulation scheme in which the **amplitude of each pulse in a periodic train** is varied in proportion to the instantaneous value of the message signal $m(t)$, while **pulse width and pulse position remain constant**.

| Feature | Natural PAM | Flat-Top PAM |
|---|---|---|
| Pulse Top Shape | Follows $m(t)$ during $\tau$ | Flat (held at $m(nT_s)$) |
| Generation | Chopper / analog gate | Sample-and-hold (S/H) |
| Spectral Sinc Attenuation | On $k$ only | On $f$ continuously |
| Ease of Regeneration | Difficult | Easy (used in TDM/PCM) |
| Demodulation | Simple LPF | LPF + equalizer |

**(1 mark)** definition, **(1 mark)** each distinction row = full 3 marks.

---

#### Q2. `[KTU University Exam – Jun 2024]`
**State the Nyquist criterion for sampling. Why is $f_s > 2 f_m$ preferred over $f_s = 2 f_m$ in practical PAM systems?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Nyquist Criterion:** A bandlimited message signal of maximum frequency $f_m$ can be uniquely reconstructed from its samples **iff** $f_s \geq 2 f_m$.

Although $f_s = 2 f_m$ is theoretically sufficient, in practice $f_s > 2 f_m$ is preferred because:

1. **(1 mark)** A practical low-pass filter cannot have an infinitely sharp cutoff; it requires a **transition band** between $f_m$ and $(f_s - f_m)$.
2. **(1 mark)** Guard band prevents **aliasing** caused by slight drifts in $f_s$ or $f_m$.
3. **(1 mark)** Allows margin for clock jitter and channel-induced phase distortion.

---

### Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

#### QUESTION A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, Module 2]`

**(a)** Derive the expression for the **spectrum of natural-sampled PAM signal**. Clearly show the spectral replicas and explain how the original message signal can be recovered. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A message signal $m(t)$ has bandwidth $f_m = 5$ kHz. It is sampled by natural PAM using pulses of amplitude $A = 8$ V, pulse width $\tau = 20 \, \mu$s and sampling frequency $f_s = 20$ kHz.
  - **(i)** Verify whether the Nyquist criterion is satisfied.
  - **(ii)** Calculate the duty cycle.
  - **(iii)** Determine the amplitude scaling factor of the baseband replica.
  - **(iv)** Find the attenuation factor of the first spectral replica. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Derivation (7 marks)**

Step 1: Express $p(t)$ as Fourier series **(1 mark)**

$$p(t) = \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} C_k \, e^{j 2\pi k f_s t}, \quad C_k = \frac{A\tau}{T_s} \, \text{sinc}(k f_s \tau)$$

Step 2: Multiply by $m(t)$ **(1 mark)**

$$s(t) = m(t) \cdot p(t) = \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} C_k \, m(t) \, e^{j 2\pi k f_s t}$$

Step 3: Take Fourier transform using frequency-shift property **(2 marks)**

$$S(f) = \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} C_k \, M(f - k f_s)$$

Step 4: Substitute $C_k$ **(1 mark)**

$$S(f) = \frac{A\tau}{T_s} \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} \text{sinc}(k f_s \tau) \, M(f - k f_s)$$

Step 5: Recovery **(2 marks)** — Pass $s(t)$ through an LPF of cutoff $f_m < f_c < f_s - f_m$. The baseband replica passes; all $k \neq 0$ replicas are rejected. Output is $(A\tau/T_s) \, m(t)$, which is the original message scaled.

**(b) Numerical (7 marks)**

**(i)** Nyquist check **(1 mark)**:

$$f_s = 20 \text{ kHz} \geq 2 f_m = 10 \text{ kHz} \quad \checkmark$$

**(ii)** Duty cycle **(2 marks)**:

$$T_s = 1/f_s = 50 \, \mu\text{s}, \quad D = \tau / T_s = 20/50 = 0.4 = 40\%$$

**(iii)** Baseband scaling **(2 marks)**:

$$\text{Scale} = \frac{A\tau}{T_s} = \frac{8 \times 20 \times 10^{-6}}{50 \times 10^{-6}} = 3.2$$

**(iv)** Attenuation of first replica ($k = 1$) **(2 marks)**:

$$k f_s \tau = 1 \times 20{,}000 \times 20 \times 10^{-6} = 0.4$$

$$\text{sinc}(0.4) = \sin(0.4\pi) / (0.4\pi) = \sin(72^\circ)/1.2566 \approx 0.9355 / 1.2566 \approx 0.7572$$

Attenuation ≈ **75.72 %**.

---

#### QUESTION B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice) `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, Module 2]`

**(a)** Explain the **generation and detection of Flat-Top PAM** with neat block diagrams. Derive its spectrum. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare **Natural and Flat-Top PAM** in terms of (i) generation complexity, (ii) immunity to noise, (iii) ease of regeneration for TDM, (iv) spectral efficiency. State **two applications** of PAM. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Generation, Detection, Spectrum of Flat-Top PAM (7 marks)**

**Generation (2 marks):** A sample-and-hold (S/H) circuit, clocked at $f_s$, samples $m(t)$ at $t = nT_s$ and holds the value for $\tau$ seconds via a capacitor. Output:

$$s(t) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} m(nT_s) \, \text{rect}\!\left(\frac{t - nT_s - \tau/2}{\tau}\right)$$

**Detection (2 marks):** LPF with cutoff $f_c$ where $f_m < f_c < f_s - f_m$, followed by an equalizer $H_{eq}(f) = 1/[\text{sinc}(f\tau) e^{-j\pi f\tau}]$ to compensate aperture droop.

**Spectrum (3 marks):**

$$S(f) = \frac{\tau}{T_s} \, \text{sinc}(f\tau) \, e^{-j\pi f\tau} \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} M(f - k f_s)$$

- The exponential $e^{-j\pi f\tau}$ represents **constant time delay** of $\tau/2$.
- The factor $\text{sinc}(f\tau)$ represents the **aperture effect** (high-frequency roll-off).

**(b) Comparison (7 marks — distribute 1.5 marks each + 1 mark for applications)**

| Parameter | Natural PAM | Flat-Top PAM |
|---|---|---|
| Generation Complexity | Simple chopper | Requires S/H circuit |
| Noise Immunity | Low (pulse top carries noise) | Better (flat top can be regenerated) |
| Regeneration for TDM | Difficult | Easy (held value is stable) |
| Spectral Efficiency | Replicas attenuated by sinc($k f_s \tau$) | Continuous sinc($f\tau$) droop |
| Equalization Needed | No | Yes (aperture equalizer) |

**Applications (1 mark):** (i) Front-end for PCM, (ii) Time-Division Multiplexing (TDM), (iii) Industrial telemetry, (iv) Biological signal sampling.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mark-Loss Traps:**
> 1. **Confusing sinc conventions** — $\sin(x)/x$ vs $\sin(\pi x)/(\pi x)$ can flip signs and numerics; KTU uses normalised sinc.
> 2. **Forgetting the exponential phase term** $e^{-j\pi f\tau}$ in flat-top derivation loses 1 mark.
> 3. **Wrong duty-cycle units** — always express as a fraction (0–1) AND percentage.
> 4. **Skipping the Nyquist check** in numericals — losing 1 easy mark.
> 5. **Equalizer missing** for flat-top PAM reconstruction — a frequent 1-mark deduction.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **PAM** varies **pulse amplitude** according to message; pulse width and position are **fixed**.
- **Two variants:** Natural (pulse top = message) and Flat-Top (pulse top held constant).
- **Nyquist rate:** $f_s \geq 2 f_m$ — strict theoretical minimum.
- **Duty cycle:** $D = \tau / T_s$; narrow pulses ($D \ll 1$) preserve spectral separation.
- **Natural PAM spectrum:** $S(f) = (A\tau/T_s) \sum_k \text{sinc}(k f_s \tau) \, M(f - k f_s)$.
- **Flat-Top PAM spectrum:** includes $\text{sinc}(f\tau) \, e^{-j\pi f\tau}$ — the **aperture effect**.
- **Demodulation:** LPF with cutoff $f_m < f_c < f_s - f_m$.
- **Equalizer** needed for flat-top PAM to undo $\text{sinc}(f\tau)$ droop.
- **PAM is the foundation** of PWM, PPM and PCM — KTU tests this hierarchy.
- **Applications:** PCM front-end, TDM, telemetry, biomedical signal acquisition.
- **KTU High-Yield Trick:** Side-replicas are attenuated by $\text{sinc}(k f_s \tau)$; for $k f_s \tau < 0.5$, attenuation is mild — choose $\tau$ carefully to avoid spectral overlap (aliasing).
- **Sinc convention:** Normalised $\text{sinc}(x) = \sin(\pi x)/(\pi x)$ — used throughout KTU 2024 papers.
- **Phase term** $e^{-j\pi f\tau}$ in flat-top PAM = **constant group delay** of $\tau/2$ seconds.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Pulse Code Modulation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET411 - ELECTRONIC INSTRUMENTATION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Pulse Code Modulation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Pulse Code Modulation (PCM)** is a digital modulation technique used to represent an analog signal in digital form. It is the standard method for converting an analog baseband signal into a binary (digital) codeword by executing three sequential operations:

1. **Sampling** — discretizing the continuous-time signal in the time domain.
2. **Quantization** — discretizing the continuous-amplitude samples into a finite set of amplitude levels.
3. **Encoding** — mapping each quantized level to a unique binary codeword.

The resulting binary stream is a Pulse Coded representation of the original analog waveform, suitable for transmission, storage, encryption, and error-correction processing.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim):** *PCM is a method of converting an analog signal into a digital signal by sampling, quantizing, and encoding. It is the most fundamental waveform-coding technique and forms the building block of all modern digital communication systems.*

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are describing a **sine wave** to a friend who is **blind** and standing far away. Instead of trying to draw the smooth curve, you do the following:

- **Sampling** → You take snapshots of the wave at regular time intervals (e.g., every millisecond). Each snapshot tells your friend *where* the wave was at that instant.
- **Quantization** → Instead of reporting the exact height (which could be 0.3728 V), you round it to the nearest of 256 standardized "rulers" (e.g., 0.37 V or 0.38 V). This makes communication finite and manageable.
- **Encoding** → You translate each rounded height into a binary word (e.g., 00010110). Now your friend can recreate a digital replica of your wave from those binary digits.

In short: **PCM = Convert smooth analog signal → digital snapshots → binary codes.**

### 1.3 Why PCM Matters in Engineering

- It is the **basis of the entire PSTN (Public Switched Telephone Network) digital trunk system (T1/E1)**.
- It underlies **CD-quality audio (44.1 kHz × 16 bits)**, **digital telephony (8 kHz × 8 bits = 64 kbps)**, and **digital instrumentation buses**.
- PCM provides **regenerative capability** — a noisy signal can be perfectly recovered by re-amplifying the binary pulse stream, since noise is largely irrelevant as long as the bits can be detected.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Audio Benchmark Values (BOLD for recall):**
> - **Voice-grade PCM: 8 kHz sampling, 8 bits/sample = 64 kbps** (ITU-T G.711 standard)
> - **CD Audio PCM: 44.1 kHz sampling, 16 bits/sample, stereo = 1411.2 kbps**
> - **Studio Audio: 48 kHz, 24 bits, 2 ch = 2304 kbps**
> - **Telephony channel: 4 kHz audio bandwidth**

### 1.4 Key Terminology at a Glance

| Term | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **$f_s$** | Sampling frequency |
| **$f_m$** | Maximum message (signal) frequency |
| **$n$** | Number of bits per sample (word length) |
| **$L = 2^n$** | Number of quantization levels |
| **$\Delta$** | Quantization step size |
| **$R_b$** | Bit rate (bits/second) |
| **SQNR** | Signal-to-Quantization-Noise Ratio |
| **Companding** | Non-uniform quantization via logarithmic compression + linear expansion |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Step-by-Step Operational Logic of PCM

The PCM system operates in a strict sequence. The transmitter chain is: **Anti-aliasing Filter → Sampler → Quantizer → Encoder → Line Coder → Channel**. The receiver chain is the exact inverse.

#### Step 1 — Anti-Aliasing Filter (Low-Pass)
- The input analog signal is band-limited to $f_m$ Hz by an LPF before sampling.
- **Why:** To prevent **aliasing**, which occurs when frequency components above $f_s/2$ fold back into the baseband and become indistinguishable from legitimate signal content.

#### Step 2 — Sampling
- The continuous-time signal $x(t)$ is sampled at a uniform rate $f_s$ to produce discrete-time samples $x(nT_s)$.
- **Sampling Theorem (Shannon-Nyquist):** $f_s \geq 2 f_m$.
- The minimum allowed rate $f_s = 2 f_m$ is called the **Nyquist Rate**; sampling at this rate is **Nyquist Sampling**. Sampling above it is **Oversampling**.

#### Step 3 — Quantization
- Each sample value is rounded to the nearest of $L$ discrete amplitude levels.
- **Uniform Quantization:** All step sizes are equal: $\Delta = (\text{Full-scale range})/L$.
- **Non-Uniform Quantization (Companding):** Step sizes are small for weak signals and large for strong signals, improving SQNR for speech.

#### Step 4 — Encoding
- Each quantized level is assigned a unique $n$-bit binary codeword.
- A sample with $n = 8$ bits yields 256 distinct levels.

#### Step 5 — Line Coding
- The binary stream is converted into a physical waveform (NRZ-L, RZ, Manchester, AMI, etc.) suitable for the channel.

#### Step 6 — Receiver (Decoder)
- The line decoder reconstructs bits → parallel encoder produces quantized levels → DAC + LPF reconstructs the analog waveform.

### 2.2 Why Sampling Works — Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Concept:** When you sample a band-limited signal fast enough, the original shape is fully determined by the sample values. This is because the highest frequency component can be reconstructed using its sampled values (a property of band-limited signals derived from Fourier analysis).

### 2.3 Quantization Error and SQNR Derivation Intuition

The act of rounding introduces a **quantization error** $e_q = x - x_q$. This error behaves like **white noise** uniformly distributed in $\left[-\Delta/2, \Delta/2\right]$.

- For a **sinusoidal input** of peak amplitude $A_m$, the signal power is $A_m^2/2$.
- The mean-square quantization noise is $\overline{e_q^2} = \Delta^2/12$.
- For full-scale $A_m = L\Delta/2$ (bipolar), the SQNR simplifies to $1.5 L^2$.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Parameter | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Nyquist sampling rate | $f_s \geq 2 f_m$ | Hz |
| 2 | Sampling interval | $T_s = 1/f_s$ | seconds |
| 3 | Number of quantization levels | $L = 2^n$ | dimensionless |
| 4 | Quantization step size (unipolar) | $\Delta = V_{FS}/L$ | Volts |
| 5 | Quantization step size (bipolar) | $\Delta = 2V_{FS}/L$ | Volts |
| 6 | Bit rate | $R_b = n \cdot f_s$ | bits/second |
| 7 | Minimum transmission bandwidth | $BW_{\min} = R_b/2$ | Hz (Nyquist) |
| 8 | PCM bit rate (n bit, fs) | $R_b = n f_s$ | bps |
| 9 | SQNR (sinusoidal, full-scale) | $\text{SQNR} = 1.5 L^2$ | ratio |
| 10 | SQNR in dB (n-bit) | $\text{SQNR}_{dB} = 6.02n + 1.76$ | dB |
| 11 | Max SQNR (n bits, full-scale sine) | $1.76 + 6.02n$ | dB |
| 12 | Mean square quantization noise | $\overline{e_q^2} = \Delta^2/12$ | V² |
| 13 | A-law output (small x) | $F(x) = \dfrac{A \vert x \vert}{1 + \ln A}$ | $0 \leq \vert x \vert \leq 1/A$ |
| 14 | A-law output (large x) | $F(x) = \dfrac{1 + \ln(A \vert x \vert)}{1 + \ln A}$ | $1/A \leq \vert x \vert \leq 1$ |
| 15 | μ-law output | $F(x) = \dfrac{\ln(1 + \mu \vert x \vert)}{\ln(1 + \mu)} \text{sgn}(x)$ | Full range |
| 16 | Companding improvement (approx) | $\sim 24$ to $\sim 30$ dB for speech | Equivalent bits: $\sim +4$ bits |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memory Aid (KTU Exam Favorite):** *Each additional bit improves SQNR by approximately **6 dB**.* This comes from the slope of $6.02n + 1.76$.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Telephony (PSTN):** 8 kHz × 8 bits = 64 kbps per voice channel (G.711).
- **Long-haul Trunks (TDM):** 24 voice channels → T1 (1.544 Mbps), 30 channels → E1 (2.048 Mbps).
- **Audio Engineering:** CD-DA, DVD-Audio, Blu-ray.
- **Instrumentation:** Digitizing transducer outputs (strain gauge, thermocouple, vibration) before feeding digital signal processors or microcontrollers.
- **Space Communication:** PCM telemetry in satellites (CCSDS standards).

> [!NOTE]
> **Course Outcome Mapping (KTU 2024):** This topic satisfies **CO2** — *Apply the principles of digital modulation techniques in electronic instrumentation systems.*
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Sampling Theorem Statement

**Statement:** A band-limited signal $x(t)$ containing no frequency components above $f_m$ Hz is completely described by its instantaneous samples taken at uniform intervals $T_s \leq 1/(2f_m)$ seconds.

**Proof Sketch (Whittaker-Shannon):**

Given a band-limited signal $x(t)$ with $X(f) = 0$ for $\vert f \vert > f_m$, its spectrum can be expressed as a **Fourier Series** in the variable $f$ over the interval $[-f_m, f_m]$.

Since the spectrum is band-limited, periodic extension of the spectrum by $f_s \geq 2 f_m$ causes **no overlap** (aliasing).

The time-domain samples $x(nT_s)$ capture exactly the Fourier series coefficients of $X(f)$, which uniquely determine $x(t)$. The original signal is recovered by ideal low-pass filtering:

$$x(t) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} x(nT_s) \cdot \text{sinc}\left(2 f_m (t - nT_s)\right)$$

This is the **Shannon-Whittaker Interpolation Formula**, proving that the sample set is a complete representation.

---

### 3.2 Derivation of Bit Rate of PCM System

**Given:**
- Maximum message frequency = $f_m$
- Number of bits per sample = $n$
- Nyquist sampling: $f_s = 2 f_m$

**Derivation:**

We sample at the Nyquist rate, so:

$$f_s = 2 f_m$$

Each sample is encoded with $n$ bits. The bit rate is the number of bits transmitted per second:

$$R_b = f_s \cdot n$$

Substituting $f_s = 2 f_m$:

$$\boxed{R_b = 2 \, n \, f_m}$$

**Units:** $n$ is dimensionless, $f_m$ is in Hz, so $R_b$ is in **bits per second (bps)**.

**Example:** For voice with $f_m = 4$ kHz and $n = 8$:
$$R_b = 2 \cdot 8 \cdot 4000 = 64{,}000 \text{ bps} = 64 \text{ kbps}$$

This matches the standard **G.711 PCM voice channel** rate.

---

### 3.3 Exhaustive Derivation of SQNR for Sinusoidal Input

This is the **highest-weight derivation in KTU exams**. Each step is shown explicitly.

**Given:**
- A sinusoidal input of peak amplitude $A_m$ (full-scale), i.e., $A_m = (L \Delta)/2$ (bipolar symmetric quantizer).
- Number of quantization levels = $L = 2^n$.
- Uniform quantizer with step size $\Delta$.

**Step 1 — Signal Power:**

For a sinusoid $x(t) = A_m \sin(\omega t)$, the average power is:

$$P_s = \frac{A_m^2}{2}$$

**Step 2 — Mean Square Quantization Noise:**

The quantization error $e_q$ is uniformly distributed in $\left[-\Delta/2, \Delta/2\right]$. The probability density function is $1/\Delta$. The mean-square value is:

$$\overline{e_q^2} = \int_{-\Delta/2}^{\Delta/2} e^2 \cdot \frac{1}{\Delta} \, de = \frac{1}{\Delta} \cdot \left[ \frac{e^3}{3} \right]_{-\Delta/2}^{\Delta/2}$$

$$= \frac{1}{\Delta} \cdot \frac{1}{3} \left[ \left(\frac{\Delta}{2}\right)^3 - \left(-\frac{\Delta}{2}\right)^3 \right]$$

$$= \frac{1}{\Delta} \cdot \frac{1}{3} \cdot 2 \cdot \frac{\Delta^3}{8}$$

$$= \frac{1}{\Delta} \cdot \frac{\Delta^3}{12} = \frac{\Delta^2}{12}$$

**Step 3 — Express $A_m$ in terms of $\Delta$ and $L$:**

For a full-scale bipolar sine wave, the peak excursion is half the full-scale range:

$$A_m = \frac{L \Delta}{2}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute into Signal Power:**

$$P_s = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \left(\frac{L \Delta}{2}\right)^2 = \frac{L^2 \Delta^2}{8}$$

**Step 5 — Compute SQNR as a Ratio:**

$$\text{SQNR} = \frac{P_s}{\overline{e_q^2}} = \frac{L^2 \Delta^2 / 8}{\Delta^2 / 12} = \frac{L^2}{8} \cdot 12 = \frac{12 L^2}{8} = 1.5 L^2$$

**Step 6 — Substitute $L = 2^n$:**

$$\text{SQNR} = 1.5 \cdot (2^n)^2 = 1.5 \cdot 2^{2n} = 1.5 \cdot 4^n$$

**Step 7 — Convert to Decibels:**

$$\text{SQNR}_{dB} = 10 \log_{10}(1.5) + 10 \log_{10}(2^{2n})$$

$$= 10 \log_{10}(1.5) + 2n \cdot 10 \log_{10}(2)$$

Using $10 \log_{10}(1.5) = 1.7609 \approx 1.76$ and $10 \log_{10}(2) = 0.3010$:

$$\text{SQNR}_{dB} = 1.76 + 2n \cdot (3.0103) = 1.76 + 6.02 n$$

**Final Result:**

$$\boxed{\text{SQNR}_{dB} = 1.76 + 6.02 n}$$

**Interpretation:**
- Each additional quantization bit adds **~6 dB** of SQNR.
- $n = 8$ bits → $\text{SQNR}_{dB} = 1.76 + 48.16 = 49.92$ dB.
- $n = 16$ bits → $\text{SQNR}_{dB} = 1.76 + 96.32 = 98.08$ dB (CD audio quality).

---

### 3.4 Derivation of Transmission Bandwidth

For a binary PCM bit stream of rate $R_b$ transmitted through a noiseless channel of bandwidth $BW$:

**Nyquist's First Criterion (No-ISI Condition):**

$$BW_{\min} = \frac{R_b}{2}$$

**Derivation logic:** A binary pulse train has spectral nulls at integer multiples of the bit rate. The **fundamental** frequency of the bit stream is $R_b/2$. Therefore, an ideal channel with bandwidth $R_b/2$ can pass the first lobe of the spectrum without intersymbol interference.

Substituting $R_b = n f_s$:

$$\boxed{BW_{\min} = \frac{n f_s}{2}}$$

For voice PCM with $f_s = 8$ kHz and $n = 8$:

$$BW_{\min} = \frac{8 \cdot 8000}{2} = 32 \text{ kHz}$$

---

### 3.5 Analytical Worked Example (Full Marks, 7 marks style)

**Problem:** An audio signal has maximum frequency $f_m = 4$ kHz. It is sampled at $f_s = 8$ kHz and quantized with $n = 8$ bits per sample. Find:
1. The bit rate $R_b$.
2. The minimum transmission bandwidth.
3. The theoretical SQNR in dB.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Verify Nyquist:**

$$f_s = 8 \text{ kHz} \geq 2 f_m = 8 \text{ kHz} \quad \text{(satisfied, Nyquist rate)}$$

**Step 2 — Bit Rate:**

$$R_b = n \cdot f_s = 8 \cdot 8000 = 64{,}000 \text{ bps} = 64 \text{ kbps}$$

**Step 3 — Minimum Bandwidth:**

$$BW_{\min} = \frac{R_b}{2} = \frac{64{,}000}{2} = 32 \text{ kHz}$$

**Step 4 — SQNR (sinusoidal full-scale input):**

$$\text{SQNR}_{dB} = 1.76 + 6.02 \cdot 8 = 1.76 + 48.16 = 49.92 \text{ dB}$$

**Final Answer:** $R_b = 64$ kbps, $BW_{\min} = 32$ kHz, $\text{SQNR} = 49.92$ dB.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram of a Complete PCM System (Transmitter + Receiver)

The diagram below shows the **full block-level functional architecture** of a PCM communication system. The transmitter (top row) and receiver (bottom row) are mirror images of each other, connected via the channel (center).

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Analog Input Signal x of t] --> B[Anti Aliasing Low Pass Filter]
    B --> C[Sampler at fs equals 2fm]
    C --> D[Quantizer L levels]
    D --> E[n bit Binary Encoder]
    E --> F[Parallel to Serial Converter]
    F --> G[Line Coder NRZ or Manchester]
    G --> H[Channel with Additive Noise]
    H --> I[Line Decoder]
    I --> J[Serial to Parallel Converter]
    J --> K[n bit Binary Decoder]
    K --> L[Sample and Hold]
    L --> M[Reconstruction Low Pass Filter]
    M --> N[Analog Output y of t]
```

**Reading the diagram (left → right):**
1. **Anti-Aliasing Filter:** LPF with cutoff = $f_m$. Removes out-of-band noise that would alias.
2. **Sampler:** Produces PAM signal at $f_s \geq 2 f_m$.
3. **Quantizer:** Maps each PAM value to one of $L$ discrete levels.
4. **Encoder:** Produces $n$-bit codeword.
5. **P/S Converter:** Serializes bits for transmission.
6. **Line Coder:** Converts bits to physical waveform (NRZ-L, RZ, AMI, etc.).
7. **Channel:** Adds noise, attenuation, distortion.
8. **Receiver:** Reverse operations in reverse order.

---

### 4.2 Block Diagram of PCM Transmitter (Detailed)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input Analog Signal] --> B[Band Limiting Filter fmax]
    B --> C[Sampling Circuit with Clock]
    C --> D[Hold Circuit for PAM Pulse]
    D --> E[Quantizer with L Levels]
    E --> F[Encoder n bit Codeword]
    F --> G[Parallel to Serial Shift Register]
    G --> H[Output Binary Bitstream]
```

**Component Function Notes:**
- **Band-Limiting Filter:** Essential to satisfy the pre-condition of the sampling theorem.
- **Sample-and-Hold (S/H):** Holds the analog value constant during quantization to prevent ambiguity in encoding.
- **Quantizer:** Rounds to nearest of $L = 2^n$ levels.
- **Encoder:** Maps each level index to a binary word; commonly **Natural Binary** or **Gray Code**.

---

### 4.3 Time-Division Multiplexing (TDM) of Multiple PCM Sources

When multiple PCM channels share a single transmission medium, TDM is used. Each channel occupies a **time slot** within a repeating **frame**.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1[Source 1 PCM] --> M1[Channel 1 Time Slot]
    S2[Source 2 PCM] --> M2[Channel 2 Time Slot]
    S3[Source 3 PCM] --> M3[Channel 3 Time Slot]
    S4[Source N PCM] --> MN[Channel N Time Slot]
    M1 --> MX[Commutator and TDM Frame]
    M2 --> MX
    M3 --> MX
    MN --> MX
    MX --> CH[Common Transmission Channel]
    CH --> DC[De commutator at Receiver]
    DC --> O1[Output 1]
    DC --> O2[Output 2]
    DC --> O3[Output 3]
    DC --> ON[Output N]
```

**Real-world use:**
- **T1 System (USA/Japan):** 24 voice channels × 8 bits = 192 bits/frame + 1 framing bit = 193 bits/frame, 8000 frames/s → **1.544 Mbps**.
- **E1 System (Europe/India):** 30 voice channels × 8 bits + 2 framing = 256 bits/frame, 8000 frames/s → **2.048 Mbps**.

---

### 4.4 Companding Process: A-law and μ-law

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Analog Input Sample] --> B[Compressor Non Linear]
    B --> C[Uniform Quantizer]
    C --> D[Encoder and Channel]
    D --> E[Decoder at Receiver]
    E --> F[Uniform De Quantizer]
    F --> G[Expander Non Linear Inverse]
    G --> H[Analog Output Sample]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Companding?** For speech, the probability density of amplitude is highest near zero (quiet sounds are more common). Uniform quantization wastes bits on rarely-occurring loud peaks. Non-uniform quantization (smaller $\Delta$ for small signals, larger $\Delta$ for large signals) equalizes the SQNR across the entire amplitude range, giving roughly **+4 bits of equivalent resolution** for the same bit count.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024] Define Pulse Code Modulation. What are its main advantages over analog transmission?**

**Model Answer:**
PCM is the process of converting an analog signal into a digital form by sampling, quantizing, and encoding each sample into a binary codeword.

**Advantages over analog transmission:**
1. **Noise Immunity:** Repeaters regenerate the binary signal, eliminating accumulated noise.
2. **Robustness:** Digital signals are less affected by channel impairments.
3. **Secure:** Encryption is easier on digital data.
4. **Multiplexing:** TDM is straightforward and efficient.
5. **Error Correction:** FEC codes can detect and correct transmission errors.
6. **Storage & Processing:** Digital storage is reliable and allows DSP.

(Any 3-4 valid points are acceptable for full 3 marks.)

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] State the Nyquist sampling theorem. What is the consequence of violating it?**

**Model Answer:**
**Statement:** A band-limited signal $x(t)$ with maximum frequency $f_m$ Hz can be completely reconstructed from its samples if the sampling frequency satisfies $f_s \geq 2 f_m$.

**Consequence of Violation:** If $f_s < 2 f_m$, the spectral replicas of the sampled signal **overlap** in the frequency domain. This phenomenon is called **aliasing** (or spectral folding). High-frequency components of the original signal appear as false low-frequency components, and the original information is permanently lost — it cannot be recovered by any filter.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

#### Question A — OR — Question B (Choose ONE)

---

**Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – July 2024 Style]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the operation of a **PCM system**. Describe the function of each block in the transmitter and receiver chain. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A band-limited analog signal has maximum frequency $f_m = 5$ kHz. It is sampled at $f_s = 20$ kHz and quantized with $n = 10$ bits per sample. Calculate:
1. The number of quantization levels.
2. The bit rate.
3. The minimum transmission bandwidth.
4. The theoretical SQNR in dB. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for Question A:**

**Part (a) — Block Diagram & Explanation (7 marks):**

The block diagram is as shown in **Section 4.1** of these notes.

**Transmitter Side Functions:**

1. **Anti-Aliasing Filter (LPF):** Restricts the input analog signal's bandwidth to $f_m$ to satisfy the pre-condition of the sampling theorem and prevent aliasing.
2. **Sampler:** Produces **PAM (Pulse Amplitude Modulated)** samples at rate $f_s \geq 2 f_m$.
3. **Quantizer:** Approximates each PAM value to the nearest of $L = 2^n$ discrete amplitude levels. Introduces **quantization error**.
4. **Encoder:** Assigns a unique $n$-bit binary codeword to each quantized level.
5. **P/S Converter (Shift Register):** Converts the parallel $n$-bit word to a serial bit stream for sequential transmission.
6. **Line Coder:** Converts the bit stream into a physical waveform (NRZ-L, RZ, Manchester, AMI) suitable for the channel.

**Receiver Side Functions:**

1. **Line Decoder:** Reconstructs binary bits from the received waveform.
2. **S/P Converter:** Groups serial bits into $n$-bit parallel words.
3. **Decoder:** Converts each $n$-bit word back to its quantized amplitude level.
4. **Sample-and-Hold (S/H):** Maintains the level constant for the duration of the LPF smoothing interval.
5. **Reconstruction LPF:** Smooths the staircase output to recover the original analog waveform.

**Mark Allocation (7 marks):**
- Block diagram — 3 marks
- Transmitter functions (each block) — 2 marks
- Receiver functions — 2 marks

---

**Part (b) — Numerical (7 marks):**

**Given:** $f_m = 5$ kHz, $f_s = 20$ kHz, $n = 10$ bits.

**Step 1 — Number of Quantization Levels:**

$$L = 2^n = 2^{10} = 1024 \text{ levels}$$

**Step 2 — Bit Rate:**

$$R_b = n \cdot f_s = 10 \cdot 20{,}000 = 200{,}000 \text{ bps} = 200 \text{ kbps}$$

**Step 3 — Minimum Transmission Bandwidth:**

$$BW_{\min} = \frac{R_b}{2} = \frac{200{,}000}{2} = 100 \text{ kHz}$$

**Step 4 — SQNR in dB:**

Using $\text{SQNR}_{dB} = 1.76 + 6.02 n$:

$$\text{SQNR}_{dB} = 1.76 + 6.02 \cdot 10 = 1.76 + 60.20 = 61.96 \text{ dB}$$

**Final Answer:** $L = 1024$, $R_b = 200$ kbps, $BW_{\min} = 100$ kHz, $\text{SQNR} = 61.96$ dB.

**Mark Allocation (7 marks):** 1.75 marks per sub-part.

---

#### Question B (Alternative, 14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Style]

**(a)** Explain the concept of **companding** in PCM. State and explain the **A-law** and **μ-law** compressor characteristics with their mathematical equations. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare **uniform quantization** and **non-uniform quantization**. Derive the expression for **SQNR of a uniform quantizer** for a sinusoidal input. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for Question B:**

**Part (a) — Companding, A-law and μ-law (7 marks):**

**Concept of Companding:**
Companding is the combination of **compressing** the signal at the transmitter and **expanding** it at the receiver. It is a non-linear amplitude transformation that compresses the dynamic range of the input signal before uniform quantization, so that weak signals get **more levels** (finer resolution) and strong signals get **fewer levels** (coarser resolution). This gives a **nearly constant SQNR** across the full input range, which is especially beneficial for speech signals.

**Mathematical Companding Laws:**

**A-law (used in Europe/India — ITU-T G.711):** $A = 87.6$

$$F(x) = \frac{A \vert x \vert}{1 + \ln A} \quad \text{for } 0 \leq \vert x \vert \leq \frac{1}{A}$$

$$F(x) = \frac{1 + \ln(A \vert x \vert)}{1 + \ln A} \quad \text{for } \frac{1}{A} \leq \vert x \vert \leq 1$$

**μ-law (used in USA/Japan — ITU-T G.711):** $\mu = 255$

$$F(x) = \frac{\ln(1 + \mu \vert x \vert)}{\ln(1 + \mu)} \cdot \text{sgn}(x)$$

**Comparison:**

| Feature | A-law | μ-law |
|---|---|---|
| Region | Segmented (linear + log) | Purely logarithmic |
| $A$ or $\mu$ | 87.6 | 255 |
| Continuity | Linear at low amplitudes, log at high | Continuous but non-linear everywhere |
| Region used | Europe, India, ITU | USA, Japan, T-carrier |

**Mark Allocation:** Definition (2 marks) + A-law derivation/equation (2 marks) + μ-law equation (2 marks) + Comparison (1 mark).

---

**Part (b) — Uniform vs Non-Uniform + SQNR Derivation (7 marks):**

**Comparison Table:**

| Feature | Uniform Quantization | Non-Uniform Quantization |
|---|---|---|
| Step size | Constant $\Delta$ | Variable (smaller for weak, larger for strong) |
| SQNR | Better for large signals | Better for weak signals (speech) |
| Complexity | Simple | Requires companding (extra compressor/expander) |
| Application | High-quality audio | Telephony (speech) |
| Equivalent bits | $n$ | $n + \Delta n$ (typically +4 bits equivalent) |

**SQNR Derivation:**

Given in **Section 3.3** of these notes. The final expression is:

$$\boxed{\text{SQNR}_{dB} = 1.76 + 6.02 n \text{ dB}}$$

(Marks allocated for each derivation step: 1 mark per major step, total 4 marks for derivation; 3 marks for comparison table.)

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing $f_s$ and $R_b$:** Students often write $R_b = f_s$ (forgetting the $\times n$ factor). Always multiply by $n$.
> 2. **Forgetting $f_s = 2f_m$ substitution:** When the problem says "sampled at Nyquist rate," substitute $f_s = 2f_m$ explicitly.
> 3. **Wrong log base for dB:** SQNR in dB uses $10 \log_{10}$, not $20 \log_{10}$. Power ratio → 10 log; amplitude ratio → 20 log.
> 4. **Sign error in μ-law:** The negative half uses $\text{sgn}(x)$ to make $F(-x) = -F(x)$ (odd symmetry).
> 5. **A-law region confusion:** Below $1/A$ it is linear, above it is logarithmic. Mixing them gives wrong output values.
> 6. **Bandwidth = $R_b$ (wrong):** Minimum bandwidth is $R_b/2$, not $R_b$. (For NRZ, first null is at $R_b$.)
> 7. **SQNR formula application:** The $1.76 + 6.02n$ formula applies to **full-scale sinusoidal** inputs only. For lower-amplitude signals, the SQNR is lower by $20 \log_{10}(A/A_{FS})$ dB.
> 8. **Aliasing reversal:** If the problem asks "what happens if you sample at 5 kHz for a 4 kHz signal," the answer is **aliasing**, not "no information." Frequency 4 kHz becomes 1 kHz in the reconstructed signal.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist (High-Density Summary):**

- **PCM = Sampling → Quantization → Encoding** (in that exact order, transmitter side).
- **Sampling Theorem:** $f_s \geq 2 f_m$ is **mandatory**; otherwise **aliasing** occurs and is irreversible.
- **Nyquist Rate:** $f_s = 2 f_m$ is the **minimum** sampling rate for lossless recovery.
- **Quantization Levels:** $L = 2^n$, where $n$ = number of bits per sample.
- **Step Size (bipolar):** $\Delta = 2V_{FS}/L$; **Quantization Noise Power:** $\Delta^2/12$.
- **Bit Rate:** $R_b = n \cdot f_s$ (bits per second).
- **Minimum Bandwidth:** $BW_{\min} = R_b/2$ (Nyquist criterion for binary transmission).
- **SQNR Formula:** $\text{SQNR}_{dB} = 1.76 + 6.02n$ — **remember "6 dB per bit" rule**.
- **Companding Laws:** A-law ($A = 87.6$, ITU Europe) and μ-law ($\mu = 255$, USA).
- **Companding Benefit:** Roughly equivalent to **+4 bits** of resolution for speech.
- **PCM Voice Standard:** 8 kHz × 8 bits = **64 kbps** (G.711).
- **T1 (US):** 24 × 64 kbps + framing = **1.544 Mbps**; **E1 (EU/India):** 32 × 64 kbps = **2.048 Mbps**.
- **Line Codes for PCM:** NRZ-L, RZ, NRZ-I, Manchester, AMI — each has its own DC content and clock-recovery property.
- **Anti-aliasing Filter is Essential:** It is the **only** way to enforce the band-limitation condition required by the sampling theorem in real-world systems.
- **Quantization Error behaves like white noise** for a large number of levels and rapidly varying signals — this is the foundation of the SQNR formula.
- **Master Formula 1:** $R_b = 2 n f_m$ (when sampled at Nyquist rate).
- **Master Formula 2:** $\text{SQNR}_{dB} = 1.76 + 6.02 n$.
- **Master Formula 3:** $BW_{\min} = n f_m$ (when substituting $f_s = 2 f_m$ into $R_b/2$).
- **Course Outcome:** Satisfies **CO2** of PEEET411 — Apply digital modulation techniques in instrumentation.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: General Telemetry Systems - Cable Transmission of Analog and Digital Data - Fibre Optic Data Transmission
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET411 - ELECTRONIC INSTRUMENTATION
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** General Telemetry Systems - Cable Transmission of Analog and Digital Data - Fibre Optic Data Transmission

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# General Telemetry Systems, Cable & Fibre Optic Data Transmission

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 General Telemetry Systems — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition**
> **Telemetry** is the process of automatic measurement and wireless (or wired) transmission of data from a remote or inaccessible source to a receiving station where the data is recorded, monitored, or processed. The word is derived from the Greek roots *tele* (remote) and *metron* (measure).

A **General Telemetry System** is a complete measurement-and-transmission chain consisting of:

1. A **primary measuring element** (sensor/transducer).
2. A **signal conditioning unit** (amplification, filtering, linearization).
3. A **transmission medium** (cable, radio, optical fibre).
4. A **receiving terminal** (decoder, display, recorder).
5. A **display / recording / processing unit**.

The system converts the physical parameter of interest (temperature, pressure, flow, displacement, etc.) into a standardized electrical (or optical) signal that can be transmitted over a defined distance without significant loss of fidelity.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Classification of Telemetry Systems**
> 1. **Based on Transmission Medium:**
>    - **Wire / Cable Telemetry** (landline)
>    - **Radio / Wireless Telemetry**
>    - **Optical Fibre Telemetry**
> 2. **Based on Signal Type:**
>    - **Analog Telemetry** (continuous voltage/current)
>    - **Digital Telemetry** (PCM, PAM-encoded bits)

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Postal Letter System"

> [!TIP]
> **Think of Telemetry Like a Postal Letter**
> - The **letter** is your sensor signal (the data you want to send).
> - The **postman / postal tube** is your transmission medium (cable, fibre, or air).
> - The **envelope encoding** is your modulation (analog voltage, current loop, or digital PCM).
> - The **receiver's mailbox** is your display/recorder.
> - A **registered post with tracking** is like a digital system (verifiable, secure), while an **open postcard** is like an analog system (anyone can read, susceptible to noise).
> - A **fibre optic cable** is like a laser-guided letter pipeline — fast, secure, and immune to electromagnetic eavesdropping.

### 1.3 Key Standard Metrics

| Metric | Value / Unit |
|---|---|
| Standard industrial current loop | **4 mA to 20 mA** |
| Standard voltage transmission | **0 V to 10 V** or **1 V to 5 V** |
| Standard frequency range for FSK | **1300 Hz (mark) / 1700 Hz (space)** |
| Speed of light in vacuum | **c = 3 × 10⁸ m/s** |
| Speed of light in silica fibre core | **≈ 2 × 10⁸ m/s** |
| Typical fibre attenuation @ 1550 nm | **≈ 0.2 dB/km** |
| Typical fibre attenuation @ 850 nm | **≈ 3 dB/km** |
| Critical angle (silica, n = 1.48) | **≈ 43.2°** |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 General Telemetry — Block Diagram & Signal Flow

A general-purpose telemetry system follows the canonical **forward–backward flow**:

$$
\text{Sensor} \rightarrow \text{Signal Conditioning} \rightarrow \text{Transmitter} \rightarrow \text{Channel} \rightarrow \text{Receiver} \rightarrow \text{Display/Recorder}
$$

### 2.2 Operational Sub-Systems Explained

#### (a) Transducer / Sensor
Converts physical quantity → electrical signal. Examples: thermocouple (temperature), strain gauge (force), LVDT (displacement), piezoelectric crystal (pressure/vibration).

#### (b) Signal Conditioning
- **Amplification** — boosts low-level mV signals.
- **Linearization** — corrects non-linear sensor curves.
- **Cold-junction compensation** — for thermocouples.
- **Filtering** — removes 50/60 Hz mains interference.

#### (c) Multiplexing (when multiple sensors share one channel)
- **TDM** (Time Division Multiplexing) — sequential time slots.
- **FDM** (Frequency Division Multiplexing) — different carrier frequencies.
- **CDM** (Code Division Multiplexing) — orthogonal codes.

#### (d) Transmission Channel
The medium (twisted pair, coaxial cable, free space, optical fibre).

#### (e) Receiver & Demultiplexer
Recovers the original signal by reversing the modulation/multiplexing process.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why we use 4–20 mA current loops in industrial telemetry:**
> - **Live-zero (4 mA)** distinguishes a broken wire (0 mA) from a true zero reading.
> - Current is **immune to lead resistance and contact resistance** (Ohm's law — current is the same in a series loop).
> - Long-distance transmission without signal loss.

---

## 2.3 Cable Transmission of Analog Data

### 2.3.1 Voltage-Mode Transmission

The sensor output is transmitted as a **proportional voltage** (0–10 V or 1–5 V).

$$
V_o = V_{FS} \cdot \frac{X}{X_{FS}}
$$

Where:
- $V_o$ = output voltage
- $V_{FS}$ = full-scale voltage
- $X$ = measured variable
- $X_{FS}$ = full-scale value of variable

**Drawbacks:** susceptible to **lead resistance**, **induced EMI**, and **ground loops**. Suitable only for **short distances (< 30 m)**.

### 2.3.2 Current-Mode Transmission (4–20 mA Loop)

$$
I_{out} = 4 + 16 \cdot \frac{X - X_{min}}{X_{max} - X_{min}} \quad \text{[mA]}
$$

The current is governed by **Ohm's Law**:

$$
I = \frac{V - V_{sensor}}{R_{loop}}
$$

Because $I$ is invariant along a series loop, **voltage drops in the cable do not affect the signal**.

### 2.3.3 Frequency-Mode Transmission (V → f)

A voltage-controlled oscillator (VCO) converts sensor voltage to a proportional frequency:

$$
f_{out} = f_0 + K_{VCO} \cdot V_{in}
$$

- **Advantages:** immune to amplitude noise; long-distance capable.
- **Disadvantages:** bandwidth limited by VCO linearity.

### 2.3.4 Phase-Mode Transmission
Voltage → phase shift $\phi$ via a phase shifter:

$$
\phi(t) = \phi_0 + K_\phi \cdot V_{in}(t)
$$

---

## 2.4 Cable Transmission of Digital Data

### 2.4.1 Why Digital?
Digital signals are **regenerable**, **noise-tolerant**, and support **error detection/correction**.

### 2.4.2 Transmission Modes

| Mode | Description | Use Case |
|---|---|---|
| **Simplex** | One-way only | Broadcast |
| **Half-duplex** | Both ways, but one at a time | Walkie-talkie |
| **Full-duplex** | Simultaneous both ways | Telephone |

### 2.4.3 Serial Communication Standards

| Standard | Voltage / Logic | Max Distance | Speed |
|---|---|---|---|
| **RS-232** | ±12 V, single-ended | 15 m | 20 kbps |
| **RS-422** | Differential ±5 V | 1200 m | 10 Mbps |
| **RS-485** | Differential ±5 V, multi-drop | 1200 m | 10 Mbps |
| **TTL UART** | 0 / 5 V | ~3 m | 1 Mbps |
| **CAN bus** | Differential | 40 m @ 1 Mbps | 1 Mbps |

> [!NOTE]
> **RS-485 vs RS-232:** RS-485 uses **differential signalling** (twisted pair, $V_{AB}$ = measured) — this rejects common-mode noise, allowing long-distance, high-noise industrial telemetry.

### 2.4.4 Bit-Rate and Bandwidth (Nyquist)

$$
C = 2B \log_2 M \quad \text{[bps]}
$$

Where:
- $C$ = channel capacity (bit rate)
- $B$ = bandwidth (Hz)
- $M$ = number of signal levels

For binary ($M = 2$):

$$
C = 2B
$$

The Shannon–Hartley theorem with noise:

$$
C = B \log_2 \left(1 + \frac{S}{N}\right)
$$

Where $S/N$ is signal-to-noise ratio.

---

## 2.5 Fibre Optic Data Transmission

### 2.5.1 Principle: Total Internal Reflection (TIR)

Light is guided inside a fibre by **total internal reflection** at the core–cladding interface. TIR occurs when:

$$
\theta_i > \theta_c
$$

Where the **critical angle** is:

$$
\theta_c = \sin^{-1}\left(\frac{n_2}{n_1}\right)
$$

- $n_1$ = refractive index of **core**
- $n_2$ = refractive index of **cladding** ($n_2 < n_1$)

The **numerical aperture (NA)** measures light-gathering ability:

$$
NA = \sqrt{n_1^2 - n_2^2} = n_0 \sin \theta_a
$$

Where $\theta_a$ is the maximum acceptance angle.

### 2.5.2 Snell's Law (foundation)

$$
n_1 \sin \theta_1 = n_2 \sin \theta_2
$$

### 2.5.3 Attenuation in Fibre (Beer–Lambert)

$$
P_{out} = P_{in} \cdot 10^{-\alpha L / 10}
$$

Where:
- $\alpha$ = attenuation coefficient (dB/km)
- $L$ = fibre length (km)

Loss in dB:

$$
L_{dB} = -10 \log_{10}\left(\frac{P_{out}}{P_{in}}\right) = \alpha L
$$

### 2.5.4 Fibre Types

| Type | Core Diameter | Mode | Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Step-index multimode** | 50 / 62.5 / 100 µm | Multimode (TIR) | LAN, short links |
| **Graded-index multimode** | 50 / 62.5 µm | Multimode (refractive index grading) | LAN, longer links |
| **Step-index single-mode** | 8–10 µm | Single-mode | Long-haul, telecom |

### 2.5.5 Components of a Fibre Optic Link

$$
\text{Source} \rightarrow \text{Connector} \rightarrow \text{Fibre} \rightarrow \text{Splice/Connector} \rightarrow \text{Detector} \rightarrow \text{Amplifier}
$$

- **Sources:** LED (850 nm) or Laser Diode / VCSEL (1310 / 1550 nm).
- **Detectors:** PIN photodiode or APD (Avalanche Photodiode).

### 2.5.6 Modal Dispersion (Multimode)

$$
\Delta t = \frac{L \cdot n_1 \cdot \Delta}{c}
$$

Where:
- $\Delta = \dfrac{n_1 - n_2}{n_1}$ = relative refractive index difference
- $L$ = fibre length
- $c$ = speed of light in vacuum

---

## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula | Meaning | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\theta_c = \sin^{-1}(n_2/n_1)$ | Critical angle for TIR | rad / ° |
| 2 | $NA = \sqrt{n_1^2 - n_2^2}$ | Numerical aperture | dimensionless |
| 3 | $NA = n_0 \sin \theta_a$ | Acceptance angle relation | dimensionless |
| 4 | $L_{dB} = \alpha L$ | Total fibre attenuation | dB |
| 5 | $P_{out} = P_{in} \cdot 10^{-\alpha L/10}$ | Output power after attenuation | W |
| 6 | $C = 2B \log_2 M$ | Nyquist channel capacity | bps |
| 7 | $C = B \log_2(1+S/N)$ | Shannon–Hartley capacity | bps |
| 8 | $I = 4 + 16(X - X_{min})/(X_{max} - X_{min})$ | 4–20 mA loop output | mA |
| 9 | $f_{out} = f_0 + K_{VCO}\cdot V_{in}$ | VCO voltage-to-frequency | Hz |
| 10 | $\Delta t = (L \cdot n_1 \cdot \Delta)/c$ | Modal dispersion pulse broadening | s |

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility**
> - 4–20 mA loops dominate **process industries** (oil, chemical, power plants).
> - RS-485 is the workhorse of **industrial telemetry buses** (Modbus, Profibus).
> - Fibre optics form the backbone of **SCADA**, **smart grids**, and **nuclear reactor** instrumentation where EMI immunity and intrinsic safety are critical.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Critical Angle Condition for TIR

We begin with **Snell's Law** at the core–cladding boundary:

$$
n_1 \sin \theta_i = n_2 \sin \theta_t
$$

We want to find the limiting angle $\theta_i$ where the refracted ray grazes along the boundary, i.e., $\theta_t = 90°$ and $\sin \theta_t = 1$.

$$
n_1 \sin \theta_c = n_2 \cdot 1
$$

$$
\boxed{\theta_c = \sin^{-1}\left(\frac{n_2}{n_1}\right)}
$$

**Interpretation:**
- If $\theta_i < \theta_c$ → refraction occurs (light leaks).
- If $\theta_i = \theta_c$ → grazing refraction.
- If $\theta_i > \theta_c$ → **total internal reflection** occurs.

This is the *fundamental* condition that makes fibre optic telemetry possible.

---

## 3.2 Derivation of the Numerical Aperture (NA)

Light enters the fibre from air ($n_0 \approx 1$) at the maximum acceptance angle $\theta_a$. Inside the fibre, it refracts to an angle $\theta_1$ relative to the fibre axis. By Snell's law:

$$
n_0 \sin \theta_a = n_1 \sin \theta_1
$$

For TIR to occur at the core–cladding boundary, the ray inside must strike at an angle $\geq \theta_c$. Geometry gives:

$$
\theta_1 + \theta_c = 90° \quad \Rightarrow \quad \theta_1 = 90° - \theta_c
$$

Substituting:

$$
\sin \theta_1 = \sin(90° - \theta_c) = \cos \theta_c = \sqrt{1 - \sin^2 \theta_c}
$$

Using $\sin \theta_c = n_2/n_1$:

$$
\sin \theta_1 = \sqrt{1 - \left(\frac{n_2}{n_1}\right)^2} = \frac{\sqrt{n_1^2 - n_2^2}}{n_1}
$$

Therefore:

$$
n_0 \sin \theta_a = n_1 \cdot \frac{\sqrt{n_1^2 - n_2^2}}{n_1} = \sqrt{n_1^2 - n_2^2}
$$

$$
\boxed{NA = n_0 \sin \theta_a = \sqrt{n_1^2 - n_2^2}}
$$

For air $n_0 = 1$, so $NA = \sqrt{n_1^2 - n_2^2}$.

---

## 3.3 Worked Example 1 — 4–20 mA Loop Calculation

> **Problem:** A temperature transmitter is calibrated for $-50 °C$ to $+150 °C$. What is the loop current at $T = 25 °C$?

**Step 1 — Identify the range:**
- $X_{min} = -50$, $X_{max} = 150$, $X = 25$
- Span: $X_{max} - X_{min} = 200$

**Step 2 — Apply the formula:**

$$
I = 4 + 16 \cdot \frac{25 - (-50)}{150 - (-50)}
$$

$$
I = 4 + 16 \cdot \frac{75}{200}
$$

$$
I = 4 + 16 \cdot 0.375
$$

$$
I = 4 + 6 = 10 \text{ mA}
$$

**Answer:** $I = 10$ mA, which corresponds to **37.5 %** of span — exactly the midpoint of -50 to +150.

---

## 3.4 Worked Example 2 — Fibre Optic Numerical Aperture

> **Problem:** A fibre has $n_1 = 1.50$, $n_2 = 1.45$. Find (a) critical angle, (b) NA, (c) acceptance angle in air.

**Step 1 — Critical angle:**

$$
\theta_c = \sin^{-1}\left(\frac{n_2}{n_1}\right) = \sin^{-1}\left(\frac{1.45}{1.50}\right) = \sin^{-1}(0.9667)
$$

$$
\boxed{\theta_c \approx 75.07°}
$$

**Step 2 — Numerical Aperture:**

$$
NA = \sqrt{n_1^2 - n_2^2} = \sqrt{(1.50)^2 - (1.45)^2} = \sqrt{2.25 - 2.1025} = \sqrt{0.1475}
$$

$$
\boxed{NA \approx 0.384}
$$

**Step 3 — Acceptance angle (in air, $n_0 = 1$):**

$$
\theta_a = \sin^{-1}(NA) = \sin^{-1}(0.384)
$$

$$
\boxed{\theta_a \approx 22.6°}
$$

**Interpretation:** Only rays entering within a **22.6° cone** will undergo TIR and propagate down the fibre.

---

## 3.5 Worked Example 3 — Fibre Attenuation Budget

> **Problem:** A fibre has $\alpha = 0.5$ dB/km. A 25 km link has 4 splices each with 0.2 dB loss, and two connectors each with 0.5 dB loss. Transmitter power = 1 mW. Find received power.

**Step 1 — Fibre loss:**

$$
L_{fibre} = 0.5 \cdot 25 = 12.5 \text{ dB}
$$

**Step 2 — Splice loss:**

$$
L_{splice} = 4 \cdot 0.2 = 0.8 \text{ dB}
$$

**Step 3 — Connector loss:**

$$
L_{connector} = 2 \cdot 0.5 = 1.0 \text{ dB}
$$

**Step 4 — Total loss:**

$$
L_{total} = 12.5 + 0.8 + 1.0 = 14.3 \text{ dB}
$$

**Step 5 — Convert dB loss to power ratio:**

$$
\frac{P_{out}}{P_{in}} = 10^{-14.3/10} = 10^{-1.43}
$$

$$
\frac{P_{out}}{P_{in}} \approx 0.0372
$$

**Step 6 — Received power:**

$$
P_{out} = 1 \text{ mW} \cdot 0.0372 \approx 37.2 \text{ μW}
$$

**Answer:** $P_{out} \approx 37$ μW, well above typical PIN photodiode sensitivities (~ -30 dBm or 1 μW), giving a healthy 15 dB system margin.

---

## 3.6 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Parameter | Voltage Loop | Current Loop (4–20 mA) | Digital (RS-485) | Fibre Optic |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Noise immunity | Poor | Excellent | Excellent | Outstanding |
| Max distance | <30 m | >1 km | 1.2 km | >100 km |
| Live-zero check | No | Yes (4 mA) | N/A | N/A |
| Bandwidth | Low | Low | High | Very high |
| EMI susceptibility | High | Low | Very low | None |
| Cost | Very low | Moderate | Moderate | High |
| Intrinsic safety | Poor | Good | Good | Excellent |
| Accuracy drift | High | Low | Very low | Very low |

---

## 3.7 Step-by-Step Derivation of Channel Capacity (Binary)

Given bandwidth $B$ (Hz), the maximum number of signal changes per second is $2B$ (Nyquist). For binary signalling ($M = 2$):

$$
C = 2B \log_2(2) = 2B \cdot 1 = 2B
$$

$$
\boxed{C_{binary} = 2B \text{ bps}}
$$

With $M = 4$ signal levels (e.g., PAM-4):

$$
C = 2B \log_2(4) = 2B \cdot 2 = 4B
$$

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 General Telemetry System — Block-Level Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    SENSOR1[SENSOR A] --> SC1[Signal Conditioning]
    SENSOR2[SENSOR B] --> SC2[Signal Conditioning]
    SENSOR3[SENSOR C] --> SC3[Signal Conditioning]
    SC1 --> MUX[Multiplexer TDM]
    SC2 --> MUX
    SC3 --> MUX
    MUX --> ADC[Analog to Digital Converter]
    ADC --> ENC[Encoder and Modulator]
    ENC --> CH[Transmission Channel]
    CH --> DEC[Decoder and Demodulator]
    DEC --> DMUX[Demultiplexer]
    DMUX --> DSP1[Display A]
    DMUX --> REC1[Recorder A]
    DMUX --> DA1[Data Acquisition]
```

## 4.2 4–20 mA Current Loop — Detailed Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    T[Transmitter and Sensor] -->|4 to 20 mA current| W1[Twisted Pair Cable]
    W1 --> R[250 ohm Receiver Resistor]
    R --> VOUT[1 to 5 V across R]
    VOUT --> DSP[Indicator or PLC Input]
    PS[24 V DC Supply] -.powers.-> T
    PS -.powers.-> DSP
```

## 4.3 Fibre Optic Data Transmission Link

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    E[Electrical Input Signal] --> DRV[Driver Circuit]
    DRV --> LED[LED or Laser Source 850 or 1310 or 1550 nm]
    LED --> COUP[Coupler and Connector]
    COUP --> FIBRE[Optical Fibre Core n1 Cladding n2]
    FIBRE --> COUP2[Connector and Splice]
    COUP2 --> PD[PIN or APD Photodetector]
    PD --> AMP[Transimpedance Amplifier]
    AMP --> OUT[Recovered Electrical Output]
```

## 4.4 Total Internal Reflection — Geometric View

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph CORE[Core n1 equals 1.50]
        direction TB
        A[Incident Ray theta i] -->|strikes boundary| B[Core Cladding Boundary]
        B -->|reflects back| C[Reflected Ray theta r equals theta i]
        B -.->|TIR if theta i greater than theta c| D[No Transmission into Cladding]
    end
    CL[Cladding n2 equals 1.45]
    CORE --- CL
```

## 4.5 Hierarchy of Telemetry Classification

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    TELE[Telemetry Systems] --> WM[Wire or Cable Telemetry]
    TELE --> RF[Radio or Wireless Telemetry]
    TELE --> FO[Fibre Optic Telemetry]
    WM --> AV[Analog Voltage]
    WM --> AC[Analog Current 4 to 20 mA]
    WM --> DG[Digital RS232 RS485 CAN]
    RF --> AM[AM]
    RF --> FM[FM]
    RF --> PCM[PCM]
    FO --> MM[Multimode]
    FO --> SM[Single Mode]
    MM --> SI[Step Index]
    MM --> GI[Graded Index]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** Define the term *telemetry*. List any four advantages of fibre optic data transmission over conventional copper cable transmission.

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:** Telemetry is the process of measuring a physical parameter at a remote or inaccessible location and transmitting the resulting signal to a distant station for display, recording, or processing.

**Four advantages of fibre optic transmission:**

1. **Immunity to electromagnetic interference (EMI)** — fibre is a dielectric, so it neither radiates nor picks up EMI.
2. **Very high bandwidth** — GHz·km product vs MHz for copper.
3. **Low attenuation** — 0.2 dB/km @ 1550 nm enables >100 km links without repeaters.
4. **Intrinsic safety** — no spark risk in explosive/hazardous environments (oil refineries, mines).

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Students often quote "high speed" alone. Always specify the **physical reason** (e.g., large bandwidth-distance product, low loss, no EMI) to score full 3 marks.

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]** Why is the **4–20 mA current loop** preferred over **0–10 V voltage transmission** in industrial process telemetry?

**Model Answer:**

1. **Live-zero capability:** A 4 mA floor distinguishes a healthy zero-signal reading from a broken wire (0 mA).
2. **Immunity to lead resistance:** Current is the same throughout a series loop (Ohm's law); voltage drops in long cables do not affect the signal.
3. **Noise immunity:** Loops can be twisted and shielded; common-mode noise is rejected.
4. **Two-wire operation:** Both power and signal share the same pair, simplifying field wiring.
5. **Intrinsic safety:** The bounded 4–20 mA is easier to limit in hazardous areas.

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]`

**(a)** With the help of a block diagram, explain the operation of a **general telemetry system**. Briefly describe the function of each block. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Compare **analog and digital telemetry systems** with respect to noise immunity, bandwidth utilization, accuracy, and hardware complexity. **(7 Marks)**

---

### Model Solution — Question A

#### Part (a) — Block Diagram & Operation (7 Marks)

```
[Sensor] → [Signal Conditioning] → [Multiplexer] → [A-D Converter]
   → [Encoder/Modulator] → [Channel] → [Decoder/Demodulator]
   → [Demultiplexer] → [Display/Recorder/Computer]
```

| Block | Function |
|---|---|
| **Sensor** | Converts physical quantity (T, P, F) into electrical signal |
| **Signal Conditioning** | Amplifies, linearizes, filters the raw transducer signal |
| **Multiplexer** | Allows multiple sensor signals to share one channel (TDM) |
| **A-D Converter** | Converts analog signal into digital binary words |
| **Encoder/Modulator** | Adds framing, error-correction, modulates onto carrier |
| **Channel** | The transmission medium (cable, RF, fibre) |
| **Decoder/Demodulator** | Recovers the digital bit-stream from the carrier |
| **Demultiplexer** | Routes individual channel data to respective displays |
| **Display/Recorder** | Real-time indicator, chart recorder, or computer storage |

**Why each block matters:**
- Without **conditioning**, low-level mV signals cannot be transmitted reliably.
- Without **multiplexing**, multiple sensors would need separate channels (costly).
- Without **encoding**, errors due to channel noise cannot be detected.
- Without **synchronization**, the receiver cannot align bits into words.

#### Part (b) — Analog vs Digital Telemetry (7 Marks)

| Parameter | Analog Telemetry | Digital Telemetry |
|---|---|---|
| **Noise immunity** | Poor — noise adds directly to signal | Excellent — noise rejected by regenerators |
| **Bandwidth utilization** | Inefficient (no compression) | Efficient (compression + multiplexing) |
| **Accuracy** | Limited by component tolerances (≈ 0.1–1 %) | Limited only by A-D resolution (16-bit → 0.0015 %) |
| **Hardware complexity** | Simple, low cost | Complex (A-D, encoder, microcontroller) |
| **Error detection** | Not possible | CRC, parity, Hamming codes possible |
| **Long-distance performance** | Degrades with distance | Regenerable, repeatable over long distances |
| **Storage** | Hard to store | Easy — direct digital recording |

**Conclusion:** Digital telemetry is the modern choice for high-accuracy, long-distance, multi-channel industrial applications. Analog remains in legacy systems where simplicity/cost dominates.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — `[KTU University Exam – Sample Paper 2024]`

**(a)** Explain the principle of **total internal reflection** with a diagram. Derive the expressions for **(i) critical angle** and **(ii) numerical aperture** of an optical fibre. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A step-index fibre has core refractive index $n_1 = 1.50$ and cladding refractive index $n_2 = 1.46$. Calculate (i) the **critical angle**, (ii) the **numerical aperture**, and (iii) the **acceptance angle in air**. Also determine the **total attenuation in dB** for a 20 km link if the attenuation coefficient is 0.4 dB/km. **(7 Marks)**

---

### Model Solution — Question B

#### Part (a) — TIR, Critical Angle & NA Derivation (7 Marks)

**Principle:** When a ray travels from a denser medium ($n_1$) to a rarer medium ($n_2$), and strikes the boundary at an angle greater than the **critical angle**, the entire ray is reflected back into the denser medium. This phenomenon is **Total Internal Reflection (TIR)**.

By Snell's law at the boundary:

$$
n_1 \sin \theta_i = n_2 \sin \theta_t
$$

At the critical angle, $\theta_t = 90°$, so $\sin \theta_t = 1$:

$$
\boxed{\theta_c = \sin^{-1}\left(\frac{n_2}{n_1}\right)}
$$

For a ray entering from air ($n_0 = 1$) at the maximum acceptance angle $\theta_a$:

$$
n_0 \sin \theta_a = n_1 \sin \theta_1
$$

Geometric condition: $\theta_1 + \theta_c = 90°$, so $\sin \theta_1 = \cos \theta_c = \sqrt{1 - (n_2/n_1)^2}$.

Substituting and simplifying:

$$
\boxed{NA = \sqrt{n_1^2 - n_2^2}}
$$

#### Part (b) — Numerical Computation (7 Marks)

**Given:** $n_1 = 1.50$, $n_2 = 1.46$, $L = 20$ km, $\alpha = 0.4$ dB/km.

**(i) Critical angle:**

$$
\theta_c = \sin^{-1}\left(\frac{1.46}{1.50}\right) = \sin^{-1}(0.9733) \approx 76.67°
$$

**(ii) Numerical aperture:**

$$
NA = \sqrt{(1.50)^2 - (1.46)^2} = \sqrt{2.25 - 2.1316} = \sqrt{0.1184} \approx 0.3441
$$

**(iii) Acceptance angle in air ($n_0 = 1$):**

$$
\theta_a = \sin^{-1}(NA) = \sin^{-1}(0.3441) \approx 20.12°
$$

**Total attenuation:**

$$
L_{total} = \alpha \cdot L = 0.4 \cdot 20 = 8 \text{ dB}
$$

**Power ratio:**

$$
\frac{P_{out}}{P_{in}} = 10^{-8/10} = 10^{-0.8} \approx 0.158
$$

So if $P_{in} = 1$ mW, then $P_{out} \approx 158$ μW — a very healthy received signal level.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Fibre Optics**
> 1. **Forgetting to convert** $L$ into km when using $\alpha$ in dB/km — instant 1-mark loss.
> 2. **Mixing up $n_1$ and $n_2$** in the critical angle formula (TIR requires $n_1 > n_2$).
> 3. **Wrong trigonometric substitution** — students often write $\sin^{-1}(n_1/n_2)$ instead of $\sin^{-1}(n_2/n_1)$. Remember: **numerator is the smaller index (cladding)**.
> 4. **Skipping the geometric step** $\theta_1 + \theta_c = 90°$ in the NA derivation — KTU examiners deduct 2 marks.
> 5. **In current-loop problems,** forgetting to subtract $X_{min}$ before scaling — leads to wrong 4 mA offset.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Telemetry = Tele (remote) + Metron (measure)** — remote measurement and transmission.
- A general telemetry chain has **sensor → conditioning → transmitter → channel → receiver → display**.
- **Voltage transmission** is simple but limited to short distances; susceptible to lead resistance and EMI.
- **4–20 mA current loop** is the industrial standard — gives live-zero, noise immunity, and long-distance capability.
- **Frequency and phase modulation** convert analog voltages into parameters immune to amplitude noise.
- **Digital transmission (RS-232, RS-485, CAN)** offers regenerability, error detection, and multi-drop capability.
- **Nyquist rate** (no noise): $C = 2B \log_2 M$; **Shannon** (with noise): $C = B \log_2(1+S/N)$.
- **Fibre optic transmission** relies on **Total Internal Reflection (TIR)** at the core–cladding boundary.
- **Critical angle:** $\theta_c = \sin^{-1}(n_2/n_1)$.
- **Numerical aperture:** $NA = \sqrt{n_1^2 - n_2^2} = \sin \theta_a$ (in air).
- **Fibre attenuation in dB:** $L = \alpha L$ (with $\alpha$ in dB/km and $L$ in km).
- **Multimode fibres** suffer modal dispersion $\Delta t = L n_1 \Delta / c$; **single-mode fibres** are used for long-haul.
- **Fibre optics advantages:** EMI immunity, low loss, high bandwidth, intrinsic safety, small size.
- **Fibre optic link components:** LED/Laser → coupler → fibre → connector → PIN/APD photodiode → transimpedance amplifier.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Principles of Time Division and Frequency Division Multiplexing - Radio-Wireless Communication
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET411 - ELECTRONIC INSTRUMENTATION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Principles of Time Division and Frequency Division Multiplexing - Radio-Wireless Communication

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Multiplexing — The Fundamental Need

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Multiplexing** is the process of simultaneously transmitting two or more information signals over a single communication channel without mutual interference, achieved by allocating distinguishable physical resources (time, frequency, or code) to each signal.

In any instrumentation or telemetry system, a large number of sensor outputs (temperature, pressure, flow, strain, vibration, etc.) need to be relayed from a remote plant to a central monitoring station. Running a separate pair of wires from every sensor is **economically impossible** and **practically unmanageable**. Multiplexing is the engineering solution that consolidates multiple low-bandwidth channels onto one high-bandwidth channel.

### 1.2 Time Division Multiplexing (TDM) — Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Time Division Multiplexing (TDM)** is a multiplexing technique in which several input signals take turns using a common transmission medium by being assigned **disjoint, sequential time slots** within a recurring frame.

**Conceptual Analogy:** Picture a railway platform with a single ticket counter. There are **N passengers** standing in line. The clerk serves passenger 1 for 5 seconds, then passenger 2 for 5 seconds, and so on. From an external observer, only **one passenger is being served at any instant**, but every passenger gets served within the cycle. In TDM, the "clerk" is the transmission channel, the "passengers" are the input signals, and the "5-second slot" is the time window allocated to each.

### 1.3 Frequency Division Multiplexing (FDM) — Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Frequency Division Multiplexing (FDM)** is a multiplexing technique in which several input signals are simultaneously transmitted over a common channel by translating each signal to a **distinct frequency band** (subcarrier), separated from neighbouring bands by unused spectrum called **guard bands**.

**Conceptual Analogy:** Imagine a multi-lane highway where each lane carries traffic at a different speed (or frequency). All lanes operate at the **same time** but vehicles in lane 1 cannot cross into lane 2. In FDM, every input signal travels **simultaneously** but is restricted to its allotted frequency band by band-pass filters.

### 1.4 Radio-Wireless Communication — Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Radio-Wireless Communication** refers to the transfer of information between a transmitter and a receiver using **electromagnetic (EM) waves propagating through free space**, requiring a high-frequency *carrier* to be modulated by the low-frequency baseband message signal.

> [!NOTE]
> **Electromagnetic Spectrum (Radio Band):** The usable radio frequencies span from **3 kHz to 300 GHz**, subdivided into VLF, LF, MF, HF, VHF, UHF, SHF, and EHF bands, as defined by the **ITU (International Telecommunication Union)**.

**Conceptual Analogy:** Voice frequencies (20 Hz – 4 kHz) cannot radiate efficiently from a practical antenna because an efficient radiator needs a physical length of approximately **λ/4**, where λ = c/f. For 1 kHz audio, λ = 300 km — an absurd antenna size. Modulation shifts the message to a high carrier frequency so that **λ/4 becomes a feasible dimension** (e.g., at 100 MHz, λ/4 ≈ 75 cm).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Principle of TDM

The TDM mechanism rests on the **Nyquist–Shannon Sampling Theorem**, which permits a band-limited signal of maximum frequency $f_m$ to be uniquely reconstructed from samples taken at a rate $f_s \geq 2f_m$.

**Step-by-Step Logic of TDM Operation:**

- Each of the **N** input signals (channels) is fed to an **electronic commutator / multiplexer switch** (in practice, an analog CMOS multiplexer like CD4051).
- The commutator rotates at a rate equal to the sampling frequency, dwelling on each input for an equal **time slot** of duration $\tau$.
