- The composite output is a **PAM (Pulse Amplitude Modulated) signal** in which samples from different channels are interleaved.
- At the receiver, a **synchronised de-commutator (demultiplexer)** routes each slot to its corresponding output reconstruction filter.
- A **sync pulse** is inserted at the end of every frame to maintain alignment between TX and RX commutators.

**Derived Relationships for TDM:**

Let $N$ = number of channels, $f_m$ = highest message frequency, $T_s$ = sampling period of one channel, and $\tau$ = slot width.

$$T_s = \frac{1}{f_s}, \quad f_s \geq 2f_m \quad \text{(Nyquist Criterion)}$$

$$\tau = \frac{T_s}{N} = \frac{1}{N \cdot f_s}$$

**Frame Duration** $T_f = N \cdot \tau = T_s$

If a **guard interval** $t_g$ is required between slots:

$$\tau_{eff} = \frac{T_s - (N-1) \cdot t_g}{N}$$

**Bit Rate (after PCM conversion, k bits/sample):**

$$R_b = N \cdot f_s \cdot k = 2 N \cdot f_m \cdot k$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Crosstalk in TDM** arises because the commutator's switching aperture is not infinitely sharp. Adjacent samples "bleed" into one another. The cure is to use a **sample-and-hold (S/H) amplifier** with a fast acquisition time and to design **reconstruction low-pass filters** with sharp roll-off at each demultiplexed output.

### 2.2 Types of TDM

- **Synchronous TDM (STDM):** Each channel gets a fixed slot regardless of whether it carries data. Simple, predictable, but inefficient for bursty traffic.
- **Asynchronous / Statistical TDM (ATDM):** Slots are assigned *only* to active channels. Higher utilisation but requires **address bits** in each slot to identify the channel — increasing overhead.

### 2.3 Operational Principle of FDM

FDM operates by **frequency translation** rather than time-sharing. Each baseband signal $m_i(t)$ is modulated onto a distinct subcarrier $c_i(t) = A_i \cos(2\pi f_i t)$, producing $s_i(t) = m_i(t) \cdot \cos(2\pi f_i t)$. The frequency-shifted spectra of all channels are then summed.

**Step-by-Step Logic of FDM Operation:**

- Each input is **band-limited** by an anti-aliasing low-pass filter to avoid inter-channel overlap.
- Each filtered signal modulates a unique **subcarrier** using AM, DSB-SC, SSB-SC, or FM.
- The modulated spectra are combined in a **linear summer** (resistor network or op-amp adder).
- Adjacent channels are separated by a **guard band** $f_{gb}$ to accommodate imperfect filters.
- At the receiver, a bank of **band-pass filters** centred on each subcarrier recovers the channel; each is then coherently demodulated.

### 2.4 Radio-Wireless Communication — Why Modulation?

> [!NOTE]
> **Modulation** is the process of varying one or more parameters (amplitude, frequency, or phase) of a high-frequency *carrier* wave in accordance with the instantaneous amplitude of the *message* (baseband) signal.

**Three primary reasons modulation is mandatory in wireless systems:**

- **Antenna Size Reduction:** For efficient radiation, antenna length $L \approx \lambda/4 = \frac{c}{4 f_c}$. Without a high $f_c$, antennas become impractically large.
- **Frequency Translation & Multiplexing:** Multiple users can occupy the same medium without interference (FDM is essentially modulated multiplexing).
- **Improved SNR & Noise Immunity:** FM and PCM trade bandwidth for noise rejection.

### 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Parameter | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|-----------|---------|-------|-------|
| 1 | Sampling Frequency | $f_s \geq 2 f_m$ | Hz | Nyquist criterion |
| 2 | TDM Slot Width | $\tau = 1 / (N f_s)$ | s | $N$ = number of channels |
| 3 | TDM Bit Rate (PCM) | $R_b = 2 N k f_m$ | bits/s | $k$ = bits per sample |
| 4 | Frame Time | $T_f = N / f_s$ | s | one full cycle |
| 5 | FDM Carrier Spacing | $\Delta f \geq 2 f_m + f_{gb}$ | Hz | $f_{gb}$ = guard band |
| 6 | AM Modulation Index | $\mu = A_m / A_c$ | dimensionless | $\mu \leq 1$ for no over-modulation |
| 7 | AM Bandwidth | $BW = 2 f_m$ | Hz | DSB-FC / DSB-SC |
| 8 | SSB Bandwidth | $BW = f_m$ | Hz | DSB-SC $\rightarrow$ single sideband |
| 9 | FM Modulation Index | $\beta = \Delta f / f_m$ | dimensionless | Carson's rule uses $\beta$ |
| 10 | FM Bandwidth (Carson's) | $BW = 2(\Delta f + f_m) = 2 f_m (\beta + 1)$ | Hz | Approx. 98% power containment |
| 11 | Carrier Power vs Total | $\eta = \mu^2 / (2 + \mu^2)$ | — | Efficiency of DSB-FC |
| 12 | Antenna Length | $L = \lambda / 4 = c / (4 f_c)$ | m | Half-wave dipole uses $L = \lambda / 2$ |
| 13 | Carrier Frequency | $f_c = 1 / T_c$ | Hz | $T_c$ = carrier period |
| 14 | Wavelength | $\lambda = c / f$ | m | $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s in free space |

> [!WARNING]
> In the table above, the vertical pipe `|` symbol has been deliberately avoided inside any mathematical expression. Where absolute value bars are needed, use the LaTeX `\vert \cdot \vert` macro to prevent Markdown table parser conflicts.

### 2.6 Real-World Utility in Engineering

- **Telemetry in Power Plants:** TDM aggregates readings from dozens of boilers, turbines, and transformers onto one fibre or wireless link.
- **FM Broadcast Radio (88–108 MHz):** A direct real-world implementation of FDM — every station occupies a 200 kHz channel.
- **Cellular Telephony (4G LTE / 5G NR):** Uses a hybrid — **OFDM** (Orthogonal FDM in frequency) plus **TDD** (Time Division Duplex).
- **Aeronautical & Maritime Radio:** SSB modulation on HF bands reduces bandwidth by half, doubling the number of voice channels.
- **Industrial SCADA Systems:** Use synchronous TDM over leased telephone lines for remote substation monitoring.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Minimum Sampling Rate in TDM

Consider a single baseband message $m(t)$ band-limited to $f_m$ Hz. By the Nyquist–Shannon sampling theorem:

The continuous-time signal can be completely reconstructed from its instantaneous samples provided the sampling frequency satisfies:

$$f_s \geq 2 f_m$$

**Reasoning step:** Two distinct sinusoidal frequencies $f_1$ and $f_2$ will produce identical sample sets if $\vert f_1 - f_2 \vert = n \cdot f_s$ for any integer $n$. To avoid this aliasing ambiguity, the sampling rate must be strictly greater than twice the highest signal frequency. Therefore:

$$\boxed{f_{s,\min} = 2 f_m}$$

### 3.2 Derivation: Bit Rate for an N-Channel PCM-TDM System

**Given:**
- $N$ channels each band-limited to $f_m$
- Each sample quantised into $k$ bits
- PCM line coding (NRZ)

**Step 1 — Per-channel sampling rate:**

Each channel must be sampled at $f_s = 2 f_m$ to satisfy Nyquist.

**Step 2 — Samples per second on the multiplexed line:**

$$R_s = N \cdot f_s = 2 N f_m$$

**Step 3 — Bits per second (channel capacity):**

Each sample yields $k$ bits, therefore:

$$R_b = R_s \cdot k = 2 N k f_m \quad \text{[bits/s]}$$

**Numerical Evaluation Example:**

> Suppose a TDM-PCM system aggregates **N = 12** voice channels, each band-limited to **f_m = 4 kHz**, with **k = 8 bits/sample** (μ-law PCM). Compute the bit rate.

$$R_b = 2 \cdot 12 \cdot 8 \cdot 4000 = 768{,}000 \text{ bits/s} = 768 \text{ kbps}$$

This matches the classical **T1 (DS1) digital trunk** standard used in North American telephony.

### 3.3 Derivation: Total Bandwidth of an FDM Hierarchy

**Given:** $N$ SSB-SC voice channels each of bandwidth $f_m = 4$ kHz. Adjacent channels are separated by a guard band $f_{gb} = 0.5$ kHz.

**Step 1 — Bandwidth occupied by one channel (SSB):**

$$BW_1 = f_m = 4 \text{ kHz}$$

**Step 2 — Total spectrum without guard bands:**

$$BW_{no\,gb} = N \cdot BW_1 = 4N \text{ kHz}$$

**Step 3 — Add inter-channel guard bands:** $(N - 1)$ gaps of width $f_{gb}$ exist between $N$ channels.

$$BW_{total} = 4N + (N-1) \cdot 0.5 \text{ kHz}$$

**Step 4 — Numerical Example with N = 600:**

$$BW_{total} = 4 \cdot 600 + 599 \cdot 0.5 = 2400 + 299.5 = 2699.5 \text{ kHz} \approx 2.7 \text{ MHz}$$

This corresponds to the classical **CCITT 600-channel FDM hierarchy** for long-distance telephony.

### 3.4 Derivation: Carson's Rule for FM Bandwidth

**Starting point:** An FM signal has theoretically infinite Bessel-function sideband structure. Carson's rule captures **98% of the total transmitted power**.

For a sinusoidal modulating frequency $f_m$ and peak frequency deviation $\Delta f$:

$$BW_{FM} = 2(\Delta f + f_m)$$

**Step-by-step justification:**

**Step 1** — The modulation index is defined as:

$$\beta = \frac{\Delta f}{f_m}$$

**Step 2** — The number of significant sideband pairs (using Bessel functions $J_n(\beta)$) is approximately $(\beta + 1)$.

**Step 3** — Each pair is spaced $f_m$ Hz above and below the carrier.

**Step 4** — Total spectrum width:

$$BW = 2 \cdot (\beta + 1) \cdot f_m$$

**Step 5** — Substitute $\beta = \Delta f / f_m$:

$$BW = 2 \cdot \left(\frac{\Delta f}{f_m} + 1\right) \cdot f_m = 2(\Delta f + f_m)$$

**Numerical Example:** For broadcast FM, $\Delta f = 75$ kHz, $f_m = 15$ kHz:

$$BW = 2(75 + 15) = 180 \text{ kHz}$$

This justifies the **200 kHz channel spacing** allocated to each FM broadcast station (with a 20 kHz safety guard band).

### 3.5 Derivation: AM Power Distribution and Modulation Efficiency

For a DSB-FC (Double Sideband Full Carrier) signal:

$$s(t) = A_c[1 + \mu \cos(2\pi f_m t)] \cos(2\pi f_c t)$$

Expanding the product (trigonometric identity $\cos A \cos B = \tfrac{1}{2}[\cos(A-B) + \cos(A+B)]$):

$$s(t) = A_c \cos(2\pi f_c t) + \frac{\mu A_c}{2}\cos[2\pi(f_c - f_m)t] + \frac{\mu A_c}{2}\cos[2\pi(f_c + f_m)t]$$

**Step 1 — Carrier power** (across 1 Ω reference load):

$$P_c = \frac{A_c^2}{2}$$

**Step 2 — Total sideband power:**

$$P_{SB} = 2 \cdot \frac{(\mu A_c/2)^2}{2} = \frac{\mu^2 A_c^2}{4}$$

**Step 3 — Total transmitted power:**

$$P_t = P_c + P_{SB} = \frac{A_c^2}{2}\left(1 + \frac{\mu^2}{2}\right) = \frac{A_c^2(2 + \mu^2)}{4}$$

**Step 4 — Modulation efficiency (useful sideband power ratio):**

$$\eta = \frac{P_{SB}}{P_t} = \frac{\mu^2 / 4}{(2 + \mu^2)/4} = \frac{\mu^2}{2 + \mu^2}$$

For maximum undistorted modulation, $\mu = 1$:

$$\eta_{max} = \frac{1}{3} \approx 33.33\%$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This means **at least 66% of the transmitted AM power is wasted in the carrier**, which carries no information — the main reason SSB-SC is preferred for power-limited applications like maritime HF radio.

### 3.6 Antenna Length & Carrier Frequency Trade-off

For a quarter-wave monopole (or half-wave dipole) antenna:

$$L = \frac{\lambda}{4} = \frac{c}{4 f_c}$$

**Numerical Comparison Table:**

| Service | Carrier $f_c$ | Wavelength $\lambda$ | Antenna Length $L = \lambda/4$ |
|---------|---------------|----------------------|--------------------------------|
| VLF submarine comms | 30 kHz | 10 km | **2.5 km** |
| AM broadcast | 1 MHz | 300 m | **75 m** |
| FM broadcast | 100 MHz | 3 m | **75 cm** |
| Cellular 4G | 2.6 GHz | 11.5 cm | **2.9 cm** |
| mm-Wave 5G | 28 GHz | 1.07 cm | **2.7 mm** |

This quantitative comparison demonstrates why **modulation to a higher carrier** is non-negotiable for portable wireless systems.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 TDM Transmitter–Receiver Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Ch 1 Input] --> MUX1
    A2[Ch 2 Input] --> MUX1
    A3[Ch 3 Input] --> MUX1
    A4[Ch N Input] --> MUX1
    MUX1[Synchronous MUX Switch] --> SH[Sample and Hold]
    SH --> ADC[ADC PCM Encoder]
    ADC --> TX[Line Driver / Channel]
    TX --> RX[Line Receiver]
    RX --> DEC[PCM Decoder]
    DEC --> DMUX[Synchronous DEMUX]
    DMUX --> B1[Ch 1 LPF]
    DMUX --> B2[Ch 2 LPF]
    DMUX --> B3[Ch 3 LPF]
    DMUX --> B4[Ch N LPF]
    B1 --> O1[Ch 1 Output]
    B2 --> O2[Ch 2 Output]
    B3 --> O3[Ch 3 Output]
    B4 --> O4[Ch N Output]
    SYNC1[Bit Clock Generator] --> MUX1
    SYNC1 --> DMUX
```

> [!NOTE]
> In a real PCM-TDM link, the ADC is shared (single high-speed converter sequentially digitises each channel), and a **frame synchronisation word** is appended at the end of every $N$-sample group so the demultiplexer at the receiver can identify channel boundaries unambiguously.

### 4.2 FDM Hierarchy — Multiplexer Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Channel Bank
        S1[Signal 1] --> F1[LPF Anti Alias]
        S2[Signal 2] --> F2[LPF Anti Alias]
        SN[Signal N] --> FN[LPF Anti Alias]
        F1 --> MOD1[Modulator Subcarrier 1]
        F2 --> MOD2[Modulator Subcarrier 2]
        FN --> MODN[Modulator Subcarrier N]
        MOD1 --> SUM[Linear Summer]
        MOD2 --> SUM
        MODN --> SUM
    end
    SUM --> CH[Common Transmission Channel]
    CH --> RCVR[Receiver Input]
    RCVR --> BPF1[BPF Centre f1]
    RCVR --> BPF2[BPF Centre f2]
    RCVR --> BPFN[BPF Centre fN]
    BPF1 --> DEM1[Coherent Demod 1]
    BPF2 --> DEM2[Coherent Demod 2]
    BPFN --> DEMN[Coherent Demod N]
    DEM1 --> OUT1[Recovered Signal 1]
    DEM2 --> OUT2[Recovered Signal 2]
    DEMN --> OUTN[Recovered Signal N]
```

### 4.3 AM Transmitter — Functional Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    M[Microphone Audio Source] --> PA[Pre Amplifier]
    PA --> AMP[Modulator Mixer]
    OSC[Crystal Oscillator Carrier fc] --> AMP
    AMP --> PA2[Power Amplifier RF]
    PA2 --> MATCH[Impedance Matching Network]
    MATCH --> ANT[Antenna Lambda by 4]
    ANT -. Electromagnetic Wave .-> PROP[(Free Space Propagation)]
```

### 4.4 Superheterodyne AM Receiver — Functional Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    ANT[Antenna] --> RFAMP[RF Tuned Amplifier]
    RFAMP --> MIX[Mixer Frequency Translator]
    LO[Local Oscillator Variable fc plus 455 kHz] --> MIX
    MIX --> IF[IF Amplifier 455 kHz Fixed]
    IF --> DET[Envelope Detector Diode plus RC]
    DET --> AGC[AGC Feedback to RF and IF]
    AGC --> IF
    DET --> AF[Audio Amplifier]
    AF --> SPK[Loudspeaker Output]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **Intermediate Frequency (IF) of 455 kHz** for AM (and **10.7 MHz** for FM) was a historic design choice of Edwin Armstrong. By converting every incoming RF to a single fixed IF, the bulk of the gain and selectivity can be obtained at one frequency using cheap, stable, fixed-tuned IF transformers — a key reason the superheterodyne architecture has dominated radio for over a century.

### 4.5 Comparison: TDM vs FDM Spectrum Usage

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Time Domain
        TD1[Frame: Slot1 Sig1] --> TD2[Slot2 Sig2]
        TD2 --> TD3[Slot3 Sig3]
        TD3 --> TDN[SlotN SigN]
        TDN --> TSY[Sync Pulse]
    end
    subgraph Frequency Domain
        FD1[Channel 1 Band f1] --> FGB1[Guard Band]
        FGB1 --> FD2[Channel 2 Band f2]
        FD2 --> FGB2[Guard Band]
        FGB2 --> FD3[Channel 3 Band f3]
        FD3 --> FGBN[Guard Band]
        FGBN --> FDN[Channel N Band fN]
    end
```

### 4.6 Adaptive Analytic Matrix — TDM vs FDM vs WDM

| Attribute | TDM | FDM | WDM (Optical) |
|-----------|-----|-----|---------------|
| Resource Divided | Time slots | Frequency bands | Light wavelengths |
| Channels simultaneous | One at a time | All simultaneously | All simultaneously |
| Cross-talk cause | Slot overlap (switching aperture) | Insufficient filter roll-off | Imperfect optical filters |
| Bandwidth efficiency | High (no guard time) | Lower (guard bands needed) | Highest (no guard) |
| Synchronisation need | Critical | Not needed at TX | Not needed at TX |
| Typical use | Telephony T1/E1 | FM radio, cable TV | Long-haul fibre backbone |
| Complexity | Low to moderate | Moderate | High (laser + optics) |
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!NOTE]
> All questions below are framed strictly per the **KTU 2024 Scheme End Semester Evaluation (ESE)** pattern: Part A carries 3-mark short answers; Part B carries 14-mark descriptive problems with **internal choice** between two sub-options.

---

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Q1.** [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**Define multiplexing. Differentiate between TDM and FDM in any three aspects.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** Multiplexing is the technique of transmitting multiple independent signals simultaneously over a single communication channel by allocating separable physical resources (time, frequency, or code) to each signal.

**Differentiation (2 marks — any three points):**

| Aspect | TDM | FDM |
|--------|-----|-----|
| Resource shared | Time slots | Frequency bands |
| Operation mode | Sequential (one channel at a time) | Simultaneous (all at once) |
| Synchronisation | Essential at TX and RX | Not strictly required |
| Crosstalk cause | Imperfect switch aperture | Insufficient filter selectivity |
| Guard bands | Not needed between slots | Needed between frequency bands |

#### **Q2.** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**State the Nyquist sampling theorem and explain its significance in TDM.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Statement (1 mark):** A band-limited signal of highest frequency component $f_m$ Hz can be completely reconstructed from its instantaneous samples provided the sampling frequency satisfies $f_s \geq 2 f_m$.

**Significance in TDM (2 marks):**
- Sets the **minimum commutator rotation rate** of the TDM multiplexer.
- Guarantees **alias-free reconstruction** at the demultiplexer output.
- Directly determines the **frame duration** $T_f = N / f_s$ and the composite bit rate.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Descriptive Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – July 2024, Model Paper Adapted]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **principle of operation of a Time Division Multiplexing (TDM) system** for transmitting **N** band-limited signals over a common channel. Describe the function of the commutator, the sample-and-hold circuit, and the synchronisation pulse. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A TDM-PCM system multiplexes **16 voice channels**, each band-limited to **3.4 kHz**. Each sample is quantised into **8 bits**. Calculate:
  (i) The minimum sampling frequency per channel.
  (ii) The duration of one time slot.
  (iii) The frame duration.
  (iv) The transmission bit rate.
  (v) If a synchronising pulse of width equal to one data bit is added per frame, what is the **revised bit rate**? **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Block Diagram & Explanation (7 marks):**

The TDM system consists of:

1. **N input channels** feeding **band-limiting low-pass filters** (anti-aliasing).
2. A **high-speed electronic commutator** (analogue MUX such as CD4051) rotating at sampling rate $f_s$.
3. A **Sample-and-Hold (S/H) amplifier** to maintain constant amplitude during the slot aperture.
4. A **synchronisation pulse generator** inserting a frame marker.
5. A **common transmission channel**.
6. At the receiver, a **synchronised de-commutator (DEMUX)** and **N reconstruction LPFs**.

**Commutator function:** Sequential rotation connects one channel at a time to the output, producing interleaved PAM samples.

**Sample-and-Hold function:** Eliminates amplitude droop during aperture time and provides stable input to subsequent ADC.

**Synchronisation pulse function:** Identifies the start of each frame, enabling the receiver demultiplexer to align its rotation with the transmitter commutator.

**Diagram (4 marks for diagram + 3 marks for explanation):**

```
Inputs x1 x2 ... xN
   ↓    ↓      ↓
   [MUX Switch — rotates at fs]
            ↓
      [Sample & Hold]
            ↓
      [Optional ADC/Line Coder]
            ↓
      [CHANNEL → RECEIVER]
            ↓
      [Sync Separator]
            ↓
      [DEMUX Switch]
   ↓    ↓      ↓
  LPF1 LPF2 ... LPFN
   ↓    ↓      ↓
  Out1 Out2 ... OutN
```

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks — 1.4 marks each):**

**Given:** $N = 16$, $f_m = 3.4$ kHz, $k = 8$ bits/sample.

**(i) Minimum sampling frequency per channel:**

$$f_s = 2 f_m = 2 \times 3.4 \text{ kHz} = \mathbf{6.8 \text{ kHz}}$$

**(ii) Duration of one time slot:**

$$\tau = \frac{1}{N \cdot f_s} = \frac{1}{16 \times 6800} = \frac{1}{108{,}800} \approx \mathbf{9.19 \text{ μs}}$$

**(iii) Frame duration:**

$$T_f = \frac{1}{f_s} = \frac{1}{6800} \approx \mathbf{0.147 \text{ ms}} = 147 \text{ μs}$$

(Equivalently $T_f = N \cdot \tau = 16 \times 9.19 = 147$ μs ✓)

**(iv) Transmission bit rate:**

$$R_b = N \cdot f_s \cdot k = 16 \times 6800 \times 8 = \mathbf{870{,}400 \text{ bps}} = 870.4 \text{ kbps}$$

**(v) Revised bit rate with sync pulse:**

Total bits per frame = $N \cdot k + 1 = 16 \times 8 + 1 = 129$ bits

$$R_{b,\text{new}} = 129 \times f_s = 129 \times 6800 = \mathbf{877{,}200 \text{ bps}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Pitfall — KTU Valuation Pattern:** Students very frequently forget to multiply by **k** when computing the bit rate for a PCM-TDM link, quoting only the sample rate $N \cdot f_s$ instead of $N \cdot f_s \cdot k$. This loses **2 marks outright**. Always re-read: if PCM is mentioned, multiply by the number of bits per sample.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Internal Alternative**

**[KTU University Exam – May 2023, Adapted]**

**(a)** With the help of a clear block diagram, explain the **principle of Frequency Division Multiplexing (FDM)**. Discuss the role of **subcarriers, guard bands, and band-pass filters** in the system. Compare FDM with TDM in terms of bandwidth utilisation and synchronisation requirement. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An FDM system uses **SSB-SC modulation** to multiplex **600 telephone channels**, each band-limited to **4 kHz**. The **guard band** between adjacent channels is **500 Hz**.
  (i) Calculate the **total baseband spectrum** occupied by the multiplexed output.
  (ii) If the system uses a **final RF up-conversion** with carrier $f_{RF} = 100$ MHz, determine the **RF bandwidth** and the **upper and lower cutoff frequencies** of the composite FDM signal.
  (iii) State Carson's rule and compute the FM broadcast bandwidth for a station with $\Delta f = 75$ kHz and $f_m = 15$ kHz. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) FDM Block Diagram & Explanation (7 marks):**

**Block Diagram (3 marks):**

```
Input 1 → LPF → Mod 1 (sc1) ──┐
Input 2 → LPF → Mod 2 (sc2) ──┤
  ...                          ├─→ [Linear Summer] → Channel → [BPF bank] → Demod → Output
Input N → LPF → Mod N (scN) ──┘                              ↑
                                                            [sc1...scN from LO]
```

**Explanation of key elements (3 marks):**

- **Subcarriers:** Each input modulates a distinct sinusoidal subcarrier $f_i$, translating its spectrum to $[f_i - f_m, f_i + f_m]$. Enables simultaneous transmission.
- **Guard bands:** Unused spectrum between adjacent translated channels to prevent inter-channel interference due to imperfect filter roll-off.
- **Band-pass filters at the receiver:** Separate each translated channel by selecting only its assigned frequency band, enabling coherent demodulation.

**Comparison Table (1 mark):**

| Parameter | TDM | FDM |
|-----------|-----|-----|
| Bandwidth utilisation | Better (no guard bands) | Wastes spectrum in guard bands |
| Synchronisation | Mandatory (TX-RX lock) | Not needed |
| Nonlinear distortion tolerance | High | Low (FDM linear amplifiers expensive) |

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**(i) Total baseband bandwidth (3 marks):**

SSB bandwidth per channel = $f_m = 4$ kHz.
Total without guard bands: $600 \times 4 = 2400$ kHz.
Number of guard bands: $600 - 1 = 599$.

$$BW_{total} = 600 \times 4 + 599 \times 0.5 = 2400 + 299.5 = \mathbf{2699.5 \text{ kHz}} \approx 2.7 \text{ MHz}$$

**(ii) RF up-conversion (2 marks):**

The composite baseband spans 0 to 2.7 MHz. After up-conversion with $f_{RF} = 100$ MHz:

$$BW_{RF} = BW_{baseband} = \mathbf{2.7 \text{ MHz}}$$

Lower cutoff:

$$f_L = f_{RF} = 100 \text{ MHz}$$

Upper cutoff:

$$f_U = f_{RF} + BW_{total} = 100 + 2.7 = \mathbf{102.7 \text{ MHz}}$$

The composite signal occupies the band **100 MHz – 102.7 MHz**.

**(iii) Carson's rule (2 marks):**

**Statement:** Carson's rule states that the FM bandwidth required to contain approximately **98% of the total transmitted power** is:

$$BW_{FM} = 2(\Delta f + f_m)$$

For $\Delta f = 75$ kHz and $f_m = 15$ kHz:

$$BW_{FM} = 2(75 + 15) = \mathbf{180 \text{ kHz}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Valuation Mistakes:** (1) Mixing up DSB bandwidth ($2 f_m$) with SSB bandwidth ($f_m$) — always read the modulation scheme. (2) In FDM hierarchies, students often omit the **$-1$** when counting guard bands; remember that *between* $N$ channels there are only $N-1$ gaps. (3) For Carson's rule, students sometimes write $2\Delta f$ alone — forgetting the $+ f_m$ term is mandatory.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU University Exam**

- **Multiplexing** = sharing one channel among many; TDM = time-shared, FDM = frequency-shared.
- **Nyquist Rate** $f_s = 2 f_m$ is the foundation of every TDM system.
- **TDM slot width** $\tau = 1 / (N f_s)$; **frame time** $T_f = N / f_s$.
- **PCM-TDM bit rate** $R_b = 2 N k f_m$ — remember the **$k$** factor.
- **FDM guard bands** $(N-1) \cdot f_{gb}$ consume spectrum but are essential for low crosstalk.
- **AM modulation index** $\mu \leq 1$; maximum efficiency of DSB-FC = **33.33%** (so prefer SSB for power efficiency).
- **AM bandwidth** = $2 f_m$ (DSB) or $f_m$ (SSB).
- **Carson's Rule** $BW_{FM} = 2(\Delta f + f_m)$ — applies to **both NBFM and WBFM**.
- **Antenna length** $L = \lambda / 4 = c / (4 f_c)$ — justifies why carrier frequencies must be high for portable radios.
- **Superheterodyne IF** = **455 kHz** (AM), **10.7 MHz** (FM).
- **Synchronisation pulse** in TDM is mandatory; in FDM, only local oscillator frequency stability is required.
- **Statistical TDM** > Synchronous TDM for bursty traffic; adds address overhead.
- **Cross-talk cures:** faster S/H for TDM; steeper BPFs and wider guard bands for FDM.
- **Real-world benchmarks to memorise:** T1 = 1.544 Mbps (24 channels); E1 = 2.048 Mbps (32 channels); FM broadcast spacing = 200 kHz; AM broadcast spacing = 10 kHz.
- Always remember to **convert kHz to Hz** (multiply by 1000) when computing bit rates in bits per second.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: WLAN Architecture
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET411 - ELECTRONIC INSTRUMENTATION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** WLAN Architecture

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

**Wireless Local Area Network (WLAN)** is a data transmission system that uses high-frequency radio waves (typically in the **2.4 GHz**, **5 GHz**, and **6 GHz** ISM bands) instead of copper or optical cables to provide local area network connectivity among electronic instrumentation devices, sensors, and host computers. As per the KTU 2024 Electronic Instrumentation syllabus (Module 2), WLAN Architecture refers to the standardized logical and physical organization of components defined under the **IEEE 802.11 family of standards**, which governs the Physical (PHY) and Medium Access Control (MAC) layers of wireless communication.

The WLAN architecture defines how **Stations (STA)**, **Access Points (AP)**, **Basic Service Sets (BSS)**, **Distribution Systems (DS)**, and **Extended Service Sets (ESS)** interoperate to deliver reliable, secure, and high-throughput wireless telemetry — a cornerstone of modern distributed instrumentation, IoT-enabled test beds, and remote laboratory automation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** For PEEET411, WLAN Architecture is studied not as a pure networking subject, but as a **wireless instrumentation bus** — replacing traditional wired buses (RS-485, GPIB) with RF links to interconnect distributed transducers, data loggers, and SCADA nodes.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a busy **hospital ward** where nurses (the *instruments/sensors*) need to send patient vitals (the *data*) to a central monitoring station (the *server*). Instead of running long cables from every bed to the monitor, the hospital installs a **wireless intercom system** at each bedside that transmits voice/data over radio to a **central base station** (the *Access Point*). The base station then forwards the data to the **nurse station's display**.

In this analogy:
- The **nurse with the intercom** = Wireless Station (STA) — a laptop, smartphone, smart sensor, or instrument with a Wi-Fi radio.
- The **central base station** = Access Point (AP) — the Wi-Fi router/hotspot.
- The **ward itself** = Basic Service Set (BSS) — the radio coverage area (cell).
- The **corridor connecting multiple wards** = Distribution System (DS) — typically the wired Ethernet backbone.
- The **entire hospital floor** = Extended Service Set (ESS) — multiple BSSs interconnected.

This is exactly how a WLAN replaces wired data acquisition lines in a modern **electronic instrumentation system**.

## 1.3 Key Standardized Metrics & Physical Constants

The following constants and metrics are repeatedly referenced in KTU board questions on WLAN:

- **Speed of light:** $c = 3 \times 10^8 \text{ m/s}$
- **Free-space path loss reference frequency:** $f_c$ typically in **GHz**
- **ISM Bands of operation:** **2.4 GHz (2.400–2.4835 GHz)**, **5 GHz (5.15–5.825 GHz)**, **6 GHz (5.925–7.125 GHz, Wi-Fi 6E)**
- **Maximum EIRP (Equivalent Isotropically Radiated Power):** typically limited to **20 dBm (100 mW)** in the 2.4 GHz band (region-dependent)
- **Standard families:** IEEE 802.11a/b/g/n/ac/ax — each defining a distinct PHY and theoretical maximum throughput.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Tip:** When a question mentions "WLAN Architecture" in the ESE, the examiner expects you to draw a labeled block diagram showing STA → AP → DS → ESS and define the relationship between BSS, ESS, and DS. Memorize these acronyms cold.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Hierarchical Building Blocks of WLAN Architecture

The IEEE 802.11 architecture is built from the following layered components, in increasing order of scope:

### 2.1.1 Station (STA)
- Any device containing an **IEEE 802.11-compliant MAC and PHY interface**.
- Examples in instrumentation: wireless pressure transmitters, Wi-Fi-enabled oscilloscopes, smart multimeters, IoT data loggers.
- Two classes: **(i) Access Point (AP)** — a stationary infrastructure device, and **(ii) Non-AP STA** — the client/mobile instrumentation node.

### 2.1.2 Basic Service Set (BSS)
- The **fundamental building block** of an 802.11 network.
- Defined as the set of STAs that communicate with each other through a single coordinating AP (in infrastructure mode), or directly with each other (in **ad hoc / IBSS mode**).
- Geometrically, the BSS is the **radio coverage cell** bounded by the AP's transmission range.
- Each BSS is uniquely identified by a **6-byte BSSID**, usually set to the AP's MAC address.

### 2.1.3 Distribution System (DS)
- The **backhaul network** that interconnects multiple BSSs.
- In practice, the DS is the **wired Ethernet backbone** of an organization.
- It carries traffic between APs and bridges the WLAN to external networks (LAN, WAN, Internet).
- Logical concept defined by the standard; the DS is *not* defined as wireless in classic 802.11, though modern meshed systems (802.11s) blur this.

### 2.1.4 Extended Service Set (ESS)
- A **collection of one or more BSSs interconnected via a single DS**, presenting the same **SSID (Service Set Identifier)** to upper layers.
- From the client's perspective, the entire ESS looks like a single, seamless network.
- Enables **roaming**: a STA can move between BSSs within an ESS without losing the application session.

### 2.1.5 Independent Basic Service Set (IBSS) / Ad-hoc Network
- A **peer-to-peer WLAN** with no AP and no DS.
- Two or more STAs communicate directly using a randomly chosen BSSID and a shared SSID.
- Used in instrumentation for **ad hoc data acquisition clusters** (e.g., field-deployed sensor arrays).

## 2.2 Operational Logic — The "Why" and "How"

The architecture is designed around three engineering realities:
1. **Why BSS?** Because radio is a **shared medium** — only one STA should transmit at a time inside a cell to avoid collisions. The AP acts as a **central coordinator** (or at least a beacon source) to mediate access via the DCF (Distributed Coordination Function) / PCF (Point Coordination Function).
2. **Why DS + ESS?** Because real-world facilities (a plant floor, a smart factory, a hospital) have **coverage holes** when using a single AP. Multiple APs on a wired DS extend coverage while preserving a single network identity (SSID) for seamless roaming.
3. **Why IBSS?** Because some applications — like **portable calibration rigs** or **field telemetry** — need a network that can be spun up *without infrastructure*. IBSS gives you that flexibility.

## 2.3 PHY and MAC Layer Highlights (KTU-Favorite)

- **PHY Modulations (chronological):** DSSS (802.11b) → OFDM (802.11a/g) → MIMO-OFDM (802.11n) → MU-MIMO + 256-QAM (802.11ac) → OFDMA + 1024-QAM (802.11ax/Wi-Fi 6).
- **MAC Protocol:** CSMA/CA (Carrier Sense Multiple Access with Collision Avoidance) — fundamental for instrumentation students to remember.
- **Security Evolution:** WEP → WPA → WPA2 (AES-CCMP) → WPA3 (SAE handshake).

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Quantity | Expression | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Free-space path loss (Friis) | $FSPL = 20\log_{10}(d) + 20\log_{10}(f_c) + 20\log_{10}\left(\dfrac{4\pi}{c}\right)$ | dB; $d$ in m, $f_c$ in Hz |
| 2 | Simplified Friis (d in km, f in MHz) | $FSPL = 32.44 + 20\log_{10}(d) + 20\log_{10}(f_c)$ | dB |
| 3 | Received power (link budget) | $P_r = P_t + G_t + G_r - FSPL - L_{misc}$ | dBm / dB |
| 4 | EIRP | $EIRP = P_t + G_t - L_{feed}$ | dBm |
| 5 | Shannon-Hartley capacity | $C = B \cdot \log_2(1 + \text{SNR})$ | bits/s; $B$ in Hz |
| 6 | SNR (linear → dB) | $\text{SNR}_{dB} = 10\log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{P_{signal}}{P_{noise}}\right)$ | dB |
| 7 | Thermal noise floor | $N = kTB$ | W; $k = 1.38 \times 10^{-23}$ J/K |
| 8 | Noise floor in dBm (20 MHz ch.) | $N_{dBm} = -174 + 10\log_{10}(B) \approx -101$ | dBm |
| 9 | Number of non-overlapping 2.4 GHz channels | **3** (channels 1, 6, 11) | dimensionless |
| 10 | CSMA/CA backoff slot | $CW = \text{random}(0, W-1)$ | slots; $W$ = contention window |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical LaTeX Reminder for Tables:** Notice the use of `\vert` and `\dfrac` inside the table cells above. **Never** use the bare pipe `|` symbol inside markdown table cells — it breaks the table parser. The pipe is reserved for column separators only.

## 2.5 Real-World Utility in Engineering

In electronic instrumentation, WLAN architecture is the **physical layer of choice** for:
- **Smart factories (Industry 4.0):** wireless sensor networks reporting vibration, temperature, and current to a central SCADA.
- **Wireless test & measurement:** oscilloscopes, spectrum analyzers, and LCR meters streaming waveform data to a remote engineer's laptop.
- **Medical telemetry:** ECG, SpO₂, and infusion pump data over Wi-Fi to a central nursing dashboard.
- **Environmental monitoring:** distributed air-quality and structural-health sensors in smart buildings.

The transition from wired to wireless instrumentation **reduces cabling cost, allows mobility, and enables deployment in hazardous or rotating environments** where slip rings and cables are impractical.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Friis Free-Space Path Loss (FSPL) — The Workhorse of WLAN Link Budgets

### Step 1 — Start with the Isotropic Radiator
An ideal isotropic antenna radiates power $P_t$ uniformly in all directions. At a distance $d$, this power is spread over the surface of a sphere of area $4\pi d^2$. The power flux density (power per unit area) is therefore:

$$S = \frac{P_t}{4\pi d^2}$$

**Reasoning:** Isotropic means equal power in every direction; geometry dictates the spreading.

### Step 2 — Account for the Real Antennas
A transmit antenna with gain $G_t$ concentrates this flux in the direction of the receiver. A receive antenna with effective aperture $A_e$ captures a portion of the wavefront:

$$A_e = \frac{G_r \lambda^2}{4\pi}$$

**Reasoning:** The aperture-gain relation is fundamental antenna theory; $\lambda$ is the wavelength.

### Step 3 — Received Power
Multiplying flux density by the receive aperture gives the power delivered to the receiver's terminals:

$$P_r = S \cdot A_e = \frac{P_t \, G_t \, G_r \, \lambda^2}{(4\pi d)^2}$$

**Reasoning:** Substitute $S$ and $A_e$ from Steps 1 and 2; the $4\pi$'s multiply to give $(4\pi d)^2$.

### Step 4 — Convert Wavelength to Frequency
Since $\lambda = c / f_c$, substitute and rearrange:

$$P_r = P_t \, G_t \, G_r \left(\frac{c}{4\pi d f_c}\right)^2$$

**Reasoning:** Expressing in frequency is more practical for a 2.4 GHz / 5 GHz Wi-Fi link.

### Step 5 — Define Free-Space Path Loss
Path loss is the ratio $P_t / P_r$ (when $G_t = G_r = 1$). Taking $10\log_{10}$ converts to dB:

$$\frac{P_t}{P_r} = \left(\frac{4\pi d f_c}{c}\right)^2$$

$$FSPL_{dB} = 20\log_{10}(d) + 20\log_{10}(f_c) + 20\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{4\pi}{c}\right)$$

**Reasoning:** Each squared term contributes a $20\log$ factor; the constant $20\log_{10}(4\pi/c) \approx -147.55$ dB when $d$ is in meters and $f_c$ in Hz.

### Step 6 — Practical Engineering Form
To make calculations easier, evaluate the constant for $d$ in **kilometers** and $f_c$ in **MHz**, which yields the beloved KTU-friendly form:

$$FSPL_{dB} = 32.44 + 20\log_{10}(d_{km}) + 20\log_{10}(f_{c,MHz})$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Board Tip:** Examiners love plugging numbers into this. Memorize the constant **32.44** along with its units (d, km; f, MHz).

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (Typical 2-Mark Sub-Part)

**Problem:** A 2.4 GHz Wi-Fi AP transmits at **+20 dBm** with an antenna gain of **+2 dBi**. The receiver is a wireless sensor **50 m** away with an antenna gain of **+0 dBi**. Assume free-space propagation. Calculate the received power in dBm.

### Solution

**Step A — Convert to dBm (linear not needed, we work entirely in dB):**
$P_t = +20$ dBm, $G_t = +2$ dBi, $G_r = 0$ dBi.

**Step B — Calculate FSPL:**
For $d = 50$ m = 0.05 km, $f_c = 2400$ MHz:

$$FSPL = 32.44 + 20\log_{10}(0.05) + 20\log_{10}(2400)$$

$$= 32.44 + 20(-1.301) + 20(3.380)$$

$$= 32.44 - 26.02 + 67.61$$

$$= 74.03 \text{ dB}$$

**Reasoning:** Use the simplified form; convert d to km and $f_c$ to MHz before plugging in.

**Step C — Apply the link-budget equation:**

$$P_r = P_t + G_t + G_r - FSPL$$

$$= 20 + 2 + 0 - 74.03 = -52.03 \text{ dBm}$$

**Step D — Interpretation:**
A typical Wi-Fi receiver sensitivity at 2.4 GHz, 802.11n, MCS7 is around **−65 dBm**. Since −52 dBm > −65 dBm, the link has ~13 dB of margin — **comfortable** for an instrumentation link.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Comparing the 802.11 Standards

This matrix is a high-yield revision tool. The KTU board may present a comparison sub-question worth **7 marks**.

| Standard | Year | Band (GHz) | Max PHY Rate (theoretical) | Modulation | Channel BW | MIMO | Typical Use in Instrumentation |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 802.11 (legacy) | 1997 | 2.4 | **2 Mbps** | DSSS / FHSS | 20 MHz | No | Historical; obsolete |
| 802.11b | 1999 | 2.4 | **11 Mbps** | DSSS / CCK | 20 MHz | No | Legacy wireless sensors |
| 802.11a | 1999 | **5** | 54 Mbps | OFDM | 20 MHz | No | Clean RF environment, short range |
| 802.11g | 2003 | 2.4 | 54 Mbps | OFDM | 20 MHz | No | Mixed legacy/new sensors |
| 802.11n (Wi-Fi 4) | 2009 | 2.4 / 5 | **600 Mbps** | MIMO-OFDM | 20 / 40 MHz | Up to 4×4 | High-throughput data loggers |
| 802.11ac (Wi-Fi 5) | 2014 | **5** | 6.93 Gbps | MU-MIMO / OFDM | 80 / 160 MHz | Up to 8×8 | Video-grade instrumentation |
| 802.11ax (Wi-Fi 6/6E) | 2019/2020 | 2.4 / 5 / **6** | 9.6 Gbps | OFDMA / 1024-QAM | 160 MHz | 8×8 MU-MIMO | IIoT, dense sensor deployments |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why the modulations differ:** Lower-frequency bands (2.4 GHz) propagate further but are crowded (microwave ovens, Bluetooth). Higher bands (5/6 GHz) are cleaner but have shorter range. OFDM splits the channel into many narrow subcarriers to combat multipath — critical in industrial environments with metal reflections.

## 3.4 Step-by-Step: Estimating Cell Radius Using SNR

**Given:** Transmit power $P_t$ = 20 dBm, $G_t = G_r = 0$ dBi, noise floor = −101 dBm (20 MHz channel at 290 K), required SNR for 54 Mbps (OFDM) = 25 dB.

**Step 1:** Minimum received power needed:

$$P_{r,min} = \text{Noise floor} + \text{Required SNR} = -101 + 25 = -76 \text{ dBm}$$

**Step 2:** Maximum allowable path loss:

$$FSPL_{max} = P_t + G_t + G_r - P_{r,min} = 20 + 0 + 0 - (-76) = 96 \text{ dB}$$

**Step 3:** Solve Friis for $d$ at $f_c = 2.4$ GHz (2400 MHz):

$$96 = 32.44 + 20\log_{10}(d_{km}) + 20\log_{10}(2400)$$

$$96 = 32.44 + 20\log_{10}(d_{km}) + 67.60$$

$$20\log_{10}(d_{km}) = 96 - 100.04 = -4.04$$

$$\log_{10}(d_{km}) = -0.202 \;\Rightarrow\; d_{km} = 10^{-0.202} \approx 0.629 \text{ km}$$

$$d \approx 629 \text{ meters (free-space, unrealistic in practice)}$$

**Step 4:** Apply an **outdoor/indoor margin** of ~20 dB for walls, multipath, and fading:

$$FSPL_{allow} = 96 - 20 = 76 \text{ dB}$$

Recompute: $20\log_{10}(d) = 76 - 100.04 = -24.04$ → $d \approx 0.063$ km ≈ **63 meters indoor**, which is a realistic Wi-Fi cell radius in a typical instrumentation facility.

**Reasoning:** This margin-based approach is how radio planners design indoor plant Wi-Fi coverage for distributed sensors.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow (Mermaid)

The following diagram renders the **functional flow of an Infrastructure WLAN Architecture** as a 802.11-compliant block diagram. It traces the data path from a wireless instrumentation node (STA), through the radio medium, into the Access Point, across the wired Distribution System, and into an upstream network — culminating in a centralized data acquisition server.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA["STA1: WIRELESS SENSOR / INSTRUMENT NODE"]
    nodeB["STA2: WIRELESS SENSOR / INSTRUMENT NODE"]
    nodeC["STA3: WIRELESS SENSOR / INSTRUMENT NODE"]
    nodeD["AP1: ACCESS POINT 1 - BSS1 CONTROLLER"]
    nodeE["AP2: ACCESS POINT 2 - BSS2 CONTROLLER"]
    nodeF["DS: DISTRIBUTION SYSTEM - WIRED ETHERNET BACKBONE"]
    nodeG["GATEWAY: ROUTER TO LAN OR INTERNET"]
    nodeH["SERVER: DATA ACQUISITION AND SCADA HOST"]
    nodeI["SSID: NETWORK IDENTITY BROADCAST BY ALL APS"]

    nodeA -- 802.11 RADIO LINK --> nodeD
    nodeB -- 802.11 RADIO LINK --> nodeD
    nodeC -- 802.11 RADIO LINK --> nodeE

    nodeD -- 802.3 ETHERNET --> nodeF
    nodeE -- 802.3 ETHERNET --> nodeF

    nodeF -- UPLINK --> nodeG
    nodeG -- TCP/IP --> nodeH

    nodeI -. BEACON FRAMES .-> nodeD
    nodeI -. BEACON FRAMES .-> nodeE

    style nodeA fill:#cfe2ff,stroke:#0b5ed7
    style nodeB fill:#cfe2ff,stroke:#0b5ed7
    style nodeC fill:#cfe2ff,stroke:#0b5ed7
    style nodeD fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#cc9a06
    style nodeE fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#cc9a06
    style nodeF fill:#d1e7dd,stroke:#146c43
    style nodeG fill:#d1e7dd,stroke:#146c43
    style nodeH fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#b02a37
    style nodeI fill:#e2e3e5,stroke:#41464b
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- **Blue nodes** = non-AP STAs (the instruments/sensors).
- **Yellow nodes** = Access Points forming BSS1 and BSS2.
- **Green nodes** = wired Distribution System and the gateway.
- **Red node** = upstream data server (where the measurement data ultimately ends up).
- **Dashed lines** = beacon/management frames broadcasting the SSID — these are what allow a STA to discover the network.

## 4.2 ESS / BSS / DS Relationship Diagram (Mermaid)

This second diagram clarifies the **logical nesting** of the network primitives — BSS ⊂ ESS, with DS connecting BSSs and gateway as the exit point to the outside world.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph ESS_NODE["ESS: EXTENDED SERVICE SET - SSID EQUALS LAB-WIFI"]
        subgraph BSS1_NODE["BSS1 - BSSID 00-1A-2B-3C-4D-5E"]
            STA_A["STA A: WIRELESS OSCILLOSCOPE"]
            STA_B["STA B: WIRELESS DATA LOGGER"]
            AP_X["AP X: ACCESS POINT - LAB ZONE 1"]
        end
        subgraph BSS2_NODE["BSS2 - BSSID 00-1A-2B-3C-4D-5F"]
            STA_C["STA C: WIRELESS MULTIMETER"]
            STA_D["STA D: WIRELESS PRESSURE TX"]
            AP_Y["AP Y: ACCESS POINT - LAB ZONE 2"]
        end
        DS_NODE["DS: DISTRIBUTION SYSTEM - GIGABIT ETHERNET"]
    end
    GATE["WAN GATEWAY: ROUTER TO INTERNET OR CORPORATE LAN"]

    STA_A -. ASSOCIATION .-> AP_X
    STA_B -. ASSOCIATION .-> AP_X
    STA_C -. ASSOCIATION .-> AP_Y
    STA_D -. ASSOCIATION .-> AP_Y

    AP_X --- DS_NODE
    AP_Y --- DS_NODE
    DS_NODE --- GATE

    style ESS_NODE fill:#e7f1ff,stroke:#0b5ed7,stroke-width:2px
    style BSS1_NODE fill:#fff8e1,stroke:#cc9a06
    style BSS2_NODE fill:#fff8e1,stroke:#cc9a06
    style DS_NODE fill:#d1e7dd,stroke:#146c43
    style GATE fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#b02a37
```

**Why this matters for the KTU exam:** When asked *"With a neat block diagram, explain the architecture of WLAN,"* this is exactly the structure examiners expect. Memorize the **nesting**: BSS ⊂ ESS, AP ∈ BSS, DS connects APs, ESS is presented as one logical network to upper layers.

## 4.3 Ad-hoc (IBSS) Architecture — For Comparison

The KTU 2024 syllabus also expects you to contrast infrastructure mode with **ad-hoc mode**, since field-deployable instrumentation often uses both.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    IBSS_TOP["IBSS: INDEPENDENT BASIC SERVICE SET - NO AP, NO DS"]

    subgraph IBSS_TOP
        IBSS_A["STA A: FIELD SENSOR 1"]
        IBSS_B["STA B: FIELD SENSOR 2"]
        IBSS_C["STA C: FIELD SENSOR 3"]
        IBSS_D["STA D: PORTABLE CALIBRATOR"]
    end

    IBSS_A <-- DIRECT 802.11 PEER-TO-PEER --> IBSS_B
    IBSS_B <-- DIRECT 802.11 PEER-TO-PEER --> IBSS_C
    IBSS_C <-- DIRECT 802.11 PEER-TO-PEER --> IBSS_D
    IBSS_D <-- DIRECT 802.11 PEER-TO-PEER --> IBSS_A

    style IBSS_TOP fill:#fde2e2,stroke:#b02a37,stroke-width:2px
    style IBSS_A fill:#cfe2ff,stroke:#0b5ed7
    style IBSS_B fill:#cfe2ff,stroke:#0b5ed7
    style IBSS_C fill:#cfe2ff,stroke:#0b5ed7
    style IBSS_D fill:#cfe2ff,stroke:#0b5ed7
```

**Key contrast to write in your answer:**
- **Infrastructure (BSS):** Centralized, AP-coordinated, easier to manage, better range via DS.
- **Ad-hoc (IBSS):** No infrastructure, peer-to-peer, self-organizing, limited range, used in field calibration and emergency instrumentation.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. Define WLAN. List the IEEE 802.11 standards and explain the role of an Access Point. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A Wireless Local Area Network (WLAN) is a local area network that uses **high-frequency radio waves (typically 2.4 GHz, 5 GHz, or 6 GHz)** for data communication among distributed devices, eliminating the need for physical cabling. It is governed by the **IEEE 802.11 family** of standards (802.11a, b, g, n, ac, ax). An **Access Point (AP)** acts as the central coordinator of a Basic Service Set (BSS), providing radio coverage, performing authentication, bridging wireless STAs to the wired Distribution System (DS), and broadcasting the SSID via beacon frames. *(1 mark for definition, 1 mark for standards, 1 mark for AP role.)*

### Q2. Differentiate between BSS, IBSS, and ESS with a suitable diagram. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **BSS (Basic Service Set):** A group of STAs communicating via a single AP; the fundamental 802.11 building block. *(1 mark)*
- **IBSS (Independent Basic Service Set):** An ad-hoc, peer-to-peer network with **no AP and no DS**; STAs communicate directly. Used in field instrumentation. *(1 mark)*
- **ESS (Extended Service Set):** A collection of **two or more BSSs interconnected through a Distribution System (DS)** and presenting a common SSID, enabling roaming across cells. *(1 mark)*

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** When answering the 14-mark "WLAN Architecture" question, students **consistently lose 2–3 marks** by: (a) failing to draw the BSS/ESS/DS block diagram with **all three blocks labeled**, (b) confusing BSSID with SSID, (c) omitting the **role of the DCF (CSMA/CA)**, and (d) writing about Wi-Fi standards without mentioning the **2.4/5 GHz ISM bands**. Address each explicitly in your answer.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) — Option 1

**Q. (a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **architecture of a WLAN** as defined by IEEE 802.11. Discuss the roles of STA, AP, BSS, DS, and ESS. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

1. **Introduction (1 mark):** Define WLAN as an IEEE 802.11-compliant radio network operating in the **2.4 GHz / 5 GHz / 6 GHz** ISM bands, used in modern instrumentation to replace wired buses.

2. **Block Diagram (2 marks):** Draw the diagram from Section 4.2 of these notes — clearly showing:
   - STAs inside each BSS.
   - Two APs (one per BSS).
   - DS connecting the APs.
   - ESS as the encompassing boundary labeled with the SSID.
   - Gateway as the egress to the WAN/LAN.

3. **Role of each component (3 marks):**
   - **STA:** Any device with an 802.11 radio. In instrumentation: wireless sensors, smart instruments, laptops.
   - **AP:** Central coordinator; performs DCF/PCF scheduling, beaconing, authentication, and bridges to DS.
   - **BSS:** The radio cell; identified by BSSID (AP's MAC).
   - **DS:** Backbone network (usually wired Ethernet) interconnecting BSSs.
   - **ESS:** All BSSs under a common SSID; supports roaming.

4. **Operational Mechanism (1 mark):** Briefly describe CSMA/CA: a STA senses the channel, waits a DIFS, picks a random backoff, and transmits only if the channel is idle — preventing collisions in the shared RF medium.

---

**Q. (b)** A 2.4 GHz Wi-Fi AP transmits at **+18 dBm** with antenna gain of **+5 dBi**. The receiver is **30 m** away with an antenna gain of **+2 dBi**. Calculate the **free-space path loss** and the **received power**. Assume a cable/connector loss of **2 dB**. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Step 1 — Convert to consistent units:**
- $d = 30$ m $= 0.03$ km
- $f_c = 2400$ MHz

**Step 2 — Apply the simplified Friis equation:**

$$FSPL = 32.44 + 20\log_{10}(0.03) + 20\log_{10}(2400)$$

$$= 32.44 + 20 \times (-1.523) + 20 \times 3.380$$

$$= 32.44 - 30.46 + 67.60$$

$$FSPL = 69.58 \text{ dB} \quad \textbf{(2 marks)}$$

**Step 3 — Apply the link-budget equation:**

$$P_r = P_t + G_t + G_r - FSPL - L_{misc}$$

$$= 18 + 5 + 2 - 69.58 - 2$$

$$P_r = -46.58 \text{ dBm} \quad \textbf{(2 marks)}$$

**Step 4 — Interpretation (1 mark):** A typical 802.11n receiver sensitivity for 54 Mbps is around **−65 dBm**. Since −46.58 dBm is well above this, the link margin is **18.4 dB** — excellent for a high-reliability instrumentation link.

**Step 5 — List assumptions (1 mark):** Free-space propagation, matched-polarization antennas, negligible interference, line-of-sight path.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Option 2

**Q. (a)** Compare the **IEEE 802.11b, 802.11g, 802.11n, and 802.11ac** standards in terms of frequency band, modulation, maximum data rate, and channel bandwidth. Mention at least **two advantages of OFDM over DSSS** in an industrial instrumentation environment. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Step 1 — Comparison Table (4 marks):**

| Standard | Band (GHz) | Modulation | Max PHY Rate | Channel BW |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 802.11b | 2.4 | DSSS / CCK | 11 Mbps | 20 MHz |
| 802.11g | 2.4 | OFDM (backward-compat DSSS) | 54 Mbps | 20 MHz |
| 802.11n | 2.4 / 5 | MIMO-OFDM | 600 Mbps | 20 / 40 MHz |
| 802.11ac | 5 | MU-MIMO / OFDM | 6.93 Gbps | 80 / 160 MHz |

**Step 2 — Two advantages of OFDM over DSSS (2 marks):**
1. **Multipath resilience:** OFDM splits the channel into many narrow orthogonal subcarriers, each with a symbol duration longer than the typical delay spread. This **eliminates ISI (inter-symbol interference)** caused by reflections from metal machinery, walls, and equipment racks in industrial plants.
2. **Spectral efficiency:** OFDM packs subcarriers tightly, achieving higher data rates within the same 20 MHz channel — critical for video-grade instrumentation and high-speed data loggers.
3. *(Bonus 1 mark if mentioned)* OFDM allows **adaptive bit-loading** per subcarrier, so a noisy subcarrier can be muted to preserve link reliability.

**Step 3 — Practical implication (1 mark):** In a factory with metal enclosures, DSSS (802.11b) suffers heavily from multipath; OFDM-based 802.11g/n/ac maintains robust connectivity — which is why modern industrial Wi-Fi deployments almost exclusively use OFDM-based standards.

---

**Q. (b)** A wireless instrumentation network must cover a circular plant floor of **radius 60 m**. Design the minimum number of APs assuming each AP has a reliable indoor coverage radius of **25 m**, an overlap of **20%** between adjacent cells is required for seamless roaming, and the APs are placed on a regular hexagonal grid. Justify your layout. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Step 1 — Effective cell radius with overlap (2 marks):**
With 20% overlap between adjacent hexagonal cells, the **effective non-overlapping coverage radius per cell** is:

$$r_{eff} = r_{ap} \times (1 - 0.20) = 25 \times 0.80 = 20 \text{ m}$$

**Step 2 — Hexagonal cell coverage area (1 mark):**
Each hexagonal cell covers:

$$A_{cell} = \frac{3\sqrt{3}}{2} \times r_{eff}^2 = \frac{3\sqrt{3}}{2} \times (20)^2 \approx 1039 \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 3 — Total plant area (1 mark):**

$$A_{plant} = \pi r^2 = \pi (60)^2 = 11310 \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 4 — Number of cells (2 marks):**

$$N = \frac{A_{plant}}{A_{cell}} = \frac{11310}{1039} \approx 10.9$$

Round up: **N = 11 APs** (minimum) for full coverage with 20% overlap.

**Step 5 — Layout justification (1 mark):** The 11 APs are arranged on a **hexagonal grid** centered on the plant, with each AP's effective coverage of 20 m overlapping its six neighbors by 5 m. This ensures **roaming continuity** as wireless instruments move between zones — critical for AGVs (Automated Guided Vehicles) and mobile calibration carts.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Print This Before the Exam**

- [ ] **WLAN = IEEE 802.11 family** operating in **2.4 / 5 / 6 GHz** ISM bands.
- [ ] **STA** = any wireless device; **AP** = a special STA that coordinates a BSS.
- [ ] **BSS** = the radio cell, identified by **BSSID** (the AP's MAC).
- [ ] **DS** = the wired backbone (usually Ethernet) connecting APs.
- [ ] **ESS** = BSSs under one **SSID**, enabling roaming.
- [ ] **IBSS / Ad-hoc** = no AP, no DS, peer-to-peer — used in field calibration.
- [ ] **MAC protocol:** CSMA/CA with random backoff; **not** CSMA/CD (no collision detection in radio).
- [ ] **Friis FSPL** constant: **32.44** (for d in km, f in MHz) — memorize it.
- [ ] **Link budget:** $P_r = P_t + G_t + G_r - FSPL - L_{misc}$ (all in dB/dBm).
- [ ] **Noise floor** in a 20 MHz channel: **−101 dBm** (kTB at 290 K).
- [ ] **3 non-overlapping channels** in 2.4 GHz: **1, 6, 11**.
- [ ] **802.11b** = DSSS, 11 Mbps, 2.4 GHz. **802.11g/n** = OFDM, 2.4 GHz. **802.11a/ac** = OFDM, 5 GHz. **802.11ax** = OFDMA, includes 6 GHz.
- [ ] **Security evolution:** WEP (broken) → WPA (TKIP) → **WPA2 (AES-CCMP)** → WPA3 (SAE).
- [ ] **Wi-Fi is a shared medium** — capacity is shared among all STAs in a BSS, unlike switched Ethernet.
- [ ] **Industrial use:** WLAN replaces RS-485/GPIB cables for distributed sensors; key trade-off is real-time determinism vs. mobility/cost.

> [!NOTE]
> **Closing Thought for PEEET411 Students:** The KTU 2024 syllabus frames WLAN not as a "networking" topic, but as a **wireless instrumentation interconnect**. Always frame your answers in terms of *which instrument data is moving*, *what reliability is required*, and *which 802.11 standard fits the application*. This is what separates a 10-mark answer from a 14-mark one in the ESE.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Protocols: Field Bus
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET411 - ELECTRONIC INSTRUMENTATION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Protocols: Field Bus

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Fieldbus** is a generic term used to describe a family of industrial computer networks used in real-time distributed control systems (DCS) and Programmable Logic Controllers (PLCs). It is a **bidirectional, all-digital, serial communication protocol** that interconnects field devices (sensors, actuators, controllers, and valves) on a single twisted-pair cable, replacing the traditional point-to-point analog 4–20 mA wiring architecture.

According to the **International Electrotechnical Commission (IEC 61158** and **IEC 61784**), a Fieldbus is defined as a *digital, serial, multidrop, bidirectional data bus communication system** that operates at the lowest level of an industrial automation hierarchy — the **field level** — where it interfaces directly with physical process variables such as temperature, pressure, flow, and level.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: The "Conference Call" vs "One-on-One Phone Call" Model**

Imagine an old-style industrial plant where every sensor (e.g., a temperature transmitter) is connected to the control room via its **own dedicated pair of wires**, similar to how in the 1990s every office employee had a personal phone line just to talk to one manager. If a plant had **1000 sensors**, it needed **2000 wires** running back to the control room — a massive cabling nightmare.

Now imagine replacing this with a **conference call** system (Fieldbus). All 1000 sensors and actuators share **a single digital communication cable**. Each device has a unique "phone number" (an address), and they all take turns speaking and listening on this shared highway. The control room can now ask any device: *"Hey, Sensor #27, what is your temperature?"* and get a digital reply, and can also send commands like *"Valve #12, open yourself to 60%"*.

**Key Intuitive Insights:**
- **4–20 mA Analog Loop** = One-to-One Phone Call (one wire per device)
- **Fieldbus** = Conference Call over a Shared Highway (many devices on one cable)
- The highway has strict traffic rules (the **Protocol**) so no two devices speak at once.

### 1.3 Key Standard Metrics

- **Typical Baud Rates:** **31.25 kbps** (H1 Foundation Fieldbus), **1 Mbps** (Profibus DP), **10 Mbps–1 Gbps** (Industrial Ethernet variants)
- **Maximum Cable Length:** Up to **1900 m** at 31.25 kbps (H1), with **repeaters** extending this
- **Number of Devices per Segment:** Typically **32 devices** (H1) or up to **126** (Profibus)
- **Operating Voltage on Bus:** **9–32 V DC** bus-powered standard

### 1.4 Position in the Automation Pyramid (CIM Model)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Fieldbus operates at **Level 0 and Level 1** of the **Computer-Integrated Manufacturing (CIM) hierarchy** — the lowest two tiers where physical I/O resides.

| Hierarchy Level | Function | Example Network |
|:---:|:---|:---|
| **Level 4 (Enterprise)** | Business Planning | ERP Systems, Internet |
| **Level 3 (Operations)** | Plant-Wide Monitoring | **Ethernet** (TCP/IP) |
| **Level 2 (Control)** | Supervisory Control (SCADA) | **Ethernet / Industrial Ethernet** |
| **Level 1 (Field Control)** | Local Controllers (PLC, DCS) | **Fieldbus** (H1, Profibus PA) |
| **Level 0 (Process)** | Sensors & Actuators | **Fieldbus** (H1, Profibus PA) |

---
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Why Fieldbus? — The Limitations of Analog 4–20 mA

Before understanding Fieldbus protocols, one must understand the engineering *why*. The traditional **4–20 mA current loop** has three critical weaknesses that Fieldbus solves:

1. **One-Device-One-Pair Wiring Burden:** A plant with N devices requires 2N wires (signal + return), making installation expensive and maintenance difficult.
2. **Single-Variable Transmission:** Only one process variable per loop (current value). No diagnostics, no device ID, no calibration data travels on the loop.
3. **Analog Noise Susceptibility:** The 4–20 mA signal can be corrupted by electromagnetic interference (EMI) from motors, VFDs, and high-voltage cables.

> [!NOTE]
> Fieldbus addresses all three by:
> (a) Using a **single shared digital cable** (multidrop topology),
> (b) Carrying **multiple variables + diagnostics** digitally,
> (c) Using **digital encoding** (Manchester, NRZ) that is inherently noise-immune.

### 2.2 The OSI 7-Layer Model Applied to Fieldbus

Unlike the full 7-layer OSI model used in IT networks, Fieldbus protocols typically implement only **3 layers** (sometimes called the *"Fieldbus 3-layer profile"*) because deterministic real-time control does not need the overhead of TCP/IP routing:

| OSI Layer | Function | Fieldbus Implementation |
|:---|:---|:---|
| **Layer 7 — Application** | User Program / Variable Access | **Function Blocks** (AI, AO, PID) — e.g., FF, Profibus |
| **Layer 2 — Data Link** | MAC, Addressing, Framing, Error Detection | **Token Ring** (Profibus), **Bus Arbitration** (CAN) |
| **Layer 1 — Physical** | Electrical Signaling, Topology | **Manchester Coded Twisted Pair**, Fiber Optic |
| *(Layers 3–6 are typically omitted)* | *(Network/Transport overhead not needed)* | *Skipped for real-time speed* |

### 2.3 Major Fieldbus Protocols — Comparative Analysis

The following table summarizes the major industrial Fieldbus protocols a KTU student must memorize:

| Protocol | Origin / Year | OSI Layers | Physical Layer | Max Devices | Max Speed | Typical Use |
|:---|:---|:---|:---|:---|:---|:---|
| **HART** | Rosemount, **1989** | App + Data Link + Phys (1.5 layer model) | 4–20 mA + Bell 202 FSK on 1200/2200 Hz | 15 with multidrop | **1200 bps** | Smart Transmitter Comms |
| **Foundation Fieldbus H1** | FF Foundation, **1994** | Full 3-layer | Manchester, 31.25 kbps | 32 / segment | **31.25 kbps** | Process Automation (Oil & Gas) |
| **Foundation Fieldbus HSE** | FF Foundation | TCP/IP Ethernet | 100 Mbps Ethernet | Many | **100 Mbps** | High-speed backbone |
| **Profibus DP** | Siemens, **1993** | Layers 1, 2, 7 | RS-485 differential | 126 | **12 Mbps** | Factory Automation |
| **Profibus PA** | Siemens | Same as DP + IEC 61158 | Manchester, 31.25 kbps | 32 / segment | **31.25 kbps** | Process Automation |
| **Modbus RTU** | Modicon, **1979** | Layers 1, 2, 7 | RS-485 / RS-232 | 247 | **115.2 kbps** | Legacy SCADA |
| **DeviceNet** | Allen-Bradley, **1994** | Layers 1, 2, 7 | CAN-based | 64 | **500 kbps** | Discrete Mfg. |
| **CAN (Controller Area Network)** | Bosch, **1986** | Layers 1, 2 | Differential | Many | **1 Mbps** | Automotive |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Note:** The most heavily tested protocols in KTU ESE are **HART**, **Foundation Fieldbus H1**, and **Profibus**. Master these three first.

### 2.4 Detailed Architecture — HART Protocol

**HART (Highway Addressable Remote Transducer)** is *the* most widely installed smart instrumentation protocol in the world, with over **40 million installed devices** as of recent industry surveys.

#### 2.4.1 Hybrid Analog + Digital Nature

HART uses the **Bell 202 Frequency Shift Keying (FSK)** standard to overlay a digital signal on top of the conventional 4–20 mA analog loop:

- **1200 Hz** sine wave = Binary **1** (Mark)
- **2200 Hz** sine wave = Binary **0** (Space)
- **Average DC value = 0** (FSK is AC-coupled, so it does not affect the 4–20 mA DC measurement)

> [!NOTE]
> This is HART's genius — it is **backward-compatible**. The same two wires carry BOTH the 4–20 mA analog signal AND a digital FSK signal at the same time. A plant can upgrade to HART without replacing existing analog wiring.

#### 2.4.2 HART Network Topologies

Two primary topologies are defined in HART:

1. **Point-to-Point:** One master, one field device. The 4–20 mA carries the primary variable (PV); HART digital carries secondary variables, diagnostics, and configuration.
2. **Multidrop (Multi-Drop):** Up to **15 devices** in parallel on the same pair of wires. The analog current is fixed at **4 mA** (for power) and **all process variables are communicated digitally**. Each device has a unique address (1–15).

#### 2.4.3 HART Frame Structure

A HART message frame is structured as follows (each frame has these fields in order):

| Field | Length | Description |
|:---|:---|:---|
| **Preamble** | 5–20 bytes | All `0xFF`, used for clock synchronization |
| **Start Delimiter** | 1 byte | Indicates master (`0x82`) or slave (`0x86`) |
| **Address** | 1 or 5 bytes | Master + Slave polling address |
| **Command** | 1 byte | HART command number (e.g., `01` = Read PV) |
| **Byte Count** | 1 byte | Number of data bytes following |
| **Data** | 0–25 bytes | Command-specific payload |
| **Checksum** | 1 byte | Longitudinal Parity (XOR) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Command #01 (Read Primary Variable):** The most common HART command used by control systems to fetch the measured PV from a smart transmitter. **Memorize this for KTU exams.**

### 2.5 Detailed Architecture — Foundation Fieldbus (FF H1)

#### 2.5.1 Physical Layer (H1)

- **Topology:** Daisy-chain or tree on a single **shielded twisted pair (STP)**
- **Signaling:** **Manchester Biphase-L** coding at **31.25 kbps**
- **Power:** Bus-powered (DC 9–32 V), with up to **8 mA per device**
- **Maximum Length:** **1900 m** per segment without repeaters
- **Devices per segment:** **32** (addressed 0–255, with 16 reserved)

#### 2.5.2 The Link Active Scheduler (LAS)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Foundation Fieldbus uses a deterministic, centralized medium access control scheme governed by a special device called the **Link Active Scheduler (LAS)**.

The LAS is the "traffic police" of an FF H1 segment. It:
- Maintains a **schedule** of when each device may transmit (called the **Scheduled Communication**).
- Grants **tokens** for unscheduled communication between devices (**Token Passing**).
- Sends **Compel Data (CD)** messages to force data updates from devices.

This deterministic scheduling is why FF is preferred in **Safety Instrumented Systems (SIS)** and **process industries** where hard real-time guarantees are essential.

#### 2.5.3 Function Blocks (FB)

Foundation Fieldbus uses standardized **Function Blocks** that allow interoperability between vendors:

- **AI** (Analog Input), **AO** (Analog Output)
- **PID** (Proportional-Integral-Derivative controller)
- **DI/DO** (Discrete I/O)
- **IS** (Input Selector), **ML** (Manual Loader)

### 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> While most Fieldbus topics are conceptual, the following quantitative relationships are commonly tested:

| Parameter | Formula / Relation | Units / Notes |
|:---|:---|:---|
| HART FSK Frequency (Mark = 1) | $f_{1} = 1200\ \text{Hz}$ | Bell 202 Standard |
| HART FSK Frequency (Space = 0) | $f_{0} = 2200\ \text{Hz}$ | Bell 202 Standard |
| HART Digital Baud Rate | $R_{b} = 1200\ \text{bps}$ | Fixed by Bell 202 |
| Max H1 Devices per segment | $N_{max} = 32$ | (Address space allows 256, but power-limited) |
| Max H1 Cable Length (no repeater) | $L_{max} = 1900\ \text{m}$ | At 31.25 kbps |
| Max H1 Cable Length (with repeaters) | $L_{ext} = 1900 \times (N_{repeaters}+1)\ \text{m}$ | Up to 4 repeaters per ISA SP50 |
| FF HSE Throughput | $R_{HSE} = 100\ \text{Mbps}$ | Standard Fast Ethernet |
| Power per FF H1 Device | $P_{device} \leq 8\ \text{mA} \times V_{bus}$ | Bus-powered |
| Current Loop Equation (Analog) | $I_{loop} = 4 + \frac{16}{S_{max} - S_{min}} \times (S_{meas} - S_{min})$ | mA |
| Signal-to-Noise Requirement | $SNR_{min} \geq 12\ \text{dB}$ | HART receiver minimum |
| HART Multidrop Limit | $N_{multi} \leq 15$ | Power and addressing limited |

---
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: HART FSK Spectrum & Bell 202 Compliance

A common KTU problem is to verify that the **Bell 202 FSK signal does not interfere with the 4–20 mA DC analog signal**. We will derive the DC component of the FSK waveform.

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Setup:** The HART FSK waveform is a continuous-phase frequency-modulated sine wave. Show that its time-averaged DC value is zero, proving it does not disturb the analog 4–20 mA measurement.

#### Step 1 — Express the FSK Waveform

Let $s(t)$ be the FSK signal. For a binary input bit $b_k \in \{0, 1\}$, the instantaneous frequency $f_i$ is:

$$f_i = f_1 \cdot b_k + f_0 \cdot (1 - b_k)$$

where $f_1 = 1200\ \text{Hz}$ (for bit = 1) and $f_0 = 2200\ \text{Hz}$ (for bit = 0).

The waveform can be written as:

$$s(t) = A \cdot \sin\!\left(2\pi f_i t + \phi_0\right)$$

where $A$ is the peak amplitude and $\phi_0$ is a constant phase offset.

#### Step 2 — Compute the Time Average (DC Component)

The DC component is the time average of $s(t)$ over one full bit period $T_b = 1/1200\ \text{s} \approx 833\ \mu s$:

$$\overline{s(t)} = \frac{1}{T_b}\int_{0}^{T_b} A \sin\!\left(2\pi f_i t + \phi_0\right) dt$$

#### Step 3 — Evaluate the Integral

Carrying out the integral:

$$\overline{s(t)} = \frac{A}{2\pi f_i T_b}\left[-\cos\!\left(2\pi f_i T_b + \phi_0\right) + \cos\!\left(\phi_0\right)\right]$$

#### Step 4 — Substitute the Numerical Values

For bit = 1: $f_1 = 1200\ \text{Hz}$, $T_b = 1/1200\ \text{s}$, so $f_1 T_b = 1$ (one full cycle), giving $\cos(2\pi + \phi_0) = \cos(\phi_0)$. Therefore:

$$\overline{s(t)}\Big\vert_{b=1} = \frac{A}{2\pi (1200)(1/1200)}\left[\cos(\phi_0) - \cos(\phi_0)\right] = 0$$

For bit = 0: $f_0 = 2200\ \text{Hz}$, so $f_0 T_b = 2200/1200 = 11/6$ (1.833 cycles), giving $\cos(2\pi \cdot 11/6 + \phi_0) = \cos(\phi_0 + 2\pi \cdot 5/6)$. After substitution:

$$\overline{s(t)}\Big\vert_{b=0} = \frac{A}{2\pi (2200/1200)}\left[\cos\!\left(\phi_0 + \frac{5\pi}{3}\right) - \cos(\phi_0)\right] \neq 0 \text{ in general}$$

#### Step 5 — Conclude with Average Across Long Sequences

> [!IMPORTANT]
> However, since HART transmits *random* data (over a long message, bits 0 and 1 are equally probable at $\approx 50\%$), the **long-term time-average DC component is zero**. This is why HART is **AC-coupled** to the loop and does not bias the 4–20 mA analog measurement.

$$\boxed{\lim_{N\to\infty} \overline{s(t)} = 0}$$

This proves that the FSK digital channel is orthogonal to the DC analog channel — a foundational property of HART.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Power Budget for Foundation Fieldbus H1 Segment

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Setup:** An FF H1 segment has a bus voltage of 24 V DC. Each field device draws at most 8 mA and the trunk cable has a loop resistance of 44 Ω/km. Find the maximum number of devices per segment given a 1900 m cable run and a 250 Ω total cable resistance budget.

#### Step 1 — Identify Constraints

Given:
- Bus voltage: $V_{bus} = 24\ \text{V}$
- Current per device: $I_d = 8\ \text{mA}$ (maximum)
- Cable resistance: $R_c = 44\ \Omega/\text{km}$
- Cable length: $L = 1.9\ \text{km}$
- Voltage drop budget: $V_{drop} \leq 9\ \text{V}$ (per IEC 61158-2, min device voltage = 9 V)

#### Step 2 — Compute Total Cable Resistance (Loop)

The H1 cable is a loop (go + return), so the loop resistance is:

$$R_{loop} = 2 \times R_c \times L = 2 \times 44\ \Omega/\text{km} \times 1.9\ \text{km}$$

$$R_{loop} = 167.2\ \Omega$$

#### Step 3 — Calculate Voltage Drop Across Cable

With $N$ devices drawing total current $N \cdot I_d$:

$$V_{drop} = (N \cdot I_d) \times R_{loop}$$

$$V_{drop} = (N \times 8 \times 10^{-3}) \times 167.2 = 1.3376\ N\ \text{V}$$

#### Step 4 — Apply Voltage Budget Constraint

The voltage at the farthest device must be ≥ 9 V:

$$V_{bus} - V_{drop} - V_{margin} \geq 9\ \text{V}$$

$$24 - 1.3376\ N \geq 9$$

$$1.3376\ N \leq 15$$

$$N \leq 11.21$$

#### Step 5 — Conclude

Therefore, the maximum number of devices under these conditions is:

$$\boxed{N_{max} = 11\ \text{devices}}$$

(The standard "32 devices per segment" assumes a higher-power bus and shorter cables.)

---

### 3.3 Analytical Decision Matrix — Which Fieldbus to Use?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A commonly tested KTU question is *"Compare and select the appropriate fieldbus for a given scenario."* Use this matrix:

| Application Scenario | Recommended Fieldbus | Justification |
|:---|:---|:---|
| Smart temperature transmitter in a refinery | **HART** | Backward-compatible with existing 4–20 mA loops; ubiquitous |
| Hard real-time control loop (e.g., PID control at 100 Hz) | **Foundation Fieldbus H1** | Deterministic LAS scheduling; intrinsic safety in Ex zones |
| High-speed factory assembly line (robotic arms) | **Profibus DP** | 12 Mbps speed; perfect for discrete I/O |
| Legacy SCADA replacement on RS-485 | **Modbus RTU** | Simple, open protocol; easiest migration |
| Hazardous explosive environment (Oil & Gas) | **FF H1** or **Profibus PA** | IEC 61158-2 supports intrinsic safety (IS) |
| Connecting 500 I/O points on a PLC | **Profibus DP** or **DeviceNet** | High device count; low latency |
| Hybrid (analog + digital) upgrade | **HART** | Only protocol that supports simultaneous analog + digital |
| Fast Ethernet backbone | **FF HSE** or **EtherNet/IP** or **PROFINET** | 100 Mbps to 1 Gbps |

---
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

### 4.1 Conceptual Block Diagram — Fieldbus Position in a DCS

> [!NOTE]
> The following Mermaid diagram shows the hierarchical position of Fieldbus in a Distributed Control System (DCS) and how field devices, controllers, and supervisory systems interact.
