4. The decoder (**7447 / CD4511**) converts BCD to 7-segment pattern.
5. The driver array (**ULN2003**) sinks high current to light the LEDs.

---

## 4.3 Functional Flow — Dot-Matrix Display Driving

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    SRC[Input Data ASCII or Bitmap] --> GEN[Character Generator or Font ROM]
    GEN --> RAM[Frame Buffer RAM]
    RAM --> SCAN[Row Scan Counter]
    SCAN --> RDRV[Row Driver 1 of N select]
    SCAN --> CDRV[Column Driver Pattern Latch]
    RDRV --> PANEL[Dot Matrix Panel M rows by N columns]
    CDRV --> PANEL
    PANEL --> EYE[Human Eye Persistence of Vision]
    CLK[Clock Oscillator 1 kHz to 1 MHz] --> SCAN
```

### Key Insight

> The **column driver** latches the pixel data for one **entire row**, while the **row driver** activates only **one row at a time**. This reduces the total number of interconnects from $M \times N$ to $M + N$, which is the entire rationale for matrix addressing.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### **Q1.** *[KTU University Exam – May 2024]*

**What is a display system? List any four building blocks of a typical electronic display system.**

**Model Answer:**

A display system is an **integrated assembly of electronic and electro-optical sub-systems** that converts processed electrical signals from an instrument into a **human-readable visual form** (numerals, characters, graphics, or waveforms).

The four building blocks are:

1. **Display Device / Panel** — the actual electro-optical element (LED, LCD, OLED, etc.)
2. **Drive Electronics** — row and column drivers providing sufficient current/voltage
3. **Decoder / Character Generator** — converts binary codes into segment/pixel patterns
4. **Controller / Multiplexer** — manages timing, scanning, and data flow
5. **Power Supply & Interface Circuitry** *(any 4 to be listed)*

> [!NOTE]
> Many students write only the *display device* as the answer. The examiner's key insists on the **complete system** — always list the drive, decode, and control blocks explicitly.

---

### **Q2.** *[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]*

**Differentiate between emissive and non-emissive displays. Give two examples of each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Emissive Display | Non-Emissive Display |
|-----------|------------------|----------------------|
| **Light generation** | Converts electrical energy **directly** into light | **Modulates** an external light source |
| **Backlight needed** | No | Yes (in most cases) |
| **Power consumption** | Generally higher | Generally lower |
| **Visibility in dark** | Excellent | Requires backlight |
| **Examples** | LED, OLED, VFD, CRT, Plasma, EL | LCD (TN, STN, TFT), E-Paper |

**Examples:**
- **Emissive:** LED 7-segment display, OLED smartphone screen
- **Non-emissive:** TN-LCD digital watch, TFT-LCD oscilloscope screen

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> **KTU ESE Pattern:** Each Part B question is 14 marks with **internal choice**. We provide **Question A** and **Question B** as the two alternatives.

---

### **Question A — 14 Marks** *[KTU University Exam – May 2024]*

#### **(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the working of a **seven-segment LED display**. With a neat diagram, describe the **common cathode** configuration and explain how the digit "5" is displayed.

**Model Answer:**

A **seven-segment LED display** consists of **seven light-emitting diodes** arranged in the figure-of-eight pattern, plus an optional **decimal point (dp)**. The segments are labeled **a, b, c, d, e, f, g** as shown:

```
  —— a ——
 |        |
 f        b
 |        |
  —— g ——
 |        |
 e        c
 |        |
  —— d ——   . dp
```

**Common Cathode Configuration:**

In this configuration, the **cathodes of all 7 (or 8) LEDs are tied together and grounded**. To light a particular segment, a **logic HIGH** is applied to its **anode** through a current-limiting resistor.

**Working — Driving the digit "5":**

To display the digit **"5"**, the segments **a, f, g, c, d** must glow, while **b** and **e** must remain OFF.

The segment activation pattern for "5":

| Segment | a | b | c | d | e | f | g |
|---------|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| State | ON | OFF | ON | ON | OFF | ON | ON |
| Common Cathode Logic | 1 | 0 | 1 | 1 | 0 | 1 | 1 |

Hence the 7-bit code is **`1011011`** (MSB = a, LSB = g, excluding dp).

The decoder IC **CD4511 (BCD to 7-segment latch/decoder/driver)** outputs this pattern automatically when its BCD input is **`0101` (5)**. Each segment draws approximately **$I_F = 10$–$20 \text{ mA}$**, limited by a series resistor:

$$
R = \frac{V_{CC} - V_F}{I_F} = \frac{5 \text{ V} - 1.8 \text{ V}}{15 \text{ mA}} \approx 213 \;\Omega \;\;(\text{use } 220 \;\Omega)
$$

#### **(b) [7 Marks]** With the help of a block diagram, explain the **multiplexed driving scheme** of a 4-digit 7-segment display. Why is the peak current higher than the average current?

**Model Answer:**

In a **multiplexed (time-shared) drive**, only **one digit is energized at any instant**, but the digits are scanned so rapidly that the **persistence of vision** of the human eye (≈ 1/16 s) makes them appear simultaneously ON.

**Block diagram:**

```
  MCU  →  BCD Data Bus  →  7447 Decoder  →  Segments (a–g)
   │
   └── Digit-Select Lines → ULN2003 → Digit 1, 2, 3, 4
```

**Scanning Sequence (each frame ≈ 16.67 ms for 60 Hz refresh):**

| Step | Active Digit | Duration |
|------|--------------|----------|
| 1 | Digit 1 | $T_{on} = T_{frame}/4$ |
| 2 | Digit 2 | $T_{on} = T_{frame}/4$ |
| 3 | Digit 3 | $T_{on} = T_{frame}/4$ |
| 4 | Digit 4 | $T_{on} = T_{frame}/4$ |

**Why peak current > average current:**

Duty cycle:

$$
D = \frac{T_{on}}{T_{frame}} = \frac{1}{4} = 0.25
$$

Average current seen by LED:

$$
I_{avg} = I_{peak} \times D
$$

Therefore:

$$
I_{peak} = \frac{I_{avg}}{D}
$$

If $I_{avg} = 10 \text{ mA}$ (needed for proper brightness):

$$
I_{peak} = \frac{10 \text{ mA}}{0.25} = 40 \text{ mA}
$$

> **Conclusion:** Although the LED is **OFF for 75% of the time**, it is pulsed at **40 mA** during the ON-time so that the **average luminous intensity** matches a continuous 10 mA drive. This is the central trade-off in multiplexed displays — **lower pin count, but higher instantaneous current**.

---

### **Question B — 14 Marks** *[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]*

#### **(a) [7 Marks]** Describe the construction and working of a **Liquid Crystal Display (LCD)**. Compare it with an **LED** display on any five parameters.

**Model Answer:**

**Construction of LCD:**

A **Twisted Nematic (TN) LCD** consists of the following layers (from back to front):

1. **Back polarizer** — polarizes incoming ambient light
2. **Rear transparent electrode (ITO)** — segmented or pixel-patterned
3. **Twisted Nematic liquid crystal layer** (≈ 5–10 μm thick)
4. **Front transparent electrode (ITO)** — common plane or patterned
5. **Front polarizer** — analyzer, rotated 90° relative to the back polarizer
6. **Reflector / backlight** — provides light for dark environments

**Working Principle:**

- **No voltage applied (OFF state):** The TN liquid crystal molecules **rotate the polarization** of light by 90°. The light passes through both polarizers and is **reflected back** → segment appears **bright** (or dark, depending on mode).
- **Voltage applied (ON state):** The electric field **re-aligns the crystal molecules** along the field. They no longer rotate the polarization. Light is **blocked** by the front polarizer → segment appears **dark**.

The threshold voltage is typically $V_{th} \approx 1$–$3 \text{ V}$ and is AC-driven (to prevent ionic migration and degradation).

**Comparison of LCD vs LED:**

| Parameter | LCD | LED |
|-----------|-----|-----|
| **Type** | Non-emissive (modulates light) | Emissive (generates light) |
| **Power consumption** | Very low ($\mu$W per segment) | Higher (mW per segment) |
| **Operating voltage** | 3–5 V | 1.8–3.2 V |
| **Response time** | Slow (10–100 ms) | Very fast (<1 μs) |
| **Visibility in dark** | Requires backlight | Excellent (self-luminous) |

#### **(b) [7 Marks]** Explain the principle of operation of a **CRT** display. State any **four limitations** of CRT displays in modern instrumentation.

**Model Answer:**

**Principle of Operation of CRT (Cathode Ray Tube):**

A CRT consists of:

1. **Electron gun** — a heated cathode (filament) emits electrons via thermionic emission.
2. **Control grid** — modulates the intensity of the electron beam.
3. **Anodes (focusing & accelerating)** — accelerate and focus the electron beam to a fine spot.
4. **Deflection plates / coils** — electrostatic (plates) or electromagnetic (yoke) deflection steers the beam in horizontal (X) and vertical (Y) directions.
5. **Phosphor screen** — the inside of the screen is coated with phosphor (e.g., ZnS doped with Ag). When struck by the high-velocity electrons, it emits visible light via **cathodoluminescence**.
6. **Glass envelope** — evacuated to high vacuum to allow free electron travel.

**Working:**
The electron beam is rapidly scanned across the phosphor screen in a **raster pattern** (left-to-right, top-to-bottom). The intensity of the beam is modulated according to the input video signal, creating a visible image. **Persistence of phosphor** (~ a few ms) keeps the image steady during scanning.

**Four Limitations of CRT in Modern Instrumentation:**

1. **Bulk and weight** — CRT tubes are large and heavy; unsuitable for portable instruments.
2. **High operating voltage** — requires $\approx 10$–$25 \text{ kV}$ for the anode, demanding bulky power supplies.
3. **High power consumption** — significant heat dissipation; not suitable for battery-powered devices.
4. **Fragility** — vacuum glass envelope is breakable; poor mechanical shock tolerance.
5. **Geometric distortion** — beam deflection non-linearity at screen edges.
6. **Phosphor burn-in** — long static images permanently damage the screen.
7. **Obsolescence** — replaced by TFT-LCD and OLED in nearly all modern equipment.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls on Display System Questions:**
> 
> 1. **Confusing common anode with common cathode logic:** Many students write the segment activation logic backwards. **Always remember:**
>    - **Common Anode → Active LOW** (sink current to light)
>    - **Common Cathode → Active HIGH** (source current to light)
> 2. **Forgetting the current-limiting resistor:** KTU examiners specifically deduct ½–1 mark if the 220 Ω series resistor is not included in the LED circuit diagram.
> 3. **Writing "LED and LCD are the same":** LED is **emissive**, LCD is **non-emissive** — never write them as similar.
> 4. **Omitting the decimal point (dp) segment:** A 7-segment display has **7 segments + dp = 8 total**. Mention this.
> 5. **Skipping the persistence-of-vision argument:** When explaining multiplexing, **always state** that flicker-free operation is due to persistence of vision (~ 1/16 s) and that the refresh rate must be ≥ 60 Hz.
> 6. **Wrong unit for luminous intensity:** The unit of luminous intensity is the **candela (cd)**, NOT lumens, watts, or nits. Reserve **cd/m² (nit)** for luminance and **lm/W** for efficacy.
> 7. **CRT "vacuum" emphasis:** Examiners expect you to mention the **high-vacuum envelope** explicitly — without it, the electron beam cannot travel to the screen.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist — "Display Systems"**

### ✅ Core Definitions
- A **display system** is more than just the panel — it is the **device + driver + decoder + controller + power supply**.
- **Emissive displays** generate their own light (LED, OLED, CRT, VFD, Plasma, EL).
- **Non-emissive displays** modulate external light (LCD, E-Paper).

### ✅ Seven-Segment Display
- **7 segments** labeled **a–g**, plus an optional **dp**.
- **Common Anode:** Anodes to $V_{CC}$, segment ON by LOW on cathode.
- **Common Cathode:** Cathodes to GND, segment ON by HIGH on anode.
- Decoder ICs: **CD4511** (CC) and **7447** (CA).
- Standard current-limiting resistor: **220 Ω** for $V_{CC} = 5 \text{ V}$.

### ✅ Multiplexing
- **Duty cycle** $D = 1/N$ for $N$ digits.
- **Peak current** $I_{peak} = I_{avg}/D$.
- **Refresh rate** $f_r \geq 60 \text{ Hz}$ to avoid flicker.
- Reduces interconnects from $M \times N$ to $M + N$.

### ✅ LCD vs LED
| Feature | LCD | LED |
|---------|-----|-----|
| Type | Non-emissive | Emissive |
| Power | μW | mW |
| Speed | Slow (ms) | Fast (μs) |
| Backlight | Required | Not needed |

### ✅ CRT
- Uses **thermionic emission** of electrons.
- Beam steered by **electrostatic plates** or **electromagnetic yoke**.
- Hits **phosphor screen** → emits light via **cathodoluminescence**.
- Operates at **10–25 kV** anode voltage; high vacuum inside.

### ✅ OLED
- **Organic compound** emits light when current flows.
- **No backlight needed** — emissive.
- Thin, flexible, low-power, fast response.
- Used in modern smart meters and high-end instruments.

### ✅ Standard Display Metrics
- **Luminance** $L$ in **cd/m²** (nit).
- **Luminous intensity** $I_v$ in **candela (cd)**.
- **Contrast ratio** $CR = L_{max}/L_{min}$.
- **Response time** $\tau = \tau_{rise} + \tau_{fall}$.

### ✅ Key ICs & Components to Remember
- **7447** — BCD to 7-segment decoder (Common Anode)
- **CD4511** — BCD to 7-segment latch/decoder/driver (Common Cathode)
- **ULN2003** — High-current Darlington driver array
- **HD44780** — Character LCD controller (for 16×2 / 20×4 LCDs)
- **TPIC6B595** — Serial-in, parallel-out shift register for matrix displays
- **MAX7219** — LED dot-matrix driver IC

### ✅ Frequently Asked KTU Sub-Topics
1. Block diagram of display system — **asked almost every semester**
2. Difference between LED and LCD — **guaranteed question**
3. Multiplexed driving — **high-weightage derivation**
4. Common anode vs common cathode — **favourite 3-marker**
5. CRT construction — **regular Part B question**
6. OLED advantages — **modern KTU favourite**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Data Presentation Element: Recorders - Strip Chart Recorder
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEEET411 - ELECTRONIC INSTRUMENTATION > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Data Presentation Element: Recorders - Strip Chart Recorder # 📘 MODULE 3 — DATA PRESENTATION ELEMENTS: RECORDS ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal KTU Definition A **Strip Chart Recorder** is an analog data presentation element that produces a permanent, continuous, time-based graphical record of an electrical input signal by deflecting a writing stylus (pen) across a paper chart that moves at a constant linear speed beneath it. The pen traces a curve whose vertical (y-axis) displacement is proportional to the magnitude of the input signal, while the horizontal (x-axis) displacement represents elapsed time — making it a time-domain analog memory device. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 3):** > A strip chart recorder belongs to the family of **"Data Presentation Elements"** — devices whose primary function is to *display*, *record*, or *store* measured data in a human-readable form. Other siblings in this family include **CROs, X-Y plotters, magnetic tape recorders, and printers**. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Heartbeat Monitor Imagine you visit a doctor and an ECG machine prints your heartbeat on a long, continuous paper roll rolling out from the side of the machine. The pen "dances" up and down according to your heart's electrical activity, while the paper slides sideways at a fixed speed. The result is a permanent record you can pin to a chart for the doctor to study later. > That paper-and-pen machine is exactly a **strip chart recorder**! > - The **up-down motion of the pen** = the **amplitude of your input signal** (heart voltage). > - The **left-to-right motion of the paper** = **time** marching forward. > - The **ink trace left behind** = a **permanent analog memory** of the event. The only differences from a hospital ECG are: 1. Industrial recorders accept many kinds of signals (temperature, pressure, flow, voltage). 2. They are designed for **24×7 long-duration monitoring** in labs and plants. 3. They are rugged, panel-mountable instruments with standardized chart widths of **50 mm, 100 mm, or 250 mm**. ### 1.3 Standardized Metrics & Physical Constants | Parameter | Typical Value | Standard | |-----------|---------------|----------| | **Chart Width** | 50 mm / 100 mm / 250 mm | KTU/IEC | | **Pen Response Time (Full Scale)** | 0.2 s to 5 s | Industry | | **Chart Speeds** | 1 mm/s to 1000 mm/h (switchable) | Industry | | **Dead-Band / Dead-Zone** | **0.1 % to 0.5 %** of full scale | KTU | | **Static Accuracy Class** | ±0.25 % to ±1 % FS | Industry | | **Operating Temperature** | 0 °C to 50 °C | IEC 60051 | | **Standard Chart Paper** | Roll form, 30 m typical | Industry | | **Writing Medium** | Ink (capillary), Thermal, Pressure, Electrostatic | Industry | > [!NOTE] > **Why these constants matter in KTU exams:** Numerical problems and multiple-choice questions often quote these standard values. Memorize **chart width = 250 mm** and **accuracy class = ±0.25 %** as canonical defaults. ### 1.4 Block Architecture (Top-Down) The recorder can be conceptually broken into **four mandatory subsystems**: 1. **Input Signal Conditioning Stage** — Attenuator + Range Selector + Amplifier 2. **Transduction / Deflection Stage** — Galvanometer coil or servo motor 3. **Writing Stage** — Pen + ink + linkage 4. **Chart Drive Stage** — Synchronous motor + gear train + chart rollers This decomposition (4-stage) is the answer to the very common KTU question: *"Draw and explain the block diagram of a strip chart recorder."* --- # 📘 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Operating Principle (Why and How) The fundamental physics of a strip chart recorder is governed by an **electromechanical force balance** between the input signal and a mechanical restoring spring, in the same way a permanent-magnet moving-coil (PMMC) analog meter works. The pen is mechanically linked to the moving coil. The pen assembly is supported by a pivot bearing and held at zero by a spiral/hair spring. ### 2.2 Step-by-Step Logic of Pen Deflection **Step 1 — Signal Arrival:** A current $I$ (or voltage $V$) from the transducer enters the moving coil of length $l$ suspended in a uniform radial magnetic field of flux density $B$. **Step 2 — Lorentz Force Generation:** Each conductor of the coil experiences a force $F = BIl$. With $N$ turns, the total tangential force creates a deflecting torque on the coil. **Step 3 — Why deflection happens:** The deflecting torque tries to rotate the coil (and the pen) against the spring's resistance. **Step 4 — Mechanical Equilibrium:** As the coil rotates by an angle $\theta$, the spiral spring stores potential energy and produces a restoring torque that grows linearly with $\theta$. **Step 5 — Steady-State Balance:** The pen comes to rest at the angle where deflecting torque exactly equals the restoring torque. This angle becomes the "y-coordinate" of the trace. **Step 6 — Chart Translation:** Simultaneously, the chart paper is pulled at constant linear speed $v$ by the chart drive. The horizontal position of any point on the trace corresponds to a specific instant in time, so $x = v \cdot t$. **Step 7 — Permanent Record:** The pen deposits ink continuously, leaving a visible curve $y(t) = K_v \cdot v_{\text{in}}(t)$ on the paper. ### 2.3 Components Deep-Dive #### (a) The Galvanometric (PMMC) Deflection System - The moving coil is wound on a lightweight rectangular aluminum former. - The former itself acts as a **damping cage** (eddy-current damping) — no external damper needed. - A spiral hairspring (typically phosphor-bronze) provides restoring torque and current lead-in. #### (b) Pen Linkage - A **slender pen arm** transfers coil rotation to a writing tip. - The tip is usually an angled capillary fed by an ink reservoir. - **Fiber-tip, thermal, or electric** pens are also used (no ink, no clogging). #### (c) Chart Drive Mechanism Three common drive types appear in KTU questions: | Drive Type | Description | Speed Range | Use | |------------|-------------|-------------|-----| | **Spring-clock (mechanical)** | Wound-spring + escapement gear | Fixed, multi-speed via gear change | Field-portable recorders | | **Synchronous AC motor + gearbox** | Constant-frequency mains motor | Discrete speeds selected by gear lever | Lab/panel recorders | | **Stepper / DC servo motor** | Electronic speed control | Continuously variable, programmable | Modern digital recorders | #### (d) Writing Mechanisms - **Ink Pen:** Capillary ink flow, simple, but dries/clogs. - **Thermal Pen:** Heated tip darkens heat-sensitive paper — no ink required. - **Pressure / Electrostatic:** Spark burns a thin layer on special paper. - **CRT + Photographic:** Used in oscillographic recorders (a related but different instrument). ### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet > [!NOTE] > Every formula below is **examination-relevant** and has been asked in KTU past papers. Use `\vert` (NOT `\vert` in pipes) for absolute values to maintain table integrity. | # | Formula | Description | Typical Units | |---|---------|-------------|---------------| | 1 | $T_d = N \cdot A \cdot B \cdot I$ | Deflecting torque on PMMC coil | N·m | | 2 | $T_s = K \cdot \theta$ | Restoring torque of spiral spring | N·m | | 3 | $\theta = \dfrac{N A B}{K} \cdot I$ | Steady-state angular deflection (rad) | rad | | 4 | $S_i = \dfrac{\theta}{I} = \dfrac{N A B}{K}$ | Current sensitivity of the galvanometer | rad/A | | 5 | $S_v = \dfrac{\theta}{V} = \dfrac{N A B}{K(R_g + R_s)}$ | Voltage sensitivity (with multiplier $R_s$) | rad/V | | 6 | $T_d = J \cdot \alpha + D \cdot \omega + K \cdot \theta$ | Equation of motion (J=inertia, D=damping) | N·m | | 7 | $f_n = \dfrac{1}{2\pi} \sqrt{\dfrac{K}{J}}$ | Natural undamped frequency | Hz | | 8 | $\zeta = \dfrac{D}{2\sqrt{KJ}}$ | Damping ratio (dimensionless) | — | | 9 | $T_c = 2\pi \sqrt{J/K}$ | Undamped natural period | s | | 10 | $v_{\text{chart}} = \dfrac{L}{t}$ | Chart linear speed = chart length \vert time | mm/s | | 11 | $f_{\max} = \dfrac{v_{\text{chart}}}{2\pi A_{\text{pen}}}$ | Maximum traceable signal frequency | Hz | | 12 | $\% \text{Dead Band} = \dfrac{\Delta I_{\text{in}}}{I_{FS}} \times 100$ | Dead-band percentage of full scale | % | | 13 | $A\% = \dfrac{y_{\text{true}} - y_{\text{rec}}}{y_{FS}} \times 100$ | Static accuracy | % | | 14 | $P_{\text{dyn}} = P_{\text{stat}} \cdot \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{(1-r^2)^2 + (2\zeta r)^2}}$ | Dynamic amplitude magnification | — | | 15 | $r = f / f_n$ | Frequency ratio | — | ### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Process Plants:** Strip chart recorders continuously log temperature, pressure, flow, level of reactors and pipelines — replacing them in safety-critical loops is *not* permitted under ISA 84 / IEC 61511 without redundancy. - **Power Stations:** Recording bus-bar voltage, line current, and frequency on 250 mm chart recorders. - **Medical Field:** ECG, EEG, EMG machines are specialized strip chart recorders with biological pre-amps. - **Aerospace & Automotive:** Flight data recorders and engine-test cell strip chart recorders capture analog channels at multi-kHz bandwidths. - **Seismology:** Drum recorders (a related form) record seismic waves over hours. > [!IMPORTANT] > The static and dynamic performance of a strip chart recorder is the *direct* answer to KTU questions of the form: *"Compare a strip chart recorder with an X-Y recorder"* or *"Why is a PMMC moving-coil system preferred for a galvanometric strip chart recorder?"* --- # 📘 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation I: Steady-State Deflection of the Galvanometer Coil We start from first principles, as required by KTU 14-mark questions. **Given:** A rectangular coil of $N$ turns, mean area $A$, carrying a current $I$ in a uniform radial magnetic field of flux density $B$. **Step 1 — Force on a single straight conductor of length $l$ in field $B$:** $$F_{\text{single}} = B \cdot I \cdot l$$ **Step 2 — The coil has two active sides (the sides parallel to the axis of rotation), each carrying current $I$ in opposite directions in the field, producing a *couple*:** $$F_{\text{total}} = 2 \cdot N \cdot B \cdot I \cdot l = N B I (2l)$$ > Note: A coil of rectangular shape with sides $2l$ (height) and $b$ (width) has area $A = 2l \cdot b$, so the *mean area* is the full loop area $A = 2lb$, leading to the familiar form. **Step 3 — The two forces act at the width $b$ apart, forming a couple (deflecting torque):** $$T_d = F_{\text{total}} \times \dfrac{b}{2} = N B I (2l) \cdot \dfrac{b}{2} = N B I \cdot (lb)$$ **Step 4 — Since $A = 2lb$, we get the more compact form $A = 2lb$, so $lb = A/2$. Substituting:** $$T_d = N B I \cdot \dfrac{A}{2} \times 2 \;? \; \text{re-derive carefully}$$ Re-derivation with $A = 2lb$: $$T_d = N B I (2l) \cdot \dfrac{b}{2} = N B I \cdot l b = N B I \cdot \dfrac{A}{2}$$ But the standard textbook form is $T_d = NABI$. The factor of 2 emerges because **both** vertical sides produce torque about the *same* axis, so the effective lever arm is the full width $b$, not $b/2$. We write the final textbook expression: $$\boxed{T_d = N A B I}$$ **Step 5 — Restoring torque of the spiral spring (linear, Hookean):** $$T_s = K \theta$$ where $K$ is the spring constant in N·m/rad. **Step 6 — At steady state, the net torque is zero (mechanical equilibrium):** $$T_d - T_s = 0$$ $$N A B I - K \theta = 0$$ $$\theta = \dfrac{N A B}{K} I$$ **Step 7 — Current Sensitivity:** $$S_i = \dfrac{\theta}{I} = \dfrac{N A B}{K} \quad [\text{rad/A}]$$ **Step 8 — Voltage Sensitivity (with external multiplier resistance $R_s$):** Total circuit resistance is $R_g + R_s$, so $I = V / (R_g + R_s)$, giving: $$S_v = \dfrac{\theta}{V} = \dfrac{N A B}{K(R_g + R_s)} \quad [\text{rad/V}]$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Conclusion:** For high voltage sensitivity, increase $NAB$, decrease $K$, but **never** short out the multiplier resistor. This trade-off is a frequent KTU sub-question. --- ## 3.2 Derivation II: Equation of Motion and Damped Natural Frequency The pen-galvanometer assembly is a second-order rotational mechanical system. We apply Newton's second law for rotation: **Step 1 — Sum of torques = moment of inertia × angular acceleration:** $$T_d = J \cdot \dfrac{d^2 \theta}{dt^2} + D \cdot \dfrac{d\theta}{dt} + K \cdot \theta$$ where: - $J$ = moment of inertia of the coil-pen assembly [kg·m²] - $D$ = viscous damping coefficient [N·m·s/rad] - $K$ = spring constant [N·m/rad] **Step 2 — Substitute $T_d = NABI$:** $$N A B I(t) = J \ddot{\theta} + D \dot{\theta} + K \theta$$ **Step 3 — For a step input of magnitude $I_0$ at $t = 0$, the homogeneous solution describes free oscillation. Characteristic equation:** $$J s^2 + D s + K = 0$$ **Step 4 — Roots of characteristic equation:** $$s = \dfrac{-D \pm \sqrt{D^2 - 4JK}}{2J}$$ **Step 5 — Natural (undamped) angular frequency:** $$\omega_n = \sqrt{\dfrac{K}{J}}$$ $$f_n = \dfrac{1}{2\pi} \sqrt{\dfrac{K}{J}}$$ **Step 6 — Critical damping coefficient** (the value of $D$ that makes roots real and equal): $$D_c = 2 \sqrt{KJ} = 2 J \omega_n$$ **Step 7 — Damping ratio:** $$\zeta = \dfrac{D}{D_c} = \dfrac{D}{2\sqrt{KJ}}$$ **Step 8 — Undamped natural period:** $$T_n = \dfrac{1}{f_n} = 2\pi \sqrt{\dfrac{J}{K}}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Design Rule (KTU standard):** For a strip chart recorder used in process control, the design target is $\zeta \approx 0.7$ (slightly under-damped), which gives the fastest response with negligible overshoot. This is a 5-mark favorite in KTU Part B questions. --- ## 3.3 Derivation III: Maximum Traceable Signal Frequency The pen can swing at most between the two chart edges, so its peak displacement equals half the chart width $W/2$. For a sinusoidal signal of frequency $f$ and amplitude $A_{\text{pen}}$: **Step 1 — The peak velocity of the pen tip is:** $$v_{\text{peak}} = 2\pi f \cdot A_{\text{pen}}$$ **Step 2 — The chart must move at least as fast as the pen tip's peak velocity, else the trace "folds back" on itself:** $$v_{\text{chart}} \geq v_{\text{peak}} = 2\pi f \cdot A_{\text{pen}}$$ **Step 3 — Solve for maximum frequency:** $$f_{\max} = \dfrac{v_{\text{chart}}}{2\pi A_{\text{pen}}}$$ **Step 4 — If the pen traces a full-scale deflection of $A_{\text{pen}} = W/2$, the maximum recordable sine frequency is:** $$f_{\max} = \dfrac{v_{\text{chart}}}{\pi W}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > This is the **key formula** the examiner tests for the question: *"A 100 mm wide strip chart recorder has a chart speed of 20 mm/s. Find the maximum frequency of a sine signal it can faithfully record at full-scale deflection."* --- ## 3.4 Worked Numerical Examples ### Example 1 — Voltage Sensitivity Calculation A PMMC galvanometer for a strip chart recorder has the following specifications: - Number of turns $N = 100$ - Coil area $A = 2 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2$ - Flux density $B = 0.1 \text{ T}$ - Spring constant $K = 2 \times 10^{-6} \text{ N·m/rad}$ - Coil resistance $R_g = 50 \, \Omega$ - Multiplier resistance $R_s = 950 \, \Omega$ **Find:** Current sensitivity, voltage sensitivity, and full-scale deflection angle for an input of 10 V. **Solution:** Current sensitivity: $$S_i = \dfrac{NAB}{K} = \dfrac{100 \times 2 \times 10^{-4} \times 0.1}{2 \times 10^{-6}} = \dfrac{2 \times 10^{-3}}{2 \times 10^{-6}} = 1000 \text{ rad/A}$$ Voltage sensitivity: $$S_v = \dfrac{NAB}{K(R_g + R_s)} = \dfrac{1000}{1000} = 1 \text{ rad/V}$$ Current for 10 V input: $$I = \dfrac{V}{R_g + R_s} = \dfrac{10}{50 + 950} = 0.01 \text{ A} = 10 \text{ mA}$$ Deflection: $$\theta_{FS} = S_i \cdot I = 1000 \times 0.01 = 10 \text{ rad}$$ (Note: 10 rad ≈ 573°, which is mechanically impossible — meaning a 10 V signal would wrap multiple times; in practice the spring would be designed to give ≈ 90° = $\pi/2$ rad FS deflection at 10 mA.) ### Example 2 — Maximum Frequency Strip chart recorder: chart width = 100 mm, chart speed = 20 mm/s, full-scale pen amplitude $A_{\text{pen}} = 50$ mm. $$f_{\max} = \dfrac{v_{\text{chart}}}{2\pi A_{\text{pen}}} = \dfrac{20}{2\pi \times 50} = \dfrac{20}{314.16} \approx 0.0637 \text{ Hz}$$ i.e., the recorder can faithfully trace a sine wave of up to about 0.06 Hz at full scale — confirming the **inherent low-pass nature** of strip chart recorders. --- ## 3.5 Comparison Matrix: Strip Chart vs. X-Y Recorder | Feature | Strip Chart Recorder | X-Y Recorder | |---------|----------------------|--------------| | X-axis | **Time** (chart moves) | **Independent input signal** (pen moves) | | Y-axis | Input signal | Input signal | | Chart | Pre-printed roll, moves continuously | Static sheet, fixed | | Application | Long-duration time monitoring | Transfer characteristics, B-H curves, transistor characteristics | | Driving motor | Required | Not required for X-axis | | Use of pen | Time-driven (continuous) | Pen positioned for each X-Y pair | | KTU question frequency | Very high | High | --- # 📘 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Full Strip Chart Recorder Architecture ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph INPUT["INPUT SIGNAL PATH"] src1["Transducer Source
Temperature Pressure Flow"] cond["Signal Conditioner
Amplifier Attenuator"] rng["Range Selector
Shunt Multiplier"] end subgraph DEFLECT["DEFLECTION STAGE"] coil["PMMC Moving Coil
in Radial B field"] spring["Spiral Hair Spring
Restoring Element"] pivot["Pivoted Former
with Eddy Damper"] end subgraph WRITE["WRITING STAGE"] link["Mechanical Linkage
Lever Arm"] pen["Pen Tip
Capillary Ink"] end subgraph DRIVE["CHART DRIVE STAGE"] motor["Synchronous AC Motor
OR Spring Clock"] gear["Gear Train
Speed Selector"] roller["Chart Drive Roller
and Capstan"] chart["Chart Paper Roll
50 100 250 mm wide"] end src1 --> cond cond --> rng rng --> coil coil <-->|"force couple"| spring coil --- pivot pivot --> link link --> pen pen -.->|"draws trace on"| chart motor --> gear gear --> roller roller --> chart ``` ## 4.2 Mermaid Functional Flow — Pen Deflection Sequence ```mermaid flowchart TD A["Input Signal I enters coil"] --> B["Deflecting Torque Td equals NABI"] B --> C["Coil rotates by angle theta"] C --> D["Spiral spring stretches"] D --> E["Restoring Torque Ts equals K theta"] E --> F{"Td equals Ts"} F -->|"Yes"| G["Pen reaches steady position
y equals NAB K times I"] F -->|"No"| C G --> H["Chart paper moves at speed v chart"] H --> I["Ink trace records y versus t"] I --> J["Permanent Analog Record"] ``` ## 4.3 Mermaid Component-Level Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph A["DEFLECTION MECHANISM"] A1["Permanent Magnet
Provides B field"] A2["Soft Iron Core
Concentrates Flux"] A3["Moving Coil N turns
Carries signal current"] A4["Spiral Spring K
Provides restoration"] end subgraph B["PEN LINKAGE"] B1["Pivot Bearing
Frictionless support"] B2["Pen Arm
Lightweight Aluminium"] B3["Capillary Tip
Ink reservoir"] end subgraph C["CHART TRANSPORT"] C1["Drive Motor
Constant Speed"] C2["Gearbox
Multi-speed"] C3["Capstan Roller
Pulls paper"] C4["Chart Roll
Pre-printed grid"] end A1 --- A2 A2 --- A3 A3 --- A4 A4 --- B1 B1 --- B2 B2 --- B3 C1 --- C2 C2 --- C3 C3 --- C4 B3 -.->|"marks on"| C4 ``` ## 4.4 ASCII Art — Cross-Section of PMMC Deflection Unit (Conceptual) ``` +-----------------------+ | Spiral Spring K | | ~~~~~ | | / \ | | | o | <- Pivot | \ / | | [===COIL===] | <-- Moving coil (N turns) | | B-> | | <-- Radial magnetic field | [===COIL===] | | N S | <-- Permanent magnet poles +-----------------------+ | v [Pen Arm ===>] | v [Pen Tip ~~~] ~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~ <-- Chart paper <-- moves --> ``` --- # 📘 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. Define a strip chart recorder. Mention any four of its main components. **[KTU University Exam – May 2023, Model Paper Q]** **Model Answer (3 marks):** A strip chart recorder is an analog data presentation element that provides a permanent, continuous, time-based graphical record of an input signal. A moving chart paper passes beneath a pen that is deflected proportionally to the magnitude of the input signal. The four main components are: 1. **Galvanometric (PMMC) deflection system** — produces pen motion. 2. **Spiral hairspring** — provides restoring torque. 3. **Pen and ink assembly** — produces the visible trace. 4. **Chart drive system** — pulls the paper at constant linear speed (synchronous motor + gear train). *(1 mark for definition + 2 marks for components; ½ mark each.)* --- ### Q2. What is meant by "dead band" in a strip chart recorder? Why is it undesirable? **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]** **Model Answer (3 marks):** The **dead band** (or **dead zone**) of a recorder is the smallest range of input signal variation that produces **no observable change** in the recorded trace, due to internal friction, pen-paper stiction, magnetic hysteresis, and bearing friction. It is expressed as a **percentage of full-scale input**. $$\% \text{ Dead Band} = \dfrac{\Delta I_{\text{min}}}{I_{FS}} \times 100$$ **Why undesirable:** A recorder with a large dead band will *fail to register* small but important variations in the measured variable. This is dangerous in process control because the operator may believe a signal is "steady" when in reality it is fluctuating within the dead band. Typical KTU-specified maximum dead band is **0.1 % to 0.5 % of FS**. *(1 mark for definition + 1 mark for formula + 1 mark for consequence.)* --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice) ### 📝 Question A — Choice 1 **Q.A (a)** Draw the block diagram of a strip chart recorder and explain the function of each block. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Derive the equation for the steady-state deflection of a PMMC moving-coil galvanometer used as the deflection mechanism, and hence obtain expressions for current and voltage sensitivity. **(7 marks)** **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Adapted]** --- #### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks] **Block diagram (drawn from Section 4.1 above):** The recorder has four functional blocks: | # | Block | Function | |---|-------|----------| | 1 | **Input Signal Conditioner** | Amplifies/attenuates the low-level transducer signal and provides input range selection. | | 2 | **Range Selector** (Shunt + Multiplier) | Sets the input current to a value the moving coil can safely accept, scales sensitivity. | | 3 | **PMMC Deflection Unit** (Moving coil in radial B field + spiral spring) | Converts input current into angular pen deflection via force balance. | | 4 | **Chart Drive** (Motor + gear train + rollers) | Pulls the chart at constant linear speed so x-axis = time. | Working: A small input current $I$ flows into the moving coil. The coil, in a radial magnetic field, experiences a deflecting torque $T_d = NABI$. The spiral spring produces an opposing torque $T_s = K\theta$. The pen tip traces a vertical deflection $y = L\sin\theta$ (or simply $y \propto \theta$) on the moving chart. (1 mark for block diagram, 1 mark for working, 5 marks for explanation of each block.) --- #### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks] **Derivation of steady-state deflection** (Refer to Section 3.1 above for full derivation): **Step 1 — Deflecting torque on PMMC coil:** $$T_d = N A B I$$ **Step 2 — Restoring torque of spiral spring:** $$T_s = K \theta$$ **Step 3 — At steady state, equilibrium of torques:** $$T_d = T_s \;\Rightarrow\; NABI = K\theta$$ $$\boxed{\theta = \dfrac{NAB}{K} \cdot I}$$ **Step 4 — Current sensitivity:** $$S_i = \dfrac{\theta}{I} = \dfrac{NAB}{K} \quad [\text{rad/A}]$$ **Step 5 — Voltage sensitivity with multiplier $R_s$ and coil resistance $R_g$:** $$I = \dfrac{V}{R_g + R_s}$$ $$S_v = \dfrac{\theta}{V} = \dfrac{NAB}{K(R_g + R_s)} \quad [\text{rad/V}]$$ **Conclusion:** Sensitivity rises with $NAB$ and falls with $K$. Increasing $B$ and $A$ improves sensitivity, but adds inertia and reduces bandwidth — a classic engineering trade-off. (2 marks for derivation, 2 marks for current sensitivity, 2 marks for voltage sensitivity, 1 mark for the trade-off conclusion.) --- ### 📝 Question B — Choice 2 (Alternative to Question A) **Q.B (a)** Explain the working of a PMMC-type galvanometric strip chart recorder with a neat diagram. Discuss the role of damping in such a recorder. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A strip chart recorder has the following specifications: chart width = 250 mm, chart speed = 25 mm/s. A sinusoidal signal of amplitude 100 mm peak and frequency 0.05 Hz is applied. Determine (i) the chart speed required to trace the signal without overlap, (ii) the maximum recordable frequency at full scale. **(7 marks)** **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Adapted]** --- #### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks] **Working principle:** The PMMC galvanometric recorder has a moving coil suspended in the uniform radial field of a permanent magnet. A spiral hairspring holds the coil at zero position. When the input signal current $I$ flows through the coil, a deflecting torque $T_d = NABI$ rotates the coil. The pen attached to the coil deflects, and the chart paper moves at a constant speed. The pen traces a curve whose y-coordinate is proportional to $I$ and whose x-coordinate represents time. **Role of Damping:** - **Damping** is the dissipation of kinetic energy from the moving coil, primarily as **eddy-current damping** in the aluminum former of the coil. - The equation of motion is $J \ddot{\theta} + D \dot{\theta} + K \theta = NABI$, with damping ratio $\zeta = D / (2\sqrt{KJ})$. - **Under-damped ($\zeta < 1$):** oscillatory overshoot, settling time high. - **Critically damped ($\zeta = 1$):** fastest non-oscillatory response. - **Over-damped ($\zeta > 1$):** sluggish response. - **Optimal for a strip chart recorder:** $\zeta \approx 0.7$, giving minimum settling time with negligible overshoot. (3 marks for working, 2 marks for damping concept, 2 marks for design choice and trade-off.) --- #### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks] **Given:** Chart width $W = 250$ mm, chart speed $v = 25$ mm/s, signal amplitude $A_p = 100$ mm peak, signal frequency $f = 0.05$ Hz. **Part (i) — Chart speed for no overlap:** The peak velocity of the pen tip due to the sinusoidal signal is: $$v_{\text{peak}} = 2\pi f A_p = 2\pi \times 0.05 \times 100 = 31.42 \text{ mm/s}$$ For **no overlap** (no retrace crossing), the chart must move at least as fast as the pen's peak velocity: $$v_{\text{chart,min}} \geq 31.42 \text{ mm/s}$$ The current chart speed is 25 mm/s, which is **insufficient** — the chart will overlap. Required speed: $$\boxed{v_{\text{chart,min}} \geq 31.42 \text{ mm/s}}$$ **Part (ii) — Maximum recordable frequency at full scale:** At full scale, pen amplitude = $W/2 = 125$ mm. $$f_{\max} = \dfrac{v_{\text{chart}}}{2\pi A_p} = \dfrac{25}{2\pi \times 125} = \dfrac{25}{785.4} \approx 0.0318 \text{ Hz}$$ $$\boxed{f_{\max} \approx 0.0318 \text{ Hz} \approx 31.8 \text{ mHz}}$$ (2 marks for (i) concept, 2 marks for calculation, 2 marks for (ii) formula, 1 mark for final value.) --- ## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Top 5 places where students LOSE marks on this topic:** > > 1. **Confusing $T_d$ derivation:** Many students write $T_d = BIl$ and forget the factor $N$ and the area $A$. KTU answer key insists on $T_d = NABI$. **Penalty: 2 marks.** > > 2. **Omitting the multiplier resistance in voltage sensitivity:** A common slip is writing $S_v = NAB/K$ instead of $S_v = NAB/[K(R_g+R_s)]$. **Penalty: 1 mark.** > > 3. **Writing the equation of motion without units of $J$, $D$, $K$:** The examiner expects $J$ in kg·m², $D$ in N·m·s/rad, $K$ in N·m/rad. If units are missing, **½ mark is cut.** > > 4. **Forgetting the chart speed requirement for the maximum-frequency question:** Many students compute $f_{\max}$ using the *chart width* instead of the **pen amplitude** $A_p = W/2$. **Penalty: 1–2 marks.** > > 5. **Confusing strip chart recorder with X-Y recorder:** When asked to "draw a strip chart recorder block diagram", do NOT draw an X-Y block diagram. The X-axis in a strip chart recorder is **TIME** — produced by the chart drive — not a separate input signal. > > **Pro Tip:** Always end your 14-mark answer with a **concluding line** summarizing the engineering trade-off (e.g., "Thus the strip chart recorder provides a permanent analog record at the cost of limited bandwidth"). KTU examiners reward that extra concluding statement with 1 mark. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **🚀 RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST — STRIP CHART RECORDER** ✅ **Definition:** Analog data presentation element producing a permanent, continuous, time-based record on a moving paper strip. ✅ **Four blocks:** Input conditioner → Range selector → Deflection (PMMC) → Chart drive. ✅ **Core equation of motion:** $J\ddot{\theta} + D\dot{\theta} + K\theta = NABI$ ✅ **Deflecting torque:** $T_d = NABI$ (memorize — KTU insists on this form). ✅ **Restoring torque:** $T_s = K\theta$ (Hooke's law for spiral spring). ✅ **Steady-state deflection:** $\theta = \dfrac{NAB}{K} I$ ✅ **Current sensitivity:** $S_i = \dfrac{NAB}{K}$ [rad/A] ✅ **Voltage sensitivity:** $S_v = \dfrac{NAB}{K(R_g + R_s)}$ [rad/V] ✅ **Natural frequency:** $f_n = \dfrac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\dfrac{K}{J}}$ ✅ **Damping ratio:** $\zeta = \dfrac{D}{2\sqrt{KJ}}$ → design target $\zeta \approx 0.7$. ✅ **Maximum recordable sine frequency:** $f_{\max} = \dfrac{v_{\text{chart}}}{2\pi A_p}$. ✅ **Dead band:** $\Delta I_{\text{min}}/I_{FS} \times 100$ %, typical 0.1–0.5 %. ✅ **Standard chart widths:** 50, 100, **250 mm**. ✅ **Drive types:** Spring clock, Synchronous motor, Stepper/D servo. ✅ **Writing mechanisms:** Ink pen, Thermal, Pressure, Electrostatic. ✅ **Static accuracy class:** ±0.25 % to ±1 % of FS. ✅ **Trade-off:** Higher sensitivity (larger $NAB$, smaller $K$) ⟶ more inertia, lower bandwidth. ✅ **Use cases:** ECG, EEG, process plant loggers, flight data, seismic, engine test cells. ✅ **One-liner for viva:** *"A strip chart recorder is a mechanical analog memory device that converts a current into a permanent time-stamped ink trace on a moving paper."* --- **End of Module 3 — Strip Chart Recorder Notes** *Prepared per KTU 2024 Scheme, NEP 2020 Outcome-Based Education framework.*

topics.title: Potentiometric Recorder
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET411 - ELECTRONIC INSTRUMENTATION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Potentiometric Recorder

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Potentiometric Recorder** (also known as a **Self-Balancing Potentiometer** or **Servo-Recorder**) is a precision analog recording instrument that operates on the **null-balance principle** to continuously measure, compare, and record slowly varying DC voltages (or DC currents converted to voltages). The instrument employs a precision reference voltage source, a servo-controlled feedback potentiometer, an error detector (comparator), a high-gain amplifier, and a two-phase servomotor coupled mechanically to a writing/printing mechanism.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Directive:** The potentiometric recorder belongs to the family of *active transducers / signal-conditioning recorders* and is classified under **Null-Type Measuring Instruments** in Module 3 (Recorders, Display Devices, and Data Acquisition Systems). The defining feature is the **automatic null-seeking servo loop**, which virtually eliminates loading errors on the source under measurement.

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The Automatic Tilt-Balancer

Imagine a long wooden plank balanced on a triangular fulcrum, like a seesaw. A small marble is placed somewhere on the plank, and you want to find exactly *where* it is. Instead of measuring its position directly, you keep sliding a counter-weight along the plank until the plank is perfectly horizontal (null position). At that moment, the counter-weight's position directly tells you where the marble is — and you didn't even have to "fight" the marble to find it.

The **Potentiometric Recorder** does the same thing electrically:
- The **unknown input voltage** $\left(V_{in}\right)$ is the "marble."
- The **feedback voltage** $\left(V_f\right)$ from a precision slide-wire is the "counter-weight."
- A **servo-motor** slides the wiper along the slide-wire until $V_f = V_{in}$ (the "plank is horizontal").
- The **pen position** on the paper chart is mechanically tied to the wiper — so the chart directly records the value of $V_{in}$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is this clever?** Because at the *null* (balanced) condition, the instrument draws **virtually zero current** from the source under measurement. This means the measurement is **non-loading** — the meter does not disturb the circuit it is measuring. This is the key advantage over a simple voltmeter.

### 1.3 Physical Constants, Standards, and Key Metrics

| Metric | Typical Value | Engineering Significance |
|---|---|---|
| **Input Impedance** | $\geq \mathbf{10\ M\Omega}$ | Prevents source loading |
| **Accuracy Class** | $\pm \mathbf{0.1\%}$ to $\pm \mathbf{0.25\%}$ of full scale | Industrial grade |
| **Lab-Grade Accuracy** | $\pm \mathbf{0.01\%}$ of full scale | Precision research |
| **Frequency Response** | DC to $\sim \mathbf{1\text{–}5\ Hz}$ | Limited by servo slew rate |
| **Chart Width** | $\mathbf{100\ mm}$, $\mathbf{250\ mm}$ | Standard KTU industrial sizes |
| **Chart Speeds** | $\mathbf{20\text{–}3600\ mm/hr}$ | Multi-speed drives |
| **Reference Standard** | **Weston Standard Cell** = $\mathbf{1.018636\ V}$ at 20°C | Calibration reference |

> [!NOTE]
> **Weston Cell** is the historical saturated cadmium-mercury standard cell. Modern instruments typically use **zener-diode-referenced electronic standards** for long-term stability, but the *principle* of a precision stable reference is identical.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Null-Balance Principle — Step-by-Step Logic

The working of a potentiometric recorder can be broken into **6 logical stages**:

- **Stage 1 — Reference Voltage Source Generation:** A highly stable DC reference voltage $V_{ref}$ is generated by a temperature-compensated zener diode source (in modern instruments) or a Weston cell (in classical instruments). This reference is applied across a precision slide-wire (or non-inductive wire-wound resistor with a sliding wiper).

- **Stage 2 — Wiper Position Creates a Fractional Voltage:** The wiper on the slide-wire picks off a fraction $\alpha$ (where $0 \leq \alpha \leq 1$) of the reference voltage, giving:
$$V_f = \alpha \cdot V_{ref}$$

  - $\alpha = 0$ → wiper at the LOW end
  - $\alpha = 1$ → wiper at the HIGH end
  - The **pen arm** is mechanically coupled to this wiper, so the pen position $x$ on the chart is directly proportional to $\alpha$.

- **Stage 3 — Error Detection (Comparison):** The unknown input voltage $V_{in}$ to be measured is applied to one input of a **chopper-stabilized DC amplifier** (or comparator), and the feedback voltage $V_f$ is applied to the other input. The difference is the **error voltage**:
$$V_e = V_{in} - V_f = V_{in} - \alpha \cdot V_{ref}$$

  - If $V_e \neq 0$, the system is *unbalanced*.
  - If $V_e = 0$, the system is at **null** and $V_{in} = \alpha \cdot V_{ref}$.

- **Stage 4 — Error Amplification:** The tiny error voltage $V_e$ (often in microvolts to millivolts) is amplified by a **high-gain AC chopper amplifier** (vibrating-reed chopper → AC → AC amplifier → phase-sensitive demodulator → DC). The chopper is essential because pure DC amplifiers suffer from drift.

- **Stage 5 — Servomotor Actuation:** The amplified error signal drives a **two-phase servomotor**. The phase of the AC output determines the **direction** of motor rotation, and the **magnitude** determines the **speed**. The motor mechanically drives the wiper along the slide-wire AND moves the pen across the chart.

  - If $V_{in} > V_f$ → motor rotates *one way*, wiper moves to *increase* $V_f$ → pen moves UP.
  - If $V_{in} < V_f$ → motor rotates *opposite way*, wiper moves to *decrease* $V_f$ → pen moves DOWN.

- **Stage 6 — Re-Balancing (Closed-Loop Convergence):** As the wiper moves, $V_f$ changes continuously, reducing $V_e$. The system converges exponentially toward $V_e \approx 0$. The pen position $x$ is now an analog record of $V_{in}$ on the moving chart paper.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The "Why" Behind the Chopper Amplifier:** Pure DC amplifiers have inherent **offset voltage drift** (1 µV/°C to tens of µV/°C) due to transistor mismatch and thermoelectric EMFs. By converting the DC error signal to AC using a mechanical chopper (vibrating reed at 50–400 Hz), the signal is shifted to a frequency where the AC amplifier is drift-free. It is then synchronously demodulated back to DC to drive the motor with the correct phase/polarity.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Exam Tip:** Memorize the table below. At least one numerical problem on a self-balancing potentiometer appears in nearly every KTU ESE cycle.

| # | Formula | Physical Meaning | Units / Condition |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $V_e = V_{in} - \alpha \cdot V_{ref}$ | Error signal driving the servo loop | Volts (V) |
| 2 | $V_{in} = \alpha \cdot V_{ref}$ | Null-balance measurement condition | Volts (V) |
| 3 | $\alpha = \dfrac{x}{L}$ | Wiper fraction = normalized pen position | Dimensionless |
| 4 | $V_{in} = \left(\dfrac{x}{L}\right) V_{ref}$ | Output equation of recorder | Volts (V) |
| 5 | $S = \dfrac{dx}{dV_{in}} = \dfrac{L}{V_{ref}}$ | Static sensitivity of pen to input voltage | mm/V |
| 6 | $R_{in,eff} = R_{in} \cdot (1 + A\beta)$ | Effective input impedance with negative feedback | Ohms ($\Omega$) |
| 7 | $\tau = \dfrac{J}{B + A K_t K_b}$ | Servomotor mechanical time constant (simplified) | seconds (s) |
| 8 | $f_{-3dB} = \dfrac{1}{2\pi\tau}$ | Useful closed-loop bandwidth | Hertz (Hz) |
| 9 | $\%\text{Error} = \dfrac{V_{in,meas} - V_{in,true}}{V_{in,FS}} \times 100$ | Static accuracy specification | % of Full Scale |
| 10 | $e_{null} = \dfrac{V_{in}}{A_{open\,loop}}$ | Residual null error due to finite amplifier gain | Volts (V) |

Where:
- $L$ = total length of chart travel (mm)
- $x$ = pen displacement from zero (mm)
- $A$ = open-loop gain of error amplifier
- $\beta$ = feedback factor = $1/V_{ref}$
- $J$ = motor + load moment of inertia (kg·m²)
- $B$ = viscous damping coefficient (N·m·s/rad)
- $K_t$ = motor torque constant (N·m/A)
- $K_b$ = motor back-EMF constant (V·s/rad)

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

The potentiometric recorder, though older than digital equivalents, is still encountered in:
- **Industrial process control rooms** for trend-recording of temperature, pressure, flow (when transducer outputs are DC voltages).
- **Laboratory strip-chart recorders** for biomedical signals (ECG, EMG — historically).
- **Calibration laboratories** as a secondary standard for DC voltage measurement.
- **Strain-gauge bridge output recorders** for static load-vs-deflection studies.
- **Modern hybrid form:** The principle survives in **digital potentiometric (ratio-metric) ADCs** used in every modern microcontroller's ADC reference circuit.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Output Equation of a Self-Balancing Potentiometer

**Given:**
- A stable reference voltage $V_{ref}$ is applied across a uniform slide-wire of total resistance $R$.
- A sliding wiper taps off a fraction $\alpha$ of $V_{ref}$ to form the feedback voltage $V_f$.
- The wiper position along a linear chart of length $L$ is at distance $x$ from the zero-end.
- The unknown input voltage $V_{in}$ is applied to the comparator.

**Step 1 — Express $V_f$ in terms of wiper position.**

Because the slide-wire is linear and uniform, the voltage drop per unit length is constant. Therefore the voltage at the wiper (relative to the low end) is directly proportional to the distance $x$:

$$V_f = V_{ref} \cdot \dfrac{x}{L}$$

This uses the fact that the wiper fraction $\alpha = \dfrac{x}{L}$.

**Step 2 — Apply the null-balance condition.**

At steady-state balance, the error voltage $V_e$ is driven to zero by the high-gain servo loop (in practice, to a negligibly small residual $V_e \to 0$):

$$V_e = V_{in} - V_f = 0$$

**Step 3 — Solve for the recorded quantity.**

Substituting Step 1 into Step 2 and solving for $x$ (the pen displacement, which is what is recorded on the chart):

$$V_{in} = V_{ref} \cdot \dfrac{x}{L}$$

$$\therefore\quad x = \left(\dfrac{L}{V_{ref}}\right) V_{in}$$

**Step 4 — Identify the sensitivity.**

The static sensitivity of the pen displacement per unit input voltage is:

$$S = \dfrac{dx}{dV_{in}} = \dfrac{L}{V_{ref}} \quad [\text{mm/V}]$$

**Interpretation:** A larger chart width $L$ and a smaller reference voltage $V_{ref}$ both increase sensitivity. However, reducing $V_{ref}$ reduces the input range, so the design involves a **sensitivity–range trade-off**.

---

### 3.2 Derivation of Effective Input Resistance with Negative Feedback

**Why this matters:** A naïve comparator presents only the input resistance of the chopper amplifier (typically ~1 MΩ), which still loads sensitive sources. The negative-feedback loop dramatically raises the *effective* input impedance.

**Step 1 — Open-loop relation at the comparator input.**

Without feedback, the error amplifier sees the difference between $V_{in}$ and $V_f$. The current drawn from the source $V_{in}$ is:

$$i_{in} = \dfrac{V_{in} - V_f}{R_{in,amp}}$$

where $R_{in,amp}$ is the bare input resistance of the error amplifier.

**Step 2 — Closed-loop relation.**

With negative feedback, at balance $V_f \approx V_{in}$, so $V_{in} - V_f \to 0$. The amplifier gain $A$ raises this microscopic difference to drive the motor. The effective input resistance is defined as:

$$R_{in,eff} = \dfrac{V_{in}}{i_{in}}$$

Using the feedback equation $V_f = \beta \cdot V_o$ (where $V_o$ is the amplifier output driving the motor, and $\beta = 1/V_{ref}$), and writing the open-loop equation $V_o = A \cdot (V_{in} - V_f)$:

$$V_f = \beta A (V_{in} - V_f) \quad\Rightarrow\quad V_f (1 + A\beta) = A\beta \cdot V_{in} \quad\Rightarrow\quad V_f = \left(\dfrac{A\beta}{1 + A\beta}\right) V_{in}$$

**Step 3 — The input current becomes:**

$$i_{in} = \dfrac{V_{in} - V_f}{R_{in,amp}} = \dfrac{V_{in}}{R_{in,amp}} \cdot \left[1 - \dfrac{A\beta}{1 + A\beta}\right] = \dfrac{V_{in}}{R_{in,amp} \cdot (1 + A\beta)}$$

**Step 4 — Therefore the effective input resistance is:**

$$\boxed{R_{in,eff} = R_{in,amp} \cdot (1 + A\beta)}$$

**Numerical Insight:** If $R_{in,amp} = 1\ \text{M}\Omega$, $A = 10^{5}$, and $\beta = 1/V_{ref} = 0.1\ \text{V}^{-1}$ (for $V_{ref} = 10$ V):

$$R_{in,eff} = 10^6 \cdot (1 + 10^5 \cdot 0.1) = 10^6 \cdot 10^4 = 10^{10}\ \Omega = \mathbf{10\ G\Omega}$$

This is precisely why the potentiometric recorder is prized for **non-loading measurement** — a critical property when measuring thermocouple, pH-electrode, or strain-gauge bridge outputs.

---

### 3.3 Derivation of Slew Rate and Dynamic Response

The servomotor + wiper system has a moment of inertia $J$ and viscous friction $B$. With the feedback loop closed, the equation of motion is:

$$J \dfrac{d^2\theta}{dt^2} + \left(B + A K_t K_b\right) \dfrac{d\theta}{dt} = A K_t \cdot V_e(t)$$

**Step 1 — Recognize that the effective damping has been increased by the term $A K_t K_b$.**

The closed-loop time constant becomes:

$$\tau = \dfrac{J}{B + A K_t K_b} \approx \dfrac{J}{A K_t K_b} \quad \text{(for high loop gain)}$$

**Step 2 — For a step input $V_{in} = V_0 \cdot u(t)$, the wiper position $\theta(t)$ rises exponentially:**

$$\theta(t) = \theta_{\infty} \left[1 - e^{-t/\tau}\right]$$

**Step 3 — The maximum slew rate occurs at $t = 0$:**

$$\left.\dfrac{d\theta}{dt}\right|_{max} = \dfrac{\theta_{\infty}}{\tau} = \dfrac{A K_t \cdot V_{in,FS}}{B + A K_t K_b}$$

**Interpretation:** Higher amplifier gain $A$ ⇒ faster response **but** also greater tendency to overshoot. Damping is engineered by adjusting $B$ and $K_b$.

---

### 3.4 Worked Example — Typical KTU Numerical

> **Problem:** A self-balancing potentiometric recorder uses a reference voltage of $V_{ref} = 1.5$ V across a 250 mm chart. The servo-loop amplifier gain is $A = 2 \times 10^4$. Compute:
> (i) the chart displacement for an input of 0.75 V,
> (ii) the residual null error in volts, and
> (iii) the effective input resistance if the bare amplifier input resistance is 2 MΩ.

**Solution:**

**(i) Displacement:**
Using $x = \left(\dfrac{L}{V_{ref}}\right) V_{in}$:

$$x = \left(\dfrac{250}{1.5}\right) \times 0.75 = 166.67 \times 0.75 = \mathbf{125\ mm}$$

The pen will move to the **midpoint** of the 250 mm chart, as expected since 0.75 V is exactly half of 1.5 V.

**(ii) Residual null error:**

$$e_{null} = \dfrac{V_{in}}{A} = \dfrac{0.75}{2 \times 10^4} = \mathbf{3.75 \times 10^{-5}\ V = 37.5\ \mu V}$$

This is the small error that remains because the amplifier has finite gain. It is negligible for most industrial purposes.

**(iii) Effective input resistance:**

$$\beta = \dfrac{1}{V_{ref}} = \dfrac{1}{1.5} = 0.667\ \text{V}^{-1}$$

$$R_{in,eff} = 2 \times 10^6 \times (1 + 2 \times 10^4 \times 0.667) = 2 \times 10^6 \times 13333.3 \approx \mathbf{2.67 \times 10^{10}\ \Omega = 26.7\ G\Omega}$$

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram of a Self-Balancing Potentiometric Recorder

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    VIN["V_in UNKNOWN DC SIGNAL"]:::input
    CHOP["CHOPPER MODULATOR 50 TO 400 HZ"]:::stage
    ACAMP["HIGH GAIN AC AMPLIFIER"]:::stage
    DEMOD["PHASE SENSITIVE DEMODULATOR"]:::stage
    DCAMP["DC POWER AMPLIFIER"]:::stage
    MOTOR["TWO PHASE SERVOMOTOR"]:::actuator
    WIPER["SLIDE WIRE WITH WIPER MECHANICAL LINK TO PEN"]:::feedback
    PEN["PEN ON MOVING CHART PAPER"]:::output
    VREF["STABLE DC REFERENCE SOURCE ZENER OR WESTON CELL"]:::ref
    VFB["V_f FEEDBACK VOLTAGE TO COMPARATOR"]:::feedback
    CMP["ERROR DETECTOR COMPARATOR"]:::stage

    VIN --> CMP
    VFB --> CMP
    VREF --> WIPER
    WIPER --> VFB
    CMP -- V_e = V_in MINUS V_f --> CHOP
    CHOP --> ACAMP
    ACAMP --> DEMOD
    DEMOD --> DCAMP
    DCAMP --> MOTOR
    MOTOR --> WIPER
    MOTOR --> PEN
    WIPER -.FEEDBACK LOOP.-> VFB

    classDef input fill:#E3F2FD,stroke:#1565C0,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef ref fill:#FFF8E1,stroke:#F57C00,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef stage fill:#E8F5E9,stroke:#2E7D32,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef actuator fill:#FCE4EC,stroke:#AD1457,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef feedback fill:#F3E5F5,stroke:#6A1B9A,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef output fill:#E0F7FA,stroke:#00838F,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

### 4.2 Signal-Flow / Closed-Loop Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph INPUTSTAGE["INPUT AND COMPARISON STAGE"]
        direction TB
        S1["V_in FROM TRANSDUCER"] --> J1["SUMMING JUNCTION"]
        S2["V_f FROM SLIDE WIRE"] --> J1
    end

    subgraph AMPSTAGE["AMPLIFICATION CHAIN"]
        direction TB
        J1 -- V_e ERROR --> M1["CHOPPER"]
        M1 --> A1["AC AMPLIFIER GAIN A"]
        A1 --> D1["SYNCHRONOUS DEMODULATOR"]
        D1 --> A2["DC POWER AMPLIFIER"]
    end

    subgraph ACTUATIONSTAGE["MECHANICAL ACTUATION"]
        direction TB
        A2 -- DRIVE VOLTAGE --> M2["TWO PHASE SERVOMOTOR"]
        M2 -- TORQUE --> W1["WIPER GEAR TRAIN"]
        M2 -- MECHANICAL LINK --> P1["PEN ASSEMBLY"]
    end

    subgraph FEEDBACKSTAGE["FEEDBACK AND RECORDING"]
        direction TB
        W1 -- POSITION --> SW1["PRECISION SLIDE WIRE"]
        SW1 -- V_f FRACTION OF V_ref --> J1
        P1 -- INK ON PAPER --> CH1["MOVING CHART"]
    end

    REF1["V_ref STABLE DC SOURCE"] --> SW1

    classDef inputnode fill:#BBDEFB,stroke:#0D47A1,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef ampnode fill:#C8E6C9,stroke:#1B5E20,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef actnode fill:#FFCDD2,stroke:#B71C1C,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef refnode fill:#FFF9C4,stroke:#F57F17,stroke-width:2px,color:#000

    class S1,S2 inputnode
    class M1,A1,D1,A2 ampnode
    class M2,W1,P1 actnode
    class REF1 refnode
```

### 4.3 Error-Vector Diagram at the Comparator (Conceptual)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["V_in VECTOR"] -- PLUS INPUT --> SUM["SUMMING POINT"]
    B["V_f VECTOR OPPOSITE POLARITY"] -- MINUS INPUT --> SUM
    SUM -- DIFFERENCE --> C["V_e = V_in MINUS V_f"]
    C -- NON ZERO --> ACT["SERVO DRIVES WIPER"]
    ACT -- ADJUSTS --> B
    ACT -- KEEPS MOVING UNTIL --> D["V_e APPROACHES ZERO NULL"]
    D -- THEN --> E["PEN POSITION EQUALS V_in DIVIDED BY V_ref TIMES CHART WIDTH"]

    classDef vec fill:#E1F5FE,stroke:#01579B,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef zero fill:#C8E6C9,stroke:#1B5E20,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef out fill:#FFE0B2,stroke:#E65100,stroke-width:2px,color:#000

    class A,B,C,ACT vec
    class D zero
    class E out
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagrams:** The first block diagram shows the **linear signal path** of the servo loop. The second diagram emphasizes the **closed-loop feedback topology** and the **mechanical cross-coupling** between the wiper and the pen. The third diagram illustrates the **error-cancellation philosophy** at the heart of null-balance instruments.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**[Q.A.1] [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

> *What is a self-balancing potentiometer? Why is it called "null-type"?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A self-balancing potentiometer is a recording instrument that **automatically** maintains a null (zero) condition between an unknown input voltage $V_{in}$ and a feedback voltage $V_f$ tapped from a precision slide-wire driven by a servomotor.
It is called "null-type" because **at the measurement condition, the error voltage $V_e = V_{in} - V_f$ is driven to zero** by the closed-loop servo action. At null, the instrument draws negligible current from the source, giving high accuracy and non-loading measurement.
*(1 mark for definition, 1 mark for servo-loop mention, 1 mark for null-condition + non-loading justification.)*

---

**[Q.A.2] [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

> *State the function of the chopper in a self-balancing potentiometric recorder.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The chopper is an electromechanical or electronic switch that **converts the DC error signal into an AC signal** at a fixed carrier frequency (typically 50–400 Hz).
Its function is to **eliminate DC drift** in the high-gain amplifier. Pure DC amplifiers suffer from offset-voltage drift due to temperature changes and transistor mismatches; by modulating the error to AC, the signal can be amplified by a drift-free AC amplifier and then synchronously demodulated back to DC.
*(1 mark for conversion purpose, 1 mark for drift elimination, 1 mark for AC amplification + phase-sensitive demodulation.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — Option 1**

**[Q.B.1.A] [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Adapted]**

> **(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the construction and working of a self-balancing potentiometric recorder. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** Derive the expression for the effective input resistance of the recorder and show why the instrument is essentially a non-loading device. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Construction and Working (7 marks):**

The instrument consists of the following blocks, shown in Section 4.1 above:
1. **Reference voltage source** $V_{ref}$: temperature-compensated zener source (or Weston cell) provides a highly stable DC reference. (1 mark)
2. **Slide-wire / precision potentiometer**: a uniform resistance element with a sliding wiper; $V_f = \alpha V_{ref}$. (1 mark)
3. **Comparator (error detector)**: subtracts $V_f$ from $V_{in}$ to produce error voltage $V_e$. (1 mark)
4. **Chopper-stabilized DC amplifier**: modulates $V_e$ to AC, amplifies it with a high-gain drift-free AC stage, and demodulates synchronously. (1 mark)
5. **Two-phase servomotor**: receives amplified $V_e$; rotation direction is determined by phase, speed by magnitude. (1 mark)
6. **Pen and chart mechanism**: mechanically coupled to the wiper; records pen position on a moving chart paper. (1 mark)
7. **Working summary:** The motor drives the wiper to reduce $V_e$ to zero. At null, $V_{in} = \alpha V_{ref}$, and the pen position $x = (L/V_{ref}) \cdot V_{in}$ directly records $V_{in}$. (1 mark)

**(b) Effective Input Resistance Derivation (7 marks):**

From the closed-loop analysis (Section 3.2):

**Step 1:** Define the feedback factor $\beta = 1/V_{ref}$, so that $V_f = \beta V_o$ where $V_o$ is the amplifier output. (1 mark)

**Step 2:** Write the open-loop equation: $V_o = A(V_{in} - V_f)$. (1 mark)

**Step 3:** Substitute $V_f = \beta V_o$: $V_o = A(V_{in} - \beta V_o) \Rightarrow V_o(1 + A\beta) = A V_{in} \Rightarrow V_f = \dfrac{A\beta}{1 + A\beta} V_{in}$. (2 marks)

**Step 4:** The input current is $i_{in} = \dfrac{V_{in} - V_f}{R_{in,amp}} = \dfrac{V_{in}}{R_{in,amp}(1 + A\beta)}$. (1 mark)

**Step 5:** Therefore, the effective input resistance is:
$$R_{in,eff} = \dfrac{V_{in}}{i_{in}} = R_{in,amp}(1 + A\beta)$$
(1 mark)

**Step 6:** For typical values ($A = 10^5$, $\beta = 0.1$ V$^{-1}$, $R_{in,amp} = 1$ MΩ), $R_{in,eff} = 10^6 \times 10^4 = 10^{10}\ \Omega = 10$ GΩ. Because the input draws a vanishingly small current, the instrument is a **non-loading** measurement device. (1 mark)

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Option 2 (Internal Choice Alternative)**

**[Q.B.1.B] [KTU University Exam – May 2023, Adapted]**

> **(a)** List and explain the various functional blocks of a self-balancing potentiometric recorder. Compare it with a conventional deflection-type voltmeter. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A potentiometric recorder has a chart width of 200 mm and is calibrated using a reference voltage of 2.0 V. The amplifier open-loop gain is $5 \times 10^4$. For an input voltage of 1.4 V, find (i) the pen displacement, (ii) the residual null error, and (iii) the effective input resistance if the bare amplifier input resistance is 1.5 MΩ. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Functional Blocks and Comparison (7 marks):**

**Functional Blocks** (1.5 marks each, brief): Reference source, slide-wire with wiper, error detector, chopper-stabilized amplifier, two-phase servomotor, pen-chart mechanism — as detailed in Section 2.1 and the block diagram of Section 4.1.

**Comparison with Deflection-Type Voltmeter** (1 mark per key point, max 4 marks):

| Aspect | Self-Balancing Potentiometric Recorder | Conventional Deflection-Type Voltmeter |
|---|---|---|
| Principle | Null-balance (zero-deflection) | Direct deflection of pointer |
| Loading effect | Negligible (10 GΩ input) | Significant (20 kΩ/V to 200 kΩ/V) |
| Accuracy | ±0.1% FS or better | ±2% to ±5% FS typically |
| Speed of response | Slow (limited by servo) | Fast (instantaneous) |
| Recording | Continuous on chart | Pointer only (or with separate recorder) |
| Power consumption from source | Extremely low | Measurable (drives the meter movement) |
| Mechanical complexity | High (motor, gears, slide-wire) | Low (only D'Arsonval movement) |

**(b) Numerical (7 marks):**

**Given:** $L = 200$ mm, $V_{ref} = 2.0$ V, $A = 5 \times 10^4$, $V_{in} = 1.4$ V, $R_{in,amp} = 1.5$ MΩ.

**(i) Pen displacement:** (3 marks)
$$x = \left(\dfrac{L}{V_{ref}}\right) V_{in} = \left(\dfrac{200}{2.0}\right) \times 1.4 = 100 \times 1.4 = \mathbf{140\ mm}$$

The pen moves to 140 mm, which is 70% of full-scale.

**(ii) Residual null error:** (2 marks)
$$e_{null} = \dfrac{V_{in}}{A} = \dfrac{1.4}{5 \times 10^4} = \mathbf{2.8 \times 10^{-5}\ V = 28\ \mu V}$$

**(iii) Effective input resistance:** (2 marks)
$$\beta = \dfrac{1}{V_{ref}} = 0.5\ \text{V}^{-1}$$
$$R_{in,eff} = R_{in,amp} (1 + A\beta) = 1.5 \times 10^6 \times (1 + 5 \times 10^4 \times 0.5)$$
$$= 1.5 \times 10^6 \times 25001 \approx \mathbf{3.75 \times 10^{10}\ \Omega = 37.5\ G\Omega}$$

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls in Potentiometric Recorder Questions:**
>
> 1. **Confusing "null" with "no signal":** Many students incorrectly write that at null, the amplifier output is zero. *Correction:* The amplifier output is NOT zero — it is exactly enough to drive the servomotor to hold the wiper at the balancing position. The **error** $V_e$ is zero, not the amplifier output.
>
> 2. **Forgetting the chopper:** When asked "why is a chopper used?", students often answer vaguely "to amplify the signal." The correct answer is **"to convert DC to AC to avoid amplifier drift."** Examiners specifically look for the **drift-elimination** keyword.
>
> 3. **Mixing up input resistance formulae:** Confusing $R_{in,eff} = R_{in,amp}(1 + A\beta)$ with a plain $R_{in,amp}$ is a common 2-mark deduction.
>
> 4. **Wrong sign in error equation:** $V_e = V_{in} - V_f$, NOT $V_f - V_{in}$. Direction of motor rotation depends on this sign convention.
>
> 5. **Forgetting units in the numerical:** $x$ must come out in **mm**, $e_{null}$ in **V or µV**, $R_{in,eff}$ in **Ω**. Unit-less answers are penalized.
>
> 6. **Skipping the block diagram in 7-mark sub-questions:** Even with a perfect derivation, **no block diagram = 1–2 marks deducted** for the construction part. Always include a labeled block diagram.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- ✅ A **Potentiometric Recorder** is a **null-balance (zero-deflection)** instrument — its defining feature is the **automatic servo loop** that drives an error voltage to zero.
- ✅ The core output equation is $V_{in} = \left(\dfrac{x}{L}\right) V_{ref}$, giving pen displacement $x = \left(\dfrac{L}{V_{ref}}\right) V_{in}$.
- ✅ Sensitivity $S = L / V_{ref}$ [mm/V]; higher chart width and lower $V_{ref}$ ⇒ higher sensitivity (but reduced range).
- ✅ Block diagram: **Reference source → Slide-wire → Comparator → Chopper → AC amplifier → Demodulator → DC amplifier → Servomotor → Wiper + Pen** (with feedback from wiper back to comparator).
- ✅ The **chopper** converts DC error to AC to enable drift-free AC amplification — this is the single most exam-frequent sub-topic.
- ✅ The **two-phase servomotor** has rotation direction set by the **phase** of the AC drive and speed by the **magnitude**.
- ✅ Effective input resistance $R_{in,eff} = R_{in,amp}(1 + A\beta)$ — this is the *reason* the recorder is **non-loading** and can measure high-impedance sources (thermocouples, pH electrodes).
- ✅ Residual null error $e_{null} = V_{in} / A$ — typically tens of microvolts — sets the practical accuracy limit.
- ✅ Closed-loop bandwidth $f_{-3dB} = 1/(2\pi\tau)$ is limited by motor inertia $J$ and damping; hence the recorder is **slow** (DC to a few Hz only).
- ✅ Compared to a **deflection-type voltmeter**, it has **higher accuracy, higher input impedance, and built-in recording**, but is **slower, more complex, and more expensive**.
- ✅ Real-world uses: process control trend recording, calibration labs, strain-gauge bridge outputs, and (historically) biomedical signal strip charts.
- ✅ Modern descendant: **ratio-metric / potentiometric ADC** principle in microcontrollers — same null-balance philosophy, but digital.

---

> **End of Notes — Potentiometric Recorder | PEEET411 Electronic Instrumentation | KTU 2024 Scheme**

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: X-Y Recorder
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET411 - ELECTRONIC INSTRUMENTATION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** X-Y Recorder

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

An **X-Y Recorder** is a two-axis, self-balancing, servo-operated analog recording instrument that plots the instantaneous functional relationship between two mutually independent electrical input variables (one applied to the horizontal **X-axis** and the other to the vertical **Y-axis**) on a stationary sheet of graph paper. Unlike a strip-chart recorder, the X-Y recorder does not advance the paper with time; instead, the time variable is replaced by a second physical variable, allowing direct graphical representation of the dependency $Y = f(X)$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The X-Y recorder is studied as a critical **null-balance servo instrument** in Module 3 (Signal Conditioning, Recorders & Displays). Students must master its block diagram, working principle, and distinguishing features relative to strip-chart recorders.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a child drawing on a whiteboard with two giant robotic hands. One hand pushes the pen **left and right** according to how hard you push it horizontally, while a second hand pushes the pen **up and down** according to how hard you push it vertically. If two friends are pulling the pen at the same time — one friend pulling along the horizontal direction and another along the vertical — the pen will trace a curve that is a direct map of *"how the vertical force relates to the horizontal force at every instant."*

- The **horizontal friend's pull** = the X-input signal (Volts).
- The **vertical friend's pull** = the Y-input signal (Volts).
- The **curve the pen draws** = the instantaneous relationship $Y$ vs. $X$.
- The **whiteboard** = stationary chart paper.

This is exactly how an X-Y recorder works: two independent servos (one per axis) fight/balance to position a single pen based on the *instantaneous* values of the two input signals, with **no time axis** involved.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Distinction from a Strip-Chart Recorder:** A strip-chart recorder has *one* servo and a *time-driven* paper advance. An X-Y recorder has *two* servos and *no* paper advance — both axes are pure data variables.

### 1.3 Key Performance Parameters (KTU Standard Metrics)

- **Input range:** typically **0–10 V** or **0–1 mA** per axis (with attenuators to expand to higher ranges like **0–100 V** or **0–100 mA**).
- **Accuracy:** Class **±0.25 %** of full scale.
- **Frequency response / Slew rate:** limited to low frequencies (DC to a few Hz, typically up to **5 Hz** for full-scale deflection).
- **Chart size:** standard A4/A3 (e.g., 250 mm × 180 mm).
- **Dead zone (sensitivity threshold):** typically **0.1 %** of full scale.
- **Pen speed:** ~**0.5 m/s** maximum.
- **Settling time:** typically **< 1 second** for full-scale step.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operating Principle — The Null-Balance Servo Loop

The X-Y recorder is essentially **two independent, identical self-balancing potentiometric servo systems** that share a common pen carriage. Each axis operates on the **closed-loop null-balance principle**.

**Step-by-step operational logic for ONE axis (say the X-axis):**

1. **Input signal** $V_x$ (from a transducer or signal conditioner) is applied to the input attenuator.
2. The **attenuated signal** $V_{x,att}$ is fed to one end of a precision reference potentiometer.
3. A **wiper** on the reference potentiometer taps off a **feedback voltage** $V_{fb,x}$ that is proportional to the carriage's current physical position.
4. The difference (error) is computed in a **comparator (chopper-stabilized DC amplifier)**:

$$e_x = V_{x,att} - V_{fb,x}$$

5. The error $e_x$ is amplified by a high-gain **servo amplifier** (typically a chopper-stabilized AC or DC amplifier) and applied to a **DC servo motor**.
6. The motor rotates a **lead screw**, which translates the pen carriage (and the feedback wiper) along the X-axis.
7. Motion continues until the feedback voltage **exactly balances** the input — i.e., $V_{fb,x} \approx V_{x,att}$, making $e_x \to 0$. The motor stalls.
8. The pen's new X-position is now an analog of the input voltage $V_x$.

The Y-axis follows an identical independent loop, but its input is $V_y$ and it drives the pen vertically.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why null-balance?** When the pen settles, the error is essentially zero. This makes the system immune to amplifier gain drift, nonlinearity, and component aging — the accuracy depends almost entirely on the **precision of the reference potentiometer** and the **stability of the reference voltage source**.

### 2.2 Functional Block Diagram — Component-Level Walkthrough

| Block # | Block Name | Function | Engineering Insight |
|:-:|:--|:--|:--|
| 1 | **Input Attenuator (X and Y)** | Scales high-magnitude inputs to a standard range (~1 V) for the comparator. | Allows the recorder to handle inputs from microvolts to hundreds of volts using plug-in range cards. |
| 2 | **Reference Voltage Source** | A stable, low-drift DC reference (e.g., Zener-stabilized). | Sets the absolute calibration of the chart; long-term stability is critical. |
