| Flash (Parallel) | Comparators in parallel | Fastest | Low (typically 4–8 bit) | Oscilloscopes, video |
| Successive Approximation (SAR) | Binary search via DAC | Medium-high | High (8–18 bit) | Multimeters, DAQ |
| Dual-Slope Integrating | Integrate then de-integrate | Slow | Very high (16–24 bit) | Digital voltmeters |
| Counter / Tracking | Ramp comparison | Slow | Medium | Simple panel meters |

**Resolution & Quantization:**

$$\text{Resolution (V/LSB)} = \frac{V_{FS}}{2^n}$$

where $n$ is the number of bits.

**DAC (Digital-to-Analog Converter):**
The most common KTU-asked type is the **R-2R Ladder DAC** (simple, monotonic, easy to integrate).

For an $n$-bit DAC with reference $V_{ref}$:

$$V_{out} = V_{ref} \cdot \frac{D}{2^n}$$

where $D$ is the digital input word (0 to $2^n - 1$).

### 2.6 Oscillators — The Timekeeper of Instrumentation

A **sine-wave oscillator** generates a stable sinusoidal signal used as a carrier (e.g., in LVDT excitation) or timebase. The **Barkhausen Criterion** governs sustained oscillation:

1. **Loop gain magnitude:** $\vert A\beta \vert = 1$
2. **Loop phase shift:** $\angle A\beta = 0^\circ \text{ (or } 360^\circ \text{)}$

**Common Types in KTU Syllabus:**

| **Oscillator** | **Frequency Formula** | **Typical Frequency Range** |
|----------------|------------------------|------------------------------|
| RC Phase Shift | $f = \frac{1}{2\pi RC \sqrt{6}}$ | Audio (Hz – kHz) |
| Wien Bridge | $f = \frac{1}{2\pi RC}$ | Audio (10 Hz – 100 kHz) |
| LC (Hartley/Colpitts) | $f = \frac{1}{2\pi \sqrt{LC_{eq}}}$ | RF (kHz – MHz) |
| Crystal | Set by crystal cut | Fixed, very stable |
| 555 Timer (astable) | $f \approx \frac{1.44}{(R_1+2R_2)C}$ | Wide range |

### 2.7 Comparators, Multiplexers, and Clamps

- **Comparator:** Op-amp with no feedback — outputs $\pm V_{sat}$ depending on which input is larger. Used in **zero-crossing detectors**, **ADC**, and **window discriminators**.
- **Multiplexer (MUX):** An analog/digital switch that connects one of $2^n$ inputs to a single output. Used in **multi-channel data acquisition**.
- **Demultiplexer (DEMUX):** Routes one input to one of $2^n$ outputs.
- **Clamper (DC Restorer):** Shifts a waveform's DC level without changing its shape.  
  $V_{out} = V_{in} \pm V_{clamp}$ (sign depends on diode orientation).
- **Clipper (Limiter):** Removes a portion of the signal above/below a reference level. Used for **over-voltage protection** and **waveform shaping**.

### 2.8 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat-Sheet

| **#** | **Formula / Relation** | **Where Used** | **Units** |
|-------|------------------------|----------------|-----------|
| 1 | $A_{dB} = 20 \log_{10}(V_{out}/V_{in})$ | Gain in dB | dB |
| 2 | $A_{v,\text{inverting}} = -R_f/R_{in}$ | Inverting amp | unitless |
| 3 | $A_{v,\text{non-inv}} = 1 + R_f/R_{in}$ | Non-inverting amp | unitless |
| 4 | $f_c = 1/(2\pi RC)$ | RC filter cutoff | Hz |
| 5 | $V_D = V_s(R_1/(R_1+R_2) - R_3/(R_3+R_4))$ | Wheatstone bridge | V |
| 6 | $V_D \approx V_s \cdot \delta R / (4R)$ | Small-signal bridge | V |
| 7 | $\text{Resolution} = V_{FS}/2^n$ | ADC/DAC step | V/LSB |
| 8 | $V_{out,DAC} = V_{ref} \cdot D / 2^n$ | DAC output | V |
| 9 | $\text{GBP} = A_{OL} \times f$ | Op-amp bandwidth limit | Hz |
| 10 | $f_{osc,Wien} = 1/(2\pi RC)$ | Wien-bridge oscillator | Hz |
| 11 | $f_{osc,555} \approx 1.44/((R_1+2R_2)C)$ | 555 astable | Hz |
| 12 | $V_{out,\text{clamp}} = V_{in} \pm V_{clamp}$ | Clamper output | V |
| 13 | $\text{CMRR} = 20 \log(A_d/A_c)$ | Op-amp/IA | dB |
| 14 | $V_{out,\text{sum}} = -R_f \sum (V_i/R_i)$ | Summing amp | V |
| 15 | $V_{out,\text{int}} = -\frac{1}{RC}\int V_{in}\, dt$ | Op-amp integrator | V |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> The derivations below are **board-style**: every algebraic step is shown, every logical transition is annotated, and the final result is in the form you would write on your answer paper.

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Gain of an Inverting Op-Amp

**Given circuit:** Standard inverting amplifier with input resistor $R_{in}$ at the inverting terminal, feedback resistor $R_f$ between output and inverting input, non-inverting terminal grounded.

**Step 1: Apply the golden rule — no current flows into the op-amp inputs.**

Therefore, the same current that flows through $R_{in}$ must flow through $R_f$.

$$I_{in} = I_f$$

**Step 2: Express each current in terms of node voltages.**

Let the inverting node be $V^-$ and the output be $V_{out}$.

$$I_{in} = \frac{V_{in} - V^-}{R_{in}}$$

$$I_f = \frac{V^- - V_{out}}{R_f}$$

**Step 3: Apply the second golden rule — virtual short, $V^- = V^+ = 0$ (since $V^+$ is grounded).**

Therefore $V^- = 0$ (this node is called **virtual ground**).

$$I_{in} = \frac{V_{in} - 0}{R_{in}} = \frac{V_{in}}{R_{in}}$$

$$I_f = \frac{0 - V_{out}}{R_f} = -\frac{V_{out}}{R_f}$$

**Step 4: Equate and solve for $V_{out}$.**

$$\frac{V_{in}}{R_{in}} = -\frac{V_{out}}{R_f}$$

$$V_{out} = -\frac{R_f}{R_{in}} V_{in}$$

**Step 5: Voltage gain (closed-loop):**

$$A_v = \frac{V_{out}}{V_{in}} = -\frac{R_f}{R_{in}}$$

The negative sign indicates a 180° phase inversion — this is the **hallmark** of the inverting configuration and is a frequent 2–3 mark question in KTU.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Limitation:** In the ideal case the input impedance of the inverting amplifier is $R_{in}$ (NOT infinite), which is a known drawback compared to the non-inverting configuration.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Cut-off Frequency of a First-Order RC Low-Pass Filter

**Given circuit:** Series resistor $R$ followed by shunt capacitor $C$ to ground; input $V_{in}$ across the series combination, output $V_{out}$ across $C$.

**Step 1: Apply the voltage divider rule in the s-domain (Laplace).**

The impedance of the capacitor is $Z_C = 1/(sC)$.

$$V_{out}(s) = V_{in}(s) \cdot \frac{Z_C}{R + Z_C} = V_{in}(s) \cdot \frac{1/(sC)}{R + 1/(sC)}$$

**Step 2: Simplify the transfer function.**

Multiply numerator and denominator by $sC$:

$$\frac{V_{out}(s)}{V_{in}(s)} = \frac{1}{1 + sRC}$$

**Step 3: Substitute $s = j\omega$ for sinusoidal steady-state.**

$$H(j\omega) = \frac{V_{out}(j\omega)}{V_{in}(j\omega)} = \frac{1}{1 + j\omega RC}$$

**Step 4: Find the magnitude of the transfer function.**

$$\vert H(j\omega) \vert = \frac{1}{\sqrt{1 + (\omega RC)^2}}$$

**Step 5: Define cut-off frequency as the point where $\vert H \vert = 1/\sqrt{2}$ (i.e., –3 dB).**

Set $\omega RC = 1$:

$$\omega_c RC = 1 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \omega_c = \frac{1}{RC}$$

**Step 6: Convert to Hz using $\omega = 2\pi f$.**

$$\boxed{\,f_c = \frac{1}{2\pi R C}\,}$$

**Step 7: Verify limiting behaviour (sanity check).**

- At $\omega \to 0$ (DC): $\vert H \vert = 1$ → passes DC. ✓
- At $\omega \to \infty$ (high freq): $\vert H \vert \to 0$ → blocks high freq. ✓
- Roll-off: when $\omega$ increases by factor 10, $\vert H \vert$ drops by factor 10, i.e., **–20 dB/decade**. ✓

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Output Voltage of an Unbalanced Wheatstone Bridge

**Given circuit:** Four arms $R_1, R_2, R_3, R_4$ form a quadrilateral. Source $V_s$ applied between top node A and bottom node C. Detector measures $V_D = V_B - V_D'$ between the other two nodes B and D.

**Step 1: Identify the two voltage dividers.**

Path A → B → C: forms a divider with $R_1$ (top) and $R_2$ (bottom).

$$V_B = V_s \cdot \frac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2}$$

Path A → D → C: forms a divider with $R_3$ (top) and $R_4$ (bottom).

$$V_D = V_s \cdot \frac{R_4}{R_3 + R_4}$$

**Step 2: Detector voltage is the difference.**

$$V_D = V_B - V_D = V_s \left( \frac{R_2}{R_1+R_2} - \frac{R_4}{R_3+R_4} \right)$$

(Note: $R_1$ and $R_3$ are typically drawn at the top, $R_2$ and $R_4$ at the bottom; the equivalent form $R_1/(R_1+R_2) - R_3/(R_3+R_4)$ is also acceptable — KTU accepts both notations.)

**Step 3: Balance condition.**

Setting $V_D = 0$:

$$\frac{R_1}{R_2} = \frac{R_3}{R_4} \quad \text{or equivalently} \quad R_1 R_4 = R_2 R_3$$

**Step 4: Linearisation for a small change $\delta R$ in a single arm.**

Suppose $R_1 \to R + \delta R$, others remain $R$. Substituting:

$$V_D = V_s \left( \frac{R}{2R + \delta R} - \frac{R}{2R} \right) = V_s \left( \frac{R}{2R + \delta R} - \frac{1}{2} \right)$$

Using the binomial approximation $\frac{1}{2+\delta R/R} \approx \frac{1}{2}(1 - \delta R/(2R))$ for $\delta R \ll R$:

$$V_D \approx V_s \left( \frac{1}{2} - \frac{\delta R}{4R} - \frac{1}{2} \right) = -\frac{V_s \cdot \delta R}{4R}$$

The negative sign only indicates the polarity convention; the **magnitude** is the key engineering result:

$$\boxed{\,\vert V_D \vert \approx \frac{V_s}{4} \cdot \frac{\delta R}{R}\,}$$

This is the **fundamental bridge sensitivity equation** that KTU loves to test in Part B numerical questions.

---

### 3.4 Derivation 4 — Resolution and LSB Size of an n-bit ADC

**Given:** An $n$-bit ADC with full-scale input range $V_{FS}$.

**Step 1: Total number of quantisation levels.**

An $n$-bit code has $2^n$ distinct codes (0 to $2^n - 1$).

**Step 2: One LSB (Least Significant Bit) corresponds to the smallest step.**

$$\text{1 LSB} = \frac{V_{FS}}{2^n - 1} \approx \frac{V_{FS}}{2^n} \quad \text{(for } n \geq 8, \text{ the difference is < 1 %)}$$

**Step 3: Worked example (KTU-style).**

For a 12-bit ADC with $V_{FS} = 5$ V:

$$\text{1 LSB} = \frac{5 \text{ V}}{2^{12}} = \frac{5 \text{ V}}{4096} \approx 1.22 \text{ mV}$$

**Step 4: Quantisation error (peak-to-peak) = 1 LSB; RMS quantisation noise ≈ LSB / $\sqrt{12}$.**

These are the values KTU students are expected to remember for any ADC specification.

---

### 3.5 Derivation 5 — Wien-Bridge Oscillator Frequency

**Given:** Non-inverting amplifier with positive feedback through a Wien network (series RC and parallel RC).

**Step 1: Feedback factor of the Wien network.**

The Wien network consists of a series arm $Z_s = R + 1/(j\omega C)$ and a parallel arm $Z_p = R \Vert 1/(j\omega C) = R/(1+j\omega RC)$.

$$\beta = \frac{Z_p}{Z_s + Z_p}$$

**Step 2: Simplify using $\omega_0 = 1/(RC)$.**

After substitution and algebraic manipulation (multiplying numerator and denominator by $j\omega C$ and simplifying):

$$\beta = \frac{1}{3 + j(\omega RC - 1/(\omega RC))}$$

**Step 3: At oscillation, $\beta$ must be real (zero phase).**

The imaginary term in the denominator must vanish:

$$\omega RC = \frac{1}{\omega RC} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \omega_0^2 R^2 C^2 = 1 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \omega_0 = \frac{1}{RC}$$

**Step 4: Substitute $\omega_0 = 2\pi f_0$ and solve for $f_0$.**

$$\boxed{\,f_0 = \frac{1}{2\pi R C}\,}$$

**Step 5: For sustained oscillation, Barkhausen requires $\vert A \beta \vert = 1$.**

At $f_0$, $\beta = 1/3$, so the amplifier gain must be $A = 3$. In practice, $A$ is set slightly greater than 3 (e.g., $A = 3 + \delta$) with non-linear amplitude stabilisation (using diodes or a thermistor) to compensate for component tolerances.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Students often write $A \geq 3$ instead of $A = 3$ for ideal sustained oscillation. Both answers earn full marks if explained, but the *ideal* condition is exactly $A = 3$.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Master Block Diagram — Generalized Instrumentation System

This diagram maps every "common system component" into its correct location in the signal chain, so the student can see how the topics interlink.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    PQ[Physical Quantity] --> TR[Transducer]
    TR --> SC[Signal Conditioner]
    SC --> AMP[Amplifier]
    AMP --> FIL[Filter]
    FIL --> SHC[Sample and Hold]
    SHC --> ADC[ADC]
    ADC --> PROC[Digital Processor]
    PROC --> DAC[DAC]
    DAC --> ACT[Actuator / Driver]
    ACT --> OUT[Output]
    PROC --> DSP[Display Device]
    OSC[Oscillator] -. timebase .-> ADC
    OSC -. carrier .-> TR
    MUX[Multiplexer] --> SHC
    BRG[Bridge Network] --> AMP
    CMP[Comparator] --> ADC
    ATT[Attenuator] --> FIL
    CLP[Clamper / Clipper] --> AMP
```

**How to read this diagram:**
- **Solid arrows** = main signal flow (forward path).
- **Dashed arrows** = auxiliary/supporting signals (clock, carrier, excitation).
- The **transducer, amplifier, filter, ADC, DAC, oscillator, display, and multiplexer** are all "common system components" — each appears as a block because each is a topic the KTU syllabus covers in depth.

---

### 4.2 Op-Amp Building-Block Architecture

Shows the standard op-amp internal stages and the common external configurations derived from it.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph IDEAL_OPAMP["Ideal Op-Amp Package"]
        IN1[Input Stage Differential] --> GAIN[Gain Stage A_OL]
        GAIN --> OUT_STG[Output Buffer]
        IN1 --- Vplus[Non Inverting V+]
        IN1 --- Vmin[Inverting V-]
        OUT_STG --- Vout[Output Vout]
    end
    subgraph CONFIG["External Configurations Formed"]
        C1[Inverting Amplifier]
        C2[Non Inverting Amplifier]
        C3[Voltage Follower]
        C4[Summing Amplifier]
        C5[Difference Amplifier]
        C6[Integrator]
        C7[Differentiator]
    end
    IDEAL_OPAMP --> CONFIG
```

---

### 4.3 Filter Family Tree (Active Realisations)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    ROOT[Filter Networks] --> P[Passive RL RC RLC]
    ROOT --> A[Active Op-Amp Based]
    P --> P1[1st Order RC]
    P --> P2[2nd Order RLC]
    A --> A1[Sallen Key Topology]
    A --> A2[Multiple Feedback Topology]
    A --> A3[State Variable Filter]
    P1 --> T1[Low Pass LPF]
    P1 --> T2[High Pass HPF]
    A1 --> T1
    A1 --> T2
    A1 --> T3[Band Pass BPF]
    A1 --> T4[Band Stop BSF Notch]
    T1 --- NOTE[Cutoff fc equals 1 over 2piRC for first order]
```

> [!NOTE]
> Every branch of this tree eventually reduces to the single equation $f_c = 1/(2\pi RC)$ (or its higher-order generalisation) — making this the single most important filter formula for KTU.

---

### 4.4 ADC Classification and Selection Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    START{Need an ADC?} --> Q1{Priority Speed?}
    Q1 -->|Very High MHz plus| FLASH[Flash ADC 4 to 8 bit]
    Q1 -->|Medium kHz to MHz| SAR[Successive Approximation SAR 8 to 18 bit]
    Q1 -->|Slow Hz| INT[Dual Slope 16 to 24 bit]
    Q1 -->|Very Low Cost| CTR[Counter or Tracking ADC]
    FLASH --> USE1[Oscilloscopes Video]
    SAR --> USE2[Multimeters DAQ cards]
    INT --> USE3[Digital Panel Meters DVMs]
    CTR --> USE4[Simple Panel Meters]
```

---

### 4.5 Wheatstone Bridge — Schematic and Equivalent

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    VS[Source Vs] --- A
    A --- R1
    R1 --- B
    B --- R2
    B --- D
    D --- C
    C --- R4
    C --- R3
    R3 --- A
    B --- VD[Detector VD]
    D --- VD
    VS --- C
    A --- VS
```

**Reading guide:**
- Top-left arm: $R_1$ between A and B.
- Bottom-left arm: $R_2$ between B and C.
- Top-right arm: $R_3$ between A and D.
- Bottom-right arm: $R_4$ between D and C.
- Detector reads $V_D = V_B - V_D$ across the diagonal B–D.

---

### 4.6 Signal Routing — Multiplexer / Demultiplexer Functional Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    I0[Input Channel 0] --> MUX[Analog Multiplexer MUX]
    I1[Input Channel 1] --> MUX
    I2[Input Channel 2] --> MUX
    I3[Input Channel 3] --> MUX
    SEL[Select Lines S0 S1] --> MUX
    MUX --> OUT[Single Output to ADC]
    OUT --> ADC2[Shared ADC]
    ADC2 --> DEMUX[Demultiplexer DEMUX]
    DEMUX --> O0[Output Channel 0]
    DEMUX --> O1[Output Channel 1]
    DEMUX --> O2[Output Channel 2]
    DEMUX --> O3[Output Channel 3]
```

**Why this matters in instrumentation:** A single expensive ADC can be **time-shared** among many sensor channels, drastically reducing cost in multi-channel data acquisition systems (DAQ).
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Differentiate between active and passive transducers. Give two examples of each.**

**Model Answer:**

> **Active Transducer:** Generates its own electrical output (EMF, charge, or current) in response to the measurand, without requiring an external power source. The energy conversion is one-way: physical → electrical.
> - *Examples:* (i) Thermocouple — converts temperature difference into EMF. (ii) Piezoelectric crystal — converts mechanical force/pressure into electric charge.

> **Passive Transducer:** Does not generate electricity on its own; it merely changes one of its electrical properties (R, L, C) in response to the measurand. An **external excitation** (voltage or current) is mandatory to produce a usable output.
> - *Examples:* (i) Thermistor — resistance changes with temperature. (ii) Strain gauge — resistance changes with mechanical strain.

> **Key Difference:** Active transducers are self-generating (no external power required for the sensing action), while passive transducers are externally energised parameter-modulating devices. (3 marks)

---

#### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**State and explain the Barkhausen criterion for sustained oscillations.**

**Model Answer:**

> For a feedback amplifier to act as a **sustained oscillator** (i.e., to generate a stable sinusoidal output without an external input), the closed loop must satisfy the **Barkhausen criterion**, which has **two simultaneous conditions**:
> 
> 1. **Magnitude Condition:** The magnitude of the loop gain must be exactly **unity**.
> $$\vert A \beta \vert = 1$$
> where $A$ is the amplifier gain and $\beta$ is the feedback factor.
> 
> 2. **Phase Condition:** The total phase shift around the loop must be **zero (or an integer multiple of $2\pi$, i.e., $360^\circ$)**.
> $$\angle A \beta = 0^\circ \text{ (or } 360^\circ \text{)}$$
> 
> If $\vert A \beta \vert < 1$, oscillations decay; if $\vert A \beta \vert > 1$, oscillations grow until non-linearities limit them (and the output becomes distorted). At $\vert A \beta \vert = 1$ with correct phase, the circuit oscillates at a **single, stable frequency** determined by the frequency-selective network (RC, LC, crystal). (3 marks)

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> - In op-amp derivations, **do not forget the virtual short and zero-input-current rules** — writing KCL equations without them leads to wrong answers and zero marks for the core step.
> - In bridge problems, always **state the balance condition** $R_1 R_4 = R_2 R_3$ explicitly before plugging in numerical values. Many students skip this and lose 1–2 marks.
> - For filter problems, give the **final formula in Hz** ($f_c = 1/(2\pi RC)$) — writing only $\omega_c = 1/RC$ is incomplete and may lose a mark if the question specifically asks for $f_c$.
> - For ADC/DAC numericals, students often confuse **$2^n$** with **$2^n - 1$**. For $n \geq 8$ the difference is < 1%, but KTU expects the textbook definition with $2^n$ for full-scale calculations.
> - **Draw the circuit diagram** wherever the question says "with the help of a circuit" — skipping the diagram can cost 2–3 marks.

---

#### **PART B — Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat circuit diagram, derive the expression for the **closed-loop voltage gain of an inverting amplifier** using an ideal op-amp. State the two assumptions (golden rules) used in the derivation. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A **Wheatstone bridge** has all four arms equal to $120 \ \Omega$. A strain gauge of nominal resistance $120 \ \Omega$ replaces one arm. When the gauge is strained, its resistance becomes $120.12 \ \Omega$. The bridge is excited by a **5 V DC** source. Calculate the **detector output voltage** and the **sensitivity** of the bridge in V per Ω. **(7 marks)**

---

##### **Model Solution — Part (a):**

**Step 1: Circuit diagram description.**
Draw the inverting amplifier: $V_{in}$ → $R_{in}$ → inverting input (–); non-inverting input (+) grounded; $R_f$ feedback between output and (–) input; output $V_{out}$ taken from the op-amp output. *(1 mark for diagram)*

**Step 2: State the two golden rules.** *(1 mark)*
- Rule 1: $V^+ = V^-$ (virtual short).
- Rule 2: $I^+ = I^- = 0$ (no current into op-amp inputs).

**Step 3: Apply Rule 1.** Since $V^+ = 0$ (grounded), $V^- = 0$ → the inverting node is at **virtual ground**. *(1 mark)*

**Step 4: Apply KCL at the inverting node.** Current through $R_{in}$ = Current through $R_f$ (by Rule 2).

$$\frac{V_{in} - 0}{R_{in}} = \frac{0 - V_{out}}{R_f}$$

**Step 5: Solve for the gain.** *(1 mark)*

$$\frac{V_{in}}{R_{in}} = -\frac{V_{out}}{R_f} \quad \Rightarrow \quad V_{out} = -\frac{R_f}{R_{in}} V_{in}$$

**Step 6: Closed-loop voltage gain.** *(1 mark)*

$$A_{CL} = \frac{V_{out}}{V_{in}} = -\frac{R_f}{R_{in}}$$

The negative sign indicates a **180° phase inversion**. *(1 mark)*

---

##### **Model Solution — Part (b):**

**Given:** $R_1 = R_2 = R_3 = R_4 = 120 \ \Omega$ (initial), source $V_s = 5$ V, and one arm (say $R_1$) changes to $R_1' = 120.12 \ \Omega$. So $\delta R = 0.12 \ \Omega$, $R = 120 \ \Omega$.

**Step 1: Use the small-signal bridge formula.** *(1 mark)*

$$V_D \approx \frac{V_s \cdot \delta R}{4 R} = \frac{5 \times 0.12}{4 \times 120}$$

**Step 2: Evaluate.** *(1 mark)*

$$V_D = \frac{0.6}{480} = 1.25 \times 10^{-3} \text{ V} = 1.25 \text{ mV}$$

**Step 3: Bridge sensitivity (V per Ω).** *(1 mark)*

$$S = \frac{V_D}{\delta R} = \frac{1.25 \text{ mV}}{0.12 \ \Omega} \approx 10.42 \text{ mV/}\Omega$$

**Step 4: Verify by exact formula** (full credit for cross-check): *(1 mark)*

$$V_D = V_s \left( \frac{R_2}{R_1'+R_2} - \frac{R_4}{R_3+R_4} \right) = 5 \left( \frac{120}{240.12} - \frac{120}{240} \right)$$

$$V_D = 5 (0.49975 - 0.5) = 5 \times (-0.00025) = -1.25 \text{ mV}$$

Magnitude matches: $\vert V_D \vert = 1.25$ mV. ✓ *(1 mark for verification, plus 1 mark for sensitivity interpretation)*

**Conclusion:** A resistance change of just **0.1 %** (0.12 Ω out of 120 Ω) produces a clearly measurable **1.25 mV** output, demonstrating the bridge's high sensitivity to small $\Delta R$ — the reason strain gauges, RTDs, and thermistors are almost always used in bridge configurations.

---

#### **PART B — Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** Explain the operation of a **first-order active low-pass filter** using an op-amp. Derive the expression for its **cut-off frequency** and **voltage gain in the pass-band**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A **Successive Approximation Register (SAR) type ADC** has a resolution of **12 bits** and an input range of **0 to 10 V**. Calculate (i) the size of **1 LSB** in volts, (ii) the **digital output code** for an analog input of **6.74 V**, and (iii) the **quantisation error** in volts. **(7 marks)**

---

##### **Model Solution — Part (a):**

**Step 1: Circuit description.** *(1 mark)*
A first-order active LPF is made by placing an RC low-pass network (R in series, C to ground) at the inverting input of a non-inverting op-amp. The non-inverting input receives $V_{in}$ through a separate buffer; the output is $V_{out}$ taken from the op-amp output. The op-amp provides gain and isolation.

**Step 2: Pass-band (DC) gain.** *(1 mark)*
For DC, the capacitor is open ($\,1/(\omega C) \to \infty\,$), so the inverting node sees only the resistor feedback. The non-inverting amplifier gives:

$$A_{DC} = 1 + \frac{R_f}{R_1}$$

This is the **pass-band gain**. *(1 mark)*

**Step 3: AC analysis (s-domain).** *(1 mark)*
At the inverting node, the impedance from C to ground is $Z_C = 1/(sC)$. The non-inverting node voltage is set by the input divider (which we assume is unity at the buffer input). The transfer function becomes:

$$H(s) = \frac{A_{DC}}{1 + sRC}$$

**Step 4: Substituting $s = j\omega$ and finding the cut-off.** *(1 mark)*

$$\vert H(j\omega) \vert = \frac{A_{DC}}{\sqrt{1 + (\omega RC)^2}}$$

Setting $\omega RC = 1$:

$$\omega_c = \frac{1}{RC} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \boxed{\,f_c = \frac{1}{2\pi R C}\,}$$

**Step 5: Limiting behaviour.** *(1 mark)*
- At $f \ll f_c$: $\vert H \vert \to A_{DC}$ (passes with gain).
- At $f \gg f_c$: roll-off at –20 dB/decade.
- At $f = f_c$: $\vert H \vert = A_{DC} / \sqrt{2}$ (–3 dB point).

**Step 6: Use.** Such filters are the workhorse of **anti-aliasing** before ADC sampling. *(1 mark)*

---

##### **Model Solution — Part (b):**

**Given:** $n = 12$ bits, $V_{FS} = 10$ V (unipolar, 0 to 10 V).

**(i) 1 LSB size:** *(2 marks)*

$$\text{1 LSB} = \frac{V_{FS}}{2^n} = \frac{10}{2^{12}} = \frac{10}{4096} \approx 2.441 \text{ mV}$$

**(ii) Digital output code for $V_{in} = 6.74$ V:** *(3 marks)*

$$D = \text{round}\left( \frac{V_{in}}{\text{1 LSB}} \right) = \text{round}\left( \frac{6.74}{0.002441} \right) = \text{round}(2761.16)$$

$$D = 2761 \text{ (decimal)} = \text{AC9}_{H}$$

Cross-check: $2761 \times 2.441 \text{ mV} = 6.7401$ V ≈ 6.74 V ✓

**(iii) Quantisation error:** *(2 marks)*
Maximum quantisation error = ± ½ LSB = ± 1.22 mV.
For our input: $e_q = 6.74 - 6.7401 = -0.1 \text{ mV}$ (well within ±½ LSB). ✓

**Conclusion:** A 12-bit ADC provides about **2.44 mV resolution** over 0–10 V, suitable for most industrial sensors (RTDs, strain gauges, pressure transducers). Higher bit counts (16, 18, 24) are used for precision laboratory instruments.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for "Common System Components" (Module 4)**

- **Op-Amp Golden Rules:** (1) Virtual short $V^+ = V^-$. (2) Zero input current $I^+ = I^- = 0$. Use these to derive *every* op-amp configuration.
- **Inverting amp gain:** $A_v = -R_f/R_{in}$. Phase = 180°. Input impedance = $R_{in}$.
- **Non-inverting amp gain:** $A_v = 1 + R_f/R_{in}$. Phase = 0°. Input impedance → ∞.
- **First-order RC filter cutoff:** $f_c = 1/(2\pi RC)$ in Hz. Roll-off = –20 dB/decade.
- **Filter types:** LPF, HPF, BPF, BSF. Active filters (op-amp + RC) preferred in instrumentation over passive filters (can have gain, no loading, no bulky inductors).
- **Wheatstone Bridge balance:** $R_1 R_4 = R_2 R_3$. Small-signal output: $V_D \approx V_s \cdot \delta R/(4R)$.
- **Transducer classification:** Active = self-generating (thermocouple, piezoelectric, LVDT). Passive = parameter-modulating, needs excitation (thermistor, strain gauge, LDR, potentiometer).
- **ADC Resolution:** 1 LSB = $V_{FS}/2^n$. SAR ADC is the "all-rounder" — used in most KTU lab DAQs and digital multimeters. Flash is fastest but low resolution. Dual-slope is slowest but highest resolution (DVMs).
- **DAC Output (unipolar):** $V_{out} = V_{ref} \cdot D/2^n$. R-2R ladder is the most common topology.
- **Barkhausen Criterion:** $\vert A\beta \vert = 1$ AND $\angle A\beta = 0°$ (or 360°). Both conditions are mandatory; missing one is a guaranteed 0-mark answer.
- **Wien-bridge oscillator:** $f_0 = 1/(2\pi RC)$, requires $A = 3$ for sustained oscillation.
- **555 astable (timer):** $f \approx 1.44 / [(R_1 + 2R_2) C]$. Duty cycle adjustable by varying $R_1$ vs $R_2$.
- **Multiplexer:** Time-shares one ADC across $2^n$ channels. Reduces system cost.
- **Clamper:** Shifts DC level without changing shape ($V_{out} = V_{in} \pm V_{clamp}$).
- **Clipper:** Limits signal amplitude above/below a reference (over-voltage protection).
- **Comparator:** Op-amp with no feedback. Output = $+V_{sat}$ or $-V_{sat}$ based on input comparison.
- **Common-Mode Rejection Ratio (CMRR):** $20 \log_{10}(A_d/A_c)$ in dB. Critical for instrumentation amplifiers in noisy environments.
- **Gain-Bandwidth Product (GBP):** $A_{OL} \times f = $ constant. Higher closed-loop gain → lower usable bandwidth (key op-amp trade-off).
- **Active vs Passive filter trade-off:** Active → gain + isolation + no inductors, but needs supply and is bandwidth-limited by op-amp. Passive → no supply needed, but $\leq 1$ gain and load-sensitive.
- **Quantisation error:** ± ½ LSB peak; RMS noise = LSB / $\sqrt{12}$.
- **Anti-aliasing filter:** Always a low-pass filter placed *before* the ADC, with $f_c \leq f_s/2$ (Nyquist).
- **Common KTU mistakes to avoid:** forgetting virtual ground in inverting amp; using $2^n - 1$ vs $2^n$ inconsistently in ADC math; missing one Barkhausen condition; writing $\omega_c$ when $f_c$ is asked.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Supervision and Control
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET411 - ELECTRONIC INSTRUMENTATION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Supervision and Control

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview of Supervision and Control

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Supervision and Control** refers to the integrated industrial framework that combines real-time data acquisition, process monitoring, automated decision-making, and remote command execution to manage complex industrial processes. It forms the **upper hierarchy** of process automation and typically encompasses three core pillars: **SCADA (Supervisory Control and Data Acquisition)**, **PLC (Programmable Logic Controller)**, and **DCS (Distributed Control System)**.

In the context of **PEEET411 – Electronic Instrumentation (KTU 2024 Scheme, Module 4)**, Supervision and Control is the culminating discipline that brings together all preceding instrumentation concepts—transducers, signal conditioning, data acquisition, and telemetry—into a unified, operator-driven industrial management ecosystem.

---

## 1.2 The Three Pillars of Supervision and Control

| Pillar | Full Form | Primary Use Case | Typical Scale |
|--------|-----------|-----------------|---------------|
| **SCADA** | Supervisory Control and Data Acquisition | Centralized remote monitoring of geographically distributed assets (power grids, pipelines) | City/Nation-wide |
| **PLC** | Programmable Logic Controller | Discrete-event control (assembly lines, packaging) | Machine-level |
| **DCS** | Distributed Control System | Continuous process control (refineries, chemical plants) | Plant-wide |

---

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Smart Highway Control Room"

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: Imagine a Modern Smart City Traffic Control Center**

Picture a city's traffic management center. Hundreds of sensors (induction loops, cameras, speed radars) embedded across roads continuously send data (vehicle count, average speed, congestion level) to a **central server room**. Operators in the control room monitor **large display walls (HMIs)** showing live traffic maps. When congestion is detected, the operator (or an automated algorithm) **issues commands**—activating variable message signs, changing signal timings, or dispatching personnel.

**Mapping the Analogy to Engineering Reality:**
- **Sensors on roads** → Field instruments (temperature, pressure, flow transmitters)
- **Communication links** → Data acquisition networks (Modbus, Profibus, Ethernet/IP)
- **Control room displays** → HMI/SCADA screens
- **Operator commands** → Supervisory set-points and override controls
- **Automated signal timing** → Closed-loop PLC/DCS logic

This analogy instantly clarifies that **Supervision and Control is NOT a single device**—it is an **orchestrated network** of hardware, software, and human operators working in concert.

---

## 1.4 Key Functional Objectives

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Primary Objectives of any Supervision and Control System:**
> 1. **Real-time Data Acquisition** — Continuously sample field parameters at rates appropriate to process dynamics.
> 2. **Centralized Visualization (HMI)** — Provide operators with a graphical, intuitive process overview.
> 3. **Alarm Management** — Detect abnormal conditions and escalate through prioritized alarms.
> 4. **Historical Trending** — Archive process data for analytics, optimization, and regulatory compliance.
> 5. **Supervisory Intervention** — Allow operators to override automatic control through manual set-points.
> 6. **Redundancy & Reliability** — Maintain operation through fault-tolerant hardware (typical MTBF goal > **100,000 hours**).

---

## 1.5 ISA-95 Automation Hierarchy (The Reference Model)

The **ANSI/ISA-95** standard defines **five levels** of industrial automation. Supervision and Control systems occupy **Levels 2 and 3**.

| Level | Name | Functions | Equipment |
|-------|------|-----------|-----------|
| **Level 0** | Process | Physical process sensing/actuation | Sensors, valves, motors |
| **Level 1** | Basic Control | Loop control, interlocking | PLC, DCS controllers, RTU |
| **Level 2** | Supervisory Control | HMI, alarm management, set-points | SCADA workstations |
| **Level 3** | Manufacturing Operations | Production tracking, batch records | MES (Manufacturing Execution System) |
| **Level 4** | Business Planning | ERP, scheduling, logistics | SAP, Oracle ERP |

---

## 1.6 Historical Evolution (Brief)

> [!NOTE]
> **Evolution Timeline:**
> - **1960s:** Relay logic panels → **Hard-wired control**
> - **1970s:** Microprocessor-based PLCs introduced (Modicon 084, **1968**)
> - **1980s:** DCS emerged (Honeywell TDC 3000) for continuous processes
> - **1990s:** PC-based SCADA with OPC (OLE for Process Control) standardization
> - **2000s:** Ethernet/IP convergence, web-based HMIs
> - **2010s–Present:** IIoT (Industrial Internet of Things), cloud SCADA, edge computing

# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Detailed Breakdown of SCADA Architecture

A SCADA system is **not a single piece of equipment** but a layered architecture. Each layer performs a distinct function:

### Layer 1: Field Instrumentation Layer (Level 0)
- **Function:** Direct interaction with the physical process.
- **Devices:** RTDs, thermocouples, pressure transmitters, flow meters, control valves, variable frequency drives.
- **Signals:** Analog (**4–20 mA**, **0–10 V**), Digital (24 V DC discrete), Smart (HART, Foundation Fieldbus).

### Layer 2: Control Layer (Level 1) — Remote Terminal Units (RTUs) / PLCs
- **Function:** Local logic, data concentration, protocol conversion.
- **Key Specs:**
  - Scan time: **5–50 ms** per I/O point
  - Operating temperature: **−40 °C to +85 °C**
  - I/O density: up to **8192 points** in modern rack PLCs
- **Communication Protocols:** Modbus RTU (RS-485), Modbus TCP, Profibus DP, DeviceNet.

### Layer 3: Communication Infrastructure (Level 1.5)
- **Wired:** RS-232, RS-485, Ethernet (CAT6, fiber optic).
- **Wireless:** GSM/GPRS, LoRaWAN (long-range, low-power, **<1% duty cycle**), satellite SCADA for offshore platforms.
- **Industrial Protocols:** IEC 60870-5-104 (power grid), DNP3 (utilities), OPC UA (modern cross-platform standard).

### Layer 4: Supervisory Stations (Level 2)
- **Function:** Data processing, alarming, trending, manual override.
- **Components:**
  - **HMI (Human-Machine Interface):** Graphical mimics, touch panels.
  - **SCADA Server:** Historian database, real-time tag database.
  - **Engineering Workstation:** Configuration of logic, screens, scripts.

### Layer 5: Enterprise Layer (Level 3–4)
- **MES/ERP integration** via SQL/ODBC bridges, REST APIs, or OPC UA.
- Functions: production orders, OEE (Overall Equipment Effectiveness) reporting, quality tracking.

---

## 2.2 Detailed Breakdown of PLC Operation

### 2.2.1 PLC Scan Cycle (The Heartbeat of PLC Operation)

Every PLC executes an **infinite loop** known as the **scan cycle**, typically broken into four phases:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **PLC Scan Cycle Phases (in strict order):**
> 1. **Input Scan** — Read all physical inputs into the **Process Image Input (PII)** memory.
> 2. **Program Scan** — Execute user logic (ladder, FBD, ST) from top to bottom, left to right.
> 3. **Output Scan** — Write the **Process Image Output (PIO)** to physical outputs.
> 4. **Housekeeping** — Communication servicing, self-diagnostics, timer updates.

### 2.2.2 Scan Time Formula

The total cycle time determines how fast the PLC can react:

$$
T_{scan} = T_{input} + T_{program} + T_{output} + T_{housekeeping}
$$

Where each term depends on **I/O count**, **program complexity**, and **CPU clock rate**. A typical mid-range PLC running 1 k of ladder logic has **$T_{scan} \approx$ 5–20 ms**.

### 2.2.3 I/O Addressing Convention

For a Siemens S7-1200 PLC, inputs and outputs follow strict address rules:
- **Digital Input:** `I` (e.g., `I0.0`, `I0.1`)
- **Digital Output:** `Q` (e.g., `Q0.0`)
- **Analog Input:** `IW` (e.g., `IW64`) — 16-bit word
- **Analog Output:** `QW` (e.g., `QW80`)
- **Memory Bit:** `M` (e.g., `M10.0`)
- **Timer:** `T` (e.g., `T37`)
- **Counter:** `C` (e.g., `C0`)

---

## 2.3 Distributed Control System (DCS) — Deep Theory

### 2.3.1 Why DCS for Continuous Processes?

A **DCS** differs fundamentally from a SCADA system in three ways:

| Feature | SCADA | DCS |
|---------|-------|-----|
| **Geographic Spread** | Wide (km to 1000s of km) | Compact (single plant) |
| **Control Type** | Mostly supervisory | Tight regulatory control |
| **Redundancy** | Often single-server | Built-in redundant controllers/power |
| **Process Type** | Discrete + monitoring | Continuous (chemicals, oil & gas) |

### 2.3.2 DCS Functional Layers

1. **Process Level (Controller):** Houses PID loops. Modern DCS executes **>500 control loops** per controller.
2. **Operations Level (HMI):** Operator consoles with dedicated function keys for set-point changes.
3. **Engineering Level:** Configuration, tuning, trending using dedicated engineering tools.

### 2.3.3 PID Controller in Supervision Context

Supervisory control often **cascades** or **overrides** PID controllers. The standard PID output equation is:

$$
u(t) = K_p \left[ e(t) + \frac{1}{T_i}\int_0^t e(\tau)\,d\tau + T_d \frac{de(t)}{dt} \right]
$$

In digital form (position algorithm):

$$
u[k] = u[k-1] + K_p \left[ (e[k] - e[k-1]) + \frac{T_s}{T_i}e[k] + \frac{T_d}{T_s}(e[k] - 2e[k-1] + e[k-2]) \right]
$$

Where:
- $K_p$ = Proportional gain (dimensionless)
- $T_i$ = Integral time (**seconds**)
- $T_d$ = Derivative time (**seconds**)
- $T_s$ = Sampling period (**seconds**)
- $e[k]$ = Error at sample $k$ (set-point − process variable)

---

## 2.4 Control Valves and Final Control Elements

The **control valve** is the most ubiquitous final control element in supervision/control loops. Its sizing follows the standard flow equation:

$$
Q = C_v \sqrt{\frac{\Delta P}{G_f}}
$$

Where:
- $Q$ = Volumetric flow rate (**m³/h** or **gpm**)
- $C_v$ = Valve flow coefficient (dimensionless, vendor-specific)
- $\Delta P$ = Pressure drop across valve (**bar** or **psi**)
- $G_f$ = Specific gravity of fluid (ratio to water at **15.6 °C**)

> [!NOTE]
> **Valve Characteristics:** A control valve may be **linear**, **equal percentage**, or **quick-opening**. Equal percentage valves are preferred for processes where $\Delta P$ varies widely, providing near-constant loop gain.

---

## 2.5 HMI Design Principles (ISA-101.01 Standard)

A **Human-Machine Interface (HMI)** is the operator's window into the control system. The ISA-101 standard mandates:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key HMI Design Rules:**
> 1. **Color Convention:** Red = High alarm, Yellow = Low alarm, Blue = Information, Green = Normal running.
> 2. **Hierarchy:** Plant overview → Area overview → Unit control → Detail faceplate.
> 3. **Situation Awareness:** All critical information (PV, SP, OP) must be visible without drilling through menus.
> 4. **Alarm Rate Limit:** Maximum **~300 alarms/hour** to prevent operator desensitization (per EEMUA-191 guideline).
> 5. **High-Performance HMI (HP-HMI):** Use neutral gray backgrounds; reserve saturated colors for actual alarm conditions.

---

## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam-Ready Formula Table (Print-Friendly)**

| # | Concept | Formula | Units |
|---|---------|---------|-------|
| 1 | PLC scan time | $T_{scan} = T_{in} + T_{prog} + T_{out} + T_{hs}$ | **ms** |
| 2 | Deadband requirement | $T_{scan} \le 0.1 \times T_{settle}$ | **ms** |
| 3 | PID analog form | $u(t) = K_p \left[ e(t) + \frac{1}{T_i}\int e\,dt + T_d \frac{de}{dt} \right]$ | **% output** |
| 4 | PID digital form | $u[k] = u[k-1] + K_p[\Delta e[k] + \frac{T_s}{T_i}e[k] + \frac{T_d}{T_s}\Delta^2 e[k]]$ | **% output** |
| 5 | Valve flow equation | $Q = C_v \sqrt{\Delta P / G_f}$ | **m³/h** |
| 6 | Control valve rangeability | $R = \dfrac{C_{v,max}}{C_{v,min}}$ | dimensionless |
| 7 | Alarm priority count | $N_{alarm} \le 300\,/\text{hr}$ (EEMUA-191) | **alarms/hr** |
| 8 | HART signal superposition | $V_{HART} = V_{4-20} \pm V_{FSK}$ | **mV peak** on **mA DC** |
| 9 | Modbus RTU frame | Frame = $\vert$ Addr(1) $\vert$ Func(1) $\vert$ Data(N) $\vert$ CRC(2) $\vert$ | **bytes** |
| 10 | Scan rate rule of thumb | $T_s \le \dfrac{T_{process}}{10}$ | **seconds** |

---

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Power Sector:** SCADA controls the **national grid** (India's Load Despatch Centers manage **>400 GW** of generation).
- **Oil & Gas:** DCS in refineries manages **distillation columns** with **>2000 I/O** and **>200 PID loops**.
- **Water Treatment:** PLC/SCADA automates **pumping stations** ensuring **24/7 clean water delivery** to millions.
- **Discrete Manufacturing:** PLCs run **automotive assembly lines** with cycle times as low as **45 seconds** per vehicle.

> [!WARNING]
> **Industrial Reality:** A poorly designed SCADA/DCS can cause **catastrophic failures**. Examples: the **2003 Northeast Blackout** (US/Canada, SCADA alarm failure) and the **Bhopal disaster** (1984, India) — both emphasize the criticality of **alarm management, redundancy, and operator training**.

# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: PID Digital Position Algorithm from Analog Form

This derivation is **high-yield for KTU exam problems** on digital controller design.

### Step 1 — Start with the Continuous-Time PID Equation

$$
u(t) = K_p\, e(t) + \frac{K_p}{T_i} \int_0^t e(\tau)\,d\tau + K_p\, T_d \frac{de(t)}{dt}
$$

### Step 2 — Define the Integral Term via Backward Rectangular Approximation

We approximate the integral using the **backward Euler method** (the most common digital PID implementation because of its **bumpless transfer** property):

$$
\int_0^{kT_s} e(\tau)\,d\tau \approx \sum_{j=1}^{k} e[j] \cdot T_s
$$

### Step 3 — Define the Derivative Term via Backward Finite Difference

$$
\frac{de(t)}{dt}\bigg|_{t=kT_s} \approx \frac{e[k] - e[k-1]}{T_s}
$$

### Step 4 — Substitute into the Continuous Equation

$$
u[k] = K_p\, e[k] + \frac{K_p\, T_s}{T_i}\sum_{j=1}^{k} e[j] + K_p\, T_d \cdot \frac{e[k] - e[k-1]}{T_s}
$$

### Step 5 — Express as a "Velocity" (Incremental) Algorithm

To find $u[k] - u[k-1]$, write the previous step:

$$
u[k-1] = K_p\, e[k-1] + \frac{K_p\, T_s}{T_i}\sum_{j=1}^{k-1} e[j] + K_p\, T_d \cdot \frac{e[k-1] - e[k-2]}{T_s}
$$

### Step 6 — Subtract $u[k-1]$ from $u[k]$

$$
\begin{aligned}
u[k] - u[k-1] &= K_p \left[ e[k] - e[k-1] \right] \\
&\quad + \frac{K_p\, T_s}{T_i} \cdot e[k] \\
&\quad + K_p\, T_d \cdot \frac{e[k] - 2e[k-1] + e[k-2]}{T_s}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 7 — Final Velocity-Form Digital PID Equation

$$
\boxed{\,u[k] = u[k-1] + K_p \left[ \Delta e[k] + \frac{T_s}{T_i}\,e[k] + \frac{T_d}{T_s}\,\Delta^2 e[k] \right]\,}
$$

Where:
- $\Delta e[k] = e[k] - e[k-1]$ (first-order error difference)
- $\Delta^2 e[k] = e[k] - 2e[k-1] + e[k-2]$ (second-order error difference)

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "Velocity Form"?** Because $u[k] - u[k-1]$ is the **change** in controller output, this form provides **bumpless transfer** during set-point changes and is **less sensitive to output saturation**. This is the standard form implemented in industrial DCS controllers (Honeywell, Yokogawa, ABB).

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Shannon Sampling Theorem Application to SCADA

A SCADA system must sample field signals without aliasing. The **Nyquist-Shannon theorem** states:

$$
f_s \ge 2 \cdot f_{max}
$$

### Example Derivation for a Temperature Loop

**Given:**
- Process time constant $\tau = 60$ seconds
- Dominant process bandwidth $f_{max} \approx \dfrac{1}{2\pi\tau}$

**Step 1:** Compute process bandwidth.

$$
f_{max} = \frac{1}{2\pi \cdot 60} = \frac{1}{376.99} \approx 2.65 \times 10^{-3}\, \text{Hz}
$$

**Step 2:** Apply Nyquist criterion.

$$
f_s \ge 2 \times 2.65 \times 10^{-3} = 5.31 \times 10^{-3}\, \text{Hz}
$$

**Step 3:** Convert to sampling period.

$$
T_s \le \frac{1}{f_s} = \frac{1}{5.31 \times 10^{-3}} \approx 188.5\, \text{seconds}
$$

**Step 4:** Apply the "rule of 10" (industry practice) for adequate control margin.

$$
T_s \le \frac{T_{process}}{10} = \frac{60}{10} = 6\, \text{seconds}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion:** The maximum allowable sampling period is **$\min(188.5,\ 6) = 6\, \text{seconds}$**. In practice, **1-second** sampling is typical for thermal processes to provide margin against unmodeled dynamics.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: SCADA vs PLC vs DCS Comparison

This **comparison table** is the most-asked KTU question style. Memorize the distinctions:

| Parameter | SCADA | PLC | DCS |
|-----------|-------|-----|-----|
| **Primary Function** | Data acquisition + supervisory control | Discrete logic / sequencing | Continuous regulatory control |
| **Geographic Scope** | Wide-area (pipelines, grid) | Single machine / cell | Single plant |
| **Data Logging** | Extensive (historian DB) | Limited (local registers) | Moderate to extensive |
| **Control Strategy** | Set-point adjustment, alarm mgmt | Boolean logic, PID blocks | Advanced regulatory control (MPC, APC) |
| **Redundancy** | Optional (depends on criticality) | Hot-standby in critical apps | Built-in (1:1 controller redundancy) |
| **Response Time** | Seconds | Milliseconds | Sub-second |
| **Programming** | Configuration-driven (WinCC, Citect) | Ladder / FBD / ST per IEC 61131-3 | Function blocks / sequential function charts |
| **Failure Mode** | Graceful degradation | Hard stop on critical faults | Bumpless transfer to backup |
| **Typical Vendor** | Siemens WinCC, Wonderware, Ignition | Siemens S7, Allen-Bradley, Schneider | Honeywell Experion, Yokogawa CENTUM, Emerson DeltaV |
| **Cost** | Medium–High | Low–Medium | Very High |
| **Best For** | Power, water, oil pipelines | Manufacturing lines | Refineries, petrochemical, power plants |

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Problem — Control Valve Sizing

> [!NOTE]
> **Sample KTU Question Style — Practice this calculation thoroughly.**

**Problem:** A control valve has $C_v = 50$ and passes a fluid with specific gravity $G_f = 0.85$ at a pressure drop of **2.5 bar**. Calculate the volumetric flow rate.

### Step 1 — Apply the valve flow equation.

$$
Q = C_v \sqrt{\frac{\Delta P}{G_f}}
$$

### Step 2 — Substitute values.

$$
Q = 50 \times \sqrt{\frac{2.5}{0.85}}
$$

### Step 3 — Compute the ratio.

$$
\frac{2.5}{0.85} = 2.9412
$$

### Step 4 — Take the square root.

$$
\sqrt{2.9412} \approx 1.7149
$$

### Step 5 — Compute final flow.

$$
Q = 50 \times 1.7149 = 85.74\, \text{m}^3/\text{h}
$$

### Step 6 — State the answer with units and remark.

**Answer:** The valve passes approximately **85.74 m³/h** of fluid. In gallons per minute (gpm, used in US units): multiply by **0.588** to get ≈ **50.4 gpm**.

---

## 3.5 Numerical Problem — PID Tuning via Ziegler-Nichols (Closed-Loop Method)

**Given:** A temperature loop has $K_p = 8$, $T_i = 120\, s$, $T_d = 15\, s$. The ultimate gain $K_u = 20$ and ultimate period $P_u = 60\, s$ were found experimentally.

**Step 1 — Apply Z-N formulas for PID tuning.**

$$
K_p^{ZN} = 0.6 \cdot K_u = 0.6 \times 20 = 12
$$

$$
T_i^{ZN} = 0.5 \cdot P_u = 0.5 \times 60 = 30\, \text{s}
$$

$$
T_d^{ZN} = 0.125 \cdot P_u = 0.125 \times 60 = 7.5\, \text{s}
$$

**Step 2 — Compare with current tuning.**

Current tuning (8, 120, 15) is significantly **sluggish** (low $K_p$, high $T_i$) compared to Z-N (12, 30, 7.5).

**Step 3 — Conclude.**

Switch to Z-N values for **faster response** but perform **fine-tuning** (Z-N is known to give ~**25% overshoot**, which may be unacceptable for temperature loops; consider detuning by multiplying $K_p$ by 0.7–0.8).

# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 ISA-95 Automation Pyramid (Mermaid Block Diagram)

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the complete **ISA-95 Five-Level Architecture** with Supervision and Control highlighted at Levels 2–3:

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    L4["LEVEL 4: ENTERPRISE PLANNING<br/>ERP - SAP ORACLE<br/>Production Scheduling and Logistics"]
    L3["LEVEL 3: MANUFACTURING OPERATIONS<br/>MES - Batch Records and OEE Tracking<br/>Plant Historian and Reporting"]
    L2["LEVEL 2: SUPERVISORY CONTROL<br/>SCADA Server and HMI Workstations<br/>Alarm Management and Trend Displays"]
    L1["LEVEL 1: BASIC CONTROL<br/>PLCs and DCS Controllers<br/>PID Loops and Interlocking Logic"]
    L0["LEVEL 0: PHYSICAL PROCESS<br/>Sensors and Transmitters<br/>Control Valves and Motors"]

    L4 --> L3
    L3 --> L2
    L2 --> L1
    L1 --> L0

    L2 -.feedback.-> L1
    L1 -.field signals.-> L2
    L3 -.production orders.-> L2
    L2 -.historical data.-> L3
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation:** Vertical arrows show the **command flow** (top-down), while dashed arrows represent **data and feedback flow** (bottom-up). Notice the **closed-loop information flow** between Level 1 and Level 2 — the essence of supervisory control.

## 4.4 Closed-Loop Process Control with Supervisory Override

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    SP["SET POINT<br/>Desired Value"] --> ERR["SUMMING JUNCTION<br/>Error Calculation"]
    PV["PROCESS VARIABLE<br/>Measured Value"] --> ERR
    ERR --> PID["PID CONTROLLER<br/>Kp Ti Td Tuning"]
    PID --> CV["CONTROL VALVE<br/>Final Control Element"]
    CV --> PROC["INDUSTRIAL PROCESS<br/>Reactor or Furnace"]
    PROC --> X["Sensor Transducer"]
    X --> PV
    SUP["SCADA SUPERVISORY<br/>Set Point Adjustment"] -.modifies.-> SP
    SUP -.alarm mgmt.-> PID
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reading the Diagram:** The inner loop (PID → Valve → Process → Sensor → Error) is **automatic regulatory control**. The SCADA supervisory layer **adjusts the set-point** and **monitors alarms** without directly controlling the valve — this separation of concerns is foundational to industrial automation.

## 4.5 Alarm Priority Pyramid (EEMUA-191 Standard)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    P1["PRIORITY 1 CRITICAL<br/>Safety Imminent Shutdown Required<br/>Acknowledgment within 1 minute"]
    P2["PRIORITY 2 HIGH<br/>Production Loss Risk<br/>Acknowledge within 5 minutes"]

    P4["PRIORITY 4 LOW<br/>Informational Only<br/>Next shift review"]

    P1 --> P2
    P2 --> P3
    P3 --> P4
```

> [!NOTE]
> **EEMUA-191 Recommendation:** A well-designed control system should generate **no more than ~150 priority-1 alarms per day** and the operator should see **no more than ~10 alarms per hour** to maintain situational awareness.
