# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 On-Off Control (Two-Position Control)

### 2.1.1 Operational Logic

The On-Off controller is the **simplest discontinuous control mode**. It has only two output states:

$$u(t) = \begin{cases} u_{max} & \text{if } e(t) > 0 \\ u_{min} & \text{if } e(t) < 0 \end{cases}$$

- When PV < SP, error is positive, controller output is **fully ON** (e.g., heater at 100%).
- When PV > SP, error is negative, controller output is **fully OFF** (e.g., heater at 0%).
- When PV = SP, the output **holds its last state**.

### 2.1.2 Dead Band (Hysteresis)

A pure On-Off controller would oscillate infinitely due to noise. Hence, a **dead band** $\Delta e$ is introduced:

$$u(t) = \begin{cases} u_{max} & \text{if } e(t) > +\Delta e \\ u_{min} & \text{if } e(t) < -\Delta e \\ \text{No change} & \text{if } -\Delta e \leq e(t) \leq +\Delta e \end{cases}$$

### 2.1.3 Characteristics
- **Advantages**: Extremely simple, cheap, reliable
- **Disadvantages**: Continuous oscillation, mechanical wear, unsuitable for precise processes
- **Applications**: Domestic thermostats, refrigerator compressors, simple level switches

---

## 2.2 Proportional Control (P Mode)

### 2.2.1 Operational Theory

The controller output is **proportional to the instantaneous error**:

$$u(t) = u_0 + K_c \cdot e(t)$$

Where:
- $u(t)$ = Controller output at time $t$
- $u_0$ = Bias value (controller output when error is zero)
- $K_c$ = **Proportional Gain** (dimensionless or in %/% units)
- $e(t)$ = Error signal

### 2.2.2 Proportional Band (PB)

In industrial controllers, instead of $K_c$, the **Proportional Band** is used:

$$PB(\%) = \frac{100}{K_c}$$

A **small PB** means a **high gain** (aggressive control), and a **large PB** means a **low gain** (gentle control).

### 2.2.3 Offset Error (Residual Error)

The major drawback of pure P-control is **offset**. When a load change occurs, the controller settles at a value **different from the set point**. The offset is given by:

$$e_{offset} = \frac{\Delta \text{Load}}{1 + K_c \cdot K_p}$$

Where $K_p$ is the process gain. Higher $K_c$ reduces offset but **never eliminates** it.

---

## 2.3 Integral Control (I Mode / Reset Action)

### 2.3.1 Operational Theory

The controller output is **proportional to the time-integral of the error**:

$$u(t) = u_0 + \frac{K_c}{T_i} \int_0^t e(\tau) \, d\tau$$

Where:
- $T_i$ = **Integral Time** (also called **Reset Time**) in minutes/seconds
- $1/T_i$ = **Reset Rate** (repeats per minute)

### 2.3.2 Why It Eliminates Offset

As long as **any error exists**, the integral term **keeps accumulating** and changing the output. The only way the system reaches steady state is when $e(t) = 0$. Hence, **integral action guarantees zero steady-state error**.

### 2.3.3 Disadvantage — Integral Windup

If the actuator saturates (cannot physically respond), the integral continues to accumulate, causing large overshoot when the error eventually reverses. This is called **integral windup**, mitigated by **anti-windup circuits**.

---

## 2.4 Derivative Control (D Mode / Rate Action)

### 2.4.1 Operational Theory

The controller output is **proportional to the rate of change of error**:

$$u(t) = u_0 + K_c \cdot T_d \cdot \frac{de(t)}{dt}$$

Where:
- $T_d$ = **Derivative Time** in minutes/seconds

### 2.4.2 Why It Anticipates

The derivative term **acts on the future** by looking at the **slope** of the error. If error is changing rapidly, the controller responds strongly even if the error itself is small. This provides:
- **Improved damping**
- **Reduced overshoot**
- **Faster response**

### 2.4.3 Limitation

Derivative action **cannot respond to constant (DC) errors** because $\frac{de}{dt} = 0$ in steady state. It is **never used alone**, always combined with P or PI.

---

## 2.5 Combined Control Modes

### 2.5.1 PI Controller

$$u(t) = u_0 + K_c \left[ e(t) + \frac{1}{T_i}\int_0^t e(\tau) \, d\tau \right]$$

- Combines **fast response** of P with **zero offset** of I
- **Most widely used** controller in industry (covers ~90% of applications)

### 2.5.2 PD Controller

$$u(t) = u_0 + K_c \left[ e(t) + T_d \cdot \frac{de(t)}{dt} \right]$$

- Adds **anticipatory action** to P-mode
- Used where **stability** is critical (e.g., servo position control)

### 2.5.3 PID Controller (The Universal Controller)

$$u(t) = u_0 + K_c \left[ e(t) + \frac{1}{T_i}\int_0^t e(\tau) \, d\tau + T_d \cdot \frac{de(t)}{dt} \right]$$

In the **Laplace (s-) domain**, the PID transfer function is:

$$G_c(s) = K_c \left[ 1 + \frac{1}{T_i s} + T_d s \right] = \frac{K_c(T_i T_d s^2 + T_i s + 1)}{T_i s}$$

This is the **workhorse of industrial automation**.

---

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Formula Reference** — Memorize these for KTU examinations.

| **S.No.** | **Parameter / Concept** | **Formula / Expression** | **Units** | **Key Remark** |
|:---:|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Error Signal | $e(t) = SP - PV$ | Same as PV | Foundation of all control modes |
| 2 | On-Off Output | $u(t) = u_{max}$ if $e>0$ | — | Discontinuous, two states |
| 3 | P-Mode Output | $u(t) = u_0 + K_c \cdot e(t)$ | % | Produces offset |
| 4 | I-Mode Output | $u(t) = u_0 + \frac{K_c}{T_i}\int e \, dt$ | % | Eliminates offset |
| 5 | D-Mode Output | $u(t) = u_0 + K_c T_d \cdot \frac{de}{dt}$ | % | Anticipatory action |
| 6 | PI-Mode Output | $u(t) = u_0 + K_c \left[ e + \frac{1}{T_i}\int e \, dt \right]$ | % | Zero offset, fast |
| 7 | PD-Mode Output | $u(t) = u_0 + K_c \left[ e + T_d \frac{de}{dt} \right]$ | % | Stable, anticipatory |
| 8 | PID-Mode Output | $u(t) = u_0 + K_c \left[ e + \frac{1}{T_i}\int e \, dt + T_d \frac{de}{dt} \right]$ | % | Universal controller |
| 9 | Proportional Band | $PB = \frac{100}{K_c}$ | % | Inverse of gain |
| 10 | Offset (P-mode) | $e_{off} = \frac{\Delta L}{1 + K_c K_p}$ | Same as PV | Goes to 0 as $K_c \to \infty$ |
| 11 | PID Transfer Function | $G_c(s) = K_c \left[ 1 + \frac{1}{T_i s} + T_d s \right]$ | — | s-domain form |
| 12 | Reset Rate | $RR = \frac{1}{T_i}$ | repeats/min | Inverse of integral time |

> [!NOTE]
> **Units Convention:** $K_c$ is **dimensionless**, $T_i$ and $T_d$ are in **minutes (industrial)** or **seconds (electronic)**. The output $u(t)$ is typically normalized to **0–100%**.

# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: PID Transfer Function from Time-Domain Equation

We start with the **ideal parallel (non-interacting) form** of the PID controller in the time domain:

$$u(t) = K_c \cdot e(t) + \frac{K_c}{T_i}\int_0^t e(\tau) \, d\tau + K_c \cdot T_d \cdot \frac{de(t)}{dt}$$

We ignore the bias $u_0$ (it represents a constant that does not affect dynamics) and apply the **Laplace Transform** term-by-term.

**Step 1 — Laplace transform of proportional term:**

$$K_c \cdot e(t) \xrightarrow{\mathcal{L}} K_c \cdot E(s)$$

**Step 2 — Laplace transform of integral term** (using the property $\mathcal{L}\{\int_0^t e(\tau)d\tau\} = \frac{E(s)}{s}$):

$$\frac{K_c}{T_i}\int_0^t e(\tau) \, d\tau \xrightarrow{\mathcal{L}} \frac{K_c}{T_i \cdot s} \cdot E(s)$$

**Step 3 — Laplace transform of derivative term** (using the property $\mathcal{L}\{\frac{de(t)}{dt}\} = s \cdot E(s)$ — assuming zero initial conditions):

$$K_c \cdot T_d \cdot \frac{de(t)}{dt} \xrightarrow{\mathcal{L}} K_c \cdot T_d \cdot s \cdot E(s)$$

**Step 4 — Combine all three terms in the s-domain:**

$$U(s) = K_c \cdot E(s) + \frac{K_c}{T_i s} \cdot E(s) + K_c T_d s \cdot E(s)$$

Factor out $K_c \cdot E(s)$:

$$U(s) = K_c \cdot E(s) \left[ 1 + \frac{1}{T_i s} + T_d s \right]$$

**Step 5 — Form the controller transfer function** $G_c(s) = \frac{U(s)}{E(s)}$:

$$G_c(s) = K_c \left[ 1 + \frac{1}{T_i s} + T_d s \right]$$

**Step 6 — Bring to common denominator** $T_i s$:

$$G_c(s) = K_c \cdot \frac{T_i s + 1 + T_i T_d s^2}{T_i s}$$

**Step 7 — Rearrange the polynomial in standard quadratic form:**

$$G_c(s) = \frac{K_c \left[ T_i T_d \cdot s^2 + T_i \cdot s + 1 \right]}{T_i \cdot s}$$

This is the **standard parallel-form PID transfer function** used universally in KTU examinations.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation:** The numerator is a **second-order polynomial in $s$** containing two zeros (which shape the transient response), and the denominator has a **single pole at the origin** (which provides the integral action that eliminates steady-state error).

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Steady-State Offset of a Pure P-Controller

Consider a **unity-feedback control system** with a P-controller $G_c(s) = K_c$ controlling a first-order process $G_p(s) = \frac{K_p}{\tau_p s + 1}$ with a load disturbance $D(s)$ entering at the process input.

The closed-loop transfer function from disturbance to error is:

$$E(s) = \frac{-D(s) \cdot G_p(s)}{1 + G_c(s) \cdot G_p(s)} = \frac{-D(s) \cdot \frac{K_p}{\tau_p s + 1}}{1 + K_c \cdot \frac{K_p}{\tau_p s + 1}}$$

Apply the **Final Value Theorem** to find steady-state offset $e_{ss}$ for a **step disturbance of magnitude $\Delta L$** (i.e., $D(s) = \frac{\Delta L}{s}$):

$$e_{ss} = \lim_{s \to 0} s \cdot E(s) = \lim_{s \to 0} s \cdot \frac{-(\Delta L / s) \cdot \frac{K_p}{\tau_p s + 1}}{1 + K_c \cdot \frac{K_p}{\tau_p s + 1}}$$

Simplify — the $s$ in the numerator cancels the $1/s$ from the step:

$$e_{ss} = \lim_{s \to 0} \frac{-\Delta L \cdot \frac{K_p}{\tau_p s + 1}}{1 + K_c \cdot \frac{K_p}{\tau_p s + 1}}$$

Evaluate at $s = 0$ (i.e., $\tau_p \cdot 0 + 1 = 1$):

$$e_{ss} = \frac{-\Delta L \cdot K_p}{1 + K_c \cdot K_p}$$

Taking magnitude:

$$\vert e_{ss} \vert = \frac{\Delta L \cdot K_p}{1 + K_c K_p}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Insight:** As $K_c \to \infty$, the offset $\to 0$, but in practice, infinite gain is impossible and instability arises. This is precisely why **integral action is required** for offset-free control.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Why Integral Action Eliminates Steady-State Error

Consider a system with controller transfer function containing an integrator:

$$G_c(s) = \frac{K_c}{T_i s}$$

For a step input $R(s) = \frac{R_0}{s}$ and unity feedback:

$$E(s) = \frac{R(s)}{1 + G_c(s) G_p(s)} = \frac{R_0/s}{1 + \frac{K_c K_p}{T_i s (\tau_p s + 1)}}$$

Multiply numerator and denominator by $T_i s (\tau_p s + 1)$:

$$E(s) = \frac{R_0 \cdot T_i (\tau_p s + 1)}{T_i s (\tau_p s + 1) + K_c K_p}$$

Apply the **Final Value Theorem**:

$$e_{ss} = \lim_{s \to 0} s \cdot E(s) = \lim_{s \to 0} \frac{R_0 \cdot T_i \cdot s \cdot (\tau_p s + 1)}{T_i s (\tau_p s + 1) + K_c K_p}$$

At $s = 0$, the numerator becomes $0$ and the denominator becomes $K_c K_p$:

$$e_{ss} = \frac{0}{K_c K_p} = 0$$

Hence, **integral action guarantees zero steady-state error for a step input**.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — P, I, D Actions

| **Property** | **P-Action** | **I-Action** | **D-Action** |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Mathematical Basis** | $K_c \cdot e(t)$ | $\frac{K_c}{T_i}\int e \, dt$ | $K_c T_d \cdot \frac{de}{dt}$ |
| **Effect on Rise Time** | Decreases | Decreases (slightly) | Minor effect |
| **Effect on Overshoot** | Increases | Increases | Decreases |
| **Effect on Settling Time** | Small change | Increases | Decreases |
| **Effect on Steady-State Error** | Reduces but not zero | **Eliminates** | No effect (zero on DC) |
| **Effect on Stability** | Degrades if $K_c$ too high | Degrades more | Improves |
| **Acts On** | Present | Past | Future |
| **Standalone Use** | Yes (limited) | Never (causes oscillation) | Never (zero on constant error) |

---

## 3.5 Numerical Worked Example — PID Response Calculation

**Problem:** A PID controller has $K_c = 2$, $T_i = 0.5$ min, $T_d = 0.1$ min. The error signal is a step of magnitude 5 (in PV units), and its rate of change at $t = 0$ is $2$ units/s. Find the controller output at $t = 0$ and at $t = 1$ minute (assuming constant error of 5).

**Step 1 — Identify the values:**
- $K_c = 2$, $T_i = 0.5$ min, $T_d = 0.1$ min
- $e(0) = 5$, $e(1) = 5$ (constant), $\frac{de}{dt}\vert_{t=0} = 2$

**Step 2 — At $t = 0$:**

$$u(0) = K_c \left[ e(0) + \frac{1}{T_i}\int_0^0 e \, dt + T_d \frac{de}{dt}\vert_{t=0} \right]$$

The integral term at $t=0$ is 0. Therefore:

$$u(0) = 2 \left[ 5 + 0 + 0.1 \times 2 \right] = 2 \times 5.2 = 10.4$$

**Step 3 — At $t = 1$ min (error constant, so $\frac{de}{dt} = 0$):**

$$u(1) = 2 \left[ 5 + \frac{1}{0.5}\int_0^1 5 \, dt + 0 \right] = 2 \left[ 5 + 2 \times 5 \right] = 2 \times 15 = 30$$

**Step 4 — Interpretation:** The proportional term contributes **10**, the integral term contributes **20** (which has grown over time), and the derivative term is **zero** because the error is constant.

# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram of a Generic Closed-Loop Control System

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates how a controller using a specific control mode fits into the closed-loop architecture. Note that the **Controller Block** can be implemented using any of the P, I, D, PI, PD, or PID algorithms discussed in the previous sections.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    SP[Set Point SP] --> SumAdd[Summing Junction]
    PVfb[Process Variable PV] --> SumAdd
    SumAdd -->|Error e of t| Cont[Controller Block P, PI, PID etc]
    Cont -->|Output u of t| Actu[Final Control Element Valve, Heater, Motor]
    Actu --> Proc[Process Plant]
    Proc --> Sensor[Sensor Transmitter]
    Sensor --> PVfb
```

**Explanation of Flow:**
- The **Summing Junction** computes the error $e(t) = SP - PV$.
- The **Controller Block** applies the chosen control mode (P, PI, PID, etc.) to generate $u(t)$.
- The **Final Control Element** physically manipulates the process (e.g., opens a valve, adjusts a heater).
- The **Process Plant** is the physical system being controlled.
- The **Sensor/Transmitter** measures the process variable and feeds it back to complete the loop.

---

## 4.2 Block Diagram of an On-Off Controller with Dead Band

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    SP[Set Point SP] --> Comp[Comparator with Hysteresis]
    PVin[Process Variable PV] --> Comp
    Comp -->|Above upper threshold| RelayOn[Relay Energized ON]
    Comp -->|Below lower threshold| RelayOff[Relay De-energized OFF]
    RelayOn --> Load[Load Heater, Motor]
    RelayOff --> Load
    Load --> Proc[Process]
    Proc --> Sensor[Sensor]
    Sensor --> PVin
```

**Explanation:** The comparator output depends on whether the error exceeds the **dead-band threshold** $\Delta e$. This prevents rapid relay chattering near the set point.

---

## 4.3 Functional Block Architecture of a PID Controller

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph ErrorPath[Error Input]
        Ein[Error e of t] --> SplitA((Split))
    end
    
    subgraph PPath[Proportional Path]
        SplitA --> GainP[Gain Kc]
        GainP --> SumOut((Summing))
    end
    
    subgraph IPath[Integral Path]
        SplitA --> IntBlock[Integrator 1 over Ti s]
        IntBlock --> GainI[Gain Kc]
        GainI --> SumOut
    end
    
    subgraph DPath[Derivative Path]
        SplitA --> DiffBlock[Differentiator s]
        DiffBlock --> GainD[Gain Kc times Td]
        GainD --> SumOut
    end
    
    SumOut --> Bias[Add Bias u0]
    Bias --> Cout[Controller Output u of t]
```

**Explanation:**
- The error signal is **split into three parallel paths** — Proportional, Integral, and Derivative.
- Each path applies its own gain and operation.
- The three contributions are **summed** and added to the bias $u_0$ to produce $u(t)$.

---

## 4.4 Time-Response Comparison of Control Modes

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Resp[Step Response Curves]
        direction TB
        OnOff["On-Off: Continuous Oscillation"]
        P["P-mode: Fast but with offset"]
        PI["PI-mode: Fast, zero offset, slight overshoot"]
        PID["PID-mode: Fast, zero offset, minimal overshoot"]
    end
    
    OnOff --- P
    P --- PI
    PI --- PID
```

**Explanation:** This conceptual chart shows how adding more terms to the controller improves performance:
- **On-Off**: oscillates forever.
- **P**: settles fast but at wrong value (offset).
- **PI**: settles at correct value with some overshoot.
- **PID**: settles at correct value with minimal overshoot and best stability.

---

## 4.5 Controller Tuning Knob Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Knobs[Operator Tuning Interface]
        KcKnob[Proportional Gain Kc]
        TiKnob[Integral Time Ti]
        TdKnob[Derivative Time Td]
    end
    
    subgraph PID[PID Algorithm Core]
        Algo[Parallel PID Equation]
    end
    
    subgraph Effect[Effect on Response]
        EffKc[Speed of Response]
        EffTi[Steady State Error]
        EffTd[Stability and Damping]
    end
    
    KcKnob --> Algo
    TiKnob --> Algo
    TdKnob --> Algo
    
    Algo --> EffKc
    Algo --> EffTi
    Algo --> EffTd
```

**Explanation:** Each tuning knob has a distinct physical effect — a critical KTU exam concept. Increasing $K_c$ speeds up response but degrades stability; $T_i$ eliminates offset; $T_d$ improves damping.

# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Define the term "control mode" and list any four commonly used control modes in process instrumentation.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **control mode** is a predefined mathematical rule or algorithm used by an automatic controller to generate its corrective output $u(t)$ in response to the error signal $e(t)$ between the set point (SP) and process variable (PV).

$$u(t) = f[e(t)]$$

The four commonly used control modes are:
1. **On-Off (Two-Position) Control** — discontinuous
2. **Proportional (P) Control** — continuous, error-proportional
3. **Integral (I) Control** — continuous, time-integral of error
4. **Derivative (D) Control** — continuous, rate of change of error
5. **PID Control** — combination of P, I, and D

*(Any four valid modes — 1 mark for definition + 0.5 each for the four modes)*

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Explain the term "offset" in proportional control. Why does pure integral control eliminate offset?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Offset (1.5 marks):** Offset is the **persistent, non-zero steady-state error** that remains in a pure proportional controller when a load change occurs. The P-controller settles at a process variable value different from the set point.

$$e_{offset} = \frac{\Delta L \cdot K_p}{1 + K_c K_p}$$

This exists because the P-controller needs a **non-zero error to produce a non-zero output** to balance the load. As $K_c$ increases, offset decreases but is never zero.

**Why Integral Eliminates Offset (1.5 marks):** The I-controller output depends on the **time-integral of the error**. As long as any error exists, the integral term continues to grow, changing the controller output. Steady state is reached **only when $e(t) = 0$**, since any non-zero error would keep shifting the output. Therefore, integral action guarantees **zero steady-state offset**.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Module 4]`

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the operation of a **Proportional-Integral (PI) controller**. Derive its transfer function and discuss how it eliminates offset. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A PI controller has $K_c = 4$ and $T_i = 0.8$ min. A step change in set point of magnitude 10 units is applied. Calculate:
1. The controller output at $t = 0$
2. The controller output at $t = 2$ min
3. The error after 2 minutes

Assume $u_0 = 25\%$ and the error remains constant at 10 throughout. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Question A(a) — 7 marks**

**Block Diagram (2 marks):** Refer to Section 4.3 of these notes, specifically the **P and I paths only** (derivative path excluded). The block diagram consists of:
- A summing junction computing $e(t) = SP - PV$
- A parallel structure with two paths: P-path ($K_c$) and I-path ($\frac{K_c}{T_i s}$)
- A summing junction adding both outputs with bias $u_0$

**Mathematical Equation (2 marks):**

$$u(t) = u_0 + K_c \cdot e(t) + \frac{K_c}{T_i}\int_0^t e(\tau) \, d\tau$$

**Transfer Function Derivation (2 marks):** Taking the Laplace transform with zero initial conditions:

$$U(s) = u_0/s + K_c E(s) + \frac{K_c}{T_i s} E(s)$$

Ignoring the bias term for the transfer function definition:

$$G_{PI}(s) = \frac{U(s)}{E(s)} = K_c \left[ 1 + \frac{1}{T_i s} \right] = \frac{K_c(T_i s + 1)}{T_i s}$$

**Offset Elimination Discussion (1 mark):** The PI controller contains an integrator ($1/s$ term) in its transfer function. For a step input disturbance, applying the **Final Value Theorem** to the closed-loop error gives $e_{ss} = 0$. This is because the integrator's pole at the origin increases the system type by one, making the steady-state error to a step input exactly zero.

---

#### **Model Solution for Question A(b) — 7 marks**

**Given:**
- $K_c = 4$, $T_i = 0.8$ min
- Step input (set point change) of magnitude 10 units
- $u_0 = 25\%$, error $e(t) = 10$ (assumed constant)

**The PI controller equation is:**

$$u(t) = u_0 + K_c \cdot e(t) + \frac{K_c}{T_i}\int_0^t e(\tau) \, d\tau$$

**Part (1) — Controller output at $t = 0$ (2 marks):**

At $t = 0$, the integral $\int_0^0 e(\tau) d\tau = 0$. Therefore:

$$u(0) = 25 + 4 \times 10 + \frac{4}{0.8} \times 0 = 25 + 40 + 0 = 65\%$$

**Part (2) — Controller output at $t = 2$ min (3 marks):**

The integral term evaluates to:

$$\int_0^2 e(\tau) \, d\tau = 10 \times 2 = 20$$

Substituting:

$$u(2) = 25 + 4 \times 10 + \frac{4}{0.8} \times 20 = 25 + 40 + 100 = 165\%$$

Since the controller output is **physically limited to 100%** (saturation), the actual output is **100%** (saturated), with the extra 65% representing the **integrator wind-up** that must be handled by anti-windup logic.

**Part (3) — Error after 2 minutes (2 marks):**

Because the output has saturated, the process variable will rise rapidly. By $t = 2$ min, the error may not have reached zero. In the idealized linear case (without saturation):

$$e_{ss} = 0 \text{ (theoretically)}$$

In practice, the error at $t = 2$ min depends on the process dynamics. Given the question's assumption of constant error, the **analytical answer is $e = 0$** (asymptotically) because the I-term is still integrating. If the question intends a snapshot at $t = 2$ min with assumed constant error, then $e(2) = 10$ (per the assumption).

---

### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module 4]`

**(a)** Explain the operation of a **PID controller** with a neat block diagram. Derive its ideal transfer function in the s-domain and explain the effect of each of the three terms on system response. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A temperature control loop uses a PID controller with $K_c = 1.5$, $T_i = 2$ min, $T_d = 0.3$ min. The process has $K_p = 2$ and time constant $\tau_p = 1$ min. For a step set-point change of 50°C:
1. Write the controller output equation.
2. Find the initial output at $t = 0^+$ (assume $e(0^+) = 50$ and $\frac{de}{dt}\vert_{0^+} = 30$ °C/min).
3. What will be the output in steady state? **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Question B(a) — 7 marks**

**Block Diagram (2 marks):** Refer to Section 4.3 of these notes — the full three-path diagram with P, I, and D blocks.

**PID Equation (1 mark):**

$$u(t) = u_0 + K_c \left[ e(t) + \frac{1}{T_i}\int_0^t e(\tau) \, d\tau + T_d \cdot \frac{de(t)}{dt} \right]$$

**Transfer Function Derivation (2 marks):** Taking the Laplace transform:

$$U(s) = U_0(s) + K_c E(s) + \frac{K_c}{T_i s} E(s) + K_c T_d s \cdot E(s)$$

$$G_{PID}(s) = K_c \left[ 1 + \frac{1}{T_i s} + T_d s \right] = \frac{K_c(T_i T_d s^2 + T_i s + 1)}{T_i s}$$

**Effect of Each Term (2 marks):**

| **Term** | **Effect on Response** |
|---|---|
| **Proportional ($K_c e$)** | Provides immediate corrective action proportional to present error; reduces rise time but cannot eliminate offset. |
| **Integral ($\frac{K_c}{T_i}\int e\, dt$)** | Eliminates steady-state offset; increases overshoot and settling time. |
| **Derivative ($K_c T_d \frac{de}{dt}$)** | Anticipates future error; reduces overshoot, improves damping and stability. |

---

#### **Model Solution for Question B(b) — 7 marks**

**Given:**
- $K_c = 1.5$, $T_i = 2$ min, $T_d = 0.3$ min
- $e(0^+) = 50$ °C, $\frac{de}{dt}\vert_{0^+} = 30$ °C/min
- Set point step = 50°C

**Part (1) — Output equation (2 marks):**

Assuming $u_0 = 0$ (no bias given, standard assumption):

$$u(t) = 1.5 \left[ e(t) + \frac{1}{2}\int_0^t e(\tau) \, d\tau + 0.3 \cdot \frac{de(t)}{dt} \right]$$

$$u(t) = 1.5 \cdot e(t) + 0.75 \int_0^t e(\tau) \, d\tau + 0.45 \cdot \frac{de(t)}{dt}$$

**Part (2) — Initial output at $t = 0^+$ (3 marks):**

At $t = 0^+$, the integral $\int_0^{0^+} e(\tau) d\tau = 0$. Therefore:

$$u(0^+) = 1.5 \times 50 + 0.75 \times 0 + 0.45 \times 30$$

$$u(0^+) = 75 + 0 + 13.5 = 88.5\%$$

**Part (3) — Steady-state output (2 marks):**

In steady state, $e_{ss} = 0$ (offset eliminated by integral action) and $\frac{de}{dt} = 0$ (no change in error). All transient terms vanish, leaving only the bias $u_0$. Since $u_0 = 0$:

$$u_{ss} = 0\%$$

This represents the **new equilibrium operating point** of the controller, where it has perfectly tracked the set point with zero error.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> - **Forgetting the bias $u_0$** in derivations: many students omit it in the final answer, losing 1 mark.
> - **Mixing up P, I, D signs**: the derivative acts on $e(t)$, not on the negative error. Sign errors cost full marks.
> - **Confusing proportional band and gain**: PB = 100/$K_c$ — direction matters.
> - **Wrong units for $T_i$, $T_d$**: must clearly state minutes vs. seconds.
> - **Stating that I-mode or D-mode can be used alone**: this is incorrect and loses 1 mark.
> - **Saying "PID is best for all applications"**: oversimplification — lead-lag and other controllers are used in specific cases.
> - **Forgetting the windup effect**: in numerical PI problems where the calculated output exceeds 100%, students must mention **saturation and anti-windup**, otherwise lose 1 mark.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU 2024 Module 4: Control Modes**

- **Control Mode = Algorithm mapping $e(t) \to u(t)$** — the heart of any closed-loop controller.
- **On-Off Control** is **discontinuous**, simplest, used in thermostats; suffers from oscillation and uses **dead band** to prevent relay chattering.
- **P-Control** has output $u = K_c \cdot e$, introduces **offset**, defined by **Proportional Band** $PB = 100/K_c$.
- **I-Control** has output $u \propto \int e\, dt$, **eliminates offset** but causes **integral windup** if not managed.
- **D-Control** has output $u \propto \frac{de}{dt}$, **anticipates** future errors, **cannot be used alone**, improves damping.
- **PI Controller** is the **most widely used** industrial controller — combines P's speed and I's zero offset.
- **PD Controller** adds **stability** to P-control, used in **servo systems** and position control.
- **PID Controller** is the **universal controller** with transfer function:

$$G_c(s) = \frac{K_c(T_i T_d s^2 + T_i s + 1)}{T_i s}$$

- **Steady-state offset** of P-controller is $e_{off} = \frac{\Delta L \cdot K_p}{1 + K_c K_p}$, and it can never be zero for finite $K_c$.
- **Integral action increases system type by 1** — for a step input, steady-state error becomes zero.
- **Derivative action increases system type zero-by-zero** — improves transient response, has no effect on DC error.
- **Tuning Knobs and Their Effects**:
  - Increasing $K_c$ → faster response, more overshoot, less stability
  - Decreasing $T_i$ → faster offset elimination, more oscillation
  - Increasing $T_d$ → more damping, less overshoot
- **Tuning Methods** to remember for KTU: **Ziegler-Nichols (Ultimate Gain & Ultimate Period)**, **Cohen-Coon**, and **Tyreus-Luyben**.
- **Laplace Domain Forms** for each mode:
  - P: $K_c$
  - I: $\frac{K_c}{T_i s}$
  - D: $K_c T_d s$
  - PI: $K_c \left(1 + \frac{1}{T_i s}\right)$
  - PD: $K_c (1 + T_d s)$
  - PID: $K_c \left(1 + \frac{1}{T_i s} + T_d s\right)$
- **Anti-Windup Schemes**: clamping, back-calculation, conditional integration — **must be mentioned in PI/PID numerical problems** where output exceeds limits.
- **Industrial Symbols**: $\%P$ or PB for proportional band, $RR$ for reset rate ($=1/T_i$), $T_d$ or $D$ for derivative time.
- **Exam Tip**: Always write the **time-domain equation first**, then the **Laplace transform**, then the **transfer function** — this three-step structure earns full marks in KTU derivations.
- **Common Mistake to Avoid**: Never write $u = K_c \cdot e$ for P-controller without the bias $u_0$, otherwise the **steady-state operating point is wrong**.
